chapters.title: The Land Between Two Rivers
sentences: Mesopotamia (the name itself is Greek, meaning "between the rivers") is a flat, hot plain in what is today southern Iraq, between two long rivers: the Tigris (the eastern river, faster-flowing and prone to violent floods) and the Euphrates (the western river, slower and more navigable).
Both rivers begin in the snowy mountains of eastern Turkey, flow southeast through what are now Syria and Iraq, and empty into the Persian Gulf.
Between them lies a long, narrow strip of land — roughly the size of Florida — that is one of the strangest agricultural environments on earth.
It is strange because it has almost nothing.
There is no stone for building, no metal ore, no decent timber, no native gold or silver.
The flatlands are baking hot in summer and cool in winter, with very little rainfall.
By every obvious measure, southern Iraq should be a marginal place to live — and indeed, the surrounding regions are mostly desert or scrubland.
What it has, instead, is silt.

Every spring the snow melts in the Anatolian mountains (Anatolia is the highland peninsula that makes up most of modern Turkey), and the meltwater rushes down the Tigris and Euphrates carrying enormous quantities of fine, fertile soil.
When the rivers overflow their banks each year, they deposit that soil across the floodplain in a thin layer of fresh earth.
Over thousands of years, that silt built up into some of the richest farmland in the world.

If you can control the water — divert it into canals, water your fields, drain the salt before it poisons the soil (in arid climates, irrigation water evaporates and leaves behind dissolved mineral salts that accumulate in the topsoil over time, eventually making it too salty for crops to grow) — you can grow more grain per acre than almost anywhere else in the ancient world.
That "if" is the entire story.
Farming Mesopotamia required cooperation on a scale that simple villages couldn't manage.
You needed canals dug by hundreds of laborers, maintained for generations, defended from upstream neighbors who might cut them off.
You needed someone to organize the work, store the surplus grain, and arbitrate disputes about water rights.
You needed, in other words, the inventions we now call government, law, writing, mathematics, and the city — and you needed all of them more or less at the same time.
Mesopotamia did not become civilized because its people were brilliant.
Its people became brilliant because the land forced them to invent civilization or starve.
That's the setting.
Now: who lived there?

The earliest farming villages on the southern Mesopotamian plain go back to about 6500 BCE, but the people we now call Sumerians appear in the archaeological record around 5000 BCE in the very southernmost part of the plain, near the marshlands at the head of the Persian Gulf.

The Sumerians settled a region they called Sumer (the southernmost zone of the Mesopotamian floodplain, roughly from the Persian Gulf coast inland about 125 miles (200 km) — an area about the size of New Jersey).
Nobody is entirely sure where the Sumerians came from.

Their language — Sumerian — was a language isolate, meaning it has no known relatives anywhere else in the world (not related to any European, Asian, African, or other Middle Eastern language family that linguists have identified).
They seem to have considered themselves the original inhabitants.
They called their land Kengir and themselves the black-headed people.
They are the first people in human history about whom we know enough to say anything detailed at all.
The Sumerians did not have the southern plain to themselves for long.
Beginning around 2500 BCE, a second group called the Akkadians began settling among the Sumerians in central Mesopotamia, just to the north of the Sumerian heartland.

The Akkadians originally came from the Syrian-Arabian steppe (the drier grasslands bordering the fertile river valley to the west and north), and what made them a distinct people from the Sumerians was language: the Sumerians spoke Sumerian, a language with no known relatives anywhere on earth, while the Akkadians spoke Akkadian, a Semitic language belonging to the same broad family that later produced Hebrew, Arabic, and Aramaic.
These two languages were as different from each other as Chinese and English.
The Akkadians adopted Sumerian writing and worshipped many of the same gods under slightly different names, but at home they spoke a completely different tongue.
It's important to understand what "a new group arrived" means in Mesopotamian history: it almost never means conquest followed by expulsion.
It means gradual settlement and intermarriage.

The Akkadians showed up as a minority moving into Sumerian territory, and the first generations had to learn Sumerian to function in the existing economy — temples, marketplaces, and laws all operated in Sumerian, and that's the language any newcomer needed.
But the Akkadians kept speaking Akkadian at home, and as more of them arrived, Sumerian merchants and officials began learning Akkadian for their own economic reasons.
Bilingual scribal schools formalized the two-language tradition; surviving cuneiform tablets include Sumerian-Akkadian vocabulary lists, the ancient equivalent of a Spanish-English dictionary.

When Akkadian-speaking kings eventually became the politically dominant group around 2334 BCE (under Sargon, who gets his own chapter later), the Sumerians weren't driven out or wiped out — they were still right there, still farming the same fields, still living in the same cities.

Over the next few centuries Sumerian stopped being an everyday spoken language and was replaced by Akkadian, the way Latin was gradually replaced by French, Spanish, and Italian after the fall of Rome.
The Sumerians as a distinct people slowly faded into the general Mesopotamian population through intermarriage and language shift.
They didn't vanish; they became the later peoples of Mesopotamia, just with a different language and a slightly altered culture.
The same pattern would repeat with each later wave of newcomers.

The Amorites (ah-MOR-ites) — yet another Semitic-speaking people, but from a different branch of the Semitic family than the Akkadians (roughly the way Spanish and Portuguese are both Romance languages but clearly distinct) — showed up around 2000 BCE.
They originally came from the western steppes, the dry grasslands stretching between the Euphrates and the Mediterranean coast (roughly the area of modern Syria and Jordan).

Like the Akkadians before them, they arrived initially as seasonal migrants and laborers, and gradually came to dominate the cities politically — the Amorites became the ruling class of what we now call Old Babylonian society, and Hammurabi (hah-moo-RAH-bee), the most famous Mesopotamian king (Chapter 7), was ethnically Amorite.
Then came the Kassites (a mountain people from the Zagros highlands east of the plain — the rugged mountain range separating the Mesopotamian lowlands from the Iranian plateau).

The Kassites spoke a language that was, like Sumerian, a language isolate — no known relatives anywhere, as different from Akkadian as Sumerian was, and as different from the Indo-European languages of Anatolia to the north.
Nobody is sure where the Kassite language came from; it died out, leaving barely any traces.
The Kassites drifted in as laborers and soldiers before taking over the throne of Babylon around 1595 BCE and ruling for over four hundred years.
Then there are the Assyrians, who deserve a slightly different note in this parade because, unlike all the other peoples just described, the Assyrians were not migrants.

They had always been in northern Mesopotamia — meaning: as far back as the written record goes, the upper Tigris valley was already populated by a Semitic-speaking people who called themselves Assyrians, who lived in a cluster of cities (Assur (ah-SHOOR), Nineveh (NIN-eh-veh), and Nimrud (nim-ROOD)) along a 60-mile stretch of the Tigris (100 km — about the distance from New York City to Philadelphia) in what is now northern Iraq, and who worshipped their own chief god Ashur.

Their region was geographically and climatically different from the southern Babylonian plain: rolling hills with more rainfall, better natural agriculture without the desperate dependence on irrigation, and direct contact with the mountain peoples of the north and east.

The Assyrians spoke a northern dialect of Akkadian, mutually intelligible with the southern Babylonian dialect the way American and British English are mutually intelligible — but they considered themselves a distinct people with their own military culture and historical identity.
While the Sumerians were the indigenous population of southern Mesopotamia, the Assyrians were the indigenous population of northern Mesopotamia.
After 900 BCE they would build the largest empire the region had yet seen.

Finally there are the Chaldeans (kal-DEE-anz) — yet another Semitic-speaking group, originally from the marshlands and tribal lands of the far south near the Persian Gulf, speaking a dialect closely related to Aramaic (a Semitic language, Chapter 9, that eventually replaced Akkadian across the entire region).

The Chaldeans were the last major wave of newcomers, and they would found the famous Neo-Babylonian Empire under Nebuchadnezzar II (neb-uh-kud-NEZ-er; the most famous Mesopotamian king, Chapter 12) in the 600s BCE.
By that point, the people of Mesopotamia carried the cultural DNA of every previous wave layered on top of each other.

Every new arrival had adopted the existing cuneiform writing system (the wedge-shaped script invented by the Sumerians around 3400 BCE for writing on wet clay tablets, Chapter 3), the existing law tradition, the existing temple religion — and added their own contributions before the next wave rolled in.
The Chaldeans were just the final layer in a stack three thousand years deep.
This is the unusual thing about Mesopotamian history: it is not the story of one people.
It is the story of one place — the river valley — through which a long parade of different peoples passed, each one inheriting and extending the traditions of the people before them.

Sumerian was still being taught in scribal schools (formal training institutions where young men spent years learning to read and write cuneiform under a master scribe's supervision) two thousand years after the last native Sumerian speaker died, the way Latin was still taught in European schools long after the fall of Rome.
Sumerian gods became Akkadian gods became Babylonian gods.

Sumerian myths became Akkadian myths became the source material for the biblical book of Genesis, written down by Hebrew scribes during the Babylonian Exile (the period in the 6th century BCE when the Babylonians conquered Jerusalem and deported the Jewish elite to Babylon, where they encountered Mesopotamian literature and wove elements of it into their own scriptures — Chapter 12).
The land was the constant; the peoples flowed over it like the floodwaters themselves.
There is one more piece of context worth setting up before we start: the city as the basic unit of Mesopotamian life.
Throughout most of its history, Mesopotamia was not unified.

It was a patchwork of independent city-states — individual cities, each ruling the farmland immediately around them, each with their own king and patron god, each warring constantly with their neighbors over land, water, and prestige.
Even when an unusually powerful king conquered all the others and built an empire, those empires were short-lived.
The default state of Mesopotamia was fragmentation.
The exceptional state was unity.

Most of the dramatic moments in the history that follows — Sargon, Hammurabi, Tiglath-Pileser (TIG-lath pih-LEE-zer), Nebuchadnezzar — are stories of individual conquerors who briefly forced unity onto the city-states before the system reverted to its natural fragmented state.

So: a flat, silty plain between two rivers, with no natural resources except dirt; populated successively by Sumerians, Akkadians, Amorites, Kassites, Assyrians, and Chaldeans across a span of time scholars call the Bronze Age (roughly 3300–1200 BCE, the era when bronze — an alloy of copper and tin — was the most advanced metal available for tools and weapons) and beyond; organized into competing city-states; producing in the process the world's first writing, first cities, first laws, first mathematics, first astronomy, first epic literature, and first historical empires.
That is what Mesopotamia is.
The rest of this tour is the story of how it happened, in roughly the order it happened, with the people, places, and inventions that made it happen.

chapters.title: Before the Cities
sentences: Our story proper begins around 5000 BCE, long before the great city-states that would make Mesopotamia famous.

The earliest people we can actually track — through the pottery, the tools, the post-holes of their houses, and eventually their temples — were farming villagers living in small settlements along the edges of the southern marshlands where the Tigris and Euphrates meet the Persian Gulf.

These people were already the ancestors of the Sumerians (the original people of southern Mesopotamia, about whom we set the scene in the previous chapter), though they wouldn't be fully recognizable as "Sumerians" for another thousand years.

They grew barley and emmer wheat (the same crops that were reaching the Nile Valley around this same date — Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 1), kept sheep and goats, fished the marshes, and built houses out of reed bundles and mud brick.
The first place that matters is Eridu (pronounced "air-ee-doo"), a small settlement on the southernmost edge of the Mesopotamian plain, just inland from the ancient Persian Gulf coastline.

Eridu was not yet a city in any meaningful sense — probably a few thousand people at most — but around 5000 BCE the villagers there did something that would turn out to echo for the next five thousand years: they built a small mud-brick platform and placed a temple on top of it. (The nearest parallel anywhere in the world is the Hongshan ritual complex at Niuheliang in northeastern China, where hilltop stone altars and a "goddess temple" appeared around 3500 BCE — about fifteen centuries later than Eridu; Ancient China Ch 4.) The temple was dedicated to Enki (the Sumerian god of fresh water, wisdom, and craftsmanship — one of the most important gods in the Mesopotamian pantheon, associated with the underground freshwater springs that made farming possible in such a dry land).
The temple was tiny by later standards — just a single room on a low mud platform — but it was the beginning of something.
The platform itself mattered for several reasons that stacked on top of each other.
The most immediate was practical.
Eridu sat near the marshlands at the head of the Persian Gulf, in flat country where seasonal floods, rising groundwater, and unpredictable river meandering could submerge low ground without warning.
A temple built directly on the dirt would be inundated periodically and its mud-brick walls would dissolve.
A platform — even a low one, just a meter or two above ground level — kept the building above the flood line.
The second reason was visibility.
Southern Mesopotamia is genuinely flat, with no natural hills or rocky outcrops to break the horizon, so even a small artificial mound becomes the highest point for kilometers around.
The temple on its platform was visible from the surrounding fields, the marshes, and the next village over.
It became the visible center of the community's landscape.
The third reason was theological, and it turned out to matter most over the long run.

Mesopotamian religion located the gods up — in the sky, on mountains, in the heavens. (This is a near-universal feature of human religion, but the Mesopotamians were unusually explicit about it: their temples were designed as literal meeting points between heaven and earth, and their priests physically climbed the temple platforms to perform rituals at the summit, as close to the sky as architecture could take them.) The problem for southern Mesopotamians was that they had no mountains.
Their gods lived "up," but there was no "up" to go to in their flat landscape.

The solution was to build an artificial mountain by hand — a place where priests could climb closer to the divine, where the gods could descend to meet humans, where the boundary between heaven and earth was symbolically thinned.
The Sumerians would later call the ziggurat — the multi-tiered stepped temple-platform that all of this would eventually grow into — the bond between heaven and earth.
By the time of the White Temple at Uruk a thousand years later, the practical flood-protection reason was almost forgotten.
The platform had become a theological requirement, the defining feature of how Mesopotamian temples were supposed to be built.

What was remarkable about Eridu's temple is that every time it deteriorated, the villagers tore it down and built a new one on exactly the same spot, using the rubble of the old one as the foundation for the new one.
They did this over and over for more than three thousand years.

Archaeologists who excavated Eridu in the 20th century found seventeen successive temples stacked directly on top of each other, with the oldest at the bottom dating to around 5000 BCE and the newest near the top dating to around 2000 BCE.

The Sumerians later said that their civilization's first king had received the gift of kingship from the gods at Eridu — "kingship descended from heaven to Eridu," says one of their oldest surviving documents — and their reverence for the place is visible in this obsessive rebuilding.
Eridu established the template for all subsequent Mesopotamian religion: temples are permanent places, not permanent buildings.
The physical structure can change, but the sacred spot must never move.

Every later ziggurat — the massive stepped temple-mountains that became the signature architectural form of Mesopotamia, including the White Temple at Uruk in the next chapter, the Great Ziggurat of Ur in Chapter 6, and the towering Etemenanki of Babylon in Chapter 12 — is a descendant of this idea that began at Eridu.

Around the same time that Eridu's first temples were being built and rebuilt, a goddess began rising in the religious imagination of the southern plain: Inanna (pronounced "ih-NAH-nah"), the goddess of love, war, fertility, and the planet Venus.

The cult of Inanna (a "cult" in the ancient-religion sense just means the organized worship of a particular deity, not anything sinister) spread from her main shrine at the city of Uruk (next chapter) to become the most important religious institution in Mesopotamia.
What the worship actually looked like in practice is worth describing, because Inanna's cult had several features unlike anything in modern Western religion.
Worship centered on her temple at Uruk (called the Eanna (eh-AH-nah), or "House of Heaven"), where a permanent staff of priests and priestesses lived and ran the daily rituals.

As in Mesopotamian temple religion generally, the goddess was understood to live physically inside her cult statue, and worship took the form of feeding her, dressing her, processing her statue through the city on festival days, and offering her food, beer, and incense — temple service in Mesopotamia was less about sermons or scripture and more about the practical care and feeding of a divine being who lived in the temple.

Inanna's cult included one specifically famous ritual called the sacred marriage (in modern scholarship, sometimes called by the Greek term hieros gamos), in which the king ritually took on the role of Inanna's bridegroom.

Surviving Sumerian hymns — including ones associated with the Ur III king Shulgi (Chapter 6) — describe the king and a high priestess representing the goddess sharing a marriage bed, with the union understood to ensure the fertility of the year's harvest and the prosperity of the city.
How literally this ritual was performed is genuinely debated by modern Assyriologists.

Some scholars read the surviving texts as evidence of an actual public sexual union between the king and a priestess; others argue the texts are poetic and the actual rite may have been more ceremonial than literal.

What the texts unambiguously establish is that the idea of the king as Inanna's bridegroom was central to royal legitimation — a Sumerian king was, theologically, married to the goddess of his city, and this marriage was the source of his right to rule.
The exact physical staging of the ritual is uncertain.
Inanna's priesthood was also unusually diverse in gender terms.

Cuneiform sources document categories of cultic personnel called gala, kurgarra, and assinnu who served in Inanna's temples in roles that didn't fit standard gender categories — some were castrated men who performed traditionally feminine ritual functions, some were women who served martial roles, some performed in deliberately cross-gendered ways during festivals.
The traditional interpretation is that Inanna's contradictory nature (love and war, masculine and feminine) was reflected in a priesthood that deliberately blurred the same boundaries.

This part is well-documented in actual Sumerian and Akkadian sources — it is not a modern projection. (One claim about Inanna's worship that has often appeared in popular history — that her temples ran "sacred prostitution" with women selling sexual services to worshippers as a religious offering — is more controversial than it sounds.

The claim mostly traces back to a passage in the Greek historian Herodotus describing Babylonian practices in the 5th century BCE, and to ambiguous Mesopotamian texts that some modern scholars argue have been misread.

A serious recent argument by Assyriologists like Stephanie Budin and Julia Assante is that "sacred prostitution" as commonly described may largely be a modern myth based on misreading of Greek and biblical sources.

The traditional view still has its defenders, but readers should know that this is contested and not the established fact it's often presented as.) Inanna herself was unlike any other deity in the ancient world: she was simultaneously the goddess of love (presiding over sex, marriage, and fertility) and the goddess of war (the patron of violent conquest and the ecstatic frenzy of battle), a contradiction that later cultures tended to split into separate deities but that Mesopotamians embraced as the core of her personality — she was powerful because she was contradictory.

Her symbol was an eight-pointed star (representing Venus, which appears alternately as the "morning star" and "evening star" — the Sumerians knew these were the same planet millennia before Western astronomy figured it out), and her sacred animal was the lion.

Over the next three thousand years Inanna would be renamed Ishtar (her Akkadian name, used by later Mesopotamian peoples) and eventually influence the Phoenician goddess Astarte, the Greek goddess Aphrodite, and arguably the Egyptian goddess Isis.
She is the oldest continuously-worshipped goddess in human history and her fingerprints are all over later Mediterranean religion.
Inanna's cult and the irrigation economy we're about to meet were not separate worlds — they were run by the same institution.
The temple that housed her priesthood also managed the grain stores, organized the labor crews, and directed the canal maintenance.
Religion and infrastructure were a single system.
And the infrastructure side of that system was solving the practical problem that made all of this possible: how to grow enough food in a place with very little rain.
Southern Mesopotamia receives only about 150 to 200 millimeters of rainfall per year (for comparison, that's about a quarter of what grows typical dryland wheat in the American Midwest).
Without irrigation, the floodplain is essentially a desert.

Around 3460 BCE, well before any real city existed, the villagers of southern Mesopotamia had already begun a project that would take generations: large-scale irrigation canals dug by coordinated labor crews, diverting water from the Tigris and Euphrates out across the flat plain to water fields of barley.

The basic technique was to cut a channel from the riverbank at an angle, leading water away from the main flow through a network of branching ditches that spread it across the fields like the veins of a leaf.
Earth dams and simple sluice gates (mud-brick barriers that could be opened or closed) controlled the flow.

The engineering was impressive — the canals had to be dug at the right gentle downward slope to keep water flowing by gravity alone, they had to be dredged of silt every year (the same fertile mud that made the soil rich would clog the channels if left alone), and they had to be defended against neighbors who might cut them off upstream — but the social organization was even more impressive.
You cannot build and maintain a regional canal network with a handful of independent farmers arguing about water rights.
You need a central authority who can compel people to work, store the surplus grain, arbitrate disputes, and punish defectors.
This is the earliest hint of what will eventually become government in southern Mesopotamia: the canal committee scaled up into the temple administration scaled up into the city-state.

One more piece of evidence about just how interconnected this early world was: in the graves and temple offerings of southern Mesopotamian villages dating back to around 3000 BCE, archaeologists find small, intensely blue-colored beads of lapis lazuli (a semi-precious blue stone that was the most prized decorative material in the ancient world — its deep blue color, flecked with gold-colored iron pyrite, was associated with the sky and the divine).
This is important because there is no lapis lazuli anywhere in Mesopotamia.

The stone came from a single source: the Sar-i Sang (SAR-ee SANG) mines in Badakhshan (bah-dahk-SHAHN), a remote mountain region in what is now northeastern Afghanistan — more than 3,000 kilometers from southern Iraq (roughly the distance from New York to Los Angeles), across deserts, rivers, and mountain passes. (The same mines also supplied the Indus Valley Civilization, which maintained a permanent trading outpost at Shortugai in Afghanistan specifically to secure lapis access — Indus Valley Ch 7.) The fact that Afghan lapis was reaching southern Mesopotamia by 3000 BCE proves that long-distance trade networks were already operating across Asia five thousand years ago — carrying not just lapis but also Oman copper, Lebanese cedar, and Indus Valley carnelian, along trade routes that predate the famous Silk Road by more than two thousand years.
The Sumerians of the early settlements were not isolated villagers.
They were connected to a world that stretched from the Indian Ocean to the Mediterranean, even before they had cities, writing, or any of the other things we associate with civilization.
This is the world on the edge of the breakthrough.
Small villages.
A sacred temple at Eridu that will be rebuilt seventeen times.
A rising goddess at Uruk.
Canal systems turning desert into farmland.
Long-distance trade bringing blue Afghan stone to the southern marshes.
It has taken from about 6500 BCE down to about 3800 BCE — almost three thousand years — to build up to this point.
And now, in the next few centuries, it is all going to come together into something the world has never seen before: the city.

chapters.title: The First City: Uruk and the Sumerian Breakthrough
sentences: Around 3800 BCE, a particular settlement on the southern Mesopotamian plain began to grow faster than any settlement in human history had ever grown before.

Its name was Uruk (pronounced "OO-rook"), and within six hundred years it would become the world's first real city — not just a big village but a new kind of human community with tens of thousands of residents, specialized workers, written records, monumental buildings, and long-distance trade.
Uruk is important enough that the era surrounding its rise is called the Uruk Period (~3800–2900 BCE).

It sits on what was then a branch of the Euphrates River (the river has since shifted course, leaving the ruins of Uruk in what is now a hot dry plain), in the southern part of Sumer (the southernmost zone of Mesopotamia, the Sumerian heartland).
The name "Uruk" may even be the source of the modern word "Iraq." Calling Uruk "the first city" deserves a quick word about what we mean by "city" and how confident we can be about the claim.

There were certainly large settlements in the world before Uruk. Çatalhöyük (chah-tahl-huh-YOOK) in Anatolia (modern Turkey) was inhabited by several thousand people as early as 7000 BCE — thousands of years before Uruk — with closely packed mud-brick houses and sophisticated wall paintings.

But Çatalhöyük was essentially a giant village: there's no clear evidence of specialized non-farming workers, no administrative apparatus, no monumental public architecture, no writing, no long-distance trade networks.
Archaeologists generally call it a "proto-city" or "large settlement," not a city in the technical sense.

Uruk is "first" in the sense of being the first settlement to clearly cross several thresholds at once: tens of thousands of inhabitants (most archaeologists estimate 40,000 to 80,000 by 3200 BCE), specialized labor (potters, weavers, metalworkers, scribes, and priests who didn't farm their own food), centralized administration through the temple, monumental architecture visible for kilometers, written record-keeping, and long-distance trade.
Anything that hits all of those is a city.
Uruk hit all of them first, and at a scale that nothing else had reached.

Recent archaeology has raised one serious complication: Tell Brak in northern Syria may have been developing comparable urbanism at roughly the same time as Uruk, possibly even slightly earlier — and some scholars in the last 15 years have argued Tell Brak deserves more credit as a "first city" candidate.
The case is real but contested.
The conventional answer remains Uruk, partly because Uruk had writing and Tell Brak didn't.
Either way, the urban breakthrough was happening in this part of the world around 3500 BCE and not anywhere else.

To put Uruk's "first" status in perspective by comparison with the other ancient civilizations the reader will eventually meet: by the time Uruk reached its peak around 3200 BCE, Egypt was just beginning its First Dynasty (~3100 BCE) and would not produce a comparably-scaled city until Memphis a few centuries later — though Upper Egypt at Hierakonpolis already had a town of 5,000–10,000 people with an industrial brewery and a temple by ~3600 BCE (Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 3).
Egyptian civilization was monumentally impressive but its population was distributed along the Nile in many smaller centers rather than concentrated in giant cities.
The Indus Valley civilization, in what is now Pakistan, would reach city-scale around 2600 BCE with Mohenjo-daro and Harappa — about 1,200 years after Uruk.

China's earliest urban-scale settlement was Liangzhu in the lower Yangtze delta around 3300–2300 BCE — only a few centuries behind Uruk, though in a different part of East Asia and on a different cultural trajectory (Ancient China Ch 5).

China's first dynastic-era capital, Erlitou, would not appear in the Yellow River plain until around 1900 BCE — nearly two thousand years after Uruk — and the Chinese tradition treats Erlitou rather than Liangzhu as the start of its civilizational story, though modern archaeology increasingly treats Liangzhu as a genuine first (Ancient China Ch 5, Ch 8).
Mesoamerica (the Olmecs, the predecessors of the Maya and Aztecs) would not reach urban scale until around 1200 BCE.

The Andes were closer than people once assumed — the Norte Chico civilization on the coast of Peru, centered on the city of Caral, was building monumental temple platforms by ~2900 BCE, only a few centuries behind Uruk (Early Andean Ch 2–3) — but without writing, without pottery, and on a radically different economic base (maritime protein rather than irrigated grain).
Uruk wasn't just first by a small margin.
It was first by a thousand years in some directions and three thousand years in others.
The Mesopotamian breakthrough happened in genuine cultural isolation, with no neighbors to copy from, and the rest of the world spent millennia catching up.
So the urban breakthrough happened here, in this corner of the world, ahead of everywhere else.
But that raises a different question: why here specifically?
Why Uruk, out of all the settlements scattered across the southern plain?
There are several reasons, and they all reinforce each other.
Uruk sat at an ideal junction for trade, where river traffic from the upstream Mesopotamian heartland met maritime traffic from the Persian Gulf.
The surrounding farmland was exceptionally fertile, and the irrigation systems serving it were already highly developed (see the previous chapter).
And crucially, Uruk was the main cult center for the goddess Inanna, whose temple complex — called the Eanna ("House of Heaven") — became the administrative heart of the growing city.
The Eanna wasn't just a place of worship.
It was the place that managed Uruk's irrigation water, stored its grain surplus, employed its craftsmen, directed its trade, and arbitrated its disputes.
The temple was simultaneously a church, a government, a warehouse, a factory, a bank, and a court.
Everything radiated out from it.

By 3200 BCE, Uruk covered six square kilometers and had a population of somewhere between forty thousand and eighty thousand people — ten to twenty times larger than any settlement that had ever existed anywhere on earth.
There had been nothing like this before.
In most of the world, humans lived in small villages of a few hundred people at most; the idea of living packed together with tens of thousands of strangers was inconceivable.
Running a city that size required solving problems that had never existed before, and in solving them the Sumerians invented almost all of the intellectual tools that civilization still uses today.
The first and most important invention was cuneiform, the wedge-shaped writing system that emerged at Uruk around 3400 BCE.
The name comes from the Latin cuneus, meaning "wedge," because the characters were made by pressing a cut reed stylus into wet clay at various angles to leave wedge-shaped marks.
Cuneiform was not invented to write poetry or record history or preserve prayers.
It was invented to do accounting.

The earliest cuneiform tablets recovered from Uruk are lists — lists of rations distributed to workers, lists of grain stored in temple warehouses, lists of sheep in the flock and oxen in the plow teams.
Writing was invented, in other words, to run spreadsheets.
The temple administration of a city of sixty thousand people could not possibly keep track of its obligations in anyone's head, and tally marks on clay were not expressive enough.
What happened next was not a single flash of inspiration by a lone genius but a gradual institutional process, driven by the practical needs of temple bureaucrats over several generations.

First came small clay tokens of different shapes (cones, spheres, discs), each representing a specific commodity or quantity, used as physical counters — the same kind of tokens had been in use across the Near East for thousands of years.
Then scribes began pressing those tokens into flat clay tablets to make a record of the count.
Then they realized they could skip the tokens and just draw the shapes directly — a drawing of an ox head meant "ox," a drawing of a bundle of grain meant "barley" — combined with numerical marks.
These pictograms were the seed of writing.

Over the next five hundred years, those pictograms were gradually stylized into combinations of wedge-impressions that could represent not just things but sounds, syllables, and eventually any word in any spoken language.

Cuneiform would go on to be used for over 3,400 years — one of the longest continuous uses of any technology in human history — and would be adopted to write at least a dozen different languages, from Sumerian and Akkadian to Hittite, Elamite, and Old Persian.
The final surviving cuneiform tablets date from the 1st century CE, written in a scribal tradition that began more than three thousand years earlier in the Eanna temple complex.

To appreciate how early this was: Egyptian hieroglyphic precursors — specifically the Tomb U-j labels at Abydos, ca. 3250 BCE — appeared within a century or two of cuneiform (Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 4), and the Proto-Elamite tablets at Susa are essentially contemporary (Elamite Civilization Ch 1).

Whether Egyptian writing was independently invented or influenced by Mesopotamian contact is debated, but the near-simultaneous emergence at Uruk, Abydos, and Susa looks less like borrowing and more like a regional explosion.

Chinese oracle bone script didn't emerge until around 1200 BCE — over two thousand years later — though China had its own long proto-symbol tradition stretching back to the Jiahu tortoise-shell markings (~7000 BCE), Banpo pottery marks (~4800 BCE), and Liangzhu jade inscriptions (~3000 BCE), none of them yet full writing but all of them part of the slow run-up to the Shang oracle bones (Ancient China Ch 2, Ch 3, Ch 5).
Mesoamerican writing systems appeared around 900 BCE.
The Sumerian invention of writing was, alongside the city itself, the single most consequential intellectual breakthrough of the ancient world.
Along with cuneiform came the cylinder seal — a small carved stone cylinder (usually about an inch long) that a person could roll across wet clay to leave a unique raised-relief impression.

The idea was that every important person — a temple official, a merchant, a land-owner — had their own custom-carved cylinder seal with a unique scene (a god, a ritual banquet, a combat between heroes and beasts).
When they needed to authorize a document or certify a package of goods, they rolled their seal across a bit of wet clay attached to it, leaving a "signature" that was effectively impossible to forge.
Cylinder seals are the ancient Mesopotamian equivalent of a notarized stamp, a signature, and a driver's license rolled into one.

The finest of them were carved from lapis lazuli (the blue Afghan stone from the previous chapter), carnelian (a red-orange gemstone from India; the Indus Valley bead-making process took weeks per batch — Indus Valley Ch 7), or hematite (a dense iron-rich stone), and the best carvers worked in microscopic detail that requires magnification to appreciate.
They are among the greatest miniature art objects of the ancient world, and they spread across the entire Near East and even reached the Indus Valley through trade.

At some point very early in the Uruk period — the exact date is debated, but archaeology suggests around 3500 BCE — the Sumerians invented something that would transform the world far beyond Mesopotamia: the wheel.

The wheel appeared first as a potter's wheel (a rotating flat platform on which a lump of clay could be shaped into a symmetrical vessel using steady hand pressure — a massive productivity improvement over hand-shaping), and then within a few centuries the same concept was adapted to transport, with wheels mounted on an axle under a wooden cart.

By 2500 BCE, Sumerian armies were using four-wheeled battle wagons drawn by teams of donkeys — the distant ancestors of the chariots that would dominate Bronze Age warfare for the next two thousand years.
The wheel is one of the rare inventions that seems so obvious in retrospect but was clearly not obvious at the time — several major civilizations never came up with it independently.

The earliest known wheeled vehicles outside Mesopotamia appear in the Pontic steppe (modern Ukraine and southern Russia) around 3300 BCE, possibly independently invented or possibly carried by trade contact.
Egypt, despite building the pyramids, did not adopt wheeled vehicles until chariots arrived around 1600 BCE via Near Eastern contact.
The pre-Columbian Americas, sub-Saharan Africa, and Oceania never developed wheeled transport at all.

The Sumerians' invention spread within a thousand years from the Mediterranean to the British Isles to the Indus Valley, carried by the same trade networks that were already moving lapis, copper, and cedar across Eurasia.
Around 3000 BCE, the Sumerians also worked out the sexagesimal number system — a base-60 counting system whose descendant survives today in every clock and every compass.
Why base 60?
Because 60 is divisible by 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 10, 12, 15, 20, and 30 — which is a ridiculously convenient set of fractions for a trading and engineering economy.

In a world where you're constantly dividing up rations among workers, calculating canal slopes, splitting a field's harvest among owners, and measuring out ingredients for beer brewing (more on that in a moment), base 60 gives you vastly more clean divisions than base 10.
The Sumerian sexagesimal system is the reason we still have 60 seconds in a minute, 60 minutes in an hour, and 360 degrees in a circle (360 = 6 × 60).
Every time you glance at a clock, you are using Sumerian mathematics that predates the pyramids of Egypt.
And now for one of the most delightful small facts about Uruk: the Sumerians were the world's first documented brewers.

By 3400 BCE, Uruk's scribes were recording beer ingredients in cuneiform tablets, and by 1800 BCE the Sumerians had composed the Hymn to Ninkasi (nin-KAH-see) — a poem dedicated to the goddess of brewing which doubles as the world's oldest surviving beer recipe (it describes a process of fermenting twice-baked bread in water with honey and wine).
Beer was not a luxury in Sumer.
It was infrastructure.
The word for "food" and the word for "beer" in cuneiform are nearly identical, and laborers working on public construction projects were partly paid in beer rations.
Hammurabi's law code (Chapter 7) regulated beer prices and punished tavern keepers who cheated customers.
The entire city of Uruk effectively ran on bread, barley porridge, and beer.
All of this was housed, administered, and orchestrated in massive temple complexes.
The most famous of these is the White Temple of Uruk, built around 3200 BCE at the height of Uruk's dominance.

The White Temple was a single-room mud-brick shrine dedicated to Anu (the Sumerian sky god, considered the father of all other gods), and it sat on top of a thirteen-meter-high whitewashed platform (roughly a four-story building) that rose out of the flat city like an artificial mountain.
It was probably the tallest building in the world at the time.

The White Temple was not a ziggurat in the later technical sense — it was simpler, just a single raised platform rather than the multi-tiered stepped pyramid that would become standard — but every later ziggurat in Mesopotamia is a direct architectural descendant of this structure.
The idea of placing a temple on top of a massive artificial platform, visible for miles across the flat plain, became the defining form of Mesopotamian religious architecture.
Running all of this was the temple economy — the particular Sumerian arrangement in which the city's religious institutions weren't just places of worship but also the primary economic engines.
The temple owned huge tracts of farmland.

The temple employed hundreds or thousands of workers on rations (these were not slaves but a dependent workforce -- some were free citizens fulfilling labor obligations, others were temple dependents who received food, clothing, and housing in exchange for their labor).
The temple ran weaving workshops and breweries.
The temple outfitted long-distance trading expeditions.
And the temple's scribes tracked all of it — with cuneiform, with standardized measures, with rations calculated in beer and grain.
Privately-owned businesses and land did exist, but the temple was the dominant economic actor, and temple administrators were effectively the first professional bureaucrats in human history.

The very idea of a bureaucracy — a class of trained specialists whose job is to keep track of resources, regulations, and obligations — is a Sumerian invention, and it was invented because running a city of sixty thousand people without one was impossible.

In the space of roughly five hundred years, a previously unremarkable river valley produced the world's first city, the world's first writing system, the world's first wheeled vehicles, the world's first base-60 mathematics, the world's first bureaucracy, the world's first monumental religious architecture, and the world's first documented brewery, all in one place.
Nothing comparable had ever happened before, and nothing comparable would happen again until the Industrial Revolution.
Uruk had proved that human beings could live in large, anonymous, organized communities governed by institutions rather than kinship — which is effectively the definition of civilization itself.
But Uruk could not stay alone.

The same template — temple, canal, scribe, surplus — was replicable, and within a few centuries more than a dozen cities had sprung up across the southern plain, each with its own king and its own patron god, each eyeing its neighbors with ambition and suspicion.
What happens when a dozen Uruks share the same narrow floodplain is the subject of the next chapter, and it involves a great deal of blood.

chapters.title: The Age of City-States
sentences: By about 2900 BCE, Uruk (the pioneering city from the previous chapter) was no longer alone.
The great Sumerian experiment had spread.

Across the southern Mesopotamian plain, in a band stretching from the Persian Gulf northward about 95 miles (150 km), more than a dozen smaller cities had grown up — each modeled on Uruk's template, each with its own temple and its own patron deity, each trying to dominate its neighbors.

This era (~2900–2334 BCE) is called the Early Dynastic Period, because it's the first time we have evidence of formal royal dynasties (sequences of rulers passing power by inheritance within a single family).

The great Sumerian city-states of this era included Uruk itself (still important but no longer the only major center), Ur (downstream near the Persian Gulf, soon to become the wealthiest city in Sumer), Lagash (an agricultural powerhouse further inland), Umma (Lagash's constant rival), Kish (a prestigious older city further north), Nippur (nih-POOR; the religious capital, home of the temple of the chief god Enlil (EN-lil; the god of wind, breath, and authority over the other gods -- Enlil was considered the king of the divine assembly, the deity who assigned fates and granted earthly kings their right to rule)), and several others.
Together these cities made up what we now call Sumer — not a unified kingdom but a network of independent competing city-states who shared a common language, religion, and writing system.
The characteristic feature of this era is war.

The Sumerian city-states were constantly fighting each other — mostly over water rights (who gets to divert the canals in which direction), border farmland (the fertile plains between cities), and prestige.
These weren't the sweeping campaigns of later empire-builders; they were neighborly grudge matches between cities whose walls were sometimes visible from each other.
The most famous ongoing rivalry was between Lagash and Umma, two neighboring cities that fought over a strip of fertile land called the Gu-edena ("edge of the plain") for generations.

The conflict produced what may be the world's first historical document: a stone carving called the Stele of the Vultures (a stele is an upright stone slab carved with text and images, the ancient equivalent of a public monument), erected around 2450 BCE by the victorious Lagash king Eannatum (eh-AH-nah-toom) to celebrate his defeat of Umma.
The stele is named for its gruesome imagery of vultures carrying off the severed heads of Umma's dead soldiers.
It also shows, for the first time in art, a military formation we now call the phalanx — a tightly-packed block of infantry with overlapping shields and long spears pointing forward.

The phalanx was devastating because it solved a basic problem of ancient warfare: a mob of individual fighters, no matter how brave, can be broken by a disciplined block of men who move as a single unit, each soldier's shield protecting the man beside him and a wall of spear-points making frontal attack suicidal.
Before the phalanx, battles between Sumerian cities were probably loose skirmishes of individual warriors — the phalanx turned warfare into something organized and terrifying.

Most people assume the phalanx is a Greek invention (the famous hoplite formation of classical Athens and Sparta (hoplites were heavily armored Greek infantry soldiers who fought in tight shoulder-to-shoulder formations called phalanxes, each man's shield protecting the soldier beside him)), but the Sumerians were using it fifteen hundred years before the Greeks.
The Stele of the Vultures is preserved in the Louvre in Paris and remains the earliest surviving contemporary record of a specific military campaign anywhere in the world.

King Eannatum of Lagash (who ruled sometime around the mid-2400s BCE; his exact dates are uncertain) is worth pausing on because he represents something new: a ruler who tried to conquer his neighbors not just to punish them but to rule them.

Eannatum's inscriptions describe him subduing Umma, Ur, Kish, and even raiding into the rival civilization of Elam (a powerful kingdom centered at the city of Susa (SOO-sah) on the southwestern edge of what is now Iran — Mesopotamia's closest and most persistent foreign rival, which recurs throughout this story; Elam was not Mesopotamian but had its own language, its own writing system, and its own long history, and it alternately traded with, fought with, and conquered its Mesopotamian neighbors for over two thousand years).

Eannatum's campaigns were the first Sumerian attempt at what we would now call hegemony — the dominance of one state over many — and although his little empire fell apart after his death, he set a precedent that later kings (most importantly Sargon of Akkad, next chapter) would take much further.

A century later, Lugal-zage-si (LOO-gal ZAH-geh-see) of Umma (ruled 2358–2334 BCE) would become the last Sumerian king to unify most of Sumer, briefly ruling from Uruk and claiming dominion "from the Lower Sea to the Upper Sea" (meaning from the Persian Gulf to the Mediterranean, a claim that was more aspirational than literal).
Lugal-zage-si's reign would end very badly, as we'll see — but he represents the high-water mark of politically independent Sumerian power.

Over the roughly six hundred years of the Early Dynastic Period, the Sumerian city-states didn't just fight each other; they also became wealthier, more sophisticated, and more culturally ambitious than anything the world had ever seen.

The clearest window into that wealth is the Royal Tombs of Ur, a cluster of sixteen elaborate burial pits from around 2600 BCE that were excavated by the British archaeologist Leonard Woolley between 1922 and 1934.
What Woolley found in those tombs genuinely shocked the archaeological world.

The tombs contained quantities of gold, silver, and lapis lazuli that rivaled anything ever found in ancient Egypt: golden helmets, harps inlaid with mother-of-pearl and topped with sculpted bull's heads, jewelry of carnelian and Afghan lapis, gaming boards inlaid with shell and red limestone.

Queen Puabi (poo-AH-bee; one of the tomb owners, identifiable from a cuneiform inscription on her cylinder seal) wore an elaborate golden headdress of leaves, ribbons, and flowers containing thousands of individual pieces.
But more shocking than the treasure were the people.

In the largest tomb, Woolley found 74 skeletons arranged in rows — soldiers in copper helmets, ladies-in-waiting in golden headdresses, musicians still holding their instruments, and teams of oxen in their harnesses.
All of them had apparently died alongside the primary burial to accompany their ruler into the afterlife.

Woolley believed they went willingly and took cups of poison; more recent forensic work has complicated the picture — forensic studies published in the 2000s, including CT scans and physical examination of some skulls, revealed evidence consistent with blunt-force trauma, suggesting at least some attendants may have been struck before or instead of being poisoned.

Whether anyone went truly "voluntarily" given the power dynamics of an Early Dynastic court is debated, but what is not debated is that dozens of people died in these tombs, and that the burials were carefully staged.

The Royal Tombs of Ur remain the clearest evidence we have that by 2600 BCE the Sumerian city-states were not primitive outposts of civilization — they were already deep, hierarchical, rich, and ideologically powerful societies, with rulers who commanded enough authority to demand that dozens of human beings die alongside them.

The contrast with the contemporary Indus Valley Civilization is striking: Harappan burials from the same period show no royal tombs, no sacrificed attendants, and only modest grave goods — a level of egalitarianism that looks almost alien next to Ur's opulence (Indus Valley Ch 8).

The same peaceful pattern shows up on the other side of the world: the roughly contemporary city of Caral on the Peruvian coast (~2600 BCE) has no weapons, no fortifications, no retainer sacrifice, and no hierarchical burial (Early Andean Ch 3).

China, on the other hand, was doing exactly what Ur was doing at roughly the same moment: at the late-Longshan walled city of Shimao in northern China (~2000 BCE), builders buried dozens of sacrificed young women in pits beneath the city walls as foundation offerings (Ancient China Ch 6).
Egypt's First Dynasty predates Ur's tombs by 400 years and exceeds them in scale — King Djer's tomb at Abydos (~3000 BCE) contained 338 subsidiary burials (Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 6).
Of the major Bronze Age cradles, it is the Indus and the Andes that are the outliers — the two civilizations that seem to have opted out of the retainer-sacrifice pattern entirely.
Sumerian culture during this period didn't just produce physical wealth; it also produced some of the earliest named literary figures.
The most important of these is Gilgamesh, legendary king of Uruk around 2700 BCE.

Gilgamesh may have been a real historical person — he appears on a Sumerian king list, and later Sumerians credited him with building Uruk's great defensive walls (which archaeologists have confirmed do exist, and were indeed built around the right time).

But he also became the central figure of ancient Mesopotamian literature: the hero of the Epic of Gilgamesh, Chapter 6, when it was written down in its most complete form, but whose oldest Sumerian components date back to this Early Dynastic era.

The story depicts Gilgamesh as two-thirds divine and one-third human, a king so powerful and cruel that his people beg the gods for relief, and so bereaved after the death of his friend Enkidu that he embarks on a futile quest for immortality.

The Epic of Gilgamesh is literature's first great meditation on mortality, friendship, the limits of human ambition, and the relationship between civilization and the wild — themes that would echo through three thousand years of Mediterranean and Middle Eastern literature and still resonate today.
The religious tradition of Sumer was also deepening during this era.

One of the oldest stories the Sumerians told is the Sumerian Flood Story, starring a pious king named Ziusudra (zee-oo-SOO-drah; the name means "life of long days") who is warned by the god Enki (the freshwater god at Eridu, Chapter 2) that the other gods plan to destroy humanity with a great flood.
Ziusudra builds a boat, loads it with animals, survives the flood, makes a sacrifice after the waters recede, and is granted immortality.

The parallels to the biblical story of Noah are so precise — the divine warning to one righteous man, the building of a boat, the loading of animals, the sending out of birds to find land, the sacrifice on a mountaintop after the waters recede — that virtually all scholars now agree the biblical account derives from the much older Mesopotamian tradition, probably transmitted through the Babylonian Exile (Chapter 12).

The Sumerian flood story may actually reflect a real catastrophic flood, or a series of them: Woolley at Ur and other archaeologists at other sites have found thick layers of flood silt interrupting the occupation levels of Sumerian cities, representing genuine catastrophic floods that must have entered the collective memory of the region.
And the Mesopotamian tradition is not alone.

China preserves its own great Bronze Age flood tradition in the figure of Yu the Great, who supposedly tamed the Yellow River through thirteen years of engineering works and earned the right to found the first Chinese dynasty — a legend that a 2016 Science paper linked to a real catastrophic outburst flood at Jishi Gorge around 1920 BCE, inside the same global climate window (Ancient China Ch 7).
Three Bronze Age civilizations, three independent flood traditions, three separate real-flood candidates — possibly all downstream of the same global climate disruption.
Commerce during the Early Dynastic Period was not just local.

Sumerian merchants sailed the Persian Gulf in boats to reach Dilmun (DIL-moon; an important trading hub centered on the modern island of Bahrain, where goods from multiple regions were collected, warehoused, and redistributed — archaeologists have found Harappan-style weights, beads, and pottery at Dilmun sites, and Dilmun merchants apparently used both Mesopotamian and Harappan measurement systems), where they exchanged Mesopotamian textiles and grain for copper from Magan (mah-GAHN; ancient Oman, along the southeastern coast of the Arabian Peninsula), timber from the mountains of Lebanon, and — most exotically — carnelian beads, cotton cloth, and other luxury goods from Meluhha (meh-LOO-hah), the Sumerian name for the Indus Valley Civilization (a major Bronze Age culture flourishing at the same time in what is now Pakistan and northwestern India, with its own great cities like Mohenjo-daro and Harappa — the Indus Valley was among the first civilizations to cultivate cotton commercially, and the Indus Valley Civilization's own narrative covers their side of this trade in Chapter 7).

The Sumerian Maritime Trade network reached across more than 1,900 miles (3,000 km) of sea and land, and archaeologists have found Indus Valley artifacts — carnelian beads, etched stone weights, distinctive shell ornaments — buried in the Royal Tombs of Ur alongside Egyptian-style beads and Afghan lapis.
By 2500 BCE, three of the four great ancient civilizations (Mesopotamia, Egypt, and the Indus Valley) were directly connected by trade.
Facilitating all this commerce required standardization.

Around 2400 BCE, the Sumerians formalized a system of standardized weights based on the shekel (a unit of weight equal to about 8.3 grams, originally derived from the weight of a specific number of barley grains — the word "shekel" is directly ancestral to the modern Israeli unit of currency of the same name).
Larger units were the mina (60 shekels) and the talent (60 minas, or 3,600 shekels, about 30 kilograms).

With standardized weights, a merchant in Ur could trust that a shekel of silver in Lagash was the same amount as a shekel of silver in Umma, enabling long-distance contracts to be negotiated with confidence.
This is the birth of a universal commercial infrastructure.
It also enabled the development of lending and debt — practices that would reach full sophistication in the Old Babylonian period (Chapter 7), but whose roots begin here.

This was not yet coinage (actual stamped coins wouldn't be invented for another two thousand years, in Lydia in modern Turkey), but already a recognizable monetary economy with silver weighed on balance scales serving as the medium of exchange.
The Sumerians were also trying to answer a question that mattered urgently to an agricultural civilization: when will the floods come, and when should we plant?
Answering that question required watching the sky, and the Early Sumerian astronomy that developed from around 2400 BCE onward was the result.

Sumerian astronomers identified the five planets visible to the naked eye (Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn), tracked the phases of the moon in a twelve-month lunar calendar, and recognized that the "morning star" and "evening star" were the same planet (Venus, associated with the goddess Inanna).

Sumerian astronomy was inseparable from religion — planetary movements were understood as messages from the gods — but the meticulous observation itself was real, and would grow over the following two thousand years into the most sophisticated astronomical tradition of the ancient world, culminating (as we'll see in Chapter 11 and Chapter 12) in the ability to predict eclipses, the development of the zodiac, and a body of observational data that Greek astronomers would later use to make their own famous discoveries.
But underneath this cultural and commercial flourishing, the constant fighting never stopped.
The last great independent Sumerian king, Lugal-zage-si of Umma, briefly unified most of southern Mesopotamia around 2340 BCE — and then, around 2334 BCE, he met an opponent he was not ready for.
From the north, from an Akkadian-speaking kingdom centered on a city we still haven't located archaeologically, a completely new kind of ruler was coming.
His name was Sargon, and he was about to end the age of independent Sumerian city-states forever.

chapters.title: The First Empire: Sargon and Akkad
sentences: Around 2334 BCE, a man named Sargon seized the throne of the Sumerian city of Kish (one of the prestigious older city-states the previous chapter) and immediately began the most ambitious campaign of conquest that any king had ever attempted.
The man himself was unusual.

He was Akkadian (a speaker of the Semitic language Akkadian — the first major non-Sumerian people to play a central role in Mesopotamian politics, the second wave in the parade of Mesopotamian peoples, Chapter 1).
He did not come from Sumerian royal stock.

His name — Sharru-kin in Akkadian, meaning "The King Is Legitimate" — has long struck historians as suspiciously defensive, suggesting his real origins may have been anything but legitimate (though it should be noted that the name could also simply be a conventional throne name without that specific implication).

The story Sargon himself circulated was that his mother was a priestess who gave birth to him in secret, set him adrift on the river in a basket of reeds, and that he was found and raised by a simple gardener before being elevated (by the goddess Inanna's favor) to become cupbearer to the king of Kish.

The parallels to the biblical story of Moses being set adrift on the Nile are so precise that most scholars believe the biblical account borrowed from Sargon's much older legend, probably during the Babylonian Exile, Chapter 12.

Exactly how Sargon went from cupbearer (a high court position, but not a royal one) to king is not recorded in any surviving document — a coup is the most likely explanation — but what came next is very well documented.
Sargon marched south with an army and met Lugal-zage-si (the last independent Sumerian king, end of previous chapter).
He defeated him.

The Akkadian sources describe the aftermath in deliberately humiliating detail: Lugal-zage-si was captured, fitted with a neck-stock (a heavy wooden collar used to chain prisoners), and paraded through the streets of Nippur (the Sumerian religious capital, where the chief god Enlil's temple was located — the most important sacred city in Mesopotamia, and the traditional place where Sumerian kings were legitimized).
Parading the defeated Sumerian king in chains through Sumer's holiest city was an enormous symbolic statement: the age of independent Sumerian power was over.
From 2334 BCE onward, Sumer would be ruled by outsiders.
But Sargon was not done.

He continued his campaigns outward in every direction from his new capital at Akkad (a city whose exact location has never been found by archaeologists — it was somewhere in central Mesopotamia, possibly under modern Baghdad, but has been lost to history).
His inscriptions describe campaigns in every direction, and while some of the details are royal boasting, the overall picture is confirmed by archaeology.

He marched northwest up the Euphrates into what is now Syria, conquering the important trading cities of Mari (on the middle Euphrates, controlling the overland trade route to the west) and Ebla (a wealthy commercial center in northwestern Syria), and reaching the Mediterranean coast and the "Cedar Forest" (the mountains of Lebanon) — the first time a Mesopotamian king had projected power to the sea.
He marched east into Elam (the rival civilization east of Mesopotamia, centered on Susa, Chapter 4), defeating Elamite forces and sacking their cities.
He may have reached as far north as Anatolia (the highland peninsula of modern Turkey), though the claims about campaigns in that direction are harder to verify.

By the end of his reign, Sargon's empire stretched roughly 930 miles (1,500 km) from the Persian Gulf in the southeast to the Mediterranean in the northwest — the first multi-ethnic empire in human history, containing Sumerians, Akkadians, Amorites, and many smaller peoples under direct central rule, with Elam occupying a contested frontier position that swung between tribute and autonomy within a generation (Elam Ch 2).
Egypt's Old Kingdom was roughly contemporary but was ethnically and linguistically homogeneous — one people along one river.
China would not see a comparable multi-ethnic empire until the Qin unification in 221 BCE, nearly two thousand years later.
Sargon's innovation was ruling different peoples as a single state.
What made this possible was not just Sargon's military talent — though he clearly had it — but a set of administrative innovations that were genuinely new.
In the earlier Sumerian era, when one city conquered another, the conqueror typically installed a puppet king from the defeated city's own royal family and demanded tribute.
Sargon did something different: he appointed his own people — trusted Akkadian-speaking governors — to rule the conquered territories directly.
This is the birth of the provincial governorship as an imperial tool.

He also maintained a standing army of 5,400 professional soldiers "who ate bread before him daily" (meaning they were fed from the king's own stores, a way of saying they were permanently on the royal payroll; the number comes from a text that mixes historical fact with legend about Sargon, so the precise figure should be taken as approximate, but the existence of a standing army is not in doubt) — men whose full-time job was to be soldiers, supported by the central treasury rather than by part-time levies from their home villages.
This is, as far as we can tell, the world's first professional standing army.
He standardized weights and measures across the empire, making it possible to assess tribute and tax across different regions on a common scale.

And he adopted Akkadian as the empire's administrative language, while letting Sumerian continue as a literary and liturgical language in Sumer — an early example of the "dual language" imperial governance strategy that later empires from Persia to the Ottomans would use.
Sargon's reign also produced the first named literary author whose name has been recorded anywhere in human history: Enheduanna (en-heh-doo-AH-nah), Sargon's own daughter.

Sargon appointed Enheduanna as high priestess of the moon god Nanna (NAH-nah; the chief god of the Sumerian city of Ur — not to be confused with Inanna, the goddess of love and war; similar names, completely different deities), a political move as much as a religious one — placing a loyal relative in charge of the most prestigious religious institution in southern Sumer gave Sargon a direct line of control into conquered territory.
But Enheduanna turned out to be much more than a political appointee.
She was a poet.

Her three major works — the Hymn to Inanna, the Sumerian Temple Hymns (a set of 42 short hymns to the various temples across Sumer), and the narrative poem Inanna and Ebih — survive in dozens of ancient copies from scribal schools across Mesopotamia, proving that her work was actively studied and recopied for centuries after her death.
Her writing uses first-person narrative voice, deliberate dramatic tension, and sophisticated theological argument — it is not the work of an amateur.

Enheduanna (ca. 2285 BCE) predates every other known named author in human history by more than a century, and predates most of the great literary works of the ancient world by hundreds or thousands of years.
She is one of the most important people in the history of literature, and almost no one outside of Mesopotamian studies has heard of her.
Sargon died around 2279 BCE after a reign of about fifty-six years, and his sons struggled to hold the empire together against rebellions.
But the dynasty's greatest king was Sargon's grandson: Naram-Sin (nah-RAHM-seen) of Akkad (ruled 2254–2218 BCE), whose ambition dwarfed even his grandfather's.

Naram-Sin pushed the empire's borders further in every direction, campaigning in Anatolia, the Sinai peninsula (between Egypt and Mesopotamia), and possibly as far as the Indus Valley through the existing Meluhha trade network (though there is no corroborating evidence from the Harappan side).
And then, at some point during his reign, he did something no Mesopotamian king had ever done before: he declared himself a god.

In Mesopotamian religious tradition, kings were considered the servants of the gods — they performed rituals, built temples, and acted as intermediaries between humanity and the divine, but they were emphatically mortal and emphatically subordinate.
To claim godhood during one's own lifetime was a massive theological rupture, and contemporary Sumerian priests almost certainly found it shocking.
Naram-Sin made the claim concrete by adding a cuneiform symbol called the divine determinative (a small star-shape normally placed before the name of a god) to his own name in inscriptions.

His title was now "Naram-Sin, god of Akkad." He backed up this theological claim visually: the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin (a six-foot-tall pink limestone monument now in the Louvre) shows the king in triumph on a mountain slope, standing over the fallen bodies of defeated enemies, depicted at larger-than-human scale with a horned helmet (a traditional symbol of divinity) and a radiant star above his head.

The composition is strikingly diagonal — widely interpreted as a deliberate artistic choice that makes the image feel dynamic and upward-moving, as if Naram-Sin is ascending to heaven, though some scholars read the composition more conservatively as simply depicting a mountain campaign.
It is one of the most sophisticated pieces of political-theological art to survive from the ancient world.
But the Akkadian Empire was about to collapse.

Around 2193 BCE, a mountain people called the Gutians (from the Zagros mountains east of Mesopotamia — the same highland frontier we described in Chapter 1 as the region Mesopotamians considered wild and uncivilized) broke through the empire's defenses and overran large parts of it.
The collapse was not instantaneous; it took several decades and multiple stages.
Modern archaeology has identified a major drought that struck the Near East around this time, lasting over a century, which would have hollowed out the empire from the inside.

In a civilization whose entire food supply depended on irrigation from rivers fed by mountain snowmelt, less rainfall meant lower river levels, less water in the canals, lower crop yields, and less surplus grain to feed the professional army and imperial bureaucracy.

The drought didn't destroy the empire directly, but it weakened the agricultural base that kept the system running — making it vulnerable when the Gutians delivered the military blow. (The same drought — now called the 4.2 kiloyear event — also contributed to the Indus Valley Civilization's decline several centuries later, as rivers shifted and monsoon patterns weakened; Indus Valley Ch 9.

China felt it too: the Liangzhu hydraulic civilization collapsed around 2400–2300 BCE on the leading edge of the event, and the catastrophic Yellow River flood at Jishi Gorge that stands behind China's Yu the Great legend dates to around 1920 BCE, inside the same window; Ancient China Ch 5, Ch 7.

One global climate disruption, four Bronze Age civilizations affected.) The Gutian Invasion was less a single conquest than a slow-motion disintegration: the empire cracked into fragments, and the Gutians took nominal control of parts of it.
Sumerian king lists record over twenty Gutian kings in rapid succession — a sign of chaos rather than stable rule.
One of the most striking things about the Gutian period is that it left many of the existing Sumerian cities relatively untouched.
The Gutians were raiders, not administrators.
In some places, traditional Sumerian governance simply continued under nominal Gutian overlordship.

This is particularly visible in the career of Gudea (goo-DAY-ah) of Lagash (ruled approximately 2144–2124 BCE, during the Gutian period), a Sumerian ruler who managed to keep his city independent by paying tribute to the Gutians rather than fighting them, and who used that peace to do something remarkable: he launched one of the greatest temple-building programs of the ancient world.

Gudea imported cedar from Lebanon, gold from Meluhha (the Sumerian name for the Indus Valley region — his own temple-building inscriptions name Meluhha among the long list of foreign sources he tapped, alongside Magan copper and Amanus cedar), and stone from across the Zagros, pouring the imported luxuries into the construction of a massive temple to the local god Ningirsu (nin-GIR-soo; the warrior god of Lagash, patron of agriculture and rain) at Lagash.

Over thirty finely-carved diorite (a hard, dense, dark volcanic stone) statues of Gudea survive, all showing him in the same pious pose — hands clasped in prayer, wearing a distinctive fleeced cap — and they constitute the largest collection of portrait statues of any single ancient ruler.
You can see them today in the Louvre and in museums around the world: the serene face of a man who kept civilization running while empires crumbled around him.
The Akkadian Empire was gone.
The Gutians were fading.

But the Sumerian cities were still there — still farming, still writing, still building temples — and they were about to produce one last, brilliant flowering before the Sumerian language fell silent forever.

chapters.title: The Sumerian Renaissance
sentences: Around 2112 BCE, a man named Ur-Nammu (oor-NAHM-oo) — governor of the ancient city of Ur (the wealthy Sumerian city near the Persian Gulf whose Royal Tombs appeared in Chapter 4, now recovering from the chaos of the Akkadian collapse and the Gutian raids) — declared himself independent and began to conquer his neighbors.
Within a few years he had driven the last Gutians out of southern Mesopotamia, taken control of the other Sumerian cities, and reestablished centralized rule over the southern plain.
The dynasty he founded is called the Third Dynasty of Ur, usually shortened to Ur III (because historians count two earlier dynasties at Ur from the Early Dynastic period).
It would only last about a century — roughly 2112 to 2004 BCE — but that century is one of the most culturally productive in the entire span of Mesopotamian history.

It is often called the Sumerian Renaissance, because it represented the last great flowering of traditional Sumerian language, literature, and bureaucracy before Sumerian was finally replaced as a living language by Akkadian.
Ur-Nammu's most lasting physical monument is the Great Ziggurat of Ur, which he began around 2100 BCE and which his son Shulgi finished.

A ziggurat (a word derived from an Akkadian term meaning "raised up") is the classic Mesopotamian multi-tiered temple platform, the architectural form that traces its lineage all the way back to Eridu and the White Temple of Uruk (Chapters 2 and 3).

The Great Ziggurat of Ur rose in three massive stepped terraces to a height of about thirty meters (roughly a ten-story building), and it was topped by a shrine to Nanna (the moon god, patron deity of the city of Ur — the same god whose priesthood Enheduanna ran in the previous chapter).
The ziggurat was built with a solid core of sun-dried mud brick and an exterior shell of much more durable baked brick, each one stamped with Ur-Nammu's royal name.
The outer layer alone used more than 720,000 baked bricks.
What makes the Great Ziggurat remarkable beyond its size is that it's still there.

Its mud-brick core has survived four thousand years in the Iraqi desert and its massive foundations still stand, one of the best-preserved ancient monumental structures in the world. (It was partially restored by Saddam Hussein in the 1980s, controversially because he used modern bricks stamped with his own name alongside the ancient ones -- a deliberate echo of the Mesopotamian royal tradition of stamping bricks with the king's name, which archaeologists found more propagandistic than preservationist.) Alongside the ziggurat, Ur-Nammu did something else that would outlive his dynasty by more than four thousand years: he wrote down the first law code in human history.
The Code of Ur-Nammu (composed around 2100 BCE) predates the more famous Code of Hammurabi (next chapter) by about three centuries.
It is not a complete code in the modern sense — we have about forty laws from it, preserved on later copies — but it covers injury compensation, marriage and divorce, slavery, and property disputes.
What's immediately striking about the Code of Ur-Nammu is how un-violent it is compared to its later descendants.
Where Hammurabi would later famously demand "an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth," Ur-Nammu mostly prescribed monetary fines.
A man who cut off another man's foot owed 10 shekels of silver.
A man who cut off another's nose owed 40 shekels.
The Ur III legal system seems to have preferred economic compensation to physical retaliation — a reflection of the highly commercial, mercantile nature of the Ur III state.

The mere existence of a written law code is itself a revolution: written law means that the rules are knowable in advance, applicable across a territory, and not dependent on the arbitrary whims of whoever happens to be judging any particular case.

Written law is the foundation of the rule of law — the idea that governing principles should be public, stable, and binding even on the people who wrote them — and it begins in Mesopotamia with Ur-Nammu.
To appreciate how early this was: Rome's foundational Twelve Tables were not written until around 450 BCE — over 1,600 years later.
China's first major legal codification came with the Qin dynasty around 221 BCE.
