Before Mohenjo-daro, the conventional narrative began with the Vedic period — the arrival of the Sanskrit-speaking Indo-Aryans — as if civilization in the subcontinent started around 1500 BCE.

After Mohenjo-daro, it became clear that the subcontinent had a urban civilization more than a thousand years before the Vedic period, one that was larger, more technologically sophisticated, and more urbanized than anything the Vedic peoples would create for another millennium.

The discovery also raised the question that has haunted South Asian archaeology ever since: what is the relationship between the Harappan civilization and the Hindu civilization that eventually developed on the same land?
Are they connected by an unbroken thread of cultural continuity — the Great Bath flowing into the temple tank, the Pashupati seal flowing into Shiva, the fire altars flowing into yajna?
Or are they separated by a rupture — the Harappan world ending, the Vedic world beginning, with whatever links exist being coincidental or superficial?

The answer, as this chapter has suggested, is probably somewhere in between: neither pure continuity nor clean break, but a complex, centuries-long process of blending, forgetting, and reinventing that produced something genuinely new from the encounter of two genuinely different worlds.
The Indus Valley Civilization lasted, in its mature urban form, for about seven hundred years — from roughly 2600 to 1900 BCE.
That is longer than the Roman Empire lasted in the west.
It covered more ground than any contemporary civilization.
It built cities that were, by the measures of public health and civic infrastructure, the most livable the ancient world produced.
It maintained civilization-wide standards of weights, measures, and construction for seven centuries without (as far as we can tell) a single named king or a single readable decree.
It traded across 1,850 miles (3,000 km) of land and sea.

It domesticated cotton, engineered the world's first dock, drilled teeth, played dice, and made a small bronze figurine of a girl standing with her hand on her hip that is, four and a half thousand years later, one of the most recognizable artworks in the world.
And then it was forgotten so completely that, for three thousand years, no one on earth knew it had existed.

The Harappan legacy survives not in monuments or texts but in the deep structures of the civilization that grew in its wake: in the ritual bathing tanks of Hindu temples, in the cotton fields that still cover the subcontinent, in the agricultural calendar still timed to the monsoon, in the DNA of every person in South Asia today.

The Indus Valley Civilization is the invisible foundation — the substrate beneath everything that came after, carrying forward the accumulated knowledge of four thousand years of pre-urban culture and seven centuries of urban brilliance into a world that forgot where it all came from.
The Vedic period begins next.
The language changes.
The gods change.
The social structure changes.

But the land is the same, the monsoon is the same, the rivers (the ones that still flow) are the same, and the people — genetically, materially, and in ways that no script can record — are the descendants of both the Harappans and the newcomers who joined them.
What comes next is not a new beginning.
It is a continuation.

chapters.title: Before China
sentences: Here is a sentence that sounds simple and isn't: China has always been China.
It sounds simple because China — the place on the map, the culture on TV, the country of a billion-plus people — seems like one of the oldest, most continuous civilizations on earth.

You can point to the Great Wall and the Forbidden City and a written language that first-graders in Beijing learn today using characters whose ancestors were carved on cattle bones three and a half thousand years ago.
Chinese civilization feels like a single long river, flowing unbroken from the beginning of recorded time to the present.
It isn't simple because, if you rewind far enough, there is no China.
There is no country, no emperor, no writing, no rice paddies, no Great Wall, no silk.
There are only river valleys, mountains, forests, and bands of people hunting deer and picking wild grasses.

This is the story of how those river valleys became the country we now call China — not in the age of Confucius or the First Emperor, but thousands of years earlier, in the muddy villages where the recognizable pieces of Chinese civilization were assembled for the first time: rice, millet, jade, dragons, ancestors, walls, kings, and the idea that Heaven chooses the ruler.
It is, in many ways, a stranger story than Mesopotamia's or Egypt's.
Those civilizations left cuneiform tablets (the wedge-shaped writing of ancient Mesopotamia, pressed into wet clay and baked hard) and hieroglyphic walls.
The people who built them wrote down their own names.

The Indus Valley civilization, farther east in modern Pakistan, is the closest parallel to what we're about to read — also pre-textual, also known almost entirely through archaeology, with a script no one has ever deciphered.
The people who built Neolithic China left no writing we can confidently read, only a handful of scratched symbols that may or may not be words.
Almost everything we know about them, we know from the dirt they left behind: their pots, their burials, their tools, the post-holes of their houses, and — more than anything else — their jade.

This is a civilization rediscovered from the ground up, thousands of years after its oldest layer was laid down, by archaeologists who are still arguing about what the symbols mean and where the boundary sits between myth and history.
Before we meet the farmers and the jade workers, though, we have to meet the land.

Chinese civilization is a story about rivers, and to understand what happened along them you need to know why the rivers are there, why they matter, and why two of them — not one — became the cradles of everything that followed.
If you stand on a very tall mountain in western China and look east, the land falls away in three giant steps, like a staircase built for a giant.
The top step is the Tibetan Plateau — the highest and largest plateau on Earth, averaging about 14,800 feet (4,500 meters) above sea level, roughly the altitude of the base camp at Everest.
Most of it is cold, thin-aired grassland, too high and dry for farming.

The plateau is hemmed in by the Himalayas to the south (the world's tallest mountain range, which blocks the Indian monsoon from pushing further north) and the Kunlun Mountains to the north (a huge range stretching into the deserts of modern Xinjiang, a province in China's far west).

Almost nothing grows on the plateau itself, but it is the water tower of Asia: the snowmelt from its ice fields feeds nearly every great river in eastern and southeastern Asia, including the Mekong, the Brahmaputra, the Salween — and, most importantly for this story, the two rivers that would give birth to Chinese civilization.
The second step is a mid-altitude zone of mountains, basins, and plateaus running through central China — cold, rugged, and in places thickly forested.
This is where the water from the plateau picks up speed, cuts through canyons, and begins to do the work of shaping the landscape.
The third and lowest step is the great plains of eastern China, stretching from the foot of the mountains out to the Pacific Ocean.

This is where the two rivers finish their journey, where they deposit enormous quantities of silt, and where — once farming arrives — some of the most fertile land on the planet supports dense human populations.
Everything in this book happens on the second and third steps.
The top step belongs to the sky.

The two rivers are the Yellow River (in Chinese, Huang He — HWONG HUH, though the English name is what the rest of the world uses) and the Yangtze (YANG-tsee — in Chinese, Chang Jiang, meaning "Long River").
They are the reason Chinese civilization exists, and they are as different from each other as two great rivers can possibly be.
The Yellow River is the northern river.
It is about 3,400 miles (5,500 km) long, making it the second-longest river in China.

Its source is on the Tibetan Plateau; it curves north, makes a huge horseshoe bend around the Ordos Desert (an arid steppe region in what is now the Inner Mongolia region), then swings east and south through the middle of northern China before emptying into the Bohai Sea.

Along most of its middle and lower course, the Yellow River runs through a region called the Loess Plateau (LESS — "loess" is a type of very fine, wind-blown silt), and the river gets its color, and its name, from this soil.
Here's where it gets interesting.
Loess is an extraordinary material.
It was deposited across northern China over hundreds of thousands of years by wind blowing fine dust off the Central Asian deserts during the ice ages.
In some parts of Shanxi and Shaanxi provinces (in north-central China, west of the modern capital Beijing), the loess is 300 feet (90 meters) deep — the thickest loess deposits anywhere on Earth.
It is fine, soft, and fertile enough that a person with a wooden digging stick can plant a crop in it.
You do not need a plow, oxen, or iron tools.
A community with sharp sticks and strong backs can farm loess, which turns out to matter enormously for who got to start farming first.
But loess also has a dark side.
It is loose and easily eroded, which is why the Yellow River looks yellow: it is carrying a vast load of silt washed out of the plateau upstream.
In some places, the river carries more sediment per gallon than any other major river in the world.
When it reaches the flat plains of eastern China, the water slows down and the silt drops out.
Over centuries, the river raises its own bed, piling up silt higher and higher between its banks until it is actually flowing above the surrounding countryside.
Then the banks give way, and the river jumps out of its channel and finds a new path to the sea — sometimes hundreds of miles from the old one, drowning cities and villages in the process.
The Yellow River has changed its course at least twenty-six times in recorded history, and several of those changes killed millions of people.

The traditional Chinese name for the Yellow River is not a term of affection: it is "China's Sorrow." This is the river that would support millet farmers, walled towns, the Xia and Shang dynasties, and the idea that the fundamental job of a legitimate ruler is flood control.
The Yangtze is a different animal entirely.

It is longer (about 3,900 miles / 6,300 km — the longest river in Asia and the third-longest in the world), wetter (fed by both snowmelt and the much heavier rains of subtropical southern China), and slower-paced in its lower course.

It begins on the Tibetan Plateau, flows east through deep canyons in Sichuan province (the Three Gorges — spectacular and unforgiving gorges now flooded by a massive modern dam), widens out across the central Chinese plains, and empties into the East China Sea near modern Shanghai.

Along most of its course through central and eastern China, it runs through a warmer, wetter world than the Yellow River — a world of lakes, marshes, floodplains, bamboo, and dense subtropical forest.
The Yangtze does not carry the apocalyptic silt loads of the Yellow River.
It does not routinely change course and erase cities.
It is a more forgiving river.
And critically for this story, the climate along its lower course is warm enough, and wet enough, to grow rice in standing water.
The Yangtze delta and lower basin became the cradle of the other great Chinese agricultural revolution: rice paddies, wet farming, fish and waterfowl, stilt houses on marshes.
Two rivers.
Two climates.
Two sets of wild plants.
Two utterly different ways of being human on the same ancient landscape.
If you remember only one fact about Chinese prehistory, make it this one: ancient China had two cradles, not one.
The north and the south were doing different things at the same time, and when they eventually blended, the combination was what gave Chinese civilization its distinctive shape.
Between the two river basins sat a world of mountains, hills, and smaller river valleys — the central Chinese plains and the uplands of Henan, Shanxi, and Shaanxi provinces.

Henan (HEH-nahn, meaning "south of the river" — specifically south of the Yellow River) in particular will come up constantly in this story: more major ancient Chinese sites have been dug up in Henan than in any other province on earth.

The site of the first Chinese oracle bones, the earliest known Chinese palace complex, the oldest flutes in the world, and several of the founding villages of Chinese civilization all lie within an area smaller than the US state of Ohio.

Henan is the elbow where the Yellow River turns east on its final run to the sea, and it is where the northern, southern, and western cultural worlds rubbed against each other and exchanged ideas, tools, and (eventually) wives, warriors, and gods.
One small note on spelling before we move on: two of the provinces that come up constantly in this book are Shanxi (SHAHN-shee) and Shaanxi (SHAHN-shee, but with a longer vowel).

They are right next to each other and their names are almost identical; the "Shaanxi" spelling with the double-a was invented by the modern Chinese government specifically to keep them distinct in romanization.
Shaanxi (the one with the double-a) is the western of the pair, containing modern Xi'an and the Wei River valley.
Shanxi (single-a) is the eastern of the pair, north of Henan.
Both are in the loess plateau country.
When you see a name, check the number of a's.
Around the edges of this core zone were things to keep an eye on.
To the north, the Mongolian steppe — vast grasslands inhabited by mobile hunting and (later) herding peoples who would loom larger and larger in Chinese history as time went on.

To the northeast, the forested lowlands of modern Liaoning province and Inner Mongolia, where a remarkable culture called the Hongshan would make the world's first dragon images out of jade (Chapter 4).
To the west, the deserts of Xinjiang and the passes leading into Central Asia, from which would eventually come tin, horses, chariots, and Buddhism.
To the east and southeast, the Pacific coast and the offshore islands — Taiwan, the Ryukyus, and eventually Japan — which in deep prehistory received migrations of rice farmers heading out to sea.

And to the south, the subtropical forests of what is now Fujian, Guangdong, and Vietnam, which in this chapter are still mostly outside the story but will slowly get pulled into it over the millennia that follow.
This is the stage.
Now for the actors.
One more thing before we meet the farmers.

The last glacial period (the most recent ice age, which locked up enormous quantities of fresh water in polar ice caps and dropped global sea levels by hundreds of feet) began to retreat around 20,000 years ago, and by 10,000 BCE the world was warming rapidly.
In East Asia, this meant milder winters, heavier summer rains, longer growing seasons, and the return of forests to regions that had been cold steppe.

Coastlines moved hundreds of miles inland as the oceans rose — much of the modern North China Plain (the low, flat region around Beijing and Tianjin) was under shallow sea around 6000 BCE, and only reappeared as silt piled up and the sea stabilized.
The people living in these changing landscapes were hunter-gatherers who had been in East Asia for at least 40,000 years.
Their sites tend to be rock shelters and open camps, and they left few burials, so they are harder to study than the farmers who come later.
But they were here, they were skilled, and they were the people who first noticed that certain wild grasses in certain river valleys produced unusually large and unusually tasty seeds.
The farmers did not drop in from the sky.
They grew out of hunter-gatherer communities whose ancestors had been watching and eating wild millet and wild rice for dozens of generations before anyone ever thought to deliberately plant them.
Around 7000 BCE, in two very different river valleys separated by almost 600 miles (1,000 km), that shift finally happened.
Somebody planted a seed on purpose and came back to tend the spot.
From the moment that happened, China was no longer just a landscape.
It was the beginning of a story.

That story starts in the next chapter, with two grains — one in the north, one in the south — and a village called Jiahu (JEE-ah-HOO), where a farmer nine thousand years ago carved a tortoise shell with marks that some scholars think might be the world's first writing, and where, in the same burial ground, archaeologists found a bone flute, crafted from the wing of a red-crowned crane, that still plays a recognizable scale when a modern musician puts it to their lips.
Nine thousand years later, we are still listening.

chapters.title: Two Grains, Two Worlds
sentences: Around 7000 BCE, something happened in East Asia that had only happened a handful of times before in the whole history of our species: people stopped being hunter-gatherers and started being farmers.
It didn't happen in one place.
It didn't even happen in one river valley.

It happened, independently and almost simultaneously, in two river valleys, on two different grains, under two different climates — and the long echo of that split would shape Chinese civilization all the way to the present day.
We need to start with the word Neolithic.

It comes from Greek and means "New Stone Age" — a label invented by 19th-century European archaeologists for the era when people were still making their tools out of stone (because metal hadn't been invented yet) but had started farming, settling in villages, making pottery, and generally behaving in ways that would look recognizable to modern eyes.
The key thing that defines the Neolithic isn't the stone tools; it's the farming.
A Neolithic community grows its own food, stores it for later, and stays in one place long enough to build a house that isn't a tent.
Almost everything else that we think of as human civilization — property, inheritance, organized religion, cities, kings, taxes — grew out of that single change.
Around 7000 BCE in China, that change hit two places at once.

The northern cradle was the Yellow River valley — specifically the loess plateau and the middle reaches of the river, in what is now Henan and Hebei provinces (the two provinces whose names mean "south of the river" and "north of the river" respectively, in both cases referring to the Yellow River).
The people here, the earliest farming communities we can identify from the ground, are called the Peiligang culture (pay-lee-GAHNG).
We do not know what the Peiligang people called themselves.

"Peiligang" is simply the name of the modern village near the first site where archaeologists dug up one of their settlements, and the label got attached to everything that looked similar — their pottery, their tools, their burials, their pit houses — all across the region.
The Peiligang were farmers of millet, not rice.
Millet is a catch-all English name for a group of small-seeded grasses that were domesticated in various parts of the world for their hardy, drought-resistant grain.
Two species matter for Chinese history: foxtail millet (Setaria italica) and broomcorn millet (Panicum miliaceum).

Both grow wild in the Yellow River loess plains; both have small, starchy seeds that can be ground into flour, boiled into porridge, or fermented into beer; both will grow in soil too dry and too cool for rice.
For the next four or five thousand years, millet — not rice, not wheat — would be the staple grain of northern Chinese civilization.
Shang dynasty nobles drank millet wine.
Han dynasty soldiers were paid in millet.
Tang dynasty granaries were full of millet.
Its dominance begins here, on the loess plateau, with Peiligang farmers working the soil with stone sickles and grinding slabs.

What a Peiligang village looked like: several dozen semi-subterranean pit houses (round or rectangular pits dug a few feet into the loess and roofed over with thatched poles), a scattering of storage pits for grain, a separate area for burials, a kiln (a clay-and-earth oven hot enough to fire pottery), and a hundred or two hundred people.
The pit houses were warm in winter, cheap to build in the soft fine soil, and a pattern that would persist in rural northern China for thousands of years.
No temples.
No rulers we can identify.
But enough food stored in the pits to last the winter — and enough surplus, by the time the culture was a few centuries old, to support craftsmanship and trade.

A few hundred miles to the north, in the foothills of the Taihang Mountains (tie-hahng, a major north-south range in modern Hebei province), was a closely related culture called the Cishan (tsuh-SHAHN).
The Cishan people farmed the same millets, lived in the same kinds of pit houses, and used the same kinds of tools as the Peiligang.

What sets Cishan apart is an archaeological discovery that still startles scholars: at the site for which the culture is named, excavators uncovered dozens of underground grain storage pits containing carbonized millet.

The originally reported figures — around 88 pits and roughly 50,000 kilograms (about 110,000 pounds) of stored grain — are sometimes contested and may overstate the total, but even conservative estimates put Cishan in a class of its own for its era.
It is the earliest evidence of large-scale grain storage anywhere in the world.

Whatever Cishan was (a single extended family community, a village of a few hundred, a communal feasting center), it was producing surpluses many times larger than the immediate daily needs of the people who lived there.
Somebody was planning ahead, organizing labor, and probably redistributing food.
That is the economic signature of emerging social complexity: not kings yet, but something in motion.
About 370 miles (600 km) south of Peiligang, in the warm, wet marshes of the Yangtze River valley, a different set of people were domesticating a different crop.
This is the southern cradle, and the crop is rice.
Rice is a cultivated descendant of a wild grass called Oryza rufipogon that still grows in the swamps and shallow lakes of the Yangtze basin.

The wild grasses drop their seeds when the plants mature — a perfectly sensible behavior from the plant's point of view, since it's how the species reproduces, but a nightmare for anyone trying to harvest the grain, because by the time you get to a stand of wild rice, most of the seeds are already on the ground.
Domesticated rice, by contrast, holds its seeds on the stalk until a human comes along and cuts them off.
That single genetic change — a mutation that happens occasionally in any wild population — is the difference between a grass you can gather and a grass you can farm.

Sometime around 7000 BCE, probably over the course of many generations of selective harvesting and replanting, Yangtze valley communities pushed wild rice through that change and turned it into the crop we know today.
The earliest rice paddies yet found in the world are in China.

Sites like Kuahuqiao (KWAH-hoo-chow) and Shangshan (shahng-SHAHN) in the lower Yangtze preserve storage pits full of carbonized rice, stone tools used for harvesting and processing grain, and in some places the distinct earthen banks and flooded fields of actual paddy agriculture.
By 5000 BCE, the Yangtze delta was dotted with rice-farming communities using wooden tools and living in thatched houses built on stilts to keep them above the marsh.
The crop that would one day feed more than half of humanity began as an experimental harvest in these Chinese swamps.
So by about 6500 BCE, you have a remarkable situation.
In the north, farmers are growing dry-field millet on loess plains, storing it in underground pits, and living in warm winters-and-cool summers pit houses.
In the south, farmers are growing wet-field rice in flooded paddies, eating fish and waterfowl from the surrounding marshes, and living on stilts above the standing water.
The climates are different, the soils are different, the tools are different, the diets are different.
The people are not yet one culture, not even close.

But both sets are doing something that almost no one else in the world is doing yet: they are Neolithic farmers in the full modern sense, producing their own food on their own land and settling down to live with the consequences. (At roughly the same date, Andean communities in South America were independently domesticating potatoes — the only other major agricultural revolution built on tubers rather than grain (Early Andean Ch 1).) Between the two cradles, a third site deserves a chapter of its own, because what was found there is out of proportion to almost anything else from this era.
The site is Jiahu (jee-ah-HOO), in central Henan province, excavated in the 1980s and 1990s and gradually recognized as one of the most extraordinary Neolithic sites on Earth.

Jiahu was a rice-farming village — interesting in itself, because it is about halfway up the Yellow River basin, well north of what we usually think of as the rice-growing zone, and suggests that the north-south grain line was fuzzier than the map makes it look.
The Jiahu people raised rice, kept pigs and dogs, fished in a nearby lake, and buried their dead in a cemetery on the edge of the settlement.
The cemetery turned out to be full of surprises.
The first surprise was the Jiahu bone flutes.

In 1986, archaeologists excavating Jiahu burials began finding long, slender bone objects with rows of small holes drilled into them — five holes, six holes, seven holes, drilled with a precision that was clearly not accidental.
Chemical analysis identified the material as the wing bones of the red-crowned crane, a large migratory bird that still nests in eastern Chinese wetlands.
Careful examination showed that the holes were spaced in ways that produce intervals our ears would recognize as a musical scale.
And then someone did the obvious experiment: they made a modern copy of one of the flutes, brought in a musician, and asked the musician to play.
The Jiahu flutes are the oldest playable musical instruments ever found — and playable is the key word.
Several of them, when reproduced using the same materials and techniques, still produce clear, controlled tones.
One intact original, approximately nine thousand years old, produces a recognizable pentatonic or diatonic scale depending on how you finger it.

A modern musician can pick up a replica of a Jiahu flute and, within minutes, be playing a tune that fits comfortably into what a present-day Chinese listener would recognize as Chinese musical tradition.
The implications are staggering.
It means that music, as a formal structured art — with intervals, scales, and instruments designed for precise tuning — was part of village life in China from the very beginning of farming.
These were not primitive bone whistles.
They were instruments.
And then there were the symbols.

In the same Jiahu cemetery, archaeologists found tortoise shells and bones bearing small, incised marks — not accidental scratches, but deliberately cut symbols, 11 or more of them identifiable as distinct recurring forms.
The shells were mostly in elite burials: richer graves with more offerings, suggesting that whoever carried tortoise shells in their grave was someone important.

A handful of the Jiahu symbols, when you squint at them, look a bit like characters from the oracle bone script — the earliest undisputed form of Chinese writing, used by the Shang dynasty about 5,000 years later to record divinations on cattle bones and turtle shells.
It is the same material (turtle shell), the same kind of context (ritual, elite, associated with spiritual practice), and at least some of the same symbolic forms.
This is where the scholars start arguing.

One school of thought — dominant among some Chinese archaeologists — holds that the Jiahu symbols represent the earliest form of Chinese writing, predating Mesopotamian cuneiform (the wedge-shaped writing developed in what is now Iraq) by several thousand years.

A more cautious school, common among Western scholars, argues that 11 symbols used in ritual contexts do not yet constitute a writing system — they might be clan marks, ownership marks, counting tallies, or purely symbolic gestures with no linguistic content.
Nobody can prove it either way, because there simply isn't enough material to say whether the Jiahu marks encode spoken language.

What is clear is that 9,000 years ago, somebody in central China was already treating incised symbols on turtle shells as meaningful enough to put them in burials — and that the same material was going to be used for the same kind of ritual purpose by the Shang, on the same kind of shells, thousands of years later.
Whether it's "writing" or not, it is the earliest ghost of a thread that runs straight through Chinese history.
The Jiahu site gives us a glimpse of what the Neolithic was actually like when you get past the potsherds and dig down to the people.
These were not grunting proto-humans eking out a bare existence.

They were farmers who played music, buried their dead with care, carved symbols on sacred objects, and had already started using the materials and gestures that would define Chinese civilization for another five thousand years.
They had rice paddies and flutes and turtle-shell divinations in the same village, and they were doing all of this while the Egyptian pyramids were still four and a half thousand years in the future.

By around 5000 BCE, the Peiligang and Cishan cultures in the north had given way to a larger, more confident successor — bigger villages, bolder pottery, longer trade links — that would take over the entire middle Yellow River valley.
The Yangtze farmers expanded their paddies and built more sophisticated villages.
Jiahu itself was abandoned — the reasons are unclear, possibly local climate shift — but its traditions of music, ritual, and symbol-making didn't die.
They moved on.
And the next wave of villages, bigger and more ambitious, was about to start painting pottery in colors so vivid that modern museums still line them up in glass cases and turn down the lights.

chapters.title: The Painted Pottery World
sentences: This chapter is about the assembly, across the fifth and fourth millennia BCE, of the cultural raw materials that would eventually feed into Chinese civilization: a recognizable artistic grammar, regional diversity between a dry northern farming world and a wet southern one, a native luxury textile, and — equally importantly — the first mythological figures the Chinese tradition would later remember as having lived in this era.
None of this is yet a state.
It is the warehouse of ingredients that the state-builders of chapters 5 through 8 will eventually find on the shelves.
By 5000 BCE, the scattered farming communities of the early Neolithic had been replaced by something much more impressive.
The villages were bigger.
The pottery was better.
The communities traded with each other across longer distances.

Craft specialization — people spending most of their time making pots, or stone tools, or jade ornaments, instead of farming — was becoming possible because some villages could now produce enough surplus food to feed a few full-time craftspeople.
And over most of the northern Chinese agricultural zone, a single cultural tradition emerged that would dominate the region for the next two thousand years.
It is called the Yangshao culture (yahng-SHAO), and it is the first phase of Chinese prehistory where you can start pointing to things and saying: that is going to be Chinese forever.

Yangshao takes its name from the village near which its defining pottery was first found in 1921 — an important date, because the discovery of Yangshao was the moment modern archaeology realized that China had its own Neolithic, independent of Mesopotamia and Egypt.

The great prehistorian Johan Gunnar Andersson, a Swede working for the Chinese government, excavated a site in western Henan province and turned up a cache of beautifully painted red pottery that turned out to be more than six thousand years old.
Before Andersson, it was quite possible to believe — as many early 20th-century European scholars did — that Chinese civilization had been imported from the Near East.
Yangshao killed that idea.
It was indisputably local, indisputably ancient, and beautiful enough that it made the front pages of Chinese newspapers.
The hallmark of Yangshao culture is its painted pottery.

Before firing, Yangshao potters coated their vessels with a reddish-brown slip and then painted bold geometric designs — spirals, triangles, zigzags, net patterns — along with stylized fish, frogs, deer, and, most strikingly, human faces with fish attached to them.

The "human face and fish" motif, which shows up on dozens of Yangshao bowls, is one of the iconic images of Chinese prehistory: a round, flat face with curved fishes where the ears should be, framed by geometric borders.
Scholars argue about what it means.
A shamanic image?
A representation of a water god?
A child's toy?
Nobody knows, but the design is so specific and so repeatable that it must have meant something important to the people who painted it, over and over, for a thousand years.
What makes Yangshao pottery historically significant isn't just that it's pretty.

It's that the specific artistic vocabulary — the spirals, the animal faces, the geometric borders, the rhythm of decoration in bands around a vessel — would resurface, almost unchanged, on Shang dynasty bronze vessels two thousand years later.
Chinese art has a very long memory, and its deep grammar starts in Yangshao.
The best-known Yangshao village is Banpo (BAHN-poh), on the outskirts of modern Xi'an in Shaanxi province.

Banpo was occupied from roughly 4800 to 3750 BCE — over a thousand years of continuous village life — and when Chinese archaeologists excavated it in the 1950s, they found one of the most completely preserved Neolithic villages anywhere on earth.
Banpo is laid out in a way that looks almost planned.
A central area held a large communal building, probably used for village meetings or shared storage.
Surrounding it were smaller pit dwellings, each with its own hearth and storage pits.
A dedicated kiln area on the edge of the village produced the painted pottery.
A cemetery — with adults buried in one section and infants buried in pottery jars in another — sat outside the living area.
And around the whole thing, the villagers dug a defensive moat — a trench several meters wide and deep.
Whatever the Banpo people were worried about, it was organized enough that they invested in earthworks to keep it out.
Banpo is roughly the size of a modern small village — five hectares (about 12 acres), housing a few hundred people.
But everything about it suggests a level of social organization beyond the bare Neolithic minimum.
There are specialized craft zones.
There is a communal building.
There is a burial ground with differentiated treatment for different kinds of dead (adults outside, infants in jars near the houses, suggesting different beliefs about their spirits).
There is a moat, which implies either defense against enemies or containment against predators, neither of which comes free.
And — in one of the details that will echo through the rest of this story — there are incised symbols on some of the pottery.
The Banpo pottery marks are a puzzle.
Over a hundred distinct geometric symbols have been catalogued on Yangshao vessel rims, mostly at Banpo and related Wei River valley sites.
Some are simple — a vertical line, a plus sign, a circle.
Some are more elaborate — linked diamonds, branching lines, curved hooks.
They are small, consistent, and clearly made on purpose.
But they are not yet a writing system.
Nobody has been able to extract from them a grammar, a set of words, or a reading.
The best guesses are that they were ownership marks (this pot belongs to family X), clan symbols (our lineage uses this sign), or counting tallies (this vessel holds five units).
A few of the Banpo symbols look vaguely like later Chinese characters, but the resemblance is suggestive rather than conclusive.
What the Banpo marks do tell us is that the habit of marking meaningful things with symbols was already deeply embedded in Yangshao culture.

Whatever these marks meant, they were part of a world in which people had started using visual signs to stand for things — a habit that, a few thousand years later, would crystallize into the Chinese writing system.
The marks are not the beginning of Chinese writing.
But they are part of the long prehistory of it.
Combined with the Jiahu symbols (Chapter 2), they make it clear that Chinese civilization was groping toward writing for thousands of years before it finally arrived.

While Yangshao was dominating the Yellow River valley, a very different kind of culture was flourishing in the Yangtze delta — a culture that has to be explained separately because its world didn't look anything like Banpo's.
This is the Hemudu culture (HUH-moo-doo), centered in modern Zhejiang province around the lower Yangtze marshes.
If you imagine Banpo — dusty loess soil, dry-farmed millet, pit houses, moats — then Hemudu is its opposite.
Hemudu sat on a tidal flat near the mouth of the Yangtze.
The ground was wet most of the year, the climate was subtropical, the local environment was a patchwork of rivers, lakes, and marshes.
You couldn't dig pit houses here; the water table was too high.
So the Hemudu people built their houses on stilts, above the water level, using wooden piles driven into the mud and a superstructure of thatched beams and mat walls.
The style was essentially a longhouse raised on a wooden platform — the same basic architecture that villagers still use today in parts of Southeast Asia and Indonesia.
The Hemudu site preserves the wooden joinery remarkably well because the waterlogged soil prevented it from rotting.

Archaeologists have recovered mortise-and-tenon joints (a woodworking technique in which a protruding "tenon" fits into a matching hole, a "mortise," holding two timbers together without nails) that are as sophisticated as anything made two thousand years later.
Hemudu farmed rice, of course — vast fields of it, flooded paddies covering the tidal flats around the villages.
They also kept domestic pigs, harvested fish, hunted deer, and maintained large groves of a native Chinese nut tree called the water chestnut.

Their tools included plentiful bone and wooden implements — picks, spades, weavers' shuttles — that show a craft tradition as refined as the Yangshao pottery tradition but focused on completely different materials and needs.

And, in one of the remarkable survivals at the site, Hemudu preserves the earliest known Chinese lacquerwork: wooden bowls painted with red lacquer (a hard, glossy coating made from the sap of the lacquer tree, Toxicodendron vernicifluum, which is native to central and southern China).
Lacquerwork would become one of the great decorative arts of Chinese civilization, and its ancestry traces back to these Yangtze stilt-house villages seven thousand years ago.

Hemudu matters because it is the first clear sign that the Yangtze world was not just lagging behind the Yellow River world — it was doing its own thing, at its own pace, with its own materials and its own traditions.
The north and the south of China, from almost the beginning of the Neolithic, were building two parallel versions of settled life.
They would stay parallel for thousands of years.
When they finally started to merge, the combination produced a richer and more varied civilization than either alone could have.

Somewhere in this painted-pottery world, at a date we can't fix precisely, the Chinese also started making silk — a technology that would eventually create the Silk Road and transform the global economy.
The silkworm (Bombyx mori) is native to China and feeds exclusively on the leaves of the white mulberry tree.

Its larvae spin cocoons from a single continuous filament of silk protein, and unwinding those filaments to make thread and cloth was a specifically Chinese achievement that the rest of the world wouldn't match for thousands of years.
Chinese legend credits Leizu (LAY-dzoo), the wife of the Yellow Emperor (Chapter 6), with discovering silk when a cocoon fell into her tea cup and unraveled.

Archaeology, characteristically, dates it earlier than the legend: a cocoon cut in half with a stone tool, recovered from Shanxi province, dates to around 3600 BCE, and silk thread fragments from the Liangzhu culture (Chapter 5) are not much younger.
By 3500 BCE, silk was being made and worked somewhere in Neolithic China.
Its parallel on the Indus side of the world — Indus Valley cotton at roughly the same date — would become the other great Asian luxury textile.
Silk and cotton, the two signature Asian fibers, were born at almost the same moment.
The final piece of this era is the legendary sage-kings — not archaeology at all, but the way Chinese tradition itself remembered this period thousands of years later.

Much later Chinese texts — particularly the Records of the Grand Historian by Sima Qian (suh-MAH chee-EN), written around 100 BCE, and the Book of Documents (Shujing), parts of which are older — describe a series of semi-divine rulers who supposedly governed China before the first dynasties.

These are the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors, a rotating cast of figures whose names and exploits vary between sources but whose role in Chinese tradition is the same: they are the culture heroes who invented the arts of civilization.
They are not history in any modern sense.
No archaeological evidence connects them to actual individuals.
But they are deeply embedded in Chinese self-understanding, and they represent how classical Chinese writers imagined the era we are living through in this chapter.
Fuxi (FOO-shee) is usually listed as the first of the Three Sovereigns.

He is depicted as a half-human, half-serpent figure, sometimes shown intertwined with his sister-wife Nüwa (NOO-wah), and he is credited with inventing fishing nets, teaching animal domestication, and — most importantly — creating the bagua (BAH-gwah), the Eight Trigrams.

The Eight Trigrams are an ancient divination system in which combinations of broken and unbroken lines encode the fundamental forces of nature (heaven, earth, fire, water, mountain, thunder, wind, lake).

They became the foundation of the I Ching (the Book of Changes, an ancient Chinese philosophical and divinatory text that Confucius reportedly studied obsessively), and they still appear today in martial arts schools, feng shui practice, and the South Korean flag.
Whether or not Fuxi was real, the idea he represents — the sage who imposes cosmic order on chaos through pattern recognition — is one of the oldest recurring themes in Chinese thought.
Shennong (shen-NONG — "the Divine Farmer") is usually listed next.

He is depicted with a transparent belly that let him watch the effect of each plant he ate, which he did in the name of discovering which species were edible, which were medicinal, and which were poisonous.
He supposedly invented the plow, taught crop cultivation, and catalogued hundreds of herbs before dying from a toxic plant he ate while testing it.
The text associated with his name, the Shennong Bencao Jing (Divine Farmer's Classic of Materia Medica), is the foundational text of traditional Chinese medicine.

Shennong's story, like Fuxi's, is mythological, but it encodes a real truth: Chinese agricultural and medical knowledge accumulated over thousands of years of observation, trial, error, and — sometimes — lethal experimentation.
Somebody had to figure out which plants were safe.
Somebody did, over generations.
Shennong is the mythological personification of that accumulated wisdom.
Chinese tradition places both Fuxi and Shennong somewhere in the fifth or sixth millennium BCE — in the middle of the Yangshao and Hemudu era we've been describing.
Obviously they were not real people.

But the Chinese placing of them in this specific era reflects an ancient Chinese intuition that something culturally important was happening right around then: villages were getting bigger, agriculture was getting better, medicine and ritual were becoming more elaborate, and the basic pattern of settled Chinese life was taking shape.
Later generations needed names and stories to attach to that transformation.
They invented them.

As the fourth millennium BCE gave way to the third, this world of painted pottery villages was about to get a jolt from an unexpected direction: a culture far to the northeast, in the forests and grasslands of what is now Liaoning and Inner Mongolia, was already carving the most extraordinary objects anyone in the Chinese Neolithic had yet produced, out of the most sacred material in Chinese history: jade.

chapters.title: Jade and the Pig-Dragon
sentences: If you are reading a book about ancient China and you want to understand the culture in a single object, don't look at a bronze or a scroll.
Look at a piece of Jade.
Jade is not a single mineral.

It's a catch-all word for two very different tough, green stones: nephrite, which is a variety of a calcium-magnesium-iron silicate mineral called tremolite-actinolite, and jadeite, a harder sodium-aluminum silicate.
The Chinese word yu (pronounced "yoo") covers both and a handful of other similar green stones as well.

In the period we're talking about in this book, the only jade the Chinese had access to was nephrite, sourced from deposits in the Kunlun Mountains of northwestern China (Chapter 1) and from lesser deposits in the northeast, near modern Liaoning province.
Jadeite, the flashier Burmese green stone most Western museum-goers think of when they hear "jade," wouldn't arrive in China until almost 4,000 years later, during the Qing dynasty.
Nephrite jade has some peculiar physical properties that explain its cultural weight.
It is extraordinarily tough — resistant to shattering in a way that even diamond isn't, because of the way its fibers interlock.
It is hard enough that until the invention of metal tools, the only way to shape it was by slowly grinding it down with abrasive sand — a process that could take weeks or months per object.
When polished, it takes on a deep greasy luster that is unlike any other stone.
And when struck gently, it resonates with a clear, bell-like musical tone — a quality classical Chinese writers associated with virtue itself.
Jade is hard, enduring, pure, and beautiful — all the qualities that Chinese thought came to associate with the ideal human being.
Later generations would explicitly identify jade with moral virtue.
Zhou dynasty nobles wore jade pendants to signal their character.
Han emperors were buried in suits stitched together from thousands of jade plaques, because the stone was believed to preserve the body and soul from decay.
All of that cultural weight traces back, ultimately, to the Neolithic.

And the most important Neolithic jade-working culture was not in the Yellow River or the Yangtze valleys, but in a corner of northeastern China that doesn't usually get much billing: the grasslands and river valleys of Liaoning and Inner Mongolia.
The culture is called Hongshan (hong-SHAHN, "Red Mountain"), and it changed the rules.

The Hongshan culture flourished from about 4700 to 2900 BCE — almost two thousand years — in a region that is today cold grassland and scrubby forest but was, in the warm postglacial climate, a more hospitable mix of marshes, lakes, and open woodland.
The Hongshan people were farmers (millet again — they were part of the northern dry-farming tradition) but they seem to have been equally invested in hunting, gathering, and ritual.

Their most famous site is Niuheliang (NYOO-huh-lee-ahng), in what is now western Liaoning, where archaeologists uncovered the earliest known Chinese ritual landscape: a series of platforms, stone-circle altars, and a structure now called the Goddess Temple — a ruined building that contained fragments of life-sized clay statues, including a famous fragmentary head with inset green jade eyes.
The Niuheliang complex is not a village.
It is a sacred precinct — the earliest one yet found in China — built on the tops of low hills, aligned with each other and with celestial features, and surrounded by elite burial grounds.

It is evidence that by around 3500 BCE, the Hongshan people had specialists whose entire job was maintaining a ritual landscape: a priestly class, or at least a category of people whose work was sacred rather than agricultural.
And the burials at Niuheliang and related Hongshan sites contain the jade.
The Hongshan jade workers, using nothing but abrasive sand, wooden drills, and cords strung with grit, carved shapes that no one in the world was carving at the time.
There are jade turtles, jade birds, jade fish, jade cloud-shaped ornaments, and small jade plaques pierced for suspension as pendants.
There are anthropomorphic figures — carved humans with stylized features, some seated cross-legged, some with their arms folded across their chests.
But the most famous Hongshan jades are the ones that aren't quite anything: C-shaped curled creatures with pig-like snouts, slit eyes, and coiled serpentine bodies.
They are called zhulong (JOO-long), which translates literally as "pig-dragon," and they are the earliest dragon images yet found anywhere in Chinese art.
The pig-dragon needs a moment of its own, because of what it eventually becomes.

The object itself is small — typically 2 to 10 inches long (5 to 25 cm) — and simple in outline: a curled body forming most of a circle, a pig-like head at one end, a tail at the other, and sometimes a hole drilled through the center for suspension.
They were found in elite Hongshan burials, laid on the chests of the deceased or around their heads.
What they were for is a matter of educated guessing.

The pig and the dragon were two of the most important mythological creatures in later Chinese thought — the pig as the embodiment of domestic wealth, fertility, and the earth; the dragon as the embodiment of water, rain, and cosmic power.
Combining them in a single coiled object may have been the Hongshan way of invoking both at once: fertility, rain, and the promise of a continuing cycle of rebirth for the buried elite.
What is historically important about the pig-dragons is that the shapes don't go away.
The same curled-serpent motif appears, slightly modified, on Shang bronzes two thousand years later.
It appears again on Han dynasty jade ornaments.

It becomes, over the millennia, the recognizable Chinese dragon — the long, serpentine, clawed and horned creature that adorned imperial robes, imperial thrones, imperial temples, and the imperial self-conception of every Chinese dynasty from the Qin to the Qing.

When a Chinese emperor of the Ming dynasty embroidered a dragon on his yellow robe, he was participating in an unbroken visual tradition that started with a Hongshan jade worker grinding a curve into a lump of nephrite in a grassland village five thousand years earlier.
This kind of deep continuity is one of the strangest and most distinctive features of Chinese civilization.

It is entirely possible, in fact common, for a symbol or a motif to start in the Neolithic, evolve through every subsequent era without ever really dying, and still be meaningful to a modern Chinese person today.
Dragons are the most famous example.
Jade ritual objects are another. ancestor worship (which we'll come back to in Chapter 6) is a third.
The cultural DNA of what will become imperial China is being laid down, piece by piece, by people who left no writing we can read and whose names no one alive knows.
One more thing deserves mention, because it sets up everything that follows.
The Hongshan burials are where a very Chinese religious idea starts to become archaeologically visible: ancestor worship.
In earlier Neolithic cemeteries, graves are broadly egalitarian.
By the Hongshan period, some graves are conspicuously richer than others, stocked with jade objects and ritual vessels — bowls, goblets, and tripod pots used to offer food and drink to the deceased.
The strong implication is that the living were feeding the dead, treating ancestors as entities who remained present and powerful.

This is the archaeological signature of ancestor worship, the defining feature of Chinese religious life for the next five thousand years — from the Shang kings consulting their ancestors through oracle bones to Chinese families today sweeping graves at the Qingming festival every spring.
Jade, for the first time in this tradition, is the material the living use to talk to the dead.

And then, around 3300 BCE, something no one had seen before happened in the Yangtze delta: a walled city of 300 hectares with a massive hydraulic infrastructure, hundreds of jade-rich burials, a ritual precinct holding the largest jade objects anyone had ever made, and proto-written symbols on pottery that scholars are still arguing about.
The place is called Liangzhu, and when Chinese archaeologists finally understood what they had found there, it rewrote the entire map of where Chinese civilization started.

chapters.title: The Liangzhu Miracle
sentences: Here is an archaeological puzzle: for almost a century, the standard story of Chinese civilization said that it began on the Yellow River.
The Yellow River, the millet farmers, the walled towns of the Longshan horizon, the Xia and Shang dynasties — that was the core narrative.

Anything that happened in the Yangtze delta was considered peripheral: rice farming, yes, stilt houses, yes, some interesting pottery, but nothing that had really mattered in the way the northern sites had.
Then archaeologists kept digging around a place called Liangzhu (lee-AHNG-joo), a few miles northwest of the modern city of Hangzhou in Zhejiang province, near the southern shore of Lake Tai.
They had known Liangzhu was a Neolithic site since 1936.
What they didn't know, for most of the 20th century, was how big it was — or what was underneath it.
As the excavations widened through the 1980s, 1990s, and especially the 2000s, the picture kept getting larger.
And larger.
And larger.

By the time they were done mapping it, the consensus was that the standard story was wrong, and that Chinese civilization had a second origin point — a very early one — that might actually have been the first.
Liangzhu is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and the phrase "first Chinese civilization" is increasingly being applied to it.
To understand why, you have to imagine what the place looked like at its peak, around 3000 BCE.
The Liangzhu city core covered about 300 hectares (roughly 740 acres, or 1.2 square miles — a bit smaller than New York City's Central Park, which is closer to 840 acres).
In the center was a roughly rectangular walled compound of about 30 hectares, likely the palace or ceremonial precinct — larger than Beijing's modern Forbidden City.
Around that, inside a second, much longer outer wall, lay residential districts, workshops, craft zones, and storage areas.

The whole thing was built on an artificial earthen platform raised several meters above the surrounding marshland, which would have made it visually striking to anyone approaching on foot: a vast flat mound rising out of the lake country, topped with wooden palaces and ceremonial terraces.
Walls this big were almost unknown anywhere in the world at this date.
A few isolated settlements in the Near East and Europe had enclosures at roughly similar scale, but nothing quite like the integrated urban-and-ceremonial complex at Liangzhu.
And the city wasn't even the most impressive part.
The most impressive part was the water system.

Surrounding Liangzhu, and extending for more than 100 square kilometers (roughly 40 square miles), was an enormous network of dams, dikes, canals, and reservoirs — an integrated hydraulic system designed to manage the runoff from the surrounding hills, prevent the rice paddies from flooding during storms, maintain a steady water supply during dry periods, and enable boat transport across the whole region.
The outer dams are several miles long, built up from layers of rammed earth held together with clay cores and stone armor.
At least eleven major dams have been mapped.
They appear to have been built as a coordinated project — not accumulated over centuries of piecemeal construction, but engineered as a single system, with a common design and a common purpose.
This is the largest and most sophisticated water control project anywhere in the world for another two thousand years.
The next thing comparable in scale is the Han dynasty's irrigation works in the 2nd century BCE.
That Liangzhu — a Neolithic culture with stone tools and no writing we can read — was running hydraulic engineering on this scale around 3000 BCE is, to put it mildly, a shock.
A water system of this size implies several things at once.

It implies centralized authority — someone, or some small group, had the power to mobilize thousands of workers over years, to feed them while they dug, and to direct their labor toward a single coordinated plan.
It implies agricultural surplus large enough to support that labor without starving everyone.
It implies engineering knowledge — not just about how to pile up earth, but about hydrology, about flow rates, about seasonal runoff, about soil mechanics.
And it implies political continuity, because a project of this scale can't be finished by a single leader in a single lifetime.
It has to be inherited, maintained, expanded.
The Liangzhu people were running, in effect, a small early state.
Once you see the water system, the rest of Liangzhu starts to make sense.
The walled city at the center was the political and ceremonial heart of a region that may have covered 35,000 square kilometers (about 14,000 square miles, roughly the size of Maryland).

Hundreds of smaller Liangzhu sites — villages, towns, ceremonial platforms, craft workshops, rice fields — have been identified within that radius, and all of them trace culturally back to the same core.
This is a regional polity, not just a single city.
The people in outlying settlements made pottery in the Liangzhu style, buried their dead with Liangzhu-style jade, and presumably paid tribute, in rice or labor, to the elite at the center.
What to call such a polity is a matter of semantics.
Some archaeologists flatly call it a state.
Others prefer "chiefdom" or "proto-state" because there is no writing to tell us what the Liangzhu people called themselves.
For our purposes, the question is mostly academic.
Whatever you call it, it was something that had never been seen in China before, and something that the rest of East Asia wouldn't match for another fifteen hundred years.
And then there were the jades.
The jade treasures of Liangzhu are what distinguish it from every previous and contemporary culture in China.
The Hongshan jade workers had been extraordinary, but Hongshan worked mostly in smallish pendants and figurines.
The Liangzhu jade workers worked on a completely different scale, with a completely different set of forms, for a completely different kind of ritual purpose.
The two defining Liangzhu jade forms are the bi (BEE) and the cong (TSONG), and they are the things you should remember from this chapter if you remember nothing else.
The bi is a flat circular disc with a hole in the middle — a large, polished, green disc the size of a dinner plate, or sometimes smaller, shaped like a donut.
Chinese tradition, preserved in much later texts, associates the bi with heaven.
The round shape, the hollow center, the way the disc catches light when held up to the sun — all of these are read as symbols of the sky, the cosmos, the realm of the spirits.
Thousands of bi discs have been excavated from Liangzhu burials.
In the richer graves, they are stacked on the chest and abdomen of the deceased, one on top of another, sometimes a dozen or more, forming a column of jade from throat to pelvis.
The deceased was being wrapped, posthumously, in a column of sky.
The cong is its counterpart.

It is a square-sectioned tube with a circular bore running through it — a hollow rectangular prism, carved from a single block of jade, often decorated on its outer faces with stylized animal-mask designs.
Chinese tradition associates the cong with earth.
The square outer shape represents the four corners of the world, while the round inner bore represents heaven passing through earth — the two realms locked together in a single object.
Cong come in all sizes.
A small one might be a few inches tall; a large ceremonial one can be a foot and a half.

They are harder to carve than bi, because cutting a square-sectioned hollow tube out of a single jade boulder is an extraordinarily demanding piece of stone work, and Liangzhu cong sometimes have as many as fifteen rings of carved decoration stacked down their length, each with precision-cut animal-mask designs.
The bi and the cong, together, encoded a cosmology.

Round sky over square earth, heaven and earth linked by the ritual objects of the king — that is a Chinese cosmological model that would be stated explicitly in much later texts, and here it is at Liangzhu, already fully formed, thousands of years before anyone was writing it down.
Alongside the bi and cong, Liangzhu workshops produced elaborate jade axes, jade headdresses, jade pendants, and small jade figurines.
The most spectacular single object is called the "King's Yue Axe" — a jade ceremonial axe carved from a single block, bearing a delicate taotie-like animal mask on its blade.
The taotie (tow-tee-YEH) is a stylized animal face — usually with bulging eyes, a wide gaping mouth, a pair of horns or a crest, and stylized body elements radiating out symmetrically.
It is one of the most iconic motifs in Chinese art, appearing on Shang bronzes, Zhou jade, and beyond.
And it is present, in proto-form, on Liangzhu jades — another of those unbroken visual threads from the Neolithic to the historical era.
The amount of jade found at Liangzhu is staggering.
The richest burials contain hundreds of jade objects per person.

The single most spectacular elite grave at the Fanshan burial ground, excavated in 1986, reportedly held more than 500 pieces of jade — dozens of bi discs, a large number of cong tubes, axes, beads, pendants, and ornaments for a headdress — all interred with one individual.
The specific counts vary between published reports, but the order of magnitude is not in dispute: a single burial could have consumed the labor of dozens of specialist jade workers for a year.
The source of the jade itself is another mystery that deepens the picture.
Nephrite doesn't naturally occur near Liangzhu.
The raw material must have been imported — possibly from distant deposits in Liaoning, possibly from even farther afield.
Nobody has found a single Liangzhu jade quarry on site.

The entire enormous jade production tradition was being fed by long-distance trade in raw stone, which means the Liangzhu elites were plugged into exchange networks that reached across much of eastern China.

They had the political power to bring precious materials hundreds of miles to their workshops, and they had the craft capacity to turn those materials into the most refined ritual objects anyone in East Asia had yet produced.
One more thing about Liangzhu, because it will come up again: the proto-symbols.

Hundreds of artifacts at Liangzhu bear incised symbols — not as individual marks on individual pots, the way Banpo symbols had been, but sometimes in sequences, two or three or even four symbols strung together on a single jade or ceramic object.
Over 600 artifacts with such marks have been catalogued.

The most tantalizing examples are symbols that appear in clusters on cong tubes or on pottery jars — clusters that look less like decorative patterns and more like short phrases, as if somebody were marking these objects with a formula or label.
Are the Liangzhu symbols writing?
The same scholarly fight as with Jiahu and Banpo plays out here, but with slightly more material to chew on.
Chinese archaeologists increasingly think so — the sequence patterns, the ritual context, the consistency across the region, all suggest a system more developed than any earlier one in China.
Western scholars tend to be more cautious, pointing out that without a known language behind the symbols, it's impossible to prove they encode speech rather than serving as elaborate ritual notation.
Nobody has read the Liangzhu symbols.
Nobody can.

But they are the most sophisticated Neolithic symbol system China has yet produced, and whatever they encode, they were important enough for the Liangzhu elite to inscribe on their most sacred objects.
And then, between about 2400 and 2300 BCE, Liangzhu collapsed.
The collapse was relatively sudden and relatively complete.
The central city was abandoned.
The water system fell into disuse.
The jade workshops stopped producing.
Population in the region dropped dramatically.
The distinctive Liangzhu burial style and pottery style disappeared from the archaeological record.
Within a few generations, the entire civilization that had taken almost a thousand years to build was simply gone.
The reasons are disputed.

The leading theory is catastrophic flooding — core samples from the region show evidence of massive water events around the end of the Liangzhu period, and the very hydraulic system that had made the civilization possible may have failed at precisely the wrong moment.

But Liangzhu's collapse also sits on the leading edge of a climate event that was about to devastate much of the ancient world: the 4.2 kiloyear event (so called because it happened about 4,200 years before the present), a prolonged global drought centered around 2200–1900 BCE.

The same climate disruption would contribute, a few centuries later, to the fall of the Akkadian Empire in Mesopotamia (the first multi-ethnic empire in human history, centered in what is now Iraq), disrupt Egypt's Old Kingdom, and trigger the slow collapse of the Indus Valley Civilization in modern Pakistan.
The fingerprints of this single global climate shift run across three Bronze Age civilizations at roughly the same time, and Liangzhu may be the easternmost of them — the first domino to fall.
There is a haunting possibility embedded in the collapse.

The Chinese tradition that would crystallize thousands of years later includes a foundational story about a Great Flood — a catastrophe that drowned the world for generations until the legendary hero Yu the Great tamed the waters and founded the first dynasty (Chapter 7).
For most of the 20th century, scholars treated the Great Flood myth as pure folklore, a mythological explanation borrowed from older traditions or invented out of ritual significance.
But the Liangzhu collapse is the kind of event that could plausibly have been remembered, distorted, and mythologized over centuries of oral tradition.
A massive flood that destroyed the greatest civilization anyone had seen, leaving a scar on the cultural memory of the Yangtze delta, is exactly the kind of event that mythology is made of.

The connection is speculative — the dates don't perfectly align, and the geography is not the same as the later legend's — but the possibility that the Chinese Great Flood tradition has a Liangzhu memory at its root is one of the more evocative hypotheses in modern Chinese archaeology.
Whatever the cause, Liangzhu's disappearance did not erase its influence.

The jade traditions — the bi and the cong, the animal masks, the prestige burial with jade stacked on the body — were inherited by later cultures, including the Longshan horizon (Chapter 6) and eventually the Shang.
Many of the specific forms the Liangzhu jade workers had perfected kept being made, almost unchanged, for another fifteen hundred years.
The city was gone.
The cosmology was forever.
And while Liangzhu was at its height and then collapsing in the south, something very different was happening in the north.
The Yellow River valley was producing a new kind of society — a society of walled towns, warlords, and legendary god-kings.
It is this world, not Liangzhu, that Chinese tradition would eventually remember as the seedbed of the first dynasties.

chapters.title: Longshan: Walls, Warlords, and the Yellow Emperor
sentences: If you had been a farmer living in the Yellow River valley around 3000 BCE, you would have noticed, over the course of your life and the lives of your parents and grandparents, that the world was getting harder and stranger.
The old Yangshao villages, with their painted pottery and their communal houses and their modest moats, had become something more serious.
Walls were getting bigger — in some places, much bigger.
Villages were piling up in clusters.
The people who lived in the biggest houses were taking more and more of the food.
People were starting to keep track of fights — old weapons, armor, burned foundations, bodies hastily buried in walls.
There was a new kind of pottery coming out of certain workshops — black, wheel-thrown, so thin you could see sunlight through it, meant for ritual use and nothing else.
And there were stories about great men: a tribal chief who commanded the spirits of the rivers, a warrior with a head of iron, a battle that had decided who got to rule everything.
This is the Longshan horizon — not a single culture, but a broad stretch of related cultures across the North China Plain between about 3000 and 1900 BCE.
It is the phase where the egalitarian village world of the early Neolithic breaks down for good, and something that looks unmistakably like early states takes its place.
By the end of it, the gap between the living conditions of the elite and the ordinary farmer was vast.
Ritual was being monopolized by specialist priests.
Warfare had become a normal feature of life.

And a particular kind of elite power — the power to command labor, monopolize sacred knowledge, and dictate what happened in the afterlife — had crystallized in ways that anticipate the first dynasties.
It is also the phase where Chinese tradition places its first named rulers.
Archaeology cannot confirm that any of them were real.
But the stories about them are older, in some cases, than the Shang dynasty itself, and they encode something about how the Longshan-era transformation was remembered.
Start with the pots.
Longshan black pottery is one of the great technical achievements of the Neolithic.
Yangshao potters had made their vessels by hand, coiling or paddling the clay into shape.
Longshan potters used the fast kick-wheel (a spinning turntable powered by a foot pedal, which allowed a skilled craftsperson to throw a symmetrical vessel in minutes).

The fast wheel wasn't invented in China — it appears earlier in Mesopotamia — but Longshan potters took it to an extreme no one else ever matched: they produced cups and goblets with walls well under a millimeter thick, literally eggshell-thin, so fragile they could not have been used for anything practical.
Some of the stem cups have stems so slender they couldn't support their own weight if you filled them with liquid.
These were not dinnerware.

They were ritual objects, made exclusively for the dead and for ceremonies — objects whose entire purpose was to display the technical mastery and economic surplus of the community that could produce them.

Black Longshan pottery is also notable for its firing technique: the potters used reduction firing, deliberately limiting oxygen in the kiln so that the clay vitrified into a glossy, jet-black surface rather than the oxidized red of earlier Chinese pottery.
The result is striking in a way that the museum photographs don't quite capture — a surface so dark and so polished that the vessels look almost like they are made of black glass.
The significance of Longshan pottery isn't just aesthetic.

The existence of eggshell-thin ritual cups means there were full-time specialist craftspeople — not farmers who made pots in their spare time, but people whose entire working life revolved around producing objects for elites who paid them (in grain, shelter, or prestige) to do nothing else.
Craft specialization at that level is one of the hallmarks of an emerging state.
It means an economy complex enough to support non-producing specialists, an elite wealthy enough to patronize them, and a distribution system that gets their products from the workshop to the burial.

The same fast-wheel technology, incidentally, would later be applied by Shang dynasty craftsmen to make the ceramic molds used for bronze casting — so the technique itself is a direct ancestor of one of the defining crafts of the Chinese Bronze Age.
And then there are the walls.

Across the Yellow River plain, from Shandong in the east to Shaanxi in the west and down into central Henan, Longshan-era communities began building rammed-earth walls around their settlements on a scale that dwarfed anything in the Yangshao period.

The technique is called hangtu (HAHNG-too): workers piled layers of loess, added a little water, and pounded each layer with wooden rammers until the soil compacted into a surface as hard as soft concrete.
Layer after layer was built up until the wall reached its intended height, sometimes 10 meters (33 feet) or more.
Longshan hangtu walls can be 16 to 50 feet (5 to 15 meters) thick at the base.
They enclose areas ranging from a few hectares to, at the largest known sites, over 400 hectares — roughly 1.5 square miles (4 square kilometers) of walled town.
That's already in the same league as the great walled cities of Bronze Age Mesopotamia (Uruk's walls at around 2700 BCE) and the early Indus (Kot Diji's defensive walls by 2800 BCE).
Across the whole arc of Eurasia, the same kind of thing was happening at roughly the same time: emerging elites, competing polities, and serious walls.
The rammed-earth technique would become the standard for Chinese monumental construction for the rest of the pre-modern era.
The first Great Wall (not the stone one tourists see today, but earlier earth versions) was hangtu.
The Han, Tang, and Ming capitals were surrounded by hangtu walls.
The Forbidden City sits on a hangtu platform.
The construction method, perfected during the Longshan horizon, was still in use thousands of years later.
And it comes out of this specific period, out of this specific reason to build walls: people were fighting each other, at a scale and a frequency that made simple palisades inadequate.
Chiefs needed real defenses.

The most famous Longshan walled sites are Taosi (TAO-suh, in Shanxi province), Liangchengzhen (lee-AHNG-chung-JEN, in Shandong province), and Shimao (SHEE-mao, in Shaanxi — the double-a, western province).
Shimao is particularly startling.

It covers about 400 hectares, with an outer wall made of stone rather than rammed earth (unusual for China), and in its lower walls, archaeologists have found decapitated human heads, deliberately buried in the foundations.
Several pits containing dozens of skulls each, concentrated on young women, arranged in deliberate rows.
Someone was sacrificing people to consecrate the walls of Shimao around 2000 BCE.

The practice of foundation sacrifice (killing people or animals and burying them in the footings of important buildings to bless the structure) would continue in China through the Shang and Zhou dynasties, but Shimao is one of its earliest and most graphic examples — and it has direct parallels: at roughly the same time, far to the west in Mesopotamia, the Royal Tombs of Ur were being filled with dozens of retainer sacrifices arranged in rows around their rulers (Mesopotamia Ch 4).
Two completely independent civilizations at opposite ends of Eurasia, burying the same kind of sacrificed bodies at the same moment.
Egypt did it even earlier and at a larger scale — King Djer's tomb at Abydos (~3000 BCE) contained 338 subsidiary burials (Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 6).
The Indus Valley, oddly, did not — no royal tombs, no sacrificed attendants, no elite wealth in graves at all.
The Andes at Caral were the same: no weapons, no warfare evidence, no retainer sacrifice (Early Andean Ch 3).
Of the major Bronze Age cradles, it is the Indus and the Andes that are the outliers.
China, Mesopotamia, and Egypt line up.

The picture you should be holding in your head is this: fortified towns within sight of other fortified towns, built with enormous investments of labor, defended with dedication rituals that cost human lives.
Warfare is not occasional.
It is structural.
There are winners and losers.
Some towns grow.
Others get burned and abandoned.
The elites inside the big walls are accumulating more and more wealth, more and more political power, more and more sacred authority over ritual and calendar.
The ingredients of the first Chinese dynasty are being assembled, in place, in the Longshan horizon.
The Xia Dynasty, when it finally appears in Chinese tradition, comes out of this world.
So does the Shang.
So does everything that follows.
The legendary stories about this era are, of course, more colorful than the archaeology.

According to much later Chinese tradition, the Longshan horizon was dominated by a series of great rulers — the Five Emperors, a list of half-divine culture heroes who ruled in sequence during this era of walled towns and battles.
The most famous of them, by a long margin, is the Yellow Emperor.

The Yellow Emperor — Huangdi in Chinese (HWAHNG-dee), which literally means "Yellow Thearch" — is the ancestor whom modern Han Chinese, which is to say over a billion people, still trace their mythological descent from.
His traditional dates are about 2698 to 2598 BCE, placing him squarely in the middle of the Longshan horizon.

He is credited with a staggering list of inventions and achievements: unifying the tribes of the Yellow River plain, inventing the calendar, developing Chinese medicine, introducing silk cultivation (through his wife Leizu, Chapter 3), creating the first writing system (through his minister Cangjie), codifying music, establishing the ritual of government, and laying down the basic pattern of legitimate rule for all subsequent Chinese dynasties.
How much of this is real?
Almost certainly none of it in the literal sense — the Yellow Emperor is no more a historical figure than King Arthur or Romulus.
But the idea of him is historical in the sense that it tells us what classical Chinese tradition thought was happening in this era.

The tradition remembered the Longshan horizon as the moment when a scatter of competing chiefdoms were forged into a coherent cultural entity under a single paramount figure, through war, wisdom, and divine legitimacy.
The archaeology shows us the walls, the weapons, the craft specialization, and the emerging elites.
The tradition adds the myth that gives this transformation a name and a face.
The Yellow Emperor's signature exploit in the legends is the Battle of Zhuolu (JWOH-loo), in which he defeats a rival chief named Chi You (CHEE YOH).
