What matters most about the C-Group, for the long arc of this story, is that they stayed.
They dug in.

They maintained their distinctive pottery tradition, their distinctive burial tradition, and their distinctive cattle-centered identity for almost a thousand years, from 2400 BCE to 1550 BCE, through multiple waves of Egyptian contact, pressure, and outright occupation.
The C-Group is the reason we can say with confidence that Nubian cultural identity under Egyptian domination was not erased.
It was stubborn.
It survived, and it was visibly and deliberately different from the Egyptian alternative even when Egypt was literally in charge.
Whether the C-Group were the descendants of the A-Group, returning after four centuries in the desert or the south, is genuinely uncertain.
Some of the continuities — the fondness for the same stretch of river, some pottery decoration elements, the broad economic pattern — suggest they could have been.

Other differences — the new grave form, the new pottery tradition, the heavier cattle emphasis — could also be the result of fresh migration from the desert west of the Nile, from the depopulated Sahara as it was drying out during the third millennium BCE.

The current best-attested explanation is probably a mix: survivors of the old A-Group tradition merging with incoming pastoralist groups from the western desert, absorbed into a new synthesis along the river.
And while the C-Group was re-settling the north, a city was rising in the south.

chapters.title: Harkhuf's Donkeys
sentences: Around 2500 BCE, in the broad floodplain south of the Third Cataract, somebody started building a wall around a cluster of houses.
This was not the first wall any Nubian community had ever built, and we will never know the name of whoever organized the labor that built it.
But the wall was big, and it enclosed something that was starting to look much more like a town than a village, and the town was starting to look as if the people running it had ideas.
The town's modern name, taken from the small Sudanese village that sits next to the site today, is Kerma (KAIR-ma).
Kerma is one of the most important sites in African archaeology, and its story is going to take up the next three chapters of this book, because Kerma is not just a place.
It is a kingdom, a culture, and a civilizational experiment that ran parallel to pharaonic Egypt for a thousand years.
At its peak, the city of Kerma would hold more than ten thousand residents — small by the standards of Uruk or Memphis but by a long way the largest settlement in Africa outside the Egyptian delta.
It would build mud-brick temples bigger than anything outside Egypt.
It would bury its kings with corridors of sacrificed retainers numbering into the hundreds.
It would trade with half the ancient world.
And it would, for a long and anxious century, make Egyptian pharaohs seriously worried about what was upstream.
But at 2500 BCE, all of that is still ahead.
At 2500 BCE, Kerma is just a walled town with ambitions.
What exactly it was at the very beginning is hard to reconstruct, because the earliest layers at Kerma sit beneath three thousand years of later construction.

It is not one of those easy sites where the ancient layers have nothing on top of them except more ancient layers (what archaeologists call a tell — a mound-like site, common in the Middle East, where a town was abandoned and rebuilt on the same spot over and over until the whole location became a small hill of accumulated rubble).
Kerma was lived on continuously, so the oldest parts are buried under the youngest.

The best picture we have is that early Kerma was a regional center for a cluster of surrounding villages, growing grain and herding cattle, trading upriver and downriver, and gradually assembling the kind of surplus wealth that allows a town to build something that isn't a house.
The something-that-isn't-a-house is what makes Kerma famous.
It is a building the Nubians called a deffufa (dih-FOO-fah), a word we've borrowed directly from the local Sudanese Arabic term.
A deffufa is a massive free-standing mud-brick structure, solid or nearly solid at the base, with chambers and passages carved into the upper levels.

The best-known example is the Western Deffufa at Kerma, which sits at the heart of the ancient city and still rises more than 50 feet (about 18 meters) above the plain — a rectangular mud-brick mountain more than 170 feet (50 meters) long.
It is one of the oldest and largest mud-brick structures standing anywhere in Africa, and it is still there today, baking in the Sudanese sun, as it has been for more than four thousand years.
What was the deffufa for?
Almost certainly a temple of some kind, though the details are not fully recoverable.
There are staircases that lead up into the structure, chambers at the top that may have held ritual equipment, and offering spaces around the base.

The overall shape is unlike anything in contemporary Egyptian architecture — it is not a pyramid, not a mastaba (an Egyptian mastaba is the rectangular flat-roofed tomb of Old Kingdom nobles, shaped roughly like a long low brick oven, built over a burial shaft), and not a processional temple in the later Egyptian sense.
It is its own form.

The scale is what stops you: to build a solid mud-brick structure fifty feet high and fifty meters long, on the Nile in the twenty-fourth century BCE, you need a sustained labor force over years, you need somebody telling them what to build, and you need enough political stability that the builders don't abandon the project halfway through.
The Western Deffufa is, in other words, a monument that proves the existence of a state.
And if you are trying to build a theory of where African states come from, the Western Deffufa becomes important evidence.
At the moment it rose, around 2400 BCE, Kerma was doing something that almost nobody else on the continent was doing outside the Nile delta of Egypt.
To place that moment in the wider Bronze Age world: Mesopotamia was already deeply urban.

The Sumerian city of Uruk (in what is now southern Iraq, the first large-scale city in human history) had peaked centuries earlier, and Hierakonpolis in Upper Egypt had already grown to 5,000–10,000 people by ~3600 BCE (Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 3), and the Sumerian city-states of the Early Dynastic period were fighting each other over water rights in the wars whose royal cemeteries at Ur we will meet in the next chapter — by 2400 BCE Mesopotamia had two thousand years of urban history behind it (Mesopotamia Chs 3–4), and a king named Sargon of Akkad was only a couple of generations away from stitching it all into the world's first empire (Mesopotamia Ch 5).

Over in the Indus River valley, in what is now Pakistan, the planned city of Mohenjo-daro and its gridded contemporaries had already crossed the urban threshold around 2600 BCE and were in full flower when Kerma's first walls went up (Indus Valley Ch 4).
In northern China, a cluster of Longshan walled towns — including the stone-walled hill complex called Shimao — were coming into their own as fortified proto-state centers (Ancient China Ch 6).
Egypt was in the middle of the Old Kingdom, finishing the great pyramids at Giza.
The Americas were centuries away from anything you could call urban.
But sub-Saharan Africa?
The whole rest of the continent, as far as the current archaeology shows, was still a world of villages and pastoralist camps and seasonal settlements.

Kerma is genuinely the oldest big thing in the archaeological record of Africa south of Egypt — and it is also the last of the great Bronze Age cradles to cross the urban threshold, arriving about 1,400 years after Uruk, 700 years after the first predynastic Egyptian towns, and roughly 200 years after Mohenjo-daro.
That puts Kerma in the same rough generation as Longshan China — and, remarkably, as Caral on the coast of Peru (~2900 BCE), the first urban center in the Americas (Early Andean Ch 3).
Two first-on-their-continent urban civilizations, developing at roughly the same moment, both systematically under-covered in world history.
Archaeology south of the Sahara is genuinely thin, and future discoveries could shift the picture, but at the current state of evidence Kerma is the fifth cradle and the African one.
First in Africa, and early.
A thousand years before Great Zimbabwe.
Two and a half thousand years before the kingdoms of Mali and Ghana.
Three thousand years before the African urban traditions that modern Western readers are more likely to have heard of.
The Kerma kingdom is so early that most general-audience books about African history skip it entirely, because they start around 500 BCE and Kerma ended a thousand years before that.
We are going to give it the attention it deserves.
The Egyptians noticed.
Old Kingdom Egypt had been trading with Nubia for a long time already, but the Nubia it traded with in the Early Dynastic and early Old Kingdom had been a Nubia of villages and herders.
What started showing up in Egyptian records in the middle Old Kingdom was different.

There are increasingly frequent Egyptian references to the "ruler of Yam," which is new — a ruler, singular, suggesting some kind of centralized political authority south of the cataracts that the Egyptian scribes recognized as rulership in their own sense of the word.

There are larger and more elaborate Egyptian expeditions heading south to trade with this ruler, bringing back not just bulk goods like gold and ebony but also exotic specialty items: live leopards, hunting dogs, incense in sealed jars, and, in at least one famous case, a pygmy dancer.
The Egyptian texts that describe these expeditions are weirdly specific about logistics.
They don't read like triumph inscriptions; they read like business trip reports.

And one of them, carved onto the front of a tomb at Aswan, has survived in almost complete form, which means we actually have a first-person account of an Egyptian expedition into Upper Nubia during the Old Kingdom.
The account is one of the most important single documents in early Nubian history, and the expedition leader's name was Harkhuf.
Harkhuf (HAR-koof) was the governor of Elephantine (ell-ef-an-TEE-nay).
Elephantine is a small island in the Nile at Aswan, sitting right at the First Cataract.
It served throughout the Old Kingdom as Egypt's southern border post and its main jumping-off point for anything heading further upstream.
The island was the provincial capital of the first nome of Upper Egypt — Ta-Seti, the Land of the Bow, Nubia's Egyptian-side name (Chapter 1).
Governing Elephantine meant governing Egypt's Nubia desk: managing the trade, taxing the river traffic, organizing the expeditions, and leading them yourself when the stakes were high enough.
Harkhuf served two pharaohs, Merenre and then Pepi II, across the second half of the twenty-fourth century BCE, right at the tail end of the Old Kingdom.
During his career he led four separate trading expeditions from Elephantine south into Yam.
Each one was a donkey caravan — a long line of pack animals, with human drivers and armed escort, heading overland along the desert routes that bypassed the worst of the cataracts.
Boats were useless past the Second Cataract in most seasons.
Donkeys, not boats, were the real infrastructure of Old Kingdom trade with Upper Nubia.
Harkhuf's fourth and largest expedition reportedly brought back 300 donkey-loads of trade goods.

Imagine a single line of three hundred pack animals, each one carrying incense, ebony, ivory, leopard skins, throw-sticks, and assorted luxuries, stretching for a mile or more along a desert road north of Kerma, and you have an accurate picture of what Harkhuf's trade relationship with Yam actually looked like on the ground.
We know all this because Harkhuf commissioned a long autobiographical inscription on the front of his tomb at Qubbet el-Hawa, a hillside cemetery overlooking the Nile at Aswan.
The inscription tells, in his own voice, the story of his four expeditions, what he brought back, and how the trip went.
This was not standard practice — most Old Kingdom officials did not carve detailed business travelogues on their tomb walls.

Harkhuf's inscription is a first-person narrative, and because it is a first-person narrative about Nubian trade from the Nubian side of the cataracts, it is one of the richest sources in all of Egyptian literature for what the south was actually like.
Two things about the inscription are worth sitting with.
The first is that Harkhuf does not talk about Yam the way he would talk about a conquered province.
He talks about it the way a merchant talks about a business partner — describing the ruler, describing the quality of the goods, describing the precarious diplomacy required to get in and out safely.

At one point, Harkhuf's caravan encountered a military situation where two Nubian groups were at war with each other, and the "ruler of Yam" provided Harkhuf with an armed escort through the trouble zone.
This is not a pharaoh's narrative of domination.
This is a Nubian king sending soldiers to protect an Egyptian trade caravan as a favor, and getting paid for it.
The Egyptian trade relationship with Upper Nubia in the late Old Kingdom was not an occupation.
It was a negotiation, between parties who could stop each other from doing what they wanted to do.
The second thing worth sitting with is the pygmy.
Toward the end of Harkhuf's inscription, he describes how he had acquired, on his fourth expedition, a small dancing man whom he intended to bring back to Egypt.

The Egyptian word used in the text is deneg (DEN-eg), usually translated as "pygmy" or "dwarf." The person in question was almost certainly one of the Pygmy peoples of central Africa (the short-statured forest-dwelling groups, today called Mbuti, Twa, or Baka, living in the Congo basin rainforests far to the south of Nubia), not a dwarf in the modern medical sense.

Harkhuf had acquired this dancer from a more distant southern land, through whatever trade chain reached from Kerma to the equatorial forests, and the idea was that the dancer would perform the "dance of the god" at the royal court.

The dance of the god was a specific Egyptian religious performance involving a small dancer and a ritual impersonation of the sun god — one of the liturgies performed around the pharaoh himself on high festival days — and it worked best when the dancer was physically small enough to fit the ritual role.

Live human rarities like this were part of the broader Bronze Age gift-diplomacy economy, the same economy that shipped carnelian beads from Harappa to Sumerian Ur and lapis lazuli from Afghanistan to the pharaoh's jewelers.

The court Harkhuf was sending the dancer to was at Memphis, the Old Kingdom capital. (Memphis sat at the point where the Nile delta opens up, just south of modern Cairo — the natural choke point controlling traffic between the delta and Upper Egypt.
It was Egypt's capital from the First Dynasty almost continuously through the Old Kingdom.

Throughout this book, when you see "Memphis" with no other qualifier, think "Old Kingdom capital, northern Egypt, near modern Cairo.") When the caravan was still traveling back through Nubia, Harkhuf sent a courier ahead to inform the pharaoh of what was coming.
Pepi II, the pharaoh in question, was eight years old.
A note about Pepi II's age is worth pausing on, because the man is an oddity of Egyptian history.
The traditional Egyptian king lists give Pepi II something close to a ninety-year reign, which would make him the longest-reigning monarch in recorded human history if taken literally.

Most modern Egyptologists think the ninety-year figure is an exaggeration in the surviving sources but that Pepi II did genuinely rule for many decades — a long reign that saw the slow unraveling of the Old Kingdom's central authority under a pharaoh who was steadily aging out of active governance.
When Harkhuf's courier reached him with news of the dancer, though, all of that was decades in the future.
He was a little boy on a big throne.
The child pharaoh wrote back.
The letter survives, copied in full onto Harkhuf's tomb wall, because Harkhuf thought it was the greatest thing that had ever happened to him.

Pepi II's reply ordered Harkhuf to guard the dwarf night and day, to ensure he did not fall off the boat on the way down the river, to protect him against all possible accidents, and to deliver him alive and well to the royal court, at which point Harkhuf would be rewarded more richly than any previous expedition leader.
The tone is unmistakable.

It is the tone of an eight-year-old who has just found out he is about to receive the coolest birthday present of his life, writing in the diplomatic register because the eight-year-old happens to be pharaoh.

Harkhuf was so proud of the letter that he copied its entire text onto his tomb — not summarized, not paraphrased, but word for word, including the royal name and titles and the breathless instructions about the dwarf.

Four and a half thousand years later, the letter is still there, carved in hieroglyphs on the Qubbet el-Hawa cliffside, and it is one of the oldest surviving pieces of personal correspondence from any ancient civilization.
For the purposes of this book, Harkhuf's inscription is valuable for three reasons.

It confirms that Upper Nubia in the twenty-fourth century BCE had a centralized political authority that the Egyptians recognized as a "ruler." It confirms that the relationship across the cataracts was one of trade and negotiation, not domination.

And it confirms, through the specific goods on the donkey caravan — the incense, the ivory, the leopard skins, the dancer — that Kerma and its neighbors were linked into a trade network that reached south and west into parts of Africa the Egyptians themselves had never seen.
Kerma was already, in the late Old Kingdom, functioning as a middleman between sub-Saharan Africa and the Mediterranean world.
The deneg dancer had not been born in Kerma.
He had traveled to Kerma from somewhere further south, where somebody else had been trading with somebody else, down chains of connection we cannot fully reconstruct.
And then, in the middle of the twenty-second century BCE, the Old Kingdom collapsed.
The collapse is a long, messy historiographical problem that belongs properly to an Egyptian history, not a Nubian one, so the short version will do.

Pepi II's enormous reign (whatever its real length) ended with a weakened central government, a string of inadequate successors, breakdowns in provincial authority, a series of low Nile floods that produced bad harvests, and eventually a full fragmentation of Egypt into competing regional powers.
Historians call the period that followed the First Intermediate Period — a polite way of saying "Egypt fell apart for about 150 years." The end of the Old Kingdom came somewhere around 2180 BCE.

Egypt would not be reunified until 2055 BCE, when a dynasty based at Thebes in southern Egypt (the city near modern Luxor we met in the kingdom-framework note in Chapter 2) succeeded in pulling the country back together and starting the Middle Kingdom.
During that century and a half, Egypt's grip on Nubia loosened.
The raiding stopped, because Egypt was busy raiding itself.
Egyptian expeditions south became infrequent or ceased.

The "ruler of Yam" probably experienced this as a gift: a prolonged vacation from the most demanding trading partner in the ancient world, and an opportunity to consolidate power along the Nile without interference.
Archaeologically, Kerma during the First Intermediate Period shows continuing growth.
The city expanded.
More houses were built.
The Western Deffufa continued in use and was gradually enlarged.
New structures appeared in the surrounding cemetery area.
Whatever Kerma's political development looked like, it did not slow down when Egypt did.
If anything, Egypt's absence gave Kerma room to grow into the kind of kingdom that would, a few centuries later, start making Egyptian pharaohs genuinely anxious.
When Middle Kingdom Egypt finally pulled itself back together and looked south again, the thing it saw upstream was not the loose network of villages and traders that the Old Kingdom had known.
It was a real kingdom, with a real city, real kings, and real armies.
And the Middle Kingdom's response to that kingdom, starting in the twentieth century BCE, was going to be one of the most thorough fortification programs in the history of the ancient world.

chapters.title: Forts Along the Cataracts
sentences: The Middle Kingdom pharaohs did not like what they saw when they looked south.

The city of Kerma had grown during Egypt's long interregnum — more houses, more walls, more deffufas rising above the skyline, and a visibly consolidated royal authority running the place from the middle of it all.
The C-Group villagers of Lower Nubia had continued their stubborn cattle-and-pottery lives under only minimal Egyptian supervision.

The gold mines of the eastern desert had been worked only sporadically in the century and a half of Egyptian disorder, which meant gold production was low and the routes to the mines were no longer secure.
From the perspective of a newly reunified Egyptian state starting over in the twenty-first century BCE, the Nile south of Aswan was a problem that had gotten worse while Egypt wasn't looking.
The Middle Kingdom's answer was to fortify the border.
Not fortify in the loose, symbolic sense that most ancient frontiers got.

Fortify in the sense of building, over several generations, a chain of roughly a dozen genuine military fortresses along a 250-mile (400 km) stretch of the Nile between the First Cataract and the Second, each one designed to house a garrison, project force up and down the river, and lock down traffic past the cataracts. (Egyptologists count the forts differently depending on whether subsidiary installations and forward watchposts are lumped in with the main strongholds — the main chain is usually given as eleven to thirteen forts, with more small outworks beyond that.) This was one of the most intensive frontier fortification programs anywhere in the ancient Bronze Age world.
The Middle Kingdom pharaohs were really, genuinely, deeply worried about Kerma.
The closest contemporary parallel anywhere on earth is in fact Chinese.

At almost exactly the same moment, in northern China along the Yellow River, the Longshan-era walled towns were being built using a rammed-earth technique called hangtu (HONG-too — a construction method where workers pounded layer after layer of damp earth between wooden forms until the wall became hard enough to resist battering, the same basic technology that would eventually build the earliest Great Wall), and the walled site of Shimao was throwing up stone-walled terraces in its own defensive posture against whatever was threatening it (Ancient China Ch 6).
The early second millennium BCE was a period in which multiple Bronze Age states, independently, were discovering how much masonry a serious frontier cost.
Egypt's twelve-fort chain and China's hangtu walls were two expressions of the same problem.
The reader should take a moment to appreciate how unusual this is.
Ancient superpowers did not usually build forts against neighbors they considered unimportant.
Egypt did not build forts against the Libyans in the western desert on anything close to this scale, and the Libyans were a recurring military nuisance.

The Middle Kingdom forts on the Nubian border are, by themselves, sufficient evidence that Middle Kingdom Egypt considered Kerma a peer competitor — not a nuisance to be raided, not a source of gold to be extracted, but a rival state powerful enough to require permanent defensive architecture.
No other ancient Egyptian frontier ever received this kind of investment.

The prime mover of the program was Senusret III (SEN-oos-ret), a pharaoh of the Twelfth Dynasty who reigned around 1860 BCE and who is remembered in Egyptian sources as one of the most aggressive, effective, and personally terrifying kings the Middle Kingdom ever produced.

Senusret III is the king who finally finished what his father and grandfather had started, extending the Nubian fortress chain all the way to the Second Cataract and making Egypt's grip on Lower Nubia something Kerma had to take seriously.

He is also the king whose statues, preserved in museums from Cairo to New York, show him with a deeply lined, grim, almost exhausted face — one of the earliest ancient portraits that looks like a specific individual human being rather than an idealized royal mask.

Whether the lines on his face were an accurate likeness or a propaganda statement ("your king bears burdens so that you don't have to"), the overall effect is of a man with work to do, and the work involved Nubia.
The forts were serious pieces of engineering.

A typical Middle Kingdom Nubian fort was rectangular, built on a high defensible site along the river, surrounded by a dry moat (sometimes two concentric moats), with walls of mud brick laid on stone foundations, punctuated by regularly spaced towers projecting outward.
The walls were thick enough to withstand battering rams and tall enough that an assault with siege ladders would be dangerous for the attackers.
The gates were protected by angled entryways that forced attackers to turn a corner with their shield side exposed to defenders.
There were internal barracks, granaries, bakeries, wells, administrative buildings, a small temple, and housing for the garrison's officers.
Some forts included dedicated areas for the garrison's families, meaning the soldiers were not just rotating through but actually living there with wives and children.
The two largest forts in the chain were Buhen (BOO-hen) and Mirgissa (mir-GIS-sah).
Both sat at the Second Cataract, the chokepoint where the river is most dangerous for boats and the land route is most predictable for caravans.

Buhen is the more famous, partly because it was more thoroughly excavated before it was drowned under Lake Nasser in the 1960s and partly because its reconstructed plans are some of the most impressive ancient military architecture drawings in any textbook.
Buhen had two concentric defensive walls, a ditch, a bastioned gatehouse, loopholes for archers, and an estimated capacity for a garrison of several hundred soldiers with supporting civilian staff.
It was a castle by any reasonable definition of the word — a thousand years before the concept of a castle is supposed to exist anywhere else.
Mirgissa was arguably even more ambitious.
In addition to its main fortress, its subsidiary outworks, and its garrison facilities, Mirgissa had something that no other Middle Kingdom fort had: a slipway.
A slipway is a long, prepared track of wood and mud plaster, lubricated with river mud, along which boats could be physically dragged overland.

The Mirgissa slipway bypassed the worst of the Second Cataract rapids by hauling river boats out of the water, sliding them along a lubricated track for more than a quarter-mile (roughly 400 meters) of parallel ground, and refloating them upstream of the whitewater.
This was engineering in service of a specific strategic goal.

The cataracts, as we have been saying since Chapter 1, are the reason Egypt and Nubia ended up as two separate countries — because boats could not cross them and a river civilization without river logistics couldn't project sustained force.
The Mirgissa slipway was Egypt's answer to that problem.
With a slipway, Egyptian river forces could get past the Second Cataract and operate upstream.
Without one, they couldn't.

The fact that the Middle Kingdom invested in building and maintaining a multi-hundred-meter wooden track for hauling boats overland tells you, more clearly than almost any other single piece of evidence, how badly they wanted to be able to reach Kerma.
And then there is the Semna stele.

Semna (SEM-nah) was one of the southernmost fortresses in the chain, sitting right at the Second Cataract itself, facing south into the territory that had historically belonged to Kerma and its neighbors.
At Semna, Senusret III erected a stone stele — a carved standing stone, the standard Egyptian medium for public inscriptions — that contained what is essentially the text of a border-closure decree.
The standard English translation of the Semna boundary stele (Miriam Lichtheim's reading in Ancient Egyptian Literature Vol.

I) runs, in rough paraphrase: the king has made this southern boundary in his year 8, and no Nubian is allowed to pass it, either by land or by river, with a ship or with herds of cattle, except for a Nubian who has come to do authorized trade at a named border post, or on a diplomatic errand, or for some other approved matter.
Any Nubian ship attempting to head north past this point is forbidden forever.
In plain terms: no Nubian crosses the line without Egyptian permission, and the river traffic south of it belongs to Egypt.
A second Semna stele, erected a few years later by the same king, adds a personal flourish that has haunted Egyptological writing ever since.

The stele addresses Senusret III's future successors and warns them: any son of his who maintains this boundary is his true son, and any son who abandons it and fails to fight for it is not his son and was not born to him.
The pharaoh, apparently anxious about what future kings might do under pressure, tried to bind his successors with a curse — using the royal filiation itself as the test of legitimacy.
This is one of the oldest surviving explicit border-closure decrees from any ancient civilization.

It is not just a military order — it is a political doctrine, written in stone, that says "Egypt ends here, Nubia begins here, nobody crosses without our permission, and we care enough about this line to threaten any future king who gives it up with posthumous disinheritance." That kind of formal border thinking is extraordinarily early.
It predates the Roman frontier concept by nearly two thousand years.
The Middle Kingdom pharaohs, confronting Kerma, had arrived at something that looks a lot like a modern nation-state's border — and then spent two centuries defending it.
What were they defending against?
Not a raiding party.
Not a population of nomads.
Raiding parties would not have justified thirteen permanent masonry forts and a slipway for getting boats past the cataract.

What the Middle Kingdom was defending against was a rival state — an organized, centralized, capable, and growing kingdom based upstream at Kerma, which had spent the First Intermediate Period and the early Middle Kingdom quietly getting more dangerous.
The Semna stele is not the grandiose propaganda of a secure empire.
It is the deliberately stern posture of a kingdom that has decided the status quo is barely tolerable and needs to be locked down hard before it deteriorates further.
The forts worked, for a while.
From around 1900 BCE to about 1700 BCE, the Second Cataract held.
Egyptian administration extended into Lower Nubia as far south as the Semna line.
Gold mining resumed at industrial scale.
The C-Group people of Lower Nubia lived under an Egyptian administrative overlay that extracted taxes, labor, and soldiers, while leaving the basics of their village life largely intact.
Kerma was contained, and the Middle Kingdom's southern frontier was as stable as any ancient frontier ever got.
And Nubians worked for Egypt in large numbers throughout this period.
Not as reluctant subjects but, often, as paid professionals.
This is one of the features of ancient Nubia that surprises modern readers most, because it runs so strongly against the monolithic "Egypt conquered Nubia" story.
Nubian mercenaries show up throughout Middle Kingdom Egyptian military records — especially archers, but also scouts, skirmishers, and desert-knowledgeable guides.

Some of them were drawn from a group the Egyptians called the Medjay (MED-jai), a desert-dwelling Nubian people whose name the Egyptians eventually turned into a common noun meaning "police" or "desert patrol." By the New Kingdom, a Medjay was not an ethnic term at all — it was a job description.
A Medjay was a scout, a lawman, a desert guard, and often a member of an internal security force within Egypt itself, regardless of where he had actually been born.

When you read about "Medjay" in later Egyptian sources, what you are reading is the linguistic fossil of a specific Nubian people so reliably good at their job that their name became the Egyptian word for the job.

Another group, the Pan-Grave Culture (so named by archaeologists for their shallow circular grave pits, which look like cooking pans when excavated), were Nubian pastoralists from the Eastern Desert who also served in the Egyptian military during the late Middle Kingdom and Second Intermediate Period.

The Pan-Grave burials contain distinctive pottery and ornaments, and enough of them have been found inside Egyptian territory to show that Pan-Grave warriors and their families were settling in Egypt itself, forming communities of Nubian-origin soldiers in the Egyptian countryside.
This was not a persecuted minority hiding from the authorities.
This was a paid warrior class that the Egyptian state was actively recruiting from, integrating into Egyptian life, and relying on for security.
Egypt's relationship with Nubia during the Middle Kingdom was, in other words, more complicated than any single word captures.
Egypt fortified the border.
Egypt issued a decree forbidding Nubian river traffic.
Egypt also hired Nubian soldiers, welcomed Nubian mercenary communities, and built its internal security around Nubian-named forces.
The border was not a wall against an enemy so much as a managed interface between two peer populations, with Egypt trying to control which Nubians got in and on what terms.
Through all of this, Kerma itself kept growing.
The Middle Kingdom's forts could contain Kerma at the Second Cataract, but they could not reach it.

An Egyptian army trying to cross the Second Cataract with intent to march on Kerma faced several hundred miles of unfriendly country, a difficult river, and an enemy whose capital was defended by Kerma's own population of several thousand adult men, most of them archers.
A quick strike was impossible.
A sustained campaign required logistical support that the Middle Kingdom could only generate at enormous cost.

So Kerma was held in a kind of stalemate during the twentieth, nineteenth, and eighteenth centuries BCE — contained at the Second Cataract but unreachable beyond it, trading with Egypt through authorized channels but running its own affairs in its own territory without interference.
This was Kerma's golden age.

Free from the threat of direct Egyptian intervention, and protected by its distance from the Egyptian supply base, the kingdom used the two centuries of containment to build out the most impressive urban and political structure in Africa outside Egypt.

By around 1750 BCE, Kerma had a population of more than 10,000, a complex of temples and royal buildings on a scale unmatched anywhere else on the continent outside the Nile Delta, a distinctive high-fired pottery tradition that was being exported as far north as Thebes, and a royal cemetery whose largest tombs contained the retinues of dead kings buried alongside them.
This is the period archaeologists call Classic Kerma, and it is the subject of our next chapter.
But before we leave the forts, one more detail.
The Middle Kingdom border held until about 1700 BCE, when the whole political situation in Egypt started coming unglued for reasons that had nothing to do with Nubia.
A group of foreign rulers known as the Hyksos (HICK-sos) were taking over northern Egypt from a power base in the Nile Delta.

The Hyksos were migrant communities from the Levant (the coastal strip east of the Mediterranean, roughly modern Lebanon, Israel, and western Syria), speakers of a Semitic language related to later Hebrew and Aramaic rather than to Egyptian, who had been drifting into the delta for generations before they took it over.
We will meet them properly in Chapter 6.

For now the only thing the reader needs to know is that they existed, they were in the north, and their presence meant the Theban pharaohs of southern Egypt were about to be squeezed between two enemies: Hyksos to the north, Kerma to the south.
At that point, Kerma stopped being a contained rival and became, briefly and dangerously, an active military threat.
The forts were still standing, but the state behind them was distracted, and Kerma was about to take advantage.

chapters.title: Classic Kerma
sentences: To understand what Kerma was by 1700 BCE, you have to walk its cemetery.
The city itself, with its towering Western Deffufa and its crowded residential quarters and its administrative buildings, is easy enough to picture from the archaeology.
The excavation teams that have worked at Kerma since the early 1900s have mapped the footprint of what is clearly a real city — streets, houses, workshops, storage areas, temples.
At its peak, Classic Kerma held somewhere over 10,000 people inside its walls.
That is not Uruk or Memphis or Mohenjo-Daro.
It is not in the same size class as the biggest Bronze Age cities.

But it is, by a long way, the biggest city in Africa outside Egypt in its era, and it is a real urban environment — not a large village, not a ceremonial center with a few attached houses, but a working city that had to feed itself, police itself, organize its labor, and project political authority over a large hinterland.
But the city is not what takes your breath away.
The cemetery is.

A few kilometers east of the city proper, on the edge of the floodplain, the kings of Kerma were buried in enormous circular tumuli (TOO-myoo-lye — the Latin-derived plural of tumulus, meaning a burial mound).

A tumulus is a mound of earth piled over a grave, and in most cultures that use them — from Neolithic Europe to Iron Age Scythia to the early Koreans — they run from a few meters to a few tens of meters in diameter.
The Kerma royal tumuli are bigger.
The largest of them are nearly the size of a football field across.
Their surfaces were originally covered in black and white pebbles arranged in patterns, so that from a distance the cemetery would have looked like a landscape of giant decorated discs.
The entire royal necropolis of Kerma stretches for more than a mile along the eastern edge of the site.
Thousands of graves, ranging from simple individual burials to the monumental royal tumuli, cluster along the cemetery avenue in an arrangement that was clearly planned and controlled.
Inside the big tumuli, the archaeology gets unsettling.

When the Harvard archaeologist George Reisner excavated the royal tumuli of Kerma in the early 1920s, he was looking for what you would expect from a royal burial ground: the main tomb chamber containing the king, with a selection of grave goods around him, probably including some evidence of sacrificed animals.
What he found was the king, the grave goods, and several hundred additional human bodies.
The bodies were arranged in long corridors radiating out from or alongside the central burial chamber.
They were not randomly distributed; they were laid out in what looked like deliberate rows.
They included men, women, and children, of varying ages and body types, in clothing that ranged from simple to elaborate.
They were not beaten or mutilated.

Several of them appeared to have been placed alive — their positions suggesting that the bodies had composed themselves into sleeping postures at the moment of death, rather than having been dropped in after being killed elsewhere.

The interpretation that Reisner reached, and that has held up in its broad strokes despite a century of argument about the specifics, is that the dead in the corridors were retainers — members of the king's household and court — who were killed (or who took their own lives) at the king's funeral in order to accompany him into the afterlife.
In the largest Kerma royal tumuli, the retainer count runs into the hundreds.

Reisner's own reports on the biggest tumuli gave figures of three to four hundred additional bodies per tomb, and while later re-analyses have quibbled with exact numbers, the broad magnitude is undisputed: we are talking about hundreds of people sacrificed at a single king's burial.
This is a genuinely shocking scale, and it is worth walking through the comparative record carefully, because the numbers put Kerma in an unusual position among bronze Age royal burial traditions.
The major Bronze Age cradles that left substantial royal cemeteries all handled the question of retainer sacrifice differently, and the comparison is illuminating.

Egypt predates them all: King Djer's First Dynasty tomb at Abydos (~3000 BCE) contained 338 subsidiary burials — the earliest large-scale retainer sacrifice in any of the Bronze Age cradles (Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 6).
The practice lasted only a few generations before the Egyptians abandoned it, but at its peak the numbers dwarf even Kerma.

Mesopotamia had the famous Royal Tombs of Ur, excavated by Leonard Woolley in the 1920s, where the largest royal grave (Queen Puabi's, around 2600 BCE) contained 74 sacrificed attendants lined up in the death pit (Mesopotamia Ch 4).
That is the upper end for Mesopotamian royal sacrifice — an order of magnitude fewer than Kerma's corridors.

Northern China has the late Longshan site of Shimao (roughly 2000 BCE), where the builders walled up decapitated young women's heads in foundation deposits, and the early Shang royal tombs at Yinxu where retainer sacrifice in the range of dozens per tomb was standard (Ancient China Ch 6).
Again, real and grim, but not at Kerma's numerical scale.
The Indus Valley has nothing comparable at all.

The Harappan cities produced almost no royal burials of any kind — no kings in gold masks, no deep shaft tombs, no retainer pits, no conspicuous accumulation of wealth in graves whatsoever (Indus Valley Ch 8).
If the Harappans had royal retinues, they did not kill them at their masters' funerals.
The Andes join the Indus as a non-participant — Caral (~2600 BCE) shows no retainer sacrifice, no weapons, no hierarchical burial at all (Early Andean Ch 3).

Interestingly, much later Andean cultures (the Moche, the Inca) would practice elaborate sacrifice, making the Andean trajectory the inverse of most Old World patterns: starting peaceful and developing sacrifice later.
The Indus and the Andes are the outliers, and they are outliers in a direction no one would have predicted: toward egalitarian burial custom in some of the world's first urban civilizations.
Kerma is the high end.
Hundreds of retainers in the largest tumuli, an order of magnitude larger than Ur's death pits, maintained across multiple royal generations as a routine part of how a Kerma king was buried.
No other Bronze Age burial tradition currently known produced corridors of that density around a single central chamber.
What does this tell us about Kerma?
Two things, mostly.
First, the Kerma state had the kind of absolute royal authority that could mobilize this level of ritual sacrifice.
Hundreds of people do not volunteer to die together on any ordinary day.
The power structure at Kerma was willing to spend a catastrophic amount of human capital on a single ceremony, and it had the political machinery to carry the ceremony out.
This is not a tribal chiefdom.
This is a state with teeth.
Second, and this is harder to reconstruct, the Kerma religious system placed extraordinary weight on the afterlife of the king.
The sacrificed retainers were not generic victims; they were the king's people, going with him into whatever continuation of existence the Kerma cosmology envisioned.

The practice presupposes a worldview in which the continuity of the royal household mattered across the death barrier, and in which belonging to that household conferred not just obligations in life but obligations after death.
Whatever the specific beliefs were — and without texts, we can't recover them in detail — they were powerful enough to sustain mass retainer burial as a repeated royal practice for generations.
We do not have to romanticize this.
Retainer sacrifice is, from any modern moral perspective, an atrocity.
But we also do not have to pretend it is uniquely Nubian barbarism, because it isn't.

The technical contribution of the Kerma evidence to world archaeology is that it shows this kind of mass royal burial running parallel to, and at a much larger scale than, the Sumerian or early Chinese examples.
Kerma was not a backward outlier.

It was the extreme case on a spectrum of Bronze Age royal practices that ran from Egypt's First Dynasty through Ur through Shimao to Kerma, all converging on the basic idea that kings should be accompanied into death by the people who served them in life.
The Harappans and the early Andeans decided not to play that game at all — and the fact that they are the outliers is one of the most interesting data points in Bronze Age cultural history.
There was a second monumental building at Kerma, beyond the famous Western Deffufa in the city proper.
The Eastern Deffufa was a separate mud-brick complex sitting out in the royal cemetery, a few kilometers from the city, and functioning as the mortuary temple associated with the royal burials.

The Eastern Deffufa is not as tall as its western cousin, but it is still a substantial construction — walled enclosures, chambers, an altar space, and a clear ritual orientation toward the cemetery itself.

At its peak, it would have been the site of the funerary processions that accompanied a royal burial out from the city to the cemetery, through the approach corridors, and into the final sealing of a royal tumulus.

Taken together — the city, the Western Deffufa, the cemetery, the Eastern Deffufa, the retainer burials, the imported luxury goods, the population over ten thousand, the defensive walls — Classic Kerma is an urban and political package that is unambiguously a state, and unambiguously a state with its own character.
It is not a miniature Egypt.
It borrows from Egypt (in architectural techniques, in some pottery forms, in trade patterns) but it does not imitate Egypt.
The royal cemetery form is Kerman, not Egyptian.
The deffufa as an architectural type is Kerman, not Egyptian.
The emphasis on cattle as grave goods is Kerman, not Egyptian.
The retainer sacrifice tradition is Kerman, and at a scale not seen in Egypt at all.
Classic Kerma is a genuine African urban civilization with its own structural logic, running parallel to pharaonic Egypt but on its own terms.

If the cemetery is what Kerma is remembered for, the tulip beaker is what Kerma was known for at the time — by Egyptian merchants, by Egyptian royal courts, by anybody in the Bronze Age Mediterranean who cared about having beautiful things on a table.
A Kerma fine ware tulip beaker is a hand-built ceramic cup, tulip-shaped, with walls so thin and so evenly potted that they look machine-made to a modern eye.
The walls are almost eggshell thin, a continuation of the A-Group craft tradition from Chapter 1 carried to a much higher level of technical refinement.
The color scheme is dramatic: a polished black rim band on top of a brilliant red lower body, with a thin gray zone separating the two.

Achieving that effect required controlling the firing atmosphere precisely, letting oxygen in during part of the firing and cutting it off during the rest, so that the same clay body produced two different colors on different parts of the same vessel.
Egyptian wheel-thrown pottery of the same era is thicker, coarser, and monochrome.
The Kerma fine ware is more technically impressive than anything Egypt was producing in pottery terms, and the Egyptians knew it.

Tulip beakers have been excavated from elite Egyptian contexts as far north as Thebes, which means Kerma was exporting its best craft products into the Egyptian luxury market as trade goods alongside the more predictable gold and ivory.
For at least one product category, the direction of luxury trade ran north from Nubia to Egypt, not the other way.
Tulip ware is the best single window into the Kerma economy because it is the product that Kerma's competitors acknowledged by paying for it.

Kerma was also producing bronze daggers and razors (borrowing and locally modifying Egyptian metalworking techniques), linen and leather and dyed textiles, and working volumes of ivory and gold that flowed through the city's workshops on their way to richer markets upstream and downstream.
But the beakers are the part that survived intact, and the beakers are the part that tells you what Kerma thought of itself: a place whose artisans made things other people wanted to buy.
Classic Kerma had, in other words, a manufacturing economy.
It wasn't just a gold-and-ivory pipeline.

It was a city whose artisans produced finished goods that Egyptian elites considered worth buying, and whose kings used trade surplus to fund the construction of deffufas, the operation of the royal cemetery, and the upkeep of a military and political apparatus large enough to make the Middle Kingdom pharaohs nervous.
One question that bothers every serious reader of Kerma history: why no writing?
Kerma had all the ingredients that other ancient civilizations used to develop writing.
It had long-distance trade that needed accounting.
It had a centralized state that needed record-keeping.
It had elite classes who had contact with Egyptian scribes and Egyptian documents.
It had religious institutions that could have underwritten a priestly scribal tradition.
It had the wealth to train and support specialists.
And yet, as far as the archaeological record currently shows, Kerma produced no writing system of its own.

The earliest surviving Nubian script — a set of characters called Meroitic (meh-ROW-it-ik), developed by the later Kingdom of Kush — does not appear until more than a thousand years after Classic Kerma ended.
There are several possible answers and no consensus.
The closest analogue in the Bronze Age is the Indus Valley Civilization (Indus Valley Ch 6).

The Harappans of Mohenjo-daro and Harappa ran a huge, sophisticated, urban society for six or seven hundred years and left behind a corpus of short inscriptions on small stone seals — the Indus script — which no one has ever successfully deciphered and which almost nobody thinks is a true writing system in the full sense of encoding a spoken language phonetically.
The Indus script and Kerma's silence are the two great "missing voices" of the Bronze Age, and they are different kinds of missing.
The Harappans had something that looks like writing and we can't read it; the Nubians appear to have had nothing that looks like writing at all.

But both cases force the same kind of question on the archaeologist: how does a state that complex govern itself without the thing the other great ancient states treated as a fundamental tool of governance?
Three candidate answers for Kerma.
First: Kerma may have had a writing system on perishable materials — papyrus, leather, wood, wax tablets — that simply didn't survive the way Egyptian papyrus survived in dry desert tombs.
If Kerma's administrative records were written in a local script on papyrus and kept in palaces rather than tombs, they could be gone without a trace.
Second: Kerma may have used Egyptian scribes and the Egyptian language for the small amount of writing it needed, rather than developing its own script.

There is some evidence for this — a few Egyptian documents found at Kerma, a few administrative seals with Egyptian markings — suggesting that Kerma elites outsourced their formal writing needs to imported or Egyptian-trained scribes.
This would be a case of a culture reaching the point where writing would be useful and solving the problem through borrowing instead of invention.
Third, and this is the one that makes some Nubianists uncomfortable: Kerma may really have run a complex urban state for a thousand years without writing.
Writing is not universally necessary to statehood.

The Incas of Peru — a fifteenth-century-CE Andean empire that ruled some twelve million people from its capital at Cuzco using a road network as long as the Roman empire's — ran that whole system using knotted cords called quipu (KEE-poo), in which information was encoded in the pattern, length, color, and knot position of dangling strings rather than in any phonetic script.

The quipu tradition is not a late Inca invention — the earliest known quipu fragments come from Caral (~2600 BCE), making this the world's longest-running non-textual information technology at roughly 4,000 years (Early Andean Ch 3).
Whether the quipu counts as "writing" in the strict sense is still debated; what is not in doubt is that the Incas did not need alphabetic writing to run their empire.

Kerma might have been an earlier version of the same phenomenon — a state that compensated for the absence of writing with oral traditions, memory specialists, physical tokens, and administrative systems we have not yet recognized in the archaeological record.
The honest answer is that we don't know which of these is right, and it is probably some mix of all three.
The absence of Kerma writing is a frustrating gap in what we can reconstruct about the kingdom, because writing is the one thing that lets ancient people speak to us in their own voice.
Without it, Kerma is silent.
Every word in this chapter about what Kerma was like has been reconstructed from material remains and Egyptian outside observation.

What Kerma kings thought of themselves, what their theology looked like in detail, what name they used for their own country — all of this is gone, probably permanently, because the people who could have told us did not leave writing we can read.
There is one more feature of Classic Kerma worth mentioning before we move into its final crisis.
Kerma had diplomatic and military relations that stretched far beyond the Egyptian frontier.

Scattered finds from the Classic Kerma period show Kerma-style objects as far north as the eastern Mediterranean coast, including in Hyksos-controlled northern Egypt, and Kerma pottery in contexts that hint at regional trade networks reaching into what is now Ethiopia and the Red Sea coast.
Kerma was not an isolated upriver kingdom.

It was plugged into the wider Bronze Age world — aware of the Hyksos in Lower Egypt, connected to the incense trade from the Horn of Africa, sending craft products to trading partners who were themselves linked to the broader ancient network that ran from Mesopotamia through the Levant and into Egypt.

And by around 1650 BCE, Kerma's diplomatic reach into the Hyksos-controlled north was about to produce a genuine two-front crisis for the Theban pharaohs who were trying to re-unify Egypt and rebuild the Middle Kingdom's collapse.
The Theban pharaohs did not like being squeezed between Hyksos to the north and Kerma to the south.
The Theban pharaohs were going to do something about it.
Their response would end the Kerma kingdom.

chapters.title: The Squeeze and the Fall
sentences: In the seventeenth century BCE, the political map of the Nile valley started to break.
The Middle Kingdom that had built the forts had unraveled.
Another interregnum, which historians call the Second Intermediate Period, was underway.
The northern delta of Egypt had fallen under the control of the Hyksos — those "rulers of foreign lands" we briefly met at the end of Chapter 4.
A fuller introduction is overdue, since the Hyksos are about to play a central role for one chapter.
The Hyksos were speakers of a Semitic language, not Egyptian.
That matters, and it matters in a way that's worth unpacking.

Egyptian belongs to a language family called Afro-Asiatic, but its closest relatives inside that family are North African languages — Berber, Cushitic tongues from the Horn of Africa — not the languages the Hyksos spoke.

The Hyksos spoke something ancestral to, or closely related to, the Semitic languages we know best: Akkadian (the ancient Mesopotamian tongue of Hammurabi and the Assyrian kings — Mesopotamia Ch 5 onward), Hebrew, Aramaic, Arabic.
The gulf between Egyptian and Hyksos is about the same as the gulf between a Western European language and a Middle Eastern one.
Modern English speakers trying to learn Arabic get a rough sense of the same linguistic distance.

The Hyksos were genuinely foreign in the strong sense of the word — originally migrants from the Levant (the coastal strip east of the Mediterranean, modern Lebanon, Israel, and western Syria) who had come into the Nile delta over several generations as traders, settlers, and mercenaries before any of them were rulers.
By around 1650 BCE, they weren't just settlers anymore.
They had established a dynasty that controlled Lower Egypt and called itself Pharaoh.

They had been in Egypt long enough to be culturally Egyptianized in the public-facing parts of their rule — they adopted pharaonic titles, royal iconography, Egyptian religious forms — but their linguistic and ethnic origins remained visibly Levantine, and the Theban Egyptians to the south considered them an illegitimate foreign occupation of the sacred throne of Upper and Lower Egypt.
The Hyksos controlled the north.
The Theban pharaohs of the Seventeenth Dynasty controlled the south, specifically the stretch of Upper Egypt from around Thebes (modern Luxor) down to the First Cataract at Aswan.

Between these two Egyptian powers ran a long, unhappy border through the middle of the country — skirmishes and raids and intermittent diplomatic exchanges but no decisive conflict for several generations.
It was a stalemate.
And south of Thebes, past the First Cataract, the old Middle Kingdom fort chain was still standing but no longer properly garrisoned.
Kerma, sensing the weakness, had moved.

The best evidence for what happened during the Kerma–Hyksos coalition comes not from any Kerma source (because we have no Kerma sources, period) and not from the Hyksos side either (almost nothing of the Hyksos royal archives has survived from their delta capital at Avaris, which sat in what is now the northeastern Nile delta).
It comes from the winner.

Specifically, it comes from a royal Theban monument called the Second Kamose Stele, erected at Karnak (the great temple complex just north of Thebes) to commemorate the wars of a pharaoh named Kamose (KAH-moh-say), the last king of the Theban Seventeenth Dynasty, older brother of the man who would eventually finish the job of throwing the Hyksos out of Egypt.
Kamose's stele tells the story of his campaign against the Hyksos from his own point of view, and the point of view is furious.

The most-quoted passage describes a specific incident: a Hyksos courier, carrying a letter from the Hyksos king in Avaris south toward Kerma, was intercepted by Kamose's troops somewhere in the desert west of the Nile.

Kamose read the letter and had its text carved onto his victory stele — not paraphrased, not summarized, but quoted, because he wanted posterity to know exactly what his two enemies had been saying to each other behind his back.

The letter is addressed from the Hyksos king (a ruler named Apophis, AP-oh-fiss, ruling from Avaris) to the "Ruler of Kush." Apophis writes, roughly: come north, don't hold back, the Theban king is hemmed in here with me and has nowhere to go, let's split the land of Egypt between us.
It is an unambiguous two-front alliance, preserved on the monument of the Theban pharaoh who intercepted it and considered it justification for the wars that followed.
This is also, incidentally, the moment when the name Kush becomes firmly attached to the Nubian kingdom in Egyptian sources.
"Ruler of Kush" is how Apophis addresses his Kerma counterpart in the intercepted letter.
The word had been drifting into use for a century or two, gradually displacing the older geographic terms Wawat and Yam.
From the Second Intermediate Period onward, when Egyptians wrote about the kingdom upstream, they wrote about Kush.

We will use "Kerma" in this book because the kingdom's capital was the city of Kerma and the archaeological culture is called Kerma, but you should know that the Egyptians of the seventeenth and sixteenth centuries BCE, and everyone afterward, called it Kush.
Kamose's counterattack was decisive in the short term but not in the long term.
He pushed the Hyksos back.
He raided northward.
He also raided southward against the Kushite holdings in Lower Nubia.
He did not, however, actually destroy either enemy before his death.
That job fell to his younger brother and successor, Ahmose I (AH-moh-say).

The two brothers' names are frustratingly close — Kamose starts with a K, Ahmose with an A, but both are pronounced with the same AH-moh-say sound at the end — and if you keep them straight, Kamose is the older brother who started the war and Ahmose is the younger brother who finished it.
Ahmose ruled from around 1550 BCE and is considered the founder of the Eighteenth Dynasty and the New Kingdom, the era of Egypt's imperial zenith.
Ahmose drove the Hyksos out of Egypt.
He pushed them back across the Sinai, captured their capital at Avaris, and ended their dynasty.
This is the famous story of Egyptian reunification, and it is told in every Egyptian history book.
What those books often leave out is what Ahmose did next.
Having cleaned out the north, Ahmose turned around and marched south.
He reoccupied the old Middle Kingdom forts, reclaimed Lower Nubia up to the Second Cataract, and began preparing a sustained southern campaign that would be finished by his successors.
The Theban dynasty had learned its lesson from the two-front squeeze.
The kingdom of Kush was not going to get another chance to coordinate with anybody.

The final destruction of Kerma came under a king named Thutmose I (THOOT-moh-say), the third pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty, who took the throne around 1504 BCE and who had an idea about what "southern boundary" should actually mean.
Thutmose I was not the first New Kingdom pharaoh to campaign against Kush.
He was not even the second.

But he was the first one who decided that containment at the Second Cataract was no longer good enough, and that the Kushite kingdom itself — the city, the royal cemetery, the deffufas, the whole Classic Kerma civilization — needed to be eliminated as a strategic threat to Egypt.
The campaign Thutmose I launched was an enormous logistical effort.

Egyptian river forces, supported by land columns, pushed past the Second Cataract, past the Third Cataract, past Kerma itself, and all the way to the region of the Fourth Cataract — more than 600 miles (1,000 km) upstream from the Egyptian border.

The logistical problem that had bottled up earlier pharaohs at the Second Cataract (the whitewater that stopped their boats) had been solved a century earlier by the Middle Kingdom slipway at Mirgissa, and New Kingdom Egypt, with its more aggressive military apparatus, had rebuilt and expanded that infrastructure.
By the time Thutmose I's force moved south, getting Egyptian boats past the cataracts was a solved engineering problem rather than an impossible one.
Along the way, Egyptian soldiers captured, sacked, and destroyed the city of Kerma.
The Western Deffufa, which had stood for more than a thousand years, was damaged but not toppled (it still stands today).
The royal cemetery was shut down.
The administrative structures of the kingdom were broken.
The ruler of Kush — we do not know his name, because Kerma did not write and the Egyptian sources do not record it — was killed, captured, or fled south into the uncontrolled interior.
What Thutmose I did next is the detail that sticks with you.
On his way back downstream, he commemorated the victory with a stele at a place called Tombos, near the Third Cataract, well inside Nubia.

The stele describes, in the formal register of Egyptian royal inscriptions, the fate of one of the Nubian captives taken on the campaign: his body was hung "head-downward at the prow of the falcon-ship of his majesty." The falcon-ship was the name of Thutmose I's royal boat.

Sailing downriver with a corpse hanging upside down from the bow was a public announcement, to every riverside village the boat passed on its way back north, that this particular southern campaign had ended with a body on a boat.
It was a message to Nubia: come back in the next generation, try your luck again, and there will be another body on another boat.
The Kingdom of Kerma, after roughly a thousand years of continuous existence, was over.
It is worth pausing here to grieve, briefly, the lost voices of Kerma.
We will never hear a Kerma poem, or a Kerma prayer, or a Kerma child's lullaby.

We will never know the name of the Kerma god who was worshipped in the Western Deffufa, or the name of the king who ordered the Eastern Deffufa built, or the names of the hundreds of retainers who went into the royal tumuli.
We will never read a Kerma account of the Hyksos alliance, or a Kerma description of what the city looked like at sunset, or a Kerma explanation of why some people chose to die with their king.

Every single thing we know about Kerma comes from its physical remains and from the outside perspective of its Egyptian enemies, who had political reasons to describe it as an enemy to be defeated rather than as a civilization to be understood.
This is the inheritance of writing's absence.

Egyptian civilization, with its papyrus and its hieroglyphs, has a thousand-year head start on Kerma in every cultural category that requires written evidence, because Egyptian voices survived and Kerma voices did not.

If Kerma had developed a script of its own and written on durable enough material — or if Egyptian archaeologists had been less efficient about removing later layers to reach earlier ones — we might know as much about Classic Kerma as we know about Old Kingdom Egypt.

The Kerma civilization, which by any reasonable measure was one of the most impressive Bronze Age African polities outside Egypt itself, survives in our books as a set of buildings, a style of pottery, a cemetery full of bones, and a sketch of its political history as written by its enemies.
The good news is that the defeat of the Kerma kingdom did not mean the end of Nubian civilization.

What the Egyptian sources present as a total conquest was actually the beginning of a new phase — a phase in which Nubian elites, Nubian religious traditions, Nubian soldiers, and Nubian cultural practices would be absorbed into the Egyptian New Kingdom as provincial subjects, but would also quietly carry forward the continuity of Nubian identity under Egyptian overlay.

Five centuries after Thutmose I destroyed Kerma, the descendants of those absorbed Nubians would become the Kushite pharaohs, the kings of the Egyptian Twenty-fifth Dynasty, and they would — incredibly — conquer Egypt itself.
That story is the next TL in this chain (Kingdom of Kush Ch 1).
For this one, we still have five centuries of Egyptian Nubia to work through, and that is the subject of the last two chapters.

chapters.title: Province of Gold
sentences: After Thutmose I, Nubia was no longer a separate country.
It was a province of Egypt.

The administrative system the New Kingdom pharaohs invented for governing it was a thing we haven't really talked about yet, and it was an innovation — at least for Egypt, and at least on this scale. (The broader claim that it was a world-first provincial administration would be an overclaim: in Mesopotamia, Sargon of Akkad had begun appointing his own loyal governors to rule conquered city-states roughly 1,800 years earlier — Mesopotamia Ch 5 — so the basic idea of running conquered territory through a pharaoh-appointed governor had precedent further east.

What was new about the Viceroy of Kush was the scale, the permanence, and the specific bureaucratic machinery Egypt developed around it.) Up until the New Kingdom, Egypt had never had to run a colonial administration of a conquered territory on this scale.
The Old Kingdom had extracted from Nubia through raids; the Middle Kingdom had contained Nubia through forts.
Neither of those approaches involved the actual civil governance of a conquered province.

Once Kerma was destroyed and the whole Nile valley from the First to the Fourth Cataract was nominally Egyptian territory, somebody had to collect the taxes, run the temples, administer the courts, manage the gold mines, handle the garrisons, deal with the local populations, and report the whole thing back to the pharaoh in Thebes.
The New Kingdom invented a governor for this job and gave him one of the most revealing titles in Egyptian administrative history: Viceroy of Kush.

The full Egyptian title was usually something like sa-nesu en Kush, meaning "King's Son of Kush," which did not necessarily mean the viceroy was an actual son of the pharaoh — it was an honorific suggesting that he held royal-level authority over his province, as if he were a member of the royal family.

In practice, the Viceroy of Kush was usually a senior Egyptian official handpicked by the pharaoh, often with military and administrative experience, and sometimes an aristocrat from a trusted Egyptian noble family.
He was the highest civil authority in Nubia.
He had responsibility for everything from tax collection to temple maintenance, and he reported directly to the pharaoh in Thebes.

Under him sat a layered bureaucracy of deputies, garrison commanders, temple priests, mine overseers, and district administrators, both Egyptian and Nubian, forming a colonial administrative structure as elaborate as anything the later Roman empire or the Achaemenid Persian empire (the first Persian empire, founded by Cyrus the Great around 550 BCE, which governed everything from the Aegean to the Indus with a similar layered provincial system) would invent.
The Viceroy's main job was gold.
Nubia's most important export to Egypt, throughout the New Kingdom, was gold.
Not the only export — ebony, ivory, cattle, incense, and slaves all moved north along with it — but gold was the indispensable one.

The gold that filled the treasuries of Thebes, the gold that plated the chapels and the obelisks, the gold that paid tribute to foreign kings and bribed allied states, the gold that gave Egypt diplomatic leverage across the Bronze Age Near East — all of that gold, or almost all of it, came out of Nubia.

When the New Kingdom pharaoh in Thebes looked at his treasury and saw the wealth that let him intimidate the Hittites, buy off the Mitannians, and build whatever monument he wanted, he was looking at Nubian gold filtered through the Viceroy's office.
Nubia's gold was mostly in two regions.
The first was the Wadi Allaqi (WAH-dee ah-LAK-ee), a desert valley east of the Nile in Lower Nubia that drained a set of gold-bearing rock formations in the Red Sea Hills.
The second was the region around the Fourth Cataract in Upper Nubia, where additional gold-bearing rock had been worked since the Old Kingdom on a smaller scale.
Both regions contained quartz veins that carried native gold — not gold dust in riverbeds, but gold embedded in solid rock that had to be extracted by hand.
The mining technique was brutal.

Gangs of laborers, many of them prisoners of war, convicted criminals, or debt-bondaged Nubians, broke up the quartz with stone and later metal hammers, carried the fragments to a central crushing area, pulverized them into powder, washed the powder to separate the heavier gold particles from the lighter rock fragments, and collected the gold.
The whole process was carried out in desert conditions with minimal water, under a broiling sun, with inadequate food and high mortality.

The best surviving eyewitness account of ancient Nubian gold mining comes not from the New Kingdom itself but from Diodorus Siculus (dye-oh-DOR-us SIK-yoo-lus — a Greek historian who wrote a world history around 30 BCE, more than a thousand years after the peak of New Kingdom mining but while the same general technology and labor methods were apparently still in use).
Diodorus visited or interviewed people who had visited the mines, and his description of what he saw is the most cited passage in the entire ancient literature on the subject.

He describes chained workers being driven into cramped underground shafts without protection, children carrying the ore, old men crushing it, women sieving it, everybody too exhausted to speak and too malnourished to stand up straight.

The translation varies but the judgment does not: "Death is more welcome to them than life, such hardship do they endure." Diodorus is writing twelve centuries after Thutmose I, so he isn't a direct witness to New Kingdom conditions, but the continuity of ancient mining methods and the Nubian archaeological record both suggest that the New Kingdom version of the job was not much kinder.
The gold that made Egypt a superpower came out of the ground at the price of Nubian bodies.
What the New Kingdom got out of this was extraordinary.

Egyptian gold production during the Eighteenth and Nineteenth Dynasties — both of which are New Kingdom dynasties in the shared framework we set up in Chapter 2, the Eighteenth running from Ahmose I through Tutankhamun and the Nineteenth containing Ramesses II — reached a scale the ancient Mediterranean would not see again for centuries.

New Kingdom Nubia was producing gold at a rate that supported one of the richest royal treasuries of the Bronze Age Mediterranean, bankrolled major monument construction projects, and gave Egyptian diplomats the resources to buy the loyalty of foreign rulers across the Levant and the Aegean.

The best surviving evidence of how important Nubian gold was to the wider Late Bronze Age diplomatic system comes from a stash of clay tablets discovered in the late nineteenth century at the Egyptian site of Tell el-Amarna, the short-lived capital built by the pharaoh Akhenaten (AH-ken-AH-ten) around 1350 BCE. (Akhenaten is famous in Egyptian history for a different reason: he attempted a religious revolution, replacing the traditional pantheon with an exclusive worship of the sun disk itself, and when he died his successors rolled back the revolution and tried to erase his name.
His son Tutankhamun is the "boy pharaoh" whose intact tomb Howard Carter found in 1922.

We'll touch Akhenaten's religious experiment only in passing; what matters here is that he briefly moved the Egyptian capital to Tell el-Amarna and that the diplomatic correspondence from his reign, preserved when the site was abandoned, is our best window into Late Bronze Age international relations.) The tablets are the Amarna Letters — more than three hundred diplomatic documents, written in Akkadian cuneiform (the Babylonian language of international diplomacy in the Late Bronze Age, the era's closest equivalent to modern English), addressed to the pharaoh by his fellow Great Kings and by the minor rulers of Syrian and Canaanite city-states who were nominally under Egyptian protection.

The Amarna Letters are covered in detail from the Mesopotamian side in Mesopotamia Chapter 8, which describes how the pharaoh was writing to and receiving letters from four fellow Great Kings: The Kassite kings of Babylon, who had taken over Mesopotamia after the old Hammurabi-era Babylonian dynasty collapsed, ruling from Babylon in what is now Iraq.
The Mitanni kings, a Hurrian-speaking dynasty (Hurrian is a language family unrelated to either Egyptian or Akkadian) ruling a large territory in what is now northern Syria and southeastern Turkey.

The Hittites, an Indo-European-speaking empire (their language is in the same broad family as later Greek, Latin, and English, and completely unrelated to either Egyptian or Akkadian) centered in central Anatolia, modern Turkey — the same empire Ramesses II would later fight at Kadesh.

The Middle Assyrian kings, who were the forerunners of the terrifying Neo-Assyrian empire that would dominate the Near East three centuries later (Mesopotamia Ch 10–11), speaking another Semitic language related to Akkadian.
All four groups, plus dozens of smaller Syrian and Canaanite rulers, wrote to Egypt maintaining a delicate brotherhood-of-kings diplomacy around marriage alliances and gold.
The single most common substantive request in the Amarna Letters is for gold.

Kassite and Mitanni kings write to the pharaoh with formulaic complaints — "my brother, your messengers have been received with honor, now please send gold, lots of it, for my temples and my palace, because in your country gold is as common as dust" — and the gold they are asking for is specifically Nubian gold.

The Kassite king Burnaburiash II in one letter explicitly asks for "much gold" because a previous shipment had been short, and scholars who have studied the letters are clear that the gold Egypt was in a position to send (or not send) was coming out of Wadi Allaqi and the upper Nile mines.
The Amarna Letters are the other end of Nubia's gold pipeline.
When Apophis and Kerma had been squeezing Thebes a century and a half earlier, Egypt was gold-poor and isolated.

When the Amarna kings were writing their "your country is full of gold" letters, Egypt had taken back the mines, and the letters are the proof that everybody else in the Bronze Age knew exactly where that gold came from.
A separate document worth mentioning briefly is the Turin Papyrus Map, dating from around 1150 BCE, at the very end of the New Kingdom.

It covers an area of Egyptian desert east of Thebes where Egypt was also mining gold on a smaller scale, and it is one of the oldest surviving geological maps in human history — labeled gold mining areas, paths to reach them, quarries, settlements, and topography sketched in two colors.
The Turin map is not itself a Nubian map, but it is the best surviving example of how the New Kingdom Egyptian state visualized its mining operations.
A map of this kind presupposes organized, repeatable, technical surveying of a mining region.
You do not draw a map of gold mines unless your state is committed to working the mines as a permanent industrial operation.
Nubia's gold mines were run like an industry.
The Viceroy of Kush did not live in Kerma.
Nobody lived in Kerma anymore, because Thutmose I's campaign had essentially ended the city as a political center.

The main administrative capital of the New Kingdom's Nubian province was a different site — a place the Egyptians called Miam (MEE-am) and the modern archaeologists call Aniba (ah-NEE-bah), located in Lower Nubia a bit north of the Second Cataract, on the west bank of the Nile.

Aniba/Miam had been occupied for centuries already as a C-Group community, but under New Kingdom administration it grew into a substantial town with Egyptian-style architecture, a garrison, a governor's residence, and a series of Egyptian-style tombs for the local nobility — tombs that are some of our best evidence for the cultural phenomenon we will talk about next chapter, the blending of Nubian and Egyptian elite identity under New Kingdom rule.
The Viceroy's administrative reach extended from Aniba south through a chain of subordinate centers and garrisons.
