Important nodes in the chain included the old Middle Kingdom forts at Buhen and Mirgissa (now refurbished as New Kingdom administrative centers rather than defensive installations), a new foundation at Sesebi, the major religious complex at Soleb (which we will get to in a moment), and the spiritual heart of Egyptian-era Nubia at Jebel Barkal.
A typical message from the pharaoh to the Viceroy would be carried south by courier from Thebes to Aniba, then passed through this network of subordinate officials to wherever it needed to go.

A typical tribute shipment would run in the other direction: gold from the mines, cattle from the C-Group villagers, ivory and ebony from deeper trade networks, all assembled at Aniba and shipped north to the royal court.
This was a functioning colonial administration.
It was running for four hundred years.

It is the direct model, whether or not anyone at the time noticed the resemblance, for the way later empires — Persians, Romans, Arabs, Ottomans, British — would eventually govern their own foreign provinces.

Of all the sites in New Kingdom Nubia, the most religiously important was Jebel Barkal (JEH-bel BAR-kal — meaning "Pure Mountain" in Arabic, a name that was assigned later; the ancient name was different).
Jebel Barkal is a flat-topped sandstone butte rising out of the plain near the modern Sudanese town of Karima, about 200 miles (320 km) upstream from Kerma, well south of the Fourth Cataract.

Viewed from certain angles, the mountain has a distinctive feature: a tall pillar of rock that sticks up near its corner, resembling an abstracted human figure or a standing cobra depending on your inclination.
To Egyptian eyes looking at this mountain, the standing rock pillar looked like a uraeus — the rearing cobra figure on an Egyptian pharaoh's crown, symbol of royal divinity.

Egyptians who reached Jebel Barkal during New Kingdom campaigns recognized it immediately as sacred, and they interpreted the pillar as a sign that the god Amun was physically present in the mountain. (Amun, AH-mun, was the king of the Egyptian pantheon during the New Kingdom.

His main temple was at Karnak, the enormous temple complex on the eastern bank of the Nile just north of Thebes, which grew across the New Kingdom into one of the largest religious structures ever built in the ancient world, with walled precincts, obelisks, hypostyle halls the size of cathedrals, and processional avenues of sphinxes.

When Egyptian sources call a god "the Amun of Karnak," they mean the specific manifestation worshipped in that temple complex.) Thutmose III, grandson of Thutmose I and the most accomplished military pharaoh of the early New Kingdom, built the first major Egyptian temple at Jebel Barkal around 1450 BCE, consecrated to Amun and explicitly linking the sacred mountain of Nubia to the god of Thebes.

The implicit theology was that Amun had two dwellings — the main one at Karnak, the other here at Jebel Barkal — and that the pharaoh who could claim both was the legitimate king of the entire Nile valley, Egypt and Nubia together.
Jebel Barkal became the southern spiritual anchor of Egyptian royal ideology.

Every New Kingdom pharaoh who cared about Nubia at all paid religious respect to the mountain, built or maintained temples at its base, and had his priests conduct ceremonies linking him to the Amun of Napata (the later name for the Jebel Barkal region).

The reason Jebel Barkal matters for the long arc of Nubian history is that Egyptian religious investment in the site took hold of the local Nubian population in a way that outlasted Egyptian political control.
When Egypt eventually withdrew from Nubia in the eleventh century BCE, the local elites continued to worship Amun at Jebel Barkal.
They maintained the temples.
They kept the priestly traditions.
They raised their children in an Amun-centered religious system that had originally come from Egypt but had now become authentically Nubian.

Five centuries later, the Kushite kings of the Twenty-fifth Dynasty — the Nubian conquerors who briefly ruled Egypt itself — would claim Jebel Barkal as their ancestral holy place and use their devotion to Amun as legitimation for their pharaonic ambitions.
The long-term consequence of Thutmose III's temple at Jebel Barkal was that Egypt effectively gave Nubia a god, and the Nubians held on to the god even after they had shaken off the givers.
This is one of the stranger ironies of ancient religious history.
Amun was a Theban god, a local deity who had risen to the top of the Egyptian pantheon during the Middle Kingdom and become the imperial god of the New Kingdom.
He was as Egyptian as a god could be.

And yet, by the ninth or eighth century BCE, the most zealous and politically ambitious worshippers of Amun on the planet were Nubian kings at Jebel Barkal, preparing the invasion that would briefly make them pharaohs of Egypt on the strength of their theological credentials.
The god had emigrated, and the god had made a new home.
Jebel Barkal was the spiritual anchor, but it was not the only monument the New Kingdom left in Nubia.

Across four hundred years of Egyptian administration, pharaohs competed to build temples up and down the Nubian Nile — Thutmose III and Amenhotep II's modest but well-preserved Temple of Amada, whose walls record the grimmest royal inscription in the province (Amenhotep II's boast about executing seven captured Syrian princes and displaying their bodies as warnings to anyone considering rebellion); Amenhotep III's elegant Temple of Soleb, with its processional avenue of ram-headed sphinxes and its finely carved reliefs integrating Nubia into the ritual geography of the New Kingdom; forts-turned-administrative-centers at Buhen, Mirgissa, Aniba; the governor's residences and priestly quarters that served the viceregal bureaucracy.

But the single most famous, and the most aggressive, Egyptian monument ever built in Nubia came a century after Soleb, under a king who understood propaganda as well as any Bronze Age ruler who ever lived.

Ramesses II (RAH-meh-seez, sometimes spelled Ramses II or Rameses II) ruled Egypt from around 1279 to 1213 BCE — a reign of roughly sixty-six years, long enough that many of his subjects never lived under any other king.

He fought the famous battle of Kadesh (KAY-desh — a frontier city in what is now western Syria, contested between Egypt and the Hittite empire of central Anatolia, the scene of one of the earliest battles in human history for which we have detailed written accounts from both sides) against the Hittites, fathered so many children that the traditional count runs into the dozens, built monuments in quantities that would be absurd in any other pharaoh's career, and is generally considered the most self-celebrating Egyptian king in recorded history.
Ramesses built temples everywhere.
But the two he built at a place called Abu Simbel (ah-boo SIM-bel), in Lower Nubia about 180 miles (290 km) south of Aswan, are in a class by themselves.
Abu Simbel consists of two rock-cut temples carved directly into the face of a sandstone cliff on the west bank of the Nile.

The larger temple is dedicated to Ramesses himself, with a nominal co-dedication to the gods Amun (king of the Egyptian pantheon, whom we met at Jebel Barkal), Ra (the sun god, often depicted as a falcon-headed figure with a sun disk above his head, associated with the daily cycle of rising and setting, the other great imperial Egyptian deity), and Ptah (the creator god of Memphis, patron of craftsmen, associated with the subterranean and the hidden).
Listing Ramesses alongside Amun, Ra, and Ptah was a theological statement: the king is their equal, of their rank, deserving of the same worship.

The smaller temple, slightly to the north, is dedicated to Ramesses's chief wife Nefertari (nef-er-TAR-ee) and to the goddess Hathor (a motherly, protective deity often associated with music, dance, joy, and female fertility, sometimes depicted with cow horns and a sun disk between them).
Both temples are carved entirely into the cliff.
They are not free-standing buildings.
You walk into them through doorways cut in the rock face, and the entire interior — columns, chambers, sanctuaries, statues — is hollowed out of solid sandstone.
The entrance to the main temple is what sticks in the visitor's mind forever.

Four enormous seated statues of Ramesses II, each about 66 feet (20 meters) tall, are carved out of the cliff face itself, staring east across the Nile toward the rising sun and the upstream lands of Nubia.
The statues are not additions stuck onto the cliff; they are the cliff, shaped into the form of the pharaoh.
A six-story office building carved out of rock is a useful mental anchor.
When you stand at their feet and look upward, the effect is overwhelming, which is exactly the effect Ramesses intended.
Abu Simbel was built as a permanent visual declaration to anyone sailing up or down the river: the pharaoh is here, he is enormous, he is watching you, and Nubia is his.

Inside the temple, the walls are covered with reliefs commemorating Ramesses's military victories, with particular emphasis on the Battle of Kadesh — a battle in which Ramesses's advance guard got surrounded by a Hittite ambush and the pharaoh had to lead a personal charge to break out, a story Ramesses considered so heroic that he had it carved on at least five different monuments across Egypt.
The innermost sanctuary contains seated statues of Amun, Ra, the deified Ramesses, and Ptah.

The temple was oriented with extraordinary astronomical care so that twice a year, on dates corresponding roughly to the anniversaries of Ramesses's birthday and his coronation, the rising sun shone directly through the entrance and illuminated the faces of three of the four sanctuary statues — all except Ptah, the god of the hidden depths, who stayed in shadow.
This is not a coincidence.

It is the product of a deliberate engineering effort to align the temple's axis with the sun's position on specific dates, and the Egyptian priests who built Abu Simbel were calculating the solar geometry with enough precision to get it right.
Abu Simbel's solar alignment has a modern sequel worth a paragraph on its own.

In the 1960s, when the Aswan High Dam was flooding the Nile valley upstream and threatening to drown the temple under Lake Nasser, an international salvage operation disassembled Abu Simbel stone by stone, hauled it 200 feet (60 meters) higher up the cliff, and reconstructed it on a new site safely above the new waterline.
It was one of the most ambitious archaeological engineering projects ever attempted.

The engineers who moved the temple made a serious effort to preserve the original solar alignment, and in the new position they nearly succeeded: the sun still illuminates the sanctuary statues on dates very close to the originals, but close inspection suggests the alignment is slightly off from the ancient builders' calculations.

The story has been told and retold as an anecdote about ancient precision, with varying degrees of accuracy — some versions overstate the offset, some understate it — but the underlying point is real: moving a temple with its astronomy intact is hard, and getting it exactly right is harder.
Abu Simbel is the most extravagant single statement of Egyptian dominance ever made in Nubia.

It was built to say, unambiguously, that Nubia was Egyptian territory, that the pharaoh's presence in Nubia was eternal, and that Nubian elites should look up at those four colossal stone faces and know whose province they were living in.

The fact that less than two centuries after Abu Simbel was carved, the pharaoh's presence in Nubia was no longer eternal — that the New Kingdom was crumbling, the Viceroy of Kush had lost contact with the court, and Nubia was slipping out of Egyptian control — is one of the more bitter ironies of ancient monument-building.
Abu Simbel outlasted the thing it was meant to represent by 3,000 years.

chapters.title: The Cultural Bridge
sentences: Of all the features of New Kingdom Nubia, the strangest and most important is the thing that happened to the elites.
Here is the situation as of, say, 1400 BCE.
Egypt has been governing Nubia as a colonial province for a century.
The Viceroy of Kush runs the administration out of Aniba.
The gold mines are operating.
The temples are being built.
Nubian laborers and soldiers are being recruited into Egyptian service in large numbers.

The C-Group pottery tradition is still alive in the Lower Nubian villages, and the cultural continuity of ordinary Nubian life has not been erased — the Egyptian presence is an administrative overlay, not a cultural replacement at the village level.
But at the elite level, something weird is happening.

The children of the Nubian nobility — the sons of local chiefs, the heirs of village headmen, the members of the minor ruling families that had survived the destruction of Kerma and the reorganization of the province — were being systematically taken to the Egyptian royal court in Thebes, educated there, and returned home as adult administrators.
This is documented in Egyptian sources as an official policy.
It is sometimes called the kap system, from the Egyptian word for the royal nursery where royal children and elite hostages were raised together.
Egyptian princes were educated alongside the sons of foreign rulers — Syrian princes, Libyan princes, and Nubian princes — under the direct supervision of the Egyptian court.

The foreign children learned the Egyptian language, the Egyptian script, Egyptian religious practice, Egyptian administrative procedure, Egyptian military technique, and Egyptian aesthetic sensibilities.
They also, importantly, formed personal relationships with the Egyptian royal heirs they grew up with.
When the foreign child reached adulthood, he was sent home to rule his native province as an Egyptian-trained, Egyptian-loyal local ruler.
When the Egyptian child became pharaoh, he had a set of foreign-born provincial administrators already in place, all of whom had been his boyhood classmates.

This is often described as the oldest systematic colonial elite-education program in world history, and it is close to the truth, but the fuller picture should acknowledge a quieter Mesopotamian precedent.

About a thousand years earlier, Sargon of Akkad (the first Mesopotamian emperor, Mesopotamia Ch 5) had installed his own daughter Enheduanna as high priestess at the temple of the moon god at Ur — not as a religious offering but as a political control mechanism, placing a trusted family member inside the ritual machinery of a conquered Sumerian city.

The Egyptian kap system was more systematic, operated on more subject elites at once, and ran for more generations than Sargon's one-daughter experiment, but the underlying idea — using close education and personal relationships to bind subject elites to the imperial center — was not invented in New Kingdom Egypt.
What New Kingdom Egypt did was operationalize it on a provincial scale.

Later ancient empires would eventually rediscover the approach: the Persians ran something similar for the sons of their satraps (Persian Empire Ch 2), the Romans educated provincial aristocrats in their own household schools, the British built their colonial administration in India on exactly this model.

And in Nubia, the result was a generation of Nubian nobles who were fluent in Egyptian, worshipped Amun, wrote in hieroglyphic, dressed in Egyptian linen, decorated their tombs with Egyptian funerary iconography, took Egyptian personal names alongside their Nubian ones, and — at the same time — retained enough of their Nubian identity to be recognized as Nubians by their own people.

They were bridge figures, fluent in both cultures, governing the province on behalf of the pharaoh while maintaining relationships with the local population that no purely Egyptian administrator could have matched.

The best-documented individual example of this Egyptianized Nubian elite is a man named Hekanefer (HEH-kah-ne-fer — the name is actually Egyptian, and we do not know what his Nubian birth name was, if he had one; we know him only by the name he used in his Egyptian-language tomb inscriptions).

Hekanefer was the Prince of Miam (the Egyptian name for Aniba in Lower Nubia) during the reign of Tutankhamun (TOOT-ankh-AH-mun, the famous boy pharaoh who ruled around 1340 BCE and whose tomb, discovered intact by Howard Carter in 1922, is one of the most famous archaeological finds of all time).
As Prince of Miam, Hekanefer was the local Nubian ruler of the region around Aniba, operating under the authority of the Viceroy of Kush and, ultimately, the pharaoh.
He was a Nubian by birth, educated in Egypt, returned home to rule, and thoroughly Egyptianized in his public presentation.

We know about Hekanefer because his tomb at Toshka (in Lower Nubia, a few miles north of Abu Simbel) has survived, and because he also appears in a famous painted scene in the Theban tomb of a man named Huy, who was the Viceroy of Kush under Tutankhamun.
In the Huy tomb scene, which is one of the most vivid surviving depictions of Egyptian–Nubian ceremonial interaction, a procession of Nubian princes is shown presenting tribute to the pharaoh.
The princes are dressed in long robes, leopard skins draped over one shoulder, with distinctive Nubian hair styles and ornaments.

They carry gold rings, ivory tusks, ebony logs, exotic animals (including giraffes, which is a particularly specific and probably accurate detail — giraffes were and still are a Nubian rather than Egyptian animal), and jars of valuable trade goods.
One of the princes in the procession is labeled "Hekanefer, Prince of Miam." Here is the interesting part.
In Huy's tomb, Hekanefer is depicted in full Nubian dress — the leopard skin, the Nubian hairstyle, the tribute-bearer's posture.
He is visibly Nubian.
He looks Nubian.
He is identified as Nubian.
This is how the Egyptian viewer of Huy's tomb painting was meant to see him.
In Hekanefer's own tomb, a few miles away in Lower Nubia, Hekanefer is depicted entirely differently.
He wears Egyptian aristocratic clothes.
His name is written in standard Egyptian hieroglyphs.

The religious iconography is Egyptian — the gods he worships are Egyptian gods, the prayers on his walls are standard Egyptian funerary prayers, and the afterlife journey shown on his walls is the standard Egyptian one: the dead person travels through a series of underworld obstacles to reach a final courtroom presided over by Osiris (the green-skinned god of the dead and of the afterlife, traditionally the brother-husband of Isis), where the dead person's heart is weighed on a set of scales against a feather representing truth and cosmic order.

If the heart balances the feather, the dead person is admitted to the Field of Iaru (EE-ah-roo, sometimes spelled Aaru), a paradise version of the Nile Delta imagined as an eternal reed field where the blessed dead farm and feast forever.
If the heart is heavier than the feather — meaning the person's deeds on earth have been too wicked — a waiting monster called Ammit devours them and they cease to exist.
This package of images (weighing of the heart, Osiris's courtroom, Field of Iaru) is the core Egyptian afterlife theology of the New Kingdom, and Hekanefer's tomb contains the full version of it.
There is almost nothing visibly "Nubian" about Hekanefer's own tomb.
He presents himself, to posterity and to the Egyptian gods, as a fully Egyptian nobleman.
This double presentation tells us everything we need to know about the cultural situation of Nubian elites during the New Kingdom.

Hekanefer was simultaneously a Nubian prince (in the context of Egyptian court ritual, where his Nubianness was part of the symbolic theater) and a fully Egyptian nobleman (in the context of his own religious preparations for the afterlife, where being Egyptian was the only acceptable identity for someone who expected to reach the fields of Iaru in the west).

He was not just "Egyptianized." He was performatively bicultural, moving between identities according to context, code-switching in a way that would be familiar to a modern bicultural immigrant elite.
He lived in two worlds at once, and the archaeological record preserves both of them.
Hekanefer is one specific case, but the pattern is visible at many Nubian elite burial sites during the New Kingdom.
Nubian tomb owners at Aniba, Toshka, Debeira, Mirgissa, and other sites present themselves as Egyptian in their own tombs while retaining Nubian identity markers in ceremonial contexts.

The pattern ran deep enough that some Egyptologists now talk about a "third space," neither fully Nubian nor fully Egyptian, in which Nubian elites operated during the New Kingdom centuries, using Egyptian religious and administrative vocabulary to manage their own social worlds.
The political importance of this bicultural elite would not become clear until the New Kingdom ended.
And the New Kingdom ended, along with a lot of other things, in one of the most consequential bad centuries in Bronze Age history.
Between roughly 1200 and 1150 BCE, the entire eastern Mediterranean went through a crisis called the Late Bronze Age Collapse.

Scholars have been arguing about its causes for a century and the best current picture involves several compounding stresses at once: prolonged drought across much of the region, crop failures and famine, migrating populations that have come to be called "Sea Peoples" in the Egyptian sources, breakdown of the international diplomatic network that had been holding the great kingdoms together (the Amarna Letters system from Chapter 7), internal rebellions, the collapse of centrally managed palace economies, and a cascading loss of the specialized knowledge that kept Bronze Age states running.
The Hittite empire of Anatolia disappeared.
The Mycenaean palaces of Greece were burned.
Ugarit, the great Syrian trading city, was destroyed.
Egypt itself was invaded by the Sea Peoples in two major battles under Ramesses III (the great-grandson of Ramesses II) and survived but was badly weakened.

Mesopotamia went through its own version of the same collapse — the Kassite dynasty of Babylon fell, the Aramean tribes overran Babylonia, and the Middle Assyrian state contracted hard (Mesopotamia Ch 9).

Egypt, weakened rather than destroyed, entered what historians now call the Third Intermediate Period — the third of the three periodic breakdowns in pharaonic central authority that we met back in Chapter 2, and the worst of them.
Central government faltered.
Regional power bases in the delta and at Thebes pulled in different directions.
The priesthoods of Amun at Karnak and of other major temples grew politically powerful at the expense of the royal court.
Royal legitimacy became a matter of who could hold a particular city rather than who sat on the throne at Memphis or Thebes.
During this long slow collapse, Egypt's grip on Nubia loosened and then released.
The Viceroy of Kush, whose office had been the linchpin of Egyptian administration in the province, became increasingly disconnected from the royal court.
Tribute shipments shrank.
Communication with Thebes became irregular.
Egyptian garrisons in Nubia went unreinforced, unpaid, and eventually unmanned.
The gold mines stopped producing on the old industrial scale.
The temples at Jebel Barkal and Soleb and Amada kept functioning, but their priests began to operate more independently, answering to local patronage networks rather than to a distant Theban court.
Around 1070 BCE, Egypt formally withdrew from Nubia, or rather stopped pretending to govern a province it no longer had the resources to administer.
The last Viceroy of Kush was a man named Panehsy (PAH-neh-see).

His name is itself a small piece of evidence about the era: in Egyptian, Panehsy simply means "the Nubian," which may suggest that by this point in the New Kingdom's decline, even the office of Viceroy had passed into the hands of a person of Nubian origin.
That would be a final irony of the colonial administration — an Egyptianized Nubian governing Egypt's Nubian province as the Egyptian state itself was falling apart.
Panehsy ended his career as a warlord in southern Egypt during the civil disorders of the late New Kingdom, rather than as a functioning provincial administrator.
When Egypt withdrew, it left Nubia to the Nubians.

Specifically, it left Nubia to the Egyptianized Nubian elites — the descendants of Hekanefer and his peers, people who had been trained to think in Egyptian categories, who worshipped Egyptian gods, who knew how to write Egyptian hieroglyphs, who had been running Egyptian-style administrations in the Nubian provinces for a century or more, and who now found themselves in charge of a landscape with no distant pharaoh to report to.
These people did not forget they were Nubians.
They also did not abandon the Egyptian cultural apparatus they had been raised with.

They kept the temples running, they kept worshipping Amun, they kept using the Egyptian language for administrative and religious purposes, and they started — quietly, over generations, without anyone in contemporary Egypt noticing — to build a new Nubian kingdom on the foundations of the old Egyptian province.

The new kingdom centered itself at Napata (NAH-pah-tah — the region around Jebel Barkal), because Jebel Barkal was the spiritual anchor of the Amun cult and because it was far enough upstream to be safe from any future Egyptian interference.
The kingdom was called, in its own later documents, the Kingdom of Kush (Kingdom of Kush Ch 1).
It is the direct political and cultural successor to the Egyptian province of Nubia, built by the bicultural Nubian elite who had inherited Egyptian institutions and made them their own.
And about three centuries after Egypt's withdrawal, the Kushite kings of Napata would do something that nobody in 1070 BCE would have predicted.
They would march north, cross the First Cataract, enter Egypt, and conquer it.

For about a hundred years during the eighth and seventh centuries BCE, the pharaohs of Egypt would be Nubian — members of the Twenty-fifth Dynasty (Kingdom of Kush Ch 2-4), worshippers of Amun at Jebel Barkal, descendants of the Egyptianized elites who had learned to run a province under Tutankhamun's Viceroy and had then, after the Egyptian state disappeared out from under them, kept running it on their own terms.
But that story belongs to the next book in this chain (Kingdom of Kush Ch 2-4).
Let's close this one by looking back at the whole arc.
We started in Chapter 1 with the A-Group culture, a pre-urban Nubian tradition that was trading with predynastic Egypt when the first pharaohs were still just the chiefs of small delta kingdoms.

We watched the A-Group build burials rich enough to produce the Qustul kingship controversy, and then we watched the whole culture disappear under early dynastic Egyptian pressure in Chapter 2's four-century silence.
We watched the C-Group re-settle Lower Nubia and quietly maintain a cattle-centered tradition that would survive a thousand years of Egyptian contact without being erased.

We followed Harkhuf's donkey caravans south to the ruler of Yam in Chapter 3, and watched the city of Kerma rise in the floodplain of Upper Nubia, becoming the first real urban center in Africa outside Egypt itself, and the first city in the history of the continent's interior that can legitimately be called a capital.
We watched Middle Kingdom Egypt, terrified of what Kerma was becoming, build thirteen fortresses along the Second Cataract and declare a formal border that was meant to last forever.

We watched Classic Kerma reach its peak in Chapter 5, burying its kings with corridors of hundreds of retainers, producing the finest pottery in the Bronze Age world, and operating a diplomatic reach that extended into Hyksos-controlled Egypt and down into the incense trade of the Red Sea.

We watched, in Chapter 6, the Kerma–Hyksos alliance squeeze Theban Egypt between two hostile powers, and we watched the Theban pharaohs — first Kamose, then Ahmose, finally Thutmose I — destroy Kerma in retaliation.

We watched, in Chapter 7, New Kingdom Egypt absorb Nubia as a province under the Viceroy of Kush, exploit its gold mines on an industrial scale, and build some of the most impressive temples in the ancient world at Jebel Barkal and Soleb and Abu Simbel.

And we watched, in this final chapter, an Egyptianized Nubian elite emerge out of the province's administrative nursery — bicultural, loyal, fluent in Egyptian categories but retaining Nubian identity — and inherit the whole apparatus when Egyptian state power withdrew during the Bronze Age collapse.
The story that runs through all eight chapters is not a story of one civilization defeating another.

It is a story of two civilizations in prolonged contact, exchanging people and goods and ideas and soldiers and gods across a border that neither of them could fully close, for almost three thousand years.

Nubia influenced Egypt at least as much as Egypt influenced Nubia — the Egyptian pantheon absorbed Nubian deities, Egyptian armies depended on Nubian mercenaries, Egyptian economic power was built on Nubian gold, and by the end of the New Kingdom, Egypt itself was about to be governed by Egyptianized Nubians who had learned the job too well.
The conventional picture of a dominant Egypt ruling a subordinate Nubia is half the story.

The other half is that Nubia, quietly and persistently, was always in the room, always holding a bow, always sending its children to the schoolroom and its gold to the treasury, and always waiting for its moment.
The moment would come.
When it did, the Nubian pharaohs would take the throne of the country that had once tried to close its border against them, and they would claim to have been the legitimate heirs of Amun all along.
And in one particular sense, they were.
Five centuries after Egypt withdrew, the most devout worshippers of the Egyptian god Amun on the planet were not in Thebes or at Karnak.
They were at Jebel Barkal, in the mountains of Upper Nubia, praying toward the sacred pillar that Thutmose III had first recognized as a uraeus fifteen hundred years before.

The priesthood of Amun, the ritual calendars of the temples, the theological language of divine kingship — all of it had migrated south during the Third Intermediate Period, taken root in Napata, and been carried forward by the Kushite rulers as their own inheritance.
When they marched north to take the pharaoh's throne, they were not foreigners claiming to be Egyptians.
They were Amun's own people coming home.
That is the end of Ancient Nubia.
The next book in this chain picks up with the Kingdom of Kush (Kingdom of Kush Ch 1-8) and the long, strange, magnificent story of what happened next.

chapters.title: Two Elams
sentences: Here is a sentence worth starting with: Elam was two places, not one.
This is the single most important thing to know about the civilization this book is about, and it is the thing most books about the ancient Near East fail to make clear.

When an Assyrian scribe wrote about "Elam," and when a Babylonian priest wrote about "Elam," and when a Persian king a thousand years later reminisced about "Anshan," they were all talking about the same political and cultural entity.
But they were talking about two connected halves of it — a lowland half on the flat plain east of the Tigris River, and a highland half in the mountain valleys of southwestern Iran.
Those two halves were different in climate, in agriculture, in dialect, in architectural style, and often in political loyalty.
Elam worked when the lowland and the highland cooperated.
It fell apart whenever they didn't.

The reader who is willing to hold two places in mind at once — Susa (SOO-sah) down on the plain, and Anshan (AHN-shahn) up in the mountains — is going to do very well with the next two and a half thousand years of this story.

The reader who tries to map "Elam" onto a single city or a single region is going to be confused roughly every other chapter, because the political center of gravity keeps sliding between the two halves.
So let's start with the land.
Southwestern Iran is a geography of contrasts, and the contrasts happen fast.
If you stand at the Persian Gulf coast and look north, the land rises in steps.

First a narrow coastal strip, then the lowland plain of Khuzestan (KHOO-zeh-stan), then the foothills, then the Zagros Mountains rearing up in long parallel ridges, then the high Iranian plateau above 5,000 feet.
From bathing in the Gulf to riding a donkey over a 7,000-foot mountain pass is about 150 miles (240 km) as the crow flies.
That vertical rise in that short horizontal distance is what made Elam possible — and what made it permanently difficult to govern.
Khuzestan, the lowland half, is a hot flat alluvial plain irrigated by three rivers flowing out of the Zagros toward the Gulf.
The biggest is the Karun, the longest river entirely inside Iran, and the second is the Karkheh, both rich with silt deposited by seasonal floods.

The climate on the plain is close to the Mesopotamian climate we have been studying in the sister volume to this book (Mesopotamia Ch 1): hot summers, mild winters, low annual rainfall, farmland sustained by irrigation rather than rain.
The crops are the same as Sumer's — emmer wheat, barley, date palms, garden vegetables, sheep and goats.
If you were dropped blindfolded into a Khuzestan village in 2500 BCE, you would think you were in lower Mesopotamia until someone started speaking.
The local language would not be Sumerian or Akkadian.
It would be Elamite, and it would be a language unrelated to anything else.
Fars (FARS — sometimes rendered "Fārs" with the Persian vowel length, the source of the English word "Persia"), the highland half, is a completely different world.

Fars is the great high plateau region of southwestern Iran, sitting at 5,000 to 6,000 feet elevation, cold in winter and dry in summer, with short growing seasons and a landscape of mountain basins fed by seasonal streams.
The main city up here, throughout the Elamite period, was Anshan — a large walled town sitting in a broad highland valley about 160 miles (260 km) east of Susa over the Zagros ridges.

Anshan is now known archaeologically as the site of Tall-i Malyan, which is a modern Persian name meaning "Mound of Malyan." The Elamite identity of the site was not confirmed until 1971, when archaeologists excavating the mound found tablets with Elamite text naming the city.
Until that identification, modern scholars knew "Anshan" from Mesopotamian and Persian royal inscriptions without being sure where it was.
The answer turned out to be: deep in the Zagros, a long walled city that Mesopotamian expeditions never reached.
And here is the thing that matters.
Khuzestan and Fars were economically, politically, and culturally bound together across the Zagros for almost three thousand years.
Caravans linked them.
Royal inscriptions named kings "of Anshan and Susa" or "of Susa and Anshan" depending on which end of the country the king considered primary.
The same gods were worshipped in both halves.
The same language was spoken in both halves, though with regional variants.
Elam was a single civilization whose center kept swinging between a hot lowland plain that looked like Mesopotamia and a cold highland plateau that looked like the Iranian interior.
This is the peculiar structural feature of Elamite history.
No other major ancient Near Eastern kingdom had the same shape.
It also meant that Elam could not be conquered simply.
When an invader took the Khuzestan lowland, the Elamite kings retreated into the highland and waited.
When an invader tried to push past Susa into the Zagros, the mountains and the narrow passes and the Elamite archers did most of the defensive work for them.

Repeatedly across the next 2,500 years, Mesopotamian and later Assyrian armies would destroy Susa, would camp in Khuzestan, would proclaim Elam conquered — and then would leave, and the Elamite kings would come back down from Anshan and rebuild Susa and start over.
The two-Elam geography was Elam's most reliable strategic asset.
Now, the city of Susa.
Susa is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on Earth.
The earliest occupation at the site — a small village — dates to around 4200 BCE, making Susa older than almost every Sumerian city across the Tigris.

The one plausible exception is Eridu (EH-ree-doo), the southernmost Sumerian settlement, whose earliest village layers reach back further still (Mesopotamia Ch 2 calls Eridu the oldest city in the world).

Susa is older than Egyptian Memphis (though roughly contemporary with the Fayum A and Merimde communities on the Nile — Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 1), older than anything in the Indus Valley, older than Longshan China.

By the standards of "cities" as the next chapter of this book will use the word, early Susa wasn't one yet; it was a village that happened to sit on a particularly good spot where three river systems intersected and where the foothills of the Zagros met the plain.
But it grew.

By 4000 BCE, Susa had a substantial population, monumental buildings, painted pottery that is now one of the signature crafts of the Near Eastern Chalcolithic (the "Copper Age," roughly 4500–3300 BCE, the transitional period between stone-tool Neolithic farming and the full Bronze Age when tin-bronze replaced pure copper), and — most interestingly — tablets.
The Susa tablets from the end of the fourth millennium BCE are the Elamite contribution to the deep prehistory of writing.
They are written in a script that modern scholars call Proto-Elamite (not a name the Elamites would have recognized), and it is one of the oldest writing systems in the world.
It is also, frustratingly, still undeciphered.

The Proto-Elamite script first appears at Susa on tablets dating from roughly 3100 BCE, essentially contemporary with the earliest Sumerian "proto-cuneiform" tablets from Uruk a few hundred miles to the west (Mesopotamia Ch 3, ~3200 BCE) and not much behind the earliest Egyptian hieroglyphs (~3200 BCE) or the earliest Indus proto-signs at sites like Harappa (Indus Valley Ch 3, ~3300–2800 BCE).

The late fourth millennium BCE was the moment four different civilizations around the ancient world all started writing at roughly the same time — the specific Egyptian evidence being the Tomb U-j labels at Abydos, ca. 3250 BCE (Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 4) — and Proto-Elamite is the one that is still a mystery.
Nobody in the last 120 years of effort has managed to convincingly read it.

We can tell from context that the Proto-Elamite tablets are accounting documents — they record quantities of commodities, they use repeated sign patterns that look like numerals and grain measurements — but the actual words the tablets say, the actual language they encode, remain a mystery.
It is one of the great unsolved problems of ancient linguistics.
This is Elam's first pattern.
It will not be the last.
Elamite civilization developed its own writing system, repeatedly, from scratch, and two of those systems are still undeciphered today.
The Proto-Elamite script is the first one.
The Linear Elamite script of the next chapter is the second.

Both of them represent independent acts of writing invention — scripts that were not Sumerian, not Akkadian, not a borrowed copy of somebody else's cuneiform, but homegrown answers to the same accounting and administrative problems the Sumerians were solving with cuneiform next door.
That tells you something important about Elam that the rest of this book will keep returning to: the Elamites were never junior partners to Mesopotamia.
They were culturally peers, developing in parallel, repeatedly hitting the same civilizational milestones at about the same time in their own distinctive way.
Proto-Elamite writing coincides with the rise of Anshan as a major highland center.
The archaeology at Tall-i Malyan shows the city growing rapidly in the early third millennium BCE, reaching something like 500 acres of walled urban area by 2700 BCE.
That is enormous.
For comparison, Uruk, the famous early Sumerian city, covered about 600 acres at its peak (Mesopotamia Ch 3).

Anshan was in the same size class as the biggest Mesopotamian cities, and it was a thousand miles away from any other comparable urban center, up in a mountain basin nobody who didn't belong there could easily reach.
We also start hearing names.
The earliest Elamite political unit we can name is the Dynasty of Awan, a line of kings whose name comes from Sumerian king lists and later Mesopotamian historical references.

Awan (AH-wahn) was probably a specific city — the location is still debated, but the best guess is somewhere in the Zagros foothills between Khuzestan and Fars — and the Dynasty of Awan ruled some portion of Elamite territory starting around 2600 BCE.

The Sumerian King List (a much later Babylonian-era document purporting to list the legitimate kings of Mesopotamia from the creation onward) gives the Awan dynasty as rulers of Sumer itself at one point, which is almost certainly propaganda reflecting the fact that Elamite armies sometimes occupied Mesopotamian cities.
The historical reality was messier: Awan was an Elamite kingdom that occasionally projected force into lower Mesopotamia, and the Sumerians noticed.
The Awan kings worshipped, among others, a god who was about to become the characteristic deity of Susa and eventually of Elam as a whole: Inshushinak (in-SHOO-shee-nak).

The name Inshushinak probably means "Lord of Susa" in Elamite, and the god was the patron deity of the city — the one whose temple sat at the heart of the urban complex, whose festivals structured the ritual year, whose name the kings invoked when they swore oaths.

Inshushinak was a judge, a protector, and an underworld figure whose most important annual role was presiding over the ritual performed at the death of a king, where Elamite texts suggest the god received the departing soul and weighed the king's life.

The cult of Inshushinak at Susa is one of the oldest continuous religious traditions in the region — documented from around 2500 BCE, still alive at the time of the Assyrian destruction of Susa in 646 BCE, and traceable into the Achaemenid period as late as 500 BCE.
More than 2,000 years of worship of a single urban god in a single city.
Inshushinak is the Elamite answer to the question of what made Susa, Susa.
Trade is the other signature of early Elam, and trade is where the highland half of the country starts to really matter.

The Iranian plateau is rich in specific commodities the lowland Mesopotamian economies did not have. (The highland-lowland complementarity that defines Elam — Susa on the plain, Anshan in the mountains — is structurally identical to the Andean vertical archipelago model, where coast produces fish and cotton, highlands produce potatoes and llamas, and jungle produces coca.

Both civilizations were organized around connecting radically different ecological zones across short vertical distances — Early Andean Ch 1.) There is tin, the rarer metal you combine with copper to make bronze, the material that defines the entire Bronze Age; the main ancient source was the Badakhshan region in northern Afghanistan, and it reached Mesopotamia either via an overland caravan route through Elam or via a maritime route through the Harappan outpost of Shortughai (the Indus Valley colony in northern Afghanistan — Indus Valley Ch 7).
There is copper, mined at places like Tepe Sialk (another site in central Iran, not in Elam proper but on the trade route).

There is lapis lazuli (LAP-iss LAH-zoo-lee), the deep blue stone, prized across the ancient world for elite jewelry, found only in a few specific sources in Afghanistan and Central Asia, which had to be carried west through Iran to reach Mesopotamia and further — the same Shortughai maritime network and the overland Elamite caravan route were the two pathways.

There is carnelian, the orange-red semi-precious stone, from sources in eastern Iran and the Indus valley, most famously worked into long-drilled etched beads in the Harappan carnelian workshops at Chanhu-daro and Dholavira (Indus Valley Ch 7) and then shipped west through the same twin pathways.
There is turquoise, from sources in northeastern Iran.
There are mountain hardwoods that the timber-poor Mesopotamian plain could not grow.
There are horses and donkeys bred in the cooler highlands.
There are obsidian flakes traded down from sources much further north.
All of these goods moved through Elamite territory, and much of the wealth of Susa and Anshan for most of their history came from taxing the caravans and operating the trading stations.

By 2500 BCE, Elamite merchants are documented in Mesopotamian records as suppliers of metals and stones the Sumerian temples urgently needed — the same metals and stones that show up at this moment in Queen Puabi's headdress and the other Royal Tombs of Ur (Mesopotamia Ch 4), assembled from Badakhshani lapis, Harappan carnelian, and Anatolian/Iranian gold all routed through the trade network Elam sat on top of.

By 2100 BCE, the trade connection is dense enough that the language of commerce along the route includes bilingual Sumerian-Elamite glossaries — the earliest surviving evidence of professional translators working between two major ancient languages.

The Iranian highland trade routes running through Elam were the economic lifeline that let the cities of southern Iraq do the things they are famous for doing: building ziggurats (stepped mud-brick temple towers — the signature monumental architecture of Mesopotamian religion, which we'll meet in detail in Chapter 5 when Elam builds the biggest one of all at Chogha Zanbil), writing literature in cuneiform (the wedge-shaped writing system pressed into wet clay tablets that the Sumerians invented around 3200 BCE and that became the standard Near Eastern script for the next three thousand years), waging wars over canal access, supporting priests who spent their days on astronomy.
The bronze that those wars were fought with came from tin carried by caravan through Elamite territory.
This means that even in the periods when Elam was politically rivals with Mesopotamia, the two regions could never fully disengage.
Elam needed Mesopotamian finished goods, grain in lean years, prestige imports, and the Mesopotamian appetite for Elamite wealth to power its own elites.
Mesopotamia needed the tin, copper, and stones that only came through Elamite traders.
When Mesopotamian armies did destroy Susa, as will happen repeatedly in this book, they would then immediately have to negotiate with Elamite successor states to keep the metal flowing.
When Elamite kings raided lower Mesopotamia, they were partly punishing rivals and partly collecting tolls in advance.
The relationship was never a clean enemy relationship.
It was a complicated, permanently entangled, mutually dependent, mutually resentful intimacy that lasted for more than two thousand years.
The Elamite language itself deserves a paragraph, because it is one of the oddities of the whole ancient Near East.
Elamite belongs to no known language family.

It is not Semitic — that is, it is not in the family that includes Akkadian (the ancient Mesopotamian language of Sargon, Hammurabi, and the Assyrian kings — Mesopotamia Ch 5+), Hebrew, Arabic, or Aramaic (a Semitic language of the Syrian desert that would later become the administrative lingua franca of the Persian Empire and the everyday spoken language of much of the Near East, including the dialect Jesus spoke a thousand years later).

It is not Indo-European, the huge language family that branches out across Eurasia to include Persian, Sanskrit and the languages of India, Greek, Latin, the Germanic languages including English, and the Celtic and Slavic branches — an Indo-European speaker today in London is a distant linguistic cousin of an Indo-European speaker in Delhi, and both are descended from a proto-language that probably started somewhere in the Pontic-Caspian steppes north of the Black Sea around 4000 BCE.
Elamite is not related to any of that.
It is also not Hurrian (HOOR-ee-an), the language of the later Mitanni kingdom of northern Syria (which we'll meet in Ch 5).
It is not Kassite, the language of the dynasty that would rule Babylon in the Middle Bronze Age (which we'll meet in Ch 6).
And it is not Sumerian, which is itself in no known family.

Strikingly, three of the four major non-Semitic ancient Near Eastern languages — Sumerian, Elamite, and Kassite — are all language isolates with no demonstrated living relatives, a cluster of linguistic loners that modern linguists still cannot explain.

Elamite is what linguists call a language isolate, a language with no demonstrated living or dead relatives, standing alone, probably the last surviving member of a language family that had already lost its other members before writing was invented.
The closest proposal for a relative is a theory called "Elamo-Dravidian," which would link Elamite to the Dravidian language family of southern India (modern Tamil, Malayalam, Kannada).
The theory is based on thin structural parallels and has never been established.
It may be true.
It may not.
The professional consensus is that Elamite stands alone.

What we can say is that Elamite was a real, full-featured, agglutinative language — meaning that grammatical information is conveyed by stacking suffixes on nouns and verbs, the way Turkish and Finnish do today — and that it was the language of ordinary conversation across most of southwestern Iran for thousands of years.

When the Persian empire wrote its administrative records in Elamite in the fifth century BCE, it was using a language that had already been spoken in that region for at least 2,600 years before Cyrus the Great was born.
How much longer before that, we don't know, because the script to write it only got invented around 3100 BCE.
One old lowland city at Susa.
One big highland city at Anshan.
One trade-based economy moving Iranian metals west to Sumer.
One undeciphered script.
One god named Inshushinak whose cult lasted 2,000 years.
One language with no relatives.
By 2500 BCE, the kingdom is ready to meet its neighbor.
And its neighbor is in the middle of inventing the world's first empire.

chapters.title: Naram-Sin's Stone
sentences: The Akkadian Empire is one of the great turning points in world history, and it is mostly told from the Akkadian side — from the side of the conquerors.
This chapter tells the other side.
The Elamite side.

The side of the people who were on the receiving end of Sargon and Naram-Sin's new idea of imperial rule, who signed the first surviving political treaty we have in any language, who developed their own script in response, and whose reaction to the Akkadians is the thing that finally makes Elam visible in written history as a specific kingdom with specific kings.
Let me set the Akkadian moment briefly, for readers who have not read the Mesopotamia book alongside this one.

Around 2334 BCE, a man we now call Sargon of Akkad seized the throne of the Sumerian city of Kish, declared himself king, and over the next five and a half decades of his reign conquered virtually the entire alluvial plain of Sumer and pushed his armies outward in every direction.
Mesopotamia Chapter 5 tells this story in detail.

The short version is that Sargon created what most historians consider the first true empire in human history — a multi-ethnic, multi-city, centrally administered territory where conquered local rulers were replaced with appointed governors loyal to the Akkadian king, and where a new written language (Akkadian, the Semitic tongue of the conquerors, written in cuneiform) became the administrative standard alongside the older Sumerian.
It was a revolution in how political power worked at scale, and it created the basic template that every later Mesopotamian empire — Babylonian, Assyrian, Neo-Babylonian — would inherit.
From the Elamite side, the first question was: would the new imperial machinery reach across the Zagros?
The first answer was yes.
Sargon himself campaigned east into Elamite territory.

His royal inscriptions (cut into stone monuments intended to record his conquests for posterity, and preserved in Babylonian copies made centuries later) boast of capturing Awan, sacking Susa, and taking Elamite captives back to Akkad as slaves.
The details are thin — the inscriptions are short and the archaeology has only been excavated in patches — but the basic pattern is not in doubt.
Sargon's campaigns reached Susa, probably around 2300 BCE, and for at least a short period the Akkadian Empire considered Elam a subject province and collected tribute from it.

The specific Elamite kings who ruled during Sargon's reign are not named in surviving Elamite sources — the only contemporary royal names we have for Elam at this date come from Mesopotamian records, which used Akkadian transcriptions of Elamite names and which were written by the winners.
Then, at some point between Sargon and his grandson, somebody on one side or the other decided to write a treaty.
The Elamite-Akkadian Treaty, preserved on a clay tablet now in the Louvre and dated to roughly 2250 BCE, is one of the most remarkable documents in the entire ancient Near Eastern archive.
It is a treaty text.
It is bilingual.
It has specific named parties on each side.
It invokes specific gods as witnesses.

It is the oldest surviving text of a full political treaty in any language from any civilization, and the best-known later parallel — the famous Egypt–Hittite treaty between Ramesses II and the Hittite king Hattusili III, signed around 1259 BCE — is nearly a thousand years younger.
The treaty binds the Akkadian king (probably Naram-Sin, Sargon's grandson — we'll get to him in a moment) and an Elamite king named Khita.

It establishes a defensive alliance — the two parties promise not to attack each other and to come to each other's aid if a third party invades — and it invokes as witnesses more than thirty gods, including both the Akkadian pantheon (Enlil, Shamash, Ishtar) and the Elamite pantheon (Inshushinak, Humban, a goddess called Pinikir who we will meet again, and a group of Elamite deities whose names are otherwise unattested).

It is written in Akkadian, not Elamite — the Akkadians were the more prestigious diplomatic language at that moment, and Elamite royal scribes evidently knew how to write in it — and the treaty text specifies that the Elamite king swears his oath "in the name of" both the Akkadian and the Elamite gods, binding himself on both sets of divine authority.
Two things are worth noticing about this document.
The first is that it treats Elam as a sovereign equal of Akkad, not as a subject province.
The treaty is not a tribute imposition; it is a negotiated bilateral agreement between two kings who consider themselves peers.
This is strikingly different from the tone of Sargon's earlier conquest inscriptions, which describe Elam as smashed and subjugated.

In just one generation, the relationship had evolved from "Sargon sacks Susa" to "Naram-Sin and the king of Elam sign a mutual defense pact as equals." Either the Elamites had reasserted themselves quickly after Sargon's death, or Sargon's original claims had been exaggerated, or — most likely — both.
The second is that the treaty's invocation of the Elamite pantheon is our earliest real list of Elamite gods.
We already knew about Inshushinak.

Now we also have Humban (King of the Elamite gods, whom we'll meet properly in Chapter 3 along with the rest of the pantheon), the goddess Pinikir (an early sky goddess associated with Venus and later partly assimilated to the Mesopotamian Ishtar), and a handful of more obscure deities whose mythology is lost.
The treaty is not just a political document.
It is a window into Elamite religion, preserved because the Elamite king swore on his own gods to validate the agreement.

That a bilateral treaty exists at all, in 2250 BCE, between two kingdoms writing in a language borrowed from one of them and invoking the gods of both, is one of the most sophisticated political moves of the entire third millennium BCE.
Elam was playing on the same diplomatic field as Akkad, and playing well.
Then Naram-Sin broke the treaty.
Naram-Sin (pronounced nah-rahm-SEEN, sometimes "Naram-Suen" in older transliterations) was Sargon's grandson and the most famous of the Akkadian kings after Sargon himself.
His reign (roughly 2254–2218 BCE) was the high point of Akkadian imperial power.

Naram-Sin expanded the empire aggressively, campaigned in every direction, and is the first ruler in Mesopotamian history whose name is written with the divine determinative — the cuneiform sign prefix used to mark the names of gods.
Naram-Sin had himself deified while still alive, claiming to be a god rather than a mere king.

It was an unprecedented theological move, and it is the single most famous propaganda gesture of his reign. (The famous Victory Stele of Naram-Sin, now in the Louvre, shows him wearing the horned crown of a god and standing above his defeated enemies — the first surviving artistic depiction of a living monarch as divine, and a source we will be returning to when it turns up in an unexpected place in Chapter 6.) Somewhere during this reign, Naram-Sin's armies invaded Elam again.

The new campaign broke the treaty Naram-Sin himself had signed — or broke one his father Manishtushu had signed, depending on whose chronology is right — and resulted in the sack of Susa a second time.
Naram-Sin's royal inscriptions boast of capturing Elam and bringing back cedars, gold, and tribute.

This invasion also produced one of the most spectacular surviving Akkadian artworks: the Stele of Naram-Sin, that same Victory Stele we just mentioned, which was carved in commemoration of the conquest of a people called the "Lullubi" (an obscure mountain group in the northern Zagros, but close enough to Elam to be part of the same campaign cycle).
The stele itself is about six feet tall, cut from pink sandstone, carved with a scene showing Naram-Sin climbing a mountain at the head of his army while his enemies collapse and die.
It is a propaganda masterpiece, and the kind of object that an Akkadian king would erect in his capital to celebrate an imperial victory.
Where did the stele end up?
We will get there in Chapter 6.

But remember this: the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin was carved in the Akkadian capital around 2250 BCE, stood there for more than a thousand years as a trophy of Akkadian military supremacy, and is today in the Louvre in Paris.
The route from the Akkadian capital to the Louvre runs through Elam.
Through Susa.

Through a specific act of revenge that an Elamite king would commit in 1158 BCE, more than a thousand years after Naram-Sin's death, which would bring this stele — and Hammurabi's famous law code, and half a dozen other Mesopotamian royal monuments — to Susa to be stored as war trophies.

When European archaeologists started digging at Susa in the nineteenth century, they found those trophies still where the Elamite king had put them, and they packed them onto ships and sent them to Paris.
The Stele of Naram-Sin will travel a long way in this book.
But in Chapter 2, at the moment of its making, it is Naram-Sin's proclamation of Akkadian supremacy over a defeated Elam.
The Elamite response to Naram-Sin was one of the most characteristic moves in their whole history: they invented their own writing system.
We have already met the Proto-Elamite script in Chapter 1 — the undeciphered tablet accountancy system from around 3100 BCE.
By the time of Naram-Sin, Proto-Elamite had fallen out of use.
Susa and Anshan were using Mesopotamian cuneiform for accounting purposes, borrowed from their Sumerian and Akkadian trading partners.
But a new system was about to appear.
Around 2200 BCE, during or immediately after the Akkadian period, someone at Susa developed a new Elamite script known today as Linear Elamite.
It is a much more clearly "writing" system than Proto-Elamite was.

Linear Elamite has a distinct set of characters (about 80 signs, including both phonograms — signs representing sounds — and logograms — signs representing whole words), a clear left-to-right reading order, and a grammatical structure that looks like it encodes actual Elamite language rather than just commodity tallies.

It appears on a small number of inscriptions, almost all of them short royal dedication texts carved into stone statues or seals, and it has been one of the great unsolved problems of ancient linguistics for over a century.

Linear Elamite was almost entirely undeciphered until 2020, when a research team led by François Desset in France used a combination of proper-name analysis and cross-comparison with bilingual Akkadian-Linear Elamite inscriptions to produce a probable partial decipherment.

The Desset proposal is still being evaluated by other scholars, and it is not yet settled consensus, but the partial decipherment suggests that Linear Elamite was indeed encoding the Elamite language phonetically, with specific signs representing specific syllables.
If the decipherment holds, Linear Elamite is one of the most significant "new old texts" to be unlocked in the twenty-first century.

The man most closely associated with Linear Elamite is one of the most interesting figures in all of early Elamite history: Puzur-Inshushinak (POO-zer in-SHOO-shee-nak, a name that means "Refuge is Inshushinak"), the last great king of the Awan dynasty.
Puzur-Inshushinak ruled around 2100 BCE, several generations after Naram-Sin's death and during the collapse of the Akkadian Empire.

He saw his opportunity and took it — campaigning westward into a fragmented Mesopotamia, proclaiming himself "king of the four quarters of the world" (a title borrowed from Naram-Sin's own propaganda), and for a brief moment making Elam an imperial rival to the dying Akkadian state.

The surviving royal statues of Puzur-Inshushinak carry bilingual inscriptions in both Akkadian (the diplomatic prestige language) and Linear Elamite (the brand-new Elamite-specific script), and they are the single richest surviving corpus of Linear Elamite text.
Puzur-Inshushinak's brief imperial moment did not last.

By the time of his death or shortly after, the Akkadian Empire was done — destroyed by drought, internal revolts, and the invasions of the Gutians (a mountain people from the Zagros whose own origins are obscure, who briefly occupied Sumer around 2150 BCE and whom the Sumerians hated enough that the later Mesopotamian literary tradition made them into a byword for uncivilized barbarism).

In the power vacuum, several new dynasties emerged in Mesopotamia — including the famous Third Dynasty of Ur, which we will meet in a moment — and in Elam, the Awan dynasty was replaced by a new royal line called Shimashki.
Elam had survived the first Mesopotamian empire.
It had signed the first surviving treaty in world history.
It had invented a new script of its own in response.
And it had learned something permanent about the shape of the two-kingdom relationship: when Mesopotamia was strong, Elam signed treaties and paid tribute.
When Mesopotamia was weak, Elam invaded.
This pattern was going to repeat, in both directions, for the next two thousand years.
In the next chapter, Elam invades.

chapters.title: Ur Falls
sentences: Around 2004 BCE, give or take a year, an Elamite army walked into the city of Ur and burned it to the ground.
This matters a great deal.

Ur was at that moment the capital of the most sophisticated state Mesopotamia had yet produced — the Third Dynasty of Ur (conventionally abbreviated "Ur III" by modern scholars, pronounced "Ur the Third"), a kingdom that had emerged after the collapse of the Akkadian Empire to reunify Sumer under native Sumerian rule.

Ur III at its peak ruled the entire alluvial plain of southern Mesopotamia from roughly 2112 to 2004 BCE, with a bureaucracy so elaborate it produced tens of thousands of surviving clay tablets recording the daily movements of sheep, barley, oil, and labor across the kingdom.

Ur III is the period when Sumerian civilization reaches one of its cultural high points — the great ziggurat of Ur is built under Ur III's founding king Ur-Nammu, the famous Code of Ur-Nammu (the oldest surviving written legal code, three centuries older than Hammurabi's) is promulgated, and Sumerian as a literary and administrative language reaches something close to its classic form.
It is also, critically, the last moment in history when Sumerian was a major living language.
After Ur III falls, Sumerian becomes a dead scholarly language used only by priests and scribes, the way Latin became in medieval Europe.
The whole spoken Sumerian world ended with the fall of Ur in 2004 BCE.
And the people who ended it were Elamites.
The Ur III kingdom had been battling Elam on and off for its entire existence.
The founder, Ur-Nammu (ur-NAH-moo, ruled 2112–2094 BCE), waged war against Elamite territory and was killed in battle — not against Elam directly but against another enemy.
His son, Shulgi (SHOOL-gee, ruled 2094–2047 BCE, one of the great administrative geniuses of Sumerian history), spent substantial effort on the eastern frontier.

Shulgi built defensive walls at a site called Der (in modern eastern Iraq, near the Iranian border) to block the passes into Iran, and his daughters were married off to Elamite princes as part of a sustained diplomatic effort to keep Elam quiet.
For a while, it worked.

During Shulgi's long reign and his son's, Elam was contained and partly integrated — Elamite soldiers even served in Ur III's army as specialized archer units, and the Elamite kings of the Shimashki dynasty (the royal house that had replaced Awan after Puzur-Inshushinak's reign) sent tribute and princesses to the Sumerian court.
The Shimashki dynasty was a highland-centered royal line — the name probably comes from a region in the southern Zagros mountains, though the exact location is still debated.
They ruled Elam from roughly 2100 BCE until 1900 BCE, and they present the first clear example of the pattern we will see repeatedly throughout Elamite history: fraternal and avuncular succession.
The Shimashki kings did not simply pass power from father to son.

Instead, succession moved through a complex set of ranked offices — "grand regent," "viceroy of Elam," "viceroy of Susa" — with brothers, half-brothers, uncles, and nephews rotating through the offices in a pattern that kept the royal family tightly bound together as a collective.
We will see this pattern formalized two centuries later in the Sukkalmah period (Chapter 4), where it becomes the single most distinctive feature of Elamite government.
But it starts here, under the Shimashki, as an informal way of keeping multiple lines of the royal family invested in the kingdom's survival.
It is worth sitting with how unusual this is.

Most ancient kingdoms used some version of primogeniture or father-to-son succession — Egypt, Sumer, Babylon, the Hittites, most of Mesopotamia, the later dynasties of China, India, Persia after Cyrus.
The Elamite royal system is an outlier, but not a complete one.

The closest structural parallel in the ancient world is actually Shang dynasty China (Ancient China Ch 3, roughly 1600–1046 BCE), where the early Shang oracle bones record repeated brother-to-brother throne transfers rather than father-to-son inheritance.

The Shang and Elamite systems almost certainly developed independently — they were on opposite sides of Asia, with no documented contact — but both arrived at the same broad solution to the problem of what to do when a king dies and his son is a child.
Elsewhere, brothers and uncles rotated through top offices while a "grand king" sat at the top, and when a king died, his younger brother or his uncle's son might succeed him rather than his own son.

The advantages of the system were that it kept the royal clan unified and reduced the frequency of succession crises — no weak boy-kings, since there was always an adult male with experience ready to step up.

The disadvantage was that it prevented any single king from building a dynastic faction of his own, and it meant Elamite rulers were almost never able to pursue long-term personal projects the way a Ramesses II or a Shulgi could.
Elamite kingship was collective in a way that set it apart from the neighbors.
By the end of the third millennium, the Shimashki kings were done pretending to be tributary allies of Ur III.
The underlying cause of the break was probably the slow disintegration of Ur III itself.

The kingdom was running into trouble by around 2020 BCE — crops failing, population movements bringing new Semitic-speaking groups (the Amorites, Mesopotamia Ch 6) into the plain from the western desert, and the Ur III administrative system becoming brittle under the strain.

The last Ur III king, Ibbi-Sin (ruled 2028–2004 BCE), inherited a kingdom that was already unraveling, and his reign is recorded in the Ur III economic tablets as a period of rising grain prices, famine in the provinces, and lost cities.
Ibbi-Sin tried to hold the state together by concentrating power at Ur and firing distant governors, but the moves alienated his own bureaucracy and the provincial capitals started defecting.
The Elamite king Kindattu (of the Shimashki dynasty) saw the opportunity.
In 2004 BCE, he led an Elamite army west into Sumer, combined with allied troops from Susa and the highland regions, and attacked Ur directly.
Ibbi-Sin's defense collapsed.
The city fell, was looted, was burned, and Ibbi-Sin himself was captured and carried off to Anshan in chains.
He died there in exile.
We do not know what happened to him afterward, or where his body ended up.

The fall of Ur is one of the best-documented disasters in early Mesopotamian history, because within a few decades Sumerian priests wrote a long literary poem called the Lament for Ur (Mesopotamia Ch 6) commemorating it.

The Lament for Ur is the oldest surviving example of a specific literary genre called the "city lament" — a ritual poem mourning the destruction of a Mesopotamian city and blaming the gods for abandoning it to its enemies.

The Lament for Ur specifically blames the Elamites and another group called the Sua people (probably a Zagros mountain tribe allied with the Elamites) for the destruction, and the text is one of the great grief documents of the ancient Near East: the city is described as empty, the temples as desecrated, the people as slaughtered or enslaved, the streets as filled with unburied corpses, and the goddess Ningal (the tutelary deity of Ur) as weeping over the ruins of her house.

It is a deeply personal, deeply emotional piece of literature, and it ensures that the Elamite sack of Ur was remembered in Sumerian and Akkadian tradition as one of the defining disasters of the early second millennium BCE.
From the Elamite side, we have almost nothing.
No Elamite chronicle survives from the Shimashki period.

We do not know what Kindattu thought he was doing beyond the basic political move (strike a weakening rival, collect loot, eliminate a long-standing enemy), and we do not know how the Elamite court commemorated the victory.
The Elamite sack of Ur is known to us almost entirely through the eyes and grief of the losers.
But it mattered.
And it mattered in a particular way that would shape the next thousand years.
After Ur fell, Elam occupied Ur and the surrounding region for a short period — probably only a few years, certainly less than a generation — and then pulled back to Susa.

The Amorites, the Semitic-speaking newcomers from the western desert, flooded into the vacated Sumerian plain and established their own series of small kingdoms, one of which would eventually become Babylon.
Sumer as a political entity was over.
The cities remained, but they were now Amorite cities, Akkadian-speaking, slowly becoming Babylonian.
The Elamites had not intended to wipe Sumer off the map — they had intended to loot a rival and go home — but the accidental consequence of the raid was the end of Sumerian political history.
Inside Elam, the sack of Ur was the prestige event that cemented the Shimashki dynasty's claim to greatness.
The Elamite treasury was enriched.

The temple of Inshushinak at Susa received votive offerings marked as "spoils of Ur." And the practice of carrying Mesopotamian religious objects back to Susa as war trophies — taking the statues of the gods of the conquered city and installing them in Elamite temples — began as a policy here.

The Elamites also took, from the Sumerian city of Uruk during these same wars, the cult statue of the goddess Nanaya (nah-NAH-ya — an early Mesopotamian goddess of love and war, closely associated with Inanna/Ishtar), which would be installed in a Susa temple and would stay there for more than 1,600 years, long enough that an Assyrian king in the seventh century BCE would eventually fight a war to get it back (Chapter 7).

This practice of trophy-hostaging foreign gods will matter enormously in Chapter 6, when Shutruk-Nahhunte does the same thing to Babylon on a much larger scale, and again in Chapter 7 when Ashurbanipal reverses the whole accumulated game.
The Elamites also came home with something else: a deeper familiarity with Mesopotamian administrative culture, and in particular with cuneiform writing.
Before the Ur III wars, Elamite scribes at Susa had been using a mix of local traditions (Linear Elamite) and imported Mesopotamian cuneiform for specific purposes.

After the sack of Ur, the Elamite court made a decision: from this point forward, royal inscriptions, temple accounting, and official correspondence would use cuneiform as the standard script, adapted to write the Elamite language phonetically.

The adaptation is awkward — cuneiform is a script designed for Sumerian and Akkadian, two languages with very different sound systems from Elamite, and it never fits Elamite comfortably the way it fit Akkadian — but it is functional, and within a generation Elamite cuneiform becomes the dominant writing system in the kingdom.
Linear Elamite falls out of use entirely by around 1950 BCE and is not revived.
The decision to adopt Mesopotamian cuneiform for the Elamite language is one of the most consequential cultural moves in Elamite history.

It means the Elamite royal scribes were now trained in the same broad scribal tradition as their Mesopotamian counterparts — using the same basic clay tablet medium, the same wedge-shaped signs, the same literary references.
Elamite priests and diplomats could read Akkadian texts (which were everywhere in the Near East by 1800 BCE) without needing a separate linguistic training.
Elamite kings could send letters in Akkadian to other Near Eastern courts and receive replies.
The price of adoption was that Elam became more culturally porous to Mesopotamian influence.
The benefit was that Elam plugged into the broader cuneiform world of diplomacy, literature, and bureaucracy that was about to become the standard of the Near East for the next 1,500 years.
From this point forward, most of what we know about Elamite history comes from Elamite-language cuneiform inscriptions alongside the Mesopotamian sources.
We can finally read what Elamite kings said about themselves, in their own words — or at least, in their own language written in somebody else's script.
One thing that cuneiform Elamite lets us see clearly is the Elamite pantheon.
We have met Inshushinak already — the city-god of Susa, the judge of the dead.
He is not going anywhere.

But around the same time the Shimashki dynasty was adopting cuneiform, the surviving written sources start to fill in the rest of the Elamite divine family, and the Elamite pantheon turns out to be large, distinctive, and organized around a divine family structure that does not look like the Mesopotamian model it sits next to.
Humban (HOOM-bahn) was the king of the Elamite gods.

His name appears in royal inscriptions as the first deity invoked before any others, he is addressed as "the king" and "the great one," and his sanctuary at the city of Liyan (on the Persian Gulf coast, probably at modern Bushehr) was one of the major pilgrimage destinations in Elamite territory.
Humban's role is roughly equivalent to the Mesopotamian Enlil — the head-of-pantheon sky god who legitimizes human kingship.

His name became a theophoric element (a name-component invoking a god) in royal names for more than 1,500 years: Humban-Numena, Humban-Haltash, Humban-menanu, Humban-Nikash — all names in the form "Humban-does-X," meaning something like "Humban has given" or "Humban has established." The last Elamite king ever to bear a Humban-name appears in Achaemenid-era texts from the fifth century BCE, long after Elam itself was politically absorbed.
The cult of Humban survived the death of the kingdom that worshipped him.
Kiririsha (kee-ree-REE-shah, meaning "Great Lady") was the mother goddess of the Elamite pantheon and the consort of Humban.

Her cult center was also at Liyan, which suggests that Humban and Kiririsha were a paired divine couple whose worship came from the southern highland and Persian Gulf region rather than from Susa itself.

Kiririsha's role in royal inscriptions is to legitimize queens, to protect childbirth, and to receive offerings alongside her husband in the standard divine triad formulas that opened official Elamite texts.

Like most ancient mother goddesses, her iconography often blurred with fertility, agriculture, and the general feminine principle, but her specific Elamite role — co-ruler of the divine family alongside Humban — is distinctive.
Nahhunte (NAH-hoon-tay, meaning "creator of the day") was the sun god — the Elamite equivalent of Mesopotamian Shamash (Mesopotamia Ch 4).

Nahhunte was the divine judge, presiding over oaths and legal disputes, and his name became the second great theophoric element in royal names: Shutruk-Nahhunte, Kutir-Nahhunte, Hallutash-Inshushinak (whose name combines Inshushinak with a different element).

The Elamite sun god and the Mesopotamian sun god probably had some cultural cross-pollination — both are judges, both are associated with the rising sun and with royal legitimacy — but they had distinct names, distinct myths, and distinct priesthoods.
The Elamite kings who took Nahhunte into their royal names were declaring themselves servants of the judge, protectors of oaths and law.
Inshushinak, we already know.
Lord of Susa, judge of the dead.
Pinikir (PIN-ee-kir) is a more puzzling figure — the great sky goddess who appears in the Elamite-Akkadian treaty of Chapter 2 and whose worship is documented from as early as 2100 BCE.

Pinikir was associated with the planet Venus and with the fertility functions that Mesopotamian Inanna/Ishtar also covered, and at some point during the second millennium BCE Pinikir's cult was partly absorbed into or merged with the Mesopotamian Ishtar cult, so that later Elamite texts use the two names interchangeably in some contexts.
Pinikir is probably the older layer — a pre-Mesopotamian Elamite goddess whose identity got complicated by the cultural traffic between Susa and Sumer.
That is the core of the Elamite divine family: Humban at the top, Kiririsha his consort, Nahhunte the sun-judge, Inshushinak the patron of Susa, Pinikir the sky-goddess.
