Dozens of lesser deities fill out the pantheon, many of them local city-gods whose worship was confined to specific towns.

The whole system is organized around a family structure (royal father, royal mother, children, specialized functional gods) that has loose parallels to Mesopotamian pantheons but is clearly its own distinct thing.
The gods have Elamite names.
Their stories, to the extent that we can reconstruct them from scraps of ritual text, are Elamite stories.
Whatever the Elamites were, they were not a cultural satellite of Mesopotamia.
They had their own theology, and it was old.
By 1900 BCE or so, the Shimashki dynasty was gone, replaced by a new royal line whose distinctive government we are about to meet.
The Elamite sack of Ur was a hundred years in the past.
Susa had been rebuilt bigger.
The cuneiform adoption was complete.
The pantheon was crystallizing.
The long imperial struggle with Mesopotamia had entered its classical middle period.
And a family called the Epartids was about to invent a political system unlike anything else in the ancient world.

chapters.title: Grand Regents
sentences: Around 1850 BCE, a man named Eparti I (eh-PAHR-tee) founded the royal dynasty that would rule Elam for the next 350 years.

His title, and the title of all his successors, was one of the strangest in all of ancient Near Eastern diplomacy: sukkalmah (SOOK-kahl-mah), meaning "grand regent" or literally "great vizier." The word "regent" is usually a red flag in historical writing.
A regent is, in most political systems, a placeholder — someone who holds power on behalf of a real king who is too young or too distant to rule personally.
The sukkalmah was different.
The Elamite sukkalmah was the real king.
He ruled in his own name, made treaties in his own name, waged wars in his own name, and commissioned royal inscriptions in his own name.
The title sukkalmah did not mean he was a stand-in for anyone.

It meant that the Elamite royal ideology refused to use the ordinary Mesopotamian vocabulary for kingship (lugal, sharrum) and instead used a political vocabulary inherited from the Mesopotamian bureaucratic hierarchy, where "sukkal" was a specific high office meaning something like "vizier" or "minister." The Elamite move was to take the idea of a minister — a trusted servant of the real king — and elevate it to the highest royal office, so that the Elamite ruler was ceremonially a "servant" of the gods rather than a "king." This is a genuinely interesting political-theology move, and it is worth spending a paragraph on.
In most ancient Near Eastern monarchies, the king claimed to be the direct earthly representative of the chief god (or, as with Naram-Sin, actually a god himself).
The king was above all mortals in rank, and below only the heavenly powers.
The Elamite sukkalmah presented himself as a servant — the chief servant — of the divine family headed by Humban, Kiririsha, and Inshushinak.
His power was delegated downward from the gods, not assumed directly.

This created a subtle but important rhetorical gap between the Elamite monarch and the Mesopotamian one: an Elamite sukkalmah could legitimately be constrained by the priesthood, by tradition, and by the expectations of the divine patrons in ways that a Mesopotamian lugal (who was closer to a god-king) could not.

The Elamite monarchy was, in its self-presentation, more humble than the Mesopotamian alternative, and the monarchy's authority flowed from the gods through the king to the people, with room for priests and family members to mediate at every step.
Along with the humble title came the most distinctive feature of Elamite government in the Sukkalmah period: the three-office ruling structure.
The Sukkalmah dynasty ran Elam through three formal offices, ranked vertically: Sukkalmah — the grand regent, at the top.
Ruled the whole kingdom.
Sat at Susa or sometimes at Anshan.
Sukkal of Elam and Simashki — the second-ranking official, responsible for the mountain regions, usually a brother or uncle of the grand regent.
Sukkal of Susa — the third-ranking official, responsible for the lowland city of Susa itself and the surrounding Khuzestan plain, usually a nephew or younger brother.
The three offices were held simultaneously by three different members of the same royal family.

When the sukkalmah died, the sukkal of Elam and Simashki was promoted to sukkalmah, the sukkal of Susa was promoted to sukkal of Elam and Simashki, and the next available adult male (usually a younger brother or a nephew) was installed in the vacant sukkal of Susa office.
Succession was horizontal before it was vertical.
A single generation of brothers would rotate through the three offices — each one holding each office in sequence — before the next generation took over.
The practical effect of this system was that Elamite royal authority was collective rather than individual.
No single sukkalmah could rule as a personal autocrat, because he had two close relatives also holding formal authority and also members of the royal family with legitimate succession claims.
Major policy decisions required consensus among the three offices.
Regional administration in Elam and Susa was handled by the sukkals of each region, who operated with substantial local autonomy.
The sukkalmah at the top was a coordinator of the royal family rather than an all-powerful king.
This is the opposite of how Mesopotamian kingship worked.
It is also, as far as I can tell, unlike any other political system in the ancient Near East.
Egypt had one pharaoh at a time.

The Hittites (in Anatolia, around 1600 BCE onward, the first great Indo-European-speaking power in the ancient Near East) had one Great King, though with considerable powers delegated to subordinate rulers of the empire's subject kingdoms.
The Assyrians had one sharru, always.
Babylonia had one king.
The Persians, later, would have one king of kings.
Elam had three sukkals at once, rotating, none of them claiming ultimate authority individually.
Why it worked is a good question.

Part of the answer is that the collective structure prevented the dynastic civil wars that destroyed many ancient monarchies — there was always an adult male ready to step up, and there was no single successor to be assassinated.

Part of the answer is that the structure gave the two major regions of the kingdom (the highland Elam/Simashki and the lowland Susa) their own direct royal representation at the top, which kept both halves of the two-Elam country politically engaged.
Part of the answer is just that it fit Elamite royal culture, which seems to have valued kin solidarity over individual glory more strongly than most of its neighbors did.
The practical result was that Sukkalmah Elam was, for most of the three hundred years of its duration, one of the most politically stable kingdoms in the Near East.

While Mesopotamia was going through a dozen dynastic crises, Amorite invasions, and the rise and fall of Isin, Larsa, and early Babylon (Mesopotamia Chapters 6–7), Elam under the Sukkalmah just kept running the way it had always run.
The same royal family, the same three-office structure, the same pantheon, the same scribal traditions, the same economy based on highland-lowland trade.
Elam in the Sukkalmah period is a quiet kingdom, and the quiet is the result of the government working.
The Sukkalmah period is also the moment when Elam starts showing up as a peer power in the diplomatic correspondence of the Mesopotamian great kings.

The richest surviving archive of early second-millennium BCE Near Eastern diplomacy is the Mari letters — a hoard of roughly 25,000 clay tablets excavated from the royal palace of the city of Mari in modern Syria, spanning roughly 1810–1760 BCE.
Mari was a middle-rank kingdom on the middle Euphrates, and its last king, Zimri-Lim, kept careful archives of the correspondence between his court and the other Near Eastern powers.

When Hammurabi of Babylon conquered Mari in 1759 BCE, the palace was burned and the tablets were baked hard by the fire, accidentally preserving them for three thousand seven hundred years until French archaeologists excavated them in the 1930s.
The Mari letters are the best single window into how the Near Eastern great powers of the early second millennium BCE actually talked to each other.
In the Mari letters, Elam — that is, the Sukkalmah Elam of the early eighteenth century BCE — appears as one of the top-ranked international powers.
Zimri-Lim of Mari writes to the Elamite sukkalmah addressing him in formal diplomatic language, using the respect-ranked kinship terms that Near Eastern diplomats used to signal relative power.

In these terms, a king whom you called "father" was your superior (not in actual kinship but in the diplomatic ranking), a king whom you called "brother" was your equal, and a king whom you called "son" was your inferior.

Zimri-Lim addressed the Elamite sukkalmah as "father," and the correspondence from Babylon during the same period shows a similar deference — Hammurabi's own officials writing to the Elamite court in the register of diplomatic subordination.

Both Mari and early Hammurabi-era Babylon considered the Elamite ruler their diplomatic superior, not merely an equal but an elder statesman at the top of the regional hierarchy. (The exact wording Hammurabi personally used in direct correspondence with the sukkalmah is harder to pin down from the surviving tablets — most of what we have is Mari's archive, not Babylon's — but the overall power relationship is unmistakable: Hammurabi's court bowed, in formal diplomatic terms, to Susa.) This is astonishing, and it goes against every later historical tradition about this period.
The later Babylonian and Assyrian literature always presents Elam as a lesser power, a frontier kingdom, an occasional nuisance that Mesopotamian armies dealt with when necessary.
The Mari letters, written at the time rather than after the fact, show something very different.
In 1770 BCE, the Elamite sukkalmah was the most senior Near Eastern monarch, acknowledged as such by Hammurabi himself before Hammurabi became the Hammurabi we remember.
The Sukkalmah dynasty was at that moment the diplomatic center of the ancient Near East, and everyone from the Zagros to the Mediterranean coast was looking to Susa for leadership.

The specific Elamite sukkalmah whose correspondence dominates the Mari letters is a man named Siwe-palar-hupak (SEE-way pah-lar HOO-pak — the name is an Elamite sentence that probably means "Servant of Hupak has extended power," but the exact etymology is debated).

Siwe-palar-hupak ruled as sukkalmah from roughly 1778 to 1745 BCE, and in that time he ran an aggressive Elamite foreign policy: he campaigned into Mesopotamia repeatedly, pressured the smaller kingdoms of the middle Euphrates, arranged political marriages to strengthen his influence, and at one point came very close to unifying the entire Near East under Elamite hegemony.
His coalition partners included Mari's Zimri-Lim, several smaller Assyrian and Syrian kingdoms, and for a while even Babylon — until Hammurabi switched sides.
Hammurabi's switch is one of the decisive moments of early second-millennium BCE history, and it is told in exquisite detail in Mesopotamia Chapter 7 from the Babylonian side.

The short version: Hammurabi had been an Elamite client for the first fifteen years of his reign, acknowledging Siwe-palar-hupak as his diplomatic "father." Around 1764 BCE, Hammurabi decided to break free, switched his alliances, and began waging the wars that would eventually conquer Mari, Elam, Assur, and most of Mesopotamia — the wars that created the Old Babylonian Empire we remember him for.
In the process, Hammurabi personally defeated Siwe-palar-hupak in battle, sacked one of the Elamite frontier fortresses, and forced the Sukkalmah dynasty to pull back behind Susa.
The Elamite supremacy was over.
A new Babylonian supremacy replaced it.
But — and this is important — Hammurabi did not destroy Elam.
He did not sack Susa.
He did not break the Sukkalmah government.
He pushed Elam out of Mesopotamia and back to its two-kingdom highlands-and-lowlands base, and then he accepted Elam as a subordinate but still-sovereign neighbor on his eastern frontier.
The Sukkalmah dynasty continued to rule Elam for another two hundred years after Siwe-palar-hupak's death.
Hammurabi's concern had been consolidating Babylon's position in Mesopotamia, not eliminating Elam as a civilization.

The two kingdoms reverted to the old mutually-suspicious mutual-dependency relationship, with Elam slightly diminished and Babylon slightly enlarged but both still recognizable as peer Near Eastern great powers.
One more thing worth noting about Sukkalmah Elam before we move on: it is the first kingdom in Elamite history from which we have substantial quantities of ordinary people's documents.

Up until the Sukkalmah period, most of what we know about Elam comes from royal inscriptions — the kind of boastful, formulaic texts that kings carved into stone to celebrate their conquests and building projects.
Royal inscriptions are useful but heavily biased; they tell you what the king wanted posterity to know, not what ordinary life was like.

Under the Sukkalmah, something changes: the archaeological record at Susa starts producing large quantities of legal contracts, drawn up in cuneiform on clay tablets, recording sales of slaves, marriages, inheritances, loans, and lawsuits between ordinary middle-class residents of the city.
These contracts are the best window we will ever have into Elamite daily life.

They show that Susa in 1700 BCE was a cosmopolitan urban center with a population of several tens of thousands, a substantial middle class of merchants and craftsmen, a functional court system that heard disputes between private parties, a legal culture in which witnesses and seals and formal contracts mattered, and a level of literacy high enough that ordinary people were regularly generating and preserving written legal documents.

The contracts are in a specific Elamite legal tradition that has some parallels to contemporary Babylonian law (the Code of Hammurabi era) but also its own distinctive features — particularly around marriage and inheritance, where Elamite women seem to have had more direct legal agency than their Babylonian counterparts.

Elamite contracts show wives owning property in their own names, initiating legal disputes, inheriting from parents on equal terms with brothers, and occasionally serving as witnesses for other people's contracts.

This is not a modern gender-equal society by any stretch — slavery exists, patriarchal structures exist, and most political offices are male-only — but it is a society in which women have more legal visibility than most of their contemporaries.
The Sukkalmah period, in short, is Elam as a functioning civilization.
Stable government.
Peer diplomacy.
Rich middle class.
Legal culture.
Distinct pantheon.
Functional writing system.
The whole package.
It is, in a lot of ways, the moment when Elam is most fully itself — not at war, not collapsing, not being destroyed by an invader, just quietly running the way it had designed itself to run.
By 1500 BCE the Sukkalmah dynasty was coming to an end, replaced by a new royal line called the Kidinuid and then by the Igihalkid dynasty.
The quiet Elam of the Sukkalmah was about to become the aggressive building-boom Elam of the Middle Elamite period.
And the most visible legacy of that new era is still standing in the desert of Khuzestan — a brick ziggurat the size of a small mountain.

chapters.title: The Ziggurat Generation
sentences: Around 1250 BCE, on a dry plain in southwestern Iran about 25 miles (40 km) southeast of Susa, an Elamite king ordered the construction of a religious complex so ambitious that it required the founding of an entirely new city around it.

The city was called Dur-Untash — "Fortress of Untash," named for the king who built it — and at the heart of the city was a stepped mud-brick tower called a ziggurat, a specifically Mesopotamian architectural form that was by 1250 BCE already fifteen centuries old as a construction tradition.
The ziggurat at Dur-Untash was not a new invention.
It was a statement.
The statement was: Elam can do Mesopotamian architecture too, and bigger.

The ziggurat at Dur-Untash is today called the Ziggurat of Chogha Zanbil (CHOH-gah ZAHN-beel — the modern Persian name for the site, meaning "basket-shaped mound"), and it is one of the most remarkable preserved monuments in the whole ancient Near East.
It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
It is the largest ziggurat standing anywhere in the world today, larger than any ziggurat still preserved in Mesopotamia itself.

It is also effectively the last great ziggurat of the ancient world — a handful of later, smaller Mesopotamian ziggurats exist, but Chogha Zanbil is generally considered the final flowering of the tradition on a monumental scale.
Nobody after the twelfth century BCE bothered to build one this big again.

And it is worth noting the global context: while Untash-Napirisha was finishing Chogha Zanbil around 1250 BCE, Ramesses II was carving the colossal rock-cut temples at Abu Simbel into the cliffs of Nubia (Ancient Nubia Ch 7), New Kingdom Egypt and Middle Elamite Elam staging parallel monument-building competitions at opposite ends of the Bronze Age Near East in almost exactly the same generation.

On the far side of the world, the Peruvian coast's U-shaped temple tradition was in full flower at roughly the same date (~1500–800 BCE), with Garagay, Cardal, and the Huaca de los Reyes rising independently on the Pacific coast (Early Andean Ch 5).
And the king who built it is the figure who dominates the entire Middle Elamite period: Untash-Napirisha (oon-TAHSH nah-pee-REE-shah), ruled roughly 1275–1240 BCE.
Before we walk into his ziggurat, a quick note on what had changed politically.
The quiet stability of the Sukkalmah period we just left ended around 1500 BCE.
The Sukkalmah dynasty fell.

A transitional period called the Kidinuid dynasty ruled briefly in the fifteenth century BCE (named for a king called Kidin-Khutran — the records are thin), followed by a long period of ambiguous rule and diminished international visibility while the rest of the Near East was absorbed in the great power politics of Egypt's New Kingdom, the Hittite Empire, and the rising Middle Assyrian state (Mesopotamia Ch 8).
From roughly 1500 to 1400 BCE, Elam mostly disappears from the diplomatic record.

The internal archaeology shows the kingdom kept functioning — Susa kept being rebuilt, the pantheon kept being worshipped, the Elamite cuneiform scribal tradition kept producing contracts — but the external projection of power dropped to near zero.

Then, around 1400 BCE, a new royal line called the Igihalkid dynasty (named for its founder, Ige-halki — the name may mean something like "he for whom Igi is a blessing") emerged from the highland regions of Elam and began rebuilding the kingdom's external ambitions.

The Igihalkid kings of the fourteenth century BCE restored the Elamite military, refortified Susa, rebuilt temples across the kingdom, and started cultivating the sort of aggressive foreign-policy posture that would culminate in the great sack of Babylon in Chapter 6.

The Igihalkid period is the Middle Elamite period proper, and it is the era when Elam goes from being a recovering provincial kingdom to being one of the three or four great powers of the Late Bronze Age Near East — the others being New Kingdom Egypt, the Hittite Empire (the Indo-European-speaking kingdom of central Anatolia, modern Turkey, whose armies fought Ramesses II at Kadesh), Kassite Babylonia (the dynasty that had taken over Babylon after Mursili I's 1595 BCE sack, ruling southern Mesopotamia for nearly four centuries using a non-Semitic language whose affinities we still can't pin down — the third big language isolate in the Bronze Age Near East, Mesopotamia Ch 8), and the Middle Assyrian state (a predecessor of the famous Neo-Assyrian empire of Chapter 7, already aggressive but not yet the terrifying regional dominator it would become).
Elam of the Igihalkid period is playing in the same league as the kingdoms Ramesses II and the later Assyrian royal founders would rule.
Untash-Napirisha was the greatest of the Igihalkid kings.

His name means roughly "Untash is the Great God" — "Untash" being a personal name element and "Napirisha" being the name of a specific Elamite god, one of the highland deities who would later be absorbed into the broader pantheon.

Untash-Napirisha came to the throne around 1275 BCE as the grandson of earlier Igihalkid kings, inherited a relatively stable kingdom, and used the stability to launch the most ambitious building program in Elamite history.
At the center of that building program was Dur-Untash and its ziggurat.
A ziggurat (ZIG-uh-rat) is a stepped tower made of mud brick, usually with a small temple at the top and ritual platforms on the lower levels.
The form was invented in Sumer in the late fourth millennium BCE and evolved over the next two thousand years into one of the signature monumental architectures of ancient Mesopotamia.

The great ziggurat at Ur, built by Ur-Nammu around 2100 BCE, is the most famous surviving example; others were built at Babylon (the "Tower of Babel" of the Hebrew Bible), at Nippur, at Eridu, and at dozens of other Mesopotamian cities.

By the Middle Elamite period, ziggurats had been a standard feature of Mesopotamian religious architecture for more than 1,500 years, and Elamite religious sites had been importing the form for almost as long.
What made Chogha Zanbil different was the scale and the ambition of Untash-Napirisha's conception.

The ziggurat rises in five stepped levels — though today only the first two or three levels survive, weathered down from the original height — to an estimated original height of about 174 feet (53 meters), or roughly a fifteen-story building.
The base is a square approximately 345 feet (105 meters) on each side.
The total volume of baked and sun-dried brick used in construction has been estimated at roughly 300 million bricks, each one stamped with Untash-Napirisha's name.

That figure may be an overestimate, but the brick stamps are genuine: archaeologists working at the site from 1951 onward have recovered hundreds of individual bricks carrying the same royal inscription, identifying the builder and dedicating the building to the god Inshushinak.
The dedication is important.
The ziggurat was not dedicated to Humban, the king of the gods; it was dedicated to Inshushinak, the lord of Susa, despite being built at Dur-Untash rather than at Susa itself.

Untash-Napirisha was trying to create a new cult center that would be specifically for the Elamite highland-lowland synthesis, with Inshushinak (the lowland patron) at the top and Humban (the highland patron) honored in the surrounding temple precincts.

Surrounding the ziggurat was the rest of the religious complex: eleven subsidiary temples dedicated to other gods of the Elamite pantheon, a royal palace, a sacred enclosure marked off from the rest of the city by three concentric walls, water-supply channels bringing fresh water from the Karkheh River seven miles away, and a small residential quarter for priests and palace staff.

Dur-Untash was conceived as a pilgrimage city rather than a commercial center, and at its founding it probably had a resident population of only a few thousand people — administrators, priests, temple servants, and royal retinue — with the main population only arriving for major religious festivals.
The most striking thing about Chogha Zanbil for a modern visitor is not the ziggurat itself, impressive as it is.
It is the quality of the brickwork.

The walls of the ziggurat are faced with fired (rather than sun-dried) brick on the outer surfaces, with the internal core built of sun-dried brick, and the face bricks are inscribed with the royal name in repeated rows.

This is extraordinarily expensive construction for the era — fired brick requires fuel and kilns and skilled labor on a much larger scale than sun-dried brick — and it means the ziggurat's outer surfaces were designed to last.

Where the weathered Mesopotamian ziggurats are now mostly unrecognizable mounds of eroded mud, Chogha Zanbil still has substantial sections of its original fired-brick face intact, preserving the royal inscriptions and letting modern archaeologists read them directly off the monument itself.
The site was rediscovered in 1935 by an oil surveyor named Browne, who was flying over southwestern Iran looking for petroleum prospects and noticed an unnatural square mound on the horizon.
Full excavation began in 1951 under the French archaeologist Roman Ghirshman and continued through the 1970s, interrupted by the Iran-Iraq War, and has resumed periodically since.

The site has survived the vagaries of twentieth-century politics remarkably well — it is protected today as a national monument — and its designation as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979 was the first Iranian site to receive UNESCO status.
For a brief moment in the Middle Elamite period, a king of Elam had commissioned the biggest religious monument anywhere in the ancient Near East outside Egypt.
Three thousand years later, his ziggurat is still standing, still recognizable, still bearing his name on every brick of its outer face.

One more object from Untash-Napirisha's reign deserves its own section, because it is arguably the single most impressive artifact that has ever come out of Elamite territory: the Bronze Statue of Queen Napir-Asu.
Queen Napir-Asu (nah-PEER AH-soo) was the wife of Untash-Napirisha.

The bronze statue, excavated at Susa in 1903 by French archaeologists, depicts her standing formally in court dress, with her hands folded in front of her body and her skirt rendered in intricate incised patterns imitating textile weave.
The figure is life-sized — about four and a half feet tall (1.4 meters) — and the head has been broken off and lost at some point in antiquity, so we are looking at a headless statue today.
Everything surviving below the neckline is intact and in remarkable condition.
What makes the Napir-Asu statue extraordinary is not the artistic quality, though the artistic quality is very high.
It is the weight.
The statue is solid bronze.
Not hollow, not cored, not a shell with internal supports.
Solid all the way through.
It weighs 3,760 pounds (1,750 kg) — nearly two tons of bronze cast in a single piece.

To appreciate what this means, you need to understand that casting solid bronze in a single piece on that scale is an extremely difficult metallurgical achievement that most ancient civilizations never attempted.
The largest surviving Egyptian bronze statues are smaller and were cast in multiple pieces welded together afterward.
The famous Greek bronze sculptures of the Classical period (the Charioteer of Delphi, the Riace Warriors) were all hollow-cast over internal cores.
Hollow casting saves enormous amounts of metal and is structurally much easier.

Solid casting on the Napir-Asu scale is a display of raw metallurgical dominance — the kind of thing you do specifically to impress visitors with how much bronze you can afford to waste on a single object.
The direct contemporary counterpart in the ancient world is across all of Asia, in the Shang dynasty bronze tradition of central China (Ancient China Ch 3).

Within a century of Napir-Asu's casting at Susa, Shang bronze workers at Anyang were producing the enormous ritual bronze vessels known as ding (DING), including the famous Houmuwu Ding cast around 1200 BCE for the tomb of the consort of a Shang king.

The Houmuwu Ding is a rectangular four-legged cauldron weighing 1,832 pounds (832 kg) — about half the weight of the Napir-Asu statue, but cast using a completely different "piece-mold" technique in which the mold itself was assembled from multiple interlocking clay sections before the pour.

The Shang bronze workers and the Elamite bronze workers had almost certainly no idea the other tradition existed, and they arrived at the same problem (how do you cast huge amounts of bronze in a single piece?) and solved it in different ways, at opposite ends of Asia, in the same century.

The mid-second millennium BCE is the first moment in history when two completely unrelated civilizations are casting massive solid-cast bronze at the same time, and the Napir-Asu and Houmuwu Ding are the two objects that prove it.
Why was it cast solid?
The most plausible explanation is religious.
The statue's inscription, carved in Elamite cuneiform around the lower part of the skirt, calls down curses on anyone who attempts to destroy or damage the figure.
Solid casting would make the statue effectively impossible to melt down and reuse — no thief with a hammer and a crucible could extract the metal easily.
The solidity was a defense against desecration.
The queen wanted her image in the temple of Inshushinak to outlast any political misfortune that might tempt later kings to recycle the bronze for their own projects.
She almost succeeded.

The statue did survive the sack of Susa in the eighth century BCE, the destruction by Ashurbanipal in 646 BCE, the Achaemenid absorption, the Alexandrine conquest, the Parthian and Sasanian periods, the Arab conquest, and the medieval Islamic period, lying buried in the rubble of the temple it had been installed in.
Only the head was lost at some point along the way, probably broken off by a looter who could not carry the whole figure.
French archaeologists found it and moved it to the Louvre, where it has been on display ever since as one of the signature pieces of the museum's Near Eastern collection.
If you visit the Louvre, you can stand next to the headless queen of Elam and look at her at close range.
The inscription is still legible around the base of her skirt.
The bronze has weathered to a dark green patina.
Her folded hands are still visible.

The statue is one of the very few surviving Late Bronze Age artworks that gives a modern viewer a direct sense of what elite Near Eastern kingship actually looked like — not as a ruined mound or a broken fragment, but as a nearly intact representation of a specific named queen standing at the center of her temple, solid as metal.
Untash-Napirisha died around 1240 BCE, having spent his reign on Chogha Zanbil, the Napir-Asu statue, and the restoration of major temples at Susa and Anshan.

He was succeeded by a series of Igihalkid kings whose specific contributions are less visible archaeologically — the building boom slowed, Dur-Untash was already complete, and the royal focus shifted to foreign policy rather than monument construction.
What the building boom did leave behind, and what matters for the next chapter, is the visible evidence of Elam as a Late Bronze Age superpower.

The scale of Chogha Zanbil, the solid-bronze casting of the Napir-Asu statue, the fortification of Susa under Untash-Napirisha, and the building programs at Anshan and the smaller Elamite cities together represent a kingdom at its peak of wealth, confidence, and organizational capacity.
The Igihalkid dynasty of the thirteenth century BCE was running an Elam that was architecturally, economically, and militarily capable of anything its Mesopotamian neighbors could do.
And by the twelfth century BCE, the Elamite kings decided to prove it.

chapters.title: Sack of Babylon
sentences: In 1158 BCE, the Elamite king Shutruk-Nahhunte (shoo-trook nah-HOON-tay) led an army west across the Zagros passes, crossed the Tigris River, and marched into Babylonia.
The Babylonian king at the time was Zababa-shuma-iddin, a late Kassite ruler whose dynasty had been weakening for a generation.
The Babylonian army met Shutruk-Nahhunte in the field, was defeated, and Babylon itself fell to Elamite assault.
The city was looted in a systematic, methodical way that made clear the Elamites had been planning this for years.
Temples were opened.
Royal palaces were stripped.
The famous cedar-wood processional barge of the god Marduk was seized.
Statues, stelae, and monuments were pulled down and loaded onto carts.

Then the whole convoy of war trophies — one of the greatest hauls of looted art in the entire ancient world — was dragged east over the Zagros passes back to Susa, where the Elamite king installed the trophies in his own temples as votive offerings to Inshushinak and as permanent displays of Elamite supremacy.
Among the trophies were two objects we have already met in this book.

The first was the Code of Hammurabi — the tall black diorite stele carved around 1754 BCE with the famous legal code and Hammurabi's portrait at the top receiving the laws from the sun god Shamash, one of the most famous artifacts in all of Mesopotamian archaeology.

The second was the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin, Sargon's grandson's victory monument from Chapter 2, carved around 2250 BCE showing the Akkadian king in divine regalia climbing a mountain at the head of his army.
Both objects had been sitting in the religious and palace districts of lower Mesopotamia for centuries when Shutruk-Nahhunte's army arrived, and both were among the most important pieces he took.
Both traveled to Susa on Elamite carts and were erected in the Elamite temple precinct as displays of the king's new prestige.
They would stay there for the next three thousand years.
Shutruk-Nahhunte is the most famous Elamite king, and the reason he is famous is the sack of Babylon.

But he was also a genuinely capable ruler who came to the throne around 1185 BCE at the head of a new royal line called the Shutrukid dynasty, which had displaced the older Igihalkid line through a palace coup or an inheritance crisis we can only partly reconstruct.

Shutruk-Nahhunte's reign represents Elam's last great imperial moment before the long collapse that will dominate the next several chapters, and it is the single clearest example in all of Elamite history of the kingdom projecting power outward across its entire neighborhood on the scale of a first-tier Near Eastern empire.
A note on the Shutrukid royal names is worth pausing on, because the reader is about to meet four kings whose names start to blur together.

The dynasty reads, in sequence: Shutruk-Nahhunte (the father, the sacker of Babylon, the subject of this chapter's middle section), then his son Kutir-Nahhunte III (KOO-teer, who continued the Babylonian wars), then Kutir-Nahhunte's brother Shilhak-Inshushinak (SHEEL-hak, the great builder and restorer whose Sit Shamshi bronze survives), then Shilhak-Inshushinak's son Hutelutush-Inshushinak (hoo-teh-LOO-tush, who lost the kingdom to Babylon).
The trick to keeping them straight: the first three are a father-and-two-brothers generation of peak imperial Elam, and the fourth is the son who inherited the collapse.
The two "-Nahhunte" names (invoking the Elamite sun god) belong to father and son; the two "-Inshushinak" names (invoking the Elamite city god of Susa) belong to uncle and nephew.

His reign started with the usual building projects — temples restored, royal inscriptions issued, frontier fortifications repaired — and for most of the first decade nothing particularly dramatic happened.

The shift came around 1175 BCE when the Kassite dynasty of Babylon, which had been a relatively quiet neighbor for nearly four centuries (Mesopotamia Chapter 8), entered a period of rapid political decline.
The late Kassite kings were weak, contested internally, and could not maintain their grip on the western and eastern frontiers.
Shutruk-Nahhunte watched the decline and began preparing his response.
The response took another decade to organize — military logistics at this scale did not happen overnight — and then in 1158 BCE the army marched.
Why did he do it?
The short answer is that he wanted the wealth and the prestige, and the opportunity was there.
The long answer is more interesting, and it involves a specifically Elamite claim to Mesopotamian legitimacy.
The Shutrukid kings asserted a blood relationship with the Kassite royal line.
Several Shutrukid royal inscriptions claim that the Elamite royal house was descended from a Kassite princess who had married an Elamite king several generations earlier.

The claim is probably true in some form — dynastic marriages between Elam and Babylon were routine in the Middle Elamite period, and a Kassite princess turning up at the Elamite court and producing heirs would be entirely consistent with what we know about Late Bronze Age diplomatic practice.

Shutruk-Nahhunte used the ancestry claim to assert that the Elamite royal house had a legitimate hereditary claim to the Babylonian throne, and that when the Kassite dynasty died out, the rightful successor was the Elamite king.
It is exactly the kind of legal fiction that ancient kingdoms used to dress up aggressive wars of conquest as legitimate succession disputes.
Whether Shutruk-Nahhunte actually believed it or was cynically manipulating the diplomatic language is impossible to say now.
What is clear is that the claim gave him a rhetorical framework for the invasion.
He could present the 1158 BCE campaign not as an Elamite attack on Babylon but as an Elamite king enforcing his family's hereditary right against a usurping Kassite dynasty.
In the royal inscriptions Shutruk-Nahhunte left behind at Susa describing the campaign, he uses language that frames the Kassite king as a rebel and himself as the legitimate restorer of order.

This is propaganda, but it is politically sophisticated propaganda, and it places the sack of Babylon within a broader Mesopotamian legal tradition of dynastic inheritance rather than treating it as a simple foreign invasion.
The actual sack of Babylon deserves to be described carefully, because it is one of the richest looting events of the ancient world.
The Elamite army besieged Babylon for a short period — probably only a few weeks — before the walls were breached.

The Kassite king Zababa-shuma-iddin was captured and deposed; what happened to him afterward is not recorded in surviving texts, though later Babylonian tradition holds that he was taken to Susa in chains and died in Elamite captivity.
Once inside the city, Shutruk-Nahhunte's forces conducted a systematic sweep of the major temples, palaces, and monuments.

They did not burn the city wholesale — Babylon continued to be occupied and was rebuilt within a generation — but they stripped every valuable object of religious or political significance they could find and loaded it onto carts for the journey home.

The loot inventory is partly reconstructible from a combination of sources: Shutruk-Nahhunte's own surviving inscriptions at Susa boasting about the campaign, later Babylonian historical texts lamenting the losses, and above all the objects themselves that nineteenth and twentieth century archaeologists excavated at Susa and identified as Mesopotamian imports.
The inventory includes: The Code of Hammurabi, the diorite stele we already mentioned.
Shutruk-Nahhunte took it from its temple at Sippar (a major religious city in northern Babylonia), carried it to Susa, and erected it in the temple of Inshushinak.
It stayed at Susa until 1901 CE, when the French archaeologist Jacques de Morgan excavated it, identified it, and shipped it to Paris.
It is today on permanent display at the Louvre, where it remains one of the most visited ancient artifacts in the museum.
The Code of Hammurabi is the most famous Mesopotamian artwork in the world, and it would not be intact today if Shutruk-Nahhunte had not carried it to Susa.
Left in Sippar, it would almost certainly have been destroyed by later wars or recycled for its expensive black stone.
The Victory Stele of Naram-Sin, same story.

Taken from the Akkadian cultural heritage sites of lower Mesopotamia, carried to Susa, installed in an Elamite temple, excavated in 1898 by the same French team, and sent to the Louvre where it is currently displayed.
A statue of Manishtushu (Sargon of Akkad's son, the brother and predecessor of Naram-Sin).
The statue, known today as the "Statue of Manishtushu" or the "Obelisk of Manishtushu," is a carved stone figure of the king seated on a throne, also excavated at Susa by French archaeologists.
The cuneiform "Obelisk of Manishtushu" — a separate four-sided pillar carved with cuneiform text recording land sales made during Manishtushu's reign, from the twenty-third century BCE.
Also at Susa, now at the Louvre.
A stone statue of Shulgi, the famous Ur III king from Chapter 3.

Dozens of smaller objects: bronze weapons, cylinder seals, royal portraits, votive statues, inscribed stones, foundation deposits, and what appear to be private temple treasures from Babylonian shrines.
The cedar-wood processional barge of Marduk — the Babylonian chief god's ceremonial boat, used in the annual New Year festival for solemn processions down the Euphrates and back.
The barge was the focal point of Babylon's most important religious ceremony, and its seizure was a gesture of extraordinary ritual aggression.
Shutruk-Nahhunte brought it back to Susa and installed it in an Elamite temple.
The statue of Marduk himself — the cult statue of the Babylonian chief god, which had been the focal point of Babylonian religion for centuries.
To capture and remove the statue of a city's chief god was the ultimate act of religious and political domination.
It meant the god had abandoned Babylon and accepted a new residence at Susa.
Shutruk-Nahhunte brought Marduk back to Elam and installed him in a temple there.
This is the object that will matter most in the counterattack in a few years. (Worth knowing: Shutruk-Nahhunte was not the first foreign king to kidnap Marduk from Babylon.

The Hittite king Mursili I had carried the statue off in his famous 1595 BCE sack of Babylon — the event that ended the Old Babylonian dynasty, Mesopotamia Ch 8 — and the Assyrian king Tukulti-Ninurta I had done it again around 1225 BCE, Mesopotamia Ch 9.
Marduk had been through two prior captivities before Shutruk-Nahhunte's men loaded him onto an Elamite cart.
Each time, a later Babylonian campaign had eventually brought the statue home.

The 1158 BCE seizure was the third round of the same kidnapping game, and the Babylonians expected it would end the same way — which, eventually, it did.) What the Elamites did with the loot is characteristic of how Near Eastern victors used captured religious objects.
They did not melt down the metal or burn the wood.
They installed the trophies in their own temples, where the physical presence of captured Mesopotamian art served both as a trophy and as a demonstration of Elamite supremacy over the rival pantheon.

To walk into the temple of Inshushinak at Susa in 1150 BCE and see the Code of Hammurabi and the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin and the Marduk statue all lined up along the walls was to see three thousand years of Mesopotamian imperial self-presentation reduced to an Elamite backdrop.
The Elamite priests had been busy.

Shutruk-Nahhunte died around 1155 BCE, only a few years after his great campaign, and was succeeded by his son Kutir-Nahhunte III (KOO-teer nah-HOON-tay — the "III" is a modern scholarly numbering to distinguish him from earlier Kutir-Nahhuntes; the Elamites themselves did not number their kings).

Kutir-Nahhunte III continued his father's wars against a weakened Babylon, leading additional campaigns west into Mesopotamia and briefly installing himself as overlord of the former Kassite territory.

His reign represents the absolute high-water mark of Elamite political reach — the closest Elam ever came to actually conquering and ruling Mesopotamia as a subject territory rather than just raiding it.
The closest Kutir-Nahhunte came was installing Elamite governors over several Babylonian cities and demanding tribute from the post-Kassite local rulers for a period of approximately a decade.

This is not quite full conquest — the Elamite imperial infrastructure never reached the depth that the Akkadian or Neo-Assyrian imperial systems did — but it is substantially further than any earlier Elamite king had pushed.

For about ten years, roughly 1155 to 1145 BCE, the Elamite kings ruled a two-region empire spanning both sides of the Zagros, with western and eastern halves connected by the same imperial bureaucracy.

Kutir-Nahhunte was succeeded by his brother Shilhak-Inshushinak (SHEEL-hak in-SHOO-shee-nak — "Shilhak" meaning "he who spreads" or "he who multiplies," and "Inshushinak" being the god's name appended as a theophoric element), whose reign from roughly 1150 to 1120 BCE represents the last moment of Elam as a great power.

Shilhak-Inshushinak was an ambitious and energetic ruler who continued the imperial wars against Babylon, expanded the Elamite temple-building program at Susa, and commissioned one of the most remarkable surviving Elamite artworks: the Sit Shamshi Bronze Tableau.

The Sit Shamshi (meaning "Sunrise") is a bronze sculpture group approximately 23 inches long (60 cm) showing a miniature scene of two nude male figures performing what appears to be a sunrise ritual at a miniature temple complex.
One figure is kneeling, pouring water from a vessel; the other is standing with hands raised in a priestly gesture.
Between them are tiny bronze representations of a ziggurat, several subsidiary temples, a sacred grove of bronze trees, and various ritual objects including miniature altars and offering stands.
The whole thing is cast from solid bronze and was designed to sit on an altar as a permanent miniature of the morning ritual it depicts.
The Sit Shamshi is one of the most detailed surviving representations of Elamite religious practice at its operational level.

It shows us what the morning ritual at an Elamite temple actually looked like: two naked priests, a pouring-of-water ceremony at dawn, ritual space marked by architectural miniatures, and a complete built environment for the ceremony.

The surviving inscription on the base identifies Shilhak-Inshushinak as the dedicator and calls the tableau "my bronze sunrise" — explicitly framing the object as the king's personal ritual offering to the god Inshushinak.
The Sit Shamshi is the clearest window we have into the daily religious operations of an Elamite temple, and it survives today in the Louvre along with most of the other great Elamite bronzes.
Shilhak-Inshushinak's reign was also the moment when Elamite kings starting commissioning restoration inscriptions for temples and monuments across the kingdom.
These are texts in which a king records his restoration of a shrine originally built by an earlier ruler, often citing the names and dates of his predecessors.

The restoration inscriptions are historiographically invaluable because they give us an Elamite view of Elamite history — kings commemorating earlier kings, citing chronological predecessors, establishing a dynastic framework that ties the current reign to the deep past of the kingdom.

Shilhak-Inshushinak's restoration inscriptions are some of the richest sources for reconstructing the sequence of earlier Elamite dynasties, because his scribes carefully listed the names of predecessors whose original monuments were being restored.

The texts are also a window into how the Elamite royal court imagined its own history: as an unbroken chain of rulers stretching back to the Proto-Elamite period, each one accountable to his predecessors and to the patron gods who had legitimized the whole sequence.
And then it collapsed.
The Elamite imperial moment ended around 1120 BCE with the reign of Hutelutush-Inshushinak (hoo-teh-LOO-tush), Shilhak-Inshushinak's son and successor.

Hutelutush-Inshushinak inherited a kingdom that had already overextended itself — the Elamite military was stretched thin garrisoning Mesopotamia, the treasury was depleted by the ongoing building programs, and the Babylonian resistance under a new dynasty was starting to organize.

The Babylonian king who organized the resistance was Nebuchadnezzar I (NEB-yoo-kad-NEZZ-ar — the first king of that name, not to be confused with the much more famous Nebuchadnezzar II of the Neo-Babylonian Empire six centuries later; Mesopotamia Chapter 12).
Nebuchadnezzar I ruled Babylon from roughly 1125 to 1104 BCE, at the head of the Second Dynasty of Isin — a new native Babylonian royal line that had replaced the collapsed Kassites.
His primary political goal was reversing the humiliation of Shutruk-Nahhunte's sack of Babylon and recovering the Marduk statue that still sat in an Elamite temple at Susa.
Nebuchadnezzar I's war against Elam is told from the Babylonian side in Mesopotamia Chapter 10.
The Elamite side is briefer: Hutelutush-Inshushinak's army met the Babylonian forces in the field, was defeated, and retreated back to Susa.
Nebuchadnezzar I pursued, besieged Susa, sacked the city, recovered the Marduk statue, and returned to Babylon in triumph.

Hutelutush-Inshushinak himself fled into the highlands and apparently lived out the rest of his reign in reduced circumstances at Anshan, ruling a contracted kingdom that no longer included the lowland plain.
The Marduk statue was recovered.
The other trophies — the Code of Hammurabi, the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin, the Manishtushu obelisk, the Statue of Shulgi — were not.

Either Nebuchadnezzar I did not know they were at Susa, or he could not find them in the sacked city, or he was focused specifically on the Marduk statue as the religiously critical object and left the older trophies behind.
Whatever the reason, the Hammurabi and Naram-Sin stelae stayed at Susa.
They would still be there, buried under rubble, when French archaeologists arrived nearly three thousand years later.
After Hutelutush-Inshushinak's death around 1110 BCE, Elam enters a period of historical silence that lasts almost three centuries.

The kingdom does not disappear — Susa is still inhabited, Anshan is still a highland center, the cuneiform tradition continues, and Elamite kings are still ruling somebody somewhere in southwestern Iran — but the external records go nearly dark.
Mesopotamian chronicles mention Elam only in passing.
Surviving Elamite inscriptions are few.
The archaeological strata at Susa and Anshan show continuous occupation but without the monumental building programs and trophy imports of the Middle Elamite period.
Between roughly 1100 and 750 BCE, Elam is going through what later historians call the Elamite Dark Age — a 350-year quiet period about which we know very little.
When Elam re-emerges into the historical record in the eighth century BCE, it is a very different kingdom.
Smaller, more decentralized, ruled by a network of highland kings rather than a single imperial sukkalmah.
And facing a new enemy who will prove to be the most destructive force Elam has ever met: the Neo-Assyrian Empire.
That is Chapter 7.

chapters.title: Til-Tuba
sentences: The Neo-Elamite period (roughly 1100–539 BCE) is a very different world from the imperial Elam of Chogha Zanbil and Shutruk-Nahhunte.
It is fragmented, regional, shifting, and difficult to narrate as a unified political history.

For most of the early Neo-Elamite centuries, "Elam" is best understood as a loose confederation of highland and lowland city-states, each with its own royal line, sometimes cooperating under a paramount king and sometimes fighting each other, all of them loosely identified as Elamite and all of them sharing the old pantheon, the old scribal tradition, and the old two-region geography.
The Sukkalmah-style centralized state is gone.
A decentralized Elam takes its place, and for three hundred years or so it manages to keep running in the background of regional politics while the big story of the Near East moves elsewhere.
The big story of the Near East in those centuries is the rise of Assyria.
Mesopotamia Chapter 11 tells the Assyrian story in detail.

The short version is that the Assyrian heartland in northern Iraq — centered on the cities of Assur, Nineveh, and Kalhu — had been a middle-rank Near Eastern power since the early second millennium BCE, but starting in the ninth century BCE under a series of aggressive and militarily innovative kings (Ashurnasirpal II, Shalmaneser III, Adad-nirari III), Assyria began expanding outward from its heartland at a pace no earlier Mesopotamian empire had matched.

By the mid-eighth century BCE, the Assyrian military machine had perfected mass cavalry warfare, siege engineering, forced-population transfer as a strategic tool, iron weaponry, and a professional standing army organized into specialized units with a reliable logistical support system.
The Neo-Assyrian Empire of the seventh century BCE was the first military-industrial complex in world history, and it was terrifying.
Between 745 BCE and 612 BCE, Neo-Assyria conquered almost the entire ancient Near East.
The Arameans (Semitic-speaking tribes from the Syrian desert, whose language Aramaic would later become the everyday lingua franca of the whole Near East) were pacified.
Syria and the Levant (including Israel and Judah) were absorbed.
Phoenicia (the coastal trading cities of modern Lebanon, famous as the inventors of the alphabet that became Greek and Latin and most modern Western scripts) was subjected.
The Kingdom of Urartu in the Armenian highlands was beaten back.
Egypt was invaded twice, briefly conquered, and made to pay tribute.

The Medes (an Indo-European people of western Iran, cousins of the Persians, who would later help destroy Assyria and then briefly run their own empire before the Persian takeover) were pressured into a client relationship.
Babylon was repeatedly occupied — sometimes ruled as a direct Assyrian province, sometimes left as a subject kingdom, sometimes destroyed and rebuilt.
By the mid-seventh century BCE, the Assyrian Empire was the largest state that had ever existed in human history, and its armies could be anywhere in the Near East within a month.
And Elam was next door.
The Assyrian-Elamite wars of the seventh century BCE are the long, bloody endgame of the story this book has been telling.

They span roughly sixty years — from the 680s BCE to the 640s BCE — and they involve at least seven separate Assyrian invasions of Elam, countless skirmishes, multiple Elamite alliances with Mesopotamian rebels, and a slowly tightening Assyrian pressure that eventually destroyed Elam as a functioning kingdom.

The wars are well-documented on the Assyrian side because the Neo-Assyrian kings commissioned extensive narrative reliefs in their palaces at Nineveh showing scenes from the campaigns — reliefs that survive in substantial quantities and are now in the British Museum, the Louvre, and other major European collections.
They are less well-documented on the Elamite side because the Elamite kingdom was too fragmented during this period to produce the kind of royal inscriptions we have from earlier centuries.
What we know about Elamite resistance to Assyria comes overwhelmingly from Assyrian sources — the winners telling the story of the losers, with all the usual biases.
The central figures on the two sides are worth introducing.

On the Assyrian side, the war eventually comes to be dominated by Ashurbanipal (ah-shur-BAH-nee-pahl, ruled 668–631 BCE), the last great king of the Neo-Assyrian Empire and one of the most complicated rulers in all of ancient history.

Ashurbanipal was a warrior — he personally led campaigns across half the Near East — but he was also a scholar who assembled the famous Royal Library at Nineveh containing over 30,000 cuneiform tablets of Mesopotamian literature, religion, medicine, and history, preserving the corpus that most of what we know about Sumerian and Akkadian civilization has come down to us.
He was also brutal even by Neo-Assyrian standards.

His royal inscriptions boast of torturing captives, flaying enemies alive, impaling resisters on stakes outside the walls of conquered cities, and — a specifically Ashurbanipal touch — commissioning artworks depicting the gory details of his victories in the throne rooms of his own palace.

The Nineveh palace reliefs of Ashurbanipal's reign are some of the most horrifying pieces of ancient propaganda that survive, and they were carved on the orders of a man who also built the biggest library in the ancient world.

On the Elamite side, during the decisive campaigns of the 650s BCE, the central figure is a king named Te-Umman — short for Tempti-Humban-Inshushinak, a throne name combining "tempti" (meaning "lord") with the names of the two Elamite divine patrons Humban and Inshushinak, which together meant something like "Lord of Humban and Inshushinak." Te-Umman was one of the few surviving rulers of the post-Sukkalmah Elamite kingdoms and seems to have controlled a substantial territory from a base at Susa.

Assyrian inscriptions describe him as a tyrant who had murdered rival claimants to consolidate his power, which may be propaganda or may be accurate; the pattern of Neo-Elamite succession crises would certainly have made palace coups common.

What is clear from the surviving sources is that Te-Umman was an aggressive and militarily capable ruler who, in 653 BCE, decided that the growing Assyrian pressure on Elam had to be met head-on rather than through diplomacy.
Te-Umman raised an army, marched west across the Tigris with Elamite cavalry and archers, and attacked Assyrian-controlled territory in southern Mesopotamia.
Ashurbanipal responded by leading a major Assyrian army east to meet him.

The two forces collided at a place the Assyrian sources call Til-Tuba (TIL-TOO-bah) — probably a hill or ridge along the east bank of a river in southern Mesopotamia, though the exact location has never been securely identified — on a day in 653 BCE that would produce one of the most famous battles in the entire ancient world.

The Battle of Til-Tuba is uniquely well-documented because Ashurbanipal commissioned a series of narrative relief panels carved into the walls of his palace at Nineveh that depict the battle in detail — frame by frame, almost like a comic book laid across a wall.

The reliefs survive and are today mounted in a single large gallery in the British Museum, where you can walk along the wall and follow the sequence of events as Ashurbanipal's sculptors chose to tell them.
Most ancient battles are known only from a single royal inscription boasting about victory.

Til-Tuba is known from a graphic multi-panel visual narrative that names specific soldiers, depicts specific moments, and tells the story with a theatrical intensity that has no real parallel in earlier Near Eastern art.
The reliefs show the battle unfolding across several connected scenes, reading from left to right: First: the armies approach each other.
Te-Umman in his chariot is shown at the front of the Elamite line, wearing his royal headdress and flanked by his closest warriors.
The Elamite soldiers are identifiable by their distinctive equipment — headbands, long hair, short spears and composite bows — which is different from the Assyrian style of armor and weaponry.
Second: the battle itself.
The Assyrian army crushes the Elamite formation.
Bodies pile up.
Horses fall.
Chariots overturn.
Soldiers drown trying to cross a river behind the Elamite line.

The reliefs show dozens of specific individual deaths with a level of grisly detail that is unprecedented in earlier Near Eastern battle art — a soldier being speared through the back as he tries to flee, another caught by the hair and decapitated, a third being trampled by a charging horse.
The Assyrian sculptors were showing off both their technical skill and their king's military mercilessness.
Third: Te-Umman is killed.
This is the central narrative moment of the reliefs, and it is depicted in a sequence of panels that reads like a cinematic chase.
First Te-Umman's chariot is seen with an arrow protruding from the king's shoulder.
The chariot begins to lose control.
Te-Umman falls out, landing on the ground.
He tries to rise.
His son (shown in a separate panel, identified by an inscription) tries to protect him.
Both are struck down.
An Assyrian soldier named in the inscription — a specific man identified by name in the Assyrian texts, though the name is now lost in the deterioration of the reliefs — steps forward with a sword.
Te-Umman's head is severed from his body on the spot.
The head is lifted up.
The body remains on the battlefield.

The whole sequence takes perhaps eight or ten individual panels across a stretch of wall, and it is the most detailed narrative depiction of a single battlefield death in all of surviving ancient art.
Fourth: the head travels.
Ashurbanipal's sculptors show the head of Te-Umman being carried back from the battlefield to Nineveh, where it is delivered to the Assyrian king.
A specific panel in the sequence shows the head being presented to Ashurbanipal himself.

And then — and this is the most famous single moment in the reliefs — the head is hung from a tree in the royal garden at Nineveh, where Ashurbanipal and his queen are shown reclining on couches, drinking wine, being served food by attendants, while the severed head of the Elamite king dangles from the branch directly above them.
The garden scene at Nineveh is one of the most famous images to survive from the ancient Near East.
It is now known as the "Garden Party Relief" or the "Banquet of Ashurbanipal," and it has been reproduced in virtually every general history of the ancient world written in the last century.

The relief shows the king and queen at ease in their palace garden, with music playing and servants fanning them, while the trophy head of their defeated enemy rotates gently in the breeze among the leaves.
It is a specific choice.
Ashurbanipal could have commissioned a simpler victory image.
He chose instead to have himself depicted drinking wine in front of the hanging head of an enemy king.
The relief is a statement about what kind of king Ashurbanipal understood himself to be — and it is a statement about what the Neo-Assyrian Empire was willing to do to the people who fought it.
Te-Umman's death did not end the Elamite resistance.
It only intensified the Assyrian pressure.
Within a year of Til-Tuba, the new Elamite king — a man named Humban-Nikash II, one of several claimants to the throne in the political chaos following Te-Umman's defeat — made a fatal decision.
He allied with a rebel movement against Ashurbanipal that was unfolding inside the Assyrian empire itself.
The rebel was Ashurbanipal's own older brother, Shamash-shum-ukin (SHAH-mash-shoom-YOO-keen).

Their father, the previous Assyrian king Esarhaddon, had divided his empire between his two sons: Ashurbanipal would rule Assyria proper from Nineveh, and Shamash-shum-ukin would rule Babylonia from Babylon as a subordinate king.
The arrangement had been intended to prevent the civil war that usually followed an Assyrian succession, and for almost seventeen years it worked.
Shamash-shum-ukin ruled Babylon loyally, deferring to his younger brother in the larger empire's affairs, while building up his own court and administrative network.
In 652 BCE, Shamash-shum-ukin rebelled.

The causes are complex and covered from the Mesopotamian side in Mesopotamia Chapter 11, but the short version is that the older brother grew increasingly resentful of being in the junior position, the Babylonian elite grew increasingly tired of Assyrian domination, and Shamash-shum-ukin saw an opportunity to lead a general Babylonian revolt against his brother.

He declared Babylon independent of Assyria, began raising troops, and began sending diplomatic messages to the powers along Assyria's frontiers — including Elam — offering alliances to anyone willing to help bring Ashurbanipal down.
Humban-Nikash II, the Elamite king, said yes.
This was probably the worst political decision any Elamite king had ever made.

The reasoning was sound in the moment: Ashurbanipal was weaker than he looked because his empire was now fighting itself, and Elam had plenty of legitimate grievances after the Til-Tuba disaster and the ongoing Assyrian pressure.
Allying with Shamash-shum-ukin seemed like a way to deflect Assyrian attention west and secure the Elamite frontier through collective action with other enemies of Assyria.
It was the standard move for a small power faced with a bigger enemy: find a distraction, back it, and hope the big enemy gets destroyed by someone else while you recover your strength.
It did not work.
Ashurbanipal was unshakable.
The rebellion dragged on for four years (652–648 BCE), during which Ashurbanipal besieged Babylon, starved the city, and eventually captured it.
Shamash-shum-ukin committed suicide in the burning royal palace rather than be captured by his brother's forces.
The rebellion collapsed.
Babylon was brutally punished, and Ashurbanipal turned his full attention to Elam — the power that had backed the wrong brother.
The Assyrian response to Elamite treason was the destruction of Elam, carried out across a series of campaigns between 647 and 645 BCE.
These campaigns are not a single event.

They are a sustained, methodical, multi-year operation in which Assyrian armies repeatedly invaded Elamite territory, captured and destroyed Elamite cities, executed captured nobles, seized treasure, relocated populations, and systematically dismantled the physical infrastructure of the kingdom.
Ashurbanipal's royal inscriptions describe the operation in exulting detail, because he was proud of it and wanted posterity to know exactly what he had done.
The highlights — if that is the right word — include: The Sack of Susa in 646 BCE, which is the symbolic end of Elamite political history.
Susa had been continuously inhabited for more than three thousand years at this point, through every earlier political upheaval.
The Assyrian campaign of 646 BCE was the first event that actually destroyed it in a meaningful way.
Ashurbanipal's army entered the city, stripped every temple of its treasures, carried off or broke every royal monument, looted the palace, and burned the central ceremonial district.

Ashurbanipal's own inscription, in the standard translation of his royal annals, describes the sack with the triumphalism of a king who has finally achieved what his ancestors only dreamed of — roughly: he destroyed the ziggurat of Susa, tore down its temples, burned the shrines of the gods of Elam, and carried off the gods and goddesses of the kingdom to Assyria.
The carrying off of the Elamite royal dead.
This is perhaps the most striking specific detail of the sack.
Ashurbanipal's forces, upon capturing Susa, opened the royal tombs of the Elamite kings.

The royal bodies — the preserved or partially decayed remains of Elamite kings from earlier centuries, buried with their regalia in the royal necropolis — were disinterred, wrapped in sacks, and carried back to Nineveh as trophies.

Ashurbanipal's inscriptions describe this in language whose standard translation runs roughly: he carried off the bones of the kings of Elam to the land of Assyria so that their ghosts should find no rest and their spirits should have no peace.
The purpose was religious as well as political.
In the Mesopotamian and Elamite understanding of the afterlife, the dead depended on their living descendants to make regular offerings at their tombs.
If the tomb was empty and the bones were somewhere far away, the dead would be cut off from the sustaining prayers and offerings of their community.
Ashurbanipal was not just humiliating the Elamite royal family.
He was consigning their dead to eternal suffering by breaking the ritual connection between living descendants and ancestral tombs.
The salting of the fields around Susa.
Ashurbanipal's inscriptions describe his army spreading salt over the agricultural fields surrounding the city to render them sterile.

The "salting the earth" detail is often repeated as the most memorable part of the destruction, though some modern scholars question whether it was actually done at that scale or whether it is a rhetorical flourish describing a more limited and symbolic action. (Modern readers sometimes encounter the same trope attached to the Roman destruction of Carthage in 146 BCE — the story that the Romans salted the fields of the defeated city — and that Roman version is also probably more rhetoric than reality.

The trope may actually originate with Ashurbanipal and have been imitated by later ancient propagandists.) Either way, the intention is clear: the king wanted to ensure that no one would be able to rebuild Susa as an agricultural center in the following generations.
He was not merely conquering the city; he was trying to render it uninhabitable.
The recovery of the statue of Nanaya.

This was the Uruk cult image we met back in Chapter 3 — the goddess statue that Elamite forces had looted from Uruk during the Shimashki-era wars of the early second millennium BCE, held at Susa as a trophy ever since (in the same way Shutruk-Nahhunte later held the Code of Hammurabi).

Ashurbanipal found the statue in a Susa temple, reclaimed it as Mesopotamian property, and returned it in triumph to Uruk, where it was reinstalled in its original temple after more than sixteen hundred years of Elamite captivity.
Nanaya came home.

After the Sack of Susa, Ashurbanipal continued his campaigns through the rest of Elamite territory, destroying Dur-Untash and Chogha Zanbil (though by this point Chogha Zanbil was already largely abandoned as a functioning religious center), capturing the highland cities, and systematically eliminating Elamite royal claimants.
By 645 BCE, the Elamite state — already fractured in the post-Sukkalmah period — was effectively destroyed.
No unified Elamite kingdom survived.

A few local rulers continued to claim the title "king of Elam" over small highland territories for several more decades, but the political and military capacity of Elam as a Near Eastern great power was finished.
And then, within fifty years, Assyria itself collapsed.
The Neo-Assyrian Empire, which had seemed invincible in Ashurbanipal's lifetime, went through a series of internal crises after his death in 631 BCE.
Succession disputes weakened the central government.
The Medes in western Iran, previously an Assyrian client, rebelled.
The Babylonians rebelled again, this time successfully, under a new dynasty that would become the Neo-Babylonian Empire.
In 612 BCE, a coalition of Medes and Babylonians besieged Nineveh, captured the city, and burned it to the ground.
Ashurbanipal had been dead for nineteen years.
His royal library survived by accident, baked hard in the fire.
His palace reliefs — including the Til-Tuba narrative panels and the Garden Party scene — were buried in the rubble and preserved for modern archaeologists to excavate.
The empire he had ruled at its peak was gone.
The Elamite response to Assyria's collapse is one of the saddest footnotes in this book.
Several Elamite local rulers in the mid-seventh century BCE tried to take advantage of Assyria's distraction to rebuild some portion of the old kingdom.

Their efforts produced a few decades of renewed Elamite political activity, during which small kingdoms called "Elam" continued to exist in the highlands around Anshan and in the lowlands around Susa, but they never regained the scale or coherence of the pre-Ashurbanipal kingdom.
The sack of Susa had done too much damage.
The kingdom was broken, and the rebuilding efforts could not restore what had been lost.
Elam as an independent state was over.
But — and this is where the story gets unexpected — the Elamite civilization was not quite finished.
The kingdom was dead, but the language, the scribal tradition, the administrative know-how, and the royal title "king of Anshan" all survived.
They survived by being picked up and used by a new people who were about to become the greatest power the ancient world had ever seen.
Chapter 8 is about how Elam became Persia.

chapters.title: The Persian Echo
sentences: Here is a fact that most general histories of the ancient world do not emphasize as much as they should: the founder of the Persian Empire was, in his own self-presentation, a king of Anshan.

Anshan is the Elamite city we have been carrying through this book — the highland capital in Fars, the half of the Elamite two-region geography that sat up on the Iranian plateau, the place excavated at Tall-i Malyan.
When the Elamite kingdom was at its height, Anshan was a major political center equal in rank to Susa.
When the Elamite kingdom was declining in the Neo-Elamite period, Anshan was one of the last highland centers that retained something like royal status.

And when the Elamite kingdom was destroyed by Ashurbanipal in the 640s BCE, Anshan was one of the few places in the former kingdom that kept on functioning as a local capital under the control of new rulers who styled themselves "king of Anshan" in continuation of the old Elamite royal titles.
The specific new rulers who inherited the Anshan throne in the seventh century BCE came from a different ethnic and linguistic background from the old Elamites.

They were Persians — that is, speakers of an early form of Persian, which is an Indo-European language (the family we met briefly in Chapter 1 — the huge language family branching from an ancestral Proto-Indo-European tongue spoken somewhere on the Pontic-Caspian steppes around 4000 BCE, eventually including Greek, Latin, Sanskrit, Persian, the Germanic languages, and the Celtic and Slavic branches).
Linguistically, the Persians were related to the later Greeks and Romans, not to the Elamites, whose language was an isolate with no relatives.

The Persians had migrated into the Iranian plateau from the north over several centuries in the late second and early first millennium BCE — a long, slow population movement that brought Iranian-speaking pastoralists down into the highland valleys of Fars and Media from an ancestral homeland somewhere in Central Asia.

The same broader migration that split off the Persians also, around the same time, brought their linguistic cousins the Indo-Aryans southeast through the Hindu Kush into the Indus Valley and the Gangetic plain, where they would become the ancestral speakers of Sanskrit and the founders of the Vedic tradition (Indus Valley Ch 10).

Persian and Sanskrit are sister languages descended from the same Indo-Iranian branch, and the two populations are effectively the eastern and western halves of the same Bronze Age population movement splitting around the Hindu Kush.

When the Persians arrived in Fars, they found Anshan already there — a long-established Elamite highland capital with a functioning court, a bureaucratic tradition, a cuneiform scribal culture, and a royal legitimacy that traced back two thousand years.
Rather than destroying what they found, the incoming Persian leaders absorbed it.
They married into the existing Elamite royal families.
They took Elamite administrative personnel into their own service.
They adopted the Elamite royal title "king of Anshan" as their own.
They used the Elamite language for official correspondence, the Elamite cuneiform scribal tradition for royal records, and the Elamite bureaucratic system for administering the region.

For several generations — through the late seventh and early sixth centuries BCE — the "Persian" kings of Anshan were functionally indistinguishable from the Elamite kings of Anshan they had replaced.
They wore Elamite regalia.
They worshipped Elamite gods alongside their own Iranian deities.
They spoke Persian at home but wrote in Elamite at court.
They were a hybrid culture, and the Elamite side of the hybrid was the more visible public face.
One of these hybrid Persian-Elamite kings of Anshan was named Cyrus I.

He ruled in the mid-seventh century BCE, around the time of Ashurbanipal's destruction of Susa, and his surviving seal impression — a clay sealing stamped with his name and royal title — identifies him as "Cyrus, king of Anshan, son of Teispes." The seal itself is in Elamite artistic style and the title is an old Elamite royal title.
Cyrus I's grandson would be the most famous king in ancient history (Persian Empire Ch 1).
Cyrus the Great (Cyrus II, ruled 559–530 BCE) is one of the handful of ancient rulers whose name is still widely known to modern Westerners who have never studied the ancient Near East.
He is the founder of the Achaemenid Persian Empire (Persian Empire Ch 1-5).

He is the king who conquered the Neo-Babylonian Empire and released the Jewish exiles held there, allowing them to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple — an act that earned him a famous blessing in the Hebrew Bible, where the book of Isaiah calls him God's "anointed" (the original Hebrew word is mashiach, from which English derives "Messiah").
He is credited in some traditions as the inventor of human rights for his policy of respecting conquered peoples' religious customs.

The Cyrus Cylinder — a small clay cylinder with a cuneiform inscription recording his conquest of Babylon in 539 BCE — is sometimes described, with some historical simplification, as the first human rights charter.
Cyrus is a founder-figure in Persian national memory in exactly the way that Romulus is a founder-figure in Roman memory or Shi Huangdi is in Chinese memory.

What is less often emphasized in popular accounts of Cyrus is that the same Cyrus Cylinder which describes his conquest of Babylon also gives a genealogical titulature in which Cyrus calls himself king of Anshan, son of Cambyses (who was also king of Anshan), grandson of Cyrus I (king of Anshan), great-grandson of Teispes (king of Anshan), of "an enduring royal line" — the standard translation, by Irving Finkel and others, chains all four generations together through the Anshan title.
The title "king of Anshan" is the one that anchors Cyrus's genealogy.
His father, grandfather, and great-grandfather were all kings of Anshan before him.
The Achaemenid royal line, in Cyrus's own official self-presentation on the Cylinder he commissioned to celebrate his greatest conquest, is first and foremost the royal line of Elamite Anshan.

The later titles ("king of Sumer and Akkad," "king of the four quarters") are Mesopotamian imperial traditions that Cyrus adopted upon conquering Babylon, but the base-level title — the title that establishes who Cyrus was before he became a world-conqueror — is an Elamite one.
This means something important.

When Cyrus the Great conquered the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BCE and established the Persian Empire as the largest political unit the ancient world had ever seen, he was not simply a Persian outsider coming in to replace a Semitic Mesopotamian dynasty with an Indo-European Iranian one.
He was the heir, in his own self-understanding, of a specifically Elamite royal tradition that went back through Anshan to the third millennium BCE.

Cyrus presented himself as the inheritor of the Elamite kingdom that Ashurbanipal had destroyed a century earlier, and he used the Elamite royal title to legitimize his claim to the wider Mesopotamian world.
From the Elamite perspective — if there still was an Elamite perspective — the conquests of Cyrus were not a foreign takeover but a restoration.
An Anshan-royal line had returned.
