The Persian Empire that Cyrus and his successors built is conventionally narrated in the context of Greek history — the Persian Empire as the great enemy that the Greek city-states fought at Marathon, Thermopylae, and Salamis in the early fifth century BCE (the Persian Wars, Greece Chs 5–6 in future).
That's a real and important story, but it is not the only story.

The Persian Empire also ran a massive internal bureaucratic apparatus that administered everything from Egypt to the Indus River — a population of perhaps 50 million people, an area of roughly 2.9 million square miles (7.5 million km²), thousands of cities and towns, thousands of miles of royal roads, a royal postal system, a uniform coinage, a tax structure, a legal framework, a religious policy, and a multi-ethnic military.

The administrative apparatus of the Persian Empire was the most elaborate government that had ever existed up to that point, and it was designed to function for centuries regardless of which particular king sat on the throne at any given time.
And the bureaucratic language of that apparatus — the everyday working language of the Persian Empire's administration — was Elamite.
This is the most remarkable fact about the relationship between Elam and Persia, and it is the fact that most general histories of the ancient world completely miss.
The Persians who conquered Babylonia, Egypt, Lydia, Phoenicia, and half the Greek world did not, for most of their history, run their bureaucracy in their own Persian language.
They ran it in Elamite.

The Elamite-speaking scribes of the old Susa tradition, whose training and methods went back to the cuneiform adoption of the Shimashki dynasty nearly 1,500 years earlier, were employed by the new Persian emperors to manage the paperwork of the largest empire in the ancient world.
The evidence for this is overwhelming.
It comes from two major archives, both of them among the most important documentary finds of the twentieth century.
The first archive is the Persepolis Fortification Archive.

Persepolis (per-SEH-po-lis, meaning "City of the Persians" in Greek — the actual name was Parsa) was the ceremonial capital of the Persian Empire from the time of Darius I onward, built around 515 BCE in the highland valleys of Fars about forty miles north of Shiraz.
It was a spectacular complex of palaces and audience halls designed to impress foreign ambassadors and emphasize the grandeur of the Persian kingship.

The site was visible from every direction, dominated by the massive fortification walls that surrounded it, and for nearly two hundred years it functioned as the administrative and ceremonial center of the empire.

When Alexander the Great conquered the Persian Empire in 330 BCE (Persian Empire Ch 5), his army burned Persepolis in what may have been an accident or may have been a calculated act of revenge for the Persian burning of Athens 150 years earlier.
The fire preserved a great deal of the site by carbonizing and hardening its archaeological remains.

When modern archaeologists excavated the fortification walls in the 1930s under the leadership of Ernst Herzfeld and later Erich Schmidt of the University of Chicago's Oriental Institute, they made a discovery that transformed our understanding of Persian administration.

Inside the fortification walls, buried under rubble from the Alexandrine fire, were approximately 30,000 clay tablets and fragments recording the daily economic activities of the Persian royal court over a 15-year period from 509 to 494 BCE — that is, the last decades of Darius I's reign.

The tablets recorded things like grain rations issued to workers, traveling expenses of court officials, wages paid to craftsmen, food supplies sent between royal estates, and the daily logistics of running the Persian royal household.
They were the archaeological equivalent of finding a file cabinet of expense reports and payroll records from the court of a medieval European monarch.
The critical fact about the Persepolis Fortification Archive is that the tablets are in Elamite.
Not in Old Persian (the language Cyrus and Darius spoke at home).
Not in Aramaic (the Semitic lingua franca that was becoming the imperial chancellery language).
In Elamite, written in the same cuneiform scribal tradition that had been the Elamite royal standard since the Shimashki dynasty of 2000 BCE.

The scribes who wrote the Fortification tablets were trained Elamite-cuneiform specialists, and the tablets use vocabulary, grammatical forms, and scribal conventions that are direct continuations of the old Elamite royal chancery.
Here is what this means, concretely.

In 509 BCE, roughly 140 years after Ashurbanipal had sacked Susa and carried off the bones of the Elamite kings, a royal scribe at Persepolis sat down with a clay tablet and wrote, in Elamite cuneiform, a ration receipt for so many liters of barley issued to so many workers on the royal estate.
That scribe was working for the Persian Empire, under the authority of the Persian king Darius I.

He was writing in the language of the supposedly-destroyed kingdom, using the writing system of the supposedly-destroyed kingdom, following the administrative conventions of the supposedly-destroyed kingdom.
Elam had been politically dead for almost a century and a half.
Elam was still running the paperwork.
How was this possible?

The answer is that when the Persians took over the old Elamite administrative region, they inherited an existing scribal training system that was producing new Elamite-cuneiform scribes every generation.
The scribal schools at Susa and Anshan had not stopped operating just because the old Elamite royal family had been destroyed.
They kept training new scribes, and those scribes kept being employed — first by the late Neo-Elamite local kings, then by the incoming Persian nobility who found them useful and hired them.
By the time Cyrus conquered Babylon, the Persian royal court at Anshan had been staffed by Elamite-cuneiform scribes for several generations already.
When Cyrus and his successors expanded the Persian Empire to cover the whole Near East, they took their existing staff of Elamite scribes with them and put them to work managing the new territories.
The Persian Empire used Elamite as its bureaucratic language because that was what its bureaucrats already spoke and wrote.
Over time, the balance shifted.

During the reign of Darius's successor Xerxes I (486–465 BCE) and especially under later Achaemenid kings, Aramaic became more common as the everyday administrative language of the empire, because Aramaic was the dominant spoken language of the Semitic-speaking populations of Babylonia and Syria who made up the single largest subject group inside the empire.
By the middle of the fifth century BCE, most routine correspondence was being conducted in Aramaic rather than Elamite.
But the Elamite scribal tradition continued alongside the Aramaic one, handling high-prestige documents and specific court functions, for another century or more after that.
Elamite-cuneiform tablets are still being produced in Persia as late as the middle of the fourth century BCE, when Alexander the Great arrived and the whole apparatus finally broke down.

That is a language tradition that stretches almost 2,800 years continuously from its first Proto-Elamite tablets around 3100 BCE to its final Achaemenid chancery use around 330 BCE — among the longest continuous scribal traditions in the ancient world. (For context, Egyptian hieroglyphics ran slightly longer, from ~3200 BCE to the fifth century CE, and Chinese script, running from the Shang oracle bones ~1200 BCE to the present day, is the longest of all.

Elamite belongs in the same rare club: scribal traditions that survived across multiple political collapses and linguistic transitions because they had become embedded in the deep training pipelines of their region's institutions.) The second archive is the Behistun Inscription.

Behistun (bee-HIS-toon — the Persian name is Bisotun, bee-soh-TOON) is a site in western Iran, along the old royal road connecting Babylon to the Iranian plateau, about 60 miles (100 km) east of modern Kermanshah.

At Behistun, a tall cliff rises dramatically above the road, and on the face of the cliff — about 200 feet (60 meters) above the ground, visible to every traveler passing below — the Persian king Darius I carved an enormous trilingual inscription commemorating his rise to power, his defeat of internal rivals, and the establishment of his authority over the empire (Persian Empire Ch 2).
The inscription was carved around 520 BCE, a few years into Darius's reign, and it is one of the most important single documents of the whole ancient Near East.

The inscription is trilingual: it exists in three versions, carved side by side on the cliff face, in three different languages: Old Persian (the everyday spoken language of Darius and his immediate court, which at that point had only recently developed its own cuneiform-based writing system specifically for this kind of monumental inscription), Babylonian (the form of Akkadian used in first-millennium Mesopotamia, the most prestigious imperial-literary language of the Near East), and Elamite.
The fact that Elamite is one of the three languages carved on the cliff tells us a great deal about its status in the Persian Empire around 520 BCE.
Elamite was not a dead language of a destroyed kingdom.
It was one of the three politically necessary languages for a Persian king addressing his subjects — alongside his own Old Persian and the culturally dominant Babylonian.

All three languages had to be there for the inscription to do its political work, and the Elamite version was written first — it was the first of the three versions to be carved on the cliff, and the other two were added afterward.
That chronological priority is telling.

When Darius commissioned the inscription, he commissioned the Elamite version first, because Elamite was the working language of his imperial bureaucracy and the natural default for expressing the royal message to the kingdom's scribal-administrative class.
The Behistun Inscription is also the most important single document in the history of deciphering cuneiform.

When European scholars in the early nineteenth century began trying to decipher the cuneiform writing systems of the ancient Near East, they had three unknowns — three mostly-unknown languages written in related but distinct cuneiform scripts — and almost no bilingual texts to work from.
Behistun provided the first major bilingual anchor.

The Old Persian version of the inscription was simpler than the other two (the Persian cuneiform system was a mostly alphabetic script rather than a fully logographic one) and could be worked out first through careful linguistic analysis.

The breakthrough came in 1837 when the British army officer Henry Rawlinson, climbing up the cliff face on ropes to make paper impressions of the inscriptions, produced the first reliable copies of the Old Persian text.

Working from those copies, and building on earlier decipherment efforts by the German scholar Georg Friedrich Grotefend, Rawlinson and his colleagues were able to decipher Old Persian cuneiform by the 1840s.

Once Old Persian was readable, the Babylonian cuneiform version of Behistun became effectively a Rosetta Stone for Babylonian Akkadian — which was then used as a Rosetta Stone for Sumerian and eventually for all the other cuneiform scripts of the ancient Near East.
The Elamite version of Behistun was deciphered along with the other two, though with more difficulty because Elamite has no living relatives to cross-reference.
Modern readings of the Elamite text are still being refined, and a few phrases remain uncertain.

But the broad content is clear: the text tells the same story as the Old Persian and Babylonian versions, describes Darius's victory over his rivals and his establishment of Persian rule, and uses the phrase "king of Anshan" (in the Elamite version, the Elamite phrase is written directly rather than being a Persian loan) as part of Darius's royal titulature.
Darius, like Cyrus before him, was a king of Anshan.
The Elamite royal tradition ran through the whole Achaemenid period as a live, active, formally recognized element of Persian imperial self-presentation.
The Persians never pretended that the old Elamite kingdom was an alien thing they had replaced.
They presented themselves, officially, as its heirs.
Here is the final, underappreciated fact of this whole book: Elam never actually died.

What died in 646 BCE, when Ashurbanipal sacked Susa and carried off the bones of the Elamite kings, was a specific political entity — the late Neo-Elamite kingdom that had descended from Shutruk-Nahhunte's imperial state through three and a half centuries of decline and fragmentation.
That kingdom was finished.
No new Elamite royal line would ever again rule an independent Elamite state after Ashurbanipal's destruction.
But the civilization continued.
The language kept being spoken across southwestern Iran for several more centuries, probably as late as the middle of the first millennium CE in some form.
The scribal tradition kept producing cuneiform tablets in Elamite for the Persian Empire's administration until the fourth century BCE.
The royal title "king of Anshan" kept being used by the Achaemenid dynasty as one of their core legitimizing titles all the way down to Alexander the Great's conquest.

The pantheon — Humban, Kiririsha, Nahhunte, Inshushinak, Pinikir — kept being worshipped in local Elamite temples for centuries after the fall of Susa, and the god Humban's name kept appearing in personal names (as a theophoric element) into the Parthian period of the second century BCE (Persian Empire Ch 6-7).

The city of Susa itself was rebuilt after Ashurbanipal's destruction, became an Achaemenid administrative center, was visited regularly by the Persian kings, was later occupied by Alexander the Great's successors, survived into the Parthian and Sasanian periods, and was still a functioning city when the Muslim conquest of Iran began in the seventh century CE.

Susa was continuously inhabited for over six thousand years — from the earliest fourth-millennium BCE village to the medieval Islamic period — one of the longest-running urban continuities in human history.

The Persian Empire we remember from Herodotus and the Hebrew Bible, the empire that fought the Greeks at Marathon and the empire that Alexander the Great conquered, was built on an Elamite foundation.
It used Elamite language, Elamite scribal methods, Elamite administrative vocabulary, Elamite royal legitimacy, and Elamite religious continuity.
It treated the Elamite heritage not as a conquered enemy's history but as its own deep ancestral past.

Cyrus and Darius and Xerxes and all the later Achaemenid kings were, in their own official self-presentation, the legitimate heirs of the Elamite kings whose sequence reached all the way back to the Proto-Elamite period.

And when Alexander the Great, at the head of his Macedonian army, burned Persepolis in 330 BCE, he was unknowingly destroying an imperial capital that was the direct administrative descendant of the scribal tradition that had begun at Susa in 2000 BCE.
The cuneiform tablets that burned in the Persepolis fire were Elamite in language, in script, in scribal convention, and in bureaucratic lineage.
The Elamite civilization had outlasted the Elamite kingdom by nearly three centuries, embedded inside the Persian imperial state as its working bureaucratic backbone.
Only with Alexander's arrival did the chain finally break.
Even then, something survived.
The Elamite language is last attested in documentary form in a few late Parthian-era tablets from the first century BCE, by which point it was a regional Iranian language on the edge of extinction.

Somewhere around the turn of the first millennium CE, the last native speakers stopped speaking it, and Elamite became a dead language for the first time in its roughly three-thousand-year history as a spoken tongue.

It left behind no living descendants — it is a language isolate even in death — but it also left behind one of the most thoroughly documented ancient civilizations in the Near East, because the Persian Empire's use of Elamite as its administrative language produced such an enormous documentary archive that modern scholarship has been able to reconstruct the language in unusual detail.
The bones Ashurbanipal carried off from Susa have never been recovered.

They were presumably interred or destroyed somewhere in Assyria after the sack of Nineveh in 612 BCE, and the ritual harm Ashurbanipal had intended to inflict on the ghosts of the Elamite kings was indeed accomplished, as much as such things can be accomplished.
But the civilization whose kings those bones had been finished the job of outlasting the kingdom that destroyed them.

When a Persian scribe in 509 BCE wrote "give thirty liters of barley to the workers at the royal estate" in Elamite cuneiform, he was continuing a scribal tradition that his teacher had learned from his teacher back through an unbroken chain of Elamite scribal instruction.

The ghosts of the Elamite kings did not find rest, but their language and their scribes and their administrative system lived on as the skeleton of the largest empire the ancient world had ever built.
That is the actual ending of the Elamite story.

Not "destroyed by Ashurbanipal in 646 BCE." Destroyed and re-absorbed and re-emerged and kept running for three more centuries as the imperial bureaucracy of Persia, visible in thirty thousand clay tablets at Persepolis and in the middle carved version of a trilingual royal inscription on a cliff at Behistun.

The Iranian Plateau chain continues with the story that grows out of this one — the Achaemenid Persian Empire proper, with Cyrus and Darius and Xerxes, the Persian Wars, the conquest of Babylon, the road system, the satrapies, the final confrontation with Alexander (Persian Empire Ch 1-5), and then onward through the Parthian and Sassanid dynasties that kept the Iranian idea alive for another eight centuries (Persian Empire Ch 6-10).
That is the next book in this chain.

If you come to it after reading this one, you will come to it knowing something most books about the Achaemenid Empire do not mention: the Persians were not simply Iranian newcomers replacing Semitic Mesopotamians.

They were a hybrid people, Persian in language and Elamite in bureaucracy, whose deepest inherited tradition reached back not to the steppes where their Indo-European ancestors had come from but to the cities of southwestern Iran where Susa and Anshan had stood for three thousand years.
The great Persian kings who will dominate that next book were, in their own bones and in their own writing and in their own officials, a continuation of Elam.

chapters.title: The Green Sahara and the Refugees
sentences: The desert wasn't always a desert.
If you went to North Africa nine thousand years ago and walked west from the Nile Valley, you would not be walking into the Sahara as we know it.

You would be walking into a savanna with shallow lakes, slow rivers, grass growing up to your knees, herds of cattle and gazelle, hippos in the water, crocodiles on the riverbanks, and stands of acacia trees thick enough to throw shade.
There were people living out there.

They herded long-horned cattle, fished the lakes with bone harpoons, hunted wild game, and painted the rocks of their shelters with images of swimming humans, dancing figures, and animals they would later watch die out as the water disappeared.
This is not a thing most people know about, so it is worth saying clearly up front.

The Sahara — the largest hot desert on Earth, the brown blank space that fills North Africa on every modern map — was, for most of the time period this book covers up to about 5000 BCE, a green and habitable landscape.
Geologists call this period the African Humid Period, or sometimes the Green Sahara (terms that are used interchangeably to describe the same wet phase).
It ran from roughly 9000 BCE to 5000 BCE, give or take a few centuries on either end depending on the region, and it ended with one of the most dramatic environmental collapses in human history.
The desert came back, slowly at first and then catastrophically, and the people and animals who had been living in the Green Sahara had to get out — or die.
Most of them ended up beside the Nile.
This is where Egypt comes from.
The pharaohs and the pyramids are not the beginning of the story.

They are the end of a much longer story, in which the survivors of a drying continent crowded into one of the few places that still had reliable water and started building the civilization the rest of the world would eventually call ancient Egypt.
Before there were pharaohs there were farmers.
Before there were farmers there were refugees.
Before there were refugees there was a green Sahara that nobody who could read or write was around to remember.
We are going to start with that Sahara, because without it nothing else makes sense.
The reason the Sahara was green is the same reason it is brown today: monsoons.
A monsoon is a seasonal wind that reverses direction with the changing of the year (the word comes from an Arabic word for "season").

In the modern world, the most famous monsoon is the South Asian one — every summer, warm air rising off the heated Indian subcontinent pulls in moisture-laden winds from the Indian Ocean, and those winds dump rain on India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh from June through September before reversing in winter.

Africa has its own monsoon system, called the West African Monsoon, which works the same way: in summer, hot air rising off the Sahara pulls in moist air from the Atlantic and the Gulf of Guinea, and that moist air dumps rain on the southern edge of the desert before it runs out of water.
Today, that rainfall mostly stays well south of the Sahara proper, watering the Sahel (the semi-arid grass belt south of the desert) and stopping there.

But during the African Humid Period, the monsoon was much stronger — the rain belt pushed hundreds of miles further north, and the heart of what is now the Sahara got enough summer rain to fill lakes and grow grass.
Why was the monsoon stronger?
The shortest answer is that Earth's orbit was wobbling.
Over very long timescales, the way Earth tilts and orbits the Sun changes in slow regular cycles.

About 11,000 years ago, the tilt of Earth's axis and the timing of its closest approach to the Sun were both in configurations that made Northern Hemisphere summers significantly warmer than they are today.
Hotter summers in North Africa meant a stronger pressure differential between the heated land and the cooler ocean, which meant a more powerful monsoon, which meant rain in the desert.
The whole system was driven by tiny, glacially slow shifts in planetary geometry.
As those shifts continued — as the orbital configuration drifted away from the optimum — the monsoon weakened.
The rain belt slid southward.
The lakes evaporated.
The grasses died.
The desert came back.
The collapse, when it finally arrived, did not happen smoothly.
For most of the African Humid Period, the Sahara was getting drier in slow incremental steps that nobody alive at the time would have noticed within a single lifetime.

But around 5500 BCE, the system seems to have crossed a tipping point — feedback loops kicked in (less vegetation meant less local moisture meant less rain meant less vegetation), and over the following few centuries the Green Sahara died off in a series of relatively rapid steps.
By 5000 BCE the central Sahara was already arid enough to be dangerous.
By 4000 BCE it was the desert we recognize on a modern map.
By 3000 BCE — when the first pharaoh sat down on the throne of a unified Egypt — the central Sahara was as empty and as dry as it is now.
Imagine being a cattle herder in this story.
Your father's father's father was a cattle herder in the same valley you grew up in.
The waterhole has been getting smaller every year for as long as anyone can remember.
The grass is patchier.
The herd is thinner.
Some years you can still graze in the old spots; some years you have to drive the cattle further than you ever did before.
Everyone in the village knows it's getting worse.
Some of the families have already left, walking east toward the rumor of a great river that nobody has ever seen but that is supposed to flood every summer no matter what the rains do.
Some of the families are staying because their grandmother is buried under that rock and they're not going to leave her.
Eventually the waterhole goes dry for a whole year and everybody who's still there has to make a choice.
This is the human face of climate collapse, and it played out over two thousand years across an area larger than the United States.
Most of the people who made the long walk east are anonymous.
Most of the herds that walked with them are anonymous.
But they walked.

The clearest evidence we have that this happened — and that the people who made the walk brought a coherent culture with them when they did — comes from a place called Nabta Playa, in the desert of southern Egypt about 60 miles (100 km) west of the Nile.
If you visit Nabta Playa today, you will find a flat dusty pan in the middle of an absolutely empty expanse of sand and rock.
There is nothing growing for as far as the eye can see.
The nearest road is two hours away.
The nearest town is half a day's drive.
Standing in the middle of the Nabta Playa basin you cannot really conceive of why anyone would have come here, much less why anyone would have built anything here.
Six thousand years ago, this place was a lake.

The "playa" in the name is a geographer's word for a lake bed that fills only seasonally — during the African Humid Period, the Nabta basin filled up every summer when the monsoon rains came, and dried out every winter when the rains stopped.
Cattle-herding people moved into the basin during the wet season to graze their herds on the temporary pastureland the lake supported, and moved out again when the lake disappeared.
They did this for centuries.
And around 4800 BCE, they did something extraordinary.
They built a calendar.

In the middle of what had been the dry lake bed, a group of these herders raised a ring of standing stones — flat slabs of sandstone wedged upright in the ground, set in a rough circle about 13 feet (4 meters) across.
The stones are not large by Stonehenge standards (Stonehenge wouldn't be built for another 1,800 years) but they are deliberately arranged.
Two pairs of stones inside the circle define sight lines.
One sight line points to the rising sun on the summer solstice — the longest day of the year, which in southern Egypt is exactly when the monsoon was about to break and fill the lake.
Another sight line aligns with the rising of certain bright stars at specific points in the year.
The whole arrangement is a working astronomical observatory, used by people who needed to know, with reasonable precision, when the rains were coming.
Nabta Playa is the oldest known astronomical site in Africa.
It predates Stonehenge by roughly two thousand years.

It is approximately contemporary with the very earliest Mesopotamian temple platforms in southern Iraq, where the first cities of the Sumerian world were also just beginning to come into focus (Mesopotamia Ch 1).

The fact that astronomy this sophisticated was being practiced in the Sahara before any pyramid was built, before any pharaoh existed, before any hieroglyph was carved — by cattle herders, in what was already turning into a desert — is one of the most under-appreciated facts about the deep past of African civilization.
Around the stone circle, archaeologists have found something else: pits with whole cattle interred under stone mounds.
These are not animal corpses thrown into the trash.
They are buried with care, with the kind of attention reserved for something sacred.
The cattle were not eaten.
They were sacrificed.
The Nabta Playa people had a cattle cult — a religion in which the cow was a being with spiritual significance, important enough to be buried like a person under a stone tumulus.
This matters because Egyptian religion, three thousand years later, is full of cows.

The goddess Hathor (HATH-or) is depicted with a cow's head, or as a woman with cow's ears and horns, and is one of the central deities of the pharaonic pantheon — patron of love, motherhood, music, drunkenness, foreign lands, and the western horizon where the sun sets.

The bull god Apis (AY-pis) was worshipped at Memphis as the living embodiment of a divine cow-spirit, and a real living bull selected for specific markings was kept in a temple, fed the best food, paraded through the city on festival days, and mummified at death.
The sky goddess Nut (noot) was sometimes pictured as a cow standing over the Earth.
Cattle horns appear on the headdresses of queens.
Cattle counts were the basic unit of Egyptian taxation through the Old Kingdom (we will meet this practice again in Chapter 7).
The cow was, throughout Egyptian history, the single most religiously charged animal in the entire pantheon.
Nabta Playa is where that obsession comes from.
The pharaohs did not invent their cow goddess in the Nile Valley.
They imported her from cattle herders fleeing the encroaching dunes.

The cattle cult walked east across the drying Sahara with the people who practiced it, and when those people landed beside the Nile and started farming, they brought the cows and the religion together.
So what did the Nile look like when the refugees arrived?

The Nile is the longest river in the world, depending on how you measure it, and it has the additional unusual property of running south-to-north for almost its entire length. (This is unintuitive enough that it is worth explaining: the headwaters of the Nile are in equatorial central Africa, in the highlands south of the Sahara, and the river flows downhill from those highlands all the way north to the Mediterranean Sea.
Most people imagining a river in their head picture water flowing south, because most rivers in Europe and North America happen to do so.
The Nile flows the other way.
"Up" the Nile means south, toward the source; "down" the Nile means north, toward the sea.

Egyptians used the river's direction as their cardinal compass — south was upstream and north was downstream, baked into the language, with the result that early pharaohs depicted on monuments are sometimes shown wearing the crown of "Upper" Egypt — meaning southern Egypt, the part nearer the headwaters.) For the predynastic period, the only part of the Nile that matters is the last 700 miles or so, from where the river crosses out of what is now Sudan through the First Cataract at Aswan and then runs north through Egypt to the Mediterranean. (A cataract is a stretch of rapids and rocky channel where the river runs over hard bedrock — not a single waterfall but a long unnavigable obstacle that blocks boat traffic.
There are six numbered cataracts on the Nile, all of them south of Egypt proper.
The First Cataract at Aswan was Egypt's southern frontier for most of dynastic history.

Everything south of it was Nubia, the subject of the Ancient Nubia book in this series.) North of Aswan, the Nile runs through a narrow valley about 10 to 15 miles (16 to 24 km) wide, walled in on both sides by desert cliffs.

The valley is green for the same reason it is green today: every summer, monsoon rains over the Ethiopian highlands swell the Blue Nile, which dumps a torrent of muddy water into the main Nile far upstream, and that torrent reaches Egypt as the annual inundation — the famous yearly flood that, until the building of the Aswan High Dam in the 1960s, used to coat the entire Egyptian floodplain in a fresh layer of fertile silt every September.
That silt is what made Egyptian agriculture possible.
It was free natural fertilizer, deposited every year without fail, in volumes large enough to keep the soil productive across forty centuries of intensive farming.
The annual inundation is the central fact of Egyptian existence; everything else flows from it.
Around 5500 BCE, as the Sahara was crossing its tipping point, the Nile floodplain was already starting to fill up with people who had walked east.
They were not sophisticated farmers yet.

They were a mixture of refugee herders from the dying west, indigenous Nile fisher-foragers, and small bands moving down the river from south, north, and along the Mediterranean coast from the Levant (the eastern Mediterranean coastal region — modern Israel, Palestine, Lebanon, and coastal Syria — which was the Egyptian word for what we would now call the Middle East).

All of them were converging on the same narrow strip of fertile land, and they were bringing with them, between them, the components of a complete agricultural package: domesticated wheat and Barley from the Levant, domesticated sheep and goats from the same region, domesticated cattle from the Sahara, fishing technologies from the Nile itself.
The Nile Valley in 5500 BCE was a melting pot in the most literal sense — different cultural traditions colliding inside a tight green ribbon and being forced to deal with each other.
Out of that collision, the first identifiable Egyptian farming villages took shape.
The earliest one we can put a name on is called the Fayum A culture, after the modern district where it was found.

The Fayum (fy-OOM) is a depression in the desert about 50 miles (80 km) southwest of modern Cairo, where a side branch of the Nile feeds into a natural basin and creates a permanent lake — not the same kind of seasonal playa as Nabta but a real standing body of water, surrounded by green farmland even today.

In the predynastic period the lake was much larger than it is now, and its shores were attractive to settlers because you could fish the lake, hunt the marshes, and farm the strip of land between the water and the desert all at the same time.
Around 5200 BCE, a community of these settlers started doing something nobody in Egypt had done before: they planted crops and stored the harvest.
The crops were Emmer wheat and barley.
Emmer wheat is an old strain of wheat, the immediate ancestor of the modern bread wheats, that produces a hard kernel inside a tough husk.

It was first domesticated in southeastern Turkey and northern Syria around 9000 BCE — that is to say, about 4,000 years before it shows up in the Fayum — and from there it spread west across the Mediterranean and east into Iran, Pakistan, and eventually the Indus Valley, where it would become the staple of another nascent civilization (Indus Valley Ch 1).
When emmer reached Egypt, it found a climate that suited it perfectly: planted just after the autumn flood waters receded, ripening in the cool dry winter, harvested before the heat of summer set in.
Barley is a cousin of wheat, even more drought-tolerant, and it was domesticated in the same region at the same time.
Both crops moved together as a package across the prehistoric world.
Both arrived in Egypt as cultural imports rather than independent inventions.
The Fayum A people did not just grow these crops.
They did something slightly more impressive: they figured out how to store them.
If you want to be a settled farmer rather than a wandering forager, you have to solve the problem of what to do with a year's worth of grain between harvests.
Grain is heavy, perishable, and attractive to mice, weevils, and thieves.
The Fayum A solution was to dig storage pits — straight-sided holes in the ground, lined with woven basketwork or matting, sometimes capped with a flat stone — and bury the grain inside.
Over a hundred such pits have been found at the major Fayum A sites, some still containing carbonized grain that survived the millennia because the pits got sealed and forgotten.
The grain pits are the technological signature of the Fayum A culture and the moment Egypt crossed the line from foraging to settled agriculture.
You cannot dig a hundred grain pits if you are still moving with the seasons.
You have to commit to a place.
Around the same time, similar communities were taking shape further north in the Nile Delta.

Merimde Beni-Salame (meh-RIM-deh ben-ee sa-LAH-meh, usually shortened to just Merimde) is the most thoroughly excavated of these — a sprawling settlement on the western edge of the Delta where, by about 4800 BCE, several hundred people were living in oval mud huts, growing wheat and barley, herding sheep and goats and a few cattle, and burying their dead inside the village in shallow graves.
Merimde is unusual among early Egyptian sites in that the houses are densely packed, suggesting something closer to a real village than a scatter of family homesteads.

Further south on the eastern Delta edge, a culture called El-Omari (al-oh-MAR-ee) was doing something similar: oval houses, grain pits, sheep, goats, the standard Neolithic farming kit. (One word that is going to come up constantly in this book: Neolithic means "new stone" in Greek and refers to the period of human prehistory after agriculture and animal domestication had been invented but before metalworking became widespread.
A "Neolithic" village is a stone-tool farming village.

The end of the Neolithic in any given region is roughly when copperworking starts, which in Egypt happens around 4000 BCE and is the topic of the next chapter.) The picture you should hold in your head for this earliest phase of Egyptian farming, around 5000 BCE, is something like this.
The Sahara to the west is dying and emptying out.
The Nile Valley is filling up with refugees from that collapse plus indigenous fisher-foragers plus small farming communities trickling in from the Levant.
Different groups are settling different reaches of the river, each with their own pottery and burial customs.
They are growing imported wheat and barley alongside their fishing and hunting.
They are running cattle that they brought with them from the dying Sahara.
They have a religion built around those cattle that traces back to the Nabta Playa stone circle and beyond.

They are not yet anything we would recognize as "Egyptian" — there is no Egyptian state, no Egyptian writing, no Egyptian art style, no shared identity that connects the Fayum villager to the Merimde villager to the El-Omari villager to the cattle herder camped at a dry waterhole somewhere in the Western Desert.
But the ingredients of all of those things are now present, in the same ribbon of land, at the same time, for the first time in history.
The mixing bowl is full.
It is going to take another two thousand years to start producing a recognizable Egyptian civilization out of it.
But the bowl is full.
It is worth zooming out for a moment to remember that none of this is happening in isolation.

At the same time the Fayum A farmers are digging their grain pits, the Sumerian world in southern Iraq is just beginning to coalesce into the first identifiable cultures of the Mesopotamian alluvium — the Hassuna and Samarra and Halaf cultures of northern Mesopotamia, and a little later the Ubaid culture of the south, the immediate ancestors of Sumerian civilization (Mesopotamia Ch 1).
They are growing the same emmer wheat and the same barley, herding the same sheep and goats, building the same kinds of mudbrick villages.

They are not Egyptian and they are not in contact with Egypt yet — there is no evidence of direct exchange between the early Nile farmers and the early Mesopotamian farmers in this period, and the two cultures were developing in parallel rather than in conversation.

But they were drawing from the same Neolithic toolkit, which had spread west and south out of the original domestication zone in southeastern Turkey, and they were going to end up in the same general place — sedentary villages, social hierarchy, religion, craft specialization, the road to cities — at roughly the same time.

Further east, on the western edge of the Indus floodplain, a culture called Mehrgarh (MAIR-gar) had been farming the same wheat and barley package since about 7000 BCE and was now experimenting with cotton, copper, and dental drilling.
They were the seed culture from which the Indus Valley civilization would eventually grow (Indus Valley Ch 1).

In the Yellow River valley of northern China, completely independently, a different Neolithic tradition was domesticating millet, raising pigs, and building villages of semi-subterranean houses (Ancient China Ch 1).
They were on a separate track and would stay on it for thousands of years before the silk roads reconnected them with the western half of Eurasia.
Five regions of the world were running the same experiment in parallel: take a fertile river valley, add domesticated crops, add a few centuries of settled village life, and see what happens.
In every one of those five regions, the answer turned out to be the same.
Settled villages produce surplus.
Surplus produces specialists.
Specialists produce hierarchy.
Hierarchy produces religion-backed elites, and elites build monuments.
The first monuments produce the first cities, and the first cities produce the first kings.
But that arc takes time.
In Egypt around 5000 BCE, none of that has happened yet.
There are villages and grain pits and a memory of cattle in the Sahara, and that is almost all there is.

The next chapter is about what happens when those villages start producing something that is recognizably Egyptian — a distinctive material culture, distinctive burials, distinctive pottery, the first hints of social inequality, and the first artisans whose work survives well enough to be recognized across the millennia.
That story starts on the east bank of the Nile, in a place called El-Badari, around 4400 BCE.

chapters.title: Badarians and the Birth of an Egyptian Look
sentences: If you handed an archaeologist a single Pottery sherd from anywhere along the Nile and asked them to tell you the moment "Egyptian" culture began — the first thing on the ground that you could pick up and recognize as belonging to the same tradition that would eventually build the pyramids — they would, almost without hesitation, point to the Badarian.

The Badarian was the first culture along the Nile that produced a material signature distinctive enough, refined enough, and widely shared enough that you can call it a coherent cultural tradition rather than just a regional grouping of villages.

It runs from roughly 4400 to 3800 BCE — about six hundred years — and it is named for El-Badari (el-bah-DAR-ee), the modern Egyptian district where its first cemeteries were excavated by the British archaeologist Guy Brunton in the 1920s.

Badari is on the east bank of the Nile in middle Egypt, about 200 miles (320 km) south of modern Cairo, and it sits in a stretch of the river where the floodplain widens out enough to support real farming villages with real surplus.

The original Badarian sites were a string of cemeteries cut into a desert escarpment overlooking the cultivated land — graves dug into the dry sand at the edge of the green strip, where the bodies and the grave goods would survive intact for the next six thousand years.
What makes the Badarian recognizable is not any one thing.

It is a package — pottery plus burials plus craft plus subsistence plus religion — that hangs together as a coherent way of being a Nile farmer, and the package shows up across enough of the Nile Valley, from the Faiyum down to as far south as the future Nubian border, that it is clearly not a single village's local style.
It is a whole cultural region thinking in the same idiom.
The most famous element of that package is the pottery.

The signature Badarian pot is a small bowl or beaker, hand-built without a wheel, polished to a high shine, and fired in a way that turns the bottom of the pot a deep glossy red and the rim of the pot a deep matte black.
This sounds like a strange combination, and it took me a while to understand the trick.
Here is how it works.
Pottery is fired clay, and the color of the finished pot depends almost entirely on how much oxygen is present during the firing.
If oxygen is plentiful — what potters call an oxidizing atmosphere — the iron in the clay turns red, and you get red pots.
If oxygen is scarce — what potters call a reducing atmosphere — the iron stays in a different chemical state, and you get gray or black pots.
Modern potters control this with the airflow on a kiln.
Badarian potters did not have kilns.
They fired their pots in shallow open pits, in a fuel of dung or twigs, and they figured out a clever workaround.
They stood the pots upside down, rim-first, in the firing pit.
The bottom of the pot stuck up out of the fuel into the open air, where it got plenty of oxygen and turned a glossy red.
The rim of the pot was buried deep in the fuel and the ash, where it was deprived of oxygen and turned a deep black.
The transition between the two zones, at the height of the ash line, was sharp.

The result was the famous black-topped red ware: a single pot, made from a single batch of clay, with a brilliant red lower body and a jet-black upper rim, the boundary between the two colors razor-clean.
It is technically beautiful.

It also seems to have been religiously meaningful — though we have to be honest that we can't read the minds of people who didn't write things down, and any guess about what a Badarian pot meant to its maker is a guess.

The leading theory, based on the fact that black-topped pots are concentrated in graves and not in everyday domestic contexts, is that the red-and-black scheme had something to do with the duality between the fertile floodplain (Egyptian "kemet," literally "the black land," referring to the dark silt deposited by the inundation) and the surrounding desert (Egyptian "deshret," "the red land," referring to the rust-colored sand and rock outside the floodplain).

Black equals life, life equals the river, the river equals the Nile, and the Nile is at the bottom — so the upside-down pot in the firing pit was, in a sense, the cosmos in miniature, with the black band of life floating above the red expanse of the desert.
This is a guess.
But the same red-black duality runs all the way through later pharaonic art and language, and the pots are the earliest place we see it.
Even if the meaning is uncertain, the craft is not.
Badarian black-topped ware is technically excellent.

The walls are thin (some pieces are barely a few millimeters thick), the surfaces are burnished to a glassy shine, and the firing is consistently controlled. (To burnish a pot is to polish its surface, before firing, by rubbing it with a smooth pebble until the clay particles align and reflect light.

Burnishing is slow, repetitive, manual work, and it produces a finish that no glaze can quite imitate.) Some pieces have a subtle rippled texture along the body — fingertip ridges left by the burnishing tool, deliberately preserved as decoration — and these "rippled ware" pieces are the highest-status pottery of the period, found mainly in the richest graves.

For comparison: at this same moment in southern Mesopotamia, the Ubaid culture was making coarser, plainer pottery with painted decoration, fired in early kilns at lower technical sophistication (Mesopotamia Ch 1).

Mesopotamian pottery would catch up and eventually surpass Egyptian craft once the potter's wheel was invented around 3500 BCE, but in the early 5th millennium, the people on the Nile were the better potters in the Bronze Age world.
They were also already producing pottery that was beautiful enough to bury, which tells you something about the value system.
The second great Badarian innovation is the cemetery itself.
A cemetery in the technical sense is a dedicated burial ground, separate from the living settlement, organized as a deliberate spatial arrangement of graves.
This is not how all early human societies dealt with their dead.
Many Neolithic peoples buried their relatives under the floors of the houses they had lived in, or in the corner of the yard, or in trash middens, or in caves that were also used for other purposes.
The decision to dedicate a piece of ground exclusively to the dead, away from the living village, is a real cultural choice — and it implies several things.
It implies that you expect to keep returning to the same spot.
It implies that the relationship between the dead and the land is important enough to mark in space.
It implies, eventually, that you can tell who is buried near whom and that those spatial relationships carry social meaning.
Badarian people made that choice.

They buried their dead in shared cemeteries cut into the desert sand at the edge of the cultivated strip — close to the village, but on the other side of the boundary between the farmed black land and the unfarmed red land.
The dead were laid on their left sides, knees drawn up to the chest in a fetal position, head pointing south, face pointing west.
The orientation matters: by the time of the historical pharaohs, west would be the direction of the setting sun and the realm of the dead, and tombs would face west for that reason.
The Badarian dead were already facing west three thousand years before any pharaoh.
The convention had been settled before there was anyone alive who could explain why.
Most graves were simple oval pits, dug just deep enough to hold the body, sometimes lined with reed mats or animal hides.

The body was usually wrapped in Linen or the skin of a gazelle or goat, and the grave was filled with goods for the afterlife: pottery, food offerings, jewelry, cosmetic palettes, ivory combs, and sometimes small clay or ivory figurines.
It is the figurines that get archaeologists excited.
A figurine is a small handmade sculpture of a person or animal, usually under a foot in height.

Badarian figurines are mostly women — stylized standing female figures, sometimes nude, sometimes with exaggerated hips or breasts, often with their arms folded across their chests or held at their sides.
The faces are abstracted; the bodies are not.
They are clearly ritual objects of some kind, but we don't know exactly what kind.

The most popular guess is that they are fertility figures, related to childbirth or the fertility of the fields, but it is also possible that they represent specific dead women, or goddesses, or ancestors, or all three at once.
They are made of unfired clay or carved ivory or polished stone.
They appear in graves of women and men alike.
They are the earliest sculpture from the Nile Valley that you can hold in your hand and recognize as religious art.
Alongside the figurines, Badarian graves often contain cosmetic palettes: small flat slabs of stone, usually green schist or siltstone, with a shallow depression worn into one face.

These were used for grinding mineral pigments — green malachite (a copper ore that produces a brilliant green powder), black galena (a lead ore that produces black), and red ochre (an iron-rich earth pigment) — into the powdery cosmetics that Egyptians of every era wore around their eyes.
The palette plus the pigment equals the eye paint, and the eye paint had practical and ritual functions both.

Practically, the dark pigment around the eyes reduced glare from the desert sun and may have had antibacterial properties that protected against the chronic eye infections endemic to a culture that lived in dust.

Ritually, the painted eye was associated, eventually, with the protection of the gods — the Eye of Horus in particular would become one of the most powerful protective symbols in Egyptian religion, appearing on amulets, sarcophagi, and the prows of ships for the next four thousand years.
The Badarian palettes are the very earliest direct evidence we have of this practice.

They are also, in their later evolved form, going to become the ceremonial palettes of the unification era — the same flat-stone-with-a-grinding-depression format, scaled up and elaborately carved, that the Narmer Palette will eventually be (Chapter 5).
Some Badarian graves are noticeably richer than others.
They contain more pottery, more figurines, more imported goods, more elaborate burial wrappings, more space.
A few outlier graves contain so much more that the gap between them and the average grave is clearly meaningful.

Social hierarchy — the existence of haves and have-nots within a single community — is showing up in the archaeological record for the first time on the Nile, and it is showing up the way it almost always does: in the cemetery, where grave wealth is the most easily preserved record of what your village thought you were worth.
The third Badarian innovation, after pottery and cemeteries, is metal.
The Badarians worked copper.
Not very much of it, and not very skillfully by later standards, but enough to count.

Copper objects from Badarian graves are mostly small — beads, pins, rings, the occasional small chisel or awl — and they are mostly cold-hammered, which means the copper was beaten into shape with stone hammers without being melted first.
Cold-hammering works for native copper (copper that occurs naturally in metallic form, as opposed to bound up in copper ores) because pure copper is soft enough to be reshaped under pressure.
You take a nugget, beat it flat, fold it, beat it flat again, and gradually it turns into a sheet or a bead or a wire.
The downside of cold-hammering is that it work-hardens the metal — the more you hammer it, the more brittle it gets, until eventually it cracks.
The Badarians solved this with annealing: heating the work-hardened copper in a fire until it became soft again, then continuing to hammer.
This is not yet smelting (extracting metal from ore by chemical reduction), and it is not yet casting (melting metal and pouring it into a mold).

It is the first step on a technological road that, three thousand years later, will produce cast bronze statues of the gods at temple shrines and copper saws long enough to cut blocks for the Great Pyramid.

The copper itself probably came from Sinai — the desert peninsula east of the Nile delta, between Egypt and the Levant — where copper-bearing ores outcrop in several places along the western escarpment.
By the dynastic period, Egyptian state expeditions to Sinai for copper and turquoise would be a regular institution, organized at royal expense, and we will meet them in Chapter 6.

In the Badarian period, the trade was probably much more informal — small parties of travelers picking up nuggets of native copper from surface deposits and carrying them back to villages along the river.
But the link was already established.
From the very beginning, Egypt's metal supply ran east to the Sinai mines and back.
Copper is the start of the long Egyptian fascination with metal-as-status.
By the late Badarian, copper objects in graves are clearly markers of wealth — the people buried with copper beads were not the same people buried without them.
The copper trade was not creating commodities; it was creating elites.
The fourth Badarian innovation is linen.

Linen is cloth woven from the long fibers of the flax plant, a slim blue-flowered annual that grows well in cool wet conditions and was already being cultivated in the Levant by the time emmer wheat reached Egypt.
Flax produces edible seeds (what we now call linseed) and stems full of long strong fibers that can be soaked, separated, spun into thread, and woven into cloth.

Linen has several useful properties: it absorbs moisture and dries fast, it gets stronger when wet (where cotton gets weaker), it is naturally cool against the skin in hot climates, and it can be woven so finely that the finished cloth is almost translucent.
For a culture living in the Egyptian summer, it is the perfect textile.
The Badarians wove linen at a level of skill that is, frankly, hard to believe for a culture this early.
Linen scraps recovered from Badarian graves include fine plain weaves, twills, fringed edges, and pieces with deliberate decorative patterns woven into the fabric.
The thread is consistent, the weave is even, and the finished textiles were clearly produced by experienced artisans working with proper looms.

The earliest horizontal ground looms — flat looms staked into the ground, with the warp threads stretched between two beams — appear in Egypt around this time, and Badarian linen was almost certainly woven on them.
These are the same kind of looms shown in tomb paintings of weavers thousands of years later.
The technology, like so much else, was settled before history starts.
The Badarian dead were wrapped in linen.
The Badarian elite were wrapped in good linen.

By the time of the dynastic kings, the highest-status burials in Egypt would involve hundreds of yards of finely woven linen wound around the body — the basis of mummification, although true mummification with deliberate dehydration was still a thousand years in the future.
The wrapping practice is older than the embalming.
The Badarians started it.
So picture a Badarian village around 4000 BCE.
It sits on the edge of the Nile floodplain, at the boundary between the dark fertile silt and the dry red desert.
It is a cluster of round or oval mudbrick houses, maybe a few dozen of them, with reed-thatch roofs and dirt floors.
There is a granary or two, sheltered from the rain (such rain as there is) under a thatch awning.
Cattle and sheep and goats are kept in pens at the edge of the village, and they are driven out every morning to graze the floodplain after the harvest.
There are dogs around.
There are children playing in the dust.

Most days, most of the adults are working the fields — sowing emmer and barley after the autumn flood pulls back, harvesting in the spring, threshing on a flat patch of beaten earth, storing the grain in the village pits, fishing the river when the work eases off.
Some of the adults are doing other things.
There is at least one potter, because the pottery is consistent enough to be the work of specialists.
There is at least one weaver.
There is probably someone who cold-hammers copper.
There is at least one person who carves stone palettes.
There is someone who makes the figurines.

None of these people are full-time specialists in the modern sense — they all also farm or fish — but they are recognized as the village's experts, and the things they make get traded along the river to other villages that lack their particular skill.
When somebody dies, the body is washed, wrapped in linen, painted with cosmetics, and laid in a pit at the cemetery on the desert edge.
Family members put offerings in the grave: a few favorite pots, a comb, a palette, sometimes a figurine, sometimes a bead necklace, sometimes (if the family is rich) a small copper ring or pin.
The grave is filled in.
The mourners walk back to the village.
Life resumes.
Multiply that village by hundreds, scattered up and down the Nile from the Delta to the Nubian border, and you have the Badarian world.

Lower Egypt — the Delta and the area immediately south of it, the part of the river where it widens out into a fan of distributary channels before reaching the Mediterranean — was on a slightly different track in this period.
The most important Lower Egyptian site is Maadi (MAH-dee), a settlement on the eastern edge of the future site of Cairo, occupied from about 3900 to 3500 BCE.

Maadi has a different cultural signature from contemporary Upper Egyptian sites: the houses are different (some are partly subterranean, dug down into the ground), the burials are different (less elaborate, with fewer grave goods), the pottery is different, and the trade contacts are different.
Maadi has imported Levantine pottery in quantities that Badarian sites do not, and it has copper that came from the same Sinai sources but reached Maadi by overland routes rather than by river.
The Maadi people were not Badarians.
They were a parallel culture in Lower Egypt, in regular contact with the Levant via Sinai, somewhat less stratified than their Upper Egyptian contemporaries, and culturally distinct.
We will see this north-south split repeatedly in the coming chapters.

Lower Egypt and Upper Egypt were on different developmental paths through the entire predynastic period, and the eventual unification of the two halves under a single king will be hard-fought and hard-won precisely because the two halves had been doing things differently for centuries.
Around 3800 BCE, the Badarian phase shades into the next phase of Upper Egyptian culture, which archaeologists call Naqada I or the Amratian.
The transition is gradual — Badarian pottery doesn't disappear so much as evolve into Naqada I pottery, the same villages keep being occupied, the same craft traditions continue.

But something is changing in Upper Egypt during the late Badarian and early Naqada I that will accelerate dramatically over the next few centuries, and it is worth naming the change explicitly because it is the engine that drives the rest of this story.
The change is scale.
Upper Egyptian villages start getting bigger.
The richest graves start getting much, much richer than the poorest.

A few specific places — Hierakonpolis in particular — start growing into something larger than a village, something that looks more like a town and is starting to look (if you squint) like a small city.
Trade with distant places picks up.
Specialized craft production picks up.
Religion gets more elaborate.
The first elite burials with imported goods from a thousand miles away start to appear.

It is the beginning of what Egyptologists call the Naqada Sequence — the long centuries of Upper Egyptian development that will, by 3100 BCE, produce the first kings and the first unification of Egypt.
The Badarian was the foundation.
The Naqada period is when Upper Egypt builds the first floors on top of the foundation, and Lower Egypt slowly falls behind.
That is the next chapter.

Cross-civ check before we move on: at the moment the Badarian is shading into Naqada I, the Mesopotamian Ubaid culture is also entering its late phase, the irrigation networks of southern Iraq are starting to coalesce around proto-urban temple centers like Eridu (Mesopotamia Ch 1), and the city of Uruk is about to begin the explosive growth that will make it the first true city in the world.
Mesopotamia is roughly half a step ahead of Egypt at this moment in the broader race toward urbanism.

By the Naqada II period, Egyptian craftsmen will be importing the cylinder-seal idea, the niched-palace facade, and other Mesopotamian inventions across what may have been a real but indirect trade network running through Syria and the Sinai land bridge.
Egypt was not going to grow up in isolation.
Some of the most important things in the next chapter come from the east.

chapters.title: Naqada I and II: Upper Egypt Pulls Ahead
sentences: The Badarian was the foundation.
Naqada is where the building starts.

For the next thousand years — from about 3800 BCE down to 2900 BCE — the Upper Egyptian portion of the Nile Valley is dominated by a single coherent cultural tradition that archaeologists divide into three phases: Naqada I, Naqada II, and Naqada III.

The whole sequence is named for the modern village of Naqada (NAH-kah-dah), about 20 miles (32 km) north of Luxor on the west bank of the Nile, where Sir Flinders Petrie excavated a massive predynastic cemetery in 1894 and used the pottery sequence he found there to define the chronology.

Petrie was the first archaeologist to figure out, by carefully sorting pots into groups based on small evolutionary changes, that you could put the predynastic period in chronological order even without dates.
His Naqada sequence is still the backbone of how Egyptologists organize this era.

This chapter covers Naqada I and Naqada II — the first thousand years of the sequence, the period when Upper Egypt pulls definitively ahead of Lower Egypt and a small market town called Hierakonpolis turns into the closest thing the Nile Valley has yet seen to a capital city.
The next chapter will pick up at Naqada III, when actual writing and named kings show up in the archaeological record. (One quick name housekeeping note.
The two phases of the Naqada sequence covered in this chapter have alternate names you will sometimes see in older books and museum labels.
Naqada I is sometimes called the Amratian period, after the modern village of El-Amra in middle Egypt where the phase was first identified.
Naqada II is sometimes called the Gerzean period, after the modern village of El-Gerza in the Faiyum region.
The "Amratian/Gerzean" terminology is older and clunkier, and modern Egyptologists mostly use Naqada I/II/III instead, but if you read older sources you will encounter the place-name versions.
They mean the same thing.) The Naqada I phase, roughly 3800 to 3500 BCE, is in many ways a continuation and intensification of the Badarian.
Same kinds of villages, same kinds of farming, same kinds of cemeteries on the desert edge.
The pottery is recognizably descended from Badarian pottery — black-topped red ware is still the dominant style, though the shapes get more varied and the proportions of the vessels stretch out.

There is a new kind of decorated ware that Badarian potters didn't make: white cross-lined pottery, where designs are painted onto a polished red surface in white pigment after firing, often showing animals (hippos, antelope, elephants, fish), human figures, boats, and geometric patterns.
The cross-lined pots are the first real narrative art the Nile has produced.
Some of them show hunting scenes; some show what might be ritual processions; some show boats with cabins and oars.
They are the earliest direct visual record of how predynastic Egyptians thought about themselves and their world.
The graves get richer and more stratified.

The very richest Naqada I graves contain dozens of pots, hundreds of beads, ivory combs and pins, slate cosmetic palettes carved into animal shapes (turtles, hippos, fish, birds), copper objects, sometimes whole imported stone vessels.
The poorest graves, in the same cemeteries, contain almost nothing.

The gap between the richest and the poorest tomb is now wide enough that you cannot reasonably read it as anything other than a class system: a small number of households are accumulating wealth, displaying it, and burying it with their dead, while the great majority of the population is being buried with what they can scrape together.
Egypt has chiefs now.
They don't have crowns yet.
But they have inheritances large enough to be visible from four thousand years away.
The most important Naqada I site — and the place where the developmental gap between Upper and Lower Egypt becomes clearest — is Hierakonpolis.
Hierakonpolis is the Greek name for an ancient Egyptian town that was actually called Nekhen (NEH-ken).
Egyptologists use both names interchangeably, and you will see Hierakonpolis on most modern maps and Nekhen in most ancient texts.

The Greek name means "City of the Hawk" or "City of the Falcon," and it refers to the town's patron deity: a falcon god who would eventually be identified with Horus, the divine prototype of the Egyptian king.
Hierakonpolis sits on the west bank of the Nile in southern Upper Egypt, about 70 miles (110 km) downstream from Aswan, and the area around it has been almost continuously excavated since the 1890s.
It is one of the most thoroughly studied predynastic sites in Egypt, and it deserves the attention.
Almost everything that "ancient Egypt" eventually became can be traced in some form to choices that were being made at Hierakonpolis between 3700 and 3000 BCE.
Around 3700 BCE, Hierakonpolis was already significantly larger than any other settlement on the Nile.
It covered something like 75 acres (30 hectares) of mudbrick houses, workshops, granaries, and ritual structures along a kilometer-long stretch of riverbank.

Its population in the late Naqada I phase was probably between 5,000 and 10,000 people — not a city by Mesopotamian standards (Uruk would have ten times as many people at its peak, Mesopotamia Ch 2), but for the Nile Valley in 3700 BCE, this was a colossal concentration of human beings.
Most of the rest of Upper Egypt at this time consisted of villages of a few hundred people each.
Hierakonpolis was an outlier by an order of magnitude.
Why was it so big?
Geography helped.

The Hierakonpolis region had unusually wide stretches of cultivable floodplain on both sides of the river, plus access to the Wadi Abu Suffian — a desert wadi that ran west into the Eastern Sahara and gave the town's hunters and traders access to game, minerals, and the long-distance routes through what was already turning into desert.
The town sat at a natural crossroads of riverine and overland trade, in a place where the agricultural carrying capacity could support a population large enough to specialize.
What it did with that advantage was build things nobody else was building yet.
The first thing it built was a brewery.
This sounds anticlimactic until you understand the scale.

Beer in the predynastic Nile world was a staple food, not a luxury — it was made from coarsely ground emmer wheat and barley, partially fermented in shallow vats, mildly alcoholic, thick enough to drink as a meal, and rich in calories and B vitamins that the bread of the same period was often missing.
Egyptians of every era drank beer.
Workers were paid in beer.
Temples offered beer to the gods.
The pyramids were built by labor crews supplied with daily beer rations measured by the jar.

Beer was so central to Egyptian existence that the standard Egyptian phrase for "to die" was sometimes "to depart for beer." The brewery at Hierakonpolis, dated to around 3600 BCE, is the oldest large-scale beer production facility known anywhere in the world.
It consists of a row of huge ceramic vats — each capable of holding tens of gallons — set into the ground in a dedicated production area, with hearths and grain-grinding stations alongside.
The vats were large enough to produce significant volumes per batch — probably tens of gallons at a time — and the facility was clearly designed for continuous production rather than one-off brewing.
That is industrial production, in the literal sense: it could not have been consumed by the brewer's family.
It was being made for distribution — to feed laborers working on town projects, to provision ritual feasts, to supply the elite households that controlled the brewery in the first place.
The presence of an industrial brewery is by itself proof that Hierakonpolis had a centralized economy and a working class large enough to need feeding at scale.
Two thousand years before the pyramids, the same beer-and-bread provisioning system that would build the pyramids was already being practiced at Hierakonpolis.
Around the same time, the Hierakonpolis people built a temple.

The structure they built around 3500 BCE was a large rectangular enclosure of mudbrick and reed mats, with a central open-air courtyard, several inner chambers, and what appears to have been a wooden flagpole or totem pole at one end.
It was associated with cattle burials, with offerings of imported flint tools, with caches of carved ivory figurines and ceremonial pottery.
There is no mistaking it for a private house or a granary.

It was built as a sacred space, and it is the oldest confirmed temple in Egypt — the great-great-grandfather, structurally and conceptually, of every later Egyptian temple from the Step Pyramid complex at Saqqara to the colossal pylons of Karnak.

The architectural plan that the Hierakonpolis temple was using — open courtyard, screening walls, inner chambers, a tall pole or standard at the focal point — is the ancestor plan that all later Egyptian temples will iterate on for the next three thousand years.
We don't know exactly which god the Hierakonpolis temple was for.

The town's later patron was Horus the falcon, and the strong likelihood is that the early temple was already a Horus shrine — possibly the very first place where the falcon was worshipped as a high god.

If that's right, then everything later Egyptians believed about the divine kingship of the pharaoh as the earthly incarnation of Horus traces back to a mudbrick courtyard at Hierakonpolis around 3500 BCE.
And then there is the painted tomb.
In 1898, two Egyptologists named Frederick Green and James Quibell were excavating the Hierakonpolis cemetery and came across a burial they labeled, in their notes, as Tomb 100.
It was the largest grave they had found at the site — a rectangular chamber dug into the desert sand, lined with mudbrick walls, big enough to walk around inside.
The body and most of the grave goods had been looted in antiquity.
But the walls, against all archaeological probability, were still painted.
The paintings on the walls of Tomb 100 are the oldest preserved figural mural art in Egypt.

They date to around 3500 BCE — the very end of Naqada I, the cusp of Naqada II — and they show scenes that we can recognize as the seedbed of pharaonic iconography even though no pharaoh has yet existed.
There are boats with cabins and rowers.
There are hunters with bows and dogs.
There are bulls and lions and antelope.

There is at least one figure that appears to show what may be a smiting pose — a man standing over kneeling figures, one arm raised — though the paintings are badly faded and scholars disagree about the details of the composition.

If the identification is correct, it is an early ancestor of the iconic pose that, three thousand years later, will appear on every temple pylon in Egypt as the standard depiction of pharaoh-as-victor.
The visual vocabulary of kingship may be older than the kingship itself.
The figure in the smiting pose at Tomb 100 is not labeled with a name (there is no writing yet — that is the next chapter's story).
But the implication is clear: somebody was buried in Tomb 100 who wanted to be remembered as a man who killed his enemies.

That somebody was important enough to be buried in the largest tomb in the largest town on the Nile, and important enough that the artists who painted his tomb chose to depict him in the same pose that would later be reserved for kings.
We do not have a name to attach to the body.
But we have, at Hierakonpolis around 3500 BCE, the unmistakable signs of something that is no longer a chiefdom and is starting to look like a kingdom.
The Naqada II phase, roughly 3500 to 3300 BCE, picks up the momentum and accelerates it.
Naqada II is when Egyptian material culture stops looking like a regional Neolithic tradition and starts looking like the early phase of a real civilization.

The pottery shifts: the old black-topped red ware fades out, and the new dominant style is a buff-colored ware decorated with painted scenes of boats, animals, and ritual processions in red ochre on a creamy background.

These painted pots — typically tall jars with strap handles — are some of the most beautiful predynastic objects to come out of Egypt, and they tell us a lot about how Naqada II people thought about themselves.
The boats they painted have multiple cabins and oar-banks, the kind of vessel you would build only if you needed to move heavy cargo or large parties of people up and down the river.

The hunting scenes show elaborate compositions with hunters, dogs, prey, and standards (long poles topped with the symbol of a particular community or god — the same standards that will later appear in royal processions and in the iconography of the nomes).
The processions show what look like ritual events, with figures in distinctive headgear carrying staffs, leading captives, or making offerings.
Naqada II is also when Faience appears.
Faience (fy-AHNCE) is a wonderful and weird material.

It is not pottery in the usual sense — the body is not made of clay but of crushed quartz mixed with a small amount of clay or natron (a naturally occurring sodium compound used as a flux), shaped into beads or amulets, then fired at high temperature.
During firing, the natron migrates to the surface of the object and forms a glassy glaze that fuses to the quartz body.
The resulting object has a brilliant glossy surface in a color that ranges from pale turquoise to deep blue to green, depending on how much copper was added to the mix.
Faience is the world's first deliberately manufactured glaze, and Egypt invented it.

Once invented, the technology spread slowly to Mesopotamia and the Levant, where it would be used to make beads and inlays, but Egypt was the original center, and Egyptian faience workshops would dominate the trade in turquoise-blue ornaments for the next three thousand years.
The blue-green color of faience was not chosen randomly.

It was the color of the Nile in flood, the color of the sky over the river, the color of the goddess Hathor's preferred turquoise jewelry, and (eventually) the color associated with rebirth and the afterlife.

Egyptians would bury their dead with faience amulets shaped like scarabs, eyes of Horus, ankh signs, and the hippopotamus goddess Taweret because the color itself meant "alive" and "protected." All of that symbolic loading begins in Naqada II, when the first beads come out of the first kilns at Hierakonpolis and the surrounding workshops.
Naqada II is also when boats become a serious technology.

Earlier predynastic Egyptians used reed rafts made by lashing bundles of papyrus stalks together — adequate for a short crossing of the river but useless for long-distance trade or for carrying cargo.

Naqada II craftsmen started building sewn-plank boats: hulls made of cedar or acacia planks, fitted together edge-to-edge, drilled with small holes, and sewn together with rope made from grass or fiber. sewn-plank boats can be much larger than rafts, can carry heavy loads, can travel long distances under sail or oar, and (crucially) can be repaired by replacing individual planks.
The boats painted on the Naqada II pots are clearly sewn-plank boats.
They have masts, sails, multiple decks, oar-banks, and cabins.
They are merchant ships and military transports and the prototype of every Nile boat the pharaohs would ever sail.
Naqada II also sees the spread of basin irrigation.

The natural Nile flood deposits silt across the floodplain in a single sheet every September, but the water doesn't stay long — it drains back into the river within a few weeks. basin irrigation is the technique of building low earthen walls along the edges of natural depressions in the floodplain so that the floodwater fills the basin and stays there for a month or two, soaking the soil deeply and giving the planted crops time to germinate before the standing water drains away.

Basin irrigation is not the same as the canal-and-pump irrigation used in Mesopotamia (Mesopotamia Ch 2) — it is much simpler, much less labor-intensive, and much better suited to a flood-pulse river like the Nile.

But it requires coordination among neighboring villages to maintain the basin walls, and that coordination requires either a chief who can issue orders or a council that can make collective decisions.
Basin irrigation is one of the technologies that makes the case for centralized authority along the Nile.
By 3400 BCE, large stretches of the Upper Egyptian floodplain are being managed as a coordinated system of basins, and the people doing the coordinating are getting richer.
The Naqada II elite are getting much richer.
