Their tombs, by 3400 BCE, contain stone vessels carved from imported alabaster and basalt, gold ornaments, copper tools and weapons, ivory pendants, faience beads, and — most strikingly — small quantities of lapis lazuli. lapis lazuli (LAP-iss LAZ-yuh-lee) is a deep blue stone found in only a few places in the ancient world, the most important of which is the Sar-i Sang valley in the mountains of northeastern Afghanistan.

To reach Egypt, lapis had to be quarried in Afghanistan, traded across Iran or central Asia to Mesopotamia, traded across Mesopotamia to the Levant, traded across the Levant to the Sinai, and carried into Egypt.
The total distance is something like 3,000 miles (4,800 km) on foot, by donkey, and (eventually) by boat.
The Naqada II elites at Hierakonpolis and other Upper Egyptian centers had small but unmistakable quantities of lapis in their graves.
Somebody was running that 3,000-mile chain at least once in a while, and the only people who could afford to pay for the resulting bead were the very richest households on the Nile.
Lapis is the first hard evidence that Egyptian elites had become wealthy enough to participate in the proto-globalized trade networks of the Bronze Age.
Egyptian goldwork also gets serious in Naqada II.
Gold was abundant in the Eastern Desert between the Nile and the Red Sea, in the same hills where the Wadi Hammamat trade route ran (we will hear more about Wadi Hammamat in the next chapter).
Predynastic Egyptians extracted alluvial gold from the wadi sands and worked it into beads, foil, and small ornaments.

By the late Naqada II, gold appears in elite burials in quantities that suggest organized expeditions rather than casual gathering — somebody was sending crews into the Eastern Desert specifically to bring back gold.
Egypt's reputation as the gold-producing power of the ancient world starts here.
Lower Egypt, meanwhile, was not keeping up.

The Maadi culture and its successors in the Delta and the area around modern Cairo continued through this period, but they did not develop the same scale, the same hierarchy, or the same craft sophistication as their Upper Egyptian contemporaries.
There are no Lower Egyptian sites of Hierakonpolis's size in Naqada II.
There are no Lower Egyptian temples comparable to the Nekhen temple.
There are no Lower Egyptian Tomb 100s with painted walls.
There are no Lower Egyptian elite cemeteries with lapis lazuli beads.

The picture you get from the archaeology is of a Lower Egypt that was still doing things the old way — small village clusters, modest hierarchies, mostly local trade networks — while Upper Egypt was sprinting ahead.
Why the divergence?
The honest answer is that we don't know for sure.

Three theories run side by side. (1) Geography: the Upper Egyptian floodplain is narrower and more clearly defined than the Delta, which made coordination easier and concentrated population around fewer key sites. (2) The Sahara migration: Upper Egypt may have absorbed more refugees from the drying western desert than the Delta did, and the cattle-herding traditions those refugees brought may have been more compatible with the kind of stratified, ritual-based society that produced kingship. (3) Trade routes: Upper Egypt sat at the junction of the Nile, the Eastern Desert (gold, Wadi Hammamat), and the Red Sea coast (long-distance trade with Punt), while Lower Egypt's trade was mostly with the closer Levant and didn't generate the same wealth.
Probably all three factors mattered.
Whatever the reason, by 3400 BCE Upper Egypt had pulled clearly ahead and was about to start pulling Lower Egypt into its orbit by force.

The next chapter is about how that final pull happened — about writing, about named kings, about the ceremonial palettes and royal cemeteries of the Naqada III period, and about the long set of regional conflicts that historians sometimes call the Wars of Unification.
By the end of those wars, Egypt will have been welded into a single political unit under a single king, and the predynastic period will be over.

Cross-civ check: as Naqada II is winding down around 3300 BCE, Uruk in southern Mesopotamia is now a city of perhaps 40,000 people — the largest human settlement in the world by a wide margin (Mesopotamia Ch 2).
Mesopotamian writing has just been invented (the earliest proto-cuneiform tablets are now being scratched out by Uruk accountants).
Mesopotamian seal-cylinders, niched temple facades, and stylized hero-and-animal motifs are starting to appear in Egyptian elite contexts as imported curiosities.
The two civilizations are now making contact, indirectly, through middlemen in Syria and the Sinai.

Whatever the route, Mesopotamian ideas were now reaching the Nile, and some of them — especially the cylinder seal and the niched-facade form of monumental architecture — would be picked up and adapted by the very first kings of Egypt.

The Nile Valley's first writing system (next chapter) is too different from cuneiform to be a copy, but the idea that you could record information by carving symbols on a hard surface may itself have been a Mesopotamian import, picked up by Egyptian elites who had seen it in action and decided to invent their own version.

chapters.title: Naqada III: Writing, Kings, and the Road to Unity
sentences: By 3300 BCE the predynastic Nile world had two centers of political gravity, and they were on a collision course.

The southern center was the one we already know: Hierakonpolis (Nekhen), the city of the falcon, with its temple and its painted tomb and its industrial brewery, sitting in the fat green stretch of floodplain in southernmost Upper Egypt.

The northern center was new — a town called This (or Thinis, both spellings appear in modern English; the ancient Egyptian name was something like Tjenu, pronounced roughly "CHEN-oo"), located somewhere in middle Upper Egypt, probably near the modern city of Girga, about 100 miles (160 km) downstream from Hierakonpolis.

We don't know exactly where Thinis was — its actual ruins have never been definitively identified, and most scholars suspect it lies under modern farmland that has been continuously cultivated for thousands of years.

What we do know is that Thinis was the political center for a powerful chiefdom that controlled the stretch of the Nile around Abydos (uh-BYE-doss), and Abydos is where the kings of Thinis were buried, in a sandy desert cemetery on the western edge of town.
Abydos is going to matter a great deal.

By the late predynastic period it was already the most important royal cemetery in Egypt — the place where the kings of Thinis dug their tombs into the desert margin, in a corner of the cemetery known to modern archaeologists as Cemetery U.
By the First Dynasty, every king of unified Egypt would be buried at Abydos.
By the Middle Kingdom, it would be a national pilgrimage site associated with the cult of Osiris (the god of the dead and the underworld).
By the New Kingdom, it would be a town with temples, processional roads, and religious schools attached to the royal tombs.
The cult of Abydos as Egypt's holy of holies starts here, with the modest sand-lined burials of forgotten predynastic kings.

This chapter is about those kings — the men and (probably) women whose names have been recovered from the labels and pot marks of Cemetery U at Abydos and who were ruling parts of Upper Egypt in the centuries before any unified state existed.
It is about the invention of writing, about the elaboration of art, about the long contest between rival Upper Egyptian centers that ended with one king on top of all the others.

It is the period the archaeologists call Naqada III, and it runs from roughly 3300 BCE down to the moment of unification around 3100 BCE — about two hundred years, the shortest of the three Naqada phases, but the most important.
By the end of this chapter, Egypt will have writing, named kings buried in named tombs, ceremonial art commissioned by named patrons, and a single ruling dynasty that has destroyed all its rivals.
The next chapter is about the man who finished the unification.
The most important single artifact of the Naqada III period is buried in a tomb labeled Tomb U-j, in the predynastic cemetery at Abydos.
Tomb U-j was excavated by a German team led by Günter Dreyer in the 1980s and 1990s, and the contents of it changed our understanding of how Egyptian writing began.
The tomb itself is a brick-lined chamber complex with twelve rooms, dated to roughly 3250 BCE.
It is the largest predynastic tomb yet found anywhere in Egypt, larger than any of the contemporary elite burials at Hierakonpolis.

The man buried inside it was clearly a king, even though no king lists from later periods record a king of Egypt this early — he was a king of part of Upper Egypt at most, ruling from Thinis over the territory around Abydos, and his name has been reconstructed from later clues as something that sounds in our ears like Scorpion.
Egyptologists call him Scorpion I to distinguish him from another and slightly later king, Scorpion II, whose famous ceremonial macehead we will meet in the next chapter.
Scorpion I is the earliest Egyptian ruler whose name has come down to us.
He probably ruled around 3250 BCE, more than a hundred years before the unification.
What was buried with him?
Two things.
First, around 700 wine jars.

The jars were imported, full, from the southern Levant — probably from vineyards in what is now Israel or Palestine — and shipped to Abydos in such quantities that they must represent either a massive trade relationship or the spoils of a successful military expedition.
The wine itself is long gone, but residue analysis on the inside of the jars confirms it was wine, and pollen and seed evidence shows the grapes were Levantine.
Scorpion I was being buried with one of the largest stockpiles of imported alcohol in the prehistoric world.
He was clearly expecting to drink very well in the afterlife.

Second — and this is the historic part — the jars and the surrounding grave goods were tagged with labels: small flat tags of carved bone or ivory, each pierced with a hole at one corner so it could be tied to a jar with a string, each carved with a small group of symbols.
The symbols are hieroglyphs.
Or rather, they are something we can recognize as the ancestors of hieroglyphs.

They are stylized images — a falcon, a scorpion, a bull, an elephant, a serpent, what may be plant signs and architectural signs and numerical strokes — arranged in tiny clusters that almost certainly encode information about where the goods came from and how much there was.
The labels are not yet a full writing system; they don't compose sentences, they don't conjugate verbs, they don't contain grammatical particles.
But they are doing the basic work of writing, which is to use carved or drawn symbols to record specific information that can be read back later by somebody who knows the convention.

The U-j labels are the earliest writing in Egypt by about two hundred years, and they push the start date of Egyptian literacy back to about 3250 BCE — almost exactly the moment when the first proto-cuneiform tablets were being inscribed in Uruk in Mesopotamia.
The two writing systems are not the same. cuneiform uses a fundamentally different set of signs and a fundamentally different grammar.

Hieroglyphic writing uses pictograms that are recognizably images of the things they name (a falcon sign for the word "falcon," at least to start with), and the system would later evolve to include phonetic signs that stood for sounds rather than meanings — so the same falcon sign could be used to write any word that started with the consonant sound the Egyptian word for "falcon" started with, regardless of meaning.

This kind of phonetic-pictographic hybrid is the thing we now call hieroglyphs (a Greek word meaning "sacred carvings"), and it is a different kind of writing system from cuneiform in important technical ways.

But the timing of the two systems' invention is so close that scholars have argued for decades over whether Egyptian writing was a totally independent invention or whether the idea of writing reached Egypt from Mesopotamia and Egyptian scribes built their own system from the ground up after seeing the Mesopotamian system in action.
The honest answer is we cannot prove either way.
There is no Egyptian sign that can be shown to be a borrowed cuneiform sign.
There is no Mesopotamian sign that can be shown to be a borrowed Egyptian sign.
The two systems are technically independent.

But the timing is suspicious enough — and the trade contacts well enough attested — that the stimulus diffusion theory (the idea that the concept of writing traveled from Mesopotamia to Egypt without any specific signs being copied) remains the most plausible reconstruction.
Egyptian elites of the late Naqada II period saw or heard about the Mesopotamian innovation, decided that they could use something similar, and built their own from scratch.
That is not the same as saying Egyptian hieroglyphs are derived from cuneiform.
It is saying that the moment when Egyptian elites decided writing was something they wanted may itself have been triggered by exposure to a foreign idea.
Either way, the result is what matters.
By 3250 BCE, Scorpion I's accountants are tagging his wine jars in the world's second writing system.
By 3100 BCE, the unifier of Egypt will be carving his name on a ceremonial slate palette in fully formed hieroglyphs.
Egyptian writing, once it starts, develops faster than any other ancient script.
From the U-j labels to the Narmer Palette is only about 150 years.
The whole conceptual leap — from picture-tag to phonetic script to royal monument inscription — happens in the same six or seven generations.
The other thing the Naqada III period invents is monumental ceremonial art.
We've already met the cosmetic palettes of the Badarian period — flat slabs of stone with shallow grinding depressions, used to grind eye-paint pigments, sometimes carved into animal shapes.
By the late Naqada II and Naqada III, the palettes have started to evolve.
They are getting bigger.
The grinding depression is shrinking and being pushed toward one corner of the slab, while the rest of the surface fills up with elaborately carved figural decoration.

By the very end of the predynastic period, some palettes are 24 inches (60 cm) across and have no functional grinding depression at all — they are pure ceremonial objects, designed to be displayed in temples or buried in royal tombs, with their entire surfaces covered in low-relief carving showing battles, processions, hunts, and ritual scenes.
These late predynastic ceremonial palettes are the immediate ancestors of the most famous artifact of the unification, the Narmer Palette (Chapter 5).

Several of them survive — the Hunters Palette, the Battlefield Palette, the Bull Palette, the Towns Palette (sometimes called the Libyan Palette) — each carved with scenes of conquest and hunting, each apparently commissioned by some predynastic ruler as a memorial to a specific victory.

We can't always read the symbols on them clearly enough to identify the ruler involved, but the iconography is consistent: a powerful figure (sometimes shown as a bull, sometimes as a lion, sometimes as a falcon-headed standard) crushing his enemies, taking captives, leading processions, and ruling cities.
The palettes are political propaganda in carved stone.

They are the visual side of the literacy revolution, and they show us the same world the U-j labels show us — a world where Upper Egyptian rulers were already commissioning art that celebrated their personal authority over rivals and conquered peoples.
Among the palettes, one of the most striking objects of the period is not a palette at all but a knife.
The Gebel el-Arak knife is a flint blade, beautifully made, with a carved ivory hilt, found in a tomb at the desert site of Gebel el-Arak in middle Egypt.
It dates to around 3300–3200 BCE.
The flint blade is a technical marvel — pressure-flaked from a single piece of stone with such precision that the cutting edge is still razor-sharp four thousand years later.
But the famous part of the object is the ivory hilt, carved on both sides with miniature scenes that have made art historians argue about it for over a century.
One side of the hilt shows a battle: men in long boats fighting other men, with bodies floating in the water between the boats.
Some of the boats have a flat, raised hull with a cabin at one end — these are recognizably Egyptian Nile boats.

Other boats have a vertical, almost crescent-shaped hull profile that looks nothing like anything else known from Egyptian art — these look much more like Mesopotamian river boats from contemporary Sumerian seal carvings.
The other side of the hilt shows a "master of animals" figure: a bearded man in long robes, standing between two rampant lions, holding them apart by their throats.
This pose is not Egyptian.

It is a classic Mesopotamian motif, used in Sumerian art for kings and gods, and it shows up at Gebel el-Arak in a context that suggests the artist had seen Mesopotamian seal-carvings and was deliberately quoting them.
Nobody quite knows what to make of the Gebel el-Arak knife.

Possible interpretations include: (1) the knife commemorates an actual battle between Upper Egyptian and Mesopotamian-influenced parties on the Nile, (2) the knife is a status object made by an Egyptian craftsman who had been trained by, or had been studying the work of, Mesopotamian seal-cutters, (3) the knife is a foreign import that was adapted and re-decorated in Egypt for an Egyptian patron.

What everybody agrees on is that the knife is direct evidence of significant contact between predynastic Egypt and Mesopotamia in the late Naqada II / early Naqada III period — contact significant enough that Egyptian elites were being buried with foreign-influenced art objects, and that Mesopotamian iconographic conventions were being incorporated into Egyptian visual culture.
The contact mostly seems to have flowed one direction: from Mesopotamia into Egypt.

Egyptian art picked up the cylinder seal (a small carved cylinder rolled across wet clay to leave a continuous stamped impression — the standard Mesopotamian way of marking property and authenticating documents), the niched-palace facade (a particular ornamental treatment of mudbrick walls that mimicked the niched temple platforms of Sumerian cities), and elements of the master-of-animals iconography.
But Egypt did not become a Mesopotamian colony.

The borrowed elements got incorporated into a fundamentally Egyptian visual and political tradition, and within a generation or two they had been digested and re-expressed in a way that no longer looked Mesopotamian at all.

By the time of the unification, only a few isolated motifs (the niched serekh frame for royal names, for instance — the device we will meet at length in Chapter 6) still carry the trace of their foreign origin.
While Upper Egypt was inventing writing, monumentalizing its art, and importing foreign ideas, its political life was getting bloodier.
By the late Naqada III, Upper Egypt was no longer a patchwork of independent chiefdoms.

It had been consolidated, by force, into a small number of competing centers — and in the standard reconstruction (which is admittedly speculative but which fits the evidence as well as anything else), there were probably only two real powers left by about 3200 BCE: the kings ruling from Thinis and burying their dead at Abydos in the north, and the kings ruling from Hierakonpolis in the south.

The two centers controlled stretches of the Upper Egyptian Nile that probably overlapped at the edges, and the historical pattern of all such situations applies: the two powers fought, they raided each other's territory, they took prisoners, and they commemorated their victories in carved palettes and rock art.
Several of the late predynastic ceremonial palettes — particularly the Battlefield Palette and the Cities Palette — show this conflict in iconographic form.
The Battlefield Palette has carved figures of a giant lion and birds of prey tearing at the bodies of fallen enemies on a battlefield strewn with corpses.
The Cities Palette shows a row of fortified towns being attacked by symbolic animal figures (lions, falcons, scorpions) representing different conquering rulers.

The artistic convention is the same one that the Narmer Palette will use a generation later: powerful animal-totems standing in for the victorious king, anonymous broken bodies standing in for the defeated.

The names of the victors are not always preserved, but the names of some of them have been recovered from contemporary inscriptions and pot-marks: figures that scholars have nicknamed "King Iry-Hor," "King Ka," and "Scorpion II." These were probably the last few rulers of the Thinite line before the unification — the predecessors and possibly the immediate ancestors of Narmer himself, the man who would finish the conquest.
What we do not have, even a hint of, is the perspective of the people on the losing end.

There are no surviving texts from the Hierakonpolis side, no monuments commissioned by the kings of Hierakonpolis claiming victory over the Thinite north, no carved palettes celebrating the triumphs of a Hierakonpolis ruler.

The archaeological record of Hierakonpolis itself shows the town continuing to be occupied through the unification and into the early dynastic period — it is not destroyed, it is not abandoned, the temple is still standing, the brewery is still running.
What we do not see is a Hierakonpolis royal cemetery comparable to Cemetery U at Abydos.
The kings of Hierakonpolis, if there were still any after about 3200 BCE, were not being buried with the kind of monumental tombs that their Thinite contemporaries were getting.
The implication is that Hierakonpolis lost its independence sometime in the late Naqada III period — peacefully or not — and was absorbed into the growing Thinite kingdom.
By the eve of the unification, the Thinite kings of Abydos had become the kings of all of Upper Egypt, and the only thing left to conquer was Lower Egypt.
There are also signs that, before the final unification, somebody had already started fighting.

Trade with Nubia picks up dramatically in the late Naqada III, with Egyptian goods moving south past the First Cataract into Lower Nubia and Nubian gold and exotic goods moving north into Egypt (Ancient Nubia Ch 1 — the Nubian end of this exchange was the early A-Group culture, and the relationship was usually peaceful and lucrative for both sides until the early dynastic period when it turned violent).

Egyptian rock inscriptions appear at Nubian sites for the first time in this period, often in the form of standardized royal names carved into cliff faces — claims of authority, or markers of expedition routes.
The expansion is southward as well as inward.
By 3150 BCE, then, the political map of Upper Egypt has been simplified almost to the breaking point.
There is one kingdom, ruled from Thinis.
There is one royal cemetery, at Abydos, with the kings of the Thinite line buried in increasingly elaborate brick-chambered tombs.
There is one writing system, derived from the U-j labels, now sophisticated enough to record royal names and titles in serekh frames.
There is one art tradition, expressed in the ceremonial palettes and in the carved knives and macehead heads of the elite.

There is one religion, centered on the falcon god of Hierakonpolis (now syncretized with the kings of Thinis as their divine patron — the kings of the Thinite line have started being called "Followers of Horus," meaning that the Horus falcon that Hierakonpolis worshipped has now been adopted as the protector and prototype of the Thinite kingship).
And there is one obvious next step.
The next step is Lower Egypt.
The conquest of Lower Egypt is the subject of the next chapter.
The man who finishes it is named Narmer.

His face — or at least his name, his crowns, and his enemies — survives on a carved ceremonial palette that has become the single most famous artifact of predynastic Egypt and the conventional starting point of the dynastic king lists.
With Narmer, the predynastic period ends and recorded history begins.

Cross-civ check before we go: at the moment Scorpion I is being buried at Abydos with his 700 wine jars and his ivory labels, the city of Uruk in southern Mesopotamia has just gone through its own period of explosive urban expansion and is the dominant power in the entire Near East (Mesopotamia Ch 2).
Uruk's "colonial" trading posts have been established as far north as the upper Tigris and as far west as Syria.
Mesopotamian cylinder seals, niched temple architecture, and proto-cuneiform writing are now the standard tools of Bronze Age statecraft from the Persian Gulf to the eastern Mediterranean.
Egypt is the late arrival to this world, the second civilization to develop writing and the first to figure out how to use it for monumental royal propaganda.

The Mesopotamian and Egyptian states are now developing in the same broad geopolitical neighborhood, and they will spend the next three thousand years influencing each other, fighting each other, trading with each other, and occasionally trying to ignore each other.

The relationship that will eventually produce Cleopatra and the Ptolemies starts, dimly, in the Naqada III period, with a Sumerian master-of-animals figure carved on the ivory hilt of an Egyptian flint knife.

chapters.title: Narmer Unifies Egypt
sentences: There is a particular slab of carved green siltstone, about 25 inches (64 cm) tall and shaped like a shield, that sits behind glass in the Egyptian Museum in Cairo.
It has been sitting there since it was found, in 1898, by James Quibell and Frederick Green during their excavations at Hierakonpolis.
If you visit Cairo and ask any museum guard where to find it, they will know what you mean.
It is the Narmer Palette.
The Narmer Palette is the most reproduced, most analyzed, and most argued-about artifact of the entire predynastic period.
It is conventionally treated as the very first monument of the unified Egyptian state — the moment when the first king put both crowns on his head and the dynastic age began.
Whether that description is strictly accurate (it may simplify a longer, messier process) is a question worth dealing with.
But we will start with what the object actually shows, because the object is spectacular, and some of what it shows has been misread so often that it is worth going through it panel by panel.
The palette is carved on both sides.
On the front (the "recto" side), a large figure dominates the upper register.
He is wearing the White Crown of Upper Egypt — a tall, bowling-pin-shaped headdress that is one of the most distinctive objects in Egyptian iconography.
He is holding a mace in his right hand, raised above his head, and with his left hand he is gripping a kneeling enemy by the hair.
He is about to smash the enemy's skull.

This is the smiting pose — the same pose we saw in the Painted Tomb at Hierakonpolis in Chapter 3, now carved in formal, polished relief by a master sculptor and inscribed with the name of the man doing the smiting.
The name is written in a serekh — a rectangular frame shaped like a palace facade, with the king's name written inside the top portion and the Horus falcon perched on top.
Inside the serekh are two hieroglyphs: a catfish (n'r, pronounced approximately "nar") and a chisel (mr, approximately "mer").
Together they spell Narmer.
The falcon on top of the serekh declares that the man named Narmer is the Horus king — the earthly manifestation of the falcon god Horus, the divine ruler of Egypt.
Above the smiting scene, between two bovine heads (probably Hathor, the cow goddess) at the top of the palette, the name "Narmer" appears again in a simple cartouche-like frame.
There is no ambiguity about whose monument this is.

The kneeling enemy is smaller — on Egyptian monuments, size always indicates importance, and the smitten are always smaller than the smiter — and beside his head there are hieroglyphs that may name him or may name his people.
The standard reading identifies the victim as a representative of Lower Egypt, specifically the Delta.

The tableau, taken as a whole, says: "Narmer, king of Upper Egypt, wearing the Upper Egyptian crown, conquers the Delta." On the back (the "verso" side), the same Narmer appears again, this time wearing the Red Crown of Lower Egypt — a flat-topped headdress with a tall back-piece and a forward-curving wire or reed at the front.
He is shown in procession, accompanied by attendants with standards, inspecting rows of decapitated enemies whose heads have been placed between their feet.

Below the main scene, a bull (almost certainly another representation of the king — Egyptian rulers are frequently shown as bulls in predynastic and early dynastic art) tramples a fallen enemy and breaks through the wall of a fortified town.
The message: Narmer has won.
He has conquered the Delta.
He wears both crowns — Upper Egyptian on one side, Lower Egyptian on the other.
He has inspected the dead.
His bull-avatar is destroying the fortifications of the north.
Egypt is unified.
A few complications.
First complication: Narmer probably was not the first king to attempt unification.
He may not even have been the one who finished it.

The ceremonial macehead of a king called Scorpion II — found at the same Hierakonpolis deposit where the Narmer Palette was discovered — shows a large figure wearing the White Crown of Upper Egypt, performing what appears to be an agricultural ritual (opening irrigation channels or plowing a field), attended by standards and bowing subjects.
The figure's name is written as a hieroglyphic scorpion beside a small rosette sign.
The macehead doesn't show the Red Crown of Lower Egypt, which has led most scholars to conclude that Scorpion II was a king who ruled Upper Egypt before Narmer and had not yet conquered the north.
He was either Narmer's immediate predecessor or possibly his father.
The macehead was deposited in the same temple at Hierakonpolis where the Narmer Palette was found, suggesting that both objects were votive offerings made by kings to the falcon god.
Scorpion II is not Scorpion I (Chapter 4).
They are probably a century or more apart.

But they may be related — the scorpion may have been the totem emblem of a particular family or lineage within the Thinite ruling house, reused by different kings in the same way that "Thutmose" or "Amenhotep" would later be reused by different rulers of the same dynasty.
Second complication: there was an even earlier predecessor whose name we barely know.
A serekh naming a ruler called Ka (or possibly Sekhen) appears on pots and jar sealings at Abydos, in contexts that predate Narmer but postdate Scorpion I.
King Ka seems to have been another late predynastic ruler of the Thinite line, bracketed between Iry-Hor (an even more shadowy figure) and Scorpion II.
We know almost nothing about him except that he existed, that he was buried at Abydos, and that he used a serekh with a Horus falcon just like Narmer's.

The names Iry-Hor → Ka → Scorpion II → Narmer form a probable sequence of predynastic Thinite rulers, and between them they cover the final century or so of the predynastic wars — the century during which Upper Egypt conquered Lower Egypt and the unified state was created.
Third complication: the later Egyptian king-list tradition gives a different name for the first king.
The Egyptian priest Manetho (mah-NEH-tho), who compiled a history of the pharaohs for the Greek Ptolemaic rulers around 300 BCE, called the first king of the First Dynasty Menes (MEH-neez).
Menes was credited with founding Memphis, uniting the Two Lands, and performing various founding acts that later tradition assigned to the first king.
For most of the history of Egyptology, the identification of Menes was the central unsolved problem: who was Menes in real life?
Somebody else?
The current consensus, such as it is: Menes is probably Narmer.
The name "Menes" may be a throne name (a formal name assumed at coronation, separate from the birth name) for the same king who was called Narmer in his serekh.

A label from the tomb of Hor-Aha at Abydos appears to show the name "Meni" in a context associated with Narmer's successor, which could mean that the title/name was passed forward from Narmer to Hor-Aha, or that Hor-Aha was retroactively claiming credit for his father's unification.
The identification is not certain.

Nobody has found an artifact that says, unambiguously, "I am both Narmer and Menes, and I did all these things." But the weight of the evidence points to Narmer as the unifier and Menes as his legacy name.
What the Narmer Palette definitely does tell us — regardless of which king did what — is what the unification meant.
It is not just a military victory memorial.
It is a theological statement.
The two crowns are the core of the message.

The White Crown (hedjet) and the Red Crown (deshret) are the visual emblems of Upper and Lower Egypt respectively — and from this point forward, every king of Egypt will be depicted wearing both, either separately on different occasions or combined into the famous Double Crown (pschent), which stacks the White Crown inside the Red Crown in a single headdress.
The White Crown comes from the south, where it appears on predynastic rock art and pot paintings.
The Red Crown comes from the north, where its earliest representations are found in Lower Egyptian contexts.
The act of wearing both crowns is the act of claiming sovereignty over both halves of the country.
It is the foundation of the Egyptian political system: the pharaoh rules the Two Lands, and the Two Lands are united in his person.
This duality is not just decorative.
It is structural.

Everything in Egyptian political theology comes in pairs after the unification: Upper and Lower Egypt, Red Land and Black Land, Horus and Seth (the gods of south and north), Nekhbet and Wadjet (the vulture and cobra, patron goddesses of Upper and Lower Egypt respectively), the Sedge and the Bee (the heraldic emblems of the two halves, which become part of the royal titulary).

The dual structure is so deeply embedded in Egyptian kingship that it persists for three thousand years, long after any meaningful political distinction between Upper and Lower Egypt has ceased to matter.
The Two Lands is the founding myth, and the myth is hard-coded into the Narmer Palette.
The other thing the Narmer Palette established is Memphis.

Memphis (Egyptian Ineb-hedj, "White Walls," later Men-nefer, from which the Greek name Memphis derives) was the capital city of unified Egypt, built at the junction point between Upper and Lower Egypt — at the apex of the Nile Delta, where the river stops being a single narrow channel and fans out into the web of distributary branches that reaches the Mediterranean.

It is the obvious place to put a capital if you have just conquered both halves of the country: close enough to the Delta to control it, close enough to Upper Egypt to administer it, and sitting on the most important transportation hub on the entire river.
Memphis was the political center of Egypt for most of the Old Kingdom and remained the largest city in Egypt (and often in the world) for centuries afterward.
The founding of Memphis is traditionally attributed to Menes/Narmer, and the story that Manetho tells is that Menes dammed a branch of the Nile to create the flat ground on which the city was built.

Whether that is literally true is debatable — it may be a folk memory of an early irrigation or flood-control project — but what is not debatable is that Memphis was a planned foundation, built to be the capital of a unified state, at a site chosen for strategic rather than historical reasons.
Thinis and Hierakonpolis were the old predynastic centers.
Memphis was new.
Putting the capital at Memphis was a deliberate statement that unified Egypt was something different from either of its predecessors.
Memphis is also where Narmer's successor, Hor-Aha, comes into focus.
The name Hor-Aha means "Horus the Fighter," and the earliest evidence for his reign comes from a set of ivory labels and jar sealings found in his tomb at Abydos.

One label shows a boat with a shrine, captioned with the name of the goddess Neith (a warrior goddess from the city of Sais in the western Delta — her cult center was deep in Lower Egyptian territory, and its prominence under the First Dynasty kings is a sign that the new dynasty was making active efforts to integrate the conquered north).
Another label records a military campaign into Nubia — the earliest documented Egyptian attack south of the First Cataract.
A third shows what may be a crocodile hunt in the Faiyum.
The picture is of a king who is consolidating the new unified state by fighting on all its frontiers: north into the Delta, south into Nubia, west into the Faiyum desert.
Hor-Aha was either Narmer's son or his chosen successor.
With his accession — if the traditional numbering is used — the First Dynasty begins.
But the word "dynasty" is misleading if you think of it as a family name or a natural historical break.
The First Dynasty is simply a numbering convention imposed on the king list by Manetho 2,800 years after the fact.
In reality there may have been no clean break between Narmer and Hor-Aha — one king died, the next took over, and the basic institutions of the new unified state continued without interruption.
What did change was scale.

The next eight kings of the First Dynasty would rule over a territory that stretched from the First Cataract at Aswan to the Mediterranean coast, a unified Nile Valley state roughly 600 miles (960 km) long, with a population that may have reached one to two million people — the second or third largest state in the Bronze Age world, after Mesopotamia.
The pharaohs were not inventing a state from scratch.

They were inheriting a set of institutions — serekhs, temples, irrigation systems, trade networks, craft specialists, an agricultural base, a writing system, a religion centered on the falcon god, a beer-and-bread provisioning economy, and a military tradition — that had been assembled over the previous two thousand years of predynastic development.
The unification was the cap on the bottle, not the bottle itself.
But the cap changed everything.
After Narmer, after Memphis, after the Two Crowns, there is an Egypt — singular, self-conscious, administratively unified — in a way that there was not before.
The pharaonic state is now operational.
The next chapter is about what the first dynasty's kings actually did with it — how they built the pharaonic system, where they took it, and what it cost to keep it running.

chapters.title: The First Dynasty: Building the Pharaonic System
sentences: Eight kings in about 200 years.
That is the First Dynasty — roughly 3050 to 2890 BCE, depending on which chronology you use.
We have already met the first one, Hor-Aha, at the end of the last chapter.
Now we are going to go through the rest of them, because the story of the First Dynasty is not just a story of who sat on the throne.
It is a story of institutional construction.

The unified state that Narmer and Hor-Aha created was a fragile thing — a military conquest held together by force and ceremony, with no infrastructure, no bureaucracy, and no guarantee that it would outlast the king who founded it.

The First Dynasty is the period when the machinery of pharaonic Egypt was actually built: the royal titulary, the jubilee festivals, the provincial administration, the international trade routes, the legal and religious framework that would hold the state together for the next three thousand years.
All of it gets assembled in these eight reigns, and it gets assembled by trial and error, with at least two serious crises along the way.
We know the kings by their serekh names.
In order: Hor-Aha, Djer, Djet, Merneith (technically a queen regent, not a king, but she ruled and was buried as one), Den, Anedjib, Semerkhet, Qa'a.
We know most of them from the same evidence: their tombs at Abydos, plus the ivory and wooden labels that were tied to grave goods inside the tombs, plus clay jar sealings stamped with their names.
A few of them also left rock inscriptions at far-flung locations — Sinai, Nubia, the Eastern Desert — that tell us where they sent expeditions.
The amount of information we have about any individual reign is still tiny by later standards, but it is enough to tell a story.
The most disturbing thing about the First Dynasty is the death toll.

When a king died, he was buried in a chamber cut into the desert at Abydos — a mudbrick-lined room, sometimes subdivided into multiple compartments, filled with grave goods and sealed with a sand-covered mound.

Around the royal chamber, in neat rows, were subsidiary graves: smaller pits, each containing a single body, each apparently killed at the time of the king's funeral and buried alongside him to serve him in the afterlife.
This is retainer sacrifice.

It is the organized killing of the king's household — servants, craftsmen, court women, dwarves, dogs, donkeys — at the moment of the king's death, so that his full court would accompany him into the next world.

The practice existed in other early civilizations (the Royal Tombs of Ur in Mesopotamia, Mesopotamia Ch 4, had a similar but smaller tradition), and it is among the most brutal institutional facts of the early state.
The people buried in the subsidiary graves did not volunteer.
They were killed.
Under Hor-Aha, the first king of the dynasty, the tomb at Abydos had 36 subsidiary burials.
Under Djer, the second king, the number exploded to 338.
Three hundred and thirty-eight men, women, and children killed at a single funeral.
The bodies show no obvious wounds, which suggests poison or strangulation rather than cutting or clubbing.

The subsidiary graves are uniform in size and layout, the bodies are laid out in the same contracted position as ordinary burials, and some of them have small grave goods of their own — beads, a copper tool, a jar.
The whole thing was organized, systematic, and matter-of-fact.
Nobody was trying to hide what they were doing.
After Djer, the numbers decline.
Djet had about 174 subsidiary burials.
Den had 136.
Qa'a, the last king of the dynasty, had about 26.
By the Second Dynasty, the practice had stopped entirely.

The decline is gradual enough that it seems to reflect a real shift in ideology rather than a shortage of victims — the state had 200 years to decide that actually killing the royal household was a bad use of resources, and it eventually figured out substitutes.

The earliest servant figurines — small carved or molded figures of humans, placed in the tomb to serve the dead — appear at the very end of the First Dynasty, and they grow more common through the Second and Third Dynasties.

By the Middle Kingdom, the small figurines had become shabtis (shah-BTEEz): blue faience or stone statuettes inscribed with a spell that commanded them to answer for the dead when the gods called on the dead to do manual labor in the afterlife.
The shabti is a magical proxy for a real human being.
It replaced the retainer sacrifice so completely that later Egyptians seem to have forgotten the practice had ever existed.

Djer himself was later confused by Egyptian pilgrims with the god Osiris, and his tomb at Abydos was treated as the actual burial place of Osiris for much of the New Kingdom — one of the most famous pilgrimage sites in ancient Egypt, visited by thousands of pious Egyptians who had no idea that the 338 subsidiary graves they were walking over belonged to real people who had been killed to keep a king company.

The third king, Djet (sometimes written Wadj, or called the "Serpent King" after the cobra hieroglyph in his name), reigned relatively briefly but left behind one of the most beautiful objects of the early dynastic period: a limestone funerary stele found at Abydos, now in the Louvre, showing his serekh carved in crisp low relief — the Horus falcon perched on top, the cobra sign inside the palace-facade frame, the whole composition clean and spare and technically perfect.
It is one of the earliest examples of the formal Egyptian relief-carving style that will dominate temple walls for the next three thousand years, and it is already fully mature.
The conventions — profile view, flat background, hieroglyphic captioning, proportional scaling — are not being experimented with.
They are being executed by someone who was trained in a workshop tradition that already knew exactly what it was doing.
Djet's brief reign is also when the First Dynasty's first real political crisis probably started.
When Djet died, his son Den was still a child.
Too young to rule.
Too young to manage the rituals, the trade routes, the expeditions, the court.
Someone had to run the country.
That someone was Merneith.
Merneith was Djet's wife and Den's mother, and she ruled Egypt as regent during her son's minority.

She is the earliest woman in the documented history of the Nile Valley — and possibly in the documented history of the world — who can be demonstrated to have actually exercised sovereign political authority.
How do we know she ruled?
Three pieces of evidence.
First: Merneith's name appears on contemporary king lists from the Abydos cemetery, in the same format and in the same position as the male kings.

A clay seal impression found in the tomb of Qa'a, the last king of the First Dynasty, lists all the kings of the dynasty in order, and Merneith's name is included in the sequence between Djet and Den.
If the scribes who made the seal had not considered her a ruler, they would not have put her there.
Second: Merneith was buried at Abydos in a tomb indistinguishable in size and plan from those of the male kings.
It has subsidiary burials (about 40 — fewer than Djer's 338 but more than most later kings).
It has the same kinds of grave goods: pottery, stone vessels, copper tools, ivory labels.
Most royal women of the First Dynasty were buried in subsidiary tombs near their husbands.
Merneith got her own royal-scale tomb in the main row.
That does not happen unless the people who buried you regarded you as a king.
Third: a large mudbrick funerary enclosure, similar to the monumental cult buildings that other First Dynasty kings erected near their Abydos tombs, was built for Merneith.

These enclosures (called funerary enclosures or sometimes "valley enclosures") were large rectangular walled courtyards, built on the desert plain near the cemetery, used for rituals associated with the dead king's ongoing cult.
Building one for a queen regent is an extraordinary gesture of royal-scale commemoration.
Merneith's regency establishes a pattern that will recur throughout Egyptian history.

When the male succession broke down — when a king died before his heir was old enough to rule — Egyptian tradition allowed a royal woman (usually the king's mother or principal wife) to govern as regent, with full royal authority, until the heir came of age.
The pattern would produce Hatshepsut, Sobekneferu, Tausret, and (in a different mode) Cleopatra.
It starts here, in the early First Dynasty, with a woman whose name we can read on a clay seal impression and whose authority we can infer from the size of her tomb.
Merneith's son, Den, was probably the most important king of the entire First Dynasty.
He reigned for about 40 years — by far the longest reign of any First Dynasty king — and under him the machinery of the pharaonic state reached its first mature form.
What does "mature form" mean?
It means the kit of institutions that we think of when we think of ancient Egyptian kingship was assembled and standardized during Den's reign.
Specifically: The Two Ladies name.
Every Egyptian king had, by the time of the later dynasties, a set of five formal names, each associated with a different aspect of his authority.
The oldest was the Horus name, written inside the serekh, proclaiming the king as the earthly embodiment of the falcon god.

The second was the nbty name (pronounced something like "NEB-tee"), proclaiming the king under the protection of the Two Ladies — the vulture goddess Nekhbet of Upper Egyptian El-Kab and the cobra goddess Wadjet of Lower Egyptian Buto. (We met Wadjet and Buto in Chapter 2.

The Two Ladies are the divine patrons of the two halves of the country, paired together to symbolize the unity of the state.) Den is the first king who is definitely attested with a nbty name alongside his Horus name.
The nbty name added a second formal declaration — a second way of saying "I rule both halves" — to the royal titulary, and every king after Den would use it.
The Heb Sed jubilee.

The Heb Sed was a royal renewal festival in which the king demonstrated his continuing fitness to rule by performing a series of rituals, the most dramatic of which was a ceremonial run between boundary stones.
The festival was supposed to be celebrated first after 30 years on the throne, and then at shorter intervals thereafter.

An ivory label from Den's tomb is the earliest depiction of the Heb Sed we have — it shows a figure (probably Den himself) running between two sets of markers, wearing the short jubilee kilt, accompanied by attendants and ritual objects.

The Heb Sed would be celebrated by Egyptian kings for the next three thousand years, and it was so central to the ideology of divine kingship that pharaohs who hadn't reached their 30th year often celebrated it early, just to claim the renewal of authority it conferred.
The smiting pose, formalized.

An ivory label from Den's tomb shows him raising a mace over a kneeling Asiatic enemy, with the caption "first time of smiting the East." This is the same smiting pose from the Painted Tomb (Chapter 3), the same one from the Narmer Palette (Chapter 5), now being depicted on a portable artifact attached to a specific product in a specific year.
Den's version is the first to caption the image with a text identifying both the act and the target.

The smiting pose would become the single most reproduced image in all of Egyptian art — every temple pylon, every obelisk base, every victory stele for the next three thousand years would feature some version of it.
Den fixed the formula.
Organized expeditions.
Den sent documented expeditions to the turquoise and copper mines in Sinai and left rock-cut inscriptions at Wadi Maghara — the earliest royal inscriptions in the Sinai peninsula.

He conducted what his labels call the "first time of counting" — probably the first organized census of livestock and grain in the unified kingdom, the precursor of the biennial cattle counts that would be the basis of Egyptian taxation through the Old Kingdom.

He fought campaigns against Asiatic peoples on the northeastern frontier, raided into Nubia, and maintained the trade relationship with Byblos (on the Lebanese coast) that supplied Egypt with the cedar wood it desperately needed for construction, shipbuilding, and coffins.
The nome system.

By Den's reign the Egyptian state was divided into roughly 42 administrative units called nomes — 22 in Upper Egypt and 20 in Lower Egypt — each governed by a local official (later called a nomarch) who answered to the central government at Memphis.
The nomes were probably not invented by the First Dynasty; they were more likely pre-existing tribal or chieftain territories that the unified state absorbed and standardized.
But it was under Den that the nome system became a working administrative framework, with each nome required to deliver grain, labor, and recruits to the crown.

The nome totems (the falcon of Nekhen, the hare of the Fifteenth Upper Egyptian Nome, the crossed arrows of Sais) appear on ivory labels from Den's reign, depicted as standards carried in royal processions — a visual reminder that the king ruled all 42 of them.
Den was, in other words, the first king of Egypt who looks like a pharaoh in the full sense.
He had the titulary, the jubilee, the expedition system, the census, the provincial administration, the art conventions, and the building program.
He did not just inherit a conquest.
He organized it.
The end of the First Dynasty was less stable.
After Den, the throne passed to Anedjib (ah-NED-jib), whose reign appears to have been contested.

His name was systematically erased from several monuments — an early form of damnatio memoriae (Latin for "condemnation of memory," the deliberate destruction of a dead person's name and image, usually by political enemies who wanted to erase them from history).
The erasure suggests that Anedjib's legitimacy was disputed, possibly by a rival faction within the royal family or by officials who refused to recognize his authority.
His tomb at Abydos is comparatively modest, with fewer subsidiary burials and less elaborate grave goods than his predecessors'.
Something went wrong.
His successor, Semerkhet (seh-MER-ket), may have been an usurper.
Semerkhet's name is missing from the Qa'a seal impression that lists the other First Dynasty kings, and some later king lists omit him entirely or add him with hesitation.
He appears to have deliberately defaced Anedjib's monuments after taking power — the same damnatio memoriae that had been applied to Anedjib, now potentially directed by the man who overthrew him.
If this reconstruction is correct, the late First Dynasty saw at least one violent succession: a king deposed and erased, his successor seizing the throne by force and being questioned by posterity.
The dynasty's final king, Qa'a (kah-AH), seems to have stabilized things.

His tomb at Abydos was neatly built, with about 26 subsidiary burials and a rich collection of ivory and wooden labels that record festivals, Sinai expeditions, royal offerings, and a Heb Sed jubilee — the standard package of a king whose reign was running normally.
Qa'a is important mostly for what happened after him: his successor, Hetepsekhemwy, moved the royal cemetery from Abydos to Saqqara and founded the Second Dynasty.
But before we get there, one more piece of the First Dynasty picture needs to be set.

Across the river from Memphis, on the high desert plateau of Saqqara (sah-KAR-uh), the elite officials of the First Dynasty were building their own tombs — and some of those tombs were bigger than the kings' tombs at Abydos.

The First Dynasty mastabas (Arabic for "bench" — a flat-topped, sloping-sided rectangular tomb of mudbrick, the standard form of Egyptian elite burial before the pyramids) at North Saqqara are among the most impressive structures of the early dynastic period.

They have elaborate niched facades (the same niched palace-facade design that decorated the serekh), multiple interior chambers filled with grave goods, sometimes subsidiary burials of their own, and footprints that in several cases exceed the footprint of the contemporary royal tomb at Abydos.
This has confused archaeologists for a century.
Why were the king's officials being buried in tombs larger than the king's?

Three theories compete. (1) The kings actually had two tombs — a ritual cenotaph (empty memorial tomb) at Abydos, the ancestral cult center, and a real working tomb at Saqqara, the capital's cemetery. (2) The Saqqara mastabas belonged to officials so powerful that they could outbuild the king in death, which tells us that the First Dynasty state was less centralized than it later became. (3) Some of the Saqqara mastabas are actually the real royal tombs, and the Abydos structures are the cenotaphs.
The question has never been fully resolved, and it may never be — the evidence is thin enough to support all three readings.

What the Saqqara mastabas do tell us, unambiguously, is that the First Dynasty state had a wealthy official class capable of building massive mudbrick monuments, and that the Memphis-Saqqara area was already established as the administrative and funerary hub of the kingdom alongside the older ritual center at Abydos.
The dual-site pattern — bureaucratic capital at Memphis, ancestral cult center at Abydos — would persist throughout the Old Kingdom.
The Saqqara mastabas are also architecturally important for another reason: they are the direct ancestors of the pyramids.

The Step Pyramid of Djoser, the first pyramid in Egypt, was built at Saqqara about 200 years after the First Dynasty, and it was built by stacking six rectangular mastabas of decreasing size on top of one another to create a stepped monument.
The architect, Imhotep, did not invent the idea from nothing.
He was working in a cemetery full of mastabas, iterating on a form he could see all around him, scaling it up.
The pyramid tradition begins with the mastaba tradition, and the mastaba tradition begins at Saqqara in the First Dynasty, in tombs built by officials of Hor-Aha, Djer, and Den.
One final thread before we leave the First Dynasty: the trade routes.
The First Dynasty established two long-distance trade relationships that would define Egyptian foreign commerce for the rest of the Bronze Age.
The first was with Byblos (BIB-loss), a port city on the coast of modern Lebanon.
Egypt had almost no usable timber of its own — the Nile valley produced palm, acacia, and tamarisk, none of which was suitable for large construction, shipbuilding, or high-quality coffins.
Cedar of Lebanon was the solution: tall, straight-grained, aromatic, and rot-resistant, available in large quantities from the forested slopes above Byblos.
Egyptian ships crossed the eastern Mediterranean to Byblos, loaded up with cedar logs, and sailed back to the Delta ports.
The trade was so important that by the Old Kingdom, Egyptians called any large seagoing ship a "Byblos boat," regardless of its actual destination.
By the Middle Kingdom, two thousand years later, Byblos would become culturally semi-Egyptian — its kings using Egyptian titles and writing their names in hieroglyphs.
In the First Dynasty, the relationship was purely commercial, but it was already indispensable.
The second was with Sinai.

The southwestern tip of the Sinai peninsula, along the western escarpment, contained deposits of turquoise (a blue-green semiprecious stone prized for jewelry and inlays) and copper ore (essential for tools and weapons).
First Dynasty kings sent armed expeditions across the desert from the eastern Delta to Sinai, mined the turquoise and copper, and brought them back.
The expeditions left rock-cut inscriptions at Wadi Maghara (WAH-dee mah-GAR-uh) recording the king's name and the expedition commander's titles — Den, Semerkhet, and Anedjib are all attested here.

These Sinai inscriptions are among the very earliest royal texts outside the Nile Valley, and they show the First Dynasty state projecting power and extracting resources from a desert frontier 150 miles (240 km) from the nearest Egyptian settlement.

The trade picture that emerges from the First Dynasty is a state that was already plugged into the broader Bronze Age world: cedar from Lebanon, copper and turquoise from Sinai, gold from the Eastern Desert, exotic goods from Nubia (ebony, ivory, incense, leopard skins), and wine and oil from the Levant.
Egypt was not yet the empire it would become, but it was already a trade hub with reach, and the economic infrastructure that would finance the pyramids was being assembled.
The next chapter is about what happened when the system the First Dynasty built started to crack.

Cross-civ check: the eight kings of the First Dynasty (roughly 3050-2890 BCE) overlap with the tail end of the Early Dynastic I period in Mesopotamia, when the Sumerian city-states of Ur, Lagash, Nippur, and Kish were consolidating as independent kingdoms and starting to compete for regional dominance (Mesopotamia Ch 3).

The two civilizations were not yet in direct political contact — there is no evidence of Egyptian embassies to Sumer or Sumerian embassies to Egypt in this period — but they were connected through the same trade networks that ran through the Levant and the Syrian interior.
Lapis lazuli from Afghanistan still reached both ends of the Fertile Crescent.
Cedar from Lebanon went east to Mesopotamia and south to Egypt.
Ideas continued to flow.

But the two civilizations were now developing on separate tracks, with different political structures, different writing systems, different religions, and different approaches to the problem of how to run a river-valley state.
Egypt had one king, one river, one state.
Mesopotamia had dozens of kings, a fan of rivers, and a permanent state of interurban competition.
The two models would produce radically different political histories.

chapters.title: The Second Dynasty Crisis
sentences: The Second Dynasty starts with a move and ends with a schism.

The move: when Hetepsekhemwy (HEH-tep-SEH-kem-wee) came to power around 2890 BCE — either by succession from Qa'a or by a more violent transition that established a new ruling family — he did something none of the First Dynasty kings had done.
He abandoned Abydos.

The royal cemetery that had been the burial ground of every Thinite king since Scorpion I, the place where Hor-Aha and Djer and Den had been laid in the desert sand, the anchor of the Upper Egyptian ritual tradition — Hetepsekhemwy left it behind and was buried at Saqqara instead.
The move is as politically significant as anything in the Second Dynasty.
Abydos had been the heart of Upper Egyptian identity since the late Naqada period.
It was the cemetery of the kings who had unified Egypt.
It was becoming the cult center of Osiris, the god of the afterlife.

The fact that Hetepsekhemwy chose to be buried at Saqqara — the Memphite plateau, the capital city's own desert margin — says that the center of gravity of the Egyptian state had shifted permanently northward.
Memphis was no longer just the administrative seat of a kingdom whose soul lived at Abydos.
Memphis was the center, full stop.
The kings would now be buried near where they worked, not near where their ancestors were buried.
Hetepsekhemwy's name is also significant.
It means "The Two Powers Are at Peace" — a reference to Horus and Seth, the falcon god and the storm god, the divine patrons of (roughly) Upper and Lower Egypt respectively.
In the normal course of things, an Egyptian king had a Horus name — his serekh was topped with the Horus falcon, identifying him as the earthly embodiment of Horus.
Seth was not the king's enemy exactly, but he was the other pole of the divine balance: the wild, desert, chaotic counterpart to the orderly, fertile, civilized Horus.

The mythology that later generations would tell says that Horus and Seth fought for the throne of Egypt after the death of Osiris, and that the gods decided in Horus's favor — which is why every king was the Horus king.
But the mythology also insists that the conflict was resolved peacefully, and that the two gods were reconciled, with Seth given the desert and Horus given the fertile land.
Hetepsekhemwy's name declares this reconciliation as a political fact.
"The Two Powers Are at Peace." The question is: why did the name need saying?
If the Two Powers were already at peace, if the Horus-Seth balance was already working, there would be no need to announce it in the new king's name.
Names that declare peace are usually chosen by people who have just ended a war.

The strong implication is that the transition from the First Dynasty to the Second was not smooth — that the late First Dynasty turbulence around Anedjib and Semerkhet (Chapter 6) had not fully resolved, that there was a real contest for the throne, and that Hetepsekhemwy's name was a public announcement that the fighting was over and the country was reunified.
We know very little about the actual events.

Hetepsekhemwy's tomb has never been definitively identified — his clay sealings were found in deep underground galleries beneath the later Step Pyramid complex at Saqqara, and his actual burial chamber was probably destroyed when Djoser's monument was built directly on top of it a few generations later.
The Second Dynasty is the most poorly documented period of Egyptian history.
The kings ruled, they died, they were buried somewhere in the Saqqara plateau, and then the Third Dynasty built pyramids on top of them and erased most of the evidence.

What we know about the Second Dynasty comes from fragmentary king lists, a handful of jar sealings and inscriptions, and the Palermo Stone (a later annal fragment that records some Second Dynasty events in terse year-by-year entries).

The third king of the Second Dynasty, Nynetjer (nee-NET-jer), is the best documented of the early Second Dynasty rulers, and he gives us our clearest picture of what normal life in the Second Dynasty looked like.

Nynetjer reigned for something like 35 to 40 years — the longest reign of any Second Dynasty king, and long enough that the Palermo Stone records the events of his reign in more detail than it does for any other early king.
The entries are formulaic and repetitive, but they tell us something important: the main event that Nynetjer's scribes chose to record, year after year, was the cattle count.

The cattle count (Egyptian tnwt, roughly "counting") was a biennial state inventory — a count of all the cattle, sheep, goats, and other livestock in the kingdom, conducted every other year by officials dispatched from the central government.
The count served two purposes.
First, it was a tax assessment: the state took a percentage of the herd as revenue, and you had to count the herd to know what that percentage was.

Second, it was a dating system: years in the Early Dynastic period were not numbered sequentially but named after the most important event that happened in them, and the cattle count was important enough to name a year after.

An Egyptian scribe in Nynetjer's reign would not say "the fourteenth year of the king" — he would say "the seventh year of the count." The cattle count was the heartbeat of the Second Dynasty's tax system, and the fact that the Palermo Stone records it as the primary event of every other year tells us that it was happening consistently, on schedule, without interruption, for decades at a stretch.
This is what a stable state looks like from the inside: regular cattle counts, biennial tax assessments, festivals celebrated on time, expeditions sent on schedule.
Nynetjer also celebrated a Heb Sed jubilee, held religious festivals at Memphis, and may have conducted military expeditions, though the details are fragmentary.

The picture is of a king running the machinery that the First Dynasty built — keeping the nome system turning, keeping the tax revenues flowing, keeping the gods fed with offerings and the army fed with bread and beer.
And then the machinery broke.
We do not know exactly what went wrong in the late Second Dynasty, because the evidence is a shambles.
What we do know is that after Nynetjer's long reign, the king list gets confused.
There may have been kings who were later struck from the record.
There are names that appear on some lists and not others.
There may have been rival kings ruling simultaneously from different parts of Egypt.

The confusion in the later king-list tradition — Manetho's account, the Abydos king list, the Turin Canon — is itself evidence that something had gone wrong, because Egyptian record-keepers were meticulous about the royal succession and only got confused when the succession had actually been disrupted.

What we can say with confidence is that at some point in the mid-to-late Second Dynasty, a king came to power who did something no Egyptian king had done before and no Egyptian king would ever do again.
His name was Peribsen (PAIR-ib-sen).
And the thing he did was replace the Horus falcon on top of his serekh with the Seth animal.
This needs to be explained carefully, because it sounds like a minor religious detail and it was anything but.
The Seth animal is one of the strangest figures in Egyptian art.

It has a long, downward-curving snout, tall rectangular ears, a stiff forked tail, and a lean body that doesn't quite match any real animal — scholars have proposed greyhound, aardvark, jackal, donkey, and okapi, among others, and none of them is a perfect fit.
The most likely explanation is that the Seth animal was always a composite, a mythological creature deliberately designed to represent something that was not of this ordered world.
Seth himself was the god of the desert, of storms, of chaos, of foreigners, of things that were dangerous and necessary but not civilized.

He was not straightforwardly evil (that reading comes from much later Egyptian theology, and it is an oversimplification) — he was the indispensable other, the force that balanced Horus's order with chaos, the dry desert that surrounded the wet cultivated strip, the foreign lands that bordered Egypt.
Every king from the very beginning had been identified with Horus.
Seth was what the king was not.
And then Peribsen put Seth on his serekh.
The implications are enormous.
By removing the Horus falcon and replacing it with the Seth animal, Peribsen was declaring himself as a king of Seth rather than a king of Horus.
In the theological logic of the Egyptian state, this was not just a personal religious preference.
It was a declaration about which god — which half of the cosmic balance — the king embodied.

Since Horus represented Upper Egypt in much of the mythology and Seth represented Lower Egypt (or, in some versions, the desert lands versus the fertile lands), the change could mean that Peribsen was claiming legitimacy from a different power base than the Horus kings — possibly a Delta or northern faction that was asserting itself against the old Upper Egyptian ruling tradition.
This is one of those moments in ancient history where the evidence is too thin to support a confident narrative but too dramatic to ignore.
Theory 1: Religious reform.

Peribsen was a sincere Seth devotee who wanted to elevate Seth above Horus in the royal cult, perhaps because he believed that the Horus-dominated theology had become corrupt or politically compromised.
This theory treats the change as an internal theological dispute, like a Christian king switching from Catholicism to Protestantism.
Theory 2: Civil war.

Egypt actually split during this period, with a Seth-named king (Peribsen) ruling Upper Egypt from the old Thinite/Abydos region and a Horus-named king (or kings) ruling Lower Egypt from the Memphis/Delta region.
The Seth name was not a religious choice but a political one — Peribsen was declaring allegiance to the Upper Egyptian tradition against a rival northern dynasty.

This theory is supported by the fact that Peribsen was buried at Abydos (the first king buried there since the First Dynasty — why go back to Abydos unless you had been pushed out of Saqqara?) and by the fact that his clay sealings are found almost exclusively in Upper Egypt.
Theory 3: Compromise.
Peribsen adopted the Seth name as part of a negotiated settlement between northern and southern factions.
The Horus kings were ruling from one capital and the Seth faction from another, and Peribsen's name was a deliberate signal that the power-sharing was working.

This theory has the advantage of explaining why Peribsen's name was not erased from the king lists after his death (a Seth-named king who was simply a heretic or an enemy would normally have been damnatio'd by his successors, but Peribsen's name survives intact).
None of these theories can be proven.
What they all agree on is that the Late Second Dynasty experienced a genuine political-religious crisis — the most serious internal rupture Egypt had faced since the unification, 200 years earlier.
The Horus-Seth balance that Hetepsekhemwy's name had declared "at peace" was no longer at peace.
Egypt needed someone to put it back together.
That someone arrived in the next generation.
His name was Khasekhemwy, and his serekh has both Horus and Seth on top of it — the only serekh in all of Egyptian history with both divine patrons perched together on the palace-facade frame.
He is the subject of the next and final chapter.

Cross-civ check: while Egypt was tearing itself apart over Horus and Seth, the Sumerian city-states of southern Mesopotamia were engaged in their own long-running internal conflicts — the wars between Kish and Uruk and Ur that would define Mesopotamian politics for the next several centuries (Mesopotamia Ch 4).

In the Indus Valley, the transition from scattered farming villages to the first planned urban centers was just beginning — Harappa and Mohenjo-daro were not yet cities, but the villages in the Indus floodplain were growing, standardizing their pottery, and starting to experiment with the brick-and-drainage infrastructure that would define the mature Indus civilization a few centuries later (Indus Valley Ch 2).
In the Yellow River valley of China, the Longshan culture was producing wheel-made black pottery and building the first rammed-earth walls around its settlements (Ancient China Ch 2).

All four of the major Bronze Age civilizations were now in the same broad phase of political consolidation — the messy, contentious, sometimes violent process of turning village-level societies into state-level societies.
Egypt just happened to be the one doing it most visibly, with a serekh-swap dramatic enough to show up in the archaeological record four thousand years later.

chapters.title: Khasekhemwy and the Eve of the Pyramid Age
sentences: The man who saved the Egyptian state was named, originally, Khasekhem — "The Powerful One Appears." At some point during his reign, after he had accomplished the thing that needed accomplishing, he changed his name to Khasekhemwy — "The Two Powerful Ones Appear." The difference is a single hieroglyphic sign.
The extra sign changes "powerful one" (singular) to "powerful ones" (dual).
The man who started with one god ended with two.
Khasekhemwy was the last king of the Second Dynasty, ruling from about 2700 to 2686 BCE.
He inherited a kingdom in crisis — the Horus-Seth schism described in the last chapter, with Peribsen's Seth-serekh hanging over the political landscape and the unity of Egypt in genuine question.

By the time he died, the kingdom was restored, the schism was resolved, the dual divine patronage of Horus and Seth was inscribed on his royal monuments as a permanent fact, and his son (probably Djoser) was about to build the first pyramid in the history of the world.
Khasekhemwy is the hinge.
Without him, there is no Third Dynasty, no Step Pyramid, no Old Kingdom, no pyramids at Giza.
The chain of events that produces the most famous monuments in human history runs through a king whose name most people have never heard.
We know more about Khasekhemwy than about any other Second Dynasty king, partly because he left more evidence and partly because his evidence is spectacular.
The most striking piece of evidence is a pair of statues found at Hierakonpolis in the temple deposit — the same deposit that yielded the Narmer Palette and the Scorpion Macehead.
The statues show Khasekhemwy seated on a throne, wearing the White Crown of Upper Egypt, his face rendered with an unusual degree of individuality for this early in Egyptian art.
His eyes are deeply set, his cheeks are broad, his expression is calm and fixed.
He is not an idealized generic king.
He is a specific man, carved by an artist who had looked at him.
The base of each statue carries an inscription: carved around the throne pedestal, in low relief, are figures of fallen enemies — sprawled bodies, piled corpses, broken limbs.
Beside the figures are numbers.
The most commonly read number is 47,209.
Forty-seven thousand two hundred and nine dead northerners.

The number is probably exaggerated — ancient Near Eastern kings exaggerated their kill counts the way fishermen exaggerate their catches, and a body count of 47,209 in an era when the total population of Egypt was likely under two million implies a campaign of almost inconceivable brutality.

But the political message is precise: Khasekhemwy fought a war against the north, and he won it decisively, and he wanted everyone who entered the temple at Hierakonpolis to know exactly how decisively he had won it.
The enemy figures on the base of the statues are identified by their postures and attributes as people of the Delta — Lower Egyptians.
The war of reunification was, like Narmer's unification 400 years earlier, a war of Upper Egypt against Lower Egypt.
The country had to be re-unified by force.

Whether Khasekhemwy was fighting against a rival Horus king in the north, or against Peribsen's surviving faction, or against some combination of breakaway Delta chieftains who had taken advantage of the schism to go independent — we cannot say.

The archaeological evidence tells us only that the conflict was large enough to warrant a body count in the tens of thousands and important enough that the king commemorated it with the most expensive art form available to him (stone statuary, which in this period was rare, technically demanding, and exclusively royal).
The resolution came in the name change.
After the victory — whether immediately after or after a period of consolidation — Khasekhem added the dual sign to his name and became Khasekhemwy.
At the same time, he did something to his serekh that had never been done before and would never be done again.
He put both gods on top.
The serekh of every Egyptian king before Peribsen had a Horus falcon on top.
Peribsen replaced the falcon with the Seth animal.
Khasekhemwy put both the falcon and the Seth animal on the serekh, facing each other, symmetrically balanced above the palace-facade frame.

Below the two gods, an additional inscription was added: "The Two Lords are at peace in him." The whole device — the doubled name, the doubled gods, the explicit declaration of peace — is a piece of political theology so compact and so deliberate that it reads like a constitutional amendment in pictographic form.
What Khasekhemwy was announcing was not just that he had won the war.

He was announcing that the fundamental structure of the Egyptian state — the dual Horus-Seth balance, the Upper-Lower partnership, the reconciliation of order and chaos within the person of the king — had been restored.
Peribsen had broken the balance by choosing Seth alone.
Khasekhemwy restored it by declaring both gods as co-patrons.

The message was aimed not at the defeated northerners (who had already been killed in sufficient numbers to fill a statue base) but at the Egyptian elite, the priests, the nomarchs, the administrators who needed to be convinced that the crisis was really over and that the state was functioning again.
"The Two Lords are at peace in him" means: you can go back to work.
After Khasekhemwy, the Horus-Seth crisis vanishes from the Egyptian record.
No later king uses a Seth serekh.
No later king needs to put both gods on his frame.
The balance was settled, and it held.

Seth would continue to be an important god in the Egyptian pantheon — he was the god who stood on the bow of the solar bark and fought the chaos serpent Apophis every night to protect the sun's journey through the underworld — but he was never again the primary patron of a sitting king.
The Horus kingship that Narmer had inaugurated was restored, permanently.
