His first act was to abandon his father's capital at Dur-Sharrukin and move the imperial seat to Nineveh — an ancient city on the east bank of the Tigris, directly across from modern Mosul, that had been a significant Assyrian settlement for millennia but had never been the primary capital.
Sennacherib transformed it into something the world had not previously imagined.
He enclosed 750 hectares within new walls — more than twice the size of Ashurnasirpal's Nimrud.
The walls stretched 7.5 miles (12 km) around the city, stood about 65 feet (20 meters) high, and were pierced by 15 monumental gates, each named after a god.

The population may have reached 100,000 to 150,000 people (comparable to a modern city like Boulder, Colorado — these are scholarly estimates from archaeological survey, not census data), making Nineveh easily the largest city in the ancient world at the time.
The centerpiece was Sennacherib's palace, which he named, with characteristic modesty, the "Palace Without Rival." And the name was not entirely unfounded.

The palace covered over 500 by 250 meters, contained at least 71 rooms, and its walls were lined with over 3,000 carved alabaster relief panels — approximately 3 kilometers (nearly 2 miles) of continuous carved narrative when laid end to end.

The reliefs depicted Sennacherib's military campaigns with extraordinary precision: siege engines being dragged up earthen ramps, archers shooting defenders off battlements, prisoners being led away in chains, cities burning.
They also showed massive construction projects, royal gardens, and religious ceremonies.
Colossal lamassu flanked the entrance.
The palace was, by any measure, the most expensive and elaborate building project undertaken anywhere in the world up to that point.
Sennacherib also built an engineering marvel to supply his new capital with water.

Nineveh's expansion far outstripped what local water sources could provide, so he commissioned an ambitious canal-and-aqueduct system that stretched roughly 50 miles (80 km) from mountain springs to the north.

The most spectacular component was the Aqueduct of Jerwan — a 300-meter-long stone bridge carrying the canal across a valley on a series of corbelled arches, built from over 2 million dressed limestone blocks and waterproofed with bitumen.

The engineering was comparable to the Roman aqueducts that would be built centuries later, and some scholars argue the Jerwan aqueduct was the first true aqueduct bridge of its type anywhere in the world.
The water system fed parks, orchards, and elaborate gardens within and around Nineveh.

The archaeologist Stephanie Dalley of Oxford University has made a provocative argument that Sennacherib's gardens at Nineveh — not Nebuchadnezzar's Babylon — were the actual Hanging Gardens of ancient fame, one of the classical Seven Wonders of the World.

The evidence is circumstantial but intriguing: no contemporary Babylonian text mentions hanging gardens at Babylon, while Sennacherib's inscriptions describe elaborate elevated gardens at Nineveh irrigated by his aqueduct system.
The question remains debated, but the possibility that one of the Seven Wonders of the World was at Nineveh rather than Babylon is itself a measure of the city's grandeur.
The palace reliefs also depict an unusual Assyrian innovation: military swimmers crossing rivers using inflated animal skins as flotation devices, breathing through reed tubes while fully armed.
These are the earliest known depictions of combat diving — nearly 2,800 years before modern military frogmen.
The detail is a reminder that behind the terror and the propaganda, the Assyrian military was a sophisticated engineering institution that constantly innovated.
Then came the campaigns that made Sennacherib famous — and infamous.

In 701 BCE, Sennacherib invaded the Kingdom of Judah (the small surviving Judean state centered on Jerusalem that had avoided destruction by paying tribute; Chapter 5) to punish King Hezekiah (hez-eh-KYE-uh) for rebelling against Assyrian authority and seeking Egyptian support.

The invasion is the single most documented military campaign of the entire ancient Near East, because three different contemporary or near-contemporary sources describe it: Sennacherib's own annals (the Assyrian perspective), the biblical books of 2 Kings and Isaiah (the Judean perspective), and the Greek historian Herodotus (heh-ROD-oh-tus; a Greek from the city of Halicarnassus, writing about two centuries later from a combination of Egyptian and Greek sources).
The three accounts disagree on important details, which is precisely what makes the campaign so valuable as a historical case study.
Sennacherib captured 46 Judean towns.
The most significant was Lachish (LAY-kish), the second most important city in Judah after Jerusalem.
The siege of Lachish was commemorated in a series of palace reliefs now in the British Museum that are among the most vivid depictions of ancient warfare ever discovered.

They show Assyrian siege engines — wheeled wooden towers covered in dampened leather to resist fire arrows, housing massive iron-tipped battering rams swinging on chains inside the structure — being pushed up earthen ramps against the city walls by crews of soldiers sheltered beneath the tower's frame.
Archers on top of the towers shoot defenders off the battlements.
Prisoners are led away.
Others are impaled.
The archaeological site at Lachish itself confirms the reliefs: excavators found the remains of the Assyrian siege ramp, still visible today, along with evidence of the city's destruction.
After Lachish, Sennacherib turned to Jerusalem.
His annals boast that he trapped Hezekiah "like a bird in a cage" and extracted a massive tribute — 30 talents of gold, 800 talents of silver, and Hezekiah's daughters as concubines.
But what Sennacherib's annals conspicuously do not claim is that he actually captured Jerusalem.
The city was not destroyed.
Hezekiah remained on his throne.
The biblical account tells a very different story.
According to 2 Kings 19, the siege ends miraculously: "The angel of the LORD went out and put to death 185,000 in the Assyrian camp.
When the people got up the next morning — there were all the dead bodies." Sennacherib withdrew.
Herodotus, working from Egyptian sources, tells a third version: a plague of mice ate through the Assyrian army's bowstrings and shield straps, rendering them unable to fight.
What actually happened?

Hezekiah had prepared for the siege by constructing the Siloam Tunnel — a remarkable 533-meter (1,750-foot) underground water channel cut through solid rock beneath Jerusalem to secure the city's water supply.

The tunnel was carved simultaneously from both ends, meeting in the middle (an inscription found inside records the dramatic moment when the two teams of diggers heard each other's picks through the rock).

Modern historians generally believe the Assyrian army was struck by an epidemic disease — possibly bubonic plague, possibly cholera or dysentery, carried in army camps during the summer heat — which forced Sennacherib to abandon the siege.
The biblical "angel of the LORD" may be a theological interpretation of a very real plague.
Whatever the cause, Jerusalem survived.
The Siege of Jerusalem became one of the founding events of Jewish religious memory: the moment when divine intervention saved the holy city from certain destruction.
It has been remembered for twenty-five centuries.
Sennacherib's most notorious act came a few years later, and it would haunt the Assyrian Empire to its end.
The Babylonian problem had been festering for decades.
Babylon rebelled against Assyrian rule repeatedly — it was the perpetual ulcer of the empire, the one city that could never be pacified for long.
Sennacherib tried the dual-kingship strategy his predecessors had used: he installed his own son, Ashur-nadin-shumi (ah-SHOOR NAH-din SHOO-mee), as puppet king of Babylon.
In 694 BCE, the Elamites (Elamite Civilization Ch 6-7) invaded Babylonia, captured Ashur-nadin-shumi, and carried him off to Elam, where he apparently died.
The loss of his son — to the same Elamites and Babylonians who kept rebelling — enraged Sennacherib beyond calculation.

In 691 BCE, he fought a massive battle against a coalition of Babylonians, Elamites, and Chaldeans (kal-DEE-unz, a Semitic-speaking tribal people who had migrated into southern Babylonia from the Arabian steppe, gaining power through a combination of pastoral wealth and urban political maneuvering) at Halule (hah-LOO-leh) on the Tigris.
Both sides claimed victory in their inscriptions; modern historians generally read it as a bloody draw.
Sennacherib's response to being fought to a standstill was incandescent.
In 689 BCE, he did something unprecedented in Mesopotamian history: he destroyed Babylon itself.
Not just sacked it — destroyed it.
He leveled the city walls.
He demolished the temples, including the great temple of Marduk (the Esagila) and the ziggurat (the Etemenanki, the probable inspiration for the biblical Tower of Babel; Mesopotamia Ch 12).
He diverted the Euphrates to flood the ruins.

He carried off the statue of Marduk (the supreme god of the Babylonian pantheon, whose statue was believed to be the god's literal presence on earth — not a representation, but the actual divine being resident in material form).
He boasted in his inscriptions of turning the holiest city in Mesopotamia into a swamp: "I destroyed it, I devastated it, I burned it with fire.
The city and its houses, from foundation to coping, I devastated, I destroyed, I burned.
The wall and outer wall, temples and gods, temple towers of brick and earth, as many as there were, I razed and dumped them into the canal." This was not just a military atrocity.
It was a theological catastrophe.
Babylon was sacred to the entire Mesopotamian world, including to the Assyrians' own religious traditions.
The Marduk priesthood was revered across the Near East.
The city's rituals — especially the Akitu festival — were believed to be necessary for the proper functioning of the cosmic order.
With Babylon in ruins and the Marduk statue in captivity, the New Year could not be properly celebrated; the cosmic order itself was, in Mesopotamian theological terms, broken.
Destroying Babylon was like burning the Vatican: an act so transgressive that it shocked even Sennacherib's allies and alienated his own priesthood.
Centuries of Babylonian resentment against Assyria crystallized into permanent, implacable hatred.
The memory of 689 BCE would fuel the Babylonian revolt that ultimately destroyed the Assyrian Empire (Chapter 8).
In 681 BCE, Sennacherib was murdered in his own palace by two of his sons, who then fled to the kingdom of Urartu.

The biblical Book of 2 Kings records the event: "One day, while he was worshipping in the temple of his god Nisroch, his sons Adrammelech and Sharezer killed him with the sword." Whether the assassination was motivated by the destruction of Babylon, by succession politics (the two sons may have been passed over in favor of Esarhaddon as heir), or by some combination is debated.
But the theological interpretation was clear to contemporaries: the gods had punished Sennacherib for his sacrilege.
The pattern of Assyrian kings murdered by their own families — Tukulti-Ninurta I (Chapter 3), Shalmaneser III's rebellion (Chapter 4), and now Sennacherib — was not coincidental.
The Assyrian political system, with its absolute royal power and ambiguous succession rules, was a machine that manufactured palace conspiracies.
The kingdom passed to a third son who had not been involved in the murder, a man named Esarhaddon, who immediately understood that his first task was to undo his father's greatest crime.

chapters.title: The Last Great King
sentences: Esarhaddon (eh-sar-HAD-on; ruled 681-669 BCE) came to power in the worst possible way — winning a civil war against his brothers who had just murdered his father — and spent his reign trying to repair the damage Sennacherib had done, both to Babylon and to Assyria's reputation.

His first major act was the rebuilding of Babylon: restoring its temples, returning the statue of Marduk, re-establishing the priesthood, and presenting himself as the pious king who had reversed his father's sacrilege.

This was both theological necessity (the entire Mesopotamian religious establishment demanded atonement for the destruction of the holiest city) and political calculation (Assyria could not govern its empire with the entire Babylonian world in outraged opposition).
But Esarhaddon was not merely a rebuilder.
Despite chronic illness (ancient sources describe symptoms that some modern scholars have tentatively identified as lupus), he proved one of the most capable and ambitious rulers in Assyrian history.

He devised the most elaborate succession treaty in Assyrian history, forcing every vassal king and high official in the empire to swear loyalty oaths to his chosen heir, Ashurbanipal (ah-shur-BAH-nih-pal).
The treaty text survives: it lists dozens of curses that would befall oath-breakers, invoking every god in the Mesopotamian pantheon, and was distributed throughout the empire and placed in temples.

Scholars have noted striking parallels between its curse formulas and passages in the biblical Book of Deuteronomy, suggesting direct literary influence from Assyrian treaty language on biblical writers.
The treaty also reveals the fatal flaw in Esarhaddon's succession plan: Ashurbanipal would rule Assyria, while his older brother Shamash-shum-ukin (shah-MASH shoom oo-KIN) would rule Babylon.
The arrangement sounded equitable but was inherently unstable: Babylon's king was subordinate to Assyria's, a status that Shamash-shum-ukin would come to resent.
Esarhaddon's boldest achievement was the conquest of Egypt.
In 674 BCE, Esarhaddon's first attempt to invade Egypt ended in a humiliating defeat at the Egyptian border — one of the few clear military failures in Assyrian records (Kingdom of Kush Ch 4).
Esarhaddon regrouped and tried again.
In 671 BCE, he marched his army across the Sinai Desert — 15 days of grueling travel across waterless wasteland — and attacked Egypt.
The Egyptian pharaoh was Taharqa (tah-HAR-kah), a king of the 25th Dynasty — which was not a native Egyptian dynasty at all but a Nubian one.
Taharqa was from the Kingdom of Kush (Kingdom of Kush Ch 4), the powerful Nubian state based at Napata in what is now Sudan, which had conquered Egypt and ruled it as pharaohs for nearly a century.
Taharqa had been supporting anti-Assyrian rebellions in the Levant, and Esarhaddon decided to eliminate the problem at its source.
The Assyrian army stormed Memphis, Egypt's ancient capital, and Taharqa fled south to Upper Egypt.

Esarhaddon installed Assyrian governors and local vassals and took the title "King of Egypt." The empire now stretched from the Nile to the Zagros Mountains — the largest state the world had yet seen, larger than the Persian Empire that would succeed it by a generation.
The achievement was extraordinary.
Egypt was by then over two thousand years old as a civilization, with its own deeply rooted religious and political traditions.

The fact that an army from northern Iraq could cross a desert, invade a country 1,000 miles (1,600 km) away, and conquer it demonstrated the sheer reach of the Assyrian military machine under Tiglath-Pileser III's reforms.
Esarhaddon died in 669 BCE while marching back toward Egypt to suppress a rebellion there.
Taharqa had returned and reconquered parts of the country.
After Taharqa died in 664, his successor Tantamani (tan-tah-MAH-nee) initially recaptured Memphis before Ashurbanipal's forces drove him back to Nubia permanently.
The conquest of Egypt, it turned out, was easier than the holding of Egypt — a lesson that every future empire would learn, from Alexander to Napoleon.

Esarhaddon's court also produced one of the ancient Near East's most widely circulated literary works: the Story of Ahiqar (ah-HEE-kar), the tale of a wise Assyrian official who served under both Sennacherib and Esarhaddon.
Ahiqar was said to have raised his nephew as his successor, only to be betrayed by that nephew and condemned to death.
He survived through the cleverness of a loyal friend and was eventually vindicated.

The story circulated with a collection of proverbs and fables attributed to Ahiqar — wisdom literature in the Mesopotamian tradition — and traveled with remarkable reach: a copy was found among the Elephantine papyri in southern Egypt (a Jewish military colony on the Nile), the story influenced the biblical Book of Tobit, and some scholars detect parallels with the Aesop tradition in Greek literature.
Ahiqar is, after the Epic of Gilgamesh, possibly the most widely distributed piece of ancient Near Eastern literature, evidence that Assyrian culture produced more than military terror.
The succession plan worked — at first.
Ashurbanipal (ruled approximately 669-631 BCE) took the Assyrian throne, and Shamash-shum-ukin took the Babylonian throne, and for a while, the arrangement held.
Ashurbanipal is usually considered the last great Assyrian king — the ruler under whom the empire reached its intellectual and territorial peak.

He is also one of the most complex figures in ancient history: a genuine scholar who could read Sumerian (a language that had been dead as a spoken tongue for over a thousand years by his time) and solve advanced mathematical problems, but also a man who boasted of flaying enemies alive and gouging out their eyes.
Ashurbanipal actually bragged about his literacy in his royal inscriptions — a remarkable departure from the traditional Assyrian warrior-king propaganda.

The basic message of every previous Assyrian king's inscriptions was "I am strong, I kill my enemies, I conquer cities." Ashurbanipal added a new note: "I have learned the hidden wisdom of Nabu, the god of scribes.

I can solve complex mathematical problems and read the obscure Sumerian tablets that come from before the flood." For an Assyrian warrior-king to brag about being good at math was like a modern military general boasting about his poetry.
It was strange.
It was also the foundation of Ashurbanipal's greatest contribution to the history of civilization.
Ashurbanipal decided to create what we now call the Library of Ashurbanipal — widely considered the ancient world's first systematically collected library.
Earlier temple archives existed (Mesopotamia Ch 3-4), but Ashurbanipal's was the first deliberate attempt to assemble a comprehensive collection of all known texts in one place.

He sent scholars and scribes across the empire with orders to find, copy, and catalog every cuneiform tablet they could locate: from temple archives in Babylon, from private libraries in Ur, from scribal schools in Nippur, from every literate community his agents could reach.
The result was a collection of over 30,000 tablets, organized by subject, stored in designated rooms in his palace at Nineveh.

The library covered divination and omen texts (a vast category, since Mesopotamian religion relied on predicting the future from signs), medical manuals (systematic diagnostic handbooks that listed symptoms and treatments in if-then format: "If the patient's eyes are yellow and he vomits bile, he will not recover"), astronomical tables, lexical lists (essentially bilingual Sumerian-Akkadian dictionaries and encyclopedias), historical chronicles, mythological narratives, and literary epics.

The Standard Babylonian version of the Epic of Gilgamesh — the oldest great work of world literature, the story of a king who seeks immortality and learns instead to accept human mortality — was preserved and standardized in Ashurbanipal's library.
So were copies of the Enuma Elish creation epic, the Atra-Hasis flood narrative, the Descent of Inanna to the underworld, and virtually every other surviving Mesopotamian literary masterpiece.

The library also housed astronomical records that Assyrian court astronomers had maintained for generations: systematic observations of lunar eclipses, planetary movements, and celestial events used both for predicting omens (an eclipse could delay a military campaign) and for building the predictive models that would eventually enable the discovery of the Saros cycle — the 18-year-and-11-day repeating pattern of lunar eclipses.

Babylonian and Assyrian astronomical records, many preserved in Ashurbanipal's library, would later be used by Greek astronomers like Hipparchus and Ptolemy to make some of the greatest discoveries of ancient science.
The cult of Nabu (NAH-boo), the god of writing and wisdom, reached its peak under Ashurbanipal.

Nabu's temple at Nimrud (the Ezida) was one of the most important religious structures in the empire, and Ashurbanipal's library project can be understood as a devotional offering to the god of scribes — a warrior-king's gift to the god who presided over everything that made the library possible.
Ashurbanipal's first major military challenge was Egypt.
Taharqa died in 664 BCE, and his successor Tantamani (tahn-tah-MAH-nee; Kingdom of Kush Ch 4) attempted to reconquer Egypt from Assyrian control.
Ashurbanipal launched a massive campaign that drove deep into Upper Egypt, far beyond anything his father had attempted.
His army reached Thebes — the ancient religious capital of Egypt, roughly 600 miles (1,000 km) up the Nile from Memphis — and sacked it in 663 BCE.
The plunder was enormous: gold, silver, obelisks, and treasures accumulated over millennia of Egyptian civilization.
The sack of Thebes was the farthest south any Mesopotamian army had ever campaigned, and it demonstrated the extraordinary reach of Assyrian military power.
The biblical prophet Nahum later used Thebes' fate as a warning to Nineveh: "Are you better than Thebes, situated on the Nile, with water around her?
Kush was her strength, and Egypt too.
Yet she was taken captive." The irony of Nahum's words was that he was using the fall of one great city to prophesy the fall of another.
Egypt could not be held.
The distance was too great, the logistics too difficult, and the Assyrians had no naval capacity to control the Nile.
After Ashurbanipal's campaign, Assyria effectively withdrew from Egypt, allowing a new native Egyptian dynasty (the 26th, the Saite dynasty) to take power.
The Saite pharaohs would, within a generation, become allies of Assyria against a common enemy — an astonishing reversal that we will see in the next chapter.
The civil war with Shamash-shum-ukin broke out in 652 BCE.

Ashurbanipal's brother, increasingly resentful of his subordinate position, organized a massive coalition that included Babylonian cities, Elamite forces, Arab tribes, and other anti-Assyrian elements.
The resulting civil war lasted four years (652-648 BCE) and devastated both kingdoms.
Ashurbanipal besieged Babylon itself — his own brother's capital — for two years.
When the city finally fell, Shamash-shum-ukin died in the flames of his burning palace, either by suicide or in the final assault.

The civil war badly weakened the empire's resources and demonstrated the fundamental problem that had plagued every Assyrian king: Babylon was too proud to accept permanent subjugation, too important to abandon, and too dangerous to destroy again after what Sennacherib had done.

After crushing the Babylonian revolt, Ashurbanipal turned on the coalition partner that had most angered him: Elam (eh-LAHM), the ancient civilization centered in southwestern Iran with its capital at Susa (SOO-sah; Elamite Civilization Ch 1-8).
The Elamites had supported Shamash-shum-ukin's rebellion, and they had been a thorn in Mesopotamia's side for over two thousand years.
In 646 BCE, Ashurbanipal launched a devastating campaign that ended Elam as a political entity.

His armies sacked Susa, destroyed Elamite temples, desecrated royal tombs, scattered the bones of Elamite kings, and carried off the statue of the goddess Nanaya that the Elamites had supposedly stolen 1,635 years earlier.

Ashurbanipal's inscription about this campaign is one of the most viciously triumphalist pieces of writing in ancient history: he boasts of making the land "empty," of sowing fields with salt (though scholars debate whether this was literal or symbolic), of ensuring that "the voice of man, the tread of cattle and sheep, the happy shouts of merry-making — I made them cease." The destruction of Elam was the most thorough annihilation of a civilization that the ancient world had yet seen.
It was also deeply ironic.

The power vacuum that Ashurbanipal created in southwestern Iran would be filled within a century by a new people — the Persians — who would inherit Elamite administrative traditions, adopt Elamite cuneiform as their bureaucratic script, and eventually build an empire that destroyed Assyria's successors (Persian Empire Ch 1).
Ashurbanipal had, without knowing it, prepared the ground for the power that would render his entire civilizational tradition obsolete.
One final image from Ashurbanipal's reign captures the essential paradox of Assyrian civilization better than any other artifact.

The Garden Party Relief from his North Palace at Nineveh (now in the British Museum) shows Ashurbanipal reclining on a couch in an idyllic garden, attended by servants with fans and musicians playing lyres.
His queen, Libbali-sharrat (lih-BAH-lee SHAH-raht), sits in a chair beside him.
Vines hang overhead.
Birds flit between trees.

It is a scene of perfect domestic tranquility — except that hanging from the third tree from the left, suspended by a ring through its lip, is the severed head of Teumman (TOO-man), the Elamite king killed at the Battle of Til-Tuba in 653 BCE.
Exquisite artistic refinement and casual brutality, occupying the same frame.

The lion hunt reliefs from the same palace — showing Ashurbanipal hunting lions from a chariot and on foot — achieve a level of anatomical realism and emotional power that was unprecedented in ancient art.
A wounded lioness dragging her paralyzed hind legs is perhaps the most famous panel.
Art historians note that this level of naturalistic animal portrayal would not be achieved again in Western art for over a thousand years.
The same civilization that pioneered systematic terror also produced some of the greatest art of the ancient world.
Ashurbanipal died around 631 BCE (the exact date is uncertain, and his final years are poorly documented).

The empire he left behind was the mightiest state the world had ever seen, stretching from Egypt to Iran, governed by the most sophisticated bureaucracy in existence, defended by the most advanced army in existence.
And within fifteen years, it would be gone.

chapters.title: The Fall
sentences: The speed of the Assyrian collapse is one of the most genuinely shocking events in ancient history.
When Ashurbanipal died around 631 BCE, the empire controlled most of the known world.
By 609 BCE — barely two decades later — Assyria had been erased so completely that its cities were in ruins, its population scattered, and the location of Nineveh itself was being forgotten.
No great power in ancient history fell faster or more completely.
The Roman Empire took centuries to decline.
The Han dynasty in China fragmented gradually over decades.
The Assyrian Empire went from apex to annihilation in a single generation.
The trigger was the same problem that had plagued Assyria for centuries: succession.
Ashurbanipal's final years are obscure — the detailed records that characterize the rest of Neo-Assyrian history dry up around 635 BCE, which is itself a sign of institutional decay.
When Ashurbanipal died, his son Ashur-etil-ilani (ah-SHOOR eh-TILL ih-LAH-nee) took the throne, but his authority was immediately contested.
A general named Sin-shumu-lishir (SEEN SHOO-moo LEE-sheer) staged a revolt, and in the resulting chaos, another son of Ashurbanipal, Sin-shar-ishkun (SEEN shar ISH-koon), seized the throne.

The details are murky — the sources for this period are fragmentary and sometimes contradictory — but the essential fact is clear: the empire descended into civil war at exactly the moment when it could least afford it.
The civil war mattered because it distracted the Assyrian military machine from the real threat gathering on the empire's periphery.

In the south, a Chaldean (Semitic-speaking people from the southern marshlands of Babylonia) leader named Nabopolassar (nab-oh-puh-LASS-ar) had seized the city of Babylon in 626 BCE and declared independence.
Nabopolassar was a charismatic military leader backed by the Babylonian priesthood, who were desperate for anyone who would restore proper worship of Marduk after decades of Assyrian misrule.

In the east, the Medes — an Iranian-speaking people from the Zagros Mountains who had recently unified into a formidable kingdom under a king named Cyaxares (sy-AX-uh-reez) — were pressing against Assyrian borders.

And from the north, Scythian (SITH-ee-un) horse-mounted nomadic raiders from the Eurasian steppe were raiding into the empire's northern and eastern periphery, their mobility and archery skills difficult to counter with conventional Assyrian tactics.
Individually, any one of these threats might have been manageable.
The Assyrian military machine was still formidable, and a united empire under a strong king could have crushed Nabopolassar's rebellion, deterred the Medes, and contained the Scythians.
But the empire was not united and it did not have a strong king.
Sin-shar-ishkun was fighting for control of his own throne while his enemies gathered strength.
The decisive moment came when Nabopolassar and Cyaxares formed an alliance.

The Babylonians from the south and the Medes from the east agreed to coordinate their campaigns against Assyria, and according to some sources, the alliance was sealed by a royal marriage between a Median princess and Nabopolassar's son (the future Nebuchadnezzar II, who would go on to build the most famous Babylon in history; Mesopotamia Ch 12).
Whether or not the marriage actually happened at this point, the alliance was real and it was fatal.

In 614 BCE, Cyaxares and his Median army marched into the Assyrian heartland and attacked Assur — the holy city where every Assyrian king had been crowned and buried for over two millennia, the seat of the god who had given the entire civilization its name.
The city fell.
Nabopolassar arrived with his Babylonian forces after the city had already been taken, and the two kings met over the ruins to formalize their alliance.
The Babylonian Chronicle records the event with characteristic understatement, but the psychological devastation was immense.
Assur was the spiritual heart of the Assyrian world.
Its fall shattered the theological foundation of the empire — if the god Ashur could not protect his own city, what confidence could anyone have in his power to protect anything else?
Two years later, in 612 BCE, the allied forces converged on Nineveh.
The siege of Nineveh was the climactic event of the ancient Near East in the seventh century BCE — the moment when the most powerful state the world had ever built was destroyed.
Nabopolassar's Babylonian forces and Cyaxares's Median army surrounded the city, and a third force — possibly including Scythian contingents, though their role remains debated — joined the assault.
Nineveh's massive walls, which Sennacherib had built to be impregnable, withstood the siege for approximately three months.

The city that had been designed as the ultimate expression of Assyrian power — 750 hectares behind 12 kilometers of walls, 15 monumental gates, the Palace Without Rival, the Library of Ashurbanipal — held.
And then the Tigris flooded.
The Babylonian Chronicle records that the river rose and breached a section of the city walls.

Whether this was a natural flood (the Tigris floods seasonally, and a siege that lasted into the summer rainy season would have coincided with high water), a deliberate diversion of the river by the besiegers (Sennacherib himself had demonstrated this technique when he destroyed Babylon), or some combination of the two is not certain from the surviving sources.

But the biblical Book of Nahum (NAY-um; a Judean prophet who composed his book either just before or just after Nineveh's fall) had prophesied: "The river gates are thrown open, and the palace is dissolved." Whether Nahum was genuinely prophetic or wrote after the event is debated, but the image is vivid: the walls breached by water, the army of the world's mightiest empire pouring through the gaps.
The city fell.
Sin-shar-ishkun, the last king of Nineveh, died in the flames of his own palace.
The parallel with Shamash-shum-ukin — who had died in his burning palace in Babylon thirty-six years earlier (Chapter 7) — was not lost on contemporaries.
The palace that Sennacherib had named the "Palace Without Rival" burned.
The rooms where Ashurbanipal's library was stored burned.

And in one of the great ironies of archaeological history, the fires that destroyed Nineveh also baked the unfired clay tablets of the library into hard, durable ceramic, preserving them far better than normal storage would have.
When British Museum archaeologists began excavating Nineveh in the 1840s, they recovered tens of thousands of these baked tablets in remarkably good condition.

In 1872, a British Museum assistant named George Smith was sorting through the Library of Ashurbanipal tablets when he identified one containing a flood narrative — a survivor named Utnapishtim describing how he built a boat, loaded two of every animal, survived a world-destroying flood, and sent out a dove and a raven to find land.

Smith recognized the parallels to the biblical Noah narrative and, according to a story that may be apocryphal but is irresistible, became so excited that he began pulling off his clothes in the middle of the British Museum workroom.
He had just discovered Mesopotamian literature.

Ashurbanipal's library, preserved in the ashes of the civilization it was meant to glorify, is now the single largest source of cuneiform texts in the world, and the primary basis for almost everything we know about Mesopotamian religion, literature, mythology, science, and daily life.
The destruction of Nineveh was celebrated across the ancient world.
The biblical Book of Nahum is essentially a poem of savage joy: "Woe to the city of blood, full of lies, full of plunder, never without victims!
The crack of whips, the clatter of wheels, galloping horses and jolting chariots!
Charging cavalry, flashing swords and glittering spears!

Many casualties, piles of dead, bodies without number, people stumbling over the corpses." After centuries of Assyrian terror — the mass deportations, the flayed rebels, the impaled prisoners, the destroyed cities — the ancient world greeted the empire's fall without a single recorded expression of mourning.
Not one ally came to Assyria's defense voluntarily.
The universal relief at Nineveh's destruction was itself a verdict on the Assyrian model of empire.
But the Assyrian Empire did not die at Nineveh.
A remnant survived.

Ashur-uballit II (not to be confused with the earlier Ashur-uballit I who had freed Assyria from Mitanni, Chapter 3) claimed the Assyrian throne and retreated westward to Harran (hah-RAHN), an ancient trade city in Upper Mesopotamia near the modern Turkish-Syrian border.

Harran had a temple of the moon god Sin (the same god whose cult Nabonidus of Babylon would later favor, leading to his own downfall; Mesopotamia Ch 12), and it was far enough from the ruins of Nineveh to be defensible — at least temporarily.
What happened next was one of the strangest reversals in ancient history.
Egypt — the same Egypt that Esarhaddon had conquered and Ashurbanipal had plundered barely half a century earlier — sent an army north to support the Assyrian remnant.

Pharaoh Necho II (NEH-koh) of the 26th (Saite) Dynasty calculated that a weakened Assyria on the Euphrates was preferable to a powerful Babylon, and he marched his forces through the Levant to link up with Ashur-uballit II at Harran.
The former conqueror and the former conquered were now allies against a common enemy.
The world had turned upside down.
But Egyptian help came too late.
In 609 BCE, Nabopolassar's Babylonian army drove Ashur-uballit II from Harran.
Necho II's Egyptian forces arrived after the city had fallen.
A combined Assyrian-Egyptian counterattack to retake Harran failed.
Ashur-uballit II vanishes from the historical record after this point — his fate is unknown, but the Assyrian Empire was finished.
There is a footnote to the fall of Harran that echoes through biblical history.

Josiah (joh-SYE-uh), the king of Judah, attempted to block Necho II's march north at Megiddo (meh-GID-oh) — either because Judah was allied with Babylon or because Josiah feared Egyptian power on his border.
Josiah was killed in the battle.
His death was a catastrophe for the Kingdom of Judah: Josiah had been the reformer king who centralized worship at the Jerusalem Temple and purged foreign religious practices.
His death left Judah weakened and vulnerable, and within a generation, Nebuchadnezzar II would destroy Jerusalem and exile the Judean population to Babylon (Mesopotamia Ch 12).

The battle of Megiddo is also the origin of the word Armageddon (from the Hebrew Har Megiddo, "Mount of Megiddo"), which the Book of Revelation uses as the name for the final apocalyptic battle — an indication of how deeply the events at Megiddo in 609 BCE burned themselves into religious memory.
The Babylonians and Medes divided the Assyrian Empire between them.
Babylonia took Mesopotamia, Syria, and the Levant.
The Medes took the Assyrian heartland, the Armenian highlands, and eastern Anatolia.

But the infrastructure that the Assyrians had built — the provincial system, the road network, the administrative procedures, the intelligence networks, the concept of a professional standing army — survived under new management.
When Cyrus the Great (Persian Empire Ch 1) conquered both the Median and Babylonian kingdoms a generation later to build the Persian Empire, he inherited the Assyrian toolkit intact.
The Persian Royal Road followed Assyrian routes.
The Persian satrapy system echoed Assyrian provinces.
Persian imperial couriers used Assyrian relay stations.

Even the Persian policy of religious tolerance can be understood as a deliberate reaction against Assyrian terror — Cyrus and his successors had seen what happened to empires that ruled through fear alone, and they chose a different path.
The destruction of Nineveh was so complete that the city was never rebuilt.
Within two centuries, its location was forgotten.

When the Greek general Xenophon (ZEN-oh-fon; an Athenian soldier and writer who led a mercenary army through Mesopotamia in 401 BCE — barely two hundred years after the fall) marched past the ruins of Nineveh, the local villagers could not identify what city had once stood there.
The mounds of rubble on the banks of the Tigris were just that — mounds.
The empire that had terrorized the ancient world for three centuries had been so thoroughly erased that even its physical remains were anonymous.
It was not until 1845, when the British archaeologist Austen Henry Layard began excavating the mounds opposite Mosul, that Nineveh was rediscovered.
Layard uncovered the palaces of Sennacherib and Ashurbanipal, the winged lamassu that had guarded the gates, and the tens of thousands of cuneiform tablets that Ashurbanipal had collected.
The discovery was a sensation in Victorian England and launched the modern discipline of Assyriology.
For the first time in over two millennia, the world could read what the Assyrians had written about themselves.
What they had written was, among other things, a warning.
Assyria was the ancient world's first attempt to solve the problem of ruling everything through organized violence.

The professional army, the provincial administration, the road network, the intelligence service, the deportation system, the siege engineers, the psychological terror — it was the most efficient machinery of domination anyone had ever built.
And it worked, brilliantly, for about three centuries.
But the system contained its own destruction.
Every atrocity created enemies.
Every deportation created communities with grievances.
Every burned city was a settlement that would never produce tax revenue again.

The empire that held itself together through fear could only survive as long as it was feared — and the moment it showed weakness, the accumulated hatred of centuries exploded simultaneously from every direction.
In the contemporary world, China's Spring and Autumn period (Ancient China Ch 6) was producing the philosophical traditions that would shape East Asian civilization for millennia.
In Greece, the city-states were developing the democratic institutions that would define Western political thought.
In Persia, Cyrus the Great was about to demonstrate that there was another way to run an empire — through absorption rather than annihilation.
The Assyrians had proven that organized violence could conquer the world.
Their successors would prove that it couldn't hold it.
END OF FIRST DRAFT — 8 chapters, all 67 events covered, comprehensive coverage from ~2500 to 609 BCE

chapters.title: The Land Before the Hittites
sentences: Before the Hittites arrived, somebody else was already there.
That sentence sounds obvious, but it matters enormously for understanding what the Hittite Empire became.
The Hittites did not build their civilization from scratch on empty land.
They walked into a world that already had cities, trade networks, metalwork traditions, and religious practices stretching back more than a thousand years.
They absorbed that world so completely that they named their own empire after the people they had replaced.
For the next five centuries, the Hittites called their kingdom Hatti (HAH-tee) and their land the "Land of Hatti," even though they were not Hattians.
It would be as if the English had conquered France and then spent the next five hundred years calling their kingdom "France" and referring to themselves in official documents as rulers of the Franks.
Understanding who the Hittites replaced, and how thoroughly they absorbed the culture they found, is the starting point for everything that follows.
Anatolia (modern Turkey) is one of the great crossroads of the ancient world.
The word itself comes from the Greek anatole, meaning "sunrise" or "the east," because for the Greeks looking across the Aegean Sea from their mainland, Anatolia was where the sun came up.

The landmass is a vast peninsula roughly 300,000 square miles (780,000 square kilometers) in area, about three times the size of the United Kingdom, bounded by the Black Sea to the north, the Aegean Sea to the west, the Mediterranean to the south, and the highlands of Armenia and the upper reaches of the Tigris and Euphrates rivers to the east.

Its eastern border shades into the northern edge of Mesopotamia (the land between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers in modern Iraq, where the world's first cities emerged around 3500 BCE; Mesopotamia Ch 1-3).
Its western coast faces the Aegean islands and, across the water, Greece.
Anatolia is literally the bridge between Europe and Asia, and every empire that has ever tried to control the Near East has had to deal with it.

The central Anatolian plateau, where the Hittite Empire would eventually be centered, is a high, arid steppe roughly 3,000 to 4,000 feet (900 to 1,200 meters) above sea level, ringed by mountain ranges on all sides.

The climate is continental: brutally cold winters with heavy snow, hot dry summers, and a short growing season that makes agriculture harder than in the irrigated river valleys of Mesopotamia or the rain-fed coastal zones of the Mediterranean.
Annual rainfall on the central plateau runs about 12 to 16 inches (300 to 400 mm), enough for barley and wheat in a good year but marginal in a bad one.
The landscape is vast, treeless, and wind-scoured, with deep river valleys cutting through the rock.
It is not a generous land.
The people who thrived there were tough.
But Anatolia's real wealth was underground.
The mountains ringing the plateau contained some of the richest mineral deposits in the ancient Near East: copper, silver, gold, lead, and (critically for later chapters) iron.
Copper from the Taurus Mountains in the south and silver from deposits near the coast made Anatolia a magnet for trade from the earliest periods.
When the Bronze Age economy ran on copper and tin, Anatolia had the copper.
When the Iron Age economy replaced bronze with iron, Anatolia had that too.
Control of Anatolian metals was a strategic prize that drew merchants, invaders, and empire-builders for millennia.
A quick note on "firsts" before we go further.

Ancient history is full of claims about who did what first, and virtually all of them should be read as "first surviving evidence of" rather than "first time this ever happened." What we find is shaped by what happens to survive: a city destroyed by fire may have its clay tablets baked into permanent ceramic, preserving them for thousands of years, while a city peacefully abandoned may have its records crumble to dust.
The Hittites left spectacular records because their capital was eventually burned and buried, preserving the archive.
Other civilizations may have done comparable things earlier and left no trace.

Keep that caveat in mind whenever this narrative says "first" or "earliest." The people living in central Anatolia before the Hittites arrived are called the Hattians (HAT-ee-uns), and they are one of the most tantalizing mysteries in ancient Near Eastern history.
We know they existed because the Hittites themselves told us.
Hittite cuneiform texts refer to the Hattic language as a separate, sacred tongue used in religious rituals, distinct from the Hittite language that the rulers spoke in daily life.
Modern linguists have identified Hattic as a language isolate, meaning it belongs to no known language family.

It is not related to the Semitic languages of Mesopotamia (Akkadian, Babylonian, Assyrian), not related to the Indo-European languages that the Hittites spoke, not related to the Hurrian or Sumerian languages, not related to anything else we have ever found.
The Hattians spoke a language that was, as far as we can tell, uniquely their own.
What we know about the Hattians comes mostly from archaeology and from the ritual texts that the Hittites preserved in the Hattic language.

The Hattians occupied central Anatolia from at least the third millennium BCE, probably much earlier, and they built a sophisticated culture centered on fortified towns, metalworking, and a religious tradition that the Hittites would later absorb wholesale.

The most spectacular surviving evidence of Hattian civilization comes from a site called Alaca Höyük (ah-LAH-jah huh-YOOK), about 125 miles (200 km) northeast of Ankara, where a series of royal tombs dating to roughly 2400-2200 BCE yielded some of the most stunning metalwork in the ancient world.

The Alaca Höyük tombs contained gold jewelry, bronze weapons, and most strikingly, a collection of bronze and gold "sun disc standards" (elaborate metal discs mounted on long poles, possibly carried in processions or placed atop temples) that have no parallel anywhere else in the ancient Near East.
These are not crude objects.
They show sophisticated casting, inlay work, and artistic design that required generations of accumulated skill.

Alongside the standards, the tombs held bronze figurines of bulls and stags (both animals that would remain central to Anatolian religion for the next two thousand years), gold diadems, and the remains of individuals who had clearly been wealthy and powerful.

The Alaca Höyük tombs tell us that by 2400 BCE, at least, central Anatolia had a stratified society with a wealthy elite, skilled metalworkers, and religious practices centered on animal symbolism and solar imagery.
This was not a primitive backwater waiting to be civilized by incoming Indo-Europeans.
It was a developed culture with its own traditions, its own art, its own gods, and its own political structures.
When the Hittites arrived, they did not impose civilization on barbarians.
They moved into someone else's house and redecorated.
The other crucial piece of Anatolia's pre-Hittite world was commercial, not cultural, and it came from the east.
Starting around 1950 BCE, merchants from the city of Assur (ah-SHOOR) on the Tigris River in northern Mesopotamia established a network of trading colonies across Anatolia.
These were not military outposts or diplomatic missions.

They were commercial districts, called karums (kah-ROOMS; literally "quay" or "harbor" in Akkadian, even though most of them were nowhere near the sea), attached to existing Anatolian cities under negotiated agreements with local rulers.
The largest and most important karum was at a city called Kanesh (kah-NESH; modern Kültepe, in central Turkey, about 150 miles or 240 km southeast of where Hattusa would later stand).
The Assyrian merchants at Kanesh left behind over 23,000 cuneiform tablets documenting their business (Assyrian Empire Ch 2).

These tablets record tin shipments from Iran, textile exports from Assur, loan contracts, insurance agreements, tax disputes, and personal letters between merchants abroad and their wives managing the family business back home.
They reveal that Anatolia in the early second millennium BCE was thoroughly integrated into the broader Near Eastern commercial world.
Tin flowed from eastern Iran through Assur to Anatolia.
Copper from Anatolian mines flowed back east.
Silver and gold moved in both directions.
The Anatolian kingdoms were not isolated hilltop communities.
They were nodes in an international trade network stretching from Afghanistan to the Aegean.
For the Hittite story, two things about the Kanesh karum matter enormously.
First, the Assyrian merchants brought cuneiform (the wedge-shaped writing system invented by the Sumerians around 3400 BCE and later adapted across the entire Near East; Mesopotamia Ch 3).
Before the karums, Anatolia had no writing system.
After the karums, cuneiform was available as a technology that anyone who learned it could adapt to their own language.
The Hittites would do exactly that, adapting Mesopotamian cuneiform to write their Indo-European language and producing the earliest known written records in any Indo-European tongue (Chapter 2).
Second, the Kanesh tablets contain the earliest known references to people with recognizably Hittite (that is, Indo-European) names living in Anatolia alongside the indigenous Hattian population.
Names like Pithana and Anitta appear in the tablets as rulers of Anatolian city-states who were doing business with the Assyrian merchants.

These names are linguistically Indo-European, not Hattic, which means that by 1900 BCE at the latest, the people who would become the Hittites were already in Anatolia, already ruling some cities, and already participating in the commercial economy.
The question of exactly when they arrived and from where is one of the great debates in ancient Near Eastern studies.
This brings us to the question that made the Hittites famous among linguists: how do we know they were Indo-European?

Indo-European is a language family (a group of languages descended from a common ancestor) that includes most of the languages spoken in Europe and western Asia today: English, French, German, Spanish, Russian, Greek, Latin, Persian, Hindi, and dozens of others.
It is the largest language family in the world by number of speakers.

The ancestral language from which all these languages descend is called Proto-Indo-European, and it was probably spoken by pastoral nomads somewhere on the Pontic-Caspian steppe (the vast grasslands north of the Black Sea and Caspian Sea, in modern Ukraine and southern Russia) sometime between 4500 and 3000 BCE.
Nobody wrote Proto-Indo-European down.
We reconstruct it by comparing its descendant languages, the same way biologists reconstruct ancestral species from their modern descendants.
The Hittites matter for this story because their language, when it was finally deciphered in 1915 (Chapter 8), turned out to be the oldest written Indo-European language ever found.

The decipherment was done by a Czech linguist named Bedřich Hrozný (pronounced "BED-rzhikh HROZ-nee"), who was reading cuneiform tablets from Hattusa and noticed that certain words looked suspiciously like words in known Indo-European languages.
The breakthrough sentence was deceptively simple: a line about bread and water that contained words recognizable from Latin and English cognates.
Hrozný realized he was looking at an Indo-European language written in Mesopotamian cuneiform script, and that realization fundamentally changed our understanding of the ancient Near East.

Before Hrozný's decipherment, scholars had assumed that the Bronze Age Near East was dominated by Semitic-speaking peoples (Babylonians, Assyrians, Canaanites) and a few language isolates (Sumerians, Hattians).
The discovery that one of the region's great empires spoke an Indo-European language forced a complete rethinking of migration patterns, cultural contacts, and linguistic history.

The Hittite language is now classified as part of the Anatolian branch of Indo-European, and it is as different from Akkadian (the Semitic language of Mesopotamia) as English is from Arabic or Chinese.
But here is the crucial point: being linguistically Indo-European did not make the Hittites culturally European.
The Hittites wrote in Mesopotamian cuneiform.
They worshipped a Hattian storm god.
They built their capital on a Hattian sacred site.
They governed through a system of vassal treaties borrowed from Mesopotamian diplomatic practice.
They dressed in Anatolian clothing, ate Anatolian food, and practiced Anatolian agricultural techniques.
Linguistically, they were distant cousins of the Greeks and the Romans.
Culturally, they were Anatolians who had absorbed the Hattian world they had entered.
Language family tells you about origins.
It does not tell you about identity.
How the Indo-European speakers came to Anatolia is debated.

Some scholars favor a gradual infiltration over centuries, with small groups of pastoralists filtering south through the Caucasus Mountains (the range between the Black Sea and the Caspian Sea) or west across the Bosphorus strait (the narrow waterway connecting the Black Sea to the Mediterranean, cutting through modern Istanbul).
Others argue for a more organized migration.

The archaeological evidence is ambiguous: there is no single "arrival" layer in Anatolian excavations, no sudden break in material culture that screams "new people showed up." What the evidence shows is a gradual linguistic shift, where Indo-European names start appearing alongside Hattic names in the commercial records, and over time, the Indo-European speakers moved from being one population among many to being the ruling class.

They may have looked, dressed, and lived almost identically to their Hattian neighbors while speaking a fundamentally different language, in the same way that Norman French-speakers and Anglo-Saxon English-speakers coexisted in medieval England for generations before their languages blended.
By roughly 1750 BCE, the stage was set.
Central Anatolia had a Hattian cultural substrate with sophisticated metalworking, religious traditions, and urban centers.
It had an international trade network linking it to Mesopotamia and beyond.
And it had a ruling class that spoke an Indo-European language, used cuneiform to write it, and was about to build something new on the foundations of everything that had come before.
The next chapter tells the story of the man who cursed the site of Hattusa, and the king who rebuilt it anyway.

chapters.title: The First Kings
sentences: Around 1750 BCE, a king named Anitta (ah-NEE-tah) of the city of Kussara (koo-SAH-rah) did something dramatic.
He conquered the city of Hattusa, burned it to the ground, and placed a curse on anyone who dared to rebuild it.

His inscription, recovered from the ruins of Kanesh, is the earliest known text written in the Hittite language, and it reads with the blunt ferocity of a man who wanted to make sure his enemies stayed dead: "At night I took the city by force.
I sowed cress in its place.
Whoever becomes king after me and resettles Hattusa, let the Storm God of Heaven strike him down." Cress.
He planted a salad green on the ashes, the ancient equivalent of salting the earth, a ritual statement that this place was finished, cursed, done.
The Storm God himself would punish anyone foolish enough to rebuild.
About a hundred years later, a king named Hattusili I (hat-too-SEE-lee; his name literally means "man of Hattusa") rebuilt Hattusa, moved his capital there, and founded the Hittite Old Kingdom.
He either did not know about Anitta's curse, did not care, or figured the Storm God had better things to do.
The Storm God, for his part, does not appear to have followed through on the threat.
Hattusa would remain the Hittite capital for the next four hundred years, through the rise, peak, and catastrophic fall of the empire.
The gap between Anitta's destruction of Hattusa and Hattusili I's refounding of it is one of the murkier periods in Hittite history.
Anitta himself is a fascinating figure: an Indo-European-speaking king who conquered most of central Anatolia from his base at Kussara, a city whose location has never been definitively identified.

His inscription at Kanesh describes military campaigns, the conquest of rival cities, and the destruction of Hattusa with enough specificity to be taken as broadly historical, though the details are impossible to verify independently.
He may have been the first ruler to unite a significant portion of central Anatolia under a single authority, but his kingdom did not survive him.
After Anitta, the record goes dark for a century.
Hattusili I (ruled approximately 1650-1620 BCE) is the first Hittite king we can see clearly.
He chose Hattusa as his capital for reasons that were probably more strategic than sentimental.
The site sits on a rugged, defensible ridge in a narrow river valley, surrounded by steep rocky outcrops that form natural walls on three sides.
At an elevation of roughly 3,600 feet (1,100 meters), it commands a broad view of the surrounding countryside.
The river provided water.
The rock provided fortification.
And the location, in the heartland of the old Hattian cultural zone, gave Hattusili I a claim to legitimacy over the indigenous population.
By naming himself after the city and the city after the Hattian land, he was signaling that his kingdom was not a foreign conquest but a continuation of what had been there before.
It was a shrewd political maneuver, and it worked.
The city Hattusili I built (and his successors expanded enormously) eventually grew into one of the largest fortified cities in the ancient Near East.

At its peak, Hattusa covered roughly 400 acres (160 hectares), roughly the size of 300 football fields, and was surrounded by a massive double wall nearly 4 miles (6 km) in circumference with over a hundred towers.

The walls enclosed a lower city (with the Great Temple and residential quarters), an upper city (with dozens of temples built during the empire's later centuries), and a fortified royal citadel called Büyükkale (boo-YOOK-kah-leh; "Great Fortress" in Turkish) perched on the highest point of the ridge.
The famous Lions Gate (flanked by massive stone lion sculptures that still stand today) and the Sphinx Gate guarded entrances to the upper city.
A tunnel roughly 230 feet (70 meters) long, the Yerkapı (YER-kah-puh) postern, ran underneath the southern wall, allowing defenders to launch surprise sorties against besiegers.
Population estimates for the city at its height range from 40,000 to 50,000 people, making it comparable to the great cities of Mesopotamia and Egypt.
But all of that lay in the future.
In Hattusili I's time, Hattusa was a new foundation built on cursed ground, and the king was more interested in expansion than in architecture.
Hattusili I was a conqueror.

He campaigned south into Syria, capturing the wealthy city of Aleppo (already ancient and important, a major trade center connecting Mesopotamia to the Mediterranean coast) and pushing Hittite power far beyond the Anatolian plateau for the first time.

These campaigns brought the Hittites into direct contact, and conflict, with the kingdoms of northern Syria and Upper Mesopotamia, including the Hurrians (HOOR-ee-uns), a people who spoke yet another unrelated language (Hurrian, which belongs to no known language family and is as different from both Hittite and Akkadian as Korean is from Arabic).
The Hurrians would become one of the Hittites' most persistent rivals, and eventually one of their most important cultural influences (Chapter 4).
Hattusili I also left behind something unusual for a Bronze Age king: an autobiography of sorts.
His "Testament" or "Political Testament" describes his reign, his military campaigns, and most revealingly, his family troubles.

The document records how Hattusili originally designated his nephew as heir, then disinherited him when the nephew turned against him, and finally chose his grandson Mursili (moor-SEE-lee) as his successor.
The text drips with frustration and betrayal.
Hattusili addresses the assembled nobles directly, warning them to support his chosen heir, recounting the treachery of his relatives, and lamenting that his own family has been his worst enemy.

It is one of the earliest surviving documents in which a ruler speaks frankly about the messy reality of court politics, and it established a pattern that would define the Hittite Old Kingdom: brilliant military expansion abroad, vicious political infighting at home.
The institution that made Hittite court politics uniquely volatile was the panku (PAHN-koo), an assembly of nobles that played a role in royal governance that scholars still debate.
Some historians describe it as a proto-parliament, a check on royal authority that had to approve the king's decisions.
Others argue it was more of a rubber stamp that the king convened when he wanted to legitimize decisions he had already made.
What is clear is that the Hittite king was not an absolute monarch in the Mesopotamian or Egyptian sense.
He ruled with the cooperation (or at least the acquiescence) of a warrior aristocracy that could, and regularly did, depose kings who lost their confidence.

The result was a political system that was more dynamic and more unstable than anything in Egypt, where the pharaoh's divine status placed him above challenge, or Babylon, where palace coups happened but were not structurally encouraged.
The reign of Hattusili I's grandson and successor, Mursili I (ruled approximately 1610-1590 BCE), produced one of the most audacious military operations in ancient history.

In approximately 1595 BCE (this date follows the "Middle Chronology" used by most scholars; other chronological systems place it as late as 1531 BCE), Mursili I marched his army out of Hattusa, south through Syria, and east down the Euphrates River on a campaign that covered roughly 600 miles (1,000 km) of hostile territory.
His target was the most prestigious city in the ancient world: Babylon (Mesopotamia Ch 7).
And he took it.

The sack of Babylon by Mursili I ended the First Babylonian Dynasty, the dynasty that had produced Hammurabi (hah-moo-RAH-bee; the king famous for his law code, who had ruled Babylon roughly 1792-1750 BCE; Mesopotamia Ch 7).
By 1595 BCE, Hammurabi's dynasty was already in decline, but Babylon remained the cultural and economic center of Mesopotamia.
No one expected a highland Anatolian kingdom to reach that far.
The raid was the ancient equivalent of a land power in Ukraine suddenly sacking Paris: geographically implausible, strategically insane, and yet it happened.
How exactly Mursili pulled it off is debated.
He may have allied with the Kassites (KAS-ites; a people from the Zagros Mountains who would go on to rule Babylon for the next four centuries after the Hittites left; Mesopotamia Ch 8).

The logistics of the campaign, moving a Bronze Age army 600 miles through hostile territory and then 600 miles back, suggest either careful advance planning with supply depots and local allies, or a much smaller raiding force than the texts imply.

The Hittites looted Babylon's treasures and carried off the statue of Marduk (MAR-dook; the patron god of Babylon, whose statue was understood to literally embody the god's presence on earth; Mesopotamia Ch 9).
In Mesopotamian theology, stealing a city's god was the ultimate humiliation, a theological knockout that declared the conqueror's gods more powerful than the conquered city's.
But Mursili I could not hold what he had taken.
The Hittite army retreated back to Anatolia with its loot, leaving a power vacuum in Babylon that the Kassites filled.
The sack of Babylon was a spectacular raid, not a permanent conquest, and it had the unintended consequence of destabilizing Mesopotamia for generations.
Meanwhile, in 1274 BCE, the Hittites would reach another famous military milestone, this time against Egypt (Chapter 5).
But Mursili I never lived to see it.
He was assassinated.
And this is where the pattern that Hattusili I's Testament had warned about repeated itself, violently.
Mursili I was murdered by his brother-in-law Hantili (han-TEE-lee), who seized the throne.
Hantili was in turn murdered by his own son-in-law Zidanta (zee-DAN-tah).
Zidanta was murdered by his son Ammuna (am-MOO-nah).

The Hittite Old Kingdom descended into a spiral of palace assassinations that reads like a particularly grim season of a prestige television drama, except that every character is related to every other character by marriage and none of them live long enough to develop a personality in the surviving records.
The violence was not random.
It was structural.
The Hittite monarchy had no clear rule of succession.

The king could choose any male relative as his heir, the panku assembly had to approve the choice (or at least not actively oppose it), and ambitious relatives who were passed over had powerful incentives to act before the choice was finalized.
The combination of an elective element (the panku) and a hereditary element (the royal family) created a system where every succession was a potential civil war.

Compare this to Egypt, where the principle of divine kingship passing from father to son was so deeply embedded that even female pharaohs like Hatshepsut had to justify their rule through elaborate theological arguments, or to Mesopotamia, where succession was less stable than Egypt but more stable than Hatti because strong bureaucratic institutions could survive a change of ruler.
The Hittites had the worst of both worlds: a royal family that expected to keep power, and a nobility that expected to have a say in who held it.
The man who finally broke the cycle was Telipinu (teh-LIH-pee-noo; ruled approximately 1525-1500 BCE), and the tool he used was a document.
Telipinu himself had come to power by overthrowing the assassins of his predecessor, which made him, paradoxically, a usurper who was trying to end usurpation.

He issued the Edict of Telipinu, one of the most remarkable political documents from the ancient world: a formal decree establishing rules for royal succession, mandating that the throne pass to the king's firstborn son (or, in the absence of sons, to other specified relatives in a fixed order), and forbidding the nobles from killing members of the royal family.
The Edict opens with a historical prologue that is, effectively, the Hittite Old Kingdom's own history of itself.

Telipinu reviews the reigns of his predecessors, notes which ones were murdered and by whom, traces the disastrous consequences of each assassination for the kingdom's military and economic health, and then presents his succession rules as the logical solution.

The document is not a constitution in the modern sense (it has no enforcement mechanism beyond the gods' displeasure), but it is one of the earliest surviving attempts by a ruler to solve a political problem through written law rather than raw force.
The Code of Hammurabi (Mesopotamia Ch 7) predated it by about two centuries, but Hammurabi's code was a collection of judicial precedents.
Telipinu's Edict was something different: a structural reform of the political system, aimed at changing how power was transferred rather than how crimes were punished.
Did it work?
The assassinations slowed down after Telipinu, though they did not stop entirely.

More importantly, the Edict established a principle that later Hittite kings could invoke: that there were rules governing succession, that those rules had been written down, and that violating them was not just politically dangerous but religiously impious, an offense against the gods who had witnessed the document.

When Hattusili III usurped the throne three hundred years later (Chapter 5), he went to extraordinary lengths to justify his actions in writing, precisely because the Edict of Telipinu had established the expectation that succession should follow rules.
Even the kings who broke the rules felt compelled to explain why.
The period after Telipinu, roughly 1500 to 1400 BCE, is the least well-documented phase of Hittite history.
Scholars call it the "Middle Hittite Period" or the "Dark Age" of the kingdom, and it was characterized by external pressure from multiple directions.
The Kaska (KAS-kah) peoples, tribal groups from the forested mountains north of Hattusa, raided Hittite territory relentlessly.

The Kaska were not a unified kingdom but a loose collection of tribes who refused to submit to Hittite authority and periodically overran northern Hittite cities, possibly sacking Hattusa itself at least once.
They were the Hittites' permanent headache, a threat that no king ever definitively solved and that persisted for the entire life of the empire.
From the southeast, the Hurrian kingdoms (particularly the powerful kingdom of Mitanni, mih-TAH-nee, centered in northern Syria and Upper Mesopotamia) pressed against Hittite territory.
The Mitanni had mastered chariot warfare and built a formidable military state that rivaled both the Hittites and Egypt.
For much of the Middle Hittite Period, the Hittites were on the defensive, losing territory in Syria that Hattusili I and Mursili I had conquered a century earlier.
Meanwhile, on the world stage, this was the age of Egypt's New Kingdom.
Pharaohs like Thutmose III (ruled approximately 1479-1425 BCE) were campaigning deep into Syria, building an Egyptian empire that overlapped with Hittite ambitions in the same contested territory.
China's Shang dynasty (Ancient China Ch 3-4) was at its height, working bronze and developing the earliest Chinese writing on oracle bones.
In the Aegean, the Minoan civilization on Crete was in its golden age (Minoan Civilization Ch 4-5).

The international world of the Late Bronze Age was taking shape: an interconnected system of great powers that communicated through cuneiform diplomacy, traded across vast distances, and fought over the rich cities of the Levant and Syria.
The Hittites, during their Middle Period, were struggling to remain part of this system.
It took a remarkable king to pull them back into it.
His name was Suppiluliuma, and his story is the subject of the next chapter.

chapters.title: The Empire Rises
sentences: In the early 1300s BCE, the Hittite Empire was a second-tier power.
A generation later, it was one of the two or three most powerful states on Earth.
The man responsible for this transformation was Suppiluliuma I (soo-pee-loo-lee-OO-mah; ruled approximately 1355-1322 BCE), and he is, by any measure, the greatest king in Hittite history.

He rebuilt the army, reconquered lost territory, destroyed the Hittites' most dangerous rival, extended the empire from the Aegean coast to the Euphrates River, and established a diplomatic network that treated the Hittite king as the equal of the pharaoh of Egypt.

He also fathered a son who was murdered by the Egyptians, unleashed a plague that killed himself and devastated his kingdom for twenty years, and left behind a political legacy that his successors spent a century managing.
Suppiluliuma's reign is the hinge of Hittite history: everything before it was prologue, and everything after it was consequence.
The empire Suppiluliuma inherited was already recovering from its Middle Period slump.

His father, Tudhaliya I (too-DAHL-ee-yah; ruled approximately 1430-1400 BCE), had begun the process of reasserting Hittite power through campaigns against the Arzawa (ar-ZAH-wah) confederation in western Anatolia and the persistent Kaska raiders in the north.

Tudhaliya I also strengthened Hittite ties with the kingdom of Kizzuwatna (kiz-zoo-WAHT-nah) in southeastern Anatolia, a Hurrian-influenced state that served as a cultural bridge between the Hittite and Hurrian worlds.

Through Kizzuwatna, Hurrian religious practices, myths, and rituals flowed into Hittite culture, a process that would eventually produce the remarkable syncretic religious system described in Chapter 4.
But Suppiluliuma I turned recovery into dominance.
His primary target was the Mitanni kingdom, the Hurrian-speaking superpower that had been the Hittites' chief rival for control of northern Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.
Mitanni was powerful enough to correspond with Egypt as an equal.
It had married princesses into the Egyptian royal family.
It controlled the rich caravan cities of Syria and the trade routes linking Mesopotamia to the Mediterranean coast.
For the Hittites to become a true great power, Mitanni had to fall.
Around 1340 BCE, Suppiluliuma launched a devastating campaign against Mitanni that fundamentally reshaped the political map of the Near East.
The campaign unfolded in stages.
First, Suppiluliuma rebuilt Hattusa's defenses and secured the Hittite heartland against the ever-present Kaska threat.
Then he moved south and east, systematically peeling away Mitanni's Syrian vassals.

The kings of Aleppo, Ugarit (oo-gah-REET; a wealthy port city on the Syrian coast), and other Syrian cities switched their allegiance from Mitanni to the Hittites, calculating that Suppiluliuma's rising power was more dangerous to resist than to accommodate.
This was the Hittite diplomatic method in action: offer vassal treaties with clear mutual obligations, and make the alternative (military destruction) vivid enough to encourage acceptance.

The final blow came when Suppiluliuma crossed the Euphrates and apparently overran Washukanni (wah-shoo-KAH-nee), the Mitanni capital (the Hittite sources are fragmentary on this point, and whether Suppiluliuma actually sacked the city or merely reduced Mitanni's Syrian vassals is debated; the capital's location has never been definitively identified).
Mitanni did not disappear overnight.
A rump state survived as a buffer between the Hittites and the rising Assyrian power to the east.
But as an independent great power, Mitanni was finished.

Suppiluliuma installed vassal kings across Syria, placed his own sons on the thrones of Aleppo and Carchemish (KAR-keh-mish; a strategically vital city on the Euphrates, which would later become the most important Neo-Hittite successor state; Chapter 8), and established Hittite dominion over a territory stretching from the Aegean coast to the banks of the Euphrates.
The conquest of Mitanni brought the Hittites into the elite club of Bronze Age "Great Powers" alongside Egypt, Babylon, and (still just barely) Assyria.

The proof of this status comes from the Amarna Letters (ah-MAR-nah; a collection of about 380 cuneiform tablets found at Tell el-Amarna in Egypt, the site of the pharaoh Akhenaten's short-lived capital).

The Amarna Letters are the diplomatic correspondence of the Late Bronze Age international system, covering roughly 1360-1330 BCE, and they reveal a world of Great Kings who addressed each other as "Brother" and exchanged elaborate gifts, marriage proposals, and passive-aggressive complaints about the quality of the gold being sent.
The Hittite letters in the Amarna archive show Suppiluliuma corresponding with the pharaoh as an equal, demanding to be addressed with the same titles of respect, and complaining when he was not.
One letter from a Hittite king (probably Suppiluliuma) reproaches the pharaoh for not sending a proper gift to congratulate him on his accession to the throne.
The tone is that of offended dignity between peers, not supplication to a superior.
The Amarna Letters are one of the best windows into Bronze Age international relations, and they show the Hittites firmly established as a superpower that Egypt had to take seriously.
And then came the incident that might have changed everything.
Around 1323 BCE, the young pharaoh Tutankhamun (too-tahn-KAH-moon) died in Egypt without an heir.
What happened next is one of the most extraordinary episodes in ancient history, and scholars are still debating the details.

A letter arrived at the Hittite court from an Egyptian queen, identified in the Hittite text as Dahamunzu (dah-hah-MOON-zoo; this is not an Egyptian name but the Hittite rendering of the Egyptian title ta hemet nesu, meaning "the king's wife").
The most common identification is Ankhesenamun (ahn-keh-SEH-nah-moon), Tutankhamun's widow and daughter of the famous pharaoh Akhenaten.
The letter, as preserved in a Hittite-language summary composed by Mursili II decades later, said (in scholarly paraphrase): "My husband has died.
A son I have not.
But to you, they say, your sons are many.
If you would give me one of your sons, he would become my husband.
Never shall I pick out a servant of mine and make him my husband.
I am afraid." This was staggering.
An Egyptian queen was asking a foreign king to send a prince to become pharaoh of Egypt.
In three thousand years of Egyptian history, nothing remotely like this had happened before.
Egypt did not import kings.
Egypt conquered foreigners; it did not marry them into the royal family at the highest level.

The letter implies a court in crisis: a widowed queen, a power struggle among factions at court, and a woman desperate enough to seek help from Egypt's most dangerous rival rather than accept a marriage to a courtier she considered beneath her.
Suppiluliuma was suspicious.

"Such a thing has never happened to me in my whole life!" he reportedly said, according to the Hittite record of the affair (written by his son Mursili II as part of a chronicle called the "Deeds of Suppiluliuma").
He sent an envoy to Egypt to investigate.
The queen wrote again, this time with evident frustration: "Why did you say 'they deceive me' in that way?
Had I a son, would I have written about my own and my country's shame to a foreign land?
You did not believe me and have even spoken thus to me.
He who was my husband has died.
A son I have not.
Never shall I take a servant of mine and make him my husband.
I have written to no other country, only to you have I written.
They say your sons are many: so give me one son of yours.
To me he will be husband, but in Egypt he will be king." Suppiluliuma relented.
He sent his son Zannanza (zan-NAN-zah) to Egypt.
Zannanza was murdered on the way.
Who killed him is unknown.
