The destruction of the First Temple became the defining catastrophe of Jewish history, commemorated annually on the fast day of Tisha B'Av (tih-SHAH beh-AHV, the ninth day of the Hebrew month of Av), a day of mourning that has been observed continuously for over two and a half millennia.
Jeremiah, the prophet who had predicted exactly this outcome and been persecuted for saying it, survived the destruction.

According to the biblical account, Nebuchadnezzar's forces, aware of his pro-Babylonian counsel, freed him from prison and offered him the choice of going to Babylon with honor or remaining in the land.
He chose to stay.
When the Judahite remnant in the land assassinated the Babylonian-appointed governor and fled to Egypt (fearing Babylonian retaliation), they dragged the elderly Jeremiah with them.
He disappears from the historical record in Egypt, still prophesying, still unwanted, still right.
The Babylonian Exile (586-539 BCE) was not the end of the Israelite story.
It was, paradoxically, its beginning in a new form.
The exile forced a revolutionary transformation in Israelite religion.
Before 586, Israelite religion had been centered on three pillars: the Temple (the physical house of God), the king (God's anointed representative), and the land (God's promised inheritance).
All three were gone.
The Temple was rubble.
The king was blind in a Babylonian prison.
The people were scattered across Mesopotamia.
A religion built on these pillars should have died.
Instead, it transformed.

In exile, the Israelites did something no conquered people had done before: they explained their defeat as the act of their own God, punishing them for their own sins, and they reframed the catastrophe as a chapter in an ongoing story rather than the end of the story.
The prophets had warned them.
The covenant had specified the punishment.
The destruction was not evidence of Yahweh's weakness but of his terrible consistency.
This reinterpretation turned defeat into theology and turned theology into survival.
The development of the Hebrew calendar, a lunisolar system coordinating agricultural seasons with lunar months, also reached a more formalized state during the monarchic and exile periods.
Like the Babylonian calendar that influenced it, the Hebrew calendar added an intercalary (extra) month periodically to keep festivals aligned with harvests.

The religious festivals tied to this calendar (Passover in spring, Shavuot at harvest, Sukkot in autumn) became anchors of communal identity that survived the destruction of the Temple and the loss of the land.
You could observe Passover in Babylon.
You could not sacrifice at a temple that no longer existed.
The calendar kept the community synchronized even in exile.
The exile period saw an explosion of literary activity.
Much of the Hebrew Bible was compiled, edited, and brought into something approaching its present form during and shortly after the exile.
The Deuteronomistic History (Chapter 7) received its final edition.
The Priestly source (a literary strand emphasizing ritual, genealogy, and the Tabernacle) was composed or compiled.

The prophecies of Second Isaiah (chapters 40-55 of the book of Isaiah, written by an anonymous prophet in Babylon, not the eighth-century Isaiah of Jerusalem) proclaimed a message of comfort and return: "Comfort, comfort my people, says your God.

Speak tenderly to Jerusalem, and cry to her that her warfare is ended." Second Isaiah also articulated, for the first time in Israelite literature, an unambiguous strict monotheism: "I am the Lord, and there is no other; besides me there is no god." Before the exile, Israelite religion had been evolving from monolatry toward monotheism (Chapter 5).
The exile completed the journey.
The exile also produced one of the most important innovations in religious history: a religion that did not need a temple.
In Babylon, the exiled Judahites could not sacrifice (sacrifice could only be performed at the Jerusalem Temple).
So they developed new forms of communal worship centered on prayer, scripture reading, and Sabbath observance.

The synagogue (the communal house of prayer and study, from the Greek synagoge, meaning "assembly"), which would become the foundational institution of Judaism and the model for the Christian church and the Islamic mosque, probably originated in some form during the exile, though the earliest archaeological evidence for synagogues dates to later centuries.
For the first time, a major religion became portable.
You did not need a temple, a priesthood, or a homeland to practice it.
You needed a text and a community.
This portability is why Judaism survived when every other ancient Near Eastern religion perished with the temples and kingdoms that sustained them.

The Ten Commandments (the Decalogue), which tradition attributed to Moses at Sinai but which reached their canonical written form during the monarchic and exile periods, became the ethical core of the emerging religion.

The two slightly different versions preserved in Exodus and Deuteronomy suggest an evolving tradition, but the substance (prohibitions on murder, theft, adultery, false witness, and coveting, alongside commands to worship one God and keep the Sabbath) became foundational to Western legal and moral thought.

The idea that moral law could be written down, applied universally, and derived from a divine source rather than royal decree was not entirely unique to Israel (Mesopotamia's Code of Hammurabi, roughly a millennium earlier, shared some of these features; Mesopotamia Ch 7), but the Ten Commandments' influence on subsequent Western civilization was incomparably greater.
When Cyrus the Great of Persia conquered Babylon in 539 BCE (Persian Empire Ch 2) and issued an edict allowing the Judahite exiles to return and rebuild the Temple, some returned and some did not.

The Cyrus Cylinder, a clay barrel-inscription found in Babylon, describes Cyrus's policy of allowing deported peoples to return to their homelands and rebuild their temples, a practice the Persian Empire applied broadly, not just to the Judahites.
For the exiles, though, it was nothing short of miraculous.
Second Isaiah called Cyrus God's "anointed" (mashiach, the word that gives us "Messiah"), the only non-Israelite in the entire Bible to receive this title.
The Second Temple was completed around 516 BCE, seventy years after the destruction of the First, a modest structure that made the old men who remembered Solomon's Temple weep with the contrast.
The kingdom was never restored.
Judah became a province of successive empires: Persian, then Greek, then Roman.
But the religion had been transformed into something that did not need a kingdom.
It needed a book.

The Hebrew Bible, the collection of texts that emerged from this small, twice-destroyed, repeatedly conquered kingdom, became the foundational scripture of Judaism and, through its adoption and reinterpretation, of Christianity and Islam.
More than half the world's population today adheres to a religion that traces its roots to these texts.
The Book of Psalms is still sung.
The prophecies of Isaiah are still read.
The story of David and Goliath is still told.
The Passover is still observed.
The destruction of the Temple is still mourned.
The Book of Psalms, traditionally attributed to David but composed over several centuries (roughly 1000-500 BCE), became the hymnal of this new portable religion.
The 150 psalms cover the full range of human religious experience: praise, lament, anger, gratitude, despair, and hope.

Psalm 137, written in exile, captures the emotional devastation of the Babylonian captivity in words that still resonate: "By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat down and wept, when we remembered Zion.
On the willows there we hung up our lyres.
For there our captors required of us songs...
How shall we sing the Lord's song in a foreign land?" The answer, it turned out, was: you sing it anyway, and you write it down so your grandchildren can sing it too.
Ancient Israel was a tiny kingdom in a terrible location.
It lasted roughly 430 years from Saul to Zedekiah, less time than the gap between Columbus and the present day.
Its army was never more than middling.
Its territory never exceeded Vermont.
Its monuments have not survived.
But it produced a body of literature so powerful that it outlasted every empire that destroyed it.
Assyria is gone.
Babylon is gone.
Persia is gone.
Rome is gone.
The book is still here.
It is, by any measure, the most improbable survival story in the history of civilization.

chapters.title: The Saite Reunification
sentences: The New Kingdom Egypt narrative ended with a country split in two (New Kingdom Egypt Ch 9).
By 1069 BCE, the pharaoh controlled the Delta from a new capital at Tanis while the High Priest of Amun ruled Upper Egypt from Thebes as an effectively independent state.
The pyramids were already two thousand years old.
The Valley of the Kings had been robbed.
The empire was gone.
But Egypt was still performing the rituals, still insisting on the divine order, still recognizably itself.
What followed was roughly four hundred years of fragmentation, foreign rule, and stubborn cultural survival.

Historians call it the Third Intermediate Period (roughly 1069 to 664 BCE), and it is the backstory that this narrative needs before it can get to the real subject: Egypt's last native dynasties, and their astonishing refusal to fade quietly into someone else's empire.
The Third Intermediate Period is complicated, and deliberately so.
Multiple dynasties ruled simultaneously in different parts of Egypt, some of them overlapping by decades.

The conventional numbering system, inherited from Manetho (a Hellenistic-era Egyptian priest who wrote a Greek-language history of the pharaohs around 280 BCE, whose dynasty-numbering system Egyptologists still use), makes it look neater than it was.
The Twenty-first Dynasty (roughly 1069 to 943 BCE) ruled from Tanis (pronounced "TAH-nis"), a city in the northeastern Delta that served as the new royal capital.
Tanis was no Memphis, no Thebes.
It was a Delta town promoted above its natural station because geography had shifted.
The old royal residences in the south were now in the High Priest's territory.

Smendes I (pronounced "SMEN-deez"), a man whose exact relationship to the Twentieth Dynasty is debated, founded the new dynasty and established the pattern: a pharaoh in the north, a priestly dynasty in the south, and a polite fiction that both sides were part of the same unified state.
The polite fiction was maintained through intermarriage.
Daughters of the Tanite pharaohs married the High Priests of Amun at Thebes.
The High Priests sent their sons to the royal court.
Both sides used pharaonic titles.
Both sides built temples.
Neither side tried to conquer the other, at least not openly.
It was less a civil war than a negotiated separation, a dual monarchy held together by family ties and a shared commitment to keeping up appearances.
The Tanis royal tombs, discovered by the French archaeologist Pierre Montet in 1939, reveal how wealthy these northern pharaohs still were.

The tombs contained gold funerary masks, silver coffins, gold and lapis lazuli jewelry, and canopic jars of exquisite craftsmanship, rivaling the famous treasures of Tutankhamun (New Kingdom Egypt Ch 7).
Montet's discovery should have been one of the great archaeological events of the twentieth century.
Instead, it was announced in the same month that Germany invaded Poland.
The world was distracted.
The Tanis treasures remain among the least-known major archaeological discoveries in history, a footnote drowned out by the Second World War.
The next shift came from an unexpected direction.
Egypt had long employed Libyan warriors as mercenaries and frontier guards, settling them in military colonies across the western Delta.
Over generations, these Libyan settlers had accumulated land, influence, and local power, much the way the Hyksos had done in the eastern Delta a thousand years earlier (New Kingdom Egypt Ch 2).
By the mid-tenth century BCE, the Libyan military commanders had become the dominant political force in the Delta.
When the Twenty-first Dynasty ran out of kings around 943 BCE, a Libyan commander named Shoshenq I (pronounced "show-SHENK") took the throne and founded the Twenty-second Dynasty.
Shoshenq I was, briefly, a reunifier.
He placed family members in key positions across the country, including the High Priesthood of Amun at Thebes, attempting to stitch the Two Lands back together through family networks.
His most famous act was a military campaign into the Levant around 925 BCE, where he sacked cities in both the kingdoms of Israel and Judah.

The campaign is recorded on the Bubastite Portal at Karnak, a massive stone gateway listing the conquered towns, and it overlaps intriguingly with the Hebrew Bible's account of a pharaoh named "Shishak" who plundered the Temple of Solomon in Jerusalem.
Whether Shoshenq I and the biblical Shishak are the same person is widely accepted by scholars, though the details don't match perfectly.
Either way, the campaign demonstrated that even a fragmented Egypt could still project military power into the Levant, at least temporarily.
But Shoshenq I's reunification didn't hold.
Within two generations, the Twenty-second Dynasty fractured.
Rival Libyan dynasties sprang up across the Delta.
The Twenty-third Dynasty operated simultaneously with the Twenty-second, ruling from different Delta cities.
A Twenty-fourth Dynasty emerged at Sais (pronounced "SAH-iss"), another Delta town.
Egypt was not just divided between north and south anymore.
The Delta itself was a patchwork of competing Libyan warlords, each calling himself pharaoh, none controlling the whole country.
Despite the political fragmentation, the Libyan period was not a cultural wasteland.
The Twenty-second Dynasty produced exquisite metalwork, including some of the finest bronze and gold funerary equipment in Egyptian history.
The God's Wives of Amun at Thebes, many of them royal women installed by various dynasties to control the Theban religious establishment, became major patrons of art and temple construction.
The divine adoratrices, as they were also called, held enormous landholdings and commissioned monuments that kept the Theban artistic tradition alive through centuries of political chaos.
The cultural continuity was real, even when the political unity was not.
But this was still Egypt at its lowest ebb since the First Intermediate Period (Old Kingdom Egypt Ch 7).

The country that had built the pyramids, conquered the Levant, and weathered the Bronze Age Collapse was now a collection of petty kingdoms, each clinging to the pharaonic title while controlling no more than a few dozen miles of the Nile.

For context: while Egypt fragmented, the Assyrian Empire was consolidating its grip on Mesopotamia and the Levant under Tiglath-Pileser III and his successors (Assyrian Empire Ch 4-5), building the most ruthless military machine the ancient world had ever seen.
The two trajectories were about to collide.
Before they did, Egypt was conquered from a direction no one in the Delta expected.

The Kingdom of Kush (Ancient Nubia Ch 5-8, Kingdom of Kush Ch 1-5), centered at the sacred mountain of Jebel Barkal (pronounced "JEB-el BAR-kal") near the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in what is now northern Sudan, had been growing in power for centuries.
The Kushites were the heirs of the ancient Nubian civilization that Egypt had colonized during the New Kingdom.

They had absorbed Egyptian religion, architecture, writing, and royal ideology so thoroughly that when they looked north at the chaos of the Third Intermediate Period, they saw not a foreign country but a broken sacred landscape that needed to be repaired.
Around 747 BCE, the Kushite king Piye (pronounced "PEE-yeh," also sometimes rendered as Piankhi) marched north into Egypt.
His Victory Stela at Jebel Barkal, one of the longest ancient Egyptian texts ever discovered, describes the campaign in vivid detail.
Piye was appalled by what he found.
The Libyan dynasts of the Delta were, in his view, impious pretenders defiling the ancient order.
His stela describes his insistence on ritual purity: he refused to admit any ruler to his presence who had eaten fish or was ritually unclean.

When one Delta ruler surrendered and offered to let Piye visit his stables, the stela records with evident pleasure that the horses were well cared for, then adds, with what sounds like genuine outrage, that the other horses in Egypt were being starved.
Piye was a conqueror who cared more about ritual correctness and horse welfare than about plunder.
Piye conquered all of Egypt, from the Fourth Cataract to the Mediterranean, founding the Twenty-fifth Dynasty.
Then he did something remarkable: he went home to Kush.

He left the Delta rulers in place as vassals, returned to his capital at Napata, and was eventually buried beneath a pyramid in the Kushite royal cemetery at El-Kurru (pronounced "el-KOO-roo"), near Jebel Barkal.
He had conquered Egypt to restore its spiritual order, not to loot it.

His successor Shabaka (pronounced "shah-BAH-kah") took a more conventional approach: he moved the royal court to Memphis, executed the remaining Delta rivals, and established direct Kushite control over the entire country.
Shabaka is remembered for one particular act of preservation.
He ordered an ancient theological text, apparently decaying on a worm-eaten papyrus, to be recopied onto stone.
The result is the Shabaka Stone, now in the British Museum, which preserves the Memphite Theology, a creation narrative in which the god Ptah creates the world through thought and speech.

Whether the text is genuinely ancient or a contemporary composition presented as ancient (scholars are divided), the Shabaka Stone represents the Twenty-fifth Dynasty's defining impulse: Egypt's past must be saved.
The greatest of the Kushite pharaohs was Taharqa (pronounced "tah-HAR-kah"), who reigned from roughly 690 to 664 BCE.
Taharqa was the most prolific builder of the Twenty-fifth Dynasty, restoring temples across both Egypt and Nubia.

At Karnak, he erected a massive colonnade in the first court whose single surviving column, nearly 70 feet (21 meters) tall with an open papyrus capital, remains one of the most impressive standing columns in Egypt.
At Jebel Barkal, he carved a temple deep into the sacred mountain itself.
He built at Thebes, at Memphis, at Kawa in Nubia.
The Twenty-fifth Dynasty was not occupying Egypt as conquerors.
They were investing in it as restorers.
Taharqa also appears in an unexpected source: the Hebrew Bible.
In 2 Kings 19:9, he is called "Tirhakah king of Ethiopia," listed as an ally of King Hezekiah of Judah against the Assyrian king Sennacherib.

Whether Taharqa actually fought the Assyrians in Palestine during this period or whether the biblical account conflates different events is debated, but the connection reveals something important about geopolitics in the late eighth century BCE.
The Kushite pharaohs were players in the great-power game of the Near East, allying with Levantine kingdoms against Assyrian expansion.
This brought them directly into conflict with the most dangerous military power on Earth.
In 671 BCE, the Assyrian king Esarhaddon (Assyrian Empire Ch 7) invaded Egypt, defeated Taharqa's army, and captured Memphis.
The Assyrians had done what no foreign army had accomplished since the Hyksos a thousand years earlier: they had conquered Egypt.
Esarhaddon set up vassal rulers in the Delta and withdrew, but Taharqa retook Memphis as soon as the Assyrian army left.
The Assyrians came back.
In 667 BCE, Esarhaddon's successor Ashurbanipal (pronounced "ah-shur-BAH-nee-pal," Assyrian Empire Ch 8) sent another army south.
This time, Ashurbanipal went further.
In 663 BCE, the Assyrian army reached Thebes and sacked it.
The Sack of Thebes was one of the most devastating events in Egyptian history.

Thebes had been the religious capital of Egypt for over a thousand years, home to the greatest temples in the ancient world, repository of centuries of accumulated treasure in the temple of Amun at Karnak.
The Assyrians plundered it systematically.

The prophet Nahum in the Hebrew Bible, writing about the fall of the Assyrian capital Nineveh decades later, uses the sack of Thebes as his standard of comparison: "Are you better than Thebes, that sat by the Nile, with water around her?
Yet she became an exile, she went into captivity." When the Assyrians wanted to demonstrate the consequences of resistance, they pointed to what they had done to the richest city in the world.
Taharqa retreated to Napata in Kush, where he died in 664 BCE.
His successor Tantamani (pronounced "tan-tah-MAH-nee") made one last attempt to retake Egypt, briefly recapturing Memphis, but the Assyrians drove him back south for good.
The Twenty-fifth Dynasty's rule over Egypt was finished.
Kushite pharaohs would continue to rule in Nubia for centuries (Kingdom of Kush Ch 5-8), maintaining Egyptian religious practices and building pyramids long after Egypt itself had abandoned them.
But their Egyptian empire was over.
What came next was one of the shrewdest political performances in the ancient world.
The Assyrians needed a local ruler to govern Egypt on their behalf.

They chose a man named Psamtik (pronounced "PSAM-tik," sometimes rendered as Psammetichus in Greek), the son of Necho I, a local dynast from the Delta city of Sais (pronounced "SAH-iss"), a town on the western Nile branch in the central Delta.
Necho I had been one of the Assyrians' client rulers.
He had been killed during Tantamani's brief reconquest.
Psamtik had been taken to the Assyrian court at Nineveh as a hostage, educated in Assyrian ways, and sent back to Egypt as a loyal vassal.
He was installed as ruler of the Delta around 664 BCE.
The Assyrians expected him to be their puppet.
He had other plans.
Psamtik I (reigned 664 to 610 BCE) is one of the most underappreciated rulers in ancient history.
Over the next fifteen years, he quietly, methodically, and without a single dramatic confrontation, dismantled Assyrian control over Egypt and reunified the country under native rule.
His method was patience.
The Assyrian Empire was vast but overstretched, fighting wars on multiple fronts from Babylon to Elam.
Psamtik waited for Assyria's attention to be drawn elsewhere, then moved.

He recruited Greek mercenaries, soldiers from the Ionian cities of western Anatolia and from Caria (pronounced "KAR-ee-ah," a region in southwestern Anatolia), who were among the finest infantry in the Mediterranean world.

These were hoplites (heavily armored foot soldiers fighting in tight formation with overlapping shields, a style of warfare that would later make Greek armies dominant across the ancient world), and they gave Psamtik a military edge that his domestic rivals and the weakening Assyrian garrison could not match.
By around 656 BCE, Psamtik had consolidated control over the entire Delta, suppressed or co-opted the remaining Libyan dynasts, and extended his authority south to Thebes.

The mechanism at Thebes was characteristically elegant: he had his daughter Nitocris (not the legendary Sixth Dynasty queen of the same name, Old Kingdom Egypt Ch 6) adopted as the God's Wife of Amun, the wealthy and powerful priestly position at Karnak that had been a significant power base since the New Kingdom (New Kingdom Egypt Ch 3).
By installing his daughter in the position, Psamtik gained control of Upper Egypt's religious establishment without needing to conquer it militarily.
The God's Wife held enormous landholdings and temple revenues.
With Nitocris at Karnak, Psamtik controlled the south.
Egypt was unified again, for the first time in over four hundred years.
Psamtik I founded the Twenty-sixth Dynasty, based at Sais, and inaugurated what historians call the Late Period of ancient Egypt (664 to 332 BCE).
His dynasty is known as the Saite Dynasty (after Sais), and the cultural revival it produced is called the Saite Renaissance.
The Saite Renaissance was not a timid restoration.
It was a deliberate, systematic effort to reach back across the centuries and reconnect with the glories of Egypt's Old Kingdom, more than two thousand years in the past.
Saite artists studied and copied Old Kingdom reliefs with painstaking precision.

Sculptors produced portraits in Old Kingdom style, with the smooth idealized faces and formal poses of the pyramid age, while also achieving a technical polish that the Old Kingdom originals had never quite matched.
Saite officials adopted Old Kingdom titles that had been obsolete for centuries.
The revival was part nostalgia, part nationalism, and part political strategy.

By anchoring themselves in the most ancient and prestigious phase of Egyptian civilization, the Saite pharaohs were saying: we are the real Egypt, the Egypt that came before the Libyans, before the Nubians, before the Assyrians.
We are going back to the source.
This kind of deliberate archaism is rare in the ancient world, and it deserves a cross-civilizational comparison.

Around the same time (roughly the seventh century BCE), the Zhou Dynasty in China was undergoing its own crisis of political fragmentation during the Spring and Autumn period, and Confucius (born 551 BCE) would soon propose his own solution: return to the idealized ritual order of the early Zhou founders (Zhou Dynasty Ch 5-6).
In both cases, a civilization in crisis looked backward for legitimacy.
The difference is that the Saite pharaohs had actual physical monuments to copy, two-thousand-year-old temples whose walls preserved the art and inscriptions of the Old Kingdom.
Confucius worked largely from memory and tradition.
The Egyptians worked from stone.
Psamtik I reigned for fifty-four years, one of the longest reigns in Egyptian history.
By the time he died in 610 BCE, Egypt was stable, prosperous, culturally vibrant, and militarily competent.
The Greek mercenaries had been settled in permanent garrisons in the Delta.
Trade was expanding.

The country that had been a collection of feuding Libyan warlords two generations earlier was, once again, a unified state with a functioning bureaucracy and an army capable of projecting power beyond its borders.
Whether it could hold that position was about to be tested, because the entire Near East was about to be reshuffled.
The Assyrian Empire, the superpower that had dominated the region for two centuries, was collapsing.
And Psamtik I's son was going to try to pick up the pieces.

chapters.title: Egypt Looks Outward
sentences: Necho II (pronounced "NEH-koh," reigned 610 to 595 BCE) inherited a reunified Egypt and immediately did something his predecessors had not attempted in centuries: he tried to build an empire.
The context was irresistible.
The Assyrian Empire, which had terrorized the Near East for two hundred years, was dying.
A coalition of Babylonians (led by the Chaldean dynasty of Nabopolassar) and Medes (an Iranian people from the Zagros highlands) was tearing it apart.
The Assyrian capital of Nineveh fell in 612 BCE, just two years after Psamtik I's death.
The last Assyrian king, Ashur-uballit II, was holding out in the city of Harran (pronounced "hah-RAHN") in northern Syria with a remnant army, begging for help from anyone who would listen.
Necho II decided to help.
This surprises people.
Why would Egypt rescue the Assyrians, who had sacked Thebes and conquered the country just fifty years earlier?
The answer is pure geopolitics: Egypt preferred a weakened but surviving Assyria as a buffer in northern Syria to a triumphant Babylon that would control the entire Fertile Crescent.
A dead Assyria meant a Babylonian superpower on Egypt's doorstep.
A crippled Assyria meant a balance of power that Egypt could exploit.
It was the same strategic logic that would drive European balance-of-power politics two thousand years later.
In 609 BCE, Necho II marched his army north through the Levant to link up with the Assyrian remnant at Harran.
Standing in his way was Josiah (pronounced "joe-SIGH-ah"), king of Judah, who had allied with the Babylonians.

The two armies met at Megiddo (pronounced "meh-GID-oh"), the ancient fortress city in the Jezreel Valley that had been the site of Thutmose III's famous battle eight centuries earlier (New Kingdom Egypt Ch 5).
The Battle of Megiddo (609 BCE) was brief and decisive.
Necho II defeated the Judean army, and Josiah was killed, either in battle or in the immediate aftermath.
The death of Josiah was a catastrophe for Judah.
He had been one of its most capable kings, and his death ended Judah's brief period of independence and religious reform.
The Hebrew Bible records the event with evident grief: "His servants carried him dead in a chariot from Megiddo" (2 Kings 23:30).
Necho II replaced Josiah's chosen successor with a puppet king, Jehoiakim, and imposed tribute on Judah.
For a brief moment, Egypt controlled the Levant again.
The moment didn't last.
Necho II pressed on to Harran, but he was too late.
The Assyrian remnant had already been crushed.
The Assyrian Empire was gone.
And the Babylonians, led by Nabopolassar's brilliant son Nebuchadnezzar II, were turning their attention south.
In 605 BCE, Nebuchadnezzar's army met the Egyptian forces at Carchemish (pronounced "KAR-keh-mish"), a strategic city on the Euphrates River in northern Syria.
The Battle of Carchemish was a decisive Babylonian victory.
The Egyptian army was routed and pursued south as far as the city of Hamath.
Necho II lost control of the Levant in a single afternoon.
Egypt would never again hold significant territory east of the Sinai.
The battle also had consequences far beyond Egypt.
With the Egyptians driven out, Nebuchadnezzar turned on the Levantine kingdoms that had been Egyptian vassals.
Judah, caught between the two superpowers, would be besieged, conquered, and its population deported to Babylon in 586 BCE (the Babylonian Exile), one of the defining events in Jewish history.
The chain of causation runs directly from Carchemish to the destruction of Solomon's Temple.
Necho II's failed bid for empire didn't just cost Egypt its Levantine ambitions.
It rearranged the entire Near East.
But Necho II was more than a failed conqueror.
He was also one of the most forward-looking rulers in Egyptian history, and his two great infrastructure projects, had they succeeded, might have changed the ancient world.
The first was the Canal of the Pharaohs, an attempt to link the Nile to the Red Sea.
The canal did not follow the route of the modern Suez Canal (which cuts directly across the isthmus from the Mediterranean to the Red Sea).

Instead, Necho's canal ran from a branch of the Nile near the city of Bubastis in the eastern Delta, eastward through the Wadi Tumilat (a dry valley connecting the Delta to the Bitter Lakes region), and then south to the Red Sea.
The total length was roughly 100 miles (160 km).
According to Herodotus (the Greek historian who visited Egypt around 450 BCE and wrote extensively about what he saw and heard), 120,000 workers died during the construction.
That number is almost certainly exaggerated, but the project was enormous by any standard.
Necho II reportedly abandoned the canal before completion after receiving an oracle warning that he was "working for the barbarian" (meaning foreigners would benefit more than Egypt).
Whether the oracle was real or a face-saving explanation for running out of money or hitting engineering problems, the canal was left unfinished.
Darius I of Persia would later complete it (Chapter 4).
The finished canal was wide enough for two triremes (warships with three banks of oars) to pass each other, according to ancient sources.
It was a forerunner of the Suez Canal, built 2,400 years before Ferdinand de Lesseps.
The second project was even more extraordinary.
According to Herodotus, Necho II commissioned Phoenician sailors to circumnavigate Africa.

The expedition departed from a Red Sea port, sailed south along the east coast of Africa, rounded the southern tip, sailed north along the west coast, and returned through the Strait of Gibraltar to Egypt.
The voyage took three years.
Herodotus reports that the sailors claimed the sun was on their right-hand side (that is, to the north) as they sailed westward around the southern tip of the continent.

Herodotus himself found this detail unbelievable, but it is actually the strongest evidence that the voyage really happened: anyone sailing west around the Cape of Good Hope, south of the Tropic of Capricorn, would indeed see the sun to the north.
The detail is too geographically correct to be a lucky guess by ancient storytellers.

If the circumnavigation occurred, and most modern scholars consider it plausible if not proven, it was the longest sea voyage in the ancient world, over two thousand years before Vasco da Gama rounded the Cape in 1498.
The Phoenician sailors who made it were not explorers in the modern sense.
They were professional mariners from the coastal cities of the Levant (Phoenicia Ch 4), and they stopped each autumn to plant and harvest grain before continuing.
The voyage produced no lasting geographic knowledge: no maps, no follow-up expeditions, no trade routes.
It was a stunt, perhaps, or a proof of concept.
But it demonstrated that the Saite Dynasty was thinking on a global scale.
Necho II's successor, Psamtik II (reigned 595 to 589 BCE), shifted Egyptian attention south instead of east.
He launched a major military campaign into Nubia around 593 BCE, marching deep into the territory of the Kingdom of Kush, which had retreated south after losing control of Egypt to the Saite Dynasty.
The campaign reached at least the Third Cataract and possibly further.
Its military results are unclear (the Kushite kingdom survived and continued to flourish from its capital at Napata), but its cultural results were dramatic.
Psamtik II systematically erased the names of the Twenty-fifth Dynasty Kushite pharaohs from Egyptian monuments.
This was not random vandalism.
It was a deliberate campaign to remove the Kushite kings from Egyptian history, to deny that they had ever legitimately ruled Egypt.
The Saite Dynasty's claim to legitimacy rested on being the authentic native Egyptian restoration.
The Kushites, despite their devotion to Egyptian gods and their investment in Egyptian temples, were now officially foreigners whose names did not belong on Egyptian stone.
The campaign left one unexpected artifact.

Greek and Carian mercenaries serving in Psamtik II's army, posted far from home in the Nubian desert, carved graffiti on the legs of the colossal statues at Abu Simbel, the great temple of Ramesses II (New Kingdom Egypt Ch 8).

The Abu Simbel graffiti include inscriptions in Greek and Carian (an Anatolian language written in its own script), naming individual soldiers and noting that they had come this far with the pharaoh's army.
These are among the earliest surviving Greek inscriptions anywhere, scratched by bored soldiers on a monument that was already eight hundred years old.
The graffiti are evidence of how deeply integrated Greek mercenaries had become in the Egyptian military establishment.
They also prove that ancient tourists were just as inclined to carve their names on famous monuments as modern ones.

Psamtik II's successor, Apries (pronounced "AH-pree-eez," reigned 589 to 570 BCE, also known by his Egyptian name Wahibre), continued the dynasty's interventionist foreign policy and paid dearly for it.
Apries tried to intervene in the siege of Jerusalem by Nebuchadnezzar in 588 BCE, sending an army into the Levant that forced the Babylonians to temporarily lift their siege.
The prophet Jeremiah had warned the Judeans not to trust Egyptian promises: "Pharaoh's army, which has come to help you, will go back to its own land, to Egypt" (Jeremiah 37:7).
Jeremiah was right.
The relief was short-lived.
The Egyptian army withdrew, the Babylonian siege resumed, and Jerusalem fell in 586 BCE.
Nebuchadnezzar destroyed the Temple of Solomon, deported the Judean elite to Babylon, and ended the Kingdom of Judah as an independent state.
A wave of Jewish refugees fled south to Egypt, settling in the Delta and establishing the communities that would produce the Elephantine colony a century later (Chapter 4).

Egypt's intervention had accomplished nothing militarily except to prolong the agony of a doomed ally, but it established a pattern that would recur throughout the Late Period: Egypt as the hopeful fallback option for smaller states facing larger empires, and Egypt consistently failing to deliver.
Worse followed.
Apries sent a military expedition to support the Greek colony of Cyrene (pronounced "sigh-REE-nee"), on the coast of modern Libya, against attacks from local Libyan tribes.
The expedition was a disaster.
The Egyptian forces were badly defeated, and the surviving soldiers blamed Apries for sending them into a meat grinder.
The defeat triggered a military mutiny.
The mutineers chose their own leader: a general named Amasis (pronounced "ah-MAH-sis," Egyptian name Ahmose II), a man of non-royal origins who had risen through the ranks on merit.
Apries sent an envoy to talk the rebels down.

According to Herodotus, the envoy addressed the rebel soldiers, and Amasis stood up behind him, placed a helmet on the envoy's head, and announced that he was crowning the man as the new pharaoh's messenger.
It was a gesture that said, unmistakably: I'm in charge here.
The army acclaimed Amasis as pharaoh.
Apries marched against him with his own forces (including his Greek mercenary bodyguard), was defeated, captured, and eventually killed.
The transition was violent, but Amasis would prove to be one of the most capable rulers Egypt ever had.

chapters.title: The Last Great Pharaohs
sentences: Amasis II (reigned 570 to 526 BCE) was, by any measure, an unlikely pharaoh.
He was not of royal blood.
He was a military officer who had risen through the ranks, declared pharaoh by mutinous soldiers.
His predecessor was killed in the civil war.

Everything about his accession screamed "usurper." Amasis understood this perfectly, and his response was one of the most sophisticated exercises in political legitimacy-building in the ancient world.
He married a princess from the old Saite royal family to anchor his claim in dynastic continuity.
He lavished gifts on the temples, winning over the priestly establishment.
And he cultivated a public image that was, by pharaonic standards, strikingly down-to-earth.

Herodotus (the Greek historian who visited Egypt around 450 BCE and remains our most detailed literary source for this period, though his accounts should be treated with the same skepticism one applies to any ancient writer blending observation with hearsay) devoted extensive space to Amasis.
Herodotus portrays him as shrewd, pragmatic, and not above a well-timed joke.
According to Herodotus, when advisors criticized Amasis for spending his leisure time drinking and socializing with commoners, the pharaoh responded: "A bow that is always strung will break.

A man who is always serious will go mad." Whether the anecdote is literally true or a Greek projection of their own values onto a foreign ruler, it captures something real about Amasis's public persona: he was accessible in a way that pharaohs traditionally were not.
Amasis's greatest achievement was his management of Egypt's relationship with the Greek world.
By the mid-sixth century BCE, the Greeks were the rising power of the Mediterranean.
Greek city-states dotted the coasts from Sicily to Asia Minor.
Greek merchants traded everywhere.
Greek mercenaries served in armies from Persia to Egypt.
The question for any Egyptian ruler was: how do you benefit from Greek energy and Greek money without being overwhelmed by Greek culture?
Amasis's answer was Naucratis (pronounced "NOW-krah-tis"), a Greek trading colony in the western Delta that had existed since Psamtik I's reign, around 630 BCE.
Amasis reorganized Naucratis into a formally designated trading emporium, the only place in Egypt where Greek merchants were legally permitted to trade.
This was deliberate containment.
By concentrating all Greek commercial activity in one location, Amasis could tax it, regulate it, and prevent Greek traders from spreading uncontrolled across the country.

Naucratis became one of the busiest trading ports in the eastern Mediterranean, a hub where Greek olive oil, wine, silver, and pottery were exchanged for Egyptian grain, linen, papyrus, and luxury goods.
The arrangement worked brilliantly for both sides.
Greek merchants got access to Egyptian markets.
Egypt got revenue and imported goods without cultural contamination.
For cross-civilizational context: this managed-trade model was unusual in the ancient world.
Most ancient empires either excluded foreign traders entirely or allowed them to operate freely.
Amasis's solution, designating a specific port city as the sole point of controlled interchange, anticipated the European "treaty port" model by over two thousand years.
The Portuguese would later operate on similar principles at Macau in China.
The comparison is not perfect, but the strategic logic is identical.
Amasis also cultivated personal relationships with Greek leaders and institutions.
He sent gifts to the Greek sanctuaries at Delphi, Cyrene, and the temple of Hera on the island of Samos.
He formed a personal friendship (or at least a diplomatic alliance) with Polycrates (pronounced "po-LIK-rah-teez"), the tyrant of Samos, one of the most powerful Greek rulers of the era.
He married a Greek woman from Cyrene, Ladice (pronounced "LAH-dih-say"), as one of his wives.
These were not casual gestures.
They were a deliberate strategy to embed Egypt in the Greek world's diplomatic and commercial networks, creating allies who would have an interest in Egypt's survival.
The Saite Renaissance reached its artistic peak under Amasis.
Saite sculpture of this period achieves a quality that scholars rank among the finest in three thousand years of Egyptian art.
The style is paradoxical: deliberately archaizing (copying Old Kingdom models with faithful precision) yet technically superior to the originals.
Saite sculptors had mastered hard stone carving to a degree that the Old Kingdom artisans had never quite achieved.

They polished basalt and greywacke (a hard dark stone favored for elite statuary) to a mirror finish, creating portrait heads with smooth, idealized features and a quality of serene abstraction that influenced Greek archaic sculpture.
This is not a minor point.
Egyptian art influenced Greek art in the seventh and sixth centuries BCE, and the channel of transmission was the Saite period.

Greek sculptors who saw Egyptian statuary, whether at Naucratis, at Greek sanctuaries where Egyptian objects were dedicated, or in Egypt itself, absorbed Egyptian conventions of standing poses, proportional systems, and the concept of the monumental stone figure.

The early Greek kouros (a standing male nude statue, the signature form of Greek archaic sculpture) bears a striking resemblance to Egyptian standing figures in pose, proportions, and the characteristic "left foot forward" stance.

Scholars debate the exact degree of Egyptian influence, but the chronological and geographic connections are clear: Greek stone sculpture emerged in the seventh century BCE, precisely when Greek contact with Saite Egypt was at its most intense.
The Greeks would eventually develop their own artistic tradition in very different directions, but the initial impulse came, at least in part, from the Nile.
The demotic script (from Greek "demotikos," meaning "popular") also reached its mature form during this period.
Demotic was a rapid cursive script developed from the older hieratic writing system (which was itself a cursive version of hieroglyphs).
It replaced hieratic as the everyday writing script of Egypt by the seventh century BCE, used for legal documents, commercial transactions, literary texts, and personal letters.
Hieroglyphs continued to be used for monumental inscriptions on temple walls.
So Late Period Egypt had three writing systems in simultaneous use: hieroglyphs (sacred and monumental), hieratic (priestly and religious), and demotic (everything else).
This coexistence would continue into the Ptolemaic period, when Greek was added as a fourth script, and the Rosetta Stone would preserve a single decree in three of them (Chapter 6).
Egyptian intellectual life in this period was deeper than the Saite artistic revival might suggest.

The temples functioned as centers of learning, maintaining archives of medical, astronomical, mathematical, and religious texts in institutions called the House of Life (the nearest ancient equivalent to a university library).

Greek intellectuals of the sixth and fifth centuries BCE traveled to Egypt to study, and while the later Greek accounts of what they learned there are often embellished, the pattern of intellectual pilgrimage was real.

Pythagoras (the Greek mathematician and philosopher, born around 570 BCE on the island of Samos) reportedly spent years studying with Egyptian priests before founding his philosophical school in southern Italy.

The ancient tradition, reported by multiple Greek writers, says he learned geometry, astronomy, and the doctrine of the transmigration of souls (the idea that the soul passes through multiple bodies after death) from Egyptian teachers.
How much of this tradition is historical and how much is later Greek mythologizing of Egypt as a font of ancient wisdom is impossible to determine with certainty.

But the geometry claim is plausible: the Egyptians had been doing practical geometry for over two thousand years (the Rhind Mathematical Papyrus, New Kingdom Egypt Ch 1, demonstrates sophisticated mathematical techniques), and Greek visitors would have encountered this tradition firsthand.
Egyptian medicine was similarly influential.
Greek physicians traveled to Egypt to study its medical traditions, which were among the most advanced in the ancient world.

Egyptian doctors had been performing surgery, setting fractures, and prescribing pharmaceutical remedies since the Old Kingdom (the Edwin Smith Papyrus, New Kingdom Egypt Ch 1, is a systematic surgical treatise).
The Greeks adopted Egyptian techniques in pharmacology and surgery, and the later Greek medical tradition openly acknowledged its debt to Egyptian knowledge.

When Herodotus wrote that Egypt was full of doctors, "each for a different disease" (specialists in eyes, teeth, the head, internal organs), he was describing a medical culture more specialized than anything the Greek world had yet produced.

For a cross-civilizational snapshot of the mid-sixth century BCE: while Amasis ruled Egypt and traded with Greeks, Cyrus the Great was building the Persian Empire from scratch, conquering Lydia in 546 BCE and Babylon in 539 BCE (Persian Empire Ch 1-2).
In China, the Spring and Autumn period was producing the political fragmentation and intellectual ferment that would generate Confucius, born in 551 BCE (Zhou Dynasty Ch 5).
In India, the Vedic period was giving way to the age of the Upanishads, and the Buddha would be born around 563 BCE (Vedic period Ch 7).
In Greece, the philosophical tradition was flowering at Miletus, where Thales, Anaximander, and Anaximenes were asking what the world was made of.
The sixth century BCE was one of those rare periods when civilizations across the entire Old World were simultaneously producing transformative ideas.
Egypt under Amasis was fully part of that moment, connected by trade, mercenary service, and intellectual exchange to the Greek world that was just discovering philosophy.
Amasis died in 526 BCE, after forty-four years on the throne.
His timing was, from his perspective, perfect: he died six months before the catastrophe.
The throne passed to his son, Psamtik III, who had been pharaoh for barely half a year when the sky fell.

The Achaemenid Persian Empire (Elamite Civilization Ch 8, Persian Empire Ch 1-2), the largest empire the world had ever seen, stretching from the Aegean Sea to the Indus River, from Central Asia to the deserts of Libya, was coming for Egypt.
Its founder Cyrus the Great had planned the invasion but died before carrying it out.
The task fell to his son, Cambyses II (pronounced "kam-BYE-seez"), who assembled the full might of the Persian military machine and marched on Egypt in 525 BCE.
Amasis had seen this coming.
He had spent years building alliances with the Greeks specifically to counter the Persian threat.
He had allied with Polycrates of Samos, with the kingdom of Lydia in western Anatolia, and with the Babylonian king Nabonidus.
But Cyrus had already conquered all of them.
Lydia fell in 546 BCE.
Babylon fell in 539 BCE.
Polycrates switched sides.
By the time Cambyses marched on Egypt, Amasis's alliance network was in ruins.
Egypt would fight alone.

chapters.title: Under the Persian Yoke
sentences: The Battle of Pelusium (pronounced "peh-LOO-see-um") in 525 BCE was the end of Saite Egypt, and it came quickly.
Pelusium was the fortress city guarding Egypt's northeastern frontier, the gateway through which every invader from the east had to pass.

Cambyses II assembled his army in Palestine, secured the cooperation of Arab tribes to supply water across the Sinai Desert (a logistical detail that Egyptian planners had traditionally relied on the desert itself to prevent), and struck.

One of the more colorful details of the battle comes from the Roman-era historian Polyaenus, who wrote that Cambyses ordered his soldiers to carry cats and other animals sacred to the Egyptians on their shields, knowing the Egyptians would not shoot arrows for fear of hitting them.

The story is almost certainly a later embellishment (no contemporary source mentions it), but it captures a real truth about the collision between Persian pragmatism and Egyptian piety that would define the next century of their relationship.
The Egyptian army under Psamtik III was defeated decisively.
The young pharaoh retreated to Memphis, which fell after a brief siege.
Psamtik III was captured.
According to Herodotus, Cambyses initially treated him with some respect, then had him executed after discovering a plot to rebel.
Egypt became a satrapy (province) of the Persian Empire, governed by a satrap (provincial governor) answerable to the Great King at Susa.

For cross-civilizational context: Cambyses's conquest of Egypt in 525 BCE came just four years after the Persian conquest of Babylon in 539 BCE and roughly twenty years after Cyrus's conquest of Lydia.
In the span of a single generation, the Persians had swallowed every major civilization in the Near East.
The only great power that remained independent was the collection of Greek city-states on the western fringe of the known world, and the Persians would come for them too.
The Battle of Marathon was thirty-five years away.
Thermopylae was forty-five.
The world was being reorganized on a scale not seen since the Bronze Age Collapse.
Cambyses II's reputation in Egypt is terrible, and the question is how much of that reputation is deserved.
The Egyptian sources, as transmitted through Herodotus and later writers, paint Cambyses as a madman.

He allegedly killed the sacred Apis bull (a living bull worshipped as an incarnation of the god Ptah at Memphis, the most important single animal in Egyptian religion), stabbing it with his dagger in the temple.
He supposedly opened royal tombs and desecrated mummies.
He reportedly mocked Egyptian religion, burned temples, and committed various acts of sacrilege.
But the contemporary Egyptian evidence tells a different story, or at least a more complicated one.

The Serapeum at Saqqara (the vast underground burial complex where mummified Apis bulls were interred in massive granite sarcophagi) has Apis burials from Cambyses's reign with proper ritual inscriptions.

An inscription left by Udjahorresnet (pronounced "oo-JAH-hor-RES-net"), an Egyptian priest and physician who served both Cambyses and his successor Darius I, describes Cambyses as performing the proper rites at the temple of Neith at Sais and ordering the expulsion of squatters from the temple precinct.

Udjahorresnet's account is not flattering (it's the self-justifying autobiography of a collaborator, carved on a naophorous statue, one of those portrait statues where the subject holds a small shrine before him), but it does not describe a raving barbarian.
The most likely explanation is that Cambyses's "madness" is at least partly Egyptian propaganda.
The Egyptians had a long tradition of demonizing foreign rulers after the fact (the Hyksos got the same treatment, New Kingdom Egypt Ch 2).
Cambyses may have made genuine errors of cultural sensitivity, and some Persian policies may have been perceived as sacrilege.
But the portrait of Cambyses as a temple-burning lunatic was probably amplified and exaggerated by later Egyptian writers who had every reason to paint Persian rule in the worst possible light.
One story about Cambyses that even skeptical historians find compelling is the Lost Army of Cambyses.

According to Herodotus, Cambyses sent a force of fifty thousand soldiers across the Western Desert to attack the Oracle of Amun at Siwa Oasis (an oracle that would play a crucial role in Alexander's conquest two centuries later, Chapter 6).
The army marched into the desert and was never seen again, allegedly swallowed by a sandstorm.
No confirmed archaeological trace of this army has ever been found, despite periodic claims of discovery.
The story may be entirely legendary, or it may preserve a garbled memory of a military disaster in the Sahara.
It remains one of antiquity's great unsolved mysteries.
Cambyses died in 522 BCE under mysterious circumstances (possibly suicide, possibly murder, possibly an accidental sword wound, the sources disagree).
After a brief succession crisis in Persia, Darius I (pronounced "dah-RYE-us," Persian Empire Ch 3) took the throne and proved to be a far more effective administrator of Egypt than Cambyses had been.
Darius I visited Egypt in person around 518 BCE.

He completed the Canal of the Pharaohs that Necho II had begun a century earlier (Chapter 2), linking the Nile to the Red Sea and opening a water route for trade between the Mediterranean and the Indian Ocean.
He erected commemorative stelae along the canal in four languages: Old Persian, Elamite, Babylonian, and Egyptian.
The canal was the single most important infrastructure project in Late Period Egypt, and Darius finished it.
Darius also built the Temple of Hibis (pronounced "HEE-bis") at Kharga Oasis (pronounced "KHAR-gah"), deep in the Western Desert about 120 miles (200 km) west of the Nile.

The Temple of Hibis is the best-preserved Persian-period temple in Egypt, dedicated to Amun and decorated in fully Egyptian style, with the Persian king depicted performing the same rituals that Egyptian pharaohs had performed for millennia.
The temple is evidence that Darius understood what Cambyses apparently did not: if you want to govern Egypt, you have to perform Egypt.
You have to build the temples, honor the gods, adopt the iconography, and present yourself as a pharaoh rather than a foreign conqueror.
Darius did this.
He used the pharaonic titulary.
He adopted the cartouche.
His name appears in hieroglyphs alongside the traditional epithets of Egyptian kingship.

Darius also commissioned a codification of Egyptian law, ordering the Egyptian legal code to be compiled and written down in both demotic and Aramaic (the administrative language of the Persian Empire).
This was not an act of cultural respect alone.
It was practical governance: to rule Egypt effectively, you needed to understand and work within Egyptian legal traditions rather than imposing Persian law wholesale.

Under Darius I, Egypt was the wealthiest satrapy of the Persian Empire, assessed at 700 talents of silver annually in tribute (by Herodotus's account), plus the revenues from the Nile fisheries and the grain of the Delta.
Its grain, its gold, its papyrus, its linen, its skilled craftsmen, and its strategic position connecting Africa to Asia made it indispensable.
Persian administrators were installed alongside Egyptian officials.
Aramaic became the language of government correspondence, layered on top of the existing demotic bureaucracy.
Persian garrisons were stationed at key points: Memphis, the Delta frontier, the oases of the Western Desert.
But the day-to-day life of most Egyptians, the farmers and fishermen and craftsmen of the Nile Valley, probably changed less than the political narrative suggests.
They paid their taxes to a Persian satrap instead of an Egyptian pharaoh.
The temples still functioned.
The festivals still occurred.
The Nile still flooded.
But being indispensable is not the same as being content.
The Egyptians paid their taxes, maintained their temples, and waited.
The waiting ended spectacularly in 460 BCE.
A Libyan prince named Inaros (pronounced "ih-NAR-ohs"), son of Psamtik (probably not related to the Saite royal family despite the name), raised a rebellion against Persian rule in the western Delta.
The timing was calculated: Persia had just been humiliated by the Greeks at Salamis (480 BCE), Plataea (479 BCE), and the ongoing Greco-Persian Wars were stretching Persian military resources.
Inaros sensed weakness.
The Inaros Revolt (460 to 454 BCE) was the most serious challenge to Persian rule in Egypt.
Inaros defeated and killed a Persian satrap in battle, seized control of the Delta, and then did something audacious: he called in the Athenians.
Athens, at the height of its maritime empire, was already at war with Persia.

When Inaros sent envoys requesting military support, Athens dispatched a fleet of two hundred triremes (some sources say fewer), which sailed into the Nile, linked up with Inaros's forces, and helped capture most of Memphis.
For a few years, it looked like the revolt might succeed.
An Egyptian-Athenian coalition controlling the Delta and most of the Nile was a genuine threat to Persian control.
But Persia was a continental empire with resources that no rebel coalition could match.
The Great King sent a massive army under the general Megabyzus, who besieged the Egyptian-Athenian forces in the Delta, cut off their supply lines, and destroyed the Athenian fleet.
Inaros was captured and eventually executed.

The Athenian expeditionary force was virtually annihilated, a disaster that haunted Athenian politics for decades and contributed to the strategic caution that characterized Athenian foreign policy in the 440s and 430s BCE.
The Inaros Revolt failed, but it demonstrated something important: even after seventy years of Persian rule, Egyptian resistance was not dead.
The desire for independence was a permanent undercurrent, waiting for the next opportunity.
The next decades of Persian rule were, from the Egyptian perspective, a long exercise in endurance.
The temples continued to function.
The priests maintained their rituals.
The demotic scribal tradition produced literary and legal texts.
Life went on under the satrap's authority.
Two remarkable sources illuminate what life was like in Persian-period Egypt.
The first is the collection of Elephantine Papyri, discovered on the island of Elephantine at the First Cataract of the Nile (the traditional southern border of Egypt proper).
These Aramaic-language papyri, dating mostly to the fifth century BCE, document a Jewish military colony stationed at Elephantine under Persian authority.
The colony maintained its own temple to Yahweh (the God of Israel), which they had built with Persian permission.
The papyri include personal letters, legal contracts, marriage documents, and official correspondence.

One letter requests permission from the Persian governor of Judah to rebuild the Elephantine temple after it was destroyed in a local conflict with the priests of the Egyptian ram-god Khnum, whose temple was next door.

The Elephantine Papyri reveal a cosmopolitan society under Persian rule: Jewish soldiers serving a Persian king in an Egyptian city, worshipping their god in a temple alongside Egyptian temples, writing in Aramaic while their Egyptian neighbors wrote in demotic.
It is a snapshot of the multilayered, multicultural reality of the Achaemenid Empire.
The second source is Herodotus himself.
The Greek historian visited Egypt around 450 BCE, during Persian rule, and devoted the entire second book of his Histories to Egyptian customs, religion, geography, and history.

Herodotus was credulous about some things (he believed that Egyptian priests could trace their records back 11,340 years) and sharp-eyed about others (his descriptions of mummification, Nile flooding, and Egyptian daily life have been largely confirmed by archaeology).

His account is invaluable as the most detailed outsider's description of Egypt before the modern era, though it should be read as a Greek writer's interpretation of a culture he admired but did not fully understand.
Herodotus recorded something that captures the Egyptian calendar's influence on the wider world.

Egypt's 365-day civil calendar, with twelve months of thirty days each plus five additional days (called epagomenal days, added at the year's end and considered unlucky), was the most practical calendar system in the ancient world.
Its elegance and predictability influenced Greek timekeeping and would later feed into Julius Caesar's calendar reform in 46 BCE, which gave the Western world the 365-day year with a leap day.
The calendar we use today is, at its root, an Egyptian invention.

chapters.title: The Last Independence
sentences: In 404 BCE, as the Persian Empire reeled from the chaos following the death of Darius II and the civil war between his sons Artaxerxes II and Cyrus the Younger (Persian Empire Ch 5), a man named Amyrtaeus (pronounced "ah-meer-TAY-us") of Sais expelled the Persian garrison and declared himself pharaoh.
He is the sole king of the Twenty-eighth Dynasty, and his reign (404 to 399 BCE) marks the beginning of the last free period in Egypt's pharaonic history.
Almost nothing is known about Amyrtaeus personally.
He may have been a descendant of the rebel leaders from the Inaros Revolt two generations earlier (Chapter 4).
He left no significant monuments.
No contemporary texts describe his methods or his campaigns.
What we know is the result: the Persians were gone, and Egypt was independent again, for the first time in over 120 years.
The independence was fragile.

Amyrtaeus was overthrown (the circumstances are obscure, but later sources suggest he was convicted of some legal violation, possibly breaking a law he himself had enacted) by a man named Nepherites I (pronounced "neh-FER-ih-teez"), who founded the Twenty-ninth Dynasty at the Delta city of Mendes (pronounced "MEN-deez").
The Twenty-ninth Dynasty (399 to 380 BCE) lasted only twenty years and produced four pharaohs, none of whom left a deep mark on history individually.
Collectively, however, they accomplished something crucial: they kept Egypt free.
They did this primarily through diplomacy, allying with Greek city-states against the constant threat of Persian reconquest.
Egypt supplied grain to Athens and other Greek cities, traded military support for diplomatic backing, and positioned itself as the natural ally of any power opposed to Persian expansion.
The grain trade between the Nile Delta and the Aegean was not new (Egypt had been exporting grain for centuries), but it now carried explicit political freight.
Egyptian grain bought Greek friendship, and Greek friendship bought time.
The Greek intellectual pilgrimage to Egypt continued during this period.

Plato (the Athenian philosopher, born around 428 BCE, whose dialogues would become the foundation of Western philosophy) reportedly visited Egypt around 390 BCE and was deeply impressed by what he found.
Later Greek writers claim he studied with Egyptian priests, learning about mathematics, astronomy, and the structure of the ideal state.

How much Plato actually borrowed from Egyptian thought is fiercely debated by scholars, but his works contain unmistakable Egyptian echoes: the myth of Atlantis (which Plato attributes to an Egyptian priest telling Solon about a lost civilization), the idea that the soul is immortal and passes through multiple existences, and the concept of a stable, hierarchical society governed by a trained elite.
Whether these parallels reflect direct borrowing or parallel development, Plato's own texts credit Egypt as a source of ancient wisdom, and that crediting was itself influential.
For the next two thousand years, Western thinkers would look to Egypt as a font of primordial knowledge, an image that owed as much to Plato's endorsement as to Egyptian reality.
The Persian Empire did not forget Egypt.
In 373 BCE, the Great King Artaxerxes II sent a massive invasion force to retake the country.

The army, commanded by the Persian general Pharnabazus and accompanied by the Athenian mercenary commander Iphicrates, reached the Delta and initially made progress, capturing the frontier fortress of Pelusium's outpost.
But the invasion stalled.
The Egyptian pharaoh Nectanebo I (pronounced "nek-TAN-eh-boh"), founder of the Thirtieth Dynasty, organized a determined defense.
The Delta's geography helped: its maze of channels, marshes, and fortified towns made it difficult terrain for a large army relying on supply lines from Palestine.
The annual Nile flood arrived, turning the low-lying areas into impassable swamps.
Pharnabazus and Iphicrates quarreled over strategy (Iphicrates wanted to strike quickly before the Egyptians could consolidate; Pharnabazus wanted to wait for reinforcements).
By the time the Persians decided to move, the flooding had made advance impossible.
The invasion force withdrew.
Egypt had repelled the Persian Empire.
The victory was partly strategic, partly geographic, and partly lucky, but it was real.
Nectanebo I (reigned 380 to 362 BCE) used the breathing space to launch one of the most ambitious building programs since the New Kingdom.
He was Egypt's last great native temple builder, and he built prodigiously.

He constructed or expanded temples at Karnak, at Philae (the island temple of Isis at the First Cataract that would become one of the last functioning temples of the ancient Egyptian religion, continuing to operate until 550 CE, a millennium after Nectanebo I's death), at Hermopolis, at Behbeit el-Hagar, and at numerous other sites.
His building program was both pious and political: every new temple was a statement that Egypt under its own pharaohs could still build on the scale of the ancients.
The Temple of Isis at Philae deserves special attention.

Built on an island in the Nile just above the First Cataract, the temple complex grew over the following centuries (most of the visible structures date to the Ptolemaic period), but Nectanebo I laid the foundations.
Philae would outlast every other temple in Egypt, its priests performing the ancient rites for the goddess Isis long after the rest of Egypt had converted to Christianity.

The last known hieroglyphic inscription, the Graffito of Esmet-Akhom, was carved at Philae in 394 CE, and the temple was not officially closed until the reign of the Byzantine emperor Justinian around 550 CE.
A temple begun by the last native pharaohs would be the last place on Earth where the ancient Egyptian gods were worshipped.
The Thirtieth Dynasty also saw a flowering of religious architecture in a specific form: the mammisi (pronounced "mah-MEE-see," a Coptic-derived term meaning "birth house").
Mammisi were small temples attached to larger temple complexes, dedicated to the ritual celebration of the divine birth of a god-child (usually Horus, born of Isis and Osiris).
The reliefs inside depicted the theological narrative of divine conception and birth in elaborate detail, showing the pharaoh as the earthly embodiment of Horus, legitimized by divine parentage.

These divine birth narratives were not new (Hatshepsut had used them, New Kingdom Egypt Ch 4), but the Thirtieth Dynasty formalized them architecturally, creating the standardized mammisi form that the Ptolemaic dynasty would later adopt and elaborate.
The religious significance was deeper than propaganda.
The mammisi narratives established a theological framework in which every pharaoh, by virtue of the office, was the child of a god.
This framework would prove remarkably elastic: it could accommodate Libyan pharaohs, Nubian pharaohs, Persian administrators who adopted the pharaonic role, and eventually Greek-speaking Ptolemies.

The institution of divine kingship in Egypt had become so abstracted from any particular ethnicity or dynasty that it could survive almost any change of management, as long as the new management was willing to perform the rituals.
Nectanebo I died around 362 BCE.

His successor, Teos (pronounced "TEE-ohs," reigned 362 to 360 BCE), attempted to invade the Persian Empire preemptively, launching a campaign into Palestine with Athenian and Spartan mercenary support.
The campaign collapsed when Teos's own family, led by his nephew Nectanebo II, staged a coup back in Egypt.
Teos fled to the Persian court, of all places, and threw himself on the mercy of the enemy.
He spent the rest of his life in Persia.
Nectanebo II (reigned 360 to 343 BCE) was the last native-born pharaoh of Egypt.
That sentence carries an enormous weight.
After Nectanebo II, no person of Egyptian birth would rule Egypt as an independent sovereign again until the modern era, over two thousand years later.

The unbroken chain of native rule that stretched back through Narmer and the unification of the Two Lands around 3100 BCE (Early Dynastic Egypt Ch 3), through the pyramid builders, through the Thutmosids and the Ramessids, through the Kushite pharaohs and the Saite restoration, ended with Nectanebo II.
The institution of pharaoh would continue under foreigners, Persians and then Greeks and then Romans, but the person wearing the crown would never again be Egyptian.
Nectanebo II does not seem to have known he was the last of anything.
He built temples vigorously, continued his grandfather's program, and ruled competently for nearly two decades.
He repelled another Persian invasion attempt, this one in 351 BCE, using the same combination of Delta geography and determined defense that had worked for Nectanebo I.
He was also attentive to the religious traditions that the Saite Renaissance had revived.

A dream stela attributed to Nectanebo II, found at the Serapeum at Saqqara (the underground burial complex for the sacred Apis bulls), follows a literary tradition stretching back to the famous Dream Stela of Thutmose IV at the Great Sphinx (New Kingdom Egypt Ch 5).
In the stela, the pharaoh receives a divine dream instructing him to care for the sacred animals.

Dream stelae were a form of religious propaganda, demonstrating the pharaoh's direct relationship with the gods, but they also tell us that Late Period pharaohs were consciously performing the same ritual gestures their predecessors had performed for two thousand years.
The tradition was the legitimacy.
But legitimacy could not stop the Persian Empire forever.
In 343 BCE, Artaxerxes III (pronounced "ar-tah-ZERK-seez"), the most aggressive Persian king since Darius I, launched a third invasion of Egypt.
This time, there would be no lucky flood, no bickering generals, no reprieve.
Artaxerxes III's army was enormous.
It included Persian troops, Greek mercenaries (fighting on the Persian side this time, an irony not lost on contemporaries), and contingents from across the empire.
The Persian force included the Rhodian mercenary commander Mentor, who brought professional Greek tactical expertise to the invasion.
Nectanebo II's army, though it included Greek mercenaries of its own, was outmatched in numbers and coordination.

The Persians broke through the Delta defenses at multiple points simultaneously, and the Greek mercenaries in Egyptian service began to negotiate their own surrenders rather than fight to the death for a doomed cause.
Nectanebo II fled south, possibly to Upper Egypt, possibly to Nubia, possibly further still.
He disappears from the historical record.
His fate is unknown.

Later legends, particularly in the Greek Romance of Alexander, claimed that Nectanebo II fled to Macedonia and magically fathered Alexander the Great, a delicious fiction that made Egypt's last pharaoh the literal father of Egypt's next conqueror.
The story is fantasy, but it reflects the psychological need for continuity: even in legend, the chain of legitimate kingship could not simply break.
It had to be handed from one pharaoh to the next, even across civilizations.
The Thirtieth Dynasty was finished.
The Second Persian Period began.
Native Egyptian rule was over.

chapters.title: Second Persian Period and Alexander
sentences: The Second Persian Period (343 to 332 BCE) was short, brutal, and deeply resented.
It lasted only eleven years, but it burned itself into Egyptian memory so thoroughly that when Alexander the Great arrived, the Egyptians greeted him as a liberator.
Artaxerxes III's occupation of Egypt was harsher than the first Persian period had been.
The sources (admittedly biased toward the Egyptian perspective) describe temples being looted, sacred animals being killed, and Egyptian wealth being stripped and sent to Persia.
The Apis bull at Memphis was reportedly killed.
Temple revenues were confiscated.

Whether all of these stories are true or whether they represent the same pattern of anti-Persian propaganda that had colored accounts of Cambyses two centuries earlier, the perception was clear: the second Persian occupation was worse than the first.
The resentment had a specific edge.
Egyptians had tasted independence.
Sixty years of native rule under the Twenty-eighth through Thirtieth Dynasties had revived national pride, rebuilt temples, and demonstrated that Egypt could govern itself.
The Persian reconquest felt not like a return to normalcy but like a theft.
Something that had been recovered was being taken away again.
Resistance continued even under occupation.
Local rebellions flared in the Delta.
A pretender named Khababash (pronounced "kah-BAH-bahsh") briefly seized the throne around 338 BCE, claiming the pharaonic title and minting coins before being suppressed by the Persian garrison.

The rebellions never succeeded on their own, but they made Egypt an expensive and unreliable satrapy, a province that required constant military attention at a time when the Persian Empire could least afford it.
Every soldier stationed in Egypt was a soldier not available to face the Macedonian army that was about to cross the Hellespont.
Artaxerxes III died in 338 BCE, probably poisoned by his own courtier Bagoas (a eunuch court official whose intrigues destabilized the late Achaemenid dynasty).
His successor, Arses (pronounced "AR-seez"), was also murdered by Bagoas.
The throne eventually passed to Darius III, the last Achaemenid king, who inherited an empire that was already fracturing at the edges.

Meanwhile, in Macedonia (the northern Greek kingdom that most Greek city-states considered barely Greek), a young king named Alexander III had just consolidated control over Greece after his father Philip II's assassination in 336 BCE.
Alexander had an army, an ego, and a plan that would reshape the ancient world.
Alexander's conquest of Egypt in 332 BCE was one of the easiest military operations of his career.
He had already destroyed the Persian army at the Battle of Issus (333 BCE) and captured the Levantine coast, including the famous seven-month siege of Tyre.
When he marched into Egypt, the Persian satrap Mazaces had no army left to oppose him.
Mazaces surrendered without a fight.
Alexander the Great entered Egypt as a liberator, and the Egyptians received him as one.
The Persian garrison was gone.
The Persian taxes would stop.
The temples would be restored.
For a population that had spent eleven years under a resented occupation, Alexander was not a new conqueror but a deliverer.
Alexander understood the opportunity perfectly.

He went straight to Memphis, the ancient capital, and was crowned pharaoh in the traditional manner, with the priests performing the rites of divine kingship that had been performed for every legitimate Egyptian ruler for nearly three thousand years.
Alexander adopted the pharaonic titles.
He sacrificed to the Apis bull, publicly honoring the sacred animal that the Persians had allegedly desecrated.
Every gesture was calibrated to communicate: I am not a Persian.
I respect your gods.
I will be your pharaoh.
Then Alexander did something strange, even by his own standards.
He took a small party and marched across the Libyan Desert to the Oracle of Amun at Siwa (pronounced "SEE-wah"), an oasis roughly 350 miles (560 km) west of the Nile Delta, deep in the Sahara.
The Oracle of Siwa was one of the most prestigious oracles in the ancient world, rivaling Delphi in Greece.
It was dedicated to Amun (or, as the Greeks understood him, Zeus-Ammon, a merged deity combining the Egyptian supreme god with the Greek king of the gods).
The oracle had been consulted by Greek visitors for at least a century before Alexander's arrival.
The Spartans had reportedly sent envoys.
Pindar, the Greek lyric poet, had composed a hymn to Zeus-Ammon.
Siwa was where the Egyptian and Greek religious worlds overlapped.
The march to Siwa was dangerous.
Ancient sources describe the party nearly dying of thirst before miraculous rain saved them, and becoming lost in sandstorms before being guided by serpents (or, in another version, by crows).
How much of this is historical and how much is the mythology that quickly accumulated around Alexander's story is impossible to say.
What happened at the oracle is equally obscure.
Alexander entered the innermost sanctuary alone, speaking privately with the priest.
He later told his companions that the oracle had confirmed what he wanted to hear, but he never revealed the exact words.
Later traditions claim that the priest greeted him as "son of Zeus-Ammon," meaning the son of God, and that Alexander interpreted this as confirmation of his divine parentage.
The visit to Siwa was politically brilliant, whatever its spiritual significance.
By receiving the oracle's blessing, Alexander gained legitimacy in both Egyptian and Greek religious frameworks simultaneously.
To Egyptians, he was confirmed by Amun, the king of the gods, as a true pharaoh.
To Greeks, he was confirmed by Zeus, the king of the gods, as a divinely chosen ruler.
The double endorsement gave Alexander a theological claim to rule that neither his Macedonian kingship nor his military conquests alone could provide.
Before leaving Egypt, Alexander made one more decision that would outlast everything else he did in his short life.
In January 331 BCE, on a strip of land between the Mediterranean Sea and Lake Mareotis in the western Delta, Alexander laid out the plans for a new city.
He named it, with characteristic modesty, after himself: Alexandria.
The site was chosen for its harbors.

The natural geography offered two harbors (protected by the offshore island of Pharos), access to the Nile via the Canopic branch, and a position at the intersection of Mediterranean and Egyptian trade routes.
It was also, pointedly, not an Egyptian city.
