What collapsed was not a people but a political and cultural system: the network of divine kings, monument-carving elites, city-state rivalries, and the elaborate superstructure of Classic Maya civilization.
The buildings were abandoned.
The script was largely forgotten.
The Long Count calendar stopped being used.
But the Maya themselves persisted, adapted, and built new societies in new places.
Calling it a "disappearance" erases the millions of living Maya descendants.
It was a political collapse, not a vanishing act.
So what happened?
The evidence points not to a single catastrophe but to a cascade of reinforcing failures that hit a system already stretched to its limits.
Start with the environment.

Paleoclimatic data (evidence from lake sediment cores, cave stalagmites, and ocean sediment records) shows that the Maya lowlands experienced a series of severe droughts during the 9th century CE, with particularly intense dry periods around 810, 860, and 910 CE.
These weren't ordinary dry spells.
They were multi-decadal reductions in rainfall, possibly the worst in over two thousand years.

Lake Chichancanab on the Yucatán Peninsula shows gypsum deposits in its sediment cores from this period, indicating that the lake partially evaporated, something that requires a severe and sustained drop in rainfall.
Recent oxygen-isotope analysis suggests that rainfall during the worst drought periods dropped by 40 to 50 percent below normal.
The timing correlates disturbingly well with the dates when individual cities stopped erecting monuments and were abandoned.
The droughts hit a landscape that was already ecologically stressed.

By the late 8th century, the Maya lowlands were densely populated (the Petén alone may have held 3 to 5 million people at its peak, a density comparable to modern pre-industrial agricultural societies), heavily deforested (the Maya cleared jungle for agriculture and burned vast quantities of limestone to make plaster for their buildings, a process that requires enormous amounts of firewood), and dependent on increasingly intensive agriculture to feed a growing population.
Raised field systems, terraced hillsides, and managed wetlands squeezed maximum production from the landscape, but there was no margin for error.

The deforestation itself may have worsened the droughts: removing tropical forest reduces local rainfall through the loss of evapotranspiration (trees release moisture that forms rain clouds, so cutting trees can literally reduce the rain).

The Maya may have been caught in a feedback loop: more people required more farmland, more farmland meant less forest, less forest meant less rain, less rain meant less food, less food meant more pressure to clear more forest.
When the rains failed on top of this already stressed system, there was nowhere to go and nothing to fall back on.

The reservoir systems that Maya cities used to store water (Tikal's reservoirs held enough water to sustain the city through an 18-month dry period) were designed for normal seasonal variation, not for multi-year drought.
Then consider the politics.
The Tikal-Calakmul rivalry and its aftermath had shattered the bipolar power structure that had, paradoxically, kept the system stable.
After Tikal's victory in 695 CE, no single city had the power to dominate the lowlands, and the resulting fragmentation led to an increase in warfare among mid-tier kingdoms.
The inscriptions from the late 8th century show a surge in "star war" events, suggesting that the political system was becoming more violent and less stable.
More warfare meant more destruction of agricultural land, more displacement of farmers, and more disruption of trade networks.
The economic system was also under strain.
The jade and obsidian trade networks that had connected the lowlands to the highlands for centuries depended on the stability of the political order.
As cities fell and routes became unsafe, trade contracted.
Elite goods became scarcer.
Kings who couldn't provide the ritual luxury goods that legitimized their authority lost prestige and, eventually, control.
And then there's the human dimension.
Maya kingship depended on the claim that the k'uhul ajaw could intercede with the gods to bring rain, ensure harvests, and maintain cosmic order.
When the rains didn't come, year after year, despite increasingly elaborate (and probably increasingly desperate) rituals, the ideological contract between ruler and ruled broke down.
Why support a king whose prayers don't work?
Why carry baskets of earth to build his pyramid when there isn't enough to eat?

The archaeological evidence from the final decades of occupation at some sites shows increased fortification (defensive walls and palisades hastily constructed around civic centers), suggesting that the elites were trying to protect themselves from their own populations.
The collapse was not simultaneous.
It rolled across the lowlands over roughly two centuries, hitting different cities at different times.
Some cities (like Dos Pilas) fell early, in the mid-8th century, before the worst droughts.
Others (like Tikal) held on until the late 9th century.
A few (like Lamanai (lah-mah-NYE) in Belize and Cobá in the northeastern Yucatán) survived into the Postclassic period.
The pattern suggests that local factors (water availability, political stability, agricultural capacity) determined when each city reached its tipping point.
The last known Long Count date in the southern lowlands was carved at the site of Toniná (toh-nee-NAH) in 909 CE.
After that, silence.
The system that had produced one of the most literate and historically self-conscious civilizations in the ancient world simply stopped recording its own existence.
But the Maya didn't stop.
Not everywhere.
As the southern lowland cities emptied, the northern Yucatán was filling up.
The northern peninsula had always been part of the Maya world, but it had been peripheral to the great Classic-period powers of the Petén.
Now it became the center.

Cities in the Puuc (POOK) region of the northern Yucatán, including Uxmal (oosh-MAHL), Kabah (kah-BAH), and Sayil (sah-YEEL), experienced a florescence during the Terminal Classic period (roughly 800–1000 CE), right as the southern cities were collapsing.

The Puuc cities developed a distinctive architectural style (the Puuc style) characterized by elaborate mosaic stone facades, intricate geometric friezes, and rain god masks carved from fitted stone blocks.
The Pyramid of the Magician at Uxmal, with its unusual rounded corners and oval base, is one of the most visually striking structures in the Maya world.
Uxmal deserves special attention.
Under a ruler whose name we read as Lord Chak (chahk), who ruled around 900 CE, Uxmal became the most architecturally inventive city in the late Maya world.

The Pyramid of the Magician (named by later Spanish-speaking inhabitants who attributed it to sorcery) is unlike any other Maya pyramid: its base is elliptical rather than rectangular, its sides are smoothly rounded, and it was built in five construction phases, each encasing the previous one.
The Governor's Palace, a long, low building atop a massive platform, is widely considered the single finest piece of architecture in all of pre-Columbian America.
Its mosaic facade contains over 20,000 individually carved stones fitted together to form an intricate geometric pattern of rain god masks, lattice work, and serpent motifs.
The labor required to carve, fit, and assemble these stones is staggering.
The Puuc cities had advantages the southern lowland centers lacked.
The northern Yucatán's limestone bedrock is closer to the surface, making cenotes more accessible and water management somewhat easier.
The region was also less deforested and less densely populated than the Petén core.
And the Puuc cities appear to have developed a more confederate political structure, with multiple elite families sharing power rather than concentrating it in a single divine king.
Whether this made them more resilient or simply lucky is debatable.
The northern Yucatán's biggest story, though, was not the Puuc cities.
It was the rise of Chichén Itzá (chee-CHEN eet-SAH), a city that would dominate the Maya world for the next two centuries and produce some of the most recognizable architecture in the Americas.
But that belongs to the next chapter.
The Classic Maya Collapse is sometimes compared to the fall of the Roman Empire, and the comparison is instructive in both its parallels and its differences.
Both involved the collapse of a complex political system over a period of roughly two centuries.
Both involved environmental stress, military pressure, economic contraction, and political fragmentation working in combination.
Both resulted in the abandonment of major urban centers and a significant reduction in literacy and monumental construction.
And both are frequently oversimplified in popular accounts (the Romans didn't "fall" to barbarians in a single dramatic event any more than the Maya "vanished" overnight).
But the differences matter.
Rome was an empire.
The Maya never were.
Rome's collapse involved the loss of central authority over a vast unified territory.
The Maya collapse involved the failure of dozens of independent city-states, each collapsing individually for locally specific reasons.
Rome's fall was largely a political and military event.
The Maya collapse had a massive environmental component (the droughts) that Rome's did not.
And Rome's successor states preserved much of Roman culture, language, and religion.

The Maya successor states in the northern Yucatán preserved the language and the basic cultural toolkit but largely abandoned the political and intellectual apparatus (divine kingship, Long Count calendar, hieroglyphic literacy) that had defined the Classic period.
The southern lowland jungle took back the cities.
Tikal's pyramids became tree-covered hills.
Palenque's palaces became nests for howler monkeys.
Calakmul's plazas became clearings in the forest.

For over eight hundred years, until European explorers and 19th-century archaeologists pushed their way through the undergrowth, the greatest cities in the ancient Americas were forgotten by everyone except the Maya farmers who still lived nearby, who told stories about ancient kings and ruined palaces hidden in the trees.

chapters.title: The Postclassic World
sentences: The Maya civilization that emerged after the Classic Collapse was different from what came before.
The divine kings of the Classic period, with their Long Count dates and their elaborate genealogical inscriptions, were gone.

The new Maya world was more commercial, more cosmopolitan, more openly syncretic (blending Maya traditions with ideas from central Mexico), and more focused on trade than on carved stelae and dynastic propaganda.
It was not a decline.
It was a transformation.

The dominant city of the early Postclassic Maya world was Chichén Itzá, a site in the northern Yucatán whose architecture and art blend Maya and central Mexican elements in ways that scholars have debated for over a century.
Chichén Itzá was occupied from at least the 7th century CE, but it reached its peak between roughly 900 and 1100 CE.
What makes it distinctive is the split personality of its architecture.
The "Old" Chichén section has buildings in recognizable Maya styles (Puuc-style facades, corbel-arched rooms, rain god masks).

The "New" Chichén section has buildings that look nothing like Classic Maya architecture and a lot like the architecture of central Mexico: the Great Ball Court (the largest in all of Mesoamerica, roughly 545 feet / 166 meters long), the Temple of the Warriors (a massive colonnade-fronted platform covered in carved warrior figures holding atlatl dart throwers and wearing distinctly non-Maya regalia), and, most famously, El Castillo (el kahs-TEE-yo), the stepped pyramid also known as the Temple of Kukulcán (koo-kool-KAHN).
The cult of Kukulcán (the Maya name for the feathered serpent deity known as Quetzalcoatl in central Mexico) became the defining religious feature of Postclassic Maya civilization.
Kukulcán worship was probably not new: feathered serpent imagery goes back to the Olmec period (Olmec Ch 4).
But at Chichén Itzá, the feathered serpent moved from background decoration to center stage.

El Castillo's most famous feature is the equinox shadow effect: on the spring and fall equinoxes, the late-afternoon sun casts triangular shadows along the northern staircase that create the illusion of a serpent's body descending the pyramid, connecting to the carved serpent head at the base.

Whether this effect was intentionally designed is debated (some scholars think it's a coincidence of geometry), but the alignment is precise enough that it draws hundreds of thousands of visitors every equinox.
El Castillo's engineering is precise.
The pyramid has 91 steps on each of its four sides, plus the platform at the top: 364 + 1 = 365, the number of days in the solar year.
This may be coincidental.
Or it may be another example of the Maya habit of embedding astronomical and calendrical information into architecture, making the building itself a calendar.

The pyramid contains an earlier, smaller pyramid inside it (discovered in the 1930s), with a jade-studded jaguar throne and a Chac Mool figure (a reclining figure holding a bowl on its stomach, probably for receiving offerings) in a buried chamber.
This nesting of pyramids within pyramids was common Maya practice: each new ruler built over his predecessor's temple, literally covering the old king's monument with his own.
The Sacred Cenote at Chichén Itzá, a large natural sinkhole about 200 feet (60 meters) in diameter and 90 feet (27 meters) deep to the water surface, served as a major pilgrimage site.
Offerings of jade, gold, pottery, obsidian, rubber, copal incense, and human beings were thrown into the cenote over centuries.

Dredging operations in the early 20th century recovered thousands of objects, including jade from Guatemala, gold from Panama and Colombia (the first significant gold artifacts in the Maya world, reflecting Postclassic trade connections with South America via Central American intermediaries), and the remains of sacrificial victims.
The cenote was a portal to Xibalba (shee-BAL-bah), the Maya underworld, and offerings cast into its waters were sent directly to the lords of death.

The range of objects recovered tells a story of Chichén Itzá's far-flung connections: jade and obsidian from the Maya highlands, copper from western Mexico, gold from lower Central America, pottery from across the Yucatán.
The cenote was not just a religious site.
It was a map of Postclassic Mesoamerican trade, every offering tracing a line on the map from its point of origin to this pool of dark water in the jungle.
Where did the central Mexican influence come from?

The traditional explanation, popularized in the 19th and early 20th centuries, was that Toltec warriors from the city of Tula (TOO-lah) in central Mexico conquered Chichén Itzá and imposed their culture on the Maya.

This "Toltec invasion" theory was supported by the striking similarities between Chichén Itzá's Temple of the Warriors and Tula's Pyramid B, the presence of warrior imagery with Toltec-style atlatl throwers, and later Aztec legends about a Toltec king named Topiltzin Quetzalcoatl (toh-PEELT-seen ket-sahl-ko-AH-tul) who was driven from Tula and sailed east to the Maya lands.
Modern scholars have largely abandoned the simple conquest theory.

The relationship between Chichén Itzá and central Mexico was probably more complex: a blend of trade connections, elite exchange, shared religious cults (especially the feathered serpent), and possibly the arrival of central Mexican warrior-merchants who integrated into Maya society rather than conquering it.
The architectural similarities between Chichén Itzá and Tula may reflect a shared culture of militarized elites across Mesoamerica in the early Postclassic, rather than one city copying the other.
And recent dating suggests that some of Chichén Itzá's "Toltec" architecture may actually predate Tula's, which would make the influence flow in the opposite direction.
What's clear is that Chichén Itzá was a cosmopolitan city.
Its art includes imagery from multiple cultural traditions.
Its architecture blends Maya and non-Maya elements.
Its trade connections reached from central Mexico to Colombia.
And its religion, centered on the feathered serpent cult, was pan-Mesoamerican rather than specifically Maya.
Chichén Itzá was a city where the Maya world met the wider Mesoamerican world, and the result was something new.
Chichén Itzá declined around 1100 CE.
The reasons, as usual, are debated: internal political conflict, competition from rising rivals, disruption of trade routes, or some combination.
The city was not destroyed (it remained a pilgrimage site for centuries), but its political dominance ended.
Power in the northern Yucatán shifted to Mayapán (mah-yah-PAHN), a walled city about 25 miles (40 km) southeast of the modern city of Mérida.
Mayapán ruled the Yucatán from roughly 1200 to 1441 CE through a political system called the League of Mayapán, a confederacy of allied noble houses led by the Cocom (ko-KOM) dynasty.
The League was, in theory, a cooperative arrangement among the major Maya noble families.

In practice, the Cocom family dominated through a combination of military force (they employed central Mexican mercenaries) and the authority of their supposed descent from the rulers of Chichén Itzá.
Mayapán was smaller and less architecturally ambitious than Chichén Itzá.
Its version of El Castillo (a stepped pyramid in the center of the city) is a smaller, cruder copy of the Chichén Itzá original.
Its wall enclosed roughly 1.6 square miles (4.2 square km), within which an estimated 15,000 to 20,000 people lived in tightly packed residential compounds.
The wall itself is significant: Classic Maya cities were not walled (they sprawled openly through the jungle, with no defensive perimeters).
The fact that Mayapán needed a wall tells us something about the security environment of the Postclassic.

The Cocom rulers held power partly through force (they kept a garrison of central Mexican mercenaries, the Canul warriors, who enforced Cocom authority and served as a standing army), and partly through the requirement that lords of the allied noble houses reside within Mayapán's walls, where they could be watched.
The system was, in essence, a hostage arrangement disguised as a confederacy.
Meanwhile, the northern Yucatán's salt trade was reshaping Maya economics.

The shallow coastal lagoons along the north coast produced high-quality salt through solar evaporation, and salt became a major export commodity, traded by canoe along coastal routes stretching from Tabasco to Honduras.

Salt, cacao, honey, and cotton cloth were the primary commodities of a Postclassic maritime trade network that was, in some ways, more commercially sophisticated than the Classic-period overland trade in jade and obsidian.
Maya coastal trading canoes were large, seagoing vessels capable of carrying up to 40 passengers and several tons of cargo.
These were not dugout canoes in the way that word is usually understood.

They were massive craft, carved from single mahogany or ceiba trunks, with freeboard added using planks, and they were paddled by crews of up to 25 men along the coast and across open stretches of the Caribbean.

The Postclassic maritime trade route ran from Tabasco around the entire Yucatán Peninsula to the Bay of Honduras, with major stops at island ports like Cozumel (koh-soo-MEL) and coastal trading towns like Tulum (too-LOOM), the clifftop city whose postcard-perfect ruins overlook the Caribbean Sea.

When Columbus encountered a Maya trading canoe off the coast of Honduras during his fourth voyage in 1502 CE, he described a vessel "as long as a galley and eight feet wide," loaded with cacao, copper bells, cotton cloth, obsidian blades, and a crew that included a merchant, sailors, and women and children.
The encounter was, as far as we know, the first recorded contact between Europeans and Maya civilization.
Columbus had no idea what he was looking at.
He sailed on.
The Maya, for their part, had no idea that the strange men in their floating castles were the advance guard of the end of their world.
Mayapán's confederacy lasted until 1441 CE, when it ended in a characteristically Maya fashion: betrayal and violence from within.
The Xiu (SHEE-oo) faction, one of the noble families in the League, led a revolt against the Cocom rulers.

The Xiu lord Ah Kukum Xiu (ah koo-KOOM SHEE-oo) organized an attack on Mayapán, sacking the city, massacring the Cocom lords (tradition says only one Cocom prince survived, because he was away on a trading voyage), and destroying the last centralized Maya political authority in the Yucatán.

After Mayapán's fall, the northern Yucatán fragmented into roughly sixteen small, quarrelsome provinces (called kuchkabalob, kooch-kah-bah-LOB), each ruled by a local lord, each feuding with its neighbors, and none strong enough to reunify the region.

This was the political situation that the Spanish found when they arrived, and the fragmentation made the Maya simultaneously harder to conquer (no single ruler to defeat and no capital to capture) and easier to exploit (the Spanish played factions against each other with devastating effectiveness).
The Dresden Codex (named for the German library where it is now housed), compiled around 1200 CE, is the oldest surviving book from the Americas.
It is a folding bark-paper manuscript, roughly 11.5 feet (3.5 meters) long when unfolded, covered in hieroglyphic text and colored illustrations.

Its contents include astronomical tables for predicting eclipses and the movements of Venus, Mars, and the Moon, as well as almanacs tying these celestial events to agricultural and ritual activities.
The astronomical tables are extraordinarily accurate (Chapter 7).
The Dresden Codex, along with three other surviving Maya codices (the Madrid, Paris, and Grolier codices), represents a tiny fraction of what was once a vast literary tradition.
The rest was lost to decay, neglect, and deliberate destruction by the Spanish (Chapter 8).

chapters.title: Words, Stars, and Zero
sentences: The Maya developed the most sophisticated writing system, the most accurate astronomy, and the most advanced mathematics of any civilization in the pre-Columbian Americas.

These three achievements are interconnected: the writing system recorded the astronomical observations, the mathematics made the calculations possible, and the calendar wove all three together into a system for tracking time from the creation of the world to the end of days.
No other civilization in the Western Hemisphere came close.

This chapter steps outside chronological order to examine these intellectual achievements as a whole, because they developed over more than a millennium and because understanding them changes how you read everything else in this narrative.
Maya hieroglyphic writing is one of the great intellectual achievements of the human species.

It is the only fully developed writing system in the pre-Columbian Americas, meaning it is the only one that could record the full range of spoken language (any sentence a Maya speaker could say, a Maya scribe could write).
The Olmec Cascajal Block (Olmec Ch 7) is probably proto-writing.
The Zapotec script and the Aztec pictographic system are partially developed systems that can record certain types of information but cannot capture the full complexity of speech.
Maya writing can.

It is a complete script, comparable in expressive power to Sumerian cuneiform (the first fully developed writing system, invented in Mesopotamia around 3200 BCE), Egyptian hieroglyphs (roughly contemporary with cuneiform), and Chinese characters (oracle bone script, roughly 1200 BCE).
The script works as a logosyllabary: a combination of logograms (symbols that represent whole words or concepts) and syllabic signs (symbols that represent consonant-vowel syllable combinations).

A scribe writing the word "balam" (jaguar) could write it with a single logogram for "jaguar," or spell it syllabically with three signs: ba-la-ma (the final vowel is silent, a convention of the script).
Most texts use both methods, mixing logograms and syllabic signs in combinations that gave the scribes extraordinary flexibility.
The same word could be written multiple ways, and Maya scribes delighted in visual wordplay, aesthetic variation, and calligraphic virtuosity.
Writing was an art form, not just a communication tool.
The script was cracked in stages.

The first breakthrough came in the 1950s, when the Soviet linguist Yuri Knorosov (YOO-ree kno-ROH-sov) proposed that Maya glyphs had phonetic (sound-based) values, challenging the dominant theory that they were purely logographic (meaning-based).

Knorosov's insight was initially rejected by Western Mayanists, partly because he was Soviet and published during the Cold War, and partly because the established view (championed by the British archaeologist J.
Thompson, who dominated the field for decades) held that Maya writing was ideographic and non-phonetic.
Knorosov was right.
By the 1980s and 1990s, a new generation of scholars (including Linda Schele, David Stuart, and Peter Mathews) had built on Knorosov's work to achieve a near-complete decipherment.
Today, roughly 80 to 90 percent of Maya glyphs can be read, and the percentage continues to climb.
The decipherment transformed Maya studies.
Before Knorosov, the Maya were a mysterious civilization known primarily through their architecture and their unreadable inscriptions.
After decipherment, the Maya became historical: named kings, dated battles, diplomatic marriages, ritual events, and dynastic genealogies all emerged from the stones.
The speed of the decipherment, once the phonetic principle was accepted, was stunning.
In 1960, scholars could read almost nothing.
By 1990, they could read most texts.

David Stuart, who began contributing to the decipherment as a teenager (he won a MacArthur Fellowship at age 18, the youngest recipient at that time), was central to the breakthrough phase, reading hundreds of previously opaque glyphs and showing that the script was far more phonetically sophisticated than anyone had realized.
The inscriptions turned out to be, overwhelmingly, political propaganda: kings boasting about their conquests, their ancestors, their divine connections, and their building programs.

They are roughly equivalent to the self-congratulatory inscriptions that Mesopotamian kings carved on their palace walls and Egyptian pharaohs carved on their temple pylons: the rulers' version of events, serving the rulers' interests.
The voice of ordinary Maya people, their songs, their jokes, their daily concerns, is almost entirely absent.
What survives is the voice of power.

The Maya wrote on stone (stelae, lintels, stairways, altars), on pottery (painted texts on ceramic vessels), on walls (painted murals), on bone (carved inscriptions on animal and human bones), on jade (carved texts on jewelry and ritual objects), on shells, and, most importantly, on bark paper.

Maya books were folding screen manuscripts made from the inner bark of fig trees, coated with a thin layer of white lime plaster, and painted with text and illustrations using fine brushes and pigments.
We know from depictions in Maya art that libraries of these books existed in every major city.
Almost none survived.
The tropical climate destroyed most of them.
The Spanish burned most of the rest (Chapter 8).
Only four Maya codices survive, and they are among the most precious documents in the Americas.
Maya astronomy was observational, predictive, and frightening in its accuracy.

Maya astronomers (who were also priests, since astronomical observation was a religious activity) tracked the movements of the Sun, Moon, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn with the naked eye, using simple tools: crossed sticks for sighting alignments, buildings oriented to specific rising and setting positions, and, above all, centuries of accumulated records.
They had no telescopes, no astrolabes, no sextants.
What they had was patience, rigorous record-keeping, and a mathematical system precise enough to perform the calculations.
The Venus tables in the Dresden Codex track the synodic period of Venus (the time between successive appearances of Venus as the morning star) across 65 Venus cycles, a span of roughly 104 years.
The Maya value for the synodic period, corrected over this span, averages to 583.92 days, which matches the modern value to an accuracy of a few minutes per cycle.
This is not luck.
This is centuries of careful observation, recorded, compared, and refined.
Because the Maya used Venus appearances to time warfare.

The appearance of Venus as the morning star (after a period of invisibility as it passed between Earth and the Sun) was considered cosmically dangerous, a time when the war god was active and military campaigns were divinely sanctioned.

The "star war" events recorded in the inscriptions (Chapter 3) correlate with Venus appearances, suggesting that Maya kings planned their most important attacks to coincide with specific astronomical events.
The astronomy was not academic curiosity.
It was strategic intelligence.
The eclipse tables in the Dresden Codex are even more impressive.

The tables predict lunar eclipses across a span of 33 years with remarkable accuracy, using a calculation based on the eclipse cycle (the interval between eclipses) of 11,960 days (roughly 46 synodic months of the Moon × 260 days).
Maya astronomers had figured out the eclipse cycle empirically, through centuries of observation, and had constructed tables that could predict eclipses decades in advance.
For a civilization without any optical instruments, this is an extraordinary achievement.

The Babylonians, working independently in Mesopotamia, had also computed eclipse cycles, but they had the advantage of a long tradition of astronomical record-keeping stretching back to the second millennium BCE (Mesopotamia Ch 10).
The Maya achieved comparable results starting from a completely independent tradition.
The mathematical system that made all of this possible was elegant and, in one crucial respect, ahead of its time.
Maya mathematics used a vigesimal (base-20) number system, in contrast to the decimal (base-10) system used in most modern cultures and the sexagesimal (base-60) system used by the Babylonians.
The base-20 system probably derives from counting fingers and toes, and it was used across Mesoamerica.
But the Maya version had a critical innovation: positional notation.
In a positional system, the value of a digit depends on its position.
In our decimal system, the 2 in 200 means something different from the 2 in 20, because its position indicates a different power of 10.
The Maya system works the same way, but with powers of 20.
The bottom position represents ones (1–19).
The next position up represents twenties (20–380).
The next represents 400s, and so on.
Each position can hold a value from 0 to 19, expressed with just three symbols: a dot (representing 1), a bar (representing 5), and a shell glyph (representing 0).
The shell glyph.
This is the innovation that matters most, because positional notation doesn't work without zero.
You need a way to indicate that a position is empty, the way the 0 in 102 indicates no tens.
Without zero, 102 is indistinguishable from 12 or 1,002.
The Maya solved this problem independently, one of only three or four civilizations in human history to do so (along with the Babylonians, the Indians, and possibly the Chinese).
The Babylonian zero (roughly 300 BCE) was a placeholder.
The Indian zero (roughly 5th–7th century CE) became a full number capable of arithmetic operations.

The Maya zero (attested from roughly 350 CE) was primarily a calendar placeholder, but it was a genuine, conceptual zero: a symbol for nothing, used in a positional system, enabling calculations that would be impossible without it.
But mathematics and astronomy were servants of the calendar, and the calendar was the spine of Maya civilization.
The Maya used three interlocking calendar systems.
The Tzolk'in (tsohl-KEEN), a 260-day ritual calendar combining 13 numbers with 20 named days, governed religious ceremonies, divination, and the naming of children.
The Haab (HAHB), a 365-day solar calendar of 18 months of 20 days plus 5 "nameless" days considered unlucky, governed agriculture and civic life.
The two calendars ran simultaneously, and any given day could be identified by its position in both cycles.
The two cycles realigned every 52 solar years (18,980 days), creating the Calendar Round, the basic unit of Mesoamerican time.
The Calendar Round was shared across Mesoamerican civilizations and almost certainly originated in the Olmec period (Olmec Ch 7).
But the Maya went further.

The Long Count calendar counted individual days from a fixed starting point (the mythological creation date of August 11, 3114 BCE), using a positional system based on powers of 20: one kin (day), 20 kins = one uinal (20 days), 18 uinals = one tun (360 days, roughly a year), 20 tuns = one katun (7,200 days, roughly 20 years), 20 katuns = one baktun (144,000 days, roughly 394 years).
A Long Count date uniquely identified any day across thousands of years, the way a GPS coordinate uniquely identifies any point on Earth.
No other pre-modern civilization developed a timekeeping system of comparable precision and range.
The Chinese used reign-year dating.
The Romans counted from the founding of their city.
The Islamic calendar counted from the Hijra.
None of these systems approached the Long Count's ability to pinpoint a single day across a span of over 5,000 years.
The Popol Vuh (POH-pol VOO, meaning "Council Book" or "Book of the Community") is the most important surviving text of Maya mythology.
It was not originally a book in the European sense.

It was an oral tradition, transmitted by memory across generations, describing the creation of the world, the adventures of the Hero Twins (Hunahpu and Xbalanque, who descend to the Maya underworld Xibalba and defeat the lords of death through cleverness and trickery), the creation of humans from maize dough, and the history of the K'iche' (kee-CHEH) Maya people of highland Guatemala.

The Hero Twins myth, depicted in Maya art from the Preclassic period onward (the San Bartolo murals from roughly 100 BCE show scenes recognizable from the Popol Vuh), was probably the most widely known Maya narrative, the Maya equivalent of the Iliad or the Ramayana.
The story of how the Popol Vuh survived belongs to the next chapter.
For now, note that the myth was central to Maya cosmology.
The Popol Vuh describes three failed attempts at creating humans.
The gods first tried mud (it dissolved), then wood (the wooden people could function but had no souls, no gratitude, and no memory, so the gods destroyed them in a flood).
Finally, the gods made humans from maize dough, and these people worked: they could think, speak, remember, and worship their creators.
The message is clear and deliberate.
Humans are made of corn.
Corn is divine substance.
Every harvest is a reenactment of creation, and every planting is an act of faith that the gods will allow humanity to continue.
The ballgame that the Hero Twins play against the lords of Xibalba was the mythological model for the real ballgame played in every Maya city (Olmec Ch 4).
The creation of humans from maize connected the agricultural cycle to cosmic creation.

The descent to the underworld and the resurrection of the Maize God connected the growing season (planting as death, sprouting as rebirth) to the daily cycle of the sun (setting as death, rising as rebirth).
Everything was connected.
The calendar tracked the connections.
The mathematics measured them.
The writing recorded them.
The Maya intellectual system was a unified whole, and its achievements in any single domain are inseparable from the whole.

chapters.title: Contact and the Last Kingdom
sentences: On his fourth and final voyage to the Americas in 1502, Christopher Columbus anchored off the Bay Islands near the coast of modern Honduras and encountered a large Maya trading canoe carrying cacao, copper, obsidian, and cotton textiles.
It was the first recorded contact between Europeans and Maya civilization.
Columbus's men seized the canoe, inspected its cargo, took what interested them, and let it go.
Columbus noted the encounter in his log and sailed west.
He had no idea that the people in the canoe belonged to a civilization that had been building cities, writing books, and predicting eclipses for two thousand years.
He was looking for China.

Fifteen years later, in 1517, a Spanish expedition led by Francisco Hernández de Córdoba (frahn-SEES-ko ehr-NAHN-deth deh KOR-doh-bah) sailed from Cuba to the Yucatán coast and encountered something the Spanish had not expected: stone cities.
The Spanish had been in the Caribbean for 25 years, and the indigenous peoples they had encountered (the Taino, the Caribs) lived in wooden villages.
The Yucatán was different.
Córdoba's men saw buildings of cut stone, temples atop pyramids, cotton-clad inhabitants, and warriors with organized military formations.
They also encountered fierce resistance.
At the town of Champotón, the Maya attacked with bows, spears, and shields, killing over 50 Spanish soldiers and wounding Córdoba himself so badly that he died shortly after returning to Cuba.
The Maya were the first Mesoamerican people to draw European blood in significant quantities.
Two follow-up expeditions (by Juan de Grijalva in 1518 and Hernán Cortés in 1519) explored the Yucatán coast before Cortés turned his attention to the Aztec Empire in central Mexico.
The conquest of the Aztecs (1519–1521 CE) was swift and dramatic, accomplished in under three years.
The conquest of the Maya would take over 170 years.
The difference is instructive.
The Aztec Empire was centralized: Cortés captured the emperor Moctezuma, conquered the capital Tenochtitlan, and the empire collapsed.
The Maya had no emperor.
They had no capital.
They had dozens of independent kingdoms, each of which had to be conquered separately.
Defeat one Maya lord, and his neighbor a few days' march away continued fighting.
The decentralized political system that had prevented the Maya from building an empire also prevented the Spanish from conquering them quickly.

The Spanish conquest of the Yucatán began in earnest in 1527, when Francisco de Montejo (frahn-SEES-ko deh mon-TEH-ho, a veteran of Cortés's Aztec campaign) launched the first of three campaigns to subjugate the Maya of the northern Yucatán.
The first campaign (1527–1528) failed.
The Maya resisted fiercely, the terrain was difficult, and the Spanish supply lines were stretched thin.
The second campaign (1531–1535) also failed.
Montejo shifted operations to the east coast, then to Tabasco, and was driven out each time by Maya guerrilla warfare and the difficulty of holding territory in dense jungle and scrubland.

The third campaign (1540–1546), led by Montejo's son (also named Francisco de Montejo, confusingly called "el Mozo," the Younger), finally succeeded in establishing permanent Spanish control over the northern Yucatán.
The younger Montejo exploited the divisions between the Xiu and Cocom factions (the same families who had been fighting since the fall of Mayapán in 1441) by allying with the Xiu against the Cocom.
In January 1542, the Spanish founded the colonial capital of Mérida on the ruins of the Maya city of T'ho.
But "conquest" is a misleading word.
Large portions of the interior remained unconquered.
Maya communities in the dense forests of the central Petén and the eastern Yucatán continued to resist Spanish authority for decades.
Some would resist for another 150 years.
Even in the "conquered" areas, the Spanish held the towns while the surrounding countryside remained effectively Maya.
Revolts broke out repeatedly.
The Great Maya Revolt of 1546 nearly drove the Spanish out of the Yucatán entirely, and minor uprisings continued for centuries.

The Maya conversion to Christianity was, for a long time, skin-deep at best: converts attended mass on Sunday and performed traditional rituals in the forest on Monday, a pattern that drove Spanish priests to fury and, eventually, to the inquisition of Diego de Landa.
The demographic catastrophe was worse than the military conquest.
European diseases (smallpox, measles, typhus, influenza) swept through Maya populations that had no immunity.
Estimates suggest the Maya population of the Yucatán dropped by 90 percent or more in the century following contact, from roughly 2 million to perhaps 200,000.
The disease did more to destroy Maya civilization than the Spanish sword ever could.
On July 12, 1562, Bishop Diego de Landa (dee-EH-go deh LAHN-dah) committed one of the greatest acts of cultural destruction in the history of the Americas.
De Landa was the Franciscan bishop of Yucatán, charged with converting the Maya to Christianity.
He was a complex figure: intelligent, energetic, genuinely interested in Maya culture, and completely certain that Maya religious practices were the work of the Devil.
When he discovered that Maya converts were continuing to practice their traditional religion in secret, he launched an inquisition.
At the town of Maní (mah-NEE), de Landa ordered the burning of thousands of Maya manuscripts, religious images, and ritual objects.

In his own words: "We found a large number of books in these characters and, as they contained nothing in which were not to be seen as superstition and lies of the devil, we burned them all, which they regretted to an amazing degree, and which caused them much affliction." Thousands of bark-paper codices, the accumulated literary, scientific, historical, and religious output of centuries of Maya civilization, were burned in a single afternoon.
The irony is bitter.
De Landa was also the most important early European recorder of Maya culture.

His manuscript Relación de las cosas de Yucatán (Account of the Things of Yucatán), written around 1566, contains the most detailed 16th-century description of Maya society, religion, calendar, and, crucially, a partial recording of the Maya hieroglyphic script (his "Landa alphabet") that would eventually contribute to the decipherment of Maya writing four hundred years later.
The man who burned the books also preserved the key that would unlock them.
De Landa's superiors in Spain were horrified by the severity of his inquisition and recalled him for trial, but he was eventually acquitted and returned to Yucatán as bishop, dying there in 1579.
Of the vast Maya literary tradition, only four codices survive: the Dresden Codex (Chapter 6), the Madrid Codex, the Paris Codex, and the Grolier Codex (sometimes called the Maya Codex of Mexico).
Four books.
That is the entire surviving library of a civilization that produced books for over a thousand years.
But not everything was lost.

Around 1554 CE, members of the K'iche' Maya nobility in the highlands of Guatemala transcribed the Popol Vuh from oral tradition into the Latin alphabet, probably working from an older hieroglyphic manuscript that has since been lost.

The transcription preserved the most complete surviving account of Maya creation mythology: the creation and destruction of the world's first humans (made of mud, then of wood, both failures), the adventures of the Hero Twins in the underworld, the final creation of humans from maize dough, and the history of the K'iche' people.

The manuscript lay in obscurity until 1701, when a Dominican friar named Francisco Ximénez (hee-MEH-neth) found it in a church archive in Chichicastenango (chee-chee-kahs-teh-NAHN-go), copied it, and translated it into Spanish.
Without the K'iche' nobles who transcribed it and Ximénez who preserved it, the most important text in Maya literature would have been lost.
Other literary traditions survived in fragmented form.

The Books of Chilam Balam (chee-LAHM bah-LAHM, meaning "Jaguar Prophet"), compiled in various Yucatán communities beginning around 1500 CE, recorded Maya history, prophecy, and calendrical knowledge using the Latin alphabet.
Each book is named after the town where it was kept (the Chilam Balam of Chumayel, of Tizimin, of Maní, and so on), and each contains a different selection of material.
The books are composite texts, blending pre-Conquest Maya traditions with colonial-era Christian influences.

They include katun prophecies (predictions tied to the recurring 20-year cycles of the Maya calendar), historical chronicles, astronomical tables, medical recipes, and accounts of the Spanish conquest from the Maya perspective.

They are difficult to interpret (the languages are archaic, the scribes mixed Maya and Spanish concepts, and some passages are deliberately cryptic), but they preserve glimpses of Maya historical consciousness and prophetic thought that would otherwise be completely lost.
The Chilam Balam prophetic tradition was cyclical: the Maya believed that history repeated in katun cycles, and events that occurred in one katun would recur when that katun came around again.
When the Spanish arrived, some Maya intellectuals tried to fit the conquest into their prophetic framework, interpreting it as a katun event that had been foretold.
The last chapter of independent Maya civilization played out in the remote jungles of the central Petén, far from the Spanish colonial centers in Mérida and Guatemala City.

The Itza Maya (eet-SAH), a group based on the island city of Nojpetén (noh-PEH-ten, also called Tayasal, tah-yah-SAHL), on Lake Petén Itzá in northern Guatemala, maintained their independence for 175 years after Montejo's first campaign.
The Itza controlled a kingdom centered on the lake, surrounded by dense, nearly impenetrable jungle, connected to the outside world by trails that could be (and were) blocked and ambushed.
Spanish missionaries and soldiers tried repeatedly to reach them.
Some were killed.
Others were turned back.
A few were received politely, preached at the Itza for a while, and sent home.
The Itza showed no interest in abandoning their gods.
The Spanish attempts to convert the Itza produced some of the most darkly comic episodes in the history of colonialism.
In 1618, two Franciscan friars traveled to Nojpetén and spent several days preaching to the Itza king, Kan Ek' (kahn EK), who listened politely, asked questions, and declined to convert.
The friars left.
In 1696, the Spanish sent a military delegation of 113 soldiers and missionaries.
The Itza killed most of them.
The survivors straggled back through the jungle with the news that the Itza were disinclined to cooperate.
The Itza maintained their calendar, their religion, and their writing through the entire colonial period.
They knew about the Spanish.
They traded with them occasionally.
They sheltered Maya refugees from the conquered territories.
And they knew their own prophecies.
Some accounts suggest that Itza priests had prophesied that their own katun cycle would bring submission to a foreign power, and that by 1697 the prophesied katun had arrived.
Whether this made the final conquest easier or harder is an open question.
Prophecy can cut both ways: it can make resistance seem futile, or it can make the fight to defy prophecy seem all the more urgent.
On March 13, 1697, a Spanish force under Martín de Ursúa y Arizmendi (mar-TEEN deh oor-SOO-ah ee ah-reeth-MEN-dee) launched a naval assault on Nojpetén across Lake Petén Itzá.
The Spanish had built a small warship (a galeota, a single-masted vessel with oars and a cannon) on the lakeshore and rowed it across to the island.
The Itza defenders paddled out in canoes, hundreds of warriors firing arrows and hurling stones.
The Spanish cannon answered with grapeshot.
The battle was short.
The cannon shattered the Itza canoes and raked the shoreline.
Spanish soldiers landed on the island, overwhelmed the defenders, and captured the city.
King Kan Ek' was captured.
The temples were destroyed.
Christian churches were built on their foundations.
The last independent Maya state, the last holdout of a civilization that had been building cities since before Rome was founded, was gone.
The fall of Nojpetén in 1697 was the last major military event of the Spanish conquest of the Americas.
It came 175 years after Montejo's first campaign, 205 years after Columbus's first voyage, and 2,700 years after the first Maya farming villages appeared in the Petén lowlands.
No other indigenous American civilization resisted European conquest for as long.

The Maya fought for every year of those 175 years, not as a unified nation but as they had always fought: as independent communities, each defending its own territory, each making its own decision about when to submit and when to resist, each stubbornly refusing to disappear.
The Maya did not disappear.
They never have.

Roughly 6 million Maya people live today in Guatemala, Mexico, Belize, and Honduras, speaking over 30 Maya languages, practicing traditions that blend pre-Columbian and Catholic elements, farming the same milpas of maize, beans, and squash that their ancestors farmed three thousand years ago.
The pyramids are ruins.
The codices are ashes.
The Long Count calendar is no longer carved on stelae.
But the Maya are not a lost civilization.
They are a living people with a continuous cultural tradition stretching back to the farming villages of Chapter 1.
They built cities in the jungle for 2,700 years.
They invented zero.
They predicted eclipses.
They wrote books.
They carved the faces of their kings into stone and the history of their dynasties into stairways.
They fought each other nonstop for six centuries and produced, in the process, one of the greatest civilizations in human history.
They never built an empire.
They never needed to.
The competition itself was the engine.
And when the last kingdom fell on an island in a jungle lake, 175 years after the first Spanish soldier set foot in the Yucatán, the people who had built it were still there.
They are still there now.

chapters.title: The New City
sentences: Around 814 BCE, according to a tradition that ancient writers considered reliable and modern archaeologists find roughly plausible, a Tyrian princess named Elissa (eh-LISS-ah), later called Dido (DY-doh) by Latin writers, sailed west from the great Phoenician port city of Tyre (rhymes with "fire"; on the coast of modern Lebanon) and founded a new city on the coast of North Africa.
She called it Qart Hadasht (kart hah-DASHT), which means, straightforwardly enough, "New City." The Greeks rendered this as Karchedon.
The Romans rendered it as Carthago.
We call it Carthage.

The founding story, as told by Greek and Roman writers (most elaborately by the Roman poet Virgil in his Aeneid, though Virgil was writing literature, not history, some seven centuries after the fact), goes like this.

Elissa was the daughter of the king of Tyre and the wife of Acerbas (ah-SER-bass; also called Sychaeus in Latin sources), who was the high priest of Melqart (MEL-kart, the patron god of Tyre) and reputedly enormously wealthy.
When Elissa's brother Pygmalion (pig-MAY-lee-on) inherited the throne, he murdered Acerbas to seize his fortune.
Elissa, warned in a dream, secretly loaded the treasure onto ships, gathered a group of loyal followers and Tyrian nobles, and fled westward across the Mediterranean.
The refugees landed on the coast of what is now Tunisia (too-NEE-zhah), in North Africa, on a triangular peninsula jutting into the Gulf of Tunis.
Local Berber inhabitants were already there.
The famous founding legend says that the local king, Iarbas (ee-AR-bass), agreed to sell Elissa as much land as could be enclosed by a single oxhide.
Elissa cut the hide into the thinnest possible strips, laid them end to end, and enclosed enough ground to build a citadel on the hill called Byrsa (BEER-sah).

The story is almost certainly legendary (the oxhide trick appears in foundation myths from several Mediterranean cultures, and the word byrsa is conveniently similar to the Greek word for "hide," which may have generated the whole tale).
But the broad outline, a Tyrian princess founding a colony on the Tunisian coast in the late ninth century BCE, fits what we know about Phoenician colonization in this period (Phoenicia Ch 5).
A word about sources, because it shapes everything in this book.
Almost nothing you will read about Carthage comes from the Carthaginians themselves.
The Carthaginians wrote extensively.
They had a literature, a historical tradition, a body of scientific and agricultural writing, and religious texts.
They wrote on papyrus and parchment, materials that rot in Mediterranean climates.
When Rome destroyed Carthage in 146 BCE (Chapter 8), it destroyed or dispersed virtually the entire written record.
The Roman Senate distributed Carthage's libraries to Numidian princes, allies who had helped Rome win the final war.
Those libraries vanished.
One single Carthaginian book was deliberately preserved: an agricultural treatise by a writer named Mago (Chapter 3).
Everything else is gone.
What we have instead are Greek and Roman sources.
Polybius (po-LIB-ee-us; a Greek historian who lived roughly 200-118 BCE and personally witnessed the destruction of Carthage) is the most reliable.
He was methodical, skeptical, and had access to Carthaginian informants and documents.
Diodorus Siculus (die-oh-DOR-us SIK-yoo-lus; a Greek historian writing in the first century BCE) drew on earlier Greek sources, including writers who lived during the Sicilian wars.
Appian (APP-ee-an; a Roman historian of the second century CE) wrote a history of the Punic Wars that preserves details found nowhere else.
Livy (LIV-ee; a Roman historian of the first century BCE) wrote the most dramatic accounts of Hannibal's campaigns but was openly patriotic and pro-Roman.

And Virgil's Aeneid (written around 20 BCE) turned Dido into one of the great tragic heroines of Western literature, abandoned by the Trojan hero Aeneas and killing herself on a pyre, but the poem tells us more about Roman literary sensibilities than about Carthaginian history.
The problem with all these sources is obvious.
They were written by people who either fought Carthage or inherited their information from people who did.
The Greeks fought Carthage in Sicily for centuries.
The Romans fought three existential wars against Carthage and ended by erasing the city from the earth.
Reading Carthaginian history through Greek and Roman sources is like reading the history of a defeated nation through the archives of its conquerors.
The conquerors are not lying, exactly, but they are choosing what to record, how to frame it, and what to emphasize.
When Roman sources describe Carthaginian cruelty, treachery, or religious barbarity, the reader should ask: would the Carthaginians have described themselves this way?
We will never know, because the Carthaginians' own account has been silenced.
Keep this in mind throughout this narrative.
When the evidence is strong, I will say so.
When we are relying on hostile witnesses, I will say that too.
The location Elissa chose was superb.
The peninsula of Carthage commanded the Gulf of Tunis on the North African coast, roughly 10 miles (16 km) east of modern Tunis.

More critically, Carthage sat near the narrowest point of the central Mediterranean, where the Tunisian coast and the island of Sicily (roughly 100 miles or 160 km to the northeast) almost pinch the sea shut.
Any ship traveling between the eastern and western Mediterranean had to pass through this bottleneck.
Controlling the bottleneck meant controlling the flow of trade.
It was the ancient equivalent of sitting astride a highway toll booth, except the highway was the entire western Mediterranean.
Byrsa Hill, the citadel where the founding legend places Elissa's original settlement, rose about 200 feet (60 meters) above the coast, offering commanding views of the harbor approaches.
The hill would eventually be crowned by the great Temple of Eshmun (esh-MOON; the Phoenician god of healing, equivalent to the Greek Asclepius), which became the most sacred building in Carthage.
But in the early decades, Byrsa was simply a defensible hill with a small colony clinging to it, dependent on its mother city Tyre for trade goods, cultural identity, and religious legitimacy.
Carthage was not the only Phoenician colony in the western Mediterranean.
Utica (YOU-tih-kah), about 20 miles (32 km) to the northwest on the Tunisian coast, may have been founded even earlier (Phoenicia Ch 5).
Gadir (GAH-deer; modern Cádiz, on the Atlantic coast of Spain) was older still by some accounts.
Phoenician trading posts dotted the coasts of North Africa, Sardinia, and Sicily.

But Carthage had the best location of any of them: the central Mediterranean chokepoint, good harbors, a defensible peninsula, and a fertile agricultural hinterland (the Cap Bon peninsula and the Medjerda River valley) that could feed a growing city.
The young colony grew quickly, and by the eighth century BCE it had begun developing trade networks independent of Tyre.
The mother city was increasingly distracted.
Tyre was under mounting pressure from the Neo-Assyrian Empire (Assyrian Empire Ch 4-6), paying heavy tribute and losing its freedom of action.
As Tyre's independence contracted, Carthage's expanded.

Carthaginian merchants developed their own commercial routes, exchanging manufactured goods (pottery, metalwork, dyed textiles) for raw materials from the peoples of the North African interior and from Phoenician colonies across the western Mediterranean.
The transition from dependent colony to independent power was gradual, probably spanning a century or more, but by roughly 700 BCE, Carthage was the dominant Phoenician city in the west.
The colony did not forget its roots entirely.

Carthage maintained the cult of Melqart, Tyre's patron god, and according to multiple ancient sources, sent annual tribute to the Temple of Melqart at Tyre for centuries after achieving political independence.
This was a religious obligation, not a political one.
The bond was sacred, maintained through priestly delegations who sailed east with offerings even as the colony outstripped its mother city in wealth and power.
It was, in a way, the Phoenician equivalent of a grown child still visiting a parent's church.
But Carthage was developing its own religious identity.

The chief deities of Carthage became Baal Hammon (BAH-ahl HAM-on; depicted as a bearded elder with curling ram's horns, a weather and fertility god whose name may mean "lord of the incense altar" or "lord of Mount Amanus") and Tanit (TAH-nit; a lunar and fertility goddess whose distinctive symbol, a triangle topped by a circle with a horizontal bar between them, is found on thousands of Carthaginian artifacts).
These gods had Phoenician roots but evolved into something distinctly Carthaginian.
Baal Hammon gradually eclipsed Melqart as the supreme god of the city, marking Carthage's theological independence from Tyre just as its political independence solidified.

Tanit would eventually eclipse Baal Hammon himself, her name appearing first on inscriptions from the fifth century BCE onward, becoming the most ubiquitous divine presence in the Punic world (Chapter 7).
The most controversial site in early Carthage, and in all of Punic archaeology, is the Tophet (TOH-fet).
Established in the city's oldest quarter, probably around 750 BCE, the Tophet was a sacred precinct dedicated to Tanit and Baal Hammon.
Over the next six centuries, thousands of urns containing cremated remains were deposited there, accumulating layer upon layer in a site that eventually covered several acres.
The remains in the urns are those of young children and, in some urns, lambs or other animals.
What the Tophet represents is fiercely debated, and we will return to the question in detail in Chapter 7.
For now, note two things.

First, Greek and Roman authors unanimously described the Carthaginians as practicing child sacrifice (the ritual killing of children as an offering to the gods, called the molk offering in Punic inscriptions).
These are hostile sources with obvious reasons to demonize Carthage.
Second, the archaeological evidence is real: there are thousands of urns with infant remains, and the site was clearly a place of significant ritual activity over many centuries.
Modern scholars are genuinely divided.
Some believe the ancient literary testimony and the archaeological evidence together point to actual sacrifice.

Others argue that the Tophet was a sacred burial ground for children who died of natural causes (infant mortality in the ancient world ran between 30 and 50 percent) and were given a special ritual cremation.
The honest answer is that we do not know for certain, and anyone who tells you the debate is settled is overstating their case.

Carthaginian scribes developed their own version of the Phoenician alphabet into what linguists call the Punic script, a cursive variant that was written more quickly and fluidly than the angular Phoenician original.
The Phoenician language and its Punic dialect are closely related (think the difference between British and American English, not the difference between English and French).
Punic inscriptions, mostly votive dedications and funerary texts, are found across the western Mediterranean from Sardinia to Morocco.

The script would survive the destruction of Carthage itself: Neo-Punic inscriptions (the latest stage of the language) appear in North Africa as late as the third or fourth century CE, over three hundred years after Rome burned the city.
By 600 BCE, Carthage had ringed its peninsula with triple fortification walls stretching over 20 miles (32 km), with the landward wall reportedly 45 feet (14 m) high and 30 feet (9 m) thick.
Ancient sources claim the walls incorporated stables for 300 war elephants and 4,000 horses within the wall structure itself, though these numbers may be exaggerated.
The walls made Carthage one of the most heavily fortified cities in the ancient Mediterranean.
Only Tyre's island fortress was comparably defensible, and Tyre achieved its defense through geography rather than engineering.
The New City had outgrown its name.
By the late seventh century BCE, Carthage was the largest and wealthiest Phoenician city in the world, surpassing the homeland cities that had given it birth.
The colony had become the capital.
What it needed now was a military to match.

chapters.title: Masters of the Western Sea
sentences: Around 580 BCE, a general named Mago I (MAH-goh) seized power in Carthage and established what historians call the Magonid dynasty, the first ruling family to dominate Carthaginian politics for multiple generations.

The details of Mago's rise are murky (our sources are late and contradictory), but the effect was clear: Carthage transformed from a wealthy trading city into an aggressive military power that would contest Greek dominance across the central and western Mediterranean.
The transformation was driven by competition.

Greek colonists had been pouring into the western Mediterranean since the eighth century BCE, founding powerful city-states in Sicily (especially Syracuse, SIR-ah-kyooz, on the island's eastern coast, which would become the largest Greek city in the west) and southern Italy.
The Phoenicians and Greeks had coexisted for centuries, with the Phoenicians generally occupying the western end of islands and the African coast while the Greeks took the eastern end.
But by the sixth century, the coexistence was breaking down.
Both sides wanted the same harbors, the same trade routes, the same agricultural hinterlands.
Commercial competition was becoming military confrontation.
Before Mago, Carthage had relied on a citizen militia for defense, supplemented by mercenaries when needed.

Mago professionalized the army, built up the navy, and forged a critical alliance with the Etruscans (eh-TRUSS-kans; a powerful non-Greek civilization in central Italy, roughly modern Tuscany, which was itself being squeezed by Greek expansion in southern Italy).
The Carthaginian-Etruscan alliance reflected a shared interest: both powers wanted to stop the Greeks from dominating the western Mediterranean.
The alliance was tested at the Battle of Alalia (ah-LAH-lee-ah) around 535 BCE, fought off the coast of Corsica (the island northwest of Sardinia).

Greek colonists from Phocaea (foh-SEE-ah; an Ionian Greek city in Asia Minor whose people had been displaced by the Persian conquest) had established a settlement on Corsica and were raiding Carthaginian and Etruscan shipping.
A combined Carthaginian-Etruscan fleet of 120 ships clashed with the Phocaean Greeks.
The Greeks claimed a tactical victory (they held the water after the battle), but their losses were so severe, roughly two-thirds of their ships, that they abandoned Corsica entirely.
The aftermath divided the western Mediterranean into spheres of influence: Carthage took Sardinia, the Etruscans took Corsica, and Greek colonization west of Sicily was effectively blocked.
Alalia established the geopolitical framework that would hold for nearly three centuries.

An earlier military figure named Malchus (MAL-kuss; the name may actually be a title meaning "king," transliterated from Punic sources) appears in the fragmentary record before Mago, leading campaigns to secure Carthaginian positions in western Sicily and Sardinia.
The details are garbled.

What survives suggests a pattern familiar from Phoenician homeland politics (Phoenicia Ch 1-4): a general who was successful abroad, was exiled after a failure, and then marched on Carthage to seize power by force before being executed.
Whether Malchus was a real individual, a composite, or a title applied to multiple leaders is debated.

The episode, if it happened, demonstrates that Carthage's political system was already struggling with the tension between military commanders and civilian authority, a tension that would define Carthaginian politics for the rest of its history.
Mago's real legacy was strategic vision.
Under the Magonids, Carthage stopped thinking of itself as a trading city that occasionally had to fight and started thinking of itself as a military power that traded.

The navy was expanded and upgraded with triremes and pentaconters, the standard warships of the sixth century. (The quinquereme, KWIN-kwee-reem, the massive five-banked warship that would become Carthage's signature vessel, was a later development, probably adopted in the early fourth century BCE.) Punic naval construction was remarkably advanced: ships were built with prefabricated, numbered components that could be assembled rapidly in dockyards, a technique so efficient that later Roman sources claimed the Romans reverse-engineered a captured Carthaginian ship to build their own fleet (Chapter 4).
Carthaginian shipbuilding was, in effect, modular manufacturing applied to warships, over two thousand years before Henry Ford applied the principle to automobiles.
The Magonids also expanded Carthaginian territory beyond the sea.
Carthage took control of existing Phoenician colonies in Sardinia, exploiting the island's metal ores (copper, lead, silver) and strategic central Mediterranean position.
Sardinia would remain one of Carthage's most important overseas possessions for three centuries, held until Rome seized it after the First Punic War (Chapter 4).

In Iberia (the ancient name for the peninsula containing modern Spain and Portugal), Carthage gradually absorbed older Phoenician coastal settlements and pushed into the interior, drawn by the peninsula's vast silver, copper, and tin deposits.
The Iberian silver trade would eventually become the economic engine that powered Carthage's military machine in its final wars against Rome (Chapter 5).
The most remarkable Carthaginian achievements of the Magonid era were not military but exploratory.
Around 500 BCE (the exact date is uncertain), two extraordinary voyages departed from Carthage in opposite directions.

Hanno (HAH-noh), later called "the Navigator," led a fleet of sixty ships carrying, according to his own account, 30,000 men and women (a number that many scholars consider exaggerated) down the West African coast.
His mission was to found colonies along the Atlantic coast of Africa and to explore as far south as possible.
Hanno's account, known as the Periplus of Hanno (a periplus is a Greek word for a written sailing guide), survives in a Greek translation inscribed at the Temple of Baal Hammon in Carthage.
It describes a voyage that may have reached as far south as modern Cameroon or Gabon, roughly 3,000 miles (4,800 km) from Carthage.

Along the way, Hanno describes founding colonies along the Moroccan coast, encountering a volcanic eruption that lit up the night sky (possibly Mount Cameroon, one of the few active volcanoes in West Africa), and observing a group of hairy, aggressive creatures that his local interpreters called "Gorillae." These were probably chimpanzees or gorillas.

When Western scientists formally described the gorilla in 1847, they named it after Hanno's creatures, making the Carthaginian explorer the unwitting godfather of a zoological classification twenty-three centuries later.

Simultaneously, or perhaps slightly before, another Carthaginian navigator named Himilco (hih-MIL-koh) sailed north from Carthage, through the Strait of Gibraltar, and up the Atlantic coast of Europe.
His voyage reached the tin-producing regions of Brittany or possibly Britain, establishing the northern leg of Carthage's Atlantic trade network.
Tin was essential for making bronze (an alloy of copper and tin), and the Atlantic tin routes were so commercially valuable that Carthage guarded them as state secrets.
One ancient source claims a Carthaginian captain deliberately ran his ship aground rather than let a trailing Roman vessel discover the route to the tin sources.
If true, the captain was reimbursed by the Carthaginian state for his sacrifice.
Trade secrets were literally worth dying for.
These two voyages, Hanno's south into tropical Africa and Himilco's north into the cold Atlantic, represent the most ambitious exploration program of the ancient world.
No other Mediterranean civilization was sending ships into the open Atlantic at this date.
The Greeks, for all their intellectual curiosity, were coastal sailors who rarely ventured beyond the Mediterranean basin.
The Egyptians had retreated from maritime activity centuries earlier.
The Persians were a land empire.
In 500 BCE, the Carthaginians were the only people in the Western world exploring waters that no one else would revisit for a thousand years.
Back in the Mediterranean, Carthage was building the diplomatic infrastructure of a regional power.
In 509 BCE, Carthage and the young Roman Republic (which had just overthrown its last king and established republican government) signed their first commercial treaty.
The text, preserved by Polybius, is revealing.

It defines spheres of influence: Rome acknowledged Carthaginian dominance over the western Mediterranean sea lanes and much of North Africa, while Carthage recognized Roman authority in Latium (the small region around Rome in central Italy).
The treaty reveals how early and how clearly Carthage saw Rome: as a minor regional power, worth dealing with but not worth worrying about.
Rome, in 509 BCE, controlled roughly 300 square miles (780 sq km) of central Italian farmland.
Carthage controlled the western Mediterranean.
The power imbalance was so extreme that the treaty was less an agreement between equals and more an acknowledgment that Rome existed.
It would take 250 years for the balance to shift, and when it did, the result would be catastrophic.

The last great Magonid ruler, Hamilcar I (HAM-il-kar; not the later Hamilcar Barca of the Second Punic War, an important distinction since the name recycled through Carthaginian aristocratic families like a family heirloom), spent his reign expanding Carthaginian territory in Sicily and Sardinia, building the military machine that his dynasty had begun to assemble.
Hamilcar was preparing for a confrontation with the Greeks of eastern Sicily, a confrontation that both sides knew was coming.
When it came, in 480 BCE, it would end in disaster for Carthage.

But the catastrophe would also force Carthage to reinvent itself, and the civilization that emerged from the wreckage would be more powerful, more sophisticated, and more dangerous than the one that went in.

chapters.title: The African Empire
sentences: In 480 BCE, the same year that the combined Greek fleet destroyed the Persian navy at Salamis (SAL-ah-miss) and saved Greece from Persian conquest (Persian Empire Ch 4), the Carthaginians suffered their own catastrophe on the other end of the Mediterranean.
Hamilcar I led a massive invasion force across the sea to Himera (hih-MAIR-ah), a Greek city on the northern coast of Sicily.

The exact size of his army is debated (ancient sources give absurd numbers like 300,000, which is logistically impossible for a premodern overseas expedition), but it was clearly the largest force Carthage had ever assembled.
The Greek tyrant Gelon of Syracuse (JEL-on), who had unified much of eastern Sicily under his rule, met the Carthaginians with a combined Sicilian Greek army.
The Battle of Himera was a catastrophe for Carthage.
Hamilcar's army was routed.
Hamilcar himself was killed.
Greek sources disagree on how: some say he fell in the fighting, others that he threw himself into the sacrificial fire he had been tending throughout the battle when he saw the day was lost.
The detail about the fire is colorful enough to be suspicious (it conveniently matches Greek stereotypes about Carthaginian religious fanaticism), but the outcome is not disputed.
Carthage lost its army, its king, and its ambitions in eastern Sicily in a single afternoon.
Ancient Greek writers drew an explicit parallel between Himera and Salamis, both fought in 480 BCE.
Some even placed both battles on the same day, though this is almost certainly a literary device rather than historical fact.

The parallel served Greek propaganda: the message was that "civilization" (Greek, naturally) had defeated "barbarism" (Persian in the east, Carthaginian in the west) simultaneously, as if by divine coordination.
The Carthaginians, who would have told the story rather differently, left no surviving version.
The defeat at Himera knocked Carthage out of Sicilian military politics for seventy years.
But unlike most ancient powers that suffered catastrophic military defeats, Carthage did not collapse.
It pivoted.
And the pivot produced the most remarkable century in Carthaginian history.
After Himera, Carthage reformed its government.
The Magonid dynasty did not survive the disaster.

In its place, the Carthaginians developed a mixed constitutional system centered on two annually elected chief magistrates called suffetes (soo-FEET-eez; from the Punic word shofet, meaning "judge," the same word used for the biblical Judges of Israel).
The suffetes served as the senior civil officials, roughly equivalent to the two consuls of the Roman Republic.

Below them sat a Council of Elders (probably called the adirim, similar to a senate), and below that, a popular assembly of citizens that had the power to ratify decisions and, in some circumstances, override the council.
This system impressed the Greek philosopher Aristotle (AIR-ih-STAH-tul; 384-322 BCE, the Athenian philosopher whose Politics is the most systematic analysis of government in ancient literature).
In his Politics, Aristotle ranked Carthage's constitution alongside Sparta and Crete as one of the best-governed states in the Mediterranean world.
He noted that Carthage had avoided both tyranny (one-man rule) and popular revolution despite its enormous wealth, a remarkable achievement for any ancient city.
The compliment is doubly striking because Aristotle was Greek, and Greeks did not normally praise "barbarian" (non-Greek) governments.

For Aristotle to rank Carthage alongside Sparta was the equivalent of a Cold War American professor praising the Soviet economic system: a grudging acknowledgment of competence across an ideological divide.
The constitutional system was not democratic in the modern sense.
Real power rested with wealthy aristocratic families who controlled the Council of Elders and used their wealth to influence the popular assembly.
Suffetes were drawn from the same elite families.
Military commands went to aristocrats.
But the system did include genuine checks on power.

When a general named Bomilcar (BOM-il-kar) attempted to seize power as tyrant during a military crisis in 308 BCE, marching troops through the streets of Carthage, the coup failed and Bomilcar was crucified in the public marketplace.
Carthage's oligarchic republic violently resisted one-man rule.
The generals served the state; the state did not serve the generals.
This principle held for centuries, right up until the Barcid family (Chapter 5) began to strain it.
Blocked from eastward expansion in Sicily, post-Himera Carthage turned its energy inward and southward, building what historians call the Carthaginian African empire.

Over the course of the fifth and fourth centuries BCE, Carthage conquered vast stretches of the Libyan interior (in ancient usage, "Libya" meant most of North Africa west of Egypt, not just the modern country).
Carthaginian agricultural estates spread across the fertile Cap Bon peninsula and the Bagradas Valley (bah-GRAH-dahs; modern Medjerda River valley), which became the breadbasket of the Punic world.

These estates, worked by Libyan tenant farmers and, increasingly, by slave labor, produced enormous grain surpluses that made Carthage one of the ancient world's great agricultural exporters alongside Egypt and Sicily.

A Carthaginian writer named Mago (MAH-goh; not the dynasty founder from Chapter 2, a different Mago entirely since the name was as common in Carthage as "John" is in English) produced a monumental 28-volume treatise on agriculture around 450 BCE.
Mago's work covered viticulture (grape growing), olive cultivation, livestock management, estate organization, and what we would now call agricultural science.
The treatise was so respected across the Mediterranean that when Rome finally destroyed Carthage in 146 BCE (Chapter 8), the Roman Senate ordered Mago's work translated into Latin.
It was the only Carthaginian book Rome deliberately saved.
Think about that: an entire civilization's written heritage was destroyed or dispersed, and the one thing Rome preserved was a farming manual.
Mago's treatise influenced Roman agricultural writers for centuries.

Fragments survive in quotations by later Latin authors, and they reveal a level of agricultural sophistication (soil analysis, grafting techniques, irrigation management, livestock breeding) that was far more systematic than anything in the contemporary Greek literature.
The Carthaginians were better farmers than the Greeks, and Rome knew it.
The African empire also reached southward, into the Saharan trade routes.
