Carthaginian merchants conducted what Greek writers called "silent trade" with peoples beyond the Sahara: goods were laid out on a beach or riverside, one side withdrew, the other approached and laid out their counter-offering, and the process repeated until both sides were satisfied.
Neither party spoke the other's language.
Neither party trusted the other enough to meet face-to-face.
But the trade worked, because both sides wanted what the other had.
The Carthaginians offered Mediterranean manufactured goods (metalwork, glass, textiles, salt).
In return, they received gold dust from the auriferous regions of West Africa.
This trans-Saharan gold trade predated the famous medieval trans-Saharan caravan routes by nearly a millennium and made Carthage one of the ancient Mediterranean's primary gold markets.
The Sicilian wars did not end permanently after Himera.
They simmered.
In 409 BCE, Hamilcar I's grandson Hannibal Mago (not the famous Hannibal of the Alps, but an earlier bearer of the name) launched a revenge expedition that sacked and razed the city of Himera itself.
It was a vendetta seventy years in the making.
Ancient sources report that 3,000 Greek prisoners were dragged to the spot where Hamilcar had died and executed as a sacrificial offering.
Whether this actually happened or whether the Greek sources are projecting their own assumptions about Carthaginian religious brutality onto a military atrocity is impossible to say.
Either way, Himera was destroyed, and Carthage was back in Sicily.
The next century saw intermittent warfare between Carthage and the Greek city-states of Sicily, a grinding cycle of invasion, siege, plague, retreat, and uneasy truce.

In 397 BCE, a Carthaginian general named Himilco (hih-MIL-koh; not the Atlantic explorer from Chapter 2) besieged Syracuse itself, the greatest Greek city in the western Mediterranean, but plague devastated his army and he retreated in disgrace and committed suicide.

Meanwhile, the general Hanno the Great (a different Hanno from the Atlantic explorer) expanded Carthaginian control deep into the Libyan interior, consolidating the African territorial empire that would sustain Carthage's wealth and manpower for the next century and a half.
In 348 BCE, Carthage and Rome renewed their commercial treaty, tightening Carthaginian trade monopolies in the western Mediterranean.
The second treaty banned Roman merchants from Africa and Sardinia entirely, while permitting limited trade in Sicily.
Polybius preserved the text, noting that even Roman scholars of his own day could barely read the archaic Latin of the original inscription.
The treaty's increasingly restrictive terms reveal Carthage's growing confidence: the commercial republic was drawing its sphere of influence tighter.

In 341 BCE, the Corinthian general Timoleon (tih-MOH-lee-on) crushed a Carthaginian army at the Battle of the Crimissus (krih-MISS-us) River, killing an estimated 10,000 members of Carthage's elite citizen infantry, the Sacred Band (the only military unit composed entirely of Carthaginian citizens from aristocratic families, roughly 2,500-3,000 strong; the rest of the army was mercenaries and allied contingents).
It was the worst single-day loss of citizen soldiers in Carthaginian history.
In 310 BCE, the Syracusan tyrant Agathocles (ah-GATH-oh-kleez) did something that nobody had ever done before: he invaded Africa.
Blockaded in Syracuse by a Carthaginian fleet, Agathocles loaded his army onto ships, slipped past the blockade, sailed to North Africa, and ravaged Carthaginian territory for three years.
He ultimately failed to take Carthage itself and had to withdraw, but he proved a crucial point: Carthage's African heartland was not invulnerable.
An enemy who was bold enough to take the war to Africa could threaten the city directly.
It was a lesson that a Roman general named Scipio would remember a century later (Chapter 6).
Through all this fighting, Carthage was building the urban infrastructure that would make it one of the greatest cities in the ancient world.
The crown jewel was the Cothon (KOH-thon), the great double harbor, probably completed in its final form around 400 BCE.
The harbor complex was unlike anything else in the ancient Mediterranean.
The outer harbor was rectangular, open to the sea, and large enough to accommodate hundreds of merchant ships loading and unloading cargo.
Behind it, connected by a narrow defensible channel, was the circular military harbor, an engineering marvel.

The military Cothon was a perfect circle of water surrounded by a ring of individual covered ship sheds, each one sized to house a single warship, arranged around a central island from which the admiralty could command operations.
The capacity was staggering: 220 warships could be sheltered simultaneously, each in its own berth, protected from the weather and hidden from the eyes of approaching ships.
No ancient city had anything comparable.
The closest parallel might be the arsenals of Venice two thousand years later.
Kerkouane (ker-koo-AHN; on the Cap Bon peninsula, about 40 miles or 65 km east of Carthage) is the only intact Punic city ever found.
Every other Carthaginian urban site was either destroyed by Rome and rebuilt or has been continuously inhabited for two millennia, burying the Punic layers under centuries of later construction.
Kerkouane was abandoned in the mid-third century BCE, probably during the First Punic War (Chapter 4), and was never reoccupied.

When archaeologists excavated it in the 1950s, they found a remarkably complete snapshot of everyday Punic urban life: gridded streets, standardized house lots with interior courtyards, indoor bathing rooms with terracotta tubs, mosaic floors with the distinctive Tanit symbol, a dedicated industrial quarter for purple dye production (complete with heaps of crushed murex shells), and a sophisticated drainage system.
The level of domestic comfort surprised archaeologists who had expected something cruder.
Kerkouane is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and it remains our best window into how ordinary Carthaginians actually lived, as opposed to how Greek and Roman writers described them.

By the mid-third century BCE, Carthage's population may have reached 300,000 to 400,000 at its peak (estimates vary widely, but even conservative figures place it among the largest cities in the ancient world, rivaling Alexandria and approaching Rome).
Byrsa Hill was packed with residential quarters where buildings rose six stories high, crammed together in a density that ancient visitors found astonishing.

The city had water infrastructure (cisterns and channeled springs), massive granaries, the double harbor, the Tophet sanctuary, temples to Tanit and Baal Hammon and Eshmun, workshops producing metalwork, glass, textiles, and pottery, and those enormous triple walls with their built-in stables for war elephants.
It was, by any measure, a great city.
And it was about to collide with the only power in the Mediterranean that could destroy it.

chapters.title: Rome
sentences: In 264 BCE, a minor dispute over the Sicilian city of Messana (meh-SAH-nah; modern Messina, at the northeastern tip of Sicily, controlling the narrow strait between Sicily and the Italian mainland) escalated into a war that neither Carthage nor Rome had planned and that neither could have imagined would last 23 years, destroy hundreds of ships, kill hundreds of thousands of men, and permanently alter the balance of power in the Mediterranean world.
The First Punic War (264-241 BCE) was the longest continuous military conflict in ancient history to that point.
The Romans called the Carthaginians Poeni (from the Greek Phoinikes, "Phoenicians"), which is why we call these conflicts the "Punic" Wars.
The war began because a group of Italian mercenaries called the Mamertines (MAM-er-teenz), who had seized Messana and were being squeezed by Syracuse, appealed to both Carthage and Rome for help.
Both intervened.
Their forces collided.
And a local Sicilian squabble became a Mediterranean war.
The fundamental problem for both sides was geographic.
Sicily is an island.
Carthage controlled the western half and its surrounding waters.
Rome controlled most of the Italian peninsula.
To fight in Sicily, Rome needed to cross the Strait of Messina and supply its armies by sea.
To defend Sicily, Carthage needed to maintain naval superiority.
The war was therefore, inevitably, a naval war.
And in 264 BCE, Rome had no navy.
Carthage had the best navy in the western Mediterranean.
Its quinqueremes (five-banked warships crewed by experienced Punic sailors) had dominated western Mediterranean waters for generations.
By any rational calculation, a naval war between a landlocked Italian city-state and the greatest maritime power in the ancient world should have been over quickly.
But the Romans were, as they would demonstrate repeatedly over the next century, constitutionally incapable of accepting defeat.
The early fighting was on land, in Sicily.

In 262 BCE, Rome besieged Carthaginian-held Agrigentum (ah-GRIH-jen-tum; one of the wealthiest Greek cities in Sicily, on the island's southern coast) for seven months, finally storming the city and selling 25,000 of its inhabitants into slavery.
The siege was brutal and expensive, but it taught Rome a critical lesson: controlling Sicilian cities was pointless as long as Carthage could resupply and reinforce them by sea.
Rome needed a navy.
According to Polybius, the Romans captured a beached Carthaginian quinquereme, reverse-engineered its design, and built a fleet of over 100 warships in sixty days.

The story may be embellished (Rome probably had some naval experience through its Greek-allied cities in southern Italy), but the core claim is plausible: Rome built a fleet from scratch using Carthaginian designs as a model.
Then, with characteristic Roman practicality, they added an innovation that neutralized Carthaginian seamanship.
The corvus (KOR-vuss; literally "crow") was a boarding bridge, roughly 36 feet (11 m) long and 4 feet (1.2 m) wide, with a heavy iron spike at the far end.
The bridge was mounted on a pivot at the bow of a Roman ship.
When a Roman vessel came alongside an enemy ship, the corvus was released.
It swung down, the spike punched through the enemy's deck, locking the two ships together, and Roman infantry charged across the bridge to fight hand-to-hand on the enemy's deck.
The corvus turned naval battles into infantry battles fought at sea.
Roman soldiers were the best infantry in the Mediterranean.
Carthaginian sailors were the best sailors.
By forcing every engagement into a boarding action, the corvus negated everything the Carthaginians were good at and leveraged everything the Romans were good at.
It was crude, it made the ships top-heavy and dangerous in rough seas, and it was devastatingly effective.
Rome's first major naval victory came at the Battle of Mylae (MY-lay) in 260 BCE, where the corvus destroyed 50 Carthaginian ships.

Then at the Battle of Cape Ecnomus (ek-NOH-mus) in 256 BCE, one of the largest naval engagements in ancient history (over 600 ships and perhaps 300,000 men on both sides), Rome's victory opened the sea route to Africa.
Rome attempted to end the war quickly by invading North Africa directly.
The consul Regulus (REG-yoo-lus) landed with a Roman army and initially won victories on Carthaginian soil.

But Carthage responded by hiring a Spartan mercenary general named Xanthippus (zan-THIP-us), who reorganized the Punic army, deployed war elephants and Numidian cavalry effectively, and routed the Roman invasion force at the Battle of Tunis (or Bagradas) in 255 BCE.
Regulus was captured.
The Roman survivors were evacuated by sea, and then a storm destroyed most of the Roman fleet on the way home.
Rome lost over 100,000 men to shipwrecks alone during the First Punic War, more than it lost in battle.
The Mediterranean did not care about the corvus.
The war ground on for another fourteen years.
Both sides built and lost fleets.
Both sides won and lost battles.
Sicily changed hands repeatedly.
The decisive moment came from a single Carthaginian general.

Hamilcar Barca (HAM-il-kar BAR-kah; "Barca" meaning "lightning," a surname earned in battle) seized Mount Eryx (EH-riks) in western Sicily in 247 BCE and waged a brilliant six-year guerrilla campaign against Roman forces with minimal support from Carthage.

Operating from mountaintop strongholds with a small, mobile force, Hamilcar raided Roman supply lines, ambushed Roman detachments, and made Roman control of western Sicily a continuous expensive headache.
He was never defeated in the field.
But he could not win the war alone.
The end came at sea.

In 241 BCE, the Romans (who had by now rebuilt their fleet yet again, this time funded by voluntary loans from wealthy Roman citizens after the state treasury was exhausted) won a decisive naval victory at the Battle of the Aegates Islands (eh-GAH-tees), off the western tip of Sicily.
The last Carthaginian fleet was destroyed.
Carthage sued for peace.
The terms were harsh.
Carthage surrendered Sicily entirely.
It agreed to pay a war indemnity of 3,200 talents (roughly 80 tons of silver) over ten years.
It agreed not to attack Syracuse or its allies.
The war that had begun over a squabble in Messana ended with Carthage losing its most important overseas possession and going bankrupt.
But the worst was yet to come.

Immediately after the war, Carthage's unpaid mercenaries (the polyglot army of Libyans, Numidians, Iberians, Balearic slingers, and assorted adventurers who did the actual fighting since Carthaginian citizens rarely served in the infantry) revolted.
The Mercenary War (241-237 BCE), which Polybius called the "Truceless War" for its extraordinary savagery, nearly destroyed the Carthaginian state.
Atrocities were committed by both sides that shocked even ancient observers accustomed to brutal warfare.

Hamilcar Barca crushed the revolt through a campaign of divide-and-conquer, exploiting ethnic divisions among the mercenaries and using the terrain of the North African hinterland to trap and destroy rebel forces piecemeal.
His performance cemented his political dominance and his reputation as the most capable military leader in Carthage.
Then Rome struck again.

While Carthage was consumed by the Mercenary War, Rome seized Sardinia and Corsica in 238 BCE, in what even the pro-Roman historian Polybius called an act of naked opportunism with no legitimate justification.
Carthage, still fighting for survival against its own mercenaries, could not resist.
Rome demanded an additional 1,200 talents on top of the existing war indemnity.
Carthage paid.
The humiliation was complete.
But Hamilcar Barca was already thinking about the next war.
He had a plan to rebuild Carthaginian power, and it began with Spain.

chapters.title: The Barcid Project
sentences: In 237 BCE, Hamilcar Barca loaded his army onto ships and crossed to Iberia (the peninsula containing modern Spain and Portugal).
He brought with him his nine-year-old son, Hannibal.

Before they left, according to a story that multiple ancient sources repeat with minor variations, Hamilcar took the boy to the Temple of Baal Hammon and made him swear an oath at the altar: eternal enmity against Rome.
Whether the oath actually happened or is a legend constructed after the fact to explain Hannibal's single-minded lifelong vendetta, the story captures something true about the Barcid family project.
This was not standard imperial expansion.
This was a revenge operation, designed from the beginning to create the military and financial resources needed for a second war against Rome.
Hamilcar spent nine years conquering much of the Iberian Peninsula's southern and eastern coast.
He subdued local Iberian and Celtiberian tribes through a combination of military force, strategic marriage alliances, and the simple fact that Carthaginian silver bought a lot of loyalty.
The key resource was silver.
The Iberian Peninsula sat atop some of the richest silver deposits in the ancient world, and Hamilcar seized the mines with the systematic focus of a mining executive.
The silver funded everything: the army's wages, the construction of new cities, and, crucially, the payoff of the remaining Roman war indemnities that were draining the Carthaginian treasury.
The strategy was clear, even if the Carthaginian Senate back home did not fully appreciate its implications.

Hamilcar was building a private army, funded by Spanish silver, loyal to the Barca family rather than to the Carthaginian state, in a territory thousands of miles from the oligarchs who controlled Carthaginian domestic politics.

It was, in effect, a state within a state: a Barcid military kingdom on the Iberian Peninsula, connected to Carthage by religion and language and nominal political allegiance, but operationally independent.
Hamilcar died in 228 BCE, either killed in battle or drowned crossing a river during a retreat (the sources disagree).

Command passed to his son-in-law, Hasdrubal the Fair (has-DROO-bal; yet another recycled Carthaginian name, not to be confused with Hannibal's brother, also named Hasdrubal, who will appear in Chapter 6).
Hasdrubal the Fair governed Iberia through diplomacy more than warfare.
His most significant achievement was founding Carthago Nova (kar-TAH-goh NOH-vah; "New New City," modern Cartagena on Spain's southeastern coast), which became the Barcid capital in Iberia.

The location was chosen with the same strategic eye that had guided the founding of Carthage itself: Carthago Nova had an excellent harbor, a defensible position on a peninsula, and, most importantly, it sat beside the richest silver mines in the western Mediterranean.
The silver output was staggering.
Ancient sources claim the mines near Carthago Nova yielded 300 pounds of silver daily, employing 40,000 workers.
Even if these figures are inflated (as ancient production statistics usually are), the scale was enormous.
The silver financed a professional army that grew steadily larger, better equipped, and more experienced.

By the time Hannibal inherited command, the Barcid army in Spain was arguably the most formidable military force in the western Mediterranean: a polyglot professional army of Iberian heavy infantry, Numidian cavalry, Balearic slingers, Libyan spearmen, and Carthaginian officers, bound together by Barcid pay, Barcid charisma, and a decade of shared campaigning.
Hasdrubal also negotiated the Ebro Treaty with Rome, establishing the Ebro River (in northeastern Spain) as the boundary between Roman and Carthaginian spheres of influence.
The treaty reflected Rome's growing nervousness about Barcid expansion.
Rome was not yet ready for another war with Carthage, and the Ebro Treaty was designed to prevent one by drawing a clear line.
Everything south of the Ebro was Carthaginian.
Everything north was off-limits.
The treaty worked as long as both sides respected it.
Hasdrubal was assassinated by a Celtic slave in 221 BCE (the motives are unclear; personal vengeance is the most common explanation in the sources).

The Barcid army in Spain, acting with the speed and decisiveness of a military organization rather than a constitutional government, acclaimed the twenty-five-year-old Hannibal Barca as their commander.
The Carthaginian Senate back home, presented with a fait accompli by an army that was effectively loyal to the Barcid family, reluctantly confirmed the appointment.

Hannibal Barca is one of the most famous military commanders in human history, ranked alongside Alexander the Great, Julius Caesar, and Napoleon by virtually every military historian who has attempted the exercise.
Ancient sources are nearly unanimous: even Roman writers who hated everything Carthage stood for acknowledged Hannibal's genius.

Livy, as patriotic a Roman as ever lived, described Hannibal as possessing "extreme daring in braving dangers" and "supreme skill in turning them to his account." Polybius, the most sober and analytical ancient historian, considered Hannibal the finest tactician the Mediterranean world had produced.
The young commander moved quickly.
In 219 BCE, Hannibal besieged and destroyed Saguntum (sah-GOON-tum), a city on the eastern Spanish coast that was allied with Rome.
Saguntum was south of the Ebro River, technically within the Carthaginian sphere, but its alliance with Rome created an ambiguity that Hannibal deliberately exploited.
The siege lasted eight months.
The Saguntines resisted with desperate courage (the city's population reportedly burned their valuables and many killed themselves rather than surrender), but Hannibal took the city and sacked it.
Rome demanded that Carthage hand Hannibal over for punishment.
The Carthaginian Senate refused.
According to Polybius, the Roman envoy held up the fold of his toga and told the Carthaginian senators that it contained either peace or war, and they could choose which fell out.
The Carthaginians told him to let fall whichever he pleased.
He let fall war.
Hannibal had planned for this.
He had been planning for it since he was nine years old at his father's altar.
What he did next is the reason military academies exist.
Rather than wait for Rome to bring the war to Spain or Africa, where Carthage had always fought its wars and always lost, Hannibal decided to do what nobody expected: take the war to Italy itself.
He would march his army overland from Spain, across southern France, over the Alps, and into the heart of Roman territory.
He would defeat Rome on its own soil, peel away Rome's Italian allies, and destroy the Roman confederation from the inside.
The plan was audacious to the point of insanity.
The overland route was roughly 1,000 miles (1,600 km).

It required crossing the Pyrenees, traversing hostile Gallic territory, fording the Rhône River, and then climbing over the highest mountain range in Western Europe in late autumn with an army that included 37 war elephants.
No one had ever attempted anything remotely comparable.
Hannibal did it.

chapters.title: The War That Changed Everything
sentences: In late October 218 BCE, Hannibal Barca led approximately 50,000 infantry, 9,000 cavalry, and 37 war elephants over the Alps.
The crossing took about fifteen days.
It was a nightmare.

The specific pass Hannibal used has been debated for two thousand years and remains unresolved (the most commonly cited candidates are the Col de Clapier, the Col du Mont Cenis, and the Col de la Traversette, all in the western Alps between modern France and Italy).
What is not debated is the cost.
Rockslides smashed through the column.
Hostile mountain tribes attacked from above, rolling boulders down onto the marching army and raiding the baggage train.
Early snowfall made the trails treacherous.
The elephants, African forest elephants that had never experienced cold weather, slipped on ice and fell into ravines.
Men died of exposure, exhaustion, and the simple catastrophe of trying to move a Mediterranean army through alpine terrain in late autumn.
When Hannibal reached the Po Valley of northern Italy, he had roughly 26,000 infantry, 9,000 cavalry (the cavalry fared better than the infantry), and a handful of surviving elephants.
He had lost nearly half his army without fighting a single battle.
The Romans, who had expected to fight Hannibal in Spain or at worst in southern France, were stunned to discover a Carthaginian army in Italy.
Hannibal's first priority was to rebuild his forces by recruiting among the Gauls (Celtic peoples of northern Italy who had been recently conquered by Rome and were eager for revenge).
Thousands of Gallic warriors joined him.
Then he began dismantling Roman armies with a methodical brilliance that still astonishes military historians.

At the Battle of the Trebia (TREH-bee-ah) in December 218 BCE, Hannibal lured a Roman army across an icy river on a freezing morning, then hit them with a cavalry charge and a concealed force of 2,000 men under his brother Mago (placed behind Roman lines the night before in a hidden ravine).
The Romans, cold, hungry, and outnumbered in cavalry, were destroyed.
Two-thirds of the Roman army was killed or captured.
At the Battle of Lake Trasimene (trah-sih-MEE-nee) in June 217 BCE, Hannibal executed the largest successful ambush in military history.
A Roman army under the consul Flaminius (flah-MIN-ee-us), marching along the northern shore of the lake through a morning fog, walked directly into a trap.
Hannibal had concealed his entire army in the wooded hills above the road.
When the Roman column was strung out along the lakeshore, Hannibal attacked from every direction simultaneously.
The Romans were pushed into the lake or cut down on the road.
Roughly 15,000 were killed, including Flaminius himself, and another 15,000 were captured.
The survivors who tried to swim the lake drowned in their armor.
It was not a battle.
It was an annihilation.
Rome responded by appointing Quintus Fabius Maximus (FAB-ee-us MAX-ih-mus) as dictator (a constitutional emergency office in the Roman Republic, not the modern sense of the word).
Fabius adopted a strategy that became known as the Fabian strategy: avoid pitched battles, shadow Hannibal's army, raid his supply lines, and wait for him to exhaust himself.
The strategy was militarily sound but politically unpopular.
Romans considered it cowardly.
Fabius was mocked as "the Delayer" (Cunctator).

When Fabius's term expired, the Roman Senate, impatient for a decisive victory, raised the largest army Rome had ever assembled: roughly 80,000 infantry and 6,000 cavalry, commanded by two consuls, Lucius Aemilius Paullus (ay-MIL-ee-us PAWL-us) and Gaius Terentius Varro (VAIR-oh).
The plan was simple: overwhelm Hannibal with numbers.
The two consuls alternated command daily.
On August 2, 216 BCE, on Varro's command day, the Roman army met Hannibal on a flat plain near the village of Cannae (KAN-eye), in southeastern Italy.
The Battle of Cannae is the most studied tactical engagement in military history.
It is still taught at West Point, Sandhurst, Saint-Cyr, and every other military academy in the world.
What Hannibal did on that August afternoon has been called the gold standard of tactical annihilation, and no one has ever improved on it.
Hannibal had roughly 50,000 men (some estimates say fewer).
The Romans had roughly 80,000 (some estimates say more).
The Romans massed their heavy infantry in a deep formation in the center, planning to punch through Hannibal's line by sheer weight of numbers.
Hannibal's response was the double envelopment.
He placed his weakest infantry (Gauls and Iberians) in the center of his line, arranged in a convex bulge facing the Romans.
On the flanks, he placed his strongest troops: veteran Libyan heavy infantry, equipped with Roman weapons and armor scavenged from previous victories.
On both wings, he deployed his cavalry: Numidian light cavalry on one wing, Iberian and Gallic heavy cavalry on the other.
The battle began.
The Roman infantry advanced into Hannibal's center.
The Gallic and Iberian troops gave ground, slowly, deliberately, bending backward under the Roman pressure.
The convex bulge flattened, then became concave, drawing the Roman infantry deeper and deeper into what felt like a collapsing enemy line.
The Romans pressed forward eagerly.
They were winning.
The Carthaginian center was breaking.
It was not breaking.
It was bending.
And while the Roman infantry pushed deeper into the trap, the Carthaginian cavalry on both wings was winning its own battle.
The Iberian and Gallic heavy cavalry routed the Roman cavalry on one wing.
The Numidian cavalry drove off the Roman horsemen on the other.
Then both cavalry forces swung around behind the Roman infantry and sealed the circle.
The Roman army, 80,000 strong, was now completely surrounded.
The Libyan heavy infantry on the flanks wheeled inward, compressing the Romans from both sides.
The cavalry closed the rear.
The Romans were packed so tightly that they could not swing their swords.
They could not maneuver, could not retreat, could not form new lines.
Ancient sources describe men being crushed to death by the pressure of bodies around them.
The killing went on for hours.
By the end of the afternoon, somewhere between 50,000 and 70,000 Roman soldiers were dead.
The consul Aemilius Paullus was killed.
Eighty senators, roughly a quarter of the Roman Senate, died on the field.
It was the worst single-day military disaster in Roman history, and arguably the worst single-day battlefield loss of any Western power until the first day of the Somme in 1916 CE.
Cannae should have ended the war.
In the normal pattern of ancient Mediterranean warfare, a defeat of this magnitude forced the loser to negotiate.
Hannibal expected Rome's Italian allies, shocked by the annihilation of the largest army Rome had ever assembled, to defect.
The important southern Italian city of Capua (KAH-poo-ah) switched to Hannibal's side.
Several Greek cities in southern Italy followed.
Syracuse, the great Greek city in Sicily, defected from Rome to Carthage.
But most of Rome's allies stayed loyal.
And Rome, in the most psychologically remarkable response in ancient military history, simply refused to negotiate.
The Roman Senate forbade even the discussion of peace terms.
They raised new armies.
They adopted the Fabian strategy that they had mocked before Cannae.

They fought Hannibal's allies one by one, besieging defecting cities, punishing defectors with exemplary brutality, and systematically isolating Hannibal in southern Italy without giving him another pitched battle.
Hannibal's strategic problem became clear.
He had won every battle.
He had destroyed three Roman armies.
He had killed perhaps 100,000 Roman soldiers in two years.
And he had not won the war.
Italy was too large, too full of fortified cities, and too politically complex for even the greatest field commander to conquer with a single expeditionary army.
Hannibal needed reinforcements, either from Carthage (which the Senate was reluctant to send, partly from political jealousy of the Barcid family) or from his brother Hasdrubal in Spain.
Neither came in time.
In 207 BCE, Hannibal's brother Hasdrubal Barca finally crossed the Alps with a relief army, following roughly the same route Hannibal had used eleven years earlier.
But the Romans intercepted Hasdrubal before he could link up with Hannibal.
At the Battle of the Metaurus (meh-TAW-rus) River, the Roman consul Gaius Claudius Nero (not the later emperor) destroyed Hasdrubal's army and killed Hasdrubal himself.
The Romans catapulted Hasdrubal's severed head into Hannibal's camp.
Ancient sources report that Hannibal, looking at his brother's face, said he now saw the fate of Carthage.
It was reportedly the first time anyone had seen Hannibal weep.

The war turned decisively when a young Roman general named Publius Cornelius Scipio (SKIP-ee-oh; later called Scipio Africanus), who had studied Hannibal's tactics with the dedication of a doctoral student, took command of the Roman forces in Spain.
Scipio was in his mid-twenties, had survived Cannae as a teenager, and possessed a combination of tactical brilliance and personal charisma that made him Hannibal's first genuine Roman equal.
He conquered Carthaginian Spain in a series of campaigns between 210 and 206 BCE, cutting off Hannibal's reinforcements and his Spanish silver revenue.
Then, in 204 BCE, Scipio did what Agathocles had done a century earlier (Chapter 3) and what Hannibal's own strategy had always been vulnerable to: he invaded Africa.
Landing in North Africa with a veteran army, Scipio threatened Carthage directly.
The Carthaginian Senate, panicking, recalled Hannibal from Italy to defend the homeland.
After fifteen years of undefeated campaigning on Italian soil, Hannibal sailed home.
The two greatest generals of the ancient world met at the Battle of Zama (ZAH-mah) in 202 BCE, fought roughly 75 miles (120 km) southwest of Carthage.

Hannibal had a larger army but inferior cavalry, because the Numidian horsemen (North African riders who fought without saddles or bridles, the finest light cavalry in the ancient world; Chapter 7) had switched their allegiance from Carthage to Rome.
Scipio used the Numidian cavalry exactly as Hannibal had used them at Cannae: to rout the enemy cavalry, swing around behind the enemy infantry, and complete the encirclement.
The student used the master's own technique against him.
Hannibal was decisively defeated for the first and only time in his career.
The peace terms ended Carthage as a great power.
Carthage surrendered its entire fleet except ten warships.
It gave up all overseas territories.
It agreed to pay an indemnity of 10,000 talents of silver over fifty years (roughly 260 tons, more than three times the First Punic War indemnity).
And it accepted the provision that would ultimately seal its fate: Carthage could never again wage war without Roman permission.
Hannibal served briefly as a suffete in post-war Carthage, reforming the city's finances and rooting out corruption.
But Roman pressure forced him into exile in 195 BCE.
He spent his remaining years as a military adviser to various eastern Mediterranean kings threatened by Rome.
When Roman agents finally cornered him in Bithynia (bih-THIN-ee-ah; a kingdom in northwestern Anatolia) in 183 BCE, he took poison rather than be captured.

His reported last words: "Let us now put an end to the great anxiety of the Romans, who have thought it too long to wait for the death of a hated old man." Scipio Africanus, ironically, died in the same year, also embittered and politically marginalized by the Roman system he had saved.

chapters.title: How the Carthaginians Lived
sentences: The previous chapters told the story of Carthage's wars.
This chapter tells the story of the civilization that fought them.

Because Punic literature was destroyed, most of what we know about Carthaginian daily life comes from archaeology, from scattered references in Greek and Roman texts, and from comparative analysis with the Phoenician homeland cities that Carthage descended from (Phoenicia Ch 1-5).
The picture is incomplete, but it is more detailed than you might expect from a civilization whose enemies burned its libraries.
Carthaginian religion was rooted in the Phoenician tradition but evolved into something distinctively Punic over the centuries.
The chief deities were Baal Hammon and Tanit (Chapter 1).
Baal Hammon was a sky and fertility god, associated with agriculture, weather, and prosperity.
His depictions show a bearded elder with ram's horns, seated on a throne, sometimes flanked by sphinxes.
Tanit was a lunar goddess, associated with fertility, war, and protection.
Her symbol, found on thousands of Punic artifacts from Spain to Libya, consists of a triangle (body) topped by a circle (head) with a horizontal line between (arms).
By the fifth century BCE, Tanit had eclipsed Baal Hammon as the most prominent deity in Carthaginian religious life.
Inscriptions at the Tophet place her name first: "To the Lady Tanit, Face of Baal, and to the Lord Baal Hammon." Carthaginian religion also absorbed influences from its neighbors.
Punic religious syncretism blended Phoenician gods with Libyan, Egyptian, and Greek elements.
Tanit absorbed aspects of the Greek goddess Demeter (goddess of grain and the harvest) and the Egyptian goddess Isis (goddess of magic and motherhood).

After a plague struck the Carthaginian army besieging Syracuse in 396 BCE, the Carthaginian state officially adopted the worship of Demeter and Kore (Persephone), building Greek-style temples and appointing Greek residents of Carthage as priests.

The adoption was pragmatic: the Carthaginians believed the plague was divine punishment for destroying Greek temples, and they hedged their theological bets by adding the offended Greek gods to their own pantheon.

This willingness to absorb foreign religious practices, to treat religion as additive rather than exclusive, was characteristically Phoenician and sharply different from the exclusivist traditions of monotheistic religions like Judaism.

The cult of Melqart, the patron god of Tyre (Phoenicia Ch 4), remained important in Carthage as a symbol of the city's Phoenician heritage, but it was a secondary cult by the fourth century BCE, overshadowed by Baal Hammon and Tanit.
The annual tribute that Carthage sent to the Temple of Melqart at Tyre was more a cultural gesture than a theological necessity, like an immigrant family sending flowers to the old family church.
The Tophet debate deserves a fuller treatment than Chapter 1 could provide, because it is the single most contested question in Punic studies.
The facts are not in dispute.
The Tophet of Carthage (and similar precincts at Punic sites across the western Mediterranean, including in Sardinia and Sicily) contains thousands of urns with cremated remains.
The remains are overwhelmingly those of very young children, many of them neonates (newborns).
Some urns contain animal remains (usually lambs) instead of, or mixed with, human remains.
The urns are accompanied by votive stelae (stone slabs carved with inscriptions and images), many bearing the sign of Tanit and dedicatory texts.
The inscriptions use formulaic language, often including the phrase mlk b'l (rendered in various ways by scholars), which some interpret as "molk sacrifice to Baal" and others interpret differently.
The case for child sacrifice rests on three pillars.

First, the ancient literary evidence: Greek and Roman authors, including Diodorus Siculus, Plutarch, and Cleitarchus (KLY-tar-kus; an early Hellenistic historian), describe the Carthaginians sacrificing children by placing them in the arms of a bronze statue of Baal Hammon, from which the children rolled into a fire below.
The descriptions are vivid and consistent across multiple independent sources.
Second, the archaeological evidence: the disproportionate number of very young infants (including prenatal remains) suggests these are not random natural deaths but selected offerings.
Some studies of the skeletal remains have found evidence consistent with death before cremation.
Third, the inscriptions: some scholars read the votive texts as recording the fulfillment of vows, where a parent promised Baal Hammon or Tanit a child in exchange for divine favor.
The case against child sacrifice also rests on three pillars.

First, the literary sources are all from Carthage's enemies: Greeks and Romans who had every reason to demonize the Carthaginians, and whose descriptions of Punic religious practices may be propaganda designed to justify wars of aggression.
Accusations of child sacrifice have been leveled at many peoples throughout history, often falsely.
Second, infant mortality in the ancient world was appallingly high, running between 30 and 50 percent in the first year of life.
A cemetery dedicated specifically to infant burials, with special ritual treatment, is not inherently sinister.
The Tophet may be a sacred burial ground for children who died naturally and were consecrated to the gods in death.
Third, the presence of animal remains in some urns suggests substitution (an animal offered instead of a child), which implies a system more nuanced than wholesale infant sacrifice.

The honest conclusion is that the evidence is genuinely ambiguous, the scholarly community is genuinely divided, and the question may never be definitively resolved because the people who could have told us what they were doing left no surviving account of their own religious practices.

This is perhaps the cruelest consequence of the destruction of Punic literature: the one question that most fascinates and horrifies modern observers is the one question the Carthaginians could have answered, if anyone had preserved their books.
The Carthaginian economy was among the most diversified in the ancient world.

The purple dye industry (inherited from Tyre; Phoenicia Ch 4) continued on an industrial scale, harvesting murex sea snails from North African waters and producing the Tyrian purple that was the ancient world's most expensive textile dye.
Agricultural exports from the African estates (grain, olive oil, wine, fruit) were massive.
The trans-Saharan gold trade and the Atlantic tin trade provided access to metals that the Mediterranean world desperately needed.
Carthage began minting its own coinage around 410 BCE, relatively late for a major Mediterranean power (Athens had been minting coins for over a century).
The early Carthaginian coins featured the head of Tanit on one side and a horse on the other, images that became iconic of Punic culture.
Coins were struck in electrum (a natural gold-silver alloy), gold, silver, and bronze, and their primary purpose was paying mercenary armies.
The Barcid coins minted in Spain, with their portraits of Melqart-Hercules (probably modeled on Hamilcar or Hannibal himself, though this is debated), are among the finest coins of the ancient world.
Carthaginian navigation was the best in the Mediterranean.

Punic sailors navigated by Ursa Minor (the Little Bear constellation, containing the star closest to true north), a technique so identified with the Phoenicians that the Greeks called the constellation "the Phoenician" (Phoenicia Ch 4).
Carthaginian pilots maintained detailed rutters (written sailing directions) covering the entire western Mediterranean, the Atlantic coast, and the West African shore.
This navigational knowledge was classified as a state secret.
The story of the captain who wrecked his ship rather than reveal the tin route (Chapter 2) illustrates how seriously Carthage took commercial intelligence.
Carthaginian coins from the third century BCE have been reported found in the Azores, 900 miles (1,450 km) into the open Atlantic.
If the finds are genuine (some scholars question the provenance), Punic sailors ventured far beyond the Pillars of Hercules into waters that no European would explore again for over a thousand years.
The Carthaginian military system was unique in the ancient world.

Unlike Rome, where citizen soldiers formed the backbone of the army, and unlike the Greek city-states, where citizen hoplites (heavy infantry) were the primary fighting force, Carthage relied overwhelmingly on mercenaries and allied contingents.

Libyan infantry from the African territories, Numidian cavalry (lightly armed riders from the kingdoms west of Carthage who fought without saddles or bridles, controlling their horses with just a rope halter, the finest light cavalry in the ancient world), Iberian swordsmen, Balearic slingers (from the islands of Majorca and Minorca, so accurate they could reportedly hit a man's head at 100 yards or 90 meters), Gallic warriors, and assorted Greek and Italian adventurers made up the bulk of every Carthaginian army.
The only citizen military unit was the Sacred Band of Carthage, an elite heavy infantry corps of roughly 2,500-3,000 men drawn from the city's aristocratic families.
The Sacred Band served as the backbone of the army, but it was a small core surrounded by a much larger mass of hired soldiers.

The mercenary system had obvious advantages (professional soldiers who fought for pay were more available and arguably better trained than citizen militias; citizen casualties were minimal, which reduced domestic political pressure during long wars) and equally obvious disadvantages (mercenary loyalty was to the paymaster, not to the state; and when the pay stopped, as it did after the First Punic War, the mercenaries did not go home quietly; Chapter 4).
War elephants were Carthage's most iconic military asset.
Carthage used North African forest elephants (a now-extinct subspecies, Loxodonta africana pharaoensis, smaller than the Indian elephants used by the Hellenistic successor kingdoms in the East).
The elephants were captured in the forests of the Atlas Mountains and trained for battle, carrying towers with archers and javelin-throwers.
Their primary value was psychological: the sight and smell of a charging elephant terrified soldiers and horses who had never encountered one.
Their military effectiveness was inconsistent.

Elephants were devastating when everything went right, but they were as dangerous to friendly troops as to the enemy when they panicked, and experienced opponents learned to open lanes in their formations and target the elephants with javelins and fire.
Hannibal's Alpine elephants are the most famous war elephants in history, but most of them died in the crossing or shortly after, and they played almost no role in his Italian campaigns.
One Carthaginian who achieved lasting cultural impact was not a general but a playwright.
Terence (TEHR-ens; Publius Terentius Afer, roughly 185-159 BCE), a Carthaginian-born slave who was educated and freed by a Roman senator, became one of Rome's greatest comic dramatists.
His six surviving plays, refined adaptations of Greek New Comedy, influenced European literature from Shakespeare to Moliere.
His famous line, "I am human, and I think nothing human is alien to me" (Homo sum, humani nihil a me alienum puto), is one of the most quoted sentences in Western literature.
The most enduring cultural export of the Punic world was a freed slave who wrote in Latin.

chapters.title: Carthago Delenda Est
sentences: After the Second Punic War, the peace terms (Chapter 6) were designed to keep Carthage permanently subordinate.
The city could not wage war without Roman permission.
Its fleet was reduced to ten warships.
It owed 10,000 talents over fifty years.

Rome installed its ally Masinissa (mah-sih-NISS-ah), king of the Numidians, as a watchdog on Carthage's western border, with the implicit understanding that Masinissa could encroach on Carthaginian territory and Carthage could not fight back without Roman approval, which Rome would never grant.
For fifty years, it worked.
Carthage obeyed the treaty.
It paid its indemnity.
It did not wage war.
And, infuriatingly for the hardliners in the Roman Senate, it prospered.
Carthage's economic recovery was so rapid that by the 190s BCE the city offered to pay off its entire remaining fifty-year debt ahead of schedule.
Rome, not wanting to lose the financial leverage, actually declined the early payment.
The commercial machine that had made Carthage the richest city in the western Mediterranean before the wars was still running.
African grain, olive oil, and trade goods flowed through the harbor.
The Cothon, stripped of its warships, hummed with merchant traffic.
Carthage without its military was still Carthage: a city built to make money.
This recovery terrified Rome.

Or rather, it terrified a faction within Rome led by the senator Cato the Elder (KAY-toh; Marcus Porcius Cato, 234-149 BCE), an archconservative who had fought in the Second Punic War as a young soldier and never forgave Carthage for the trauma of Hannibal's invasion.
Cato visited Carthage as part of a diplomatic delegation around 157 BCE and returned to Rome alarmed by the city's prosperity.
The Carthaginian figs he brought back were so fresh and plump that he waved them before the Senate as evidence of how close and how dangerous the old enemy remained.

From that point until his death in 149 BCE, Cato ended every speech in the Senate, regardless of the topic under discussion, with the same phrase: "Ceterum censeo Carthaginem esse delendam" ("Furthermore, I am of the opinion that Carthage must be destroyed"), shortened in popular memory to "Carthago delenda est." Whether or not the anecdote is literally true in every instance (it is widely attested in ancient sources, though some scholars suspect embellishment), it captures the obsessive quality of Roman anxiety about Carthage.
A city that had been stripped of its military, its fleet, its territory, and its sovereignty was still perceived as an existential threat fifty years after its defeat.
The anxiety was not entirely irrational.
Rome had nearly been destroyed by Hannibal.
The institutional memory of Cannae and Trasimene and fifteen years of a Carthaginian army roaming freely through Italy was seared into the Roman political class.
Cato's campaign was fear dressed up as policy.
In 149 BCE, Rome manufactured the pretext it needed.
Masinissa, the Numidian king who had been steadily encroaching on Carthaginian territory for decades, finally provoked Carthage into a defensive war.
Carthage fought back against Numidian aggression, which technically violated the treaty prohibition against waging war without Roman permission.
Rome declared the Third Punic War.
But Rome did not simply declare war.
It demanded that Carthage surrender all weapons and military equipment, which Carthage did, handing over 200,000 sets of armor and 2,000 catapults.
Then, with Carthage disarmed, Rome delivered its final demand: Carthage must abandon its coastal city entirely and resettle at least 10 miles (16 km) inland.
For a maritime civilization whose identity, economy, and physical infrastructure were built around the sea, this was a death sentence.
Rome was not demanding submission.
It was demanding suicide.
Carthage refused.
The city that had surrendered its weapons decided to fight anyway.
In a scene that even Rome's own historians described with grudging admiration, the entire population mobilized.
Workshops were converted into weapons factories.
Citizens melted down household metal to forge swords and spearheads.
Women cut their hair to braid into bowstrings.
The city's walls, which Rome had not demanded be torn down (perhaps an oversight, perhaps confidence that a disarmed city could not resist), became the defensive perimeter for a three-year siege.
The early stages of the siege went badly for Rome.
Carthage's walls were formidable, the population was desperate, and the Roman commanders were initially incompetent.

Two years of inconclusive fighting passed before Rome sent its best general: Scipio Aemilianus (SKIP-ee-oh ay-mil-ee-AH-nus), the adopted grandson of Scipio Africanus, the man who had defeated Hannibal at Zama.
The younger Scipio was methodical, patient, and ruthless.
He sealed off the harbor, cut the supply lines, and slowly tightened the noose.
In the spring of 146 BCE, Scipio Aemilianus breached the walls and the final assault began.
What followed was six days of street-by-street, house-by-house urban combat, some of the most brutal fighting in ancient history.
The Carthaginians fought from the rooftops, from barricades, from the upper stories of buildings that lined the streets leading up to Byrsa Hill.
Roman soldiers advanced by breaking through the walls of buildings, fighting room to room, and then pulling down the structures behind them to clear paths for reinforcements.
The three streets leading from the harbor to the Byrsa were lined with six-story apartment buildings, and the fighting raged through every floor.
The last defenders retreated to the Temple of Eshmun on the summit of Byrsa Hill.
Ancient sources describe the scene: the temple surrounded, the remaining defenders crowded inside, the building set alight.

The commander's wife, having refused to surrender, dressed in her finest clothes, cursed her husband (who had offered himself to Scipio's mercy), killed her children, and threw herself and the children into the flames.
The last Carthaginians burned alive in their holiest temple rather than submit.
Whether the details are accurate or literarily enhanced by Roman historians who wanted a dramatic ending, the temple's fall was the end.
The destruction of Carthage was systematic and deliberate.
The city was razed.
Buildings were demolished.
Temples were looted and torn down.
The harbor complex, the great Cothon with its 220 warship berths, was destroyed.
An estimated 50,000 survivors were sold into slavery.
According to later tradition, the Romans plowed the ruins and sowed the fields with salt to ensure nothing would ever grow there.
The salt story is almost certainly apocryphal (no ancient source mentions it; the detail first appears in the nineteenth century CE), but the spirit of the legend is accurate.
Rome intended to erase Carthage from the map and from memory.
The city's territory became the Roman province of Africa (the origin of the continent's name).
The city burned for seventeen days.
The destruction extended to the mind as well as the city.
Rome distributed Carthage's libraries to Numidian princes, allies who had helped win the final war.
Those libraries vanished into oblivion.

Every history the Carthaginians wrote about themselves, every epic poem, every religious text, every philosophical work, every play, every personal letter that had been preserved in the city's archives was gone.

The sole exception was Mago's agricultural treatise (Chapter 3), which the Roman Senate, in an act of selective mercy that reveals Rome's priorities with painful clarity, ordered translated into Latin.
Rome saved the farming manual and burned everything else.
The loss of Punic literature is one of the great intellectual catastrophes of the ancient world.
We know Carthage almost entirely through the words of its enemies.
The Greeks who fought Carthage in Sicily for two centuries.
The Romans who fought three existential wars against Carthage and ended by obliterating the city.
These are our sources.
They are not inventing facts wholesale, but they are choosing which facts to record and how to interpret them.
When Polybius describes Carthaginian "treachery" (fides Punica, "Punic faith," was a Roman proverb meaning dishonesty), he is using a moral framework supplied by Carthage's enemies.
When Diodorus describes child sacrifice, he is drawing on a literary tradition that served Greek self-congratulation.
We have no Carthaginian rebuttal.
We have no Punic Herodotus, no Punic Thucydides, no Punic Livy.
The silence is total.
In one of history's darkest coincidences, Rome also sacked and destroyed Corinth, the great Greek commercial city, in the same year, 146 BCE.
The twin destructions of Carthage and Corinth are conventionally taken as the moment when Rome became the undisputed master of the entire Mediterranean world.
Carthage's seven centuries of civilization ended in fire.
Corinth's glory was ground under Roman boots.
The message was clear: Rome would tolerate no rival, no matter how weakened, no matter how compliant.

Carthage would eventually be refounded, first by Julius Caesar as a Roman colony in 46 BCE (a century after the destruction) and later expanded under Augustus into one of the great cities of the Roman Empire.

Roman Carthage became the capital of Roman Africa, a center of Latin literature, a hub of early Christianity (Saint Augustine was born in Punic North Africa and spent years in Carthage), and eventually one of the largest cities in the Roman world.
But Roman Carthage was a Latin-speaking Roman city, not a Phoenician one.

The Punic language survived in rural North Africa for centuries (Saint Augustine noted that his parishioners still spoke it in the early fifth century CE), and Punic religious practices lingered in folk tradition long after the old temples were gone.
But the civilization that had built the Cothon and produced Hannibal and written twenty-eight volumes on agriculture was finished.
What remains?
More than you might think.
The sign of Tanit appears on thousands of artifacts in museums across Europe and North Africa.
The excavated streets of Kerkouane (Chapter 3) preserve the grid plan and bathing rooms of ordinary Punic houses.
The Tophet urns, whatever they represent, are archaeological evidence of sustained ritual practice spanning six centuries.
Punic inscriptions, mostly votive and funerary, provide a corpus of the written language even if the literature is lost.

And the military campaigns, especially Hannibal's, are documented in such exhaustive detail by Greek and Roman historians that we know more about the Second Punic War than about most wars in ancient history, albeit entirely from the Roman perspective.
The Carthaginian legacy is, above all, a lesson in what happens when the losers of history leave no books.

Carthage was a great civilization by any reasonable standard: wealthy, sophisticated, well-governed, innovative in agriculture and navigation and urban planning, capable of producing military genius of the highest order.
It was also, according to its enemies, cruel, treacherous, and religiously barbaric.
We cannot weigh these claims because the defendants never got to speak.
The Romans wrote the verdict, served as the prosecution, and burned the defense's archives.
Every letter in every alphabet you have ever read descends from the Phoenician script that Carthage carried across the western Mediterranean (Phoenicia Ch 3).
The name of the entire continent of Africa derives from the Roman province that replaced Carthage.
The gorilla is named after creatures spotted by a Carthaginian admiral.
The word "Punic" survives as a synonym for treachery, which tells you who wrote the dictionary.
Carthage built something remarkable, and Rome destroyed it so thoroughly that we can only glimpse the outline through the rubble and the hostile testimony of the people who struck the match.
Seven centuries of civilization, silenced in seventeen days of fire.

chapters.title: The Steppe and Its People
sentences: Here is a civilization that built nothing.
No temples.
No palaces.
No city walls, no ziggurats, no pyramids, no aqueducts.
No writing system, no law codes, no tax records.
No permanent settlement of any kind that you could point to on a map and say: this is where the Scythians lived.
They didn't live in places.
They lived in motion — on horseback, in felt-covered wagons, following their herds across a landscape so vast that it made the empires of the settled world look like garden plots.
And yet the Scythians terrified every civilization they touched.
The Assyrians, who bragged about skinning their enemies alive, made alliances with them.
The Persians, who ruled the largest empire on earth, sent an army of 700,000 men (or so they claimed) into Scythian territory and came back with nothing but exhausted horses and wounded pride.
The Greeks, who thought of themselves as the pinnacle of human culture, were so fascinated by Scythian women warriors that they invented an entire mythology around them.

And when the Scythians died, they were buried in graves so lavish, so filled with gold and sacrifice, that the tombs have been drawing grave robbers and archaeologists for three centuries and are still producing masterpieces that fill the great museums of Europe.
This is the story of the civilization that didn't build cities.
It is also the story of one of the most dramatic problems in ancient history: how do you write the history of a people who didn't write?
The Scythians left no texts.

Everything we know about them comes from three sources: the writings of their neighbors (especially the Greeks, who were fascinated by them), the archaeology of their graves (which are spectacularly rich), and the frozen tombs of the Altai Mountains (which preserved organic material that normally rots away in centuries).
These three windows — literary, archaeological, and frozen — give us a picture of the Scythians that is vivid but uneven.
We know what their enemies thought of them.
We know what they buried with their dead.
We know what their tattoos looked like.
But we do not know what they called themselves, what stories they told around their campfires, or what songs they sang while riding.
A note on "firsts" before we go further.

The Scythians are credited with perfecting mounted archery, pioneering the military tactics that would define Eurasian steppe warfare for two millennia, and creating trade networks that prefigured the Silk Road.
Some of these claims are solid.
Some are contested.
And all of them carry the caveat that "first" in ancient history means "first surviving evidence of." Nomadic peoples by definition leave fewer traces than settled ones.
The Scythians may have inherited some of these innovations from earlier steppe peoples whose names we will never know.
Keep that in mind.
The stage is the Eurasian steppe — and you need to understand its scale to understand why the Scythians were what they were.
The steppe is a belt of grassland stretching roughly 5,000 miles (8,000 km) from the plains of Hungary in central Europe to the mountains of Mongolia and Manchuria in East Asia.

It is the largest grassland on earth, wider than the continental United States, and for most of human history it was one continuous ecological zone: flat or gently rolling terrain, too dry for forest, too wet for desert, covered in grasses that grow waist-high in summer and die back to frozen stubble in winter.
The climate is continental in the extreme — baking in July, frozen solid in January, with winds that blow unobstructed across thousands of miles of open terrain.
There are no natural barriers.
No mountain ranges cross the steppe east to west (though the Ural Mountains divide it roughly in half, they are low enough and broken enough that they have never been a serious obstacle to movement).
A person on horseback can ride from the Danube River to the Altai Mountains without encountering a single physical feature that requires them to stop.
This geography created a specific kind of human society.
You could not farm the steppe in any sustainable way with ancient technology (the rainfall is too low and too unpredictable, the growing season too short).
But you could herd animals on it — sheep, goats, cattle, and above all horses, which could survive the winter by pawing through snow to reach the frozen grass beneath.
The people who lived on the steppe were pastoral nomads: they didn't grow food, they grew herds, and they moved with those herds in seasonal circuits, following the grass.
Their homes were portable (felt tents, which the later Mongols called yurts; the Scythians used both tents and felt-covered wagons drawn by oxen).
Their wealth was measured in livestock.

Their political structures were tribal — loose confederacies of extended families and clans, united by kinship, marriage alliances, and the personal authority of a chief or king who held power as long as he could hold the loyalty of his warriors.
This was not primitive.
It was a sophisticated adaptation to one of the harshest environments on the planet.
But it produced a civilization that was invisible by the standards of the settled world.
No walls to dig up.
No foundations to excavate.
No pottery sequences to calibrate.
A nomadic camp that was occupied for a season and abandoned leaves almost no archaeological trace — a few postholes, some animal bones, a scatter of broken artifacts in the grass.
The only places where the Scythians left substantial, permanent, archaeologically recoverable evidence of their existence were their graves.

The Scythians (SITH-ee-uns, from the Greek Skythai; we don't know what they called themselves) were Iranian-speaking pastoral nomads who dominated the western and central Eurasian steppe from roughly the ninth to the third century BCE.

"Iranian-speaking" does not mean they came from modern Iran — it means they spoke a language belonging to the Iranian branch of the Indo-European language family, the same family that includes Persian, Kurdish, and the ancient Avestan language of the Zoroastrian scriptures.
Scythian was as different from Greek as Persian is from German — same distant family tree, completely different languages.
The Scythians were linguistically related to the Persians, but culturally they were worlds apart: the Persians built Persepolis; the Scythians built nothing.
Where exactly they came from is debated.

The traditional view, based on later Greek and Persian sources, is that the Scythians migrated from Central Asia — from the grasslands east of the Caspian Sea, in the region of modern Kazakhstan and western Siberia — and moved west into the Pontic steppe (the grasslands north of the Black Sea, in modern Ukraine and southern Russia) sometime around 900–800 BCE.

The archaeological evidence broadly supports this: Scythian-type artifacts and burial practices appear in the eastern steppe earlier than in the west, suggesting a migration direction from east to west.
But "migration" is a misleading word for what nomads do.
They don't pack up and move to a new homeland.
They shift their seasonal routes, push into new pastures, absorb or displace the peoples already there, and gradually extend the zone they control.
The Scythian "migration" was probably a gradual westward expansion over generations, not a single dramatic event.
The people they displaced were the Cimmerians (sih-MEER-ee-uns), another group of Iranian-speaking steppe nomads who had previously dominated the Pontic steppe.

Around 750 BCE, the Scythians drove the Cimmerians out of their grasslands and south through the Caucasus Mountains into Anatolia (modern Turkey) and the Near East, where the displaced Cimmerians proceeded to cause havoc among the settled kingdoms of the region.
The Cimmerians raided Phrygia, attacked Lydia, and menaced the Assyrian frontier before eventually being absorbed into the populations of Anatolia.
The Greek poet Homer, writing roughly around this time, placed the Cimmerians at the edge of the known world, near the entrance to the land of the dead.
By the time the real Cimmerians showed up at the gates of civilized cities, they had already become mythological.
The single most important thing about the Scythians was the horse.
The horse nomad culture that the Scythians perfected around 800 BCE was one of the most revolutionary military and social innovations in human history.

Horses had been domesticated on the steppe by roughly 3500 BCE (the exact location is debated; the site of Botai in Kazakhstan was long the leading candidate, but recent DNA analysis suggests Botai horses are ancestors of Przewalski's horse, not modern domestics, and the Pontic-Caspian steppe is now favored).
People had been riding them for nearly two thousand years before the Scythians.
But the Scythians developed something qualitatively new: a complete way of life built around mounted combat.
The key innovation was mounted archery — the ability to shoot a bow accurately while riding a horse at full gallop.
This sounds simple.
It is extraordinarily difficult.

You need to control a horse with your legs alone (because both hands are occupied with the bow), you need to aim at a target while bouncing on a moving animal, you need to fire in the brief moment when all four of the horse's hooves are off the ground (the only instant when the rider is not being jolted), and you need a bow short enough to use on horseback but powerful enough to punch through armor at distance.
The Scythian composite bow — a compact, recurved weapon made of layers of wood, horn, and sinew glued together and cured for months — was the technological solution.
It was about 3 feet (90 cm) long (short enough to clear the horse's neck), but its composite construction gave it a draw weight and range comparable to bows twice its size.
A skilled Scythian horse archer could hit a man-sized target at 50 to 75 yards while riding at a gallop, with maximum range well beyond that from a stationary position.
This combination of mobility and firepower made Scythian warriors effectively impossible for infantry-based armies to defeat in open terrain.
A Scythian raiding force could appear, loose a storm of arrows, and vanish before the enemy could close to sword range.

If pursued, the Scythians could retreat while firing backward over their horses' rumps — a technique that would later be called the Parthian shot (from the Parthians, who inherited it from the steppe tradition, and from which the English expression "parting shot" may derive, though linguists debate the connection).
If cornered, they could scatter in every direction, regroup at a prearranged point, and attack again.
They carried everything they needed on horseback: food (dried meat and mare's milk), shelter (portable tents), water (leather skins), and a seemingly inexhaustible supply of arrows.
The logistics of steppe warfare were built into the Scythian way of life.
Every man was a soldier.
Every meal was a ration.
Every horse was a mount, a source of milk, and, in extremis, a source of meat.
The Scythians also bred their horses carefully.

Scythian horse breeding produced sturdy steppe horses prized for endurance and speed, capable of sustained long-distance riding that would have exhausted the larger but less hardy breeds of the settled world.

Scythian horses were typically smaller than the warhorses of later cavalry armies (about 13–14 hands, roughly the size of a large modern pony), but they were tough, sure-footed, and capable of surviving on grass alone — no grain feeding required.

A Scythian warrior might bring three or four remounts on a long campaign, switching horses throughout the day to keep them fresh while covering 50 or 60 miles (80–100 km) per day, a pace that no infantry army could match.

Hippocrates (the Greek physician, writing around 400 BCE) described the Scythian way of life in his medical treatise On Airs, Waters, and Places, noting that the Scythians spent so much time on horseback that their legs became bowed and their bodies adapted to a life in the saddle.
He described their wagon dwellings — felt-covered wagons drawn by oxen in which entire families traveled with their herds, the wagons serving as mobile homes on the grasslands.
The wagon was the basic unit of Scythian domestic life: a household on wheels, following the grass.
The Scythians also developed sophisticated military intelligence and communication systems suited to the steppe.
Mounted relay riders could carry messages across the Pontic steppe in days — a distance that would take a walking messenger weeks.
Signal fires on hilltops could alert tribes across hundreds of miles within hours.

And the decentralized tribal structure, which looked like a weakness compared to the Persian Empire's chain of command, was actually a strength in wartime: each tribal unit could fight independently, make local tactical decisions, and coordinate through pre-arranged meeting points rather than waiting for orders from a central authority.
The Scythian military system was not an army in the settled-world sense.
It was a distributed network of warrior bands that could mass, disperse, and re-mass faster than any infantry force could react.
The settled civilizations of the Near East, the Mediterranean, and China would spend the next two thousand years trying to figure out how to fight horse nomads.
The Scythians were the first to demonstrate the problem.
The Huns, the Turks, the Mongols, and the Comanche all practiced variations of the same mounted archery that the Scythians pioneered.
Every time a steppe army met a civilized army in open terrain, the result was the same, from the Pontic steppe in 513 BCE to the plains of Hungary in 1241 CE: the horse archers won.

Cross-civ check: the Scythians' entry into the Pontic steppe (~900–750 BCE) coincides with the Assyrian Empire at its height in Mesopotamia, the Zhou dynasty in China (where the feudal Western Zhou system was about to collapse into the Spring and Autumn period), the early Greek Dark Ages transitioning into the Archaic period, and the Nubian kingdom of Kush establishing itself at Napata.
In the Andes, the Chavín culture was just beginning its rise.
In Mesoamerica, the Olmec civilization at San Lorenzo was at its peak.

The Scythians were not an isolated phenomenon — they were the western end of a steppe world that stretched all the way to China's northern frontier, where the Zhou Chinese were already dealing with their own nomadic raiders.
The steppe connected civilizations that didn't know each other existed, and the Scythians sat at the western end of that connection.

chapters.title: Raiders of Empires
sentences: Around 680 BCE, the Scythians came south.
Not to settle.
Not to build.
To raid, to plunder, and to insert themselves into the power politics of the ancient Near East with the confidence of people who knew they could ride away from any army that tried to catch them.

For the next eighty years, the Scythians would be a major player in the most politically complex region on earth, allying with and fighting against the great empires of Mesopotamia and Iran, reaching as far as the borders of Egypt, and then vanishing back into the grasslands as abruptly as they had appeared.
The timing was not accidental.
The early seventh century BCE was a period of extraordinary political upheaval in the Near East.

The Assyrian Empire (the world's first superpower, ruling from the Tigris River across Mesopotamia, the Levant, and Egypt) was at the peak of its territorial extent under kings like Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal, but it was also under constant pressure from rebellions, frontier wars, and the rising power of two peoples on its borders: the Medes (an Iranian-speaking people building a kingdom in western Iran, in the Zagros Mountains) and the Babylonians (who would eventually destroy Assyria in 612 BCE).
Into this three-way struggle between Assyria, Media, and Babylon rode the Scythians, a wild card that none of the settled powers fully understood and none could fully control.
The first documented Scythian leader in the Near East was Bartatua (bar-tah-TOO-ah, also spelled Protothyes in Greek sources), who appears in Assyrian records around 670 BCE.
Bartatua did something that reveals the Scythians' political sophistication: he allied with the Assyrian Empire.

According to an Assyrian text, Bartatua asked to marry an Assyrian princess — a request that the Assyrian king Esarhaddon apparently granted, after consulting the oracle of the god Shamash about whether the Scythian chief could be trusted. (The oracle's answer, if it was recorded, has not survived.

The marriage apparently happened anyway.) The alliance gave Bartatua access to Assyrian diplomacy, Assyrian intelligence, and Assyrian prestige, and it gave Assyria a mobile strike force on its northern frontier that could be aimed at the Medes and Cimmerians.
The deal was pragmatic on both sides.

The Assyrians had been dealing with the Cimmerians — the same people the Scythians had driven out of the Pontic steppe (Chapter 1) — for decades, and the Cimmerians were causing serious problems in Anatolia, raiding Phrygia (FRIJ-ee-uh, a kingdom in central Anatolia whose king, the legendary Midas, was said to have turned everything he touched to gold — the real Midas was a historical figure who committed suicide when the Cimmerians overran his capital) and threatening Lydia (the wealthy kingdom in western Anatolia whose king Croesus would later be conquered by Cyrus the Great, Persian Empire Ch 1).
The Scythians, who had displaced the Cimmerians in the first place, were natural allies against them.

And the Scythians got what nomadic peoples always wanted from settled empires: access to manufactured goods (metalwork, textiles, luxury items), diplomatic recognition, and the right to raid someone else's territory with at least tacit permission.

The Assyrian sources are frustratingly brief about the Scythians — the Assyrians were prolific record-keepers but they cared mostly about their own victories, and alliance partners got less ink than enemies — but the records confirm that the Scythians were a recognized political entity by the 670s BCE, with a king who could negotiate treaties, marry royalty, and command a fighting force significant enough to interest the most powerful empire on earth.
Bartatua's son Madyes (MAH-dee-eez) took the Scythian presence in the Near East to a different level entirely.

Around 650 BCE, Madyes led the Scythians to a position of dominance that Herodotus (the Greek historian, writing about two centuries later) described as a 28-year period during which the Scythians "ruled all of Asia." This is almost certainly an exaggeration — Herodotus was writing long after the fact, using oral sources, about events in a region he had never visited — but the core claim is taken seriously by most scholars: the Scythians, under Madyes, defeated the Medes and established a period of military dominance over parts of western Asia that lasted for a generation.
Herodotus says the Scythians overthrew Median rule outright.

The Medes under their king Cyaxares (sye-AX-ar-eez) had been building a powerful kingdom in western Iran, and they had already begun the campaigns against Assyria that would eventually destroy the Assyrian Empire.
But the Scythians intervened, defeating Cyaxares in battle and imposing themselves as the dominant military power in the region.

For roughly a generation — the "28 years" of Herodotus, though the exact chronology is uncertain — the Scythians collected tribute, raided at will, and behaved as overlords of a territory they had no interest in governing.
The Scythian raids reached astonishing distances.

Around 625 BCE, Scythian raiding parties struck deep into the Levant (the coastal region of modern Syria, Lebanon, and Israel), reaching as far south as the Philistine city of Ascalon (modern Ashkelon in Israel), where they sacked the city and plundered the temple of Aphrodite Urania (or rather, whatever local goddess the Greeks later identified with Aphrodite).
Herodotus says the raiders reached the borders of Egypt itself, and that the Egyptian pharaoh Psammetichus I (psam-MEH-tih-kus) bought them off with gifts to prevent an invasion.
The image is striking: Scythian horse archers, born on the grasslands of Central Asia, standing on the Mediterranean coast and staring at the pyramids.
Whether they actually saw Egypt is uncertain, but they were close enough to scare the pharaoh.
The Near Eastern adventure ended as abruptly as it had begun.
Herodotus tells a story — probably legendary, definitely dramatic — about how the Medes finally expelled the Scythians.
Cyaxares, who had been biding his time under Scythian domination, invited the Scythian leaders to a feast.
The Scythians came, drank themselves insensible (a weakness that Herodotus and other Greek writers attributed to them consistently), and were massacred.
The surviving Scythians withdrew north through the Caucasus and back to the Pontic steppe.
The feast-and-massacre story is a literary trope — similar tales appear in many ancient cultures — and may well be fiction.
What is not fiction is that the Scythians were expelled from the Near East sometime around 625–600 BCE and did not return.
The Medes, freed from Scythian pressure, immediately turned on Assyria.

In 612 BCE, a combined Median and Babylonian army sacked the Assyrian capital of Nineveh, and the Assyrian Empire — the most feared military power the ancient world had ever known — collapsed within three years.
The Scythians had been a critical factor in delaying that collapse: their alliance with Assyria and their domination of the Medes had given the Assyrian Empire an extra generation of life.
When the Scythians left, the balance of power shifted, and Assyria died.
What did the Scythians get out of their Near Eastern adventure?
First, they got rich.

The plunder from decades of raiding in Mesopotamia, the Levant, and Iran — gold, silver, metalwork, textiles, weapons — poured back into the Pontic steppe and fueled the explosion of luxury goods that characterizes Scythian culture from the seventh century onward.
