Around 750 BCE, give or take a few decades (the exact date is debated, and "Homer" may represent a tradition rather than a single person), the oral traditions about the Mycenaean heroic age were set down in written form as the Iliad and the Odyssey, the two epic poems that became the foundational texts of Greek literature and, eventually, of Western literature.
The Mycenaean narrative (Mycenaean Ch 5, 8) covered what Homer preserved and what he invented.
Here, what matters is what the poems meant to the Greeks who heard them.
The Iliad and the Odyssey were not just stories.
They were the shared cultural curriculum of the Greek world.
Every Greek city-state, from Athens to Syracuse to Miletus, knew Homer.
Every educated Greek child memorized passages.
The poems provided a common mythology, a common set of heroes, a common moral vocabulary (honor, glory, fate, hospitality, the relationship between mortals and gods), and a common identity.

In a world of fiercely independent city-states that spoke different dialects, worshipped local variants of the same gods, and spent much of their time fighting each other, Homer was the one thing all Greeks shared.
The poems did not create Greek identity, but they gave it a language.
A generation after Homer, around 700 BCE, another poet named Hesiod (HEE-see-od) composed two works that filled the gaps Homer had left.
His Theogony (thee-OG-uh-nee) told the origin story of the Greek gods, from primordial Chaos through the Titans to the reign of Zeus on Mount Olympus, creating the canonical Greek cosmology.

His Works and Days was something entirely different: a practical guide to farming, morality, and surviving in a harsh world, addressed to Hesiod's lazy brother Perses, full of advice about when to plant, when to harvest, and when to stay in bed.
Where Homer sang about heroes, Hesiod sang about ordinary people.
Together, they established the two poles of Greek literature: the heroic ideal and the daily grind.
While the poets were composing, the Greeks were inventing a new way of organizing human communities.
The polis (POH-lis, plural poleis, poh-LAYS) is usually translated as "city-state," but neither word quite captures what it meant.
A polis was not just a city.

It was a self-governing political community with its own laws, its own army, its own gods (or at least its own patron deity), its own festivals, its own coinage, and its own fierce sense of independence.
The urban center (the asty) was only part of it.
The surrounding agricultural territory (the chora) was equally integral.
A farmer working his fields ten miles from the town center was as much a citizen of the polis as a merchant living next to the marketplace.
The polis was defined not by walls but by membership: who belonged, who had rights, who owed obligations, who could participate in governance.
The polis emerged gradually during the Dark Ages and the early 8th century BCE, as the small, scattered communities that survived the Mycenaean collapse began to coalesce into larger political units.
The process was not uniform.
Some poleis formed by the merging of neighboring villages (a process the Greeks called synoikismos, sin-oy-KIZ-mos, "living together").
Others formed around a natural stronghold or a particularly important sanctuary.
The result, by roughly 700 BCE, was a Greek world composed of hundreds of independent poleis, most of them tiny (a few thousand citizens at most), each jealously guarding its autonomy.
The two that would matter most, Athens and Sparta, were among the largest, but they were not yet dominant.
The physical and political heart of every polis was the agora (AG-or-ah), the central open space that served as marketplace, meeting ground, and public square.
The agora was where citizens gathered to trade, argue, gossip, and (in poleis that developed democratic or semi-democratic institutions) vote.
It was flanked by temples, public buildings, and colonnaded walkways called stoas (STOH-ahs).
Above many poleis, on the highest ground, sat the acropolis (ah-KROP-uh-lis, "high city"), originally a defensible citadel and later the site of the most important temples.

Athens' Acropolis would eventually become the most famous hilltop in the world, but in the 8th century BCE, most acropoleis were modest affairs: a small temple, a defensive wall, and a place to retreat when raiders came.
Nothing like the polis existed elsewhere in the ancient world at this time.
Mesopotamia and Egypt had cities, but they were governed by kings, priests, and imperial bureaucracies.
The Phoenician cities were merchant oligarchies run by wealthy trading families.
The Mycenaean kingdoms had been palace-centered autocracies.
The polis was something new: a community in which ordinary free men (not kings, not priests, not hereditary aristocrats) had, at least in theory, a voice in how they were governed.
The degree of that voice varied enormously from polis to polis.
Some were ruled by aristocratic councils.
Some were dominated by single strongmen.
A few would develop radical experiments in popular self-governance.
But the basic idea, that the community of citizens is the state, was a Greek invention, and it would take centuries for its full implications to unfold.
The third major development of the 8th century was the explosive expansion of the Greek world through colonization.

Starting around 750 BCE and accelerating through the 7th and 6th centuries, Greek city-states began sending out organized groups of settlers to found new communities across the Mediterranean and Black Sea coasts.
This was not empire-building.
The mother city (metropolis, meh-TROP-oh-lis, literally "mother city," the origin of the English word) did not usually control its colonies politically.
The colonies were independent poleis from the start, linked to the metropolis by culture, religion, and family ties but governing themselves.

The colonists left home because their home poleis were running out of arable land, because population was growing faster than the thin, rocky soil of Greece could feed, or because political losers in factional disputes at home preferred founding a new city to living under the winners.
The result was the scattering of Greek-speaking communities across an enormous arc.
Massalia (mah-SAL-ee-ah, modern Marseilles) was founded around 600 BCE by settlers from Phocaea in Ionia.
Neapolis (nee-AH-poh-lis, modern Naples, meaning "new city") was established by Euboean colonists on the Italian coast.

Syracuse (SEER-ah-kyooz) on the eastern coast of Sicily, founded around 733 BCE by Corinthians, would grow into one of the largest and wealthiest Greek cities anywhere, eventually rivaling Athens itself.
Cyrene (sy-REE-nee) in North Africa (modern Libya) became a prosperous agricultural colony.
Byzantion (bih-ZAN-tee-on, later Constantinople, now Istanbul) was founded around 657 BCE by colonists from Megara, controlling the straits between the Aegean and the Black Sea.
Along the Black Sea coast, Greek colonies spread from the Crimea to the coast of modern Georgia, establishing trade connections with the Scythian nomads of the steppe.
The colonies were not just a safety valve for population pressure.
They were an engine of cultural exchange.
Greek colonists in southern Italy and Sicily encountered the native Italic peoples and the Etruscans.
Greek colonists in North Africa traded with Egyptians and Libyans.
Greek colonists on the Black Sea coast dealt with Scythians and Thracians.
Each encounter brought new ideas, new goods, and new artistic influences back into the Greek world.

The philosophical revolution that would transform Greek thought in the 6th century BCE began not in Athens but in the colonial and Ionian cities of the eastern and western Mediterranean, where contact with non-Greek cultures forced Greeks to ask new kinds of questions.
One institution united this fragmented, far-flung world of independent poleis and distant colonies: the Olympic Games.
The first recorded Olympic Games were held in 776 BCE at Olympia (oh-LIM-pee-ah), a sanctuary in the western Peloponnese sacred to Zeus (the king of the gods, ruler of sky and thunder).

The Games were held every four years, and during the festival, a sacred truce called the ekecheiria (eh-keh-KYE-ree-ah) was declared across the Greek world: all warfare was to cease so that athletes and spectators could travel safely to Olympia.
The truce did not always hold, but the fact that it was declared at all is significant.
In a world of fiercely independent, frequently warring city-states, the Olympics were a periodic reminder that all Greeks shared something larger than their local quarrels.
The early Games were modest: the only event was a single footrace of about 600 feet (192 meters), the stadion (STAY-dee-on, the origin of the English word "stadium").

Over the centuries, events were added: longer footraces, wrestling, boxing, the pentathlon (five events: discus, javelin, long jump, stadion race, and wrestling), chariot racing, and the brutal pankration (pan-KRAH-tee-on, a combination of boxing and wrestling with almost no rules).
Athletes competed naked and were crowned with olive wreaths, not medals.
Victors became celebrities across the Greek world, and their home poleis showered them with honors.
The Olympics were not the only pan-Hellenic (meaning "all-Greek") festival.

The Pythian Games at Delphi (every four years, one year before and after the Olympics), the Isthmian Games at Corinth (every two years), and the Nemean Games (every two years) formed a four-year cycle of festivals that defined the Greek athletic calendar.
Together, they reinforced a shared Greek identity: you were Greek if you were allowed to compete.
Foreigners (called barbaroi, bar-BAR-oy, because their languages sounded like "bar-bar" to Greek ears, the origin of the English word "barbarian") were excluded.
The Oracle at Delphi (DEL-fy), perched on the slopes of Mount Parnassus in central Greece, became the most important religious institution in the Greek world during this same period.

At Delphi, the Pythia (PITH-ee-ah), a priestess of the god Apollo (uh-POL-oh, god of light, music, prophecy, and healing), delivered prophecies to anyone who came seeking divine guidance, from individual farmers to the kings of great empires.

The Pythia sat on a tripod over a fissure in the rock, entered a trance-like state (modern geologists have found that the fissure emits ethylene gas, which can produce altered consciousness), and uttered ambiguous pronouncements that the temple priests interpreted for the inquirer.
Cities consulted Delphi before founding colonies, before going to war, and before making major political decisions.
The Lydian king Croesus (KREE-sus) famously asked Delphi whether he should attack Persia and was told that if he did, he would destroy a great empire.
He attacked.
The empire he destroyed was his own.
The Eleusinian Mysteries (el-yoo-SIN-ee-un), conducted at the sanctuary of Eleusis (eh-LOO-sis) near Athens, were the most sacred religious rites in the Greek world.

The Mysteries, dedicated to the goddesses Demeter (deh-MEE-ter, goddess of grain and harvest) and Persephone (per-SEF-oh-nee, her daughter, queen of the underworld), promised initiates a blessed afterlife.
The rituals were kept so secret that we still do not know exactly what happened during the initiation ceremony, despite the Mysteries being practiced for over a thousand years.
Initiates included ordinary citizens and kings alike.
The Mysteries may have roots stretching back to the Mycenaean period (Mycenaean Ch 6), making them one of the oldest continuous religious practices in the Greek world.
By 600 BCE, the Greek world was transformed.
Writing had returned.
Hundreds of independent poleis dotted the coastlines of the Mediterranean and the Black Sea.
Pan-Hellenic festivals and religious sanctuaries provided a shared cultural framework across political boundaries.
The alphabet made it possible to write down laws, compose poetry, and record knowledge in a way the oral tradition never could.
The polis created a civic culture in which ordinary citizens had stakes in governance.
And colonization had connected the Greeks to the wider world, exposing them to Egyptian mathematics, Phoenician commerce, Babylonian astronomy, and Lydian coinage.
All of this was prelude.
The Greek experiments that would change the world, in politics, philosophy, and the arts, were about to begin.

chapters.title: Tyrants and Lawmakers
sentences: The Greek city-states of the 7th and 6th centuries BCE had a problem: they were run by aristocrats who had no intention of sharing power, governing populations that increasingly demanded a say.

Most early poleis were governed by small groups of wealthy landowners, often calling themselves the aristoi (ar-ISS-toy, "the best"), who controlled the best farmland, dominated the courts, monopolized military leadership, and made the laws.
The gap between rich and poor was extreme and getting worse.
Small farmers who fell into debt could be sold into slavery to pay what they owed, a practice that was perfectly legal and routinely enforced.
Justice was administered by aristocratic judges who, unsurprisingly, tended to favor aristocratic interests.
The laws themselves were unwritten, which meant the judges could interpret them however they pleased.
This was not a stable arrangement.

Population growth, expanding trade, and the military revolution of the hoplite phalanx (HOP-lite FAY-lanks, the dense infantry formation of heavily armed citizen-soldiers, carrying large round shields, long spears, and wearing bronze helmets and breastplates, fighting shoulder-to-shoulder in tight ranks) were creating new classes of people who had economic and military importance but no political voice.
A successful merchant who could afford hoplite armor and stood in the battle line defending the city had an increasingly compelling argument for why he should have a say in how the city was governed.
The answer from the aristocrats was, essentially: because we said so.
That answer was wearing thin.
The result was two centuries of political experimentation, as city-states across the Greek world tried different solutions to the question of who rules.
Some solutions came from above, imposed by strongmen who seized power by force or popular acclaim.
Others came from the slow, painful process of writing laws and expanding political participation.
Two cities, Athens and Sparta, produced the most influential answers, and the contrast between them would define Greek history for centuries.
The strongmen came first.
In Greek, they were called tyrannoi (tih-RAH-noy, singular tyrannos), a word borrowed from Lydian that originally meant simply "sole ruler" without the negative connotation it carries in English.
A Greek tyrant was not necessarily cruel or oppressive (though some were both).
He was a man who seized power outside the normal channels, usually with popular support, and ruled personally rather than through the aristocratic institutions he had displaced.
Many tyrants were themselves aristocrats who broke ranks with their class to champion popular causes.

The pattern was common across the Greek world in the 7th and 6th centuries: a charismatic leader exploits popular anger at aristocratic rule, seizes power with a bodyguard of supporters, redistributes some wealth, builds public works, patronizes the arts, and rules until he dies or is overthrown.
His sons usually inherit, and the dynasty usually falls within a generation or two.
The most important tyranny for later history was at Athens.
Peisistratos (pie-SIS-trah-tos) seized power in Athens in 561 BCE, was expelled twice, and returned each time with a mixture of military force, foreign allies, and theatrical flair.

On one memorable occasion, he staged his return by riding into the city in a chariot alongside a tall, beautiful woman dressed as the goddess Athena, as if the city's patron deity were personally escorting him back to power.
The Athenians, according to the historian Herodotus (who tells the story with undisguised amazement), fell for it.
Peisistratos ruled Athens, on and off, until his death in 527 BCE.
His tyranny was, by the standards of the time, relatively benign.
He left the existing laws and political institutions largely in place, merely ensuring that his allies held the key positions.
He funded major building projects, including a temple to Athena on the Acropolis and improvements to the Agora.

He patronized the arts, commissioning the first official written text of Homer's Iliad and Odyssey and establishing the Great Dionysia (dy-oh-NIS-ee-ah), the annual dramatic festival in honor of the god Dionysus (dy-oh-NY-sus) that would become the birthplace of Greek theater (Chapter 4).
He promoted Athenian trade and diplomacy, making Athens a significant player in Aegean affairs for the first time.
He left the city richer, more culturally vibrant, and more politically cohesive than he had found it.
His sons, Hippias (HIP-ee-as) and Hipparchus (hip-PAR-kus), were less successful.

Hipparchus was assassinated in 514 BCE by two aristocrats, Harmodius and Aristogeiton (har-MOH-dee-us and ar-IST-oh-GY-ton), who became celebrated in Athenian tradition as the "Tyrannicides" (tyrant-killers), though their motives were personal rather than political.
Hippias, the surviving brother, grew increasingly paranoid and repressive, and was finally overthrown in 510 BCE with the help of Spartan military intervention.
The tyranny was over.
What came next would change the world.
But before Athens got to democracy, it had to go through lawgivers.
The problem with unwritten law is that nobody can check what the law actually says.
When the judges are the aristocrats, and the laws are whatever the aristocrats say they are, justice is whatever power wants it to be.
The first step toward fixing this was simple: write the laws down.
Draco (DRAY-ko) did this for Athens in 621 BCE, producing the city's first written law code.
The code was notorious for its harshness.
Later Athenians said Draco's laws were "written in blood, not ink," and the word draconian (dray-KOH-nee-un) still means excessively severe in English.
The death penalty was prescribed for crimes as minor as stealing a cabbage.
When asked why, Draco supposedly replied that small crimes deserved death and he could think of no harsher punishment for large ones.
The severity was the point: at least now everyone knew what the rules were.
Written law, even harsh written law, was an improvement over the arbitrary whims of aristocratic judges.

The real reformer was Solon (SOH-lon), appointed in 594 BCE as a special mediator (archon, AR-kon, a chief magistrate) with extraordinary powers to resolve the social crisis that was tearing Athens apart.
By this point, the debt problem had become catastrophic.
Small farmers who could not repay their debts were being sold into slavery, some of them shipped overseas and lost forever.
The rich were getting richer, the poor were getting desperate, and civil war seemed imminent.
Solon's reforms were sweeping.

He cancelled all existing debts (seisachtheia, say-SAKH-thee-ah, the "shaking off of burdens"), freed Athenians who had been enslaved for debt, and made it illegal to use personal freedom as collateral for loans.

He restructured Athenian political classes based on wealth rather than birth, creating four tiers based on agricultural production: the pentakosiomedimnoi (pen-tah-KOS-ee-oh-med-IM-noy, "five-hundred-bushel men," the richest), the hippeis (hip-PAYS, "horsemen," wealthy enough to maintain a horse for cavalry service), the zeugitai (ZYOO-gih-tie, "yoke men," farmers who could afford a yoke of oxen and hoplite equipment), and the thetes (THEH-tees, the poorest laborers).
Each class had defined political rights.
The top two classes could hold the highest offices.
The zeugitai could serve on juries and in the assembly.
The thetes could attend the assembly and vote, though they could not hold office.
This was not democracy.
It was a property-based oligarchy that was wider than what had come before but still excluded most people from real power.
Solon himself was clear about his intentions: he was trying to prevent revolution by giving the poor enough to survive and the rich enough to stay invested.
He compared himself to a man standing between two armies.
After completing his reforms, Solon left Athens for ten years, reportedly to prevent people from pressuring him to change the laws.
He told the Athenians that his laws were the best they would accept, not the best he could imagine.
Solon's reforms did not stick.
Within a generation, Peisistratos had seized power as tyrant, bypassing the very institutions Solon had tried to strengthen.

But the principles Solon established, especially the abolition of debt slavery and the idea that political rights should be based on wealth rather than birth, created the foundation on which democracy would later be built.
That foundation was completed by Cleisthenes (KLYS-theh-neez), an Athenian aristocrat who, in 508 BCE, introduced the most radical political reform in the ancient world.
Cleisthenes had come to power in the messy aftermath of the tyranny's overthrow.
Rival aristocratic factions were fighting for control of Athens.

Cleisthenes, outmaneuvered in the aristocratic power struggle, made a revolutionary move: he appealed to the ordinary people of Athens for support, promising them political rights in exchange for their backing.
The strategy worked.
With popular support, Cleisthenes pushed through a complete reorganization of the Athenian political system.
The key innovation was breaking the power of the old aristocratic kinship groups.

Cleisthenes divided all Athenian citizens into ten new tribes (phylai), each composed of demes (DEE-mees, local districts) drawn from three different regions: one from the city, one from the coast, and one from the inland countryside.
This meant that each tribe was a cross-section of the entire population, mixing urban merchants with coastal fishermen and inland farmers.
The old aristocratic families could no longer dominate politics by controlling their local kinship networks, because the new tribes deliberately cut across those networks.
He established the Boule (BOO-lay), a Council of 500 composed of fifty citizens from each tribe, selected by lot (random drawing, not election).
The Boule set the agenda for the Ekklesia (eh-KLAY-see-ah), the assembly of all citizens, which had the final vote on laws, treaties, and war.
He also introduced ostracism (OSS-trah-sizm), a mechanism by which citizens could vote to exile any individual for ten years.
Each citizen scratched a name on a piece of broken pottery (an ostracon, OSS-trah-kon), and if enough votes were cast against one person (a minimum of 6,000), that person was banished.
Ostracism was designed to prevent any one individual from becoming too powerful, a safeguard against tyranny.
This was demokratia (deh-moh-KRAH-tee-ah), literally "power of the people" (from demos, the people, and kratos, power).
Athens had invented democracy.
It is worth pausing to appreciate how radical this was.
In 508 BCE, every other major civilization on earth was governed by kings, emperors, or priestly elites.
The Persian Empire under Darius (Persian Empire Ch 2) ruled fifty million people through appointed provincial governors.
Egypt had been ruled by pharaohs for over two thousand years.
Mesopotamia had never known anything but monarchy.
China under the Zhou Dynasty (Zhou Dynasty Ch 7-9) was fragmented into warring states ruled by hereditary aristocrats.
India's Mahajanapadas were kingdoms and tribal republics, some with oligarchic assemblies but nothing approaching universal male citizen suffrage.
In this world, the idea that every citizen, regardless of wealth, had an equal vote in the assembly was genuinely unprecedented.
Athens was not the first place to have assemblies or councils.
But it was the first to give final sovereign authority to the assembled body of all citizens.
The limitations were real.
Athenian "citizens" meant free adult men born to Athenian parents.

Women, slaves, and resident foreigners (called metics, MEH-tiks) were excluded, which meant that the democratic citizenry was probably 30,000 to 50,000 out of a total Athenian population (including women, children, slaves, and metics) of perhaps 250,000 to 300,000.
The system was democratic for its participants but built on a foundation of exclusions that modern observers rightly find troubling.
Still, within its own terms, it was the most radically egalitarian political system the ancient world had ever produced.
While Athens was experimenting with popular government, its future rival was perfecting a completely different model.

Sparta (SPAR-tah), located in the valley of the Eurotas River in the region of Laconia (lah-KOH-nee-ah) in the southern Peloponnese, had solved the problem of social instability in a way that horrified and fascinated the rest of Greece in equal measure.

The Spartans had conquered the neighboring region of Messenia (mess-EH-nee-ah) in the 8th and 7th centuries BCE and enslaved its entire population as helots (HEL-uts), state-owned serfs who worked Spartan-assigned land and turned over a fixed portion of their harvest to their Spartan masters.
The helot population vastly outnumbered the Spartans, by a ratio of perhaps seven to one, and the ever-present threat of helot revolt shaped every aspect of Spartan life.

The Spartan constitution, traditionally attributed to a legendary lawgiver named Lycurgus (ly-KUR-gus, whose historical existence is debated; he may be a mythical composite), created a society organized entirely around military readiness.
At the top were two hereditary kings from two royal families, who served as military commanders and religious leaders.
Below them sat the Gerousia (geh-ROO-see-ah), a council of 28 elders (men over sixty) plus the two kings, which proposed laws and served as the supreme court.
The Apella (ah-PEL-ah), the assembly of all full Spartan citizens, voted on proposals by acclamation (shouting, not ballot), but could not propose or amend legislation.
Five elected ephors (EH-forz, "overseers") supervised the kings and managed daily government, holding office for one year.
The military training system was the core of the whole arrangement.
Spartan boys were inspected at birth by tribal elders.
Those judged unfit were supposedly exposed on a mountainside, though modern scholars debate how systematically this was practiced.
At age seven, boys entered the agoge (ah-GOH-gee, "upbringing"), a state-run program of physical training, military education, and deliberate hardship that lasted until age twenty.

Boys were kept perpetually hungry, lightly clothed in all weather, trained in wrestling and weapons, organized into units with elected leaders, and taught that the highest virtue was obedience to the state and courage in battle.
They slept in communal barracks until age thirty, even if married, and ate at communal mess halls called syssitia (sis-SIH-tee-ah) for their entire adult lives.
Spartan women had more freedoms than women in most Greek poleis.
They managed households and property while their husbands were in barracks, exercised publicly (shocking to other Greeks), and were expected to produce healthy sons for the state.
The Spartan view was brutally functional: healthy mothers produce healthy warriors.
The result was the most formidable military force in Greece.
A Spartan army in the 6th century BCE was, polis for polis, unmatched in discipline, training, and fighting quality.
Spartans defined themselves through warfare and were defined by others the same way.
Their reputation would be tested, spectacularly, within a generation.
Outside Athens and Sparta, the rest of the Greek world was producing its own innovations.
In the Ionian cities on the coast of Anatolia, something entirely new was happening in how people thought about the world.

Around 585 BCE, Thales of Miletus (THAY-leez of my-LEE-tus), a man from the prosperous trading city of Miletus on the Anatolian coast, reportedly predicted a solar eclipse and proposed that the fundamental substance underlying all of nature was water.
This does not sound revolutionary.
But it was.
Thales was the first person in the surviving record to propose a natural explanation for natural phenomena, seeking causes in the physical world rather than in the actions of gods.
When he said "everything is water," he was not making a religious claim.
He was proposing a testable (even if wrong) hypothesis about the nature of reality.

Thales was followed by other Ionian thinkers: Anaximander (ah-NAK-sih-MAN-der), who proposed that the fundamental substance was an indefinite, boundless material called the apeiron; Anaximenes (ah-NAK-SIM-eh-neez), who argued it was air; Xenophanes (zen-OFF-ah-neez), who noted that Thracians depicted their gods as red-haired and that Ethiopians depicted theirs as dark-skinned, and concluded that mortals create gods in their own image rather than the reverse.

Xenophanes also recognized fossils of marine creatures in mountain rock and correctly deduced that the land had once been under the sea, some 2,400 years before modern geology confirmed the same conclusion.
This was the birth of philosophy (fih-LOSS-uh-fee, literally "love of wisdom"), the systematic attempt to understand the world through reason rather than tradition or revelation.
It was also the birth of what we would now call natural science, the search for physical explanations of physical phenomena.
These early thinkers were not building on any known precedent.

Egyptian and Mesopotamian scholars had made extraordinary advances in mathematics, astronomy, and medicine, but their work was typically embedded in religious and practical contexts (predicting floods, computing taxes, reading omens).
The Ionian innovation was asking "what is reality made of?" as a question worth pursuing for its own sake.
Across the Greek colonial world, another thinker was approaching the same questions from a different angle.

Pythagoras (pih-THAG-or-as), born on the island of Samos around 570 BCE, traveled to Egypt and possibly Babylon before founding a philosophical and mathematical community at Croton (KROH-ton) in southern Italy around 530 BCE.
The Pythagoreans (as his followers were called) discovered that musical intervals could be expressed as mathematical ratios and concluded that the universe itself was structured by number.

The Pythagorean theorem (the square of the hypotenuse of a right triangle equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides) may have been known earlier in Mesopotamia, but the Pythagoreans proved it deductively, from first principles, a method that would become the hallmark of Greek mathematics.
The Pythagorean community was part religious brotherhood, part philosophical school, and part political organization, with strict rules about diet, behavior, and secrecy.
It was also one of the ancient world's oddest social experiments: a commune of mathematicians who believed that numbers were divine.
Around the same time, the first Greek dramatic performance was taking shape.

In 534 BCE, at the Great Dionysia festival in Athens (the festival that Peisistratos had established), a performer named Thespis (THES-pis) reportedly stepped out of the chorus and spoke as an individual character, inventing the concept of the actor.
Before Thespis, religious performances at festivals were choral: groups singing and dancing together.
Thespis created the radical innovation of one person stepping forward and pretending to be someone else.
The word thespian (meaning "actor") still honors his name.

Meanwhile, on the island of Lesbos (LEZ-boss) in the northeastern Aegean, a poet named Sappho (SAF-oh) was composing lyric poetry of such emotional intensity that Plato would later call her "the Tenth Muse." Sappho's poems, composed around 610-570 BCE and surviving only in fragments, are intensely personal: love, jealousy, desire, loss, the beauty of moonlight.
In a world where poetry was typically public and heroic (Homer, Hesiod), Sappho wrote about the interior life of individual feeling.
She was the first great poet of personal experience in the Western tradition.

Greek religion during this period was not a unified theology but a vast, flexible, sometimes contradictory collection of local cults, myths, and rituals organized around the twelve Olympian gods (Zeus, Hera, Poseidon, Athena, Apollo, Artemis, Ares, Aphrodite, Hermes, Hephaestus, Demeter, and either Hestia or Dionysus, depending on the tradition).
The gods were worshipped through animal sacrifice, libation (pouring wine or oil), prayer, and festival.
The Eleusinian Mysteries (Chapter 1) promised initiates a blessed afterlife.
Other mystery cults offered similar promises through secret initiation rituals.
But Greek religion had no bible, no creed, no professional clergy with theological authority, and no prohibition on questioning the gods.
This openness to questioning, combined with the competitive intellectual culture of the poleis, created the conditions for philosophy to flourish.
You could ask "what is the nature of reality?" without being executed for blasphemy, at least usually.
By 500 BCE, the Greek world was poised.

It had literacy, philosophy, democratic governance (in Athens, at least), military discipline (in Sparta, at least), a network of colonies stretching across the Mediterranean, and a shared cultural identity expressed through Homer, the Olympics, and Delphi.
What it did not yet have was a unifying crisis, a test that would force the fractious Greek poleis to stop fighting each other and fight together.
That test was about to arrive from the east.
The Persian Empire, the largest the world had ever seen, was coming.

chapters.title: The Persian Wars
sentences: In 499 BCE, the Greek cities of the Anatolian coast did something reckless.
They revolted against the most powerful empire on earth.

The Ionian Greeks (eye-OH-nee-un), the communities along the western coast of Anatolia that had been flourishing for centuries as part of the broader Greek cultural world, had been under Persian control since Cyrus the Great conquered the Lydian kingdom in 547 BCE (Persian Empire Ch 1).
Persian rule was not particularly oppressive.

The Persians governed through local tyrants (strongmen installed as rulers of each city, loyal to Persia), collected tribute, and required military service, but otherwise left the Greek cities to manage their own affairs.
The arrangement worked well enough when the Persian-backed tyrants were competent.
When they were not, resentment built.

The Ionian Revolt began in 499 BCE, led by Aristagoras (ar-is-TAG-or-as), the deputy tyrant of Miletus who, after a failed military expedition on behalf of Persia, switched sides and encouraged the Ionian cities to rebel.
Aristagoras sailed to mainland Greece looking for allies.
Sparta refused.
Its king, Cleomenes (klee-OM-eh-neez), asked how far it was to the Persian capital.
When told it was a three-month march, he sent the Ionians away.
Athens and the small city of Eretria (eh-REE-tree-ah) on the island of Euboea agreed to help, sending twenty and five ships respectively.
A joint Greek force marched inland and burned Sardis (SAR-dis), the former Lydian capital and the seat of the Persian provincial governor.
The burning of Sardis changed everything.

It was a direct slap to the face of the Persian king, Darius I (dah-RY-us, the great organizer of the Persian Empire who had built the Royal Road, the satrap system, and the palace at Persepolis; Persian Empire Ch 2).

According to the Greek historian Herodotus (heh-ROD-uh-tus, writing roughly fifty years later in the first major work of historical prose), Darius asked who these "Athenians" were, and when told, reportedly ordered a servant to remind him three times at every dinner: "Master, remember the Athenians." The Ionian Revolt dragged on until 494 BCE, when the Persian navy crushed the Greek fleet at the Battle of Lade (LAH-dee), near Miletus.
The Persians retook the rebel cities, punished the ringleaders, and destroyed Miletus itself, the most important Ionian city.
The revolt was over.
But Darius had not forgotten the Athenians.
The Greek mainland had helped his rebel subjects.
Athens and Eretria had sent ships and soldiers to burn a Persian provincial capital.
An example needed to be made.
In 490 BCE, Darius sent a naval expedition across the Aegean with orders to punish Eretria and Athens.
The force was substantial but not enormous, probably 20,000 to 25,000 infantry and cavalry transported in roughly 600 ships.
It was a punitive expedition, not a full-scale invasion.

The Persians sacked Eretria, enslaving its population, and then landed on the plain of Marathon (MAR-uh-thon), a flat coastal strip about 25 miles (40 km) northeast of Athens, where there was enough open ground for the Persian cavalry to operate.
The Athenians sent a runner named Pheidippides (fye-DIP-ih-deez) to Sparta, covering the 150 miles (240 km) in two days, to request help.
The Spartans agreed to come but said they could not march until after a religious festival, which would take several days.
Athens could not wait.
The Battle of Marathon, fought in September 490 BCE, was one of the most consequential military engagements in ancient history.
The Athenian army, roughly 10,000 hoplites led by the general Miltiades (mil-TY-uh-deez), faced a Persian force that probably outnumbered them two to one.
The Athenians were reinforced by about 1,000 soldiers from the small city of Plataea (plah-TAY-ah), Athens' loyal ally.
No other Greek state sent help.
This was, in the critical moment, Athens' fight.
Miltiades made a bold tactical decision.
He thinned the center of his line and strengthened both wings.

When the two armies clashed, the Persian center pushed back the weakened Greek center as expected, but the strong Greek wings overwhelmed the Persian flanks and then turned inward, enveloping the Persian center in a double envelopment.
The result was devastating.
Herodotus reports that the Athenians killed 6,400 Persians while losing only 192 of their own, numbers that are almost certainly exaggerated in Athens' favor but convey the scale of the victory.
The Persians retreated to their ships and sailed away.
The legend of the marathon runner dates to this battle, though the details are confused.
The ancient sources say Pheidippides ran to Sparta before the battle, not from Marathon to Athens after it.
The story of a runner collapsing and dying after announcing "We have won!" to the Athenian assembly is a later embellishment, probably from Plutarch writing five centuries after the fact.
The modern marathon race (26.2 miles or 42.2 km) was invented for the first modern Olympic Games in 1896, inspired by the legend.
Marathon was not a decisive victory in the grand strategic sense.
The Persian Empire was not seriously damaged.
Darius had lost a punitive expedition, not an army.
But the psychological impact was enormous.
Athens, a single city-state, had beaten the Persian Empire in an open battle.

The victory proved that the hoplite phalanx could defeat Persian infantry and cavalry, that Greek heavy infantry was qualitatively superior to Persian light-armed troops in a head-on engagement, and that the vast numerical superiority of the Persian Empire was not automatically decisive.
Marathon gave the Greeks something they had never had before: confidence that Persia could be beaten.
It also gave Athens a sense of destiny.
The other Greek city-states had not shown up.
Sparta arrived a day late.
Athens, with its small Plataean ally, had won the defining battle of the age alone.
The Athenians would never let anyone forget it.
Darius died in 486 BCE before he could launch a second expedition.

His son Xerxes I (ZURK-seez) spent the next six years preparing something far larger: not a punitive raid but a full-scale invasion designed to conquer the entirety of mainland Greece and incorporate it into the Persian Empire.
The force Xerxes assembled in 480 BCE was the largest military expedition the ancient world had seen.
Herodotus claims 1.7 million soldiers and over 1,200 warships, numbers that are wildly exaggerated.
Modern estimates suggest perhaps 70,000 to 300,000 land troops (the range is wide because the evidence is uncertain) and roughly 600 to 1,200 warships.
Even the conservative estimates make it the largest amphibious operation attempted before the modern era.
Xerxes built a bridge of boats across the Hellespont (HEL-uh-spont, the narrow strait separating Europe from Asia, modern Dardanelles) and marched his army from Asia into Europe.
The Greek response was, for once, coordinated.
A congress of Greek city-states met and agreed to resist under joint Spartan command.
The strategy was defensive: slow the Persian advance at narrow chokepoints where numbers counted for less and Greek heavy infantry could fight on favorable terms.

The first position chosen was the pass at Thermopylae (ther-MOP-ih-lee, "Hot Gates," named for the hot springs at the site), a narrow coastal pass in central Greece where the mountains pressed close to the sea, creating a bottleneck that even a vast army could not easily force.
The Battle of Thermopylae in August 480 BCE is the most famous last stand in Western history.

King Leonidas I (lee-ON-ih-das) of Sparta led a force of roughly 7,000 Greeks, including his personal guard of 300 Spartans (the only Spartan contingent, each one a full citizen who had been through the agoge), to hold the pass against Xerxes' entire army.
The plan was to buy time for the main Greek fleet, stationed at nearby Artemisium (ar-teh-MIS-ee-um), to engage the Persian navy.
For two days, the Greeks held.
The narrow pass negated the Persian numerical advantage.

The Spartans, rotating in shifts, repelled wave after wave of Persian infantry, including the Immortals (the elite 10,000-strong Persian royal guard, so called because their number was always kept at exactly 10,000; when one fell, another immediately replaced him).
Xerxes, watching from a throne on a hilltop, is said to have leaped to his feet in frustration at the repeated failure of his attacks.
Then a local Greek named Ephialtes (eh-fee-AL-teez) betrayed the defenders by showing the Persians a mountain path that led behind the Greek position.

Leonidas, learning that he was about to be surrounded, dismissed most of the Greek force but stayed with his 300 Spartans, along with 700 Thespians (from the city of Thespiae, who refused to leave) and 400 Thebans.
They fought to the death.
According to Herodotus, 20,000 Persians died at Thermopylae, though this number is probably exaggerated.
All 300 Spartans were killed.

The epitaph on the monument at Thermopylae, composed by the poet Simonides, became one of the most famous inscriptions in Western history: "Go, tell the Spartans, stranger passing by, that here obedient to their laws we lie." In Greek, the phrasing is even more compressed: the word for "obedient to their laws" is a single word, peithomenoi, and it carries the entire Spartan ethic in one verb.
Thermopylae was a military defeat.
The Persians passed through and marched on Athens.
But it was a propaganda victory of incalculable value.
The 300 Spartans became the symbol of Greek resistance to Persian power, proof that Greeks would die rather than submit.
The story has been retold for 2,500 years and is still retold.
It is the founding myth of the Western military tradition.
With Thermopylae lost, central Greece was open and the Persians marched on Athens.
Themistocles (theh-MIS-toh-kleez), the most brilliant Athenian strategist of the war, had foreseen this possibility.

Years earlier, in 483 BCE, when Athens discovered a rich new vein of silver at the Laurium mines (LAW-ree-um, in southern Attica), Themistocles had convinced the assembly to spend the silver windfall on building a massive fleet of 200 triremes (TRY-reemz, warships with three banks of oars, the standard naval vessel of the ancient Mediterranean, requiring roughly 170 rowers each).
Many Athenians wanted to distribute the silver as a cash dividend.
Themistocles argued that a fleet was the only thing that could save Athens from Persia.
The assembly voted for the fleet.
It was one of the most consequential democratic decisions in history.
When Xerxes approached Athens in September 480 BCE, Themistocles ordered the city evacuated.
The entire civilian population of Athens crossed the narrow strait to the island of Salamis (SAL-uh-mis), while the men of military age manned the fleet.
Xerxes entered an empty city and burned it, including the temples on the Acropolis.
The sacred olive tree of Athena, the ancient temples, the early buildings of the young democracy, all went up in smoke.
Athens was destroyed.
But its fleet was intact.
The Battle of Salamis, fought in the narrow strait between Salamis and the Athenian coast in late September 480 BCE, was the decisive battle of the Persian Wars.
Themistocles lured the Persian fleet into the narrow strait, where the superior numbers of the Persian navy (perhaps 600-800 ships against 370-380 Greek triremes) became a liability.
The strait was so narrow that the Persian ships could not maneuver.
They jammed together, blocking each other's oars, while the heavier Greek triremes rammed them at close quarters.
Xerxes watched the destruction of his fleet from a golden throne on the shore, reportedly in grim silence.
The Greek victory at Salamis was catastrophic for the Persian war effort.
Without naval superiority, Xerxes could not supply his army overland through hostile territory for another winter.
He withdrew to Asia with most of his forces, leaving a substantial army under his general Mardonius (mar-DOH-nee-us) to finish the campaign the following year.

In the summer of 479 BCE, the largest Greek army ever assembled, roughly 40,000 hoplites under the Spartan general Pausanias (paw-SAY-nee-as, regent for the young Spartan king), met Mardonius' force near the town of Plataea in Boeotia.
The Battle of Plataea was a grinding, confused engagement, fought over several days of maneuvering and skirmishing before a decisive Greek assault shattered the Persian line.
Mardonius was killed.
His army was routed.
The survivors retreated to Asia.

On the same day, according to tradition (the simultaneity may be legendary), the Greek fleet under the Spartan king Leotychidas destroyed the remnant of the Persian navy at the Battle of Mycale (MY-kah-lee) on the Anatolian coast.
The Persian Wars were effectively over.
Greece was free.
The significance of the Greek victory cannot be overstated, but it should not be caricatured either.
This was not a clash between "Western freedom" and "Eastern despotism," as Victorian historians loved to frame it.
The Persians governed a remarkably tolerant, multicultural empire that respected local customs, religions, and languages.
The Greeks were slaveholders whose democracy excluded women, immigrants, and enslaved people.
The war was fought over power, territory, and strategic control of the Aegean, not over abstract political principles.

What the Persian Wars did accomplish was preserving the independence of the Greek poleis at the precise moment when those poleis were producing the political, philosophical, and artistic experiments that would define Western civilization.
If Xerxes had won at Salamis, Athens would have become a Persian subject city.
There would have been no Parthenon, no Sophocles, no Socrates, no Plato, no Aristotle, at least not in the forms we know.
This is counterfactual speculation, not history.
But it is the reason the Persian Wars matter: they kept the Greek experiment running at the moment it was beginning to produce its most extraordinary results.
The Greeks knew they had done something remarkable.
The generation that fought at Marathon and Salamis became the most celebrated in Greek history, the standard against which all subsequent Greeks measured themselves.

Herodotus wrote his Histories (composed around 440 BCE) specifically to explain how and why the Greeks defeated the Persians, producing in the process the first major work of narrative history in the Western tradition.

Herodotus traveled across the known world, from Egypt to Babylon to Scythia, interviewing witnesses and collecting stories, and wove them into a sprawling, digressive, endlessly entertaining narrative.

He called it historiai, "inquiries," the origin of the English word "history." It was the first time anyone had tried to explain a major event through systematic investigation rather than myth or divine causation.
For this, Herodotus is called the "Father of History." The wars also tilted the balance of power in Greece.
Sparta had led the land campaigns, and its soldiers had fought magnificently at Thermopylae and Plataea.
But Athens had provided the fleet that won at Salamis, the battle that actually decided the war.
Athens emerged from the Persian Wars as a naval superpower with an enormous fleet, a burning sense of mission, and a democratic citizenry that had just proven it could beat an empire.
The combination was intoxicating, and dangerous.
Athens was about to build an empire of its own.

chapters.title: The Golden Age of Athens
sentences: In the spring of 478 BCE, the Greek city-states that wanted to continue the war against Persia formed an alliance.
They did not know they were creating an Athenian empire.
The Delian League was established that year, with its treasury on the sacred island of Delos (DEE-los) in the central Aegean.

The idea was simple: a voluntary alliance of Greek city-states, each contributing ships or money to maintain a combined fleet that would liberate the remaining Greek cities under Persian control and protect the Aegean from future Persian aggression.
Sparta, having no interest in naval warfare or overseas campaigns, stayed out.
Athens, with its massive fleet, was the natural leader.
Each member state swore an oath of alliance, sealed by dropping lumps of metal into the sea (symbolizing that the alliance would last until the metal floated).
The contributions were assessed by an Athenian named Aristides (ar-ISS-tih-deez), known as "the Just," who set the initial levels so fairly that no one complained.
Within twenty years, the voluntary alliance had become an Athenian protection racket.
The transformation was gradual but relentless.
Member states that tried to leave the league were forced back by Athenian military action.
The island of Naxos revolted around 470 BCE and was besieged and compelled to rejoin.
Thasos revolted in 465 BCE over a dispute about gold mines; Athens crushed the revolt, tore down Thasos' walls, confiscated its fleet, and imposed tribute.
In 454 BCE, Athens transferred the League treasury from Delos to Athens itself, ostensibly for safekeeping after a military setback in Egypt but in reality to tighten Athenian control over the money.
The tribute, originally intended to fund a common fleet, was now flowing directly into the Athenian treasury, and Athens was spending it on whatever Athens wanted.
What Athens wanted was to become the most beautiful city in the Greek world.

The man who drove this transformation was Pericles (PAIR-ih-kleez), the dominant figure in Athenian politics from roughly 461 to 429 BCE, a period so identified with him that it is simply called the Age of Pericles.
Pericles was an aristocrat by birth, a democrat by conviction, and a politician of extraordinary skill.

He was elected strategos (strah-teh-GOS, "general," the most important elected office in Athens, one of ten generals elected annually) year after year, holding the position almost continuously for over thirty years, not through coercion but through his ability to persuade the Athenian assembly to follow his vision.
Pericles' vision was that Athens should be the undisputed cultural and political capital of the Greek world, and that the tribute from the Delian League was the proper means to achieve this.

When critics objected that using allied money to beautify Athens was effectively stealing from the alliance, Pericles had a blunt response: Athens protected the allies from Persia, and as long as Athens was providing that protection, how Athens spent the money was Athens' business.
It was a compelling argument if you were Athenian and an infuriating one if you were a tribute-paying ally.
Under Pericles, Athens completed a series of building projects that transformed the city from a war-damaged provincial capital into the most architecturally spectacular city in Greece.
The centerpiece was the Acropolis (ah-KROP-uh-lis), the rocky hilltop above the city that Xerxes had burned in 480 BCE.
Where the Persians had left ruins, Pericles built monuments.
The Parthenon (PAR-theh-non) was begun in 447 BCE and completed in 432 BCE.
It is a temple to Athena Parthenos (Athena the Virgin), the patron goddess of Athens, and it remains, even in its ruined state, the most famous building of the ancient world.
The Parthenon was designed by the architects Ictinus (ik-TY-nus) and Callicrates (kah-LIK-rah-teez) and supervised by the sculptor Phidias (FID-ee-as), the greatest artist of the classical period.

It is a Doric temple (the simplest and most severe of the three Greek architectural orders, characterized by thick, fluted columns without bases, topped by plain capitals), 228 feet (69.5 meters) long and 101 feet (30.9 meters) wide, with 46 outer columns.

Inside stood a colossal statue of Athena, over 38 feet (11.5 meters) tall, made of gold and ivory (chryselephantine, kris-EL-eh-fan-teen, a technique in which gold sheets and carved ivory were applied over a wooden core).
The statue alone consumed roughly 2,500 pounds (1,140 kg) of gold, a staggering expenditure that only made sense as a display of imperial wealth.
The Parthenon's perfection is not accidental.

It contains deliberate optical refinements that correct for visual distortions: the columns lean slightly inward, the corner columns are slightly thicker than the others, the floor curves gently upward toward the center, and the horizontal lines of the building are subtly curved so they appear straight from a distance.
These corrections are so precise that they required extraordinary mathematical and engineering skill.
The Parthenon looks simple.
It is anything but.
Phidias also created the colossal Statue of Zeus at Olympia, another chryselephantine masterwork, roughly 40 feet (12 meters) tall, showing Zeus seated on an elaborate throne.
The Statue of Zeus was counted among the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.
Between the Athena at Athens and the Zeus at Olympia, Phidias produced two of the most celebrated artworks of antiquity.
Neither survives.
Both are known only from descriptions and small-scale copies.

The Erechtheion (eh-REK-thee-on), another temple on the Acropolis, is famous for its Porch of the Caryatids (KAR-ee-AT-idz), six sculpted female figures serving as columns, each carrying the weight of the roof on her head.
The Propylaea (prop-ih-LEE-ah), the monumental gateway to the Acropolis, and the small Temple of Athena Nike (Nike meaning "Victory") completed the complex.
Below the Acropolis, the Odeon of Pericles provided a roofed concert hall for musical competitions.

Athens' port at Piraeus (py-REE-us) was connected to the city by the Long Walls, two parallel fortification walls roughly four miles (6.4 km) long that created a secure corridor between the city and the sea.
As long as the Long Walls stood and the fleet controlled the sea, Athens could not be starved into submission by a land siege.
The Long Walls were the physical expression of Athens' strategic doctrine: the city was a naval power, and its lifeline was the sea.
Athens in the mid-5th century BCE was producing art, literature, and thought at a rate and quality that has no parallel in any other time or place of comparable size.
The population of Athens and its surrounding territory of Attica was perhaps 250,000 to 300,000 (including women, children, slaves, and resident foreigners).
The citizen body, the men who voted, served on juries, and fought in the army, was perhaps 30,000 to 50,000.
This is a small city by modern standards.
What it produced in roughly two generations is staggering.
Greek tragedy reached its peak in three playwrights who lived and worked in this period.
Aeschylus (ESS-kih-lus, roughly 525-456 BCE), who had fought at Marathon, was the first of the three great tragedians.
His Oresteia (or-es-TY-ah), premiered in 458 BCE, is the only complete trilogy to survive from ancient Greek drama.

It tells the story of Agamemnon's murder by his wife Clytemnestra upon his return from Troy, the vengeance taken by their son Orestes, and the trial of Orestes before Athena and a jury of Athenian citizens.
The trilogy traces the evolution of justice from blood feud to civic law, from personal revenge to institutional judgment.

It is, among other things, a founding myth for Athenian democracy: the play ends with the goddess Athena establishing a court of law in Athens, replacing the cycle of vendetta with the rule of reason.
Sophocles (SOF-oh-kleez, roughly 496-406 BCE) wrote over 120 plays, of which seven survive complete.

His Antigone (an-TIG-oh-nee), performed around 441 BCE, explores the conflict between divine law and human authority: a woman who defies the king's edict to bury her dead brother, and the catastrophe that unfolds when the king refuses to back down.
His Oedipus Rex (EH-dih-pus, "Oedipus the King") is often cited as the most perfect Greek tragedy, a devastating study of a good man destroyed by the very qualities that made him great.
Aristotle would later use it as his primary example when defining tragedy in the Poetics.
Euripides (yoo-RIP-ih-deez, roughly 480-406 BCE) was the most psychologically complex and controversial of the three.
His Medea (meh-DEE-ah), performed in 431 BCE, gives voice to a foreign woman betrayed by her Greek husband, who takes revenge by murdering their children.
Euripides' characters are more human and less heroic than those of Aeschylus and Sophocles.
His women are fierce, his heroes are flawed, and his gods are indifferent or cruel.
He questioned the values that his audience held dear, and the audience sometimes resented it.
He won fewer prizes than Sophocles but was far more popular in later centuries.
Greek comedy flourished alongside tragedy.
Aristophanes (ar-is-TOF-ah-neez, roughly 446-386 BCE) wrote satirical comedies that mercilessly lampooned Athenian politicians, philosophers, and social norms.
His The Clouds mocked Socrates as a charlatan.
His Lysistrata (ly-SIS-trah-tah) imagined the women of Athens and Sparta refusing to have sex until their husbands stopped fighting the Peloponnesian War.
His The Birds depicts two Athenians founding a utopian city in the sky.

Aristophanes' comedies are crude, brilliant, politically sharp, and often very funny, and they are the only complete examples of Old Comedy (the free-wheeling, no-holds-barred political satire of 5th-century Athens) to survive.
All of these plays were performed at the Great Dionysia, the annual festival of Dionysus.
Three tragedians each presented a trilogy of tragedies plus a satyr play (a short comic afterpiece), and five comedians each presented a comedy.
The performances were competitions, judged by panels of citizens.
The plays were staged in the open-air Theater of Dionysus on the south slope of the Acropolis, which could hold roughly 17,000 spectators.
Attendance may have been subsidized by the state, ensuring that even poor citizens could watch.
Theater in Athens was not entertainment in the modern sense.

It was a civic institution, funded by wealthy citizens (who paid for the productions as a form of public service), performed for the entire community, and engaged with the political, moral, and religious questions that the community was grappling with.
The theater was the place where Athens argued with itself.
The intellectual revolution was not limited to literature.
Athens in the 5th century BCE became the center of a philosophical inquiry that would reshape how humans think about the world.
Socrates (SOK-rah-teez, roughly 470-399 BCE) never wrote a single word.

Everything we know about his thought comes from his students, primarily Plato (Chapter 6), who cast Socrates as the main character in a series of dialogues that are simultaneously philosophy, literature, and drama.
Socrates taught by asking questions, relentlessly probing the assumptions that people took for granted.
What is justice?
What is courage?
What is virtue?
Can virtue be taught?
Do you really know what you think you know?
The Socratic method (soh-KRAT-ik), the technique of teaching through questions rather than lectures, was not just a pedagogical tool.
It was a philosophical position: that most people do not actually understand the concepts they use most confidently, and that the first step toward wisdom is recognizing your own ignorance.

Socrates' contemporaries included Anaxagoras (an-ak-SAG-or-as), who came to Athens from Ionia, was reportedly Pericles' teacher, and proposed that the sun was not a god but a mass of burning metal larger than the Peloponnese, an idea that got him prosecuted for impiety.

Democritus (deh-MOK-rih-tus, roughly 460-370 BCE), working from the town of Abdera in Thrace, proposed that all matter is composed of tiny, indivisible particles called atomoi (AT-oh-moy, "uncuttable," the origin of the English word "atom"), moving through empty void.
His theory, developed with his teacher Leucippus (loo-KIP-us), would not be confirmed for over two thousand years but would prove to be one of the most prescient guesses in the history of science.

Empedocles (em-PED-oh-kleez) proposed that all matter consists of four elements (earth, water, air, and fire), combined and separated by forces he called Love and Strife, a theory that dominated Western natural philosophy until the Scientific Revolution.
Hippocrates (hip-POK-rah-teez) of the island of Kos, working around 430 BCE, founded the tradition of rational medicine.

The Hippocratic Corpus (a collection of medical texts attributed to Hippocrates and his followers) insisted that diseases have natural causes, not supernatural ones, and should be treated through observation, diagnosis, and treatment rather than prayer and magic.
The Hippocratic Oath, still taken (in modified form) by doctors today, established ethical standards for medical practice: do no harm, maintain patient confidentiality, do not exploit the vulnerable.
Hippocrates did not cure many diseases.
But he established the principle that disease is a natural phenomenon amenable to rational investigation, which was the necessary first step toward everything that followed.
Herodotus (Chapter 3) invented history.

Thucydides (thoo-SID-ih-deez, roughly 460-400 BCE), an Athenian general who was exiled for losing a battle during the Peloponnesian War, wrote a history of that war (Chapter 5) that is considered the first truly analytical work of political and military history.
Where Herodotus wandered and told stories, Thucydides dissected causes, analyzed motives, and insisted on verifiable evidence.
His account of the plague of Athens (Chapter 5) is the earliest clinical description of an epidemic.

His Melian Dialogue, in which Athenian envoys tell the people of a neutral island that "the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must," remains one of the most unflinching statements of political realism ever written.
The Athenian Agora (AG-or-ah), the civic center below the Acropolis, was where much of this intellectual life took place.
Philosophers argued under the colonnades.
Politicians debated in the open air.
Merchants sold goods in the market stalls.
Jurors heard cases in the law courts.
Socrates walked the Agora every day, cornering anyone willing to talk.
The Agora was not just a marketplace.
It was the physical space where democratic politics, commercial life, and intellectual inquiry intersected.
Athens' golden age was real, and its achievements were extraordinary.
But it was funded by empire, sustained by slavery, and marked by an arrogance that would prove catastrophic.

Pericles himself, in his famous Funeral Oration (delivered in 431 BCE over the Athenian dead of the first year of the Peloponnesian War, as recorded by Thucydides), celebrated Athens as "the school of Greece" and its democratic institutions as a model for the world.
It was a beautiful speech.
It was also delivered on the eve of a war that Athens would lose, a war largely caused by the imperial overreach that the Funeral Oration was, in part, justifying.
The golden age was about to end.
It would be destroyed not by Persia but by Greeks.

chapters.title: The Peloponnesian War
sentences: The war between Athens and Sparta lasted twenty-seven years, wrecked the Greek world, and proved that the people who invented democracy, philosophy, and tragedy were also fully capable of destroying themselves.
The Peloponnesian War (431-404 BCE) was not a surprise.
The two great power blocs of the Greek world, the Athenian maritime empire and the Spartan-led land alliance called the Peloponnesian League, had been on a collision course for decades.

Athens was expanding aggressively, converting the Delian League into an empire, demanding tribute, punishing dissent, and interfering in the affairs of states that Sparta considered within its sphere of influence.
Sparta was conservative, land-based, and alarmed by the growth of Athenian power.

Thucydides, whose history of the war is the primary source and one of the greatest works of political analysis ever written, identified the fundamental cause with brutal clarity: "The real cause I consider to be the one which was formally most kept out of sight.

The growth of the power of Athens, and the alarm which this inspired in Sparta, made war inevitable." The immediate triggers were disputes over relatively minor issues: a conflict over the city of Corcyra (kor-SY-rah, modern Corfu), which had allied with Athens against its mother city Corinth (a Spartan ally), and the Athenian economic embargo against Megara (MEG-ar-ah), a small city on the isthmus between Athens and the Peloponnese.
Corinth, feeling threatened by Athens' alliance with Corcyra, pressured Sparta to act.
Sparta issued an ultimatum: Athens must give its allies their freedom.
Athens refused.
In the spring of 431 BCE, a Spartan army marched into Attica.
Pericles' strategy was counterintuitive and, within its own logic, brilliant.
He knew Athens could not beat the Spartan army in an open land battle.
The Spartans were simply too good.
But Athens had the Long Walls, the fleet, and the tribute from the empire.

Pericles' plan was to bring the rural population of Attica inside the Long Walls, abandon the countryside to Spartan devastation, and use the fleet to raid the Peloponnesian coastline while the empire's tribute funded the war effort.
Athens would not try to win on land.
It would survive behind its walls and win at sea.
The Spartans could burn every olive tree in Attica, and Athens would still be fed by grain ships from the Black Sea, protected by the fleet, and funded by the empire.
Eventually, Sparta would exhaust itself.
The strategy was psychologically brutal.
Athenian farmers watched from the walls as Spartan soldiers burned their farms, cut down their orchards, and destroyed their homes.
Olive trees take fifteen to twenty years to mature.
Every tree the Spartans cut was a generation of lost income.
But the walls held, the fleet sailed, and the tribute kept flowing.
In the first two years of the war, Pericles' strategy was working.
Then the plague hit.

In the summer of 430 BCE, a devastating epidemic (modern scholars have debated whether it was typhus, typhoid fever, smallpox, or something else; the identification remains uncertain) broke out in the overcrowded city.
The rural population crammed inside the walls, with inadequate sanitation and close quarters, was devastated.
Thucydides, who contracted the disease himself and survived, left a clinical description that is the earliest detailed account of an epidemic in Western literature.
He describes the symptoms with terrifying precision: high fever, inflammation of the eyes and throat, violent coughing, intestinal distress, and a rash of blisters and ulcers.
The sick died within seven to nine days.
Those who survived were often left blinded or with damaged extremities.
The death toll may have reached a quarter of the Athenian population.
Among the dead, in 429 BCE, was Pericles himself.

The man who had guided Athens for thirty years, who had built the Parthenon and the Long Walls, who had turned a maritime alliance into an empire, died of the same disease that was ravaging the city he had packed behind its walls.
The irony was not lost on contemporaries.
The very strategy that was supposed to protect Athens, withdrawing the population behind the Long Walls, had created the overcrowded conditions that made the plague catastrophic.
After Pericles' death, Athenian politics became increasingly reckless.
The dominant figure of the next phase of the war was Cleon (KLEE-on), a populist demagogue whom Thucydides despised and Aristophanes mocked.
Cleon advocated aggressive military action and harsh treatment of rebellious allies.

When the island of Mytilene (mih-tih-LEE-nee) revolted from the Delian League in 428 BCE, Cleon persuaded the assembly to vote for the execution of all adult males and the enslavement of all women and children.

The assembly reversed the vote the next day (a ship was sent to overtake the one carrying the original order, and arrived just in time), but the episode reveals how brutal the democratic assembly could be when fear and anger drove policy.
Thucydides included both debates, the one for massacre and the one against, as a study in how democracies make decisions under stress.
A temporary peace, the Peace of Nicias (NY-shee-as, named after the Athenian general who negotiated it), was concluded in 421 BCE.
It lasted six years on paper, though neither side fully honored its terms.
During the uneasy truce, a young, wealthy, charismatic, and spectacularly reckless Athenian aristocrat named Alcibiades (al-sih-BY-uh-deez) rose to prominence.
Alcibiades was brilliant, ambitious, utterly unscrupulous, and possessed of a talent for self-destruction that even his considerable gifts could not overcome.
In 415 BCE, Alcibiades persuaded the Athenian assembly to launch the most ambitious and disastrous military expedition of the war: the Sicilian Expedition.
The target was Syracuse (SEER-ah-kyooz), the largest and most powerful Greek city in Sicily, allied with Sparta.
Alcibiades argued that conquering Sicily would give Athens the resources to crush Sparta and dominate the entire Mediterranean.
The assembly voted to send 134 triremes, 5,000 hoplites, and thousands of support troops on a campaign that was essentially a gamble for total victory.

On the eve of departure, someone mutilated the Hermai (HER-my, stone pillars topped with the head of the god Hermes that stood at doorways and crossroads throughout Athens), smashing their faces in an act of sacrilege that horrified the city.
Alcibiades was accused of involvement.
He sailed with the fleet but was recalled to stand trial.
Rather than face judgment, he defected to Sparta, where he proceeded to advise the Spartans on how to defeat his own city.
Treason did not trouble him.
The Sicilian Expedition, now led by the cautious and unlucky Nicias, was a catastrophe.
The Athenians besieged Syracuse but could not take it.
Spartan reinforcements arrived.
The Athenian fleet was trapped and destroyed in Syracuse's harbor.
The army, attempting to retreat overland, was hunted down, surrounded, and captured.
Roughly 40,000 Athenians and their allies were killed or enslaved.
The survivors were thrown into the stone quarries of Syracuse, where most of them died of thirst, disease, and exposure.
Nicias was executed.
The Sicilian disaster in 413 BCE was the turning point of the war.
Athens lost the core of its fleet, a significant portion of its fighting manpower, and, crucially, its aura of invincibility.
Allies that had been cowed into submission began to revolt.
Persia, sensing opportunity, began funding the Spartan war effort.
Athens rebuilt its fleet with remarkable speed and fought on for another nine years, but the tide had turned.
The war's final act was driven by two ironies.
The first was that Sparta, the land power that had never built a significant navy, won the war at sea, using Persian gold to build a fleet that could challenge Athens on its own element.
The Spartan admiral Lysander (ly-SAN-der) developed a close relationship with the Persian prince Cyrus the Younger, who provided the funds to pay rowers at a higher rate than Athens could match.

The second irony was that Athens, the democracy, briefly overthrew its own democratic government in 411 BCE, installing an oligarchic council of 400 in a coup driven by the argument that democracy was too unstable to win a war.
The oligarchy lasted four months before being overthrown, and democracy was restored.
But the episode showed how thin the commitment to democratic governance could become under extreme stress.
The end came in 405 BCE at the Battle of Aegospotami (ee-goh-SPOT-ah-my, "Goat Rivers"), a strait in the Hellespont.
Lysander caught the Athenian fleet beached and unguarded, capturing almost the entire force.
Of roughly 180 Athenian triremes, only 9 escaped.
With the fleet gone, Athens could not protect its grain supply from the Black Sea.
Lysander sailed to Athens and blockaded the harbor.
By the spring of 404 BCE, Athens was starving.
Athens surrendered in April 404 BCE.

The terms were humiliating: the Long Walls were torn down to the sound of flute music (Sparta's allies wanted them destroyed as a symbol of Athenian power), the fleet was reduced to twelve ships, the empire was dissolved, and an oligarchic government called the Thirty Tyrants was installed under Spartan supervision.

The Thirty, led by Critias (KRIH-tee-as, ironically a former student of Socrates), ruled with calculated brutality, executing an estimated 1,500 Athenians and confiscating property on a massive scale.
Democracy was restored within a year when a democratic resistance force overthrew the Thirty, but the Athens that emerged was diminished in power, wealth, and confidence.
The Peloponnesian War killed roughly half of Athens' male citizen population through combat, plague, and the Sicilian disaster.
It destroyed the Athenian empire.

It radicalized Greek politics, replacing the relatively stable balance of power that had existed before the war with a series of destructive hegemonies in which whoever was on top used force to stay there.
And it demonstrated that the Greek city-state system, for all its cultural brilliance, contained a fatal flaw: the poleis could not stop fighting each other.
The same fierce independence that had produced democracy, philosophy, and tragedy also produced endless, self-destructive warfare.
Thucydides understood this better than anyone, which is why his history remains required reading in military academies twenty-five centuries later.

chapters.title: Crisis and Philip
sentences: Sparta won the Peloponnesian War and then proved spectacularly incapable of running an empire.
The problem was structural.
Sparta had been designed, from the ground up, as an inward-looking military society organized around the control of its helot population.
Its constitution was built for stability, not for projecting power across the Greek world.

Spartan kings and generals who spent extended periods abroad, commanding garrisons and managing allies, tended to be corrupted by the wealth and freedom they encountered outside Sparta's austere system.
Lysander, the admiral who had won the war, accumulated personal power and Persian connections that alarmed the Spartan government.
Other Spartan commanders, stationed as military governors in former Athenian allied cities, behaved like petty tyrants.
Within a decade of its victory, Sparta had managed to make itself more hated than Athens had ever been.
The result was a cascade of wars.

The Corinthian War (395-387 BCE) pitted Sparta against an alliance of Athens, Thebes, Corinth, and Argos, all of them former Spartan allies who had switched sides out of frustration with Spartan arrogance.

Persia, playing both sides as usual, initially funded the anti-Spartan coalition, then switched to backing Sparta when Athens started rebuilding its fleet and threatening Persian interests in the Aegean.

The war ended with the King's Peace (387 BCE), also called the Peace of Antalcidas (an-TAL-sih-das), which was essentially a Persian dictation: all Greek cities in Asia Minor were to be Persian subjects, all other Greek cities were to be autonomous.
The irony was devastating.
The generation that had fought and died at Marathon, Thermopylae, and Salamis to keep Greece free from Persia had produced grandchildren who accepted Persian arbitration of Greek affairs.
While Sparta fumbled and Athens tried to rebuild, a third power was rising.
Thebes (THEEBZ), the leading city of the region of Boeotia (bee-OH-shuh) in central Greece, had been a second-tier power for most of Greek history.

It had the misfortune of having sided with Persia during the Persian Wars (the Thebans at Thermopylae were there under compulsion and surrendered to Xerxes), which earned it lasting suspicion from the rest of Greece.
But in the 370s and 360s BCE, Thebes produced two of the most remarkable military innovators in Greek history.
Epaminondas (eh-PAM-ih-NON-das) was a Theban general who reinvented infantry tactics.

At the Battle of Leuctra (LOOK-trah) in 371 BCE, Epaminondas defeated a full Spartan army (including a Spartan king) using a formation called the oblique phalanx: instead of the traditional uniform line, he stacked his left wing to a depth of fifty shields (compared to the normal eight to twelve) and held back his right wing, creating an overwhelming concentration of force at the decisive point.
The Spartan right wing, composed of the elite Spartan citizen-soldiers, was crushed.
Roughly 400 of the 700 full Spartan citizens in the battle were killed, including the king.
It was the first time a Spartan army had been decisively beaten in a pitched battle, and the shock reverberated across Greece.
Epaminondas followed up Leuctra with invasions of the Peloponnese that struck at the foundations of Spartan power.
He liberated Messenia, freeing the helots who had been enslaved for over three centuries and founding the city of Messene (mess-EE-nee) as the capital of a newly independent state.
Without the helot labor that had sustained the Spartan system (the helots farmed while the Spartans trained), Sparta's military economy was permanently crippled.
Epaminondas also founded the city of Megalopolis (meg-ah-LOP-oh-lis, "Great City") in Arcadia as a counterweight to Spartan influence in the central Peloponnese.
These were not just military victories.
They were strategic demolitions.
Epaminondas was systematically dismantling the structures that had made Sparta powerful.
Fighting alongside Epaminondas was Pelopidas (peh-LOP-ih-das) and the Sacred Band of Thebes, an elite unit of 300 soldiers composed of 150 pairs of male lovers.
