The theory, attributed to Plato's Symposium, was that men fighting alongside their beloved would fight more fiercely and never flee the battlefield for shame of showing cowardice before their partner.

The Sacred Band justified the theory: it was undefeated in battle for over thirty years, from its formation around 378 BCE until it was annihilated by Philip of Macedon at the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE.
Theban hegemony lasted barely a decade.
Epaminondas was killed at the Battle of Mantinea (man-tih-NEE-ah) in 362 BCE, winning the battle but dying on the field.
With his death, Theban power collapsed.
He had been the system.
No successor of comparable genius emerged.
But the damage he had done to Sparta was permanent.
Sparta, reduced in citizen numbers, stripped of its helot workforce, and hemmed in by newly freed neighbors, would never again be a first-rate power.
The fourth century BCE was a period of exhaustion and intellectual ferment.
While the Greek city-states fought each other into mutual weakness, the philosophers kept working.
In 399 BCE, Athens had executed Socrates.
The charges were impiety (introducing new gods) and corrupting the youth.

The real reasons were political: Socrates had been associated with several members of the Thirty Tyrants (Critias was his former student, Alcibiades had been his protege), and in the traumatized atmosphere of post-war Athens, this was enough.
Socrates was tried before a jury of 501 citizens, found guilty by a narrow margin, and sentenced to death by drinking hemlock.
He was seventy years old.

The trial and death of Socrates became the foundational event of Western philosophy, not because of the legal issues (which were fairly straightforward by Athenian law) but because of what Socrates said and did.
In Plato's account (the Apology), Socrates refused to apologize, argued that the unexamined life is not worth living, and told the jury that he would not stop questioning even if they acquitted him.
Given the choice between exile and death, he chose death, arguing that a philosopher who fled his own city to save his skin would have nothing left worth saving.
He drank the hemlock surrounded by his students, one of whom, Plato, spent the rest of his life trying to understand what had happened and why.
Plato (PLAY-toh, roughly 427-347 BCE) was Socrates' most important student and, after Socrates' death, became the most influential philosopher in Western history.
In 387 BCE, he founded the Academy, a school in a grove of olive trees sacred to the hero Academus outside the walls of Athens.
The Academy was the first institution of higher learning in the Western world, and it operated continuously for nearly 900 years, until the Roman emperor Justinian closed it in 529 CE.
Plato's students included mathematicians, astronomers, political theorists, and the man who would become the second most influential philosopher in history.
Plato's Republic (written around 375 BCE) is his most famous work: a dialogue exploring the nature of justice, the ideal state, and the nature of reality.

The Allegory of the Cave (in which prisoners chained in a cave mistake shadows on the wall for reality, and the philosopher is the one who escapes into the sunlight and sees things as they truly are) is the single most famous image in Western philosophy.
The Republic also proposes a utopian state ruled by philosopher-kings, wise guardians who have seen the truth and govern for the common good.

Whether Plato meant this as a serious political program or a thought experiment remains debated, but the idea that the best rulers are the wisest, rather than the strongest or the most popular, has haunted political theory ever since.
Meanwhile, another philosopher was taking a very different approach to life.

Diogenes of Sinope (dy-OJ-eh-neez, roughly 404-323 BCE) was the most famous of the Cynics (SIN-iks, from kynikos, "dog-like," a name the Cynics embraced), a philosophical movement that rejected all social conventions, material possessions, and political ambitions in favor of living according to nature.

Diogenes lived in a large ceramic jar (or barrel), owned nothing, begged for food, and deliberately violated every social norm he could find, including (according to tradition) performing bodily functions in public.

When Alexander the Great visited him and asked if there was anything he could do for him, Diogenes reportedly replied: "Yes, stand out of my sunlight." Alexander, impressed, is said to have remarked: "If I were not Alexander, I would be Diogenes." Around 370 BCE, Xenophon (ZEN-oh-fon), an Athenian gentleman-soldier and student of Socrates, published his Anabasis (ah-NAB-ah-sis, "The March Up-Country"), an account of his participation in a Greek mercenary expedition into the Persian Empire in 401 BCE.

Ten thousand Greek soldiers, hired to support a Persian prince's failed attempt to seize the throne, found themselves stranded 1,500 miles (2,400 km) from home in hostile territory after their employer was killed.
Xenophon helped lead the retreat through the mountains of modern Kurdistan and Armenia to the Black Sea coast.

The Anabasis is one of the great adventure narratives of antiquity, and it demonstrated something that Philip of Macedon would notice: a relatively small Greek army could march deep into the Persian Empire and survive.
Persia was not invincible.
Persia was, perhaps, conquerable.

While the Greek city-states exhausted themselves in the Corinthian War, the Theban hegemony, and endless minor conflicts, a kingdom on the northern fringe of the Greek world was quietly becoming dangerous.
Macedon (MASS-eh-don), located north of Thessaly in the broad plains and mountains of what is now northern Greece, was barely considered Greek by the poleis to the south.

The Macedonians spoke a Greek dialect, worshipped the Greek gods, and competed in the Olympic Games (where their kings' right to participate had been repeatedly challenged on the grounds that they were not really Greek).
But their kingdom was run by a hereditary monarchy, not by democratic assemblies or oligarchic councils.
Their elite fought on horseback, not on foot.
Their court culture was rough, hard-drinking, and often violently unstable.
The southern Greeks regarded them as semi-barbaric cousins, and the Macedonians resented it.

In 359 BCE, Philip II (FILL-ip) became king of Macedon at the age of twenty-three, inheriting a kingdom that was nearly bankrupt, threatened by hostile neighbors on all sides, and still recovering from the assassination of its previous king.
Within twenty years, Philip would transform Macedon into the dominant military power in Greece and end the era of independent city-states forever.
Philip's genius was military.
He had spent three years as a teenage hostage in Thebes (368-365 BCE), where he had studied the tactical innovations of Epaminondas at first hand.
He returned to Macedon and applied what he had learned on a much larger scale.
He created the Macedonian phalanx, armed not with the eight-foot spear of the traditional hoplite but with the sarissa (sah-RIS-ah), a pike eighteen to twenty feet (5.5-6 meters) long.
Wielded in a tight formation, the sarissa phalanx projected a hedge of spear points five rows deep in front of the formation, outreaching any hoplite line.

He combined the phalanx with heavy cavalry (the Companion cavalry, the Macedonian aristocratic elite who fought on horseback with lances), light infantry, archers, slingers, and siege engineers into a combined-arms force more versatile than anything Greece had seen.
He was also a diplomat of extraordinary cunning.
He bribed, threatened, cajoled, and married his way to power (he had at least seven wives, each marriage a political alliance).

He exploited the rivalries between the Greek city-states, supporting one side and then the other, making promises he never intended to keep, and always positioning himself as the indispensable mediator.

Demosthenes (deh-MOS-theh-neez), the greatest Athenian orator, delivered a series of speeches called the Philippics (fih-LIP-iks, the origin of the English word for a bitter verbal attack) warning Athens of the Macedonian danger.

Demosthenes saw clearly what was happening: Philip was systematically dismantling the Greek city-state system, absorbing one city after another through a combination of military pressure and diplomatic manipulation.
But Athens was exhausted, divided, and slow to act.
The endgame came at the Battle of Chaeronea (kair-oh-NEE-ah) in Boeotia in 338 BCE.
Philip's army, roughly 30,000 infantry and 2,000 cavalry, faced a combined force of Athenians and Thebans.
The Macedonian left wing, commanded by Philip's eighteen-year-old son Alexander, shattered the Theban line and annihilated the Sacred Band of Thebes.
The 300 warriors who had been undefeated for forty years died to a man, fighting in their pairs.
Their burial mound and lion monument at Chaeronea still stand.
Chaeronea ended Greek city-state independence.
Philip organized the conquered Greeks into the League of Corinth, a Macedonian-dominated alliance that declared war on Persia.
The invasion was planned, the advance force was sent.

And then, in 336 BCE, Philip was assassinated at the wedding of his daughter, stabbed by one of his own bodyguards in what was probably a personal vendetta, though conspiracy theories involving his estranged wife Olympias and his son Alexander have circulated ever since.

Philip left behind the most powerful military machine in the Mediterranean, a concrete plan to invade the Persian Empire, and a twenty-year-old son who was about to become the most successful conqueror in human history.
Aristotle (AR-ih-STOT-ul, roughly 384-322 BCE), Plato's most brilliant student, had been Alexander's personal tutor from age thirteen to sixteen.
In 335 BCE, after Alexander departed for his Persian campaign, Aristotle returned to Athens and founded his own school, the Lyceum (ly-SEE-um), in a grove sacred to Apollo.
Where Plato dealt in abstract ideals and mathematics, Aristotle dealt in observation, classification, and systematic analysis.
He wrote on logic, metaphysics, ethics, politics, biology, physics, rhetoric, and Poetics.
His Nicomachean Ethics defined moral philosophy for millennia.
His Politics analyzed every known form of government.
His biological works classified hundreds of species.
His Poetics defined what tragedy is.

His logical system, the syllogism (a form of deductive reasoning: if all men are mortal, and Socrates is a man, then Socrates is mortal), remained the foundation of formal logic until the 19th century.
Between Plato's Academy and Aristotle's Lyceum, Athens in the mid-4th century BCE contained the two most important intellectual institutions in the ancient world.

The Theater of Epidaurus (ep-ih-DOR-us), built around 340 BCE in the Peloponnese, is the best-preserved ancient Greek theater and demonstrates the extraordinary acoustic engineering the Greeks achieved.
Its 14,000-seat auditorium is so precisely designed that a coin dropped on the central stage can be heard in the highest row.
The acoustics were not accidental.
They represent the accumulated technical knowledge of over a century of theater construction.

The Mausoleum at Halicarnassus (hal-ih-kar-NAS-us), built around 350 BCE in what is now southwestern Turkey for Mausolus (maw-SOH-lus), the governor of the Persian province of Caria, was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World and gave the English language the word "mausoleum" for any monumental tomb.

Its construction by Greek architects and sculptors, paid for by a Persian provincial governor, illustrates the deep cultural entanglement between the Greek and Persian worlds that Alexander was about to make permanent.

chapters.title: Alexander
sentences: Alexander crossed into Asia in 334 BCE with roughly 37,000 soldiers, a copy of Homer's Iliad that Aristotle had annotated for him, and the stated intention of conquering the Persian Empire.
He was twenty-two years old.
Within eleven years, he would control more territory than any individual before him.
Within thirteen years, he would be dead.
Alexander III of Macedon (356-323 BCE), known to history as Alexander the Great, is simultaneously one of the best-documented and most distorted figures in ancient history.
The contemporary sources are almost entirely lost.
What survives are later accounts, written centuries after his death, based on lost memoirs by his companions.

The most reliable are Arrian (AR-ee-un, a second-century CE Greek historian who drew on the lost accounts of Ptolemy and Aristobulus, two of Alexander's generals), Plutarch (PLOO-tark, a first-century CE biographer and moralist), and Diodorus Siculus (dy-oh-DOR-us SIK-yoo-lus, a first-century BCE historian).
Each had biases.
All wrote long after the events.
The "real" Alexander is filtered through centuries of literary construction, and separating the man from the legend is probably impossible.
What is certain is the military record.
It is unlike anything before or after in ancient history.

Alexander's first major engagement was the Battle of the Granicus (grah-NY-kus) in May 334 BCE, fought at a river crossing in northwestern Anatolia against a Persian force led by local satraps (provincial governors).

Alexander personally led the Companion cavalry in a charge across the river and came within inches of being killed: a Persian nobleman struck him on the helmet with a sword, and Alexander was saved only when his companion Cleitus (KLY-tus) cut off the attacker's arm.
The battle was a decisive Macedonian victory and opened Anatolia to Alexander's advance.

He liberated the Greek cities of the Ionian coast (the same cities whose failed revolt against Persia had started the Persian Wars; Chapter 3), presenting himself as a liberator of Greeks from Persian tyranny.
The message was calculated: he was finishing the job that the Greek city-states had started a century and a half earlier.
The second major battle was the Battle of Issus (ISS-us) in November 333 BCE, fought in a narrow coastal plain in what is now southern Turkey.

The Persian king Darius III (dah-RY-us, the last Achaemenid ruler) had assembled a large army and chosen favorable ground, but Alexander, leading the Companion cavalry on the right wing, punched through the Persian left and drove straight for Darius himself.
The Great King fled the battlefield, leaving behind his family, his war chest, and his royal tent.

The capture of Darius' mother, wife, and children was a propaganda bonanza: Alexander treated them with conspicuous chivalry, and the story of his courtesy was widely circulated to demonstrate that the Macedonian conqueror was a gentleman as well as a warrior.
After Issus, Alexander turned south along the Mediterranean coast, methodically reducing the Phoenician cities that provided Persia's naval power.
The seven-month siege of Tyre (TY-er, the island fortress-city of the Phoenicians; Phoenicia Ch 4-5) in 332 BCE was the hardest fighting of the campaign.

Alexander built a half-mile causeway from the mainland to the island (the causeway silted up over the centuries and turned Tyre into a peninsula, as it remains today), endured months of resistance, and upon taking the city, killed or enslaved most of the population.
From Tyre, he marched into Egypt, where he was welcomed as a liberator from Persian rule.
The Egyptian priests at the oracle of Amun (AH-mun) at Siwa declared him the son of the god, a claim Alexander seems to have taken seriously, or at least found useful.
In Egypt, Alexander founded the city of Alexandria in 331 BCE, on the Mediterranean coast at the western edge of the Nile Delta.
It would become the greatest city of the Hellenistic world: a center of learning, commerce, and cultural exchange that would outlast Alexander by centuries.
He spent, characteristically, almost no time there.
The war was waiting.
The decisive battle came at Gaugamela (gaw-gah-MEE-lah) in October 331 BCE, on a flat plain in what is now northern Iraq.

Darius III had assembled the largest army he could muster, probably 50,000 to 100,000 soldiers (ancient sources claim a million, which is logistically impossible), and had chosen open ground where his numerical superiority and his scythed chariots could be brought to bear.
Alexander had roughly 47,000.
What happened at Gaugamela was a masterclass in combined-arms tactics.

Alexander used his phalanx to pin the Persian center, drew the Persian cavalry into pursuing his own left wing (creating a gap in the Persian line), and then led the Companion cavalry in a wedge charge through the gap, driving straight at Darius.
For the second time, the Great King fled.
This time, the flight was final.
The Persian army dissolved.

Within months, Alexander occupied the three Persian capitals: Babylon (which surrendered without a fight), Susa (which handed over its treasury), and Persepolis (PURR-seh-poh-lis), the ceremonial capital and the jewel of Achaemenid architecture.
At Persepolis, Alexander burned the palace complex.
Whether this was a deliberate act of revenge for Xerxes' burning of Athens in 480 BCE, a drunken accident, or a calculated political statement remains debated.
The ancient sources disagree.

What is clear is that the burning of Persepolis destroyed one of the most magnificent architectural complexes in the ancient world (Persian Empire Ch 2) and sent a message that the Achaemenid Empire was finished.
Darius III fled east and was murdered by his own courtier, Bessus (BESS-us), in 330 BCE.
Alexander hunted down Bessus, had him executed, and declared himself the legitimate successor of the Achaemenid kings.
For the next seven years, Alexander pushed east.

He fought through the mountains of Afghanistan, crossed into Central Asia, and reached the Indus Valley (modern Pakistan), where he defeated the Indian king Porus (POR-us) at the Battle of the Hydaspes (hy-DAS-peez) in 326 BCE.
At the Hydaspes, Alexander faced war elephants for the first time and devised tactics to neutralize them, demonstrating his ability to adapt to unfamiliar military challenges.
He wanted to press further into India.
His army refused.
They had been marching and fighting for eight years and were over 3,000 miles (4,800 km) from home.
Alexander reluctantly turned back.
The return march through the Gedrosian Desert (jeh-DROH-shun, in modern Balochistan) in 325 BCE was catastrophic.
Historians estimate that a quarter to a third of the army died of thirst, heat, and starvation.
Why Alexander chose this route is debated: some sources claim he was trying to outdo the legendary marches of the mythical Queen Semiramis and the Persian king Cyrus.
If so, the price was paid by his soldiers.

Alexander spent his final years at Babylon, planning further campaigns (possibly an invasion of Arabia), integrating Persian and Greek administrative practices, attempting to merge the Greek and Persian aristocracies through mass marriages, and drinking heavily.
In June 323 BCE, after a prolonged fever following a drinking bout, Alexander died.
He was thirty-two.
The empire Alexander built stretched from Greece to the borders of India, from Egypt to Central Asia.
It was, by territory, the largest empire the world had yet seen, though it was surpassed within a century by the Maurya Empire in India (Maurya Empire Ch 1-3).

Alexander had conquered the Persian Empire in four years of active campaigning, founded over twenty cities (most named after himself), and permanently changed the cultural map of the ancient world by spreading Greek language, art, religion, and urban planning across three continents.
But Alexander left no succession plan.
He reportedly said his empire should go "to the strongest." He had no adult heir.
His wife Roxana was pregnant with a son, the future Alexander IV, who was eventually murdered as a teenager.

The result was forty years of war among his generals for control of the empire, wars that would shatter Alexander's creation into pieces but spread Greek culture further and more permanently than the conquest itself.
The question that has been debated for 2,300 years is: what was Alexander?
A military genius who also had a genuine vision of a multicultural empire?
A megalomaniac drunk on his own legend?
The greatest conqueror in history or history's greatest mass murderer?
The answer is probably all of these.
He was extraordinary and terrible in equal measure, and the world he left behind was utterly different from the one he had entered thirteen years earlier.

chapters.title: The Hellenistic World and Rome
sentences: Alexander's empire did not survive Alexander.
It took his generals forty years to make sure of that.

The Wars of the Diadochi (dy-AD-oh-ky, "successors," 322-281 BCE) were a series of brutal conflicts among Alexander's former companions and their descendants for control of the territories he had conquered.
The wars were complex, shifting, and relentlessly violent.
Alliances formed and broke with dizzying speed.
Generals who had fought side by side under Alexander now fought each other across the same battlefields.
The nominal heirs (Alexander's infant son Alexander IV and his intellectually disabled half-brother Philip III Arrhidaeus) were murdered.
By 281 BCE, the empire had stabilized into three major successor kingdoms, each ruled by a dynasty founded by one of Alexander's generals.

The Ptolemaic Kingdom (TOL-eh-MAY-ik), ruled by the descendants of Ptolemy I Soter (TOL-eh-mee, roughly 367-282 BCE, one of Alexander's most trusted commanders), controlled Egypt, Cyrenaica (eastern Libya), Cyprus, and parts of the Levant and Anatolia.
Ptolemy, the shrewdest of the Diadochi, had secured Egypt early and defended it against all comers.
He made Alexandria (the city Alexander had founded) his capital, and under the Ptolemies, Alexandria became the intellectual capital of the Mediterranean world.

The Ptolemies ruled Egypt as pharaohs, adopted Egyptian religious practices, married their sisters (following Egyptian royal tradition), and built a hybrid Greco-Egyptian culture that endured for nearly three centuries.

The Seleucid Empire (seh-LOO-sid), ruled by the descendants of Seleucus I Nicator (seh-LOO-kus, roughly 358-281 BCE), controlled the vast eastern territories: Mesopotamia, Persia, Central Asia, and parts of Anatolia.
The Seleucid Empire was the largest of the successor states by territory and the most diverse, encompassing dozens of peoples, languages, and cultures.

Seleucus founded new cities across his realm (including Antioch on the Syrian coast, which became one of the largest cities in the ancient world) and attempted to maintain the Greco-Persian fusion that Alexander had envisioned.

The empire gradually shrank as eastern territories broke away: the Parthians (PAR-thee-unz, Iranian nomads who would found their own empire; Persian Empire Ch 6-7) seized Persia, and the Greco-Bactrian Kingdom (in modern Afghanistan) became independent.

The Antigonid Kingdom (an-TIG-oh-nid), ruled by the descendants of Antigonus (an-TIG-oh-nus) and his son Demetrius (deh-MEE-tree-us), controlled Macedon and maintained a fluctuating hegemony over the Greek city-states.
The Antigonids were the poorest and least powerful of the three major successor states, and they spent most of their existence dealing with Greek revolts and Celtic invasions.

The world these kingdoms created is called the Hellenistic Age (roughly 323-30 BCE), from the Greek word hellenizein, "to make Greek." The Hellenistic Age was characterized by the spread of Greek language, culture, art, and urban planning across an area stretching from the western Mediterranean to the borders of India.
Greek became the common language (koine, koy-NAY, "common") of commerce, diplomacy, philosophy, and literature across the entire eastern Mediterranean and the Near East.
Cities were built or rebuilt on Greek plans, with agoras, theaters, gymnasiums (athletic and educational complexes), and temples in the Greek style.
Local elites across Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia adopted Greek customs, sent their children to Greek schools, and participated in Greek athletic competitions.
This was not simple cultural imperialism.
Hellenistic culture was a fusion.
Greek settlers in Egypt worshipped Isis and Osiris alongside Athena and Apollo.
The god Serapis (seh-RAH-pis), invented by the Ptolemies as a deliberate Greco-Egyptian hybrid deity, combined Elements of the Egyptian gods Osiris and Apis with the Greek gods Zeus and Hades.

In Gandhara (modern Pakistan and Afghanistan), Greek sculptural techniques were applied to Buddhist subjects, producing the first anthropomorphic images of the Buddha, a fusion that would spread across East Asia.

In Alexandria, Jewish scholars translated the Hebrew Bible into Greek, creating the Septuagint (SEP-too-ah-jint, named for the legendary seventy translators), which made Jewish scripture accessible to the Greek-speaking world and would later become the Old Testament of early Christianity.
The Hellenistic age was one of the greatest periods of scientific achievement in human history.

The concentration of wealth, libraries, and patronage in the successor kingdoms, particularly in Ptolemaic Alexandria, created conditions for intellectual work that would not be matched for over a thousand years.
Alexandria was the center.
The Library of Alexandria, founded under Ptolemy I around 295 BCE, aimed to collect every book in the world.
At its peak, it may have held between 100,000 and 400,000 scrolls (the numbers are uncertain and probably exaggerated, but the scale was unprecedented).

The Library was part of a larger institution called the Mouseion (moo-SAY-on, "shrine of the Muses," the origin of the English word "museum"), a research institute where scholars were housed, fed, and funded by the Ptolemaic government to pursue knowledge.
It was, in effect, the world's first state-funded research university.
The Lighthouse of Alexandria (the Pharos, FAR-os), completed around 283 BCE under Ptolemy II, stood over 330 feet (100 meters) tall at the entrance to Alexandria's harbor.
It was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, and its name gave the word for "lighthouse" in French (phare), Italian (faro), and Spanish (faro).
Euclid (YOO-klid), working in Alexandria around 300 BCE, compiled the Elements, a systematic treatment of geometry that organized the field into axioms, postulates, and logical proofs.
The Elements remained the standard geometry textbook for over two thousand years and is, after the Bible, possibly the most widely published book in Western history.
Every geometry student who has ever constructed a proof is working in the tradition Euclid established.
Archimedes (ar-kih-MEE-deez, roughly 287-212 BCE), working in the Greek city of Syracuse in Sicily, was the greatest scientist and mathematician of the ancient world.
He calculated pi to a degree of accuracy not surpassed for centuries.

He discovered the principle of buoyancy (a body immersed in fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced, the story of shouting "Eureka!" in the bath, which may be legendary but the principle is real).
He invented siege engines that held off a Roman army for two years.
He was killed by a Roman soldier during the sack of Syracuse in 212 BCE, reportedly while working on a mathematical diagram in the sand.

Eratosthenes (air-ah-TOS-theh-neez, roughly 276-194 BCE), the chief librarian at Alexandria, calculated the circumference of the Earth using nothing more than the angle of shadows in two cities at noon on the same day.
His result was within 2 percent of the modern value: approximately 25,000 miles (40,200 km).
The method was brilliantly simple: he knew that at noon on the summer solstice, the sun was directly overhead at Syene (modern Aswan) in southern Egypt, because sunlight fell straight down a well.
At the same moment in Alexandria, roughly 500 miles (800 km) to the north, a vertical stick cast a shadow indicating the sun was about 7.2 degrees from vertical.
Since 7.2 degrees is one-fiftieth of a circle, the Earth's circumference must be fifty times the distance between the two cities.
The calculation took geometry and turned it into geography.
Aristarchus of Samos (ar-ih-STAR-kus, roughly 310-230 BCE) proposed that the Earth revolves around the Sun, anticipating Copernicus by 1,800 years.

His heliocentric model was not accepted in antiquity (the philosopher Cleanthes reportedly said Aristarchus should be tried for impiety), but it was the right answer, arrived at through observation and logical reasoning, two millennia before anyone could prove it.

Herophilus (heh-ROF-ih-lus) of Chalcedon, working in Alexandria around 280 BCE, performed systematic dissections of human cadavers (possibly even vivisections of condemned criminals, though this is debated), distinguishing arteries from veins, mapping the nervous system, and earning the title "Father of Anatomy." His work represented a level of empirical investigation into the human body that would not be matched in Europe until the Renaissance.

The Antikythera Mechanism (an-tih-kih-THEER-ah), built around 200 BCE and recovered from a shipwreck off the Greek island of Antikythera in 1901, is a corroded bronze device the size of a shoebox containing a complex system of gears.
Modern analysis has revealed it to be an analog computer that tracked the positions of the sun, moon, and planets, predicted eclipses, and may have calculated the dates of the Olympic Games.
The technology is so sophisticated that nothing comparable would be built in Europe for over a thousand years.
It is the single most astonishing artifact of Greek science: proof that Hellenistic engineers understood precision gearing centuries before anyone else.
Alongside the sciences, the Hellenistic age produced new philosophical schools that would shape thought for centuries.

Stoicism (STOH-ih-sizm) was founded around 300 BCE by Zeno of Citium (ZEE-noh, from the city of Citium on Cyprus), who taught in the Stoa Poikile (stoh-AH poy-KIH-lay, "Painted Porch"), a colonnaded walkway in the Athenian Agora.

The Stoics taught that virtue is the only true good, that external circumstances (wealth, health, reputation) are "indifferent," and that the wise person achieves tranquility by accepting what cannot be changed and acting rightly in what can.
Stoicism would become the dominant philosophy of the Roman world, influencing emperors (Marcus Aurelius), statesmen (Seneca), and slaves (Epictetus) alike.
Its influence on Christianity, modern psychology, and popular self-help culture is incalculable.
Epicureanism (ep-ih-kyoo-REE-an-izm) was founded around 306 BCE by Epicurus (ep-ih-KYOO-rus), who established a philosophical community called the Garden in Athens.
Epicurus taught that the highest good is ataraxia (ah-tah-RAK-see-ah), tranquility of mind, achieved by moderate pleasure, the avoidance of pain, and the cultivation of friendship.

The Epicureans also taught that the universe is composed of atoms moving through void (drawing on Democritus' earlier atomic theory), that the gods exist but do not intervene in human affairs, and that death is nothing to fear because it is simply the dissolution of atoms.
Epicureanism has been consistently misrepresented as a philosophy of hedonistic indulgence.
In practice, it advocated a modest, withdrawn life of intellectual pleasures and close friendships, as far from excess as possible.
While the Hellenistic kingdoms flourished and feuded, a new power was rising in the western Mediterranean that none of them took seriously until it was too late.

Rome, a city-state in central Italy that had spent the 4th and 3rd centuries BCE conquering the Italian peninsula, made its first major contact with the Greek world when King Pyrrhus (PEER-us) of Epirus (eh-PIE-rus, a kingdom in northwestern Greece) invaded southern Italy in 280 BCE to protect the Greek colonies there from Roman expansion.

Pyrrhus won several battles against the Romans but suffered such heavy casualties that the victories were worthless, giving the English language the phrase "Pyrrhic victory" (a victory so costly it is effectively a defeat).
Pyrrhus returned to Greece.
The Romans conquered the Greek cities of southern Italy.
The pattern would repeat.
Rome pushed east into the Greek world, defeating the Antigonid kingdom of Macedon in a series of wars (the Macedonian Wars, 214-148 BCE) and methodically dismantling Macedonian power.
The Romans declared the "freedom of the Greeks" at the Isthmian Games of 196 BCE, a gesture that was genuine in intent but temporary in practice.
The Greek city-states, freed from Macedon, immediately resumed fighting each other, and the Romans found themselves drawn into Greek affairs as arbiter, ally, and eventually conqueror.

The Achaean League (ah-KEE-an), a federation of city-states in the Peloponnese that represented one of the ancient world's most sophisticated experiments in federal government, was the last major Greek political entity to resist Rome.
In 146 BCE, when the League defied Roman authority, Rome responded with overwhelming force.
The Roman general Lucius Mummius sacked and destroyed Corinth, one of the wealthiest and most culturally significant cities in Greece.
The population was slaughtered or enslaved, the city was leveled, and its art was shipped to Rome.
The destruction of Corinth in 146 BCE effectively ended Greek political independence.
In the same year, Rome destroyed Carthage (the Phoenician colony in North Africa that had been Rome's greatest rival; Phoenicia Ch 6-8), a coincidence that was not lost on ancient observers.
Two of the Mediterranean's oldest and most influential civilizations were crushed in the same year by the same rising power.
But conquest was not the end of the story.
It was, in a sense, the beginning of a different one.
The Romans conquered Greece, but Greek culture conquered Rome.
Roman aristocrats spoke Greek, read Greek literature, studied Greek philosophy, imitated Greek art, and sent their sons to Athens and Rhodes for education.
The Romans adopted Greek gods (renaming them: Zeus became Jupiter, Athena became Minerva, Aphrodite became Venus).
They copied Greek architecture.
They absorbed Greek science.
They preserved Greek literature by copying manuscripts.
Latin literature was, in its early centuries, essentially an imitation of Greek models.
The phrase attributed to the Roman poet Horace captures the dynamic: "Captive Greece took captive her fierce conqueror." The Hellenistic kingdoms fell one by one.
The Seleucid Empire shrank to a Syrian rump state and was annexed by Rome in 64 BCE.

The last Ptolemaic ruler was Cleopatra VII (klee-oh-PAT-rah, 69-30 BCE), the most famous woman of the ancient world, who allied with Julius Caesar and then Mark Antony in an attempt to preserve Egyptian independence.

Her defeat and suicide in 30 BCE, following the Battle of Actium (AK-tee-um) in 31 BCE, ended the Ptolemaic dynasty, made Egypt a Roman province, and formally closed the Hellenistic age that Alexander had opened three centuries earlier.
Ancient Greece, as an independent political entity, was finished.
But the Greek experiment, those two and a half centuries of furious creativity between 750 and 146 BCE, had produced something no army could destroy.

Democracy, philosophy, tragedy, comedy, history, geometry, atomic theory, the alphabet, the concept of the citizen, the idea that the universe operates according to rational principles discoverable by human reason: these were not Greek possessions that died with Greek independence.
They were ideas, and ideas do not respect borders or bow to conquering armies.
The Romans preserved them.
The Byzantines transmitted them.
The Arabs translated them.
The Renaissance rediscovered them.

Every parliament that votes, every scientist who constructs a proof, every theater that stages a tragedy, every doctor who takes the Hippocratic Oath, every philosopher who asks "what is justice?" is, at some remove, continuing an experiment that a handful of small, quarrelsome city-states on a rocky Mediterranean peninsula began twenty-five centuries ago.
Democracy, philosophy, drama, and science were not inevitable discoveries.
They were experiments by a tiny collection of city-states that spent most of their time fighting each other, and the experiments only lasted a few centuries before being swallowed by empires.
But the experiments worked well enough that the rest of human history decided to keep running them.
Ancient Greece was conquered by Macedon and then by Rome.
The next chapter in the Greco-Roman chain is Ancient Rome.

chapters.title: Seven Hills
sentences: Rome was not supposed to happen.
The site itself was unpromising.

Seven low hills (the tallest barely 150 feet or 46 meters above sea level) rose above a marshy flood plain along the Tiber (TY-ber) River, about 16 miles (26 km) inland from the western coast of central Italy.
The marshes bred mosquitoes.
The Tiber flooded regularly.
The hills were separated by swampy valleys that turned into stagnant pools every spring.
If you were looking for an ideal place to build a city in ancient Italy, you would pick almost anywhere else.

The Greeks had already planted prosperous colonies on the southern coasts of Italy and Sicily (the region they called Magna Graecia, "Greater Greece"), where the harbors were better, the soil was richer, and the climate was friendlier.
The Etruscans (ih-TRUHS-kuhnz), the sophisticated civilization that dominated northern and central Italy, had built elegant cities on well-drained hilltops with proper planning.
Rome squatted in a swamp.
But the swamp had advantages that were not obvious at first glance.
The Tiber was navigable from the sea, making the site accessible to trade.
The hills, however modest, were defensible.

The location sat at the intersection of two important routes: the north-south corridor connecting the Etruscan cities of the north with the Greek colonies of the south, and the east-west salt route (the future Via Salaria, "Salt Road") connecting the salt flats at the mouth of the Tiber with the pastoral communities of the interior Apennine mountains.

Salt was one of the most valuable commodities in the ancient world (the word "salary" comes from the Latin salarium, possibly related to salt payments), and anyone who controlled the point where the salt road crossed the Tiber controlled a choke point in the economy of central Italy.

By the eighth century BCE, small farming villages had clustered on the Palatine and neighboring hills, burying their dead in the marshy valley below (the area that would later become the Roman Forum, the civic heart of the city).
These were modest settlements.
Oval huts with thatched roofs, wattle-and-daub walls, and packed-earth floors.
Pottery fragments and bronze fibulae (brooches).
Nothing that would suggest a future world capital.
At the same time, the great civilizations of the Mediterranean were already ancient.
Egypt was over two thousand years old.
Mesopotamia's cities had been thriving for three millennia (Mesopotamia Ch 3).

The Mycenaean palace civilization had risen and fallen centuries earlier (Mycenaean Ch 7), and the Greek world was emerging from its Dark Ages into the Archaic period that would produce the city-states, the alphabet, and the philosophers.
The Phoenicians were sailing the Mediterranean, planting colonies, and spreading their alphabet to the Greeks (Phoenicia Ch 3-5).
Rome, in 753 BCE, was a village.
A quick note on "firsts" before we go further.
When this narrative says Rome did something "first," it means first in the Roman context.
Rome was a latecomer to civilization by almost any measure.
The Sumerians had cities three thousand years before Rome existed.
The Egyptians had writing, monumental architecture, and centralized government when the hills of Rome were covered with nothing but grass and goats.
The Greeks had already developed philosophy, drama, and democratic institutions before Rome was anything more than a provincial town.

What made Rome exceptional was not originality but scale: the ability to take what others had invented, improve it, systematize it, and spread it across an empire larger than anything the Western world had seen.
Rome did not invent the arch, the road, the aqueduct, the legal code, or representative government.
It perfected all of them and made them standard equipment for half the known world.

Also: almost everything we "know" about Rome's first two and a half centuries comes from writers who lived five hundred or more years after the events they describe, working from oral traditions, family legends, and political propaganda.

The Roman historian Livy (LIV-ee, Titus Livius, who wrote a monumental 142-book history of Rome beginning around 27 BCE) is our main source for the early period, and he was writing seven centuries after the supposed founding.
Treat the stories of early Rome the way you would treat Homer's account of the Trojan War (Mycenaean Ch 5): probably based on something real, heavily embellished, and impossible to verify in detail.
The founding legend is one of the most famous stories in Western civilization.

Twin brothers, Romulus (ROM-yoo-lus) and Remus (REE-mus), were born to a princess of the city of Alba Longa (AL-buh LONG-guh, a Latin settlement in the Alban Hills about 12 miles or 19 km southeast of Rome) and the god Mars.
Their great-uncle, who had usurped the throne, ordered the infants drowned in the Tiber.
But the river deposited them safely on the bank, where a she-wolf suckled them until a shepherd found and raised them.
As young men, they overthrew the usurper, restored their grandfather, and decided to found a new city.
They quarreled over which hill to build on.
Romulus chose the Palatine (PAL-uh-tyne), Remus chose the Aventine (AV-en-tyne).
When birds and omens favored Romulus, he began plowing a furrow to mark the city's boundary.
Remus mocked him by jumping over the furrow.
Romulus killed his brother, named the city after himself, and became its first king.
The traditional date was April 21, 753 BCE.
Everything about this legend is carefully constructed.
The divine father (Mars, god of war) brands Rome as a warrior civilization from birth.
The she-wolf signals wild toughness.
The fratricide establishes that Rome would tolerate no disrespect to its boundaries.

The plowed furrow (the pomerium, poh-MEER-ee-um, the sacred boundary of the city, within which no weapons could be carried and no army could march) became a real legal concept that Romans enforced for centuries.
Even the name "Palatine" gave us the word "palace" (the emperors would later build their residences on this same hill).
Myth and reality were braided together from the very beginning.
The Romans also told a second origin story, one that connected them to the wider Mediterranean world.

Aeneas (eh-NEE-us), a Trojan prince who fled the burning city of Troy (Mycenaean Ch 5), sailed west across the Mediterranean, landed in Italy, and founded the dynasty that would eventually produce Romulus and Remus.
The poet Virgil (VER-jil) gave this legend its definitive form in the Aeneid, Rome's national epic, written under Augustus around 19 BCE.

The Aeneas legend served a political purpose: it gave Rome a pedigree older than Greece's, connecting Roman origins to the heroic age of the Trojan War while claiming the losing side as ancestors (a characteristically Roman move, turning defeat into the foundation of something greater).
The Roman tradition named seven kings who ruled Rome between its founding and the establishment of the Republic in 509 BCE.
Whether all seven existed as historical individuals is uncertain.
The first four (Romulus, Numa Pompilius, Tullus Hostilius, and Ancus Marcius) are essentially legendary.
The last three are more plausible because they have Etruscan names and connections, and the Etruscan presence in Rome during this period is confirmed by archaeology.
Numa Pompilius (NOOM-uh pom-PIL-ee-us), the second king, is credited with establishing most of Rome's religious institutions.

He created the college of Vestal Virgins (six priestesses who tended the sacred fire of the goddess Vesta in the Forum, maintaining a flame that symbolized the life of the city, sworn to thirty years of celibacy on pain of death).
He reformed the calendar, adding the months of January and February to a previously ten-month year.
He established the office of Pontifex Maximus (PON-tih-fex MAX-ih-mus, the chief priest of Rome, a title that would later pass to the emperors and eventually to the Pope).

Whether Numa really did all this or whether later Romans attributed their religious institutions to a single wise founder is impossible to determine, but the institutions themselves were real and ancient.
The last three kings are the ones who matter most historically, because they are associated with the Etruscan influence that transformed Rome from a village into a city.
The Etruscans were the dominant civilization of central Italy from roughly the eighth through the fifth centuries BCE.
They lived in a loose confederation of city-states (the "Dodecapolis," twelve major cities) in the region called Etruria (ih-TROOR-ee-uh, roughly modern Tuscany, which takes its name from them).
The Etruscans were wealthy, urbanized, and culturally sophisticated.
They built planned cities with grid-pattern streets, stone temples, and elaborate underground tombs painted with vivid frescoes of banquets, dancing, and athletic games.

They were skilled metalworkers, accomplished traders, and enthusiastic adopters of Greek culture (Etruscan tombs are one of the richest sources of imported Greek pottery anywhere in the Mediterranean).

Their language (Etruscan is a language isolate, unrelated to any other known language, as different from Latin as Basque is from Spanish) remains only partially understood despite thousands of surviving inscriptions, mostly short funerary texts.
Where the Etruscans came from was a question the ancient Greeks debated.

The Greek historian Herodotus (heh-ROD-uh-tus, the fifth-century BCE "father of history" who traveled the Mediterranean world and wrote a sprawling account of its peoples) claimed they migrated from Lydia in western Anatolia.

Modern DNA studies have produced mixed results, but the current consensus leans toward the Etruscans being indigenous to Italy, developing their distinctive culture from local roots with heavy Greek influence absorbed through trade and cultural contact.

Rome's last three kings (traditionally Tarquinius Priscus, Servius Tullius, and Tarquinius Superbus, reigning roughly from 616 to 509 BCE) were either Etruscan or heavily Etruscanized, and under their rule, Rome was physically transformed.

The marshy valley between the Palatine and Capitoline hills was drained by a great sewer called the Cloaca Maxima (KLOH-ah-kuh MAX-ih-muh, "Greatest Drain"), one of the world's earliest sewage systems, an engineering project on a scale that only the Etruscans had the expertise to execute.
Parts of the Cloaca Maxima are still functioning today, over 2,500 years later, which is a sentence that tells you everything about the durability of Roman (originally Etruscan) infrastructure.
The drained valley became the Roman Forum, the open-air civic center where Romans would conduct their business, their politics, their religion, and their arguments for the next thousand years.

On the Capitoline Hill (the smallest but most sacred of Rome's seven hills), King Tarquinius Priscus began construction of the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus (Jupiter "Best and Greatest"), Rome's most important temple, dedicated to the king of the gods along with the goddesses Juno and Minerva (the so-called Capitoline Triad).
The temple was completed and dedicated in 509 BCE, the same year the Republic was founded (a coincidence that the Romans probably manufactured after the fact).

It was an Etruscan-style structure: a high podium, deep front porch, three inner chambers (one for each deity), and a terracotta roof with brightly painted sculptural decorations made by Etruscan craftsmen.
King Servius Tullius (SER-vee-us TUL-ee-us), traditionally the sixth king, is credited with two enormous achievements.
First, he built the Servian Wall, the first defensive wall enclosing all seven hills of Rome, defining the city's physical boundaries for centuries.

Second, he reorganized Roman society into classes based on wealth rather than birth, creating the centuriate assembly (a political body organized around military centuries, in which citizens were grouped by wealth class and voted as units).
This was not democracy.
The wealthiest classes voted first and had the most votes.
But it was the germ of the idea that political power should be organized systematically rather than handed out by kings, and it would become one of the foundations of the Republican constitution.
The end of the monarchy came (according to tradition) in 509 BCE, and it came with a story the Romans loved to tell because it cast the Republic's founding as a moral drama.
Tarquinius Superbus (tar-KWIN-ee-us soo-PER-bus, "Tarquin the Proud"), the last king, was a tyrant.
His son, Sextus Tarquinius, raped a Roman noblewoman named Lucretia (loo-KREE-shuh), who told her husband and father what had happened and then killed herself rather than live with the shame.
Her kinsman Lucius Junius Brutus (BROO-tus) led a revolt, expelled the Tarquin family from Rome, and the Romans swore they would never have a king again.
The story is almost certainly legendary in its details.
But the underlying event (a revolution that replaced monarchy with an elected magistracy) probably reflects something real.

Rome in the late sixth century BCE was caught up in a wider wave of political change: across the Greek world, tyrants were being overthrown and replaced with oligarchies or democracies during the same period.
In Athens, the tyrant Hippias was expelled in 510 BCE, one year before Rome's traditional date.
The idea that concentrating power in a single hereditary ruler was dangerous was in the air across the Mediterranean.
Rome's solution was characteristically pragmatic: instead of one king, elect two consuls who serve for one year and can veto each other.
Instead of hereditary rule, competitive elections (limited to the aristocracy, but elections nonetheless).
Instead of trusting any individual, trust the system.
The word "king" (rex) became the most powerful insult in the Roman political vocabulary.

For nearly five centuries, from 509 BCE until Julius Caesar's dictatorship in the 40s BCE, the accusation that someone was seeking to become a king was the surest way to destroy a political career and, in several cases, to justify assassination.
The Romans' hatred of monarchy was not philosophical.
It was visceral, baked into their founding narrative, repeated in their education, and enforced by a political culture that punished any appearance of excessive personal power.

Understanding this hatred is essential to understanding everything that happened in the Late Republic, because the crisis that destroyed the Republic was, at its core, a struggle over whether one man could accumulate enough power to become king in all but name.

The Republic began with two consuls, a Senate (an advisory body of former magistrates and leading citizens, originally dominated by the hereditary aristocracy), and a set of religious, military, and legal institutions inherited from the kings.
It was not designed.

And the first two and a half centuries of its existence would be defined by two simultaneous struggles: outward expansion across the Italian peninsula, and an internal fight between the old aristocracy and the common citizens over who would have a share in political power.

chapters.title: The Early Republic
sentences: The Roman Republic was born fighting, and it never stopped.
The expulsion of the Tarquins in 509 BCE did not make Rome the dominant power in central Italy.
It made Rome a small city-state surrounded by rivals.
The Etruscans to the north.
The Volsci (VOHL-sky) and Aequi (EH-kwee) in the Apennine foothills to the east and south.

The other Latin cities (members of the same ethnic and linguistic group as Rome, speaking dialects of the same language, worshipping the same gods) that regarded Rome as one city among equals, not as a leader.
And hovering over everything, the much larger and more culturally advanced Etruscan and Greek civilizations, both of which could have snuffed Rome out in its early decades without much effort.
What saved Rome was a combination of military stubbornness, political flexibility, and a social system that, for all its inequalities, proved remarkably good at absorbing former enemies.
But before Rome could fight its neighbors, it had to fight itself.

The first two centuries of the Republic were dominated by the Conflict of the Orders (roughly 494 to 287 BCE), a prolonged political struggle between the patricians (puh-TRISH-unz, the hereditary aristocratic families who monopolized political and religious office) and the plebeians (plih-BEE-unz, everyone else, from wealthy commoners to poor farmers).
The conflict was not a revolution.

It was a grinding, two-century negotiation conducted through a uniquely Roman tactic: the secession (a mass walkout in which the plebeians physically left the city and refused to serve in the army until their demands were met).

The first secession, traditionally dated to 494 BCE, produced the office of the tribune of the plebs (TRIB-yoon), a magistrate with the sacred power to veto any action by any other magistrate and to protect individual plebeians from arbitrary punishment.
The tribune's person was sacrosanct (touching a tribune was a capital offense), and the office gave the common people a permanent champion within the system.
No other ancient civilization invented anything quite like it.
The Greek democracies gave citizens direct voting power but no individual official whose job was to block the government on behalf of the poor.
The Near Eastern empires had no concept of popular veto at all.
The tribune was a Roman original, and it would become one of the most powerful offices in the Republic.
The next major milestone came in 450 BCE with the Twelve Tables, Rome's first written law code.
Before this, law had been a patrician monopoly: the priests (all patrician) interpreted customary law, and since the law was unwritten, they could interpret it however they liked.
The plebeians demanded that the law be written down so everyone could read it.
A commission of ten men (the decemvirs, deh-SEM-virz) was appointed to draft the code, and the resulting twelve bronze tablets were displayed in the Forum for all to see.
The Twelve Tables were not sophisticated.
They dealt with property disputes, inheritance, debt, assault, and procedure.
They included harsh provisions (debtors who could not pay could be sold into slavery, or literally cut into pieces by their creditors, though whether this was ever enforced is debated).
But the principle was revolutionary: law should be written, public, and apply equally to patrician and plebeian alike.

For comparison, the Babylonian Code of Hammurabi (Mesopotamia Ch 6) had established the principle of written law roughly 1,300 years earlier, but Hammurabi's code was promulgated by a king as an act of royal authority.
The Twelve Tables were demanded by the people from their government.
The difference matters.
Over the next two centuries, the plebeians won concession after concession.
The Lex Canuleia of 445 BCE legalized marriage between patricians and plebeians (previously forbidden).
The Licinio-Sextian laws of 367 BCE required that one of the two consuls be a plebeian.
Plebeians gained access to all major magistracies, including the dictatorship and the censorship.
Finally, in 287 BCE, the Lex Hortensia (hore-TEN-see-uh) made decisions of the plebeian assembly binding on all citizens, including patricians, without requiring Senate approval.
This was the culmination of the Conflict of the Orders.
The patricians retained their social prestige and religious privileges, but the plebeians had won legal and political equality.
The result was a political system unlike anything in the ancient world.
Rome had no single sovereign.

Power was divided among magistrates (consuls, praetors, censors, tribunes), the Senate (which controlled foreign policy and finance but technically had only advisory authority), and the assemblies (which passed laws and elected magistrates).

The system was designed to prevent any one person or faction from accumulating too much power, through annual elections, collegial magistracies (two consuls, not one), the tribune's veto power, and a deep cultural suspicion of personal ambition.
It was cumbersome, conservative, and frequently gridlocked.
It was also astonishingly durable.

While fighting over its internal constitution, Rome simultaneously fought its way to control of the entire Italian peninsula, a process that took roughly 250 years (from the expulsion of the kings in 509 BCE to the eve of the First Punic War in 264 BCE).
The early wars were small-scale affairs against immediate neighbors: the Volsci, the Aequi, the Sabines, and the other Latin cities.
Rome won some, lost some, and gradually expanded its territory through a mix of military victory and diplomatic alliance.
The key Roman innovation was not military but political: the system of alliances and colonies that bound defeated peoples to Rome without fully absorbing them.
In 390 BCE, disaster struck.

A band of Gauls (GAHLZ, Celtic-speaking peoples from the Po Valley and beyond the Alps, as different from the Latin-speaking Romans as English is from Arabic) swept south, smashed a Roman army at the Battle of the Allia (AH-lee-uh, a small river about 11 miles or 18 km north of Rome), and sacked the city itself.

According to legend, the Gauls found the senators sitting motionless in their chairs in their homes, draped in their togas, waiting for death with aristocratic dignity (the Romans loved this kind of story).
The Gauls occupied Rome for months, and the Romans paid a ransom in gold to get them to leave.
The Gallic chieftain Brennus (BREN-us) reportedly threw his sword onto the scales when the Romans complained the weights were rigged, declaring "Vae victis!" ("Woe to the conquered!").
The humiliation of the Gallic sack left a permanent scar on Rome's collective memory.
Never again, the Romans swore.
They rebuilt the Servian Wall (the existing wall may not have been sufficient), reformed their military, and from this point forward treated northern threats with particular seriousness.
Three hundred and fifty years later, when Julius Caesar conquered the Gauls of France, the cultural memory of 390 BCE was still powerful enough to serve as justification.
The Samnite Wars (three rounds of fighting between 343 and 290 BCE) were the wars that made Rome the dominant power in Italy.

The Samnites (SAM-nyts) were a tough, warlike people of the central Apennine mountains who spoke Oscan (a language related to Latin but mutually unintelligible, as different as Spanish and Portuguese).
They were Rome's most dangerous Italian opponents: organized, well-armed, and fighting on home terrain in rugged mountain country that neutralized Rome's advantages in open-field combat.

The Second Samnite War (326-304 BCE) included Rome's most humiliating military defeat of the early Republic: the Battle of the Caudine Forks (321 BCE), where an entire Roman army was trapped in a narrow mountain pass and forced to surrender, the soldiers made to pass under a yoke of spears in ritual degradation.
Rome won the Samnite Wars not through superior tactics but through superior resilience.
The Roman system could absorb defeats that would have destroyed other states.
When an army was lost, Rome raised another one.
When allies defected, Rome replaced them.
The Romans called this quality virtus (vir-TOOS, a word that encompasses courage, endurance, discipline, and civic duty, far broader than the English "virtue" that descends from it).
It was not glamorous.
It was grinding, stubborn persistence, the willingness to take a beating and come back for more, year after year, until the enemy was exhausted.
By 290 BCE, Rome controlled most of the Italian peninsula south of the Po Valley.
But the method of control was as important as the fact of it.
Rome did not simply annex conquered territories.

It created a layered system of relationships: full Roman citizens, Latin allies (who had some Roman legal rights but could not vote), and Italian allies (who were bound by treaty to provide military contingents to Rome in exchange for protection).
The allies kept their local governments, their languages, and their customs.
What they gave up was independent foreign policy and the right to make war on their own.
In return, they gained access to Roman trade networks, the protection of Roman military power, and, crucially, a share of the spoils when Rome conquered new territory.
This alliance system was Rome's secret weapon.
It turned defeated enemies into military assets.
Every war Rome fought in Italy produced not just conquered territory but new allied manpower for the next war.

By the mid-third century BCE, Rome could field armies that dwarfed anything the Greek city-states or the Hellenistic kingdoms could muster, not because Rome itself was so populous but because half of Italy marched under Roman command.
In 312 BCE, the censor Appius Claudius Caecus (AP-ee-us KLOW-dee-us SEE-kus) began two projects that embodied the Roman genius for infrastructure.

The first was the Appian Way (Via Appia), the first major Roman road, running 132 miles (212 km) from Rome southeast to Capua (KAH-poo-uh), the largest city in the fertile region of Campania (kam-PAY-nee-uh).
The road was straight, paved with fitted stone blocks, drained by ditches on both sides, and wide enough for two carts to pass.
It became the model for every Roman road that followed, and the eventual network (over 250,000 miles or 400,000 km of roads spanning three continents) would be the circulatory system of the empire.
The second project was the Aqua Appia, Rome's first aqueduct, a mostly underground channel that brought fresh water from springs 10 miles (16 km) east of the city into Rome's growing neighborhoods.

By the second century CE, eleven aqueducts would be delivering over a million cubic meters of water daily to a city of over a million people, a feat of hydraulic engineering unmatched until the nineteenth century.
The Romans did not invent roads (the Persians had built the Royal Road across their empire centuries earlier; Persian Empire Ch 2).
They did not invent aqueducts (the Assyrians had built impressive water channels; Assyrian Empire Ch 5).
But they perfected both technologies, standardized them, and replicated them at a scale nobody had attempted before.
This is the Roman pattern: borrow the idea, improve the engineering, and build it everywhere.
By 264 BCE, Rome had unified peninsular Italy under its leadership and built the infrastructure to hold it together.
It was a regional power, impressive by Italian standards but still a backwater compared to the great Hellenistic kingdoms that Alexander the Great's conquests had created in the east.
That was about to change, because Rome was about to collide with the other great power of the western Mediterranean: Carthage.

chapters.title: The Punic Wars
sentences: The Punic Wars were the most important military conflict in Western history.
That claim is not made lightly.
In 264 BCE, Rome was the dominant power on the Italian peninsula.
Carthage (KAR-thij) was the dominant power of the western Mediterranean, a Phoenician colony-turned-superpower on the coast of modern Tunisia, about 400 miles (640 km) south of Rome across the sea.
The two powers had coexisted peacefully for centuries, even signing treaties dividing their spheres of influence.
Within 118 years, Rome would destroy Carthage utterly, erase it from the map, and become the unchallenged master of the Mediterranean world.
The Punic Wars (the name comes from Punicus, the Latin word for Phoenician) decided whether the western Mediterranean would be Semitic and Phoenician or Latin and Roman.
The consequences shaped everything that followed, from the spread of Latin languages to the spread of Christianity to the political geography of modern Europe.
The First Punic War (264-241 BCE) started over Sicily, the large island sitting at the narrow waist of the Mediterranean between Italy and North Africa.
Both Rome and Carthage wanted to control it.
Neither could tolerate the other doing so.
The problem for Rome was that Carthage was a naval power and Rome was not.
Carthage had been a maritime trading empire for centuries, with a professional navy and experienced sailors.
Rome had never fought a significant naval battle.
The Romans were an army.
Their legions could march anywhere in Italy, but they could not walk across the Strait of Messina.
What Rome did next is one of the most remarkable acts of improvisation in military history.

According to the ancient sources (the Greek historian Polybius, poh-LIB-ee-us, who wrote a detailed history of Rome's rise based partly on interviews with Roman participants and who is our best source for the Punic Wars), the Romans captured a wrecked Carthaginian warship, used it as a template, and built an entire fleet of 120 warships in sixty days.
They had never built warships before.
They trained their rowers on benches set up on dry land while the ships were being constructed.

And they invented a boarding device called the corvus (KOR-vus, Latin for "raven"), a hinged gangplank with a heavy spike at the end that could be dropped onto an enemy ship, locking the two vessels together and allowing Roman soldiers to swarm across and fight hand-to-hand, turning a naval battle into something more like a land battle at sea.
The Romans won their first major naval battle at Mylae (MY-lee) in 260 BCE, sinking or capturing about fifty Carthaginian ships.

They went on to win several more naval engagements, lost entire fleets to storms (Rome lost more ships to bad weather than to Carthage, a testament to their inexperience at sea), rebuilt their fleets each time with that grinding Roman resilience, and eventually wore Carthage down.
The war ended in 241 BCE with Carthage ceding Sicily to Rome.
Sicily became Rome's first overseas province (the first territory governed directly by Rome rather than administered through the alliance system that managed Italy).
The significance of the First Punic War goes beyond the acquisition of Sicily.
Rome had proved it could fight outside Italy, build a navy from scratch, absorb catastrophic losses, and outlast a richer, more experienced opponent through sheer stubbornness.
Carthage had proved that it could not.
The Second Punic War (218-201 BCE) was the war that made Rome, and it was dominated by one of the most extraordinary military minds in history.
Hannibal Barca (HAN-ih-bul BAR-kuh) was the son of Hamilcar Barca, a Carthaginian general who had fought in the First Punic War and spent the years afterward building Carthaginian power in Spain.
According to tradition, Hamilcar made the young Hannibal swear an oath of eternal enmity against Rome.
Whether the story is true or not, Hannibal spent his entire adult life trying to destroy the Roman state.
In 218 BCE, Hannibal did something the Romans thought was impossible.

He marched an army of roughly 50,000 infantry, 9,000 cavalry, and 37 war elephants from Spain across southern Gaul and over the Alps in the autumn, arriving in the Po Valley of northern Italy with about half his force intact (the crossing killed thousands).
The Alpine crossing remains one of the most audacious military operations ever attempted.

The specific route is still debated (researchers have literally analyzed ancient horse dung deposits in Alpine passes to try to identify Hannibal's path), but the achievement is undeniable: Hannibal brought an army, including elephants, over one of the highest mountain ranges in Europe in conditions that modern hikers find challenging with Gore-Tex and energy bars.
Once in Italy, Hannibal proceeded to inflict a series of devastating defeats on every Roman army sent against him.
At the Trebia (TREH-bee-uh) in December 218 BCE, he destroyed a Roman army using a hidden flanking force.

At Lake Trasimene (trah-sih-MEE-nee) in June 217 BCE, he ambushed an entire Roman army marching along a lake shore in morning fog, killing or capturing nearly 30,000 men (the largest ambush in military history).
And then, in August 216 BCE, came Cannae (KAH-nee).
The Battle of Cannae was one of the most lopsided military victories ever achieved and remains a textbook study in tactical genius.

Hannibal, facing a massive Roman army of roughly 80,000 men (the Romans had concentrated their forces, determined to crush him through sheer numbers), arranged his line with weak infantry in a convex center, strong African infantry on the flanks, and superior cavalry on the wings.
As the Roman infantry pushed forward and the weak Carthaginian center bent backward (exactly as Hannibal intended), the Roman mass crowded together into an increasingly compressed space.
Then the African flanks pivoted inward, the cavalry swept around to close the rear, and the entire Roman army was surrounded.
The result was annihilation.

Ancient sources report between 50,000 and 70,000 Roman soldiers killed in a single afternoon, the highest single-day death toll in Western military history until the first day of the Battle of the Somme in 1916, over two thousand years later.
After Cannae, Hannibal expected Rome to surrender.
Any other Mediterranean state would have.
Carthage would have.
The Hellenistic kingdoms would have.
But Rome did not surrender.
The Senate refused to ransom the captured soldiers, refused to negotiate, and declared that any citizen who spoke of peace would be executed.
They raised new armies.

They changed their strategy: instead of meeting Hannibal in open battle, they adopted a war of attrition, shadowing his army, attacking his foragers, and targeting his allies, the strategy of Fabius Maximus (FAY-bee-us MAX-ih-mus, nicknamed "the Delayer," whose cautious approach gave us the term "Fabian strategy").
They contained Hannibal in southern Italy for thirteen years without ever defeating him in battle but without letting him win the war either.
The man who finally defeated Hannibal was Publius Cornelius Scipio (SKIP-ee-oh), later given the honorary name Africanus.
Scipio was a tactical innovator who had studied Hannibal's methods and learned from them.
Rather than attacking Hannibal in Italy, Scipio took the war to Africa, invading Carthaginian territory and threatening Carthage itself.
Hannibal was recalled from Italy (after fifteen years of campaigning on enemy soil, undefeated in the field) to defend his homeland.
At the Battle of Zama in 202 BCE, Scipio defeated Hannibal using some of Hannibal's own tactics, particularly the use of superior cavalry to envelop the enemy's flanks.
Carthage surrendered.
The peace terms stripped it of its overseas possessions, its navy, and its independence in foreign policy.

Hannibal himself survived the war, served briefly in Carthaginian politics, and eventually fled to the eastern Mediterranean, where he spent years advising various kings against Rome before taking poison to avoid Roman capture around 183 BCE.
He was, by any measure, one of the greatest military commanders in history.

He lost the war not because he was outgeneraled but because he was outproduced: Rome's alliance system meant it could replace destroyed armies with fresh ones, while Hannibal, operating far from home with limited reinforcements, could not replace his losses.
With Carthage neutralized, Rome turned east.

Between 200 and 146 BCE, Rome fought a series of wars against the Hellenistic kingdoms (the successor states created by the breakup of Alexander the Great's empire: Macedon, the Seleucid Empire in the Near East, and Ptolemaic Egypt).
The details are less dramatic than the Punic Wars, but the result was the same: Roman military power, systematically applied, dismantled every major rival in the Mediterranean.
In 197 BCE, Rome defeated Macedon at the Battle of Cynoscephalae.
In 190 BCE, Rome defeated the Seleucid Empire at the Battle of Magnesia.
In 168 BCE, Rome crushed Macedon again at the Battle of Pydna and abolished the Macedonian monarchy.
In 146 BCE, Rome destroyed the Greek city of Corinth (KOR-inth) and absorbed Greece as the province of Achaea (ah-KAY-uh).
The conquest of Greece brought Rome into direct contact with the culture that would transform it.
The Roman poet Horace later wrote the famous line: "Graecia capta ferum victorem cepit" ("Conquered Greece took captive her savage conqueror").

Greek art, literature, philosophy, architecture, and religion flooded into Rome, and the Roman elite embraced it with the enthusiasm of people who knew their own culture was, in sophisticated terms, provincial.
In the same year, 146 BCE, Rome finally settled the Carthaginian question.
The Third Punic War (149-146 BCE) was less a war than a siege.
Carthage had recovered economically under the peace terms but was militarily helpless, forbidden to raise an army without Roman permission.

A faction in the Roman Senate led by Cato the Elder (KAY-toh, Marcus Porcius Cato, a conservative politician who ended every speech, regardless of topic, with the phrase "Carthago delenda est," "Carthage must be destroyed") agitated for war.
The pretext was thin; the intent was annihilation.
Roman forces besieged Carthage for three years.

When the city finally fell, the Romans systematically destroyed it: buildings torn down, population killed or enslaved, the site (according to later tradition) sown with salt so nothing would grow there.
The salting is probably a later legend, but the destruction was real and total.
One of the great cities of the ancient world was erased.
Whether the Romans really felt threatened by Carthage or simply wanted to eliminate a potential rival and seize its remaining territory is debated.
The elderly Cato, who had actually visited Carthage on a diplomatic mission, reportedly held up fresh figs in the Senate and said "These were picked in Carthage three days ago.
That's how close the enemy is." The argument was geographic, not military.
Carthage was too close and too capable to be allowed to exist.
By 146 BCE, Rome controlled the entire Mediterranean: Spain, North Africa, Greece, Macedonia, and much of Anatolia, in addition to Italy and the western Mediterranean islands.
The Romans called the Mediterranean Mare Nostrum ("Our Sea"), and by the mid-second century BCE, this was not a boast.
It was a description.

The city that had started as a cluster of huts on a swampy riverbank now ruled a territory larger than any state in Western history, and the machinery of the Republic, designed to govern a single city-state, was about to be crushed under the weight of its own success.

chapters.title: The Late Republic
sentences: The Republic conquered the world and then could not govern it.
The problem was structural.
Rome's political system was designed for a city-state: annual elections, shared power, civilian control of the military, a Senate of a few hundred leading citizens who knew each other personally.
That system worked when Rome's wars were fought within a few days' march of the city and the soldiers were farmer-citizens who went home after the campaigning season.
It broke down when Rome's commitments spanned the Mediterranean, wars lasted years or decades, and the soldiers who fought them became professionals loyal to their generals rather than to the state.
The breakdown began in 133 BCE with a murder in the Forum, and it ended a century later with the permanent abolition of republican government.
The intervening years were the most violent, dramatic, and consequential period in Roman political history.
Tiberius Gracchus (ty-BEER-ee-us GRAK-us) was a tribune of the plebs in 133 BCE, and he had a plan to save the Republic by reforming it.
The core problem was land.
Rome's Italian wars had been fought by small farmers who owned their own land and served in the army because property ownership was a requirement for military service.
But centuries of warfare had disrupted the small farming economy.

Rich senators and equestrians (the wealthy non-senatorial class) had bought up vast tracts of public land and worked them with slave labor (the wars of conquest, particularly the destruction of Carthage and the conquest of Greece, had produced enormous numbers of enslaved people).

Small farmers, unable to compete with slave-worked plantations, lost their land and drifted to Rome, swelling the ranks of an urban poor who had no property, no military obligation, and no economic prospects.

Tiberius proposed a land reform: enforce the existing (but ignored) limits on how much public land any individual could hold, redistribute the excess to landless citizens, and restore the class of small farmer-soldiers that had been the Republic's backbone.
The Senate, whose members were the primary beneficiaries of the existing system, was furious.

When Tiberius sought reelection as tribune (an unprecedented move that his opponents claimed was illegal), a mob of senators and their supporters beat him to death with chair legs and fragments of benches on the steps of the Capitol.
It was the first political murder in the Forum in the history of the Republic.
It would not be the last.

Tiberius's younger brother Gaius Gracchus (GY-us GRAK-us) picked up the reform agenda ten years later, broadening it to include subsidized grain for the urban poor, road-building projects, and the extension of citizenship to Italian allies.
He too was killed, hunted down in 121 BCE by a senatorial posse that also massacred three thousand of his supporters.
The message from the Senate was clear: reform that threatened the interests of the governing class would be met with violence.
The Gracchi failed, but their failure cracked the foundation of the Republic.
They had demonstrated two things.
First, that the problems of the Republic (displaced farmers, a growing urban underclass, resistance to reform from a self-interested Senate) were real and urgent.
Second, that the traditional political system had no mechanism for resolving deep conflicts peacefully.
When the Senate could not win an argument, it resorted to murder.
This precedent set the pattern for the next century: every major political dispute in the Late Republic would eventually be settled by violence.
The next crack came from the army.
In 107 BCE, the consul Gaius Marius (GAY-us MAHR-ee-us) reformed the Roman military in ways that would prove fatal to the Republic.
The traditional Roman army was a citizen militia: property-owning farmers who served for a campaign and then went home.
Marius opened enlistment to the landless poor (the proletarii, citizens who had no property and therefore no stake in the existing system).

He equipped them at state expense, organized the legions into standardized cohorts (administrative units of roughly 480 men), and trained them to a level of professional discipline that part-time farmer-soldiers had never achieved.
The result was a better army.
