It was also an army whose soldiers, having no land to go back to, depended on their general to provide for them after their service: retirement land, cash bonuses, a future.
The soldiers' loyalty shifted from the Republic to whatever general could deliver those rewards.
Marius himself exploited this new reality.

He won five consecutive consulships (unprecedented and technically illegal), defeated invading Germanic tribes in a pair of dramatic battles (102 and 101 BCE), and became the most powerful individual in Rome.
When his political enemies in the Senate tried to sideline him, his veteran soldiers backed him with force.
The precedent was set: a general with a loyal army was more powerful than the Senate.
The Social War (91-87 BCE) added another layer of crisis.

Rome's Italian allies (the socii, from which the war takes its name) had been fighting and dying in Rome's wars for generations but were denied Roman citizenship and the political rights that came with it.
When reform efforts failed (again, killed by senatorial resistance), the allies revolted.
The war was brutal and brief.
Rome won militarily but lost the argument: in order to end the war, the Senate granted citizenship to all Italian allies, roughly doubling the Roman citizen body overnight.
The principle that had been resisted for decades (that Rome's allies deserved full citizenship) was conceded under military pressure.
The lesson was not lost on future ambitious men: what the Senate would not give willingly, force could extract.
The social war had barely ended when Lucius Cornelius Sulla (SUH-luh) did something no Roman had ever done.
He marched his army on Rome.
Sulla was a patrician general who had served under Marius and then broken with him.

When the Senate assigned him command of a lucrative war against Mithridates (mith-rih-DAY-teez), king of Pontus in Anatolia, and a political faction loyal to Marius tried to transfer the command, Sulla simply turned his legions around and marched on the capital.
His officers refused to accompany him.
His soldiers did not.
They followed Sulla because Sulla was the man who would give them land and money after the war, not the Senate.
Sulla captured Rome, killed or exiled his enemies, left for the east to fight Mithridates, and returned in 83 BCE to capture Rome again, this time permanently.
He declared himself dictator (a legal office in Roman law, designed as an emergency measure, limited to six months), but without the traditional time limit.

He then conducted a systematic program of political murder called the proscriptions (pro-SKRIP-shunz): published lists of enemies whose property was confiscated and who could be legally killed by anyone.
Thousands died.
The heads of the proscribed were displayed on the Rostra (the speaker's platform in the Forum), and informers were rewarded with a share of the victims' property.
Sulla's dictatorship lasted two years (82-79 BCE).
He reformed the constitution in conservative directions (strengthening the Senate, weakening the tribunes), then resigned and retired to his country estate, where he died the following year.
He was the proof of concept that one man with an army could seize and hold power in Rome.
Every ambitious general who came after him knew it was possible, because Sulla had done it.
The generation after Sulla was a cascade of crises.

In 73 BCE, a Thracian gladiator named Spartacus (SPAR-tuh-kus) escaped from a gladiatorial training school at Capua with about seventy companions and launched the largest slave revolt in Roman history.
Within months, his army had swelled to 70,000 or more (some sources say 120,000), composed of enslaved people from across Italy who joined the uprising in desperation.
Spartacus defeated multiple Roman armies in the field, demonstrating both tactical skill and the terrifying fragility of Rome's slave economy.

The revolt was eventually crushed by Marcus Licinius Crassus (KRASS-us), the wealthiest man in Rome, who crucified 6,000 captured rebels along the Appian Way from Capua to Rome, a 130-mile (210 km) corridor of crosses that remained standing as a warning for years.
The Spartacus revolt exposed the ugly foundation of Roman prosperity.
By the late Republic, perhaps one-third of the population of Italy consisted of enslaved people, the human spoils of centuries of conquest.
They worked the great estates, the mines, the households of the rich.
The slave economy was essential to the Roman elite's wealth and to the cheap grain that fed the urban poor.
Spartacus did not threaten Rome militarily (he could not have conquered the city).
He threatened the system, and that was worse.

Meanwhile, Marcus Tullius Cicero (SIS-uh-roh), Rome's greatest orator and a "new man" (the first in his family to reach the Senate), was trying to defend the Republic through the only weapon he had: words.
Cicero's speeches, legal arguments, philosophical treatises, and personal letters (over 900 survive) are among the most important texts of Western civilization.
His defense of the Republic against populist politicians, corrupt governors, and would-be dictators was eloquent, passionate, and ultimately futile.
Cicero believed the Republic could be saved by the Senate if the Senate would act with virtue and unity.
The Senate would not, and could not.
The system had already broken beyond what rhetoric could repair.

By 60 BCE, three men dominated Roman politics: Pompey (POM-pee, Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus, "Pompey the Great"), a brilliant general who had cleared the Mediterranean of pirates and conquered the eastern Mediterranean; Crassus, the plutocrat who had crushed Spartacus and whose wealth gave him enormous political influence; and Julius Caesar, a rising politician from an old patrician family who had charm, ambition, and debts.
None of them could achieve his goals through the normal political system.
Together, they could bypass it.
What happened next would kill the Republic.

chapters.title: Caesar and the Death of the Republic
sentences: In 60 BCE, three men made a deal that ended five centuries of republican government.
They probably did not know it at the time.
The First Triumvirate (try-UM-vih-ruht, from Latin triumviri, "three men") was not a formal office.

It was a private political alliance: Caesar, Pompey, and Crassus agreed to pool their resources (Caesar's popularity, Pompey's military prestige, Crassus's money) and ensure that nothing happened in Roman politics without their collective approval.
Caesar was elected consul for 59 BCE and used the office to push through legislation that benefited all three partners.
Then he got what he really wanted: a five-year command in Gaul (roughly modern France and Belgium), with four legions and permission to recruit more.
The Gallic Wars (58-50 BCE) made Caesar.
Over eight years, he conquered the entirety of Gaul, extending Roman control from the Pyrenees to the Rhine, from the Atlantic to the Alps.
He fought dozens of battles, besieged dozens of hill-forts, and killed or enslaved (by his own account, which is probably inflated) over a million people.
He crossed the Rhine into Germania and the English Channel into Britain, the first Roman general to do either.
He wrote a detailed account of his campaigns, the Commentarii de Bello Gallico ("Commentaries on the Gallic War"), in elegant, spare Latin prose that is still used to teach the language today.

The Commentaries were also propaganda: Caesar published them in real time, sending dispatches back to Rome that burnished his image as a military genius while his political enemies tried to destroy him in his absence.
The Triumvirate did not survive.

Crassus, seeking military glory to match Pompey's and Caesar's, invaded Parthia (the Iranian empire that had replaced the Seleucids in the Near East) and was catastrophically defeated at the Battle of Carrhae (KAR-ee) in 53 BCE.
His army was destroyed and he was killed, reportedly by having molten gold poured down his throat by the Parthian king (a possibly apocryphal punishment for his legendary greed).
With Crassus dead, the Triumvirate collapsed into a two-man rivalry between Caesar and Pompey, and Pompey aligned himself with the Senate against Caesar.
In January 49 BCE, the Senate ordered Caesar to disband his army and return to Rome as a private citizen, where he would face prosecution by his political enemies.
Caesar had a choice: obey the law and face ruin, or break the law and start a civil war.

On January 10, he led a single legion across the Rubicon (ROO-bih-kon), a small river in northeastern Italy that marked the boundary between Gaul (where Caesar had legal military authority) and Italy proper (where bringing an army was treason).
"Alea iacta est," he reportedly said.
"The die is cast." The phrase "crossing the Rubicon" has meant "passing the point of no return" in every Western language since.
The civil war between Caesar and Pompey was swift and decisive.
Pompey, caught unprepared, fled to Greece with most of the Senate.

Caesar pursued, defeated Pompey at the Battle of Pharsalus in August 48 BCE, and chased him to Egypt, where the young pharaoh Ptolemy XIII (TOHL-eh-mee), hoping to curry favor with the winner, had Pompey murdered on the beach as Caesar's ship approached.
Caesar was reportedly displeased.
He had wanted to capture Pompey alive and pardon him, a gesture of clemency that would have established Caesar's magnanimity.

Instead, he got a severed head and a political mess in Egypt, where he became entangled with Cleopatra VII (klee-oh-PAT-ruh, the last pharaoh of the Ptolemaic dynasty, descended from one of Alexander the Great's generals, more Greek than Egyptian, and, at 21, one of the most politically astute people in the Mediterranean).
Caesar and Cleopatra became lovers.
She bore him a son, Caesarion (seh-ZAHR-ee-on, "Little Caesar").
Caesar installed her on the Egyptian throne and moved on to defeat the remaining Pompeian forces in North Africa and Spain.
By 45 BCE, Caesar was the undisputed master of the Roman world.
He was appointed dictator perpetuo ("dictator in perpetuity"), a title that amounted to king in everything but name.
He reformed the calendar (the Julian calendar, with its 365-day year and leap year, is the direct ancestor of the calendar you use today).
He granted citizenship to communities in Gaul and Spain.
He launched ambitious building programs.
He settled veterans on land.
He reorganized provincial governance.
He was, by the standards of ancient rulers, remarkably competent and surprisingly merciful (he pardoned many of his enemies, a generosity that would cost him his life).

On March 15, 44 BCE (the Ides of March, the middle day of the month in the Roman calendar), a group of about sixty senators, led by Marcus Junius Brutus (BROO-tus) and Gaius Cassius Longinus (KASH-ee-us), stabbed Caesar to death at the foot of a statue of Pompey in the Senate house.
Brutus was a man Caesar had pardoned, trusted, and possibly loved (ancient gossip claimed Brutus was Caesar's illegitimate son, which is probably false but was believed at the time).

Caesar's reported last words, "Et tu, Brute?" ("You too, Brutus?"), are Shakespeare's invention, but the ancient sources say he pulled his toga over his face when he saw Brutus among the assassins, which is more devastating.
The assassins called themselves the Liberatores ("Liberators") and believed they were restoring the Republic.
They were wrong.
Caesar's murder did not restore republican government.
It triggered thirteen more years of civil war, during which the Republic's last institutions were permanently destroyed.
Caesar's death created a power vacuum that three men rushed to fill.
Mark Antony (Marcus Antonius, AN-toh-nee), Caesar's co-consul and chief lieutenant.
Marcus Aemilius Lepidus (LEP-ih-dus), Caesar's Master of the Horse (deputy dictator).
And Gaius Octavius (ok-TAY-vee-us), Caesar's eighteen-year-old grandnephew and adopted heir, who would later take the name Augustus.
Octavian (as historians call him before he became Augustus) was, by every appearance, an improbable candidate for supreme power.
He was sickly, physically unimpressive, and had no military experience.

What he had was Caesar's name (Caesar's will adopted him and made him his heir), Caesar's money (a massive fortune that Octavian used to buy loyalty), and a political intelligence that would prove to be the most formidable in Roman history.
The Second Triumvirate (formally established in 43 BCE, unlike the first one, this was a legally constituted office) divided the Roman world among Antony, Octavian, and Lepidus.
Their first act was to conduct proscriptions even more savage than Sulla's.
The most famous victim was Cicero, the old republican orator, whose head and hands were cut off and displayed on the Rostra from which he had delivered his greatest speeches.
Antony's wife reportedly pierced Cicero's tongue with a hairpin, a punishment for the words that had attacked her husband.
The Triumvirs then hunted down and defeated the assassins Brutus and Cassius at the Battle of Philippi in 42 BCE.
Both assassins committed suicide.
The Republic's last defenders were dead.
What followed was a slow-motion power struggle between Antony and Octavian that played out across the Mediterranean over the next decade.
Lepidus was sidelined early.
Antony took the wealthy east, establishing himself in Egypt and beginning a political and romantic alliance with Cleopatra that scandalized Rome (Antony was married to Octavian's sister at the time).

Octavian kept the west and used the years to build his political base in Italy, cultivating the Senate, settling veterans, and conducting a propaganda campaign that painted Antony as a decadent traitor who had abandoned Rome for an eastern queen.
The final showdown came at the Battle of Actium on September 2, 31 BCE, a naval engagement off the western coast of Greece.
Octavian's fleet, commanded by his general Agrippa (uh-GRIP-uh), defeated the combined forces of Antony and Cleopatra.
The battle itself was something of an anticlimax (Cleopatra's squadron broke through and fled, Antony followed, and the rest of their fleet surrendered).

Antony and Cleopatra retreated to Egypt, where both committed suicide the following year: Antony by his own sword, Cleopatra (according to the famous but possibly legendary account) by the bite of an asp (a venomous snake).
Cleopatra's death ended the Ptolemaic dynasty that had ruled Egypt since the breakup of Alexander's empire nearly three centuries earlier.
Egypt became a Roman province, and its grain fed Rome for the next five centuries.
Octavian was, at 33, the sole ruler of the Roman world.
The Republic was dead.
The question was what would replace it.
The answer Octavian found was the most brilliant political performance in the history of government.

chapters.title: Augustus and the Principate
sentences: On January 16, 27 BCE, a man named Gaius Octavius stood before the Roman Senate and announced that he was giving up all his extraordinary powers and restoring the Republic.
It was a lie.
It was also a masterpiece.
The Senate, as Octavian surely expected, begged him to reconsider.
They voted him the honorific name Augustus (aw-GUS-tus, "the revered one," a title with religious overtones that no Roman had ever held before).

They granted him control of the most important provinces (the ones with the legions), the right to propose legislation, and supreme imperium (im-PEER-ee-um, the legal authority to command), while he ceremoniously "returned" the peaceful provinces to Senate administration.
The result was a system that looked like the Republic but functioned as a monarchy.
Augustus held supreme power while maintaining the fiction that the Senate, the consuls, the tribunes, and the assemblies still governed.
He called himself princeps (PRIN-keps, "first citizen"), not king, not dictator.
The system he created is called the Principate (PRIN-sih-put), the rule of the first citizen.
This fiction mattered.
Julius Caesar had been murdered for looking too much like a king.
Augustus learned the lesson.
He dressed modestly, lived in a relatively simple house on the Palatine Hill, walked to the Forum without bodyguards, and treated senators with elaborate courtesy.
He was, behind the scenes, the most powerful individual in the Mediterranean world.
In public, he was a humble servant of the state.
The performance was so convincing that it lasted, in various forms, for three centuries.
Augustus's reign (27 BCE to 14 CE, forty-one years) was one of the most consequential in human history.
He established a professional standing army of twenty-eight legions (roughly 150,000 soldiers), stationed on the frontiers and paid from the state treasury.

He created the Praetorian Guard (pree-TOR-ee-un), an elite bodyguard of roughly 4,500 soldiers stationed in Rome, which would later become a political force powerful enough to make and unmake emperors.

He established a professional civil service to administer the provinces, replacing the corrupt system of senatorial governors who had treated their provinces as personal piggy banks during the Republic.
He reformed taxation, built roads, founded colonies for veterans, and constructed the infrastructure that would support the empire for centuries.

The Pax Romana (Latin for "Roman Peace") is conventionally dated from Augustus's accession in 27 BCE to the death of Marcus Aurelius in 180 CE, a period of roughly two hundred years during which the Mediterranean world experienced a degree of peace, stability, and prosperity unprecedented in its history.
This does not mean there were no wars (there were plenty, mostly on the frontiers) or no civil conflicts (there were several, including a ghastly year with four emperors).
But the interior of the empire, from Spain to Syria, from Britain to Egypt, was largely free from major warfare, and the result was an explosion of trade, urbanization, and cultural exchange.

At its peak, the Roman Empire contained roughly 60 to 70 million people (about a quarter of the world's population), connected by a road network of over 250,000 miles (400,000 km), a common legal system, and two common languages (Latin in the west, Greek in the east).

The Pax Romana was, in economic terms, a single market: goods, people, and ideas moved freely across three continents with a degree of integration that would not be matched in Europe until the twentieth century.
Augustus also presided over a golden age of Latin literature.

The poet Virgil (VER-jil, Publius Vergilius Maro) wrote the Aeneid, Rome's national epic, connecting Roman origins to the fall of Troy and casting Augustus's reign as the fulfillment of divine destiny.

Ovid (AH-vid, Publius Ovidius Naso) wrote the Metamorphoses, a sprawling mythological epic of transformation that became one of the most influential works of Western literature (Augustus exiled him to the Black Sea in 8 CE for reasons that remain mysterious).
Horace (HOR-us) wrote odes and satires that set the standard for Latin lyric poetry.
Livy began his monumental history of Rome.

The architect Vitruvius (vih-TROO-vee-us) wrote De Architectura, the only surviving ancient treatise on architecture and engineering, which would influence Renaissance architects over a thousand years later.

The Pont du Gard (PONT doo GAR), a three-tiered aqueduct bridge 160 feet (49 meters) high in southern Gaul, was built around this time, carrying water 31 miles (50 km) to the city of Nimes (NEEM) and standing as one of the most impressive engineering works of the Roman period.
Roman concrete (opus caementicium, a revolutionary mixture of volcanic ash, lime, and rock that could set underwater) was perfected during the late Republic and early Empire.
This single material enabled everything that makes Roman architecture Roman: the arches, the vaults, the domes, the harbors, the aqueducts.
Concrete is not glamorous.
It is arguably the most important building material in human history.

Roman concrete has lasted two thousand years without reinforcement, a feat that modern Portland cement cannot match (Roman harbor concrete, studied by modern materials scientists, actually gets stronger over time through a chemical reaction between the volcanic ash and seawater).
The Pantheon (Chapter 7), the most perfectly preserved Roman building, has an unreinforced concrete dome that remains the largest of its kind after nearly nineteen centuries.
Augustus also reshaped Rome's relationship with the wider world.
The annexation of Egypt in 30 BCE (after Cleopatra's death) gave Rome control of the most productive agricultural region in the Mediterranean.

Egyptian grain fed the city of Rome through the annona (ah-NOH-nuh, the state grain supply), a massive logistical operation that shipped grain from the Nile Valley to the port of Ostia (AHS-tee-uh, at the mouth of the Tiber) and distributed it to Rome's million-plus population.
The annona was a political necessity: a Roman mob deprived of bread was a Roman mob that rioted.
Augustus's system of regular, subsidized grain distribution kept the city fed and the population quiescent for centuries.
The Roman appetite for luxury goods also created the first truly global trade network.
Roman gold flowed east along the Silk Road (the network of overland and maritime routes connecting the Mediterranean to China) in exchange for Chinese silk, Indian spices, and Arabian incense.
The silver denarius (deh-NAHR-ee-us, introduced in 211 BCE during the Second Punic War), already Rome's standard coin, now became the common currency from Britain to Mesopotamia.
Roman coins have been found in India, Vietnam, and even China.
The Pax Romana was not just a military achievement.
It was an economic system, and it reached farther than the legions ever marched.
Augustus died on August 19, 14 CE, at the age of 75.
His reported last words were: "Have I played my part well?
Then applaud as I exit." The actor metaphor was apt.
He had played the role of princeps for forty-one years, and the performance had been so convincing that nobody alive could remember what the Republic had actually looked like.
The problem was succession.
Augustus had spent decades trying to find an heir, and one by one his chosen successors died.
His nephew Marcellus.
His general and son-in-law Agrippa.
His grandsons Gaius and Lucius.
In the end, the man who inherited the empire was Tiberius (ty-BEER-ee-us), Augustus's stepson, a capable but gloomy military commander whom Augustus had never particularly liked.
The Principate's succession mechanism, such as it was, came down to adoption: the reigning emperor adopted his chosen successor, and the Senate confirmed the choice.

It was not hereditary monarchy in the traditional sense (there was no fixed dynasty), but it produced the same result: power passed within a small family group, and the choice depended more on proximity to the emperor than on merit.
The Julio-Claudian dynasty (Augustus through Nero, 27 BCE to 68 CE) demonstrated both the strengths and pathologies of this system.

Tiberius was competent but unpopular, eventually withdrawing to the island of Capri (KAH-pree) and governing through his prefect Sejanus (seh-JAH-nus), who ran a reign of terror in Rome before Tiberius turned on him.

Caligula (kah-LIG-yoo-luh, formally Gaius Julius Caesar Germanicus, emperor 37-41 CE) began with promise and descended into what the sources describe as madness (he reportedly planned to make his horse a consul, though this may have been a deliberate insult to the Senate rather than genuine insanity).
He was assassinated by the Praetorian Guard after less than four years.

Claudius (KLAW-dee-us, emperor 41-54 CE), dragged from behind a curtain by the Praetorians who found him hiding after Caligula's murder, turned out to be a surprisingly effective administrator who conquered Britain in 43 CE, expanding the empire to its northwestern limit, and began the massive expansion of the Port of Ostia to handle Rome's growing trade.
And then came Nero (NEER-oh, emperor 54-68 CE), the last of the Julio-Claudians, who has been remembered for two thousand years as the embodiment of imperial decadence and cruelty.
The historical Nero is harder to assess than the legendary monster.
He was a patron of the arts (particularly fond of performing on stage, which scandalized the Roman elite).
He may or may not have been responsible for the Great Fire of Rome in 64 CE, which burned for six days and destroyed ten of the city's fourteen districts.
The story that Nero "fiddled while Rome burned" is an anachronism (fiddles did not exist), but the ancient sources do say he sang and played the lyre while watching the fire.
He used the cleared land to build the Domus Aurea (DOH-mus OW-ree-uh, "Golden House"), a lavish palace complex covering perhaps 100 to 300 acres of central Rome.
And he blamed the fire on the city's small Christian community, launching the first organized persecution of Christians in Roman history.

Nero's reign ended in 68 CE when the army revolted, the Senate declared him a public enemy, and he committed suicide with the help of a secretary, reportedly gasping "Qualis artifex pereo!" ("What an artist dies in me!").
His death ended the Julio-Claudian dynasty and plunged Rome into a year of civil war.

chapters.title: The Flavians and the Five Good Emperors
sentences: The Year of the Four Emperors (69 CE) proved a truth that the historian Tacitus (TAS-ih-tus) stated with his usual acid precision: "the secret of empire was out, that an emperor could be made elsewhere than in Rome." After Nero's suicide, four men claimed the throne in rapid succession.

Galba (GAL-buh), the elderly governor of Spain, took power first but was murdered by the Praetorian Guard after seven months for being stingy with donatives (bribes to the soldiers, a tradition Augustus had started and no emperor could afford to ignore).
Otho (AH-thoh), Galba's former ally, seized the throne but was defeated by Vitellius (vy-TEL-ee-us), the commander of the Rhine legions, and committed suicide.

Vitellius held Rome for eight months before being overthrown and killed by the forces of Titus Flavius Vespasianus (ves-PAY-zhee-AH-nus), better known as Vespasian, the commander of the legions besieging Jerusalem in Judaea.
Vespasian (emperor 69-79 CE) was everything the Julio-Claudians' later members were not: practical, frugal, and ruthlessly competent.
He came from an Italian equestrian family, not from the old Roman aristocracy.

He stabilized the finances (when criticized for taxing public latrines, he held up a coin and asked "Does this smell?"), rebuilt the city, and began construction of the building that would become Rome's most famous monument.
The Colosseum (properly the Flavian Amphitheatre) was completed in 80 CE under Vespasian's son Titus (TY-tus, emperor 79-81 CE).

Built on the site of Nero's private lake in the grounds of the Domus Aurea, the Colosseum was a deliberate political statement: where Nero had built a pleasure palace for himself, the Flavians built an entertainment venue for the people.
The structure is an engineering marvel.
Four stories of arched concrete and stone, 157 feet (48 meters) high, seating roughly 50,000 spectators.
It had a retractable canvas awning (the velarium) operated by sailors from the Roman navy.

Beneath the arena floor, a network of tunnels, holding cells, and mechanical lifts (operated by counterweights and pulleys) could raise animals, scenery, and gladiators through trapdoors into the arena.

The gladiatorial games held there were spectacular, brutal, and enormously popular: men fought men, men fought animals, animals fought animals, and occasionally the arena was flooded for mock naval battles.
The games were not mindless entertainment.
They were political theater, demonstrations of Roman power, wealth, and the emperor's generosity.

In 79 CE, the year before the Colosseum's completion, the volcano Mount Vesuvius (veh-SOO-vee-us) erupted catastrophically, burying the cities of Pompeii (pom-PAY) and Herculaneum (her-kyoo-LAY-nee-um) under volcanic ash and pyroclastic flows.

The disaster killed thousands, including the naturalist Pliny the Elder (PLIN-ee, Gaius Plinius Secundus, who had written a 37-volume encyclopedia of the natural world and who sailed toward the eruption to observe it and rescue survivors, dying in the process).
His nephew Pliny the Younger watched from across the Bay of Naples and wrote a detailed eyewitness account that remains the earliest surviving description of a volcanic eruption.
The buried cities became time capsules.

When excavation began in the eighteenth century, archaeologists found streets, houses, shops, taverns, bathhouses, brothels, temples, and theaters preserved under the ash exactly as they had been in 79 CE.
They found bread in ovens, food on tables, graffiti on walls ("I don't want to sell my husband," one woman scratched into a wall; "Vote for Vatia!
All the drunks support him!" reads an election notice).
They found political campaign slogans, laundry lists, love notes, and insults.

Pompeii gives us something no other ancient site can: an intimate, unfiltered snapshot of ordinary Roman daily life, preserved not by the careful hand of a historian but by the random violence of a volcano.
Before the eruption, Titus had overseen another event with far-reaching consequences.

In 70 CE (before he became emperor, serving as his father Vespasian's military commander), Titus besieged and destroyed Jerusalem, including the Second Temple (the center of Jewish worship, originally built after the Babylonian exile and expanded by Herod the Great).

The destruction of the Temple was a catastrophic event for Judaism, ending the sacrificial cult that had been the religion's ritual center and accelerating the transformation of Judaism from a temple-based religion to a text-based one centered on synagogue worship, rabbinic interpretation, and the study of Torah.
The spoils of Jerusalem (including, according to tradition, the golden menorah from the Temple) were paraded through Rome and depicted on the Arch of Titus, which still stands in the Forum.

The event also scattered Jewish communities across the Roman world, reinforcing the diaspora (dy-AS-puh-ruh, the dispersion of Jewish communities beyond their homeland) that had been growing since the Babylonian exile six centuries earlier (Ancient Israel Ch 8).
The Flavian dynasty ended with Vespasian's younger son Domitian (doh-MISH-un, emperor 81-96 CE), who proved to be an autocratic and paranoid ruler.
His assassination by court officials in 96 CE brought to power a very different kind of emperor: Nerva (NER-vuh), an elderly senator chosen by the Senate itself.

Nerva's great contribution was the principle of adoptive succession: instead of passing power to a biological heir (who might be incompetent or insane), the emperor would adopt the most capable available man as his son and successor.

Nerva adopted the Spanish-born general Trajan, establishing a precedent that produced what the eighteenth-century historian Edward Gibbon called "the period in the history of the world during which the condition of the human race was most happy and prosperous." The Five Good Emperors (Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, Antoninus Pius, and Marcus Aurelius, 96-180 CE) represent the Roman Empire at its peak.
Trajan (TRAY-jun, emperor 98-117 CE) expanded the empire to its greatest territorial extent.

He conquered Dacia (DAY-shuh, roughly modern Romania), a wealthy kingdom north of the Danube whose gold mines enriched the Roman treasury, and commemorated the victory with Trajan's Column, a 98-foot (30-meter) marble column in Rome carved with a continuous spiral frieze of over 2,500 figures depicting the Dacian campaigns in extraordinary detail (it remains one of the most important sources for Roman military equipment and tactics).
He also briefly conquered Mesopotamia, pushing Rome's eastern frontier to the Persian Gulf, though these eastern conquests were abandoned by his successor.

Trajan was so admired that later Roman senators greeted each new emperor with the formulaic wish: "Be luckier than Augustus, better than Trajan." Hadrian (HAY-dree-un, emperor 117-138 CE) consolidated what Trajan had expanded.
He abandoned the eastern conquests, pulled back to defensible borders, and spent much of his reign traveling the empire, inspecting defenses, and building.
In northern Britain, he ordered the construction of Hadrian's Wall, a 73-mile (117 km) fortification running coast to coast across the island, marking the empire's northernmost frontier.
The wall (much of which still stands) was not just a barrier.
It was a customs point, a patrol road, and a visible statement of Roman authority on the edge of the known world.

In Rome, Hadrian rebuilt the Pantheon (PAN-thee-on), a temple to all the gods, with a revolutionary unreinforced concrete dome 142 feet (43 meters) in diameter, the largest such dome in the world for nearly two millennia (the oculus, a 27-foot or 8.2-meter circular opening at the dome's apex, provides the only source of light and still lets rain fall on the marble floor below).
The Pantheon's dome was wider than any structure built before the twentieth century, including the domes of Hagia Sophia in Constantinople (built 400 years later, Chapter 9) and St.
Peter's Basilica in Rome (built 1,400 years later).
It is the best-preserved building from the ancient world, and it remains in use as a church.
That a concrete dome built in 125 CE is still structurally sound tells you everything about Roman engineering.

Hadrian was also a lover of Greek culture (a philhellene, fil-HEL-een, "lover of Greece"), and his reign saw the full integration of Greek and Roman high culture into a single Greco-Roman civilization.

By the second century CE, educated Romans and Greeks read the same literature, worshipped the same gods (under different names), and shared the same philosophical traditions (particularly Stoicism, the philosophy founded by the Greek Zeno of Citium, which taught that virtue, reason, and self-discipline were the highest goods, that emotions should be controlled by rational thought, and that all people shared a common humanity under natural law, regardless of social station or nationality).
The road network reached its peak under the Five Good Emperors: over 250,000 miles (400,000 km) of roads, including 50,000 miles (80,000 km) of paved highways.
By the second century CE, eleven aqueducts supplied Rome with over a million cubic meters of water daily, feeding public baths, fountains, and private homes.

The Baths of Caracalla (completed in 216 CE, under a later emperor but representative of the building tradition) could accommodate 1,600 bathers at once and included libraries, gardens, exercise areas, and heated floors.
Public bathing was not a luxury in Rome.
It was a civic institution, available to nearly everyone, and the bath complexes were among the most impressive buildings in any Roman city.

The astronomer Claudius Ptolemy (TOHL-eh-mee, no relation to the Egyptian royal dynasty), working in Roman Alexandria around 100-170 CE, wrote the Almagest, the definitive astronomical treatise of antiquity, and the Geographia, which mapped the known world.
His geocentric model of the solar system (Earth at the center, everything else revolving around it) would dominate astronomy for 1,400 years until Copernicus.
The physician Galen (GAY-lun) of Pergamon, who served as physician to several emperors, wrote anatomical and medical treatises that dominated Western medicine for 1,300 years.

The historian Tacitus wrote the Annals and Histories, the most detailed and politically sophisticated accounts of the early empire, in a mordant, epigrammatic style that has never been surpassed in historical writing.

Marcus Aurelius (aw-REE-lee-us, emperor 161-180 CE), the last of the Five Good Emperors, was a philosopher on the throne: a Stoic who spent much of his reign fighting wars he did not want against Germanic tribes pressing on the Danube frontier.
His Meditations, a personal journal of Stoic philosophy written during military campaigns in the field, was never intended for publication.

It is the most intimate surviving document from any Roman emperor, a record of a man trying to be good in a world that made goodness difficult, and it remains one of the most read works of ancient philosophy.
"The universe is change; our life is what our thoughts make it," he wrote while commanding legions on the frozen Danube.

But Marcus Aurelius's reign also saw the arrival of the Antonine Plague (165-180 CE), a devastating pandemic (probably smallpox) brought back by soldiers returning from the east, which killed an estimated 5 to 10 million people across the empire, depopulated cities, disrupted trade, and weakened the military.
The plague did not destroy the empire, but it weakened it at the precise moment when external pressures were increasing.
Marcus Aurelius spent his final years fighting Germanic incursions along the Danube and died on campaign in 180 CE.
And then he made the worst decision of any of the Five Good Emperors: he abandoned the adoptive succession and bequeathed the empire to his biological son, Commodus.

chapters.title: Crisis and Recovery
sentences: Commodus (KOM-uh-dus, emperor 180-192 CE) was everything the Five Good Emperors were not.
Where Trajan was disciplined, Hadrian cultured, and Marcus Aurelius philosophical, Commodus was vain, violent, and spectacularly uninterested in governing.

He renamed the months of the year after himself, fought as a gladiator in the Colosseum (an act that horrified the aristocracy, since gladiators were legally infames, persons of no legal standing), and was eventually strangled in his bath by his wrestling partner on New Year's Eve, 192 CE.
His death proved Tacitus's observation about emperors being made outside Rome.
Civil war erupted.

Four men claimed the throne in rapid succession (the Year of the Five Emperors, 193 CE), until Septimius Severus (seh-VER-us), the commander of the Danube legions, fought his way to power and established the Severan dynasty (193-235 CE).
Severus was born in Leptis Magna (LEP-tis MAG-nuh) in modern Libya, the first emperor from Africa.

He expanded the army, raised military pay, and told his sons on his deathbed: "Enrich the soldiers, ignore everyone else." His son Caracalla (kah-ruh-KAL-uh, emperor 211-217 CE) took the advice literally, lavishing money on the army while issuing the Edict of Caracalla in 212 CE, which extended Roman citizenship to all free inhabitants of the empire.
This was one of the most sweeping legal acts in ancient history, but its motivation was probably fiscal rather than idealistic: citizens paid more taxes than non-citizens.
In a single decree, Caracalla expanded the tax base by millions.
He also built the enormous Baths of Caracalla, whose ruins in Rome still testify to the scale of Roman public building.

After the Severan dynasty ended in 235 CE (the last Severan emperor, the teenage Severus Alexander, was murdered by his own soldiers), the empire fell into the Crisis of the Third Century (235-284 CE), the most dangerous period in its history.
The Crisis was everything going wrong at once.
In fifty years, more than twenty men claimed the title of emperor.
The average reign was about two years.

Most emperors died violently: killed by their own troops, by rival claimants, or by Persians (the Sassanid Empire, which had replaced Parthia in the east in 224 CE, was a far more aggressive and organized enemy than the Parthians had been).
In 260 CE, Emperor Valerian (vuh-LEER-ee-un) was captured in battle by the Sassanid king Shapur I (shah-POOR), the first and only Roman emperor ever taken prisoner by a foreign enemy.
According to Persian sources (which may be propagandistic), Shapur used Valerian as a human footstool when mounting his horse.
The empire fragmented.
In the west, a breakaway Gallic Empire (controlling Gaul, Britain, and parts of Spain) operated independently from 260 to 274 CE.

In the east, the city of Palmyra (pal-MY-ruh, a wealthy oasis trading city in the Syrian desert) under its queen Zenobia (zeh-NOH-bee-uh) carved out an independent empire that briefly controlled Egypt, Syria, and much of Anatolia.
For over a decade, the "Roman Empire" was three empires, and it looked like none of them would survive.
The economy collapsed.
The silver content of the denarius, which had been progressively debased by emperors needing to pay their soldiers, fell from roughly 95% pure silver under Augustus to less than 5% by the 260s.
Inflation spiraled.
Trade networks that had operated smoothly for two centuries broke down.
Cities shrank.
Rural populations withdrew into defensible hilltop settlements.
The archaeological record shows a sharp decline in building activity, trade goods, and urban life across the western provinces.
Plague returned.

The Plague of Cyprian (roughly 249-262 CE, named for the Christian bishop who described it) killed thousands daily in Rome at its peak and swept across the empire, further depleting the population and the military.
Germanic and Gothic peoples pressed the northern frontiers with increasing force.
The Alamanni (al-uh-MAN-ee) raided into Italy itself.
The Goths attacked the Balkans and the Black Sea coast.
For the first time since the Gallic sack of 390 BCE, barbarian armies penetrated deep into the Roman heartland.
The man who saved the empire was an unlikely figure.
Diocletian (dy-oh-KLEE-shun, Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus, emperor 284-305 CE) was a soldier of humble Dalmatian origins who rose through the ranks and was proclaimed emperor by his troops.
He was not a philosopher, a builder, or a literary patron.

He was an organizer, and he reorganized the empire so thoroughly that the government Augustus had designed for a city-state-turned-empire was finally replaced by a structure adequate to govern a world-spanning superpower.

Diocletian's first innovation was the Tetrarchy (teh-TRAR-kee, "rule of four"), a system in which the empire was divided between two senior emperors (each called Augustus) and two junior emperors (each called Caesar), with each ruler responsible for a specific region.
Diocletian took the east; his co-Augustus Maximian (mak-SIM-ee-un) took the west; two Caesars governed the sub-regions and were groomed to succeed the Augusti.

The system was designed to solve three problems at once: the empire was too large for one man to defend (four rulers could respond to threats on multiple frontiers simultaneously), succession needed to be orderly (the Caesars were pre-selected successors), and the emperor needed to be present on the frontier (instead of one emperor stuck in Rome while barbarians attacked Britain and Persia attacked Mesopotamia).
Diocletian also reformed the administration.
He roughly doubled the number of provinces (from about 50 to over 100), making each small enough to prevent any governor from becoming a military threat.
He separated civil and military authority in the provinces (previously, governors had commanded both the bureaucracy and the army).
He reformed taxation, creating a new system based on regular censuses of land and population.

He tried (unsuccessfully) to control inflation with the Edict on Maximum Prices (301 CE), fixing maximum prices for over a thousand goods and services, with death as the penalty for violations (the edict failed because it is impossible to legislate prices in a market economy, as every government that has tried since has also discovered).
Most controversially, Diocletian transformed the role of emperor itself.
Augustus had been princeps, first citizen, a man who walked to the Forum and shook hands with senators.
Diocletian was dominus et deus, "lord and god." He wore purple silk robes, a gem-studded diadem, and gold-embroidered shoes.
Visitors prostrated themselves before him.

The elaborate court ceremony was borrowed from the Sassanid Persian court, and it served a purpose: by elevating the emperor above ordinary humanity, Diocletian made it harder for a random general to declare himself emperor and be taken seriously.
The bar for claiming divinity was higher than the bar for claiming political office.
Diocletian also launched the Great Persecution of Christians (303-311 CE), the last and most severe of the Roman persecutions.
Churches were demolished, scriptures burned, clergy imprisoned, and Christians who refused to sacrifice to the traditional gods were tortured and executed.

The persecution was not uniform across the empire (it was harshest in the east, where Diocletian ruled directly, and mildest in the western provinces under the more tolerant Constantius), and it ultimately failed: Christianity had grown too large, too organized, and too deeply rooted to be destroyed by state violence.
The persecution's main long-term effect was to create a generation of Christian martyrs whose suffering strengthened the faith rather than breaking it.
Diocletian did something no emperor before him had done: he retired.
In 305 CE, he and Maximian abdicated simultaneously (Diocletian voluntarily, Maximian reluctantly), and the two Caesars stepped up to become the new Augusti.
The transition was peaceful.

Diocletian retired to a massive palace he had built at Spalatum (SPAL-uh-tum, modern Split, Croatia), where he famously grew cabbages and told an envoy who urged him to return to power: "If you could show him the cabbages I planted with my own hands, he would not ask me to do that." The Tetrarchy did not survive his retirement.
Within two years, the system collapsed into civil war as multiple claimants fought for supremacy.
But Diocletian's administrative reforms (the reorganized provinces, the separated civil and military commands, the professional bureaucracy, the reformed tax system) lasted for centuries.
He had saved the empire by fundamentally changing it.

The Rome that emerged from the Crisis of the Third Century was a different state from the one that had entered it: more authoritarian, more bureaucratic, more openly monarchical, and more centralized.
It was also Christian, or about to be.

chapters.title: Constantine and Christianity
sentences: The civil wars that followed Diocletian's retirement produced the most consequential emperor since Augustus.

Constantine (KON-stan-teen, Flavius Valerius Constantinus, emperor 306-337 CE) was the son of Constantius, one of Diocletian's Caesars, and he spent two decades fighting his way to sole control of the empire.
What made his reign transformative was not military conquest but a decision about religion that reshaped Western civilization.
Before we get to that decision, some context on the religion itself.
Christianity in the early fourth century was roughly three centuries old.
It had begun as a Jewish sect in the Roman province of Judaea following the crucifixion of Jesus of Nazareth around 30 CE, executed by the Roman prefect Pontius Pilate (PON-shus PY-lut).

The movement's early growth owed much to Paul of Tarsus (originally Saul, a Pharisee who persecuted Christians before a dramatic conversion), who traveled the eastern Mediterranean between roughly 45 and 64 CE founding churches in Asia Minor, Greece, and Rome.

Paul's letters (epistles) to these churches form a major portion of the New Testament and articulated the theological framework that distinguished Christianity from its Jewish parent: salvation through faith in Christ, open to all peoples regardless of ethnicity or circumcision.
Christianity spread rapidly through the Roman Empire's communication networks (the same roads and sea lanes that carried trade goods carried missionaries).

By the time of Diocletian's persecution, perhaps 10 to 15 percent of the empire's population was Christian, with the highest concentrations in the eastern provinces, in North Africa, and in Rome itself.

Christians had their own organizational structure (bishops governing local congregations, with the bishops of Rome, Alexandria, Antioch, Constantinople, and Jerusalem claiming special authority), their own literature, their own charitable networks, and their own internal theological disputes.
They were, in effect, a state within the state, and Diocletian's persecution was a response to that reality.
In October 312 CE, Constantine marched on Rome to fight his rival Maxentius (mak-SEN-tee-us).

Before the Battle of the Milvian Bridge (on the Tiber just outside Rome), Constantine reportedly had a vision: a cross of light in the sky with the words "In hoc signo vinces" ("In this sign, conquer").
He ordered his soldiers to paint the Chi-Rho (KY-ROH, the first two letters of "Christ" in Greek, superimposed to form a symbol that looks like a P crossed with an X) on their shields.
He won the battle.
Maxentius drowned in the Tiber.
Whether the vision was genuine, politically calculated, or a later literary invention is unknowable.
What matters is what Constantine did next.

In February 313 CE, Constantine and his eastern co-emperor Licinius (lih-SIN-ee-us) issued the Edict of Milan, granting religious toleration throughout the empire and specifically ending the persecution of Christians.
The Edict did not make Christianity the state religion (that would come later, under Theodosius).
What it did was legalize Christian worship, return confiscated church property, and establish the principle that the state would not persecute people for their religious beliefs.
This was genuinely revolutionary.

The Roman state had always tied religion to politics: the emperor was the Pontifex Maximus, and failure to sacrifice to the traditional gods was considered not a private matter of conscience but a public act of disloyalty.
The Edict of Milan broke that link.
Constantine went further.
He showered the Christian church with money, land, and legal privileges.
He built basilicas (the architectural form of the Roman public hall, adapted for Christian worship, and the direct ancestor of every Christian church building since).
He exempted clergy from taxes and public service obligations.
He intervened in Christian theological disputes, not as a priest but as a political patron who needed his religion to be unified.
The most important intervention was the Council of Nicaea (ny-SEE-uh, in modern northwestern Turkey) in 325 CE, the first ecumenical (empire-wide) council of Christian bishops.

Constantine convened the council to resolve a theological dispute called the Arian controversy (after Arius, AH-ree-us, an Alexandrian priest who argued that Christ was a created being, subordinate to God the Father, not co-eternal with him).

The council produced the Nicene Creed, a statement of faith declaring Christ "of one substance with the Father" (homoousios, a single Greek word that caused centuries of argument), which became the doctrinal standard for mainstream Christianity.
Constantine did not dictate the theology, but he provided the venue, paid the expenses, and made clear that he expected a resolution.

The Council of Nicaea established a pattern that would define the relationship between church and state for the next millennium: the emperor as protector and arbiter of Christianity, the church as the spiritual legitimizer of imperial authority.
This partnership would be the foundation of Byzantine civilization (the next TL in this chain) and, in a different form, of medieval European Christendom.
In 330 CE, Constantine founded a new capital.

Constantinople (kon-stan-tih-NOH-pul, "City of Constantine," modern Istanbul) was built on the site of the ancient Greek colony of Byzantium (bih-ZAN-tee-um), on a triangular peninsula at the southern end of the Bosporus (the strait connecting the Sea of Marmara to the Black Sea).

The location was superb: surrounded by water on three sides, controlling the straits between Europe and Asia, accessible by sea from both the Mediterranean and the Black Sea, and positioned far closer to the empire's most threatened frontiers (the Danube and the Persian border) than Rome was.
Constantinople was designed as a Christian city from the start: no pagan temples, but churches, a Senate house, a hippodrome for chariot racing, and a massive set of defensive walls.
It would become the greatest city in the medieval world and would not fall to a foreign enemy for over eleven centuries.
The founding of Constantinople was arguably Constantine's most lasting achievement.
Rome, the old capital, had been losing its practical importance for decades (Diocletian had governed from Nicomedia, not Rome; Maximian from Milan).
Constantinople gave the empire an eastern capital that was strategically located, economically vital, and symbolically charged.
When the western half of the empire collapsed in the fifth century, Constantinople kept the eastern half alive for another thousand years.
Constantine was baptized on his deathbed in 337 CE.
Whether his conversion was sincere or political (or both) is one of the great questions of religious history.
It does not matter.

What matters is that the most powerful man in the Western world threw his weight behind Christianity, and the religion went from a persecuted minority to the empire's most powerful institution within a single generation.
The decades after Constantine saw Christianity transform from a tolerated religion to the empire's sole legal faith.
Julian (JOO-lee-un, emperor 361-363 CE), Constantine's nephew, tried to reverse the process.

He was a committed pagan (a word Christians used for followers of the traditional Greco-Roman religions) who had been raised Christian and rejected it in favor of a philosophical neo-Platonist paganism.
He restored pagan temples, stripped Christian clergy of their privileges, and attempted to revive traditional worship.
But he was killed fighting the Sassanid Persians after only twenty months in power, and the pagan revival died with him.
The Christians called him Julian the Apostate.
No subsequent emperor would attempt to restore paganism.
The decisive step came under Theodosius I (thee-oh-DOH-shee-us, emperor 379-395 CE).
In 380 CE, Theodosius issued the Edict of Thessalonica, declaring Nicene Christianity the official state religion and ordering all subjects to adopt it.
In 391 CE, he banned pagan sacrifice, closed the temples, and forbade the Olympic Games (which had been celebrated for over a thousand years, since 776 BCE).
Christianity was no longer a tolerated faith.
It was the only legal religion in the Roman Empire.
One more event from Theodosius's reign proved the church's new power.

In 390 CE, after Theodosius ordered a massacre of civilians in Thessalonica in retaliation for the murder of a Roman general, the bishop of Milan, Ambrose (AM-brohz), excommunicated the emperor and refused to readmit him until he did public penance.
Theodosius submitted.
An emperor prostrated himself before a bishop.
The church had achieved moral authority over the state, a development that would shape Western politics for the next thousand years.
The theologian Augustine of Hippo (aw-GUS-tin, 354-430 CE), writing in North Africa, produced two works that would define Western Christianity for the next millennium.

His Confessions (the first autobiography in Western literature, an intimate account of his conversion from dissolute youth to baptized believer) and his City of God (a massive philosophical and theological response to the Visigothic sack of Rome in 410 CE, arguing that the true "city" of the Christian was heavenly, not earthly) established the intellectual framework for medieval Christian thought.
In 395 CE, Theodosius died and divided the empire between his two sons: Arcadius (ar-KAY-dee-us) in the east and Honorius (oh-NOR-ee-us) in the west.
The division was intended to be administrative, not permanent.
It became permanent.
The two halves of the Roman Empire would never be reunited under a single ruler.
The eastern half, centered on Constantinople, would survive for another thousand years as the Byzantine Empire.
The western half had less than a century left.

chapters.title: The Fall
sentences: The fall of the Western Roman Empire is the most discussed ending in history, and historians have been arguing about its causes since before it was over.

The traditional date is September 4, 476 CE, when a Germanic chieftain named Odoacer (OH-doh-AY-ser) deposed the last Western Roman emperor, a teenager named Romulus Augustulus (ROM-yoo-lus aw-GUS-tyoo-lus, whose name, containing the names of both Rome's legendary founder and its first emperor, was an irony that even contemporaries noticed).
Odoacer did not destroy Rome.
He did not burn the city.

He simply informed the Senate that the Western Empire no longer needed an emperor and sent the imperial regalia to Constantinople, acknowledging the Eastern Emperor as the sole legitimate ruler of the Roman world.
The end of the Western Roman Empire was not a thunderclap.
It was a shrug.
But the shrug had been preceded by eighty years of catastrophe.
The Western Empire's collapse was a death by a thousand cuts, and the cuts came from every direction.
The first shock was the Visigothic sack of Rome on August 24, 410 CE.

The Visigoths (western Goths, a Germanic people who had crossed the Danube into Roman territory decades earlier, been alternately fought and employed by the empire, and become increasingly restless) were led by Alaric (AL-uh-rik), a chieftain who had served as a Roman military commander before turning against the empire when promises of land and status went unfulfilled.
After years of negotiations, threats, and two previous sieges, Alaric's forces entered Rome through the Salarian Gate and plundered the city for three days.
The sack was relatively restrained by ancient standards (churches were generally respected, and the Visigoths, who were Arian Christians, did not massacre the population wholesale).
But the psychological impact was enormous.
Rome had not been taken by a foreign enemy in eight hundred years, not since the Gallic sack of 390 BCE.

Jerome, a Christian scholar writing from Bethlehem, captured the shock: "The city which had taken the whole world was itself taken." Augustine wrote the City of God in direct response, arguing that Rome's fall proved the inadequacy of earthly power and the need to invest hope in the heavenly city.
The deeper problem was not the Visigoths specifically but the broader migration of Germanic peoples into Roman territory, a process that had been accelerating since the late fourth century.
The Battle of Adrianople (ay-dree-uh-NOH-pul) in 378 CE was the watershed.

The Visigoths, who had been admitted into Roman territory as refugees fleeing the Huns (a nomadic steppe people surging westward from Central Asia), were mistreated by corrupt Roman officials, revolted, and destroyed a Roman army at Adrianople in Thrace, killing Emperor Valens (VAY-lenz) on the battlefield.
It was one of the worst Roman military defeats since Cannae.

The empire never recovered the lost legions, and from this point forward, the Roman army was increasingly composed of Germanic foederati (FEH-duh-RAH-tee, barbarian allies serving under their own chiefs in exchange for land and pay within the empire).
The empire was increasingly defended by the very peoples it was trying to defend against.
The Huns themselves arrived as a direct threat in the mid-fifth century.

Attila (AT-ih-luh, ruler of the Hunnic Empire from roughly 434 to 453 CE) united the Hunnic tribes and terrorized both halves of the Roman Empire, extracting massive tributes from Constantinople and launching a devastating invasion of Gaul in 451 CE.

Attila was stopped (though not defeated) at the Battle of the Catalaunian Plains by a coalition of Romans and Visigoths commanded by the Roman general Aetius (ay-EE-shus), one of the last competent Western military commanders.

Attila invaded Italy the following year but withdrew (according to Christian legend, persuaded by Pope Leo I; more likely deterred by plague in his army and the threat of Eastern Roman forces in his rear).
Attila died in 453 CE (reportedly of a nosebleed on his wedding night), and the Hunnic Empire disintegrated immediately, but the damage was done.
The Huns had driven the great migrations of Goths, Vandals, Burgundians, and other peoples into the Western Empire, and those peoples were not leaving.
In 455 CE, the Vandals under King Gaiseric (GY-seh-rik) sacked Rome far more thoroughly than Alaric had.

The Vandals (a Germanic people who had migrated from central Europe to Spain to North Africa, conquering the wealthy province of Africa and its crucial grain supply) looted Rome for two weeks, stripping temples, palaces, and public buildings of everything portable, including (according to tradition) the treasures that Titus had taken from the Temple of Jerusalem nearly four centuries earlier.
The word "vandalism" derives from their name, though the connection is a later invention and probably unfair to the actual Vandals, who were organized raiders, not mindless destroyers.
The final decades of the Western Empire were a parade of puppet emperors manipulated by Germanic generals.
The real power lay with the barbarian commanders who controlled what was left of the Western army.
When Odoacer deposed Romulus Augustulus in 476 CE, he was not overthrowing a functioning state.
He was tidying up a formality.
The Western Empire had already ceased to exist in any meaningful sense.
Its provinces (Gaul, Spain, Britain, Africa) had been taken over by Germanic kingdoms.
Its army was Germanic.
Its treasury was empty.
Its capital had been sacked twice.
The last emperor ruled little more than the Italian peninsula, and even that was contested.

In 438 CE, near the end, Emperor Theodosius II in Constantinople had ordered the compilation of the Codex Theodosianus, the first official compilation of Roman imperial law, systematizing centuries of legislation into a single collection.
The Codex would profoundly influence medieval European law and the later Justinian Code compiled in the sixth century.
Even as the Western state crumbled, the legal tradition survived, embedded in the institutions that the Germanic successor kingdoms would adopt and adapt.
Roman law did not fall with Rome.
It endured, and it shaped every legal system in continental Europe.
Why did the Western Roman Empire fall?
The question has generated more scholarly debate than almost any other in history.

Edward Gibbon's The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire (published 1776-1789) attributed the collapse partly to the enervating effects of Christianity, which diverted energy from civic life to otherworldly concerns.
Modern historians have proposed dozens of explanations, and the honest answer is that no single cause is sufficient.
The fall was overdetermined: so many things went wrong that the question is less "what caused it?" and more "which combination of causes was decisive?" The main candidates: Military pressure.
The Germanic migrations and the Hunnic invasions put sustained pressure on frontiers that the empire could no longer adequately defend.
The army was smaller, less well-funded, and increasingly composed of barbarian foederati whose loyalty was unreliable.
But the eastern half of the empire faced the same pressures (and also the Sassanid Persians) and survived.
Military pressure alone does not explain why the west fell and the east did not.
Economic decline.
The western provinces were poorer than the east.
The loss of Africa's grain supply to the Vandals (after 439 CE) was economically catastrophic.
Tax revenue declined as provinces were lost.
Currency debasement and inflation destroyed savings and disrupted trade.
The wealthy retreated to self-sufficient rural estates, reducing the tax base further.
But the economy had been in decline for decades before the final political collapse.
Economic weakness was a condition, not a cause.
Political dysfunction.
The western court was paralyzed by factionalism, assassination, and the dominance of Germanic generals who used the emperors as puppets.
The eastern court, while not immune to political violence, maintained a more stable institutional structure centered on Constantinople's impregnable position.

The difference was partly geographical (Constantinople was harder to attack than Rome or Ravenna) and partly institutional (the eastern bureaucracy was more professional and less dependent on the military).
Size and geography.
The western empire stretched from Britain to North Africa, an enormous territory connected by sea lanes that became increasingly dangerous.
The eastern empire was more compact, with Constantinople at its center and the Aegean as its internal sea.
Defending the long western frontiers (the Rhine, the Danube, the British coast, the African coast) required more resources than the west could generate.
The loss of revenue base.

As Germanic kingdoms carved out territories within the empire (the Visigoths in Spain and southern Gaul, the Vandals in Africa, the Burgundians in eastern Gaul, the Franks in northern Gaul), the Western Empire lost not just territory but the tax revenue that paid for its army.
Each loss of territory made the remaining territory harder to defend, which led to further losses, in a vicious spiral.
Christianity?
Gibbon's argument has not aged well in its simplest form (the eastern empire was equally Christian and survived).

But Christianity did redirect resources (wealthy Christians donated to the church rather than to civic building projects), create a parallel power structure (bishops competed with imperial officials for authority), and encourage a theology that devalued earthly power in favor of heavenly reward.
Whether this was a cause of decline or merely a symptom is debated.

The most honest answer is probably the most unsatisfying: the Western Empire fell because too many things went wrong simultaneously, and the system, which had survived individual crises repeatedly (the Gallic sack, the Punic Wars, the Crisis of the Third Century), finally encountered a combination of crises that exceeded its capacity to recover.
The eastern half survived because it was richer, better positioned, and better governed.
The western half, poorer, more exposed, and more politically dysfunctional, could not absorb the same shocks.
But here is the question the simple narrative of "fall" obscures: what exactly fell?
The Roman state in the west ended in 476 CE.
Roman civilization did not.

The Germanic kingdoms that replaced the empire spoke Latin (or learned it), adopted Roman law (or adapted it), worshipped in Roman churches (using Roman liturgy), and governed through Roman administrative structures (or imitations of them).

The Frankish king Clovis, who united Gaul in the early sixth century, was baptized as a Catholic Christian, took a Roman title, and governed through a bureaucracy staffed by literate clergy trained in Roman traditions.
The Visigothic law codes were written in Latin and drew heavily on Roman legal principles.

The Pope in Rome, the bishop who had inherited the prestige of the old capital, became the spiritual authority of the western world, conducting business in Latin, preserving Roman texts in monastery libraries, and maintaining an administrative structure modeled on Roman provincial government.
The roads endured.
The aqueducts lasted (some are still functioning).
The legal principles survived in every European legal system.
The Latin language evolved into French, Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, and Romanian.
The calendar Augustus and Caesar gave the world is still in use.
The concrete domes are still standing.

The Catholic Church, the institution that most directly inherited Rome's organizational genius, still governs from Rome, still uses Latin in its liturgy, and still calls its leader Pontifex Maximus, the title of Rome's chief priest.
Rome did not fall.
It transformed.
The empire became the church.
The legions became the feudal armies.
The provinces became the nations.
The language became the romance languages.
The law became the common inheritance of Europe.
The roads became the routes of medieval commerce.

The idea that a single civilization could unite diverse peoples under common law, common language, and common institutions became the aspiration of every European empire that followed, from Charlemagne to Napoleon to the European Union.
The real question is not why Rome fell.
It is how a cluster of mud-brick huts on a marshy riverbank became a system so durable that we are still living in its shadow, fifteen hundred years after the last emperor handed over his crown.
